Parameter Target HbA1c <7% (individualize) Fasting glucose 80-130 mg/dL Postprandial glucose <180 mg/dL BP <130/80 mmHg LDL cholesterol <100 mg/dL (<70 if CAD)

NEET PG: HbA1c target individualized (stricter in young, relaxed in elderly/comorbid)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Gold standard for DM diagnosis? Answer: HbA1c ≥6.5%

  2. Most common chronic complication? Answer: Diabetic neuropathy

  3. Leading cause of blindness in adults? Answer: Diabetic retinopathy

  4. Leading cause of ESRD? Answer: Diabetic nephropathy

  5. Earliest sign of nephropathy? Answer: Microalbuminuria

  6. 1st line drug for Type 2 DM? Answer: Metformin

  7. DKA vs HHS - key difference? Answer: HHS has higher glucose, no ketosis

  8. Pathognomonic for diabetic nephropathy? Answer: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules

docs.title: Complications (5M)
docs.text: "**M**icro **M**acro **M**etabolic **M**ental **M**alaria" - **M**icrovascular (Retinopathy, Nephropathy, Neuropathy) - **M**acrovascular (CAD, Stroke, PAD) - **M**etabolic (DKA, HHS, Hypoglycemia)

docs.title: DKA Treatment (FISH)
docs.text:
• **F**luids
  • **I**nsulin
  • **S**upport (K+)
  • **H**unt for cause

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know diagnostic criteria (HbA1c, FPG, OGTT)
  • Understand Type 1 vs Type 2
  • Learn DKA vs HHS differences
  • Memorize microvascular complications
  • Know treatment algorithm for Type 2 DM
  • Understand metformin contraindications
  • Learn target goals
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on diabetes

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-7 questions)

docs.title: CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (CKD) - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text:
CKD: Kidney damage or GFR <60 ml/min/1.73m² for ≥3 months

Kidney damage markers: - Albuminuria (≥30 mg/24hr) - Hematuria - Structural abnormalities (imaging) - Pathological abnormalities (biopsy)

NEET PG: Duration ≥3 months differentiates CKD from AKI

docs.title: Based on GFR (G Stage)
docs.text: Stage GFR (ml/min/1.73m²) Description Management G1 ≥90 Normal/high (with kidney damage) Treat underlying, slow progression G2 60-89 Mildly decreased Estimate progression G3a 45-59 Mild to moderately decreased Evaluate & treat complications G3b 30-44 Moderately to severely decreased Evaluate & treat complications G4 15-29 Severely decreased Prepare for RRT G5 <15 Kidney failure (ESRD) RRT (dialysis/transplant)

docs.title: Based on Albuminuria (A Stage)
docs.text:
Stage Albumin excretion ACR (mg/g) Description A1 <30 mg/24hr <30 Normal to mildly increased A2 30-300 mg/24hr 30-300 Moderately increased A3 >300 mg/24hr >300 Severely increased

NEET PG High-Yield: - Stage 3: Most common (subdivided into 3a & 3b) - Stage 5 (GFR <15): ESRD, requires dialysis - Albuminuria: Independent risk factor for progression

docs.title: ETIOLOGY
docs.text: Top causes (vary by region):

docs.title: Developed Countries
docs.text:
1. Diabetes mellitus (30-40%) - Most common
  2. Hypertension (25-30%)
  3. Glomerulonephritis (10-15%)
  4. Polycystic kidney disease
  5. Others: Obstructive uropathy, reflux nephropathy

docs.title: Developing Countries (India)
docs.text:
1. Chronic glomerulonephritis (30-40%)
  2. Diabetic nephropathy (30-35%)
  3. Hypertensive nephrosclerosis
  4. Obstructive uropathy

NEET PG: Diabetes = Leading cause of ESRD worldwide

docs.title: Mechanisms of Progression
docs.text:
1. Hyperfiltration theory:
  2. Nephron loss → Remaining nephrons hyperfiltrate

  3. ↑ Intraglomerular pressure → Further damage

  4. Proteinuria: Tubular toxicity

  5. Hypertension: Accelerates progression

  6. Tubulointerstitial fibrosis: Final common pathway

NEET PG: ACE-I/ARBs slow progression (↓ intraglomerular pressure)

docs.title: Uremia (Symptomatic Renal Failure)
docs.text: Usually symptomatic when GFR <10-15 ml/min

docs.title: 1. Fluid & Electrolyte Disturbances
docs.text:
• Hyperkalemia: Life-threatening (ECG changes, arrhythmias)
  • Metabolic acidosis: High anion gap (↓ H+ excretion)
  • Hyperphosphatemia + Hypocalcemia
  • Volume overload: Edema, pulmonary edema, hypertension

docs.title: 2. Cardiovascular
docs.text:
• Hypertension (90%)
  • Pericarditis: Uremic pericarditis (friction rub, ECG: diffuse ST elevation)
  • Accelerated atherosclerosis: #1 cause of death
  • Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)
  • Heart failure

NEET PG: #1 cause of death in CKD = Cardiovascular disease

docs.title: 3. Hematological
docs.text:
• Anemia: Normocytic normochromic
  • Cause: ↓ Erythropoietin production
  • Treatment: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA - epoetin alfa)
  • Bleeding tendency: Platelet dysfunction (↑ BT, normal PT/aPTT)
  • Treatment: Desmopressin (DDAVP), dialysis

docs.title: 4. Renal Osteodystrophy (Mineral & Bone Disorder)
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: 

↓ GFR → ↓ Phosphate excretion → Hyperphosphatemia
        ↓
↓ Calcitriol (Vit D) → Hypocalcemia
        ↓
Secondary hyperparathyroidism (↑ PTH)
        ↓
Bone disease

Types: 1. Osteitis fibrosa cystica: High turnover (↑ PTH) - Brown tumors, bone cysts, subperiosteal resorption 2. Osteomalacia: Low turnover (aluminum toxicity) 3. Adynamic bone disease: Very low turnover 4. Mixed

X-ray findings: - Rugger-jersey spine: Sclerosis of vertebral endplates - Salt & pepper skull: Granular osteopenia - Subperiosteal resorption: Phalanges, clavicle

NEET PG High-Yield: - Hyperphosphatemia + Hypocalcemia + ↑ PTH = CKD - Treatment: Phosphate binders, Vitamin D, Calcimimetics (cinacalcet)

docs.title: 5. Neurological
docs.text:
• Uremic encephalopathy: Confusion, asterixis, seizures, coma
  • Peripheral neuropathy: Restless leg syndrome, sensorimotor neuropathy
  • Autonomic neuropathy

NEET PG: Asterixis (flapping tremor) in uremia

docs.title: 6. Gastrointestinal
docs.text:
• Anorexia, nausea, vomiting
  • Uremic fetor: Ammonia breath (urea → ammonia)
  • Gastritis, peptic ulcer
  • GI bleeding

docs.title: 7. Dermatological
docs.text:
• Pruritus (itching)
  • Uremic frost: Urea crystals on skin (severe uremia)
  • Skin pigmentation: Yellowish-brown

docs.title: 8. Endocrine
docs.text:
• Sexual dysfunction: Impotence, amenorrhea, infertility
  • Glucose intolerance

NEET PG: Uremic frost = Severe uremia (rare now)

docs.title: Assessment of Kidney Function
docs.text:
1. Serum Creatinine: - ↑ Creatinine (inversely proportional to GFR) - Doubles when GFR halves

2. GFR Estimation:

Cockcroft-Gault formula: 

CrCl = [(140 - Age) × Weight in kg] / (72 × Serum Cr)
       × 0.85 (for females)

MDRD (Modification of Diet in Renal Disease): More accurate CKD-EPI: Most accurate (recommended)

3. Urine Analysis: - Proteinuria: Albumin-creatinine ratio (ACR) - Hematuria - Urine sediment: Broad waxy casts (chronic), RBC casts (glomerular)

NEET PG: Broad waxy casts = Chronic kidney disease

docs.title: Laboratory Findings in CKD
docs.text: Parameter Finding Hemoglobin ↓ (Normocytic normochromic anemia) Creatinine ↑ BUN ↑ (BUN:Cr ratio >20:1 if volume depleted) Potassium ↑ (Hyperkalemia) Calcium ↓ (Hypocalcemia) Phosphate ↑ (Hyperphosphatemia) PTH ↑ (Secondary hyperparathyroidism) Bicarbonate ↓ (Metabolic acidosis) Anion gap ↑ Albumin ↓ (Malnutrition, proteinuria)

docs.title: Imaging
docs.text:
USG Kidneys: - Bilateral small kidneys (<9 cm) = Chronic disease - Large kidneys: Diabetes, ADPKD, amyloidosis, HIV nephropathy - Increased echogenicity - Loss of corticomedullary differentiation

NEET PG: Small echogenic kidneys = CKD

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
1. Slow progression
  2. Treat complications
  3. Prepare for RRT

docs.title: 1. Blood Pressure Control
docs.text:
Target: <130/80 mmHg (if proteinuria <140/90)

Drugs: - ACE inhibitors or ARBs: First-line (especially if proteinuria/diabetes) - ↓ Intraglomerular pressure - Antiproteinuric effect - Slow progression - Others: CCBs, diuretics, β-blockers

NEET PG: ACE-I/ARBs = First-line in CKD (renoprotective)

docs.title: 2. Glycemic Control
docs.text: Target HbA1c: <7% (individualized) - Prevents/delays nephropathy

docs.title: 3. Dietary Protein Restriction
docs.text: Target: 0.6-0.8 g/kg/day (Stage 3-5) - ↓ Uremic toxins - Slow progression

docs.title: 4. Control Proteinuria
docs.text: Target: <0.5 g/day - ACE-I/ARBs - SGLT2 inhibitors (new evidence)

docs.title: 1. Anemia
docs.text:
Target Hb: 10-11.5 g/dl (avoid >13 g/dl)

Treatment: - Iron supplementation (oral/IV): Maintain ferritin >100 ng/ml, TSAT >20% - ESA (Erythropoietin): Epoetin alfa, darbepoetin - Start if Hb <10 g/dl despite iron repletion - Adverse effects: Hypertension, thrombosis, pure red cell aplasia (PRCA)

NEET PG: - Correct iron deficiency FIRST before starting ESA - Target Hb 10-11.5 g/dl (↑ mortality if >13)

docs.title: 2. Mineral & Bone Disorder
docs.text:
Step 1: Control Phosphate - Dietary restriction: <800-1000 mg/day - Phosphate binders (with meals): - Calcium-based: Calcium carbonate, calcium acetate (cheap, first-line) - Non-calcium based: Sevelamer, lanthanum (if hypercalcemia, vascular calcification) - Target: 3.5-5.5 mg/dl

Step 2: Correct Hypocalcemia - Vitamin D supplementation: Calcitriol, alfacalcidol - Target Ca: 8.5-9.5 mg/dl

Step 3: Control PTH - Calcimimetics: Cinacalcet (sensitizes Ca-sensing receptor) - ↓ PTH secretion - Use if PTH persistently high despite Vit D

NEET PG: - Phosphate binders: Take with meals - Sevelamer: Non-calcium based (if vascular calcification) - Cinacalcet: Calcimimetic (↓ PTH)

docs.title: 3. Metabolic Acidosis
docs.text:
Target HCO3-: 22-26 mEq/L

Treatment: - Sodium bicarbonate: Oral - Dialysis (if severe)

docs.title: 4. Hyperkalemia
docs.text: Dietary restriction: <2 g/day Avoid: NSAIDs, K-sparing diuretics Treatment: - Diuretics (loop) - Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (kayexalate) - Patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (newer) - Dialysis (if severe)

docs.title: 5. Volume Overload
docs.text: Dietary salt restriction: <2 g/day Diuretics: Loop diuretics (furosemide) Dialysis: If refractory

docs.title: Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT)
docs.text:
Indications for Dialysis (mnemonic: AEIOU): - **A**cidosis (severe, refractory) - **E**lectrolyte abnormalities (hyperkalemia >6.5 mEq/L, refractory) - **I**ngestions (toxins - methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, aspirin) - **O**verload (volume - pulmonary edema, refractory) - **U**remia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)

Start dialysis when: GFR <10-15 ml/min + symptoms

NEET PG: AEIOU = Indications for emergent dialysis

docs.title: 1. Hemodialysis (HD)
docs.text:
Frequency: 3 times/week, 4 hours/session Access: AV fistula (preferred), AV graft, tunneled catheter

Complications: - Hypotension (most common during dialysis) - Muscle cramps - Disequilibrium syndrome: Cerebral edema (rapid urea removal) - Access-related: Infection, thrombosis, steal syndrome

NEET PG: AV fistula = Preferred vascular access (mature in 6-8 weeks)

docs.title: 2. Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)
docs.text:
Types: - CAPD (Continuous Ambulatory PD): 4-5 exchanges/day - APD (Automated PD): Cycler at night

Advantages: - Home-based - Hemodynamic stability - Better residual renal function preservation

Complications: - Peritonitis (most common): Cloudy fluid, abdominal pain - Treatment: Intraperitoneal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime) - Catheter-related: Exit site infection, tunnel infection - Ultrafiltration failure - Hernias - Malnutrition (protein loss in dialysate)

NEET PG: Peritonitis most common complication of PD

docs.title: 3. Kidney Transplantation
docs.text:
Best RRT (survival & quality of life)

Donor types: - Living related (best outcomes) - Living unrelated - Deceased donor (brain-dead, cardiac death)

Immunosuppression: - Induction: Basiliximab (IL-2 receptor antagonist), ATG - Maintenance: - Calcineurin inhibitors: Tacrolimus (preferred) or Cyclosporine - Antimetabolites: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) - Steroids: Prednisolone

Complications: - Acute rejection: Within 6 months - Treatment: Pulse steroids, ATG - Chronic rejection: Months to years - Infections: CMV (most common), PCP, fungal - Malignancy: Skin cancer, PTLD (post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder) - Drug toxicity: Nephrotoxicity (calcineurin inhibitors)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Tacrolimus: Preferred calcineurin inhibitor - CMV: Most common opportunistic infection - PTLD: EBV-related lymphoma

docs.title: CKD vs AKI - Differentiation
docs.text:
Feature CKD AKI Duration ≥3 months <3 months Kidney size Small (<9 cm) Normal/large Anemia Present Absent (unless chronic) Renal osteodystrophy Present Absent Reversibility Irreversible Potentially reversible Progression Gradual Rapid

NEET PG: Small kidneys + anemia = CKD (NOT AKI)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Leading cause of ESRD worldwide? Answer: Diabetes mellitus

  2. Leading cause of death in CKD? Answer: Cardiovascular disease

  3. GFR defining ESRD? Answer: <15 ml/min/1.73m² (Stage 5)

  4. Target Hb in CKD with ESA therapy? Answer: 10-11.5 g/dl

  5. First-line antihypertensive in CKD? Answer: ACE inhibitors or ARBs

  6. Indications for emergent dialysis? Answer: AEIOU

  7. Most common complication of peritoneal dialysis? Answer: Peritonitis

  8. Preferred vascular access for hemodialysis? Answer: AV fistula

docs.title: AEIOU (Dialysis Indications)
docs.text:
• **A**cidosis
  • **E**lectrolytes (hyperkalemia)
  • **I**ngestions (toxins)
  • **O**verload (volume)
  • **U**remia (symptoms)

docs.title: CKD Complications (5 H's)
docs.text:
• **H**ypertension
  • **H**yperkalemia
  • **H**yperphosphatemia
  • **H**ypocalcemia
  • **H**eart disease

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know KDIGO classification (stages 1-5)
  • Understand pathophysiology of progression
  • Learn causes of CKD
  • Know complications (anemia, bone disease, CVD)
  • Understand mineral & bone disorder
  • Learn management strategies
  • Know dialysis indications (AEIOU)
  • Understand transplant immunosuppression
  • Differentiate CKD from AKI
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on CKD

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: ISCHEMIC HEART DISEASE (IHD) - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text: Ischemic heart disease results from myocardial ischemia due to imbalance between oxygen supply and demand, primarily caused by coronary artery disease (CAD).

docs.title: Spectrum of IHD
docs.text:
Stable Angina → Unstable Angina → NSTEMI → STEMI
    ↓              ↓                 ↓        ↓
  Reversible   Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS)
  ischemia     ↓                      ↓        ↓
            No necrosis        Partial  Complete
                              thickness thickness
                               necrosis  necrosis

docs.title: Non-Modifiable
docs.text:
• Age: ♂ >45 years, ♀ >55 years
  • Gender: Male > Female (until menopause)
  • Family history: CAD in first-degree relative (♂ <55, ♀ <65)
  • Genetics: Familial hypercholesterolemia

docs.title: Modifiable
docs.text:
• Hypertension (most important)
  • Diabetes mellitus
  • Dyslipidemia: ↑ LDL, ↓ HDL
  • Smoking (most preventable)
  • Obesity (BMI >30)
  • Sedentary lifestyle
  • Stress

NEET PG: Diabetes = CAD equivalent (same risk as patient with prior MI)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Predictable chest pain triggered by exertion, relieved by rest/nitroglycerin

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Typical Angina (must have ALL 3): 1. Substernal chest discomfort (pressure, tightness, heaviness) 2. Provoked by exertion or emotional stress 3. Relieved by rest or nitroglycerin within 5 minutes

Atypical Angina: 2 out of 3 criteria Non-cardiac chest pain: ≤1 criterion

Levine's Sign: Clenched fist over chest (classic)

Radiation: Left arm, jaw, neck, back, epigastrium

Associated Symptoms: Dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea

NEET PG: Stable angina does NOT occur at rest

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Canadian Cardiovascular Society (CCS) Classification:

Class Activity Limitation I Ordinary activity does not cause angina (angina with strenuous exertion) II Slight limitation (angina walking >2 blocks, stairs >1 flight) III Marked limitation (angina walking 1-2 blocks) IV Inability to perform any activity without angina (angina at rest)

docs.title: ECG
docs.text:
• At rest: Usually normal
  • During angina: ST depression, T-wave inversion (reversible)

docs.title: Exercise Stress Test (TMT - Treadmill Test)
docs.text:
Indications: Diagnosis of stable angina, risk stratification Positive test: - ≥1 mm horizontal/downsloping ST depression - Chest pain during test - Hypotension, arrhythmias

Contraindications: Acute MI (<2 days), unstable angina, severe AS, uncontrolled arrhythmias

NEET PG: TMT positive = ≥1 mm ST depression

docs.title: Stress Imaging
docs.text:
• Stress Echo: Wall motion abnormalities
  • Myocardial perfusion scan (Thallium/Technetium): Reversible perfusion defect

docs.title: Coronary Angiography
docs.text:
Gold standard for diagnosis Indications: - High-risk features on non-invasive tests - Failed medical therapy - Pre-revascularization assessment

Significant stenosis: ≥70% in major coronary artery OR ≥50% in left main

docs.title: Immediate Relief
docs.text: Sublingual nitroglycerin: 0.4 mg (repeat every 5 min × 3 doses)

docs.title: Long-term Management
docs.text:
Goals: 1. Relieve symptoms 2. Prevent MI 3. Improve survival

Medications:

Drug Class Mechanism Drugs Benefits Antiplatelet ↓ Platelet aggregation Aspirin 75-150 mg (all patients) ↓ MI, ↓ Mortality Beta-blockers ↓ HR, ↓ BP, ↓ O2 demand Metoprolol, Atenolol 1st line, ↓ Mortality Nitrates Vasodilation Long-acting nitrates, Isosorbide mononitrate Symptom relief CCB Vasodilation, ↓ HR Amlodipine, Diltiazem If β-blockers contraindicated Statins ↓ LDL, plaque stabilization Atorvastatin 40-80 mg (all patients) ↓ MI, ↓ Mortality ACE inhibitors ↓ Remodeling Ramipril, Enalapril If LV dysfunction, DM, HTN

NEET PG High-Yield: - All IHD patients: Aspirin + Statin - 1st line antianginal: Beta-blocker - Alternative: CCB (if β-blocker contraindicated) - Target LDL: <70 mg/dL (high-risk), <55 mg/dL (very high-risk)

docs.title: Revascularization
docs.text:
PCI (Percutaneous Coronary Intervention): - Indications: 1-2 vessel disease, failed medical therapy - Procedure: Balloon angioplasty + Stent (Drug-eluting stent preferred) - Post-PCI: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) - Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (Clopidogrel/Ticagrelor) × 12 months

CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting): - Indications: - Left main disease (>50% stenosis) - 3-vessel disease - 2-vessel disease with proximal LAD involvement - LV dysfunction with multivessel disease - Diabetes with multivessel disease - Conduits: LIMA (left internal mammary artery) to LAD (best graft patency)

NEET PG: CABG preferred over PCI in left main disease, 3-vessel disease, diabetes with multivessel disease

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Spectrum of conditions caused by acute myocardial ischemia: 1. Unstable Angina (UA) 2. NSTEMI (Non-ST elevation MI) 3. STEMI (ST elevation MI)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Atherosclerotic plaque rupture/erosion
         ↓
    Thrombus formation
         ↓
   Partial occlusion ────→ UA/NSTEMI
         ↓
   Complete occlusion ───→ STEMI

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Angina at rest or with minimal exertion, without myocardial necrosis (normal troponins)

docs.title: Braunwald Classification
docs.text: Class Definition I New-onset, severe, or accelerated angina II Angina at rest (subacute - within 1 month but not within 48 hours) III Angina at rest (acute - within 48 hours)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Crescendo angina: Increasing frequency, severity, duration
  • Angina at rest: >20 minutes
  • New-onset angina: Severe (CCS III or IV)
  • Post-MI angina: <2 weeks after MI

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
• ECG: ST depression, T-wave inversion (NO ST elevation)
  • Cardiac biomarkers: Troponin NEGATIVE

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Myocardial necrosis due to prolonged ischemia

Universal Definition (requires ≥1): 1. ↑ Cardiac troponin + ≥1 of: - Symptoms of ischemia - ECG changes (ST-T changes, LBBB, Q waves) - Imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium 2. Cardiac death with symptoms suggestive of MI

docs.title: Types of MI
docs.text:
Type Cause Type 1 Spontaneous (plaque rupture, erosion) - MOST COMMON Type 2 Secondary to ischemia (↑ demand or ↓ supply): Anemia, tachycardia, hypotension, vasospasm Type 3 Cardiac death before biomarkers obtained Type 4a PCI-related Type 4b Stent thrombosis Type 5 CABG-related

NEET PG: Type 1 MI = Spontaneous (atherosclerotic plaque rupture)

docs.title: Diagnostic Criteria
docs.text: ECG (must have ≥1): 1. New ST elevation at J-point in ≥2 contiguous leads: - ≥2.5 mm in men <40 years (V2-V3) - ≥2 mm in men ≥40 years (V2-V3) - ≥1.5 mm in women (V2-V3) - ≥1 mm in other leads 2. New LBBB (Sgarbossa criteria)

docs.title: ECG Localization
docs.text:
ECG Leads Territory Artery Complications V1-V4 Anterior/Anteroseptal LAD LV dysfunction, CHF, arrhythmias V5-V6, I, aVL Lateral LCX Usually benign II, III, aVF Inferior RCA (80%) Bradycardia, AV blocks, RV infarct V7-V9 Posterior RCA/LCX Often with inferior MI V3R-V4R RV infarction RCA Hypotension, ↑ JVP

NEET PG High-Yield: - Anterior MI: Worst prognosis (large infarct size) - Inferior MI: Associated with bradycardia, AV blocks (vagal stimulation) - RV infarct: Hypotension, ↑ JVP, clear lungs (triad) - Posterior MI: Tall R waves in V1-V2, ST depression (reciprocal)

docs.title: ECG Evolution of STEMI
docs.text: Time ECG Changes Minutes-hours Hyperacute T waves → ST elevation Hours-days ST elevation, Q wave development Days-weeks Q waves persist, ST normalizes, T-wave inversion Weeks-months Persistent Q waves (pathological Q = ≥0.04 sec width, ≥25% of R wave depth)

docs.title: Cardiac Biomarkers
docs.text:
Marker Rise Peak Return to Normal Notes Troponin I/T 3-4 hours 24-48 hours 7-14 days Most sensitive & specific (Gold standard) CK-MB 4-6 hours 24 hours 48-72 hours Useful for re-infarction Myoglobin 1-2 hours 6-8 hours 24 hours Earliest, but non-specific LDH 12-24 hours 48-72 hours 10-14 days Delayed marker

NEET PG: - Troponin: Most sensitive & specific (diagnostic) - CK-MB: Useful for re-infarction (normalizes faster) - Myoglobin: Earliest marker, but not specific

docs.title: Immediate Management (MONA)
docs.text:
**M**orphine (if severe pain, pulmonary edema) **O**xygen (if SpO2 <90%) **N**itroglycerin (sublingual, then IV) **A**spirin 300 mg (chew immediately)

NEET PG: Aspirin is the FIRST drug to give (chew, not swallow)

docs.title: Reperfusion Therapy - TIME IS MUSCLE
docs.text:
Goal: Door-to-needle (thrombolysis) <30 min OR Door-to-balloon (PCI) <90 min

Indications: STEMI within 12 hours of symptom onset

Primary PCI (Preferred if available): - Advantages: ↓ Mortality, ↓ Reinfarction, ↓ Stroke vs thrombolysis - Indications: - Available within 90-120 minutes - Cardiogenic shock - Contraindications to thrombolysis - Failed thrombolysis - DAPT: Aspirin + Ticagrelor/Prasugrel (preferred) or Clopidogrel

Thrombolytic Therapy (if PCI not available):

Agents: - Streptokinase: 1.5 million units IV over 60 min (non-fibrin specific, antigenic) - Tenecteplase (TNK): Single IV bolus (fibrin-specific, preferred) - Alteplase (tPA): 90-min infusion

Contraindications:

Absolute Relative Prior ICH Ischemic stroke >3 months ago Ischemic stroke <3 months BP >180/110 mmHg Intracranial neoplasm/AVM Active bleeding (not menses) Active internal bleeding Traumatic CPR Suspected aortic dissection Recent surgery (<3 weeks) Significant head trauma <3 months Pregnancy

NEET PG High-Yield: - Primary PCI > Thrombolysis (if available within 120 min) - Thrombolysis: If PCI not available, give within 12 hours - Streptokinase: Cannot repeat within 5 years (antigenic) - Absolute contraindication: Prior ICH, ischemic stroke <3 months

docs.title: Adjunctive Therapy
docs.text:
Antiplatelet: - Aspirin: 75-150 mg daily (lifelong) - P2Y12 inhibitor: Ticagrelor/Prasugrel (preferred) or Clopidogrel × 12 months

Anticoagulation: - Heparin: UFH or Enoxaparin (with thrombolysis or PCI)

Beta-blockers: - Start within 24 hours (if no contraindications) - Contraindications: Cardiogenic shock, heart block, severe bronchospasm

ACE inhibitors: - Start within 24 hours (all patients, especially if anterior MI, LV dysfunction, HTN, DM) - Contraindications: Hypotension, renal failure

Statins: - High-intensity statin: Atorvastatin 80 mg (all patients) - Start immediately

NEET PG: All post-MI patients get Aspirin + Statin + Beta-blocker + ACE inhibitor

docs.title: Diagnostic Criteria
docs.text:
• Symptoms of acute ischemia
  • ↑ Troponin (myocardial necrosis)
  • NO ST elevation on ECG (may have ST depression, T-wave inversion)

docs.title: Risk Stratification
docs.text:
GRACE Score (Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events): - Age, HR, BP, creatinine, cardiac arrest, ST deviation, troponin - High-risk: >140 (early invasive strategy)

TIMI Risk Score (0-7 points): - Age ≥65 - ≥3 CAD risk factors - Known CAD (stenosis ≥50%) - Aspirin use in past 7 days - ≥2 anginal events in 24 hours - ST deviation ≥0.5 mm - ↑ Cardiac biomarkers

High-risk features: - Recurrent angina - Hemodynamic instability - LV dysfunction (EF <40%) - Sustained VT - ↑↑ Troponin - Dynamic ST-T changes

docs.title: Medical Therapy (Same as STEMI)
docs.text:
• Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (DAPT)
  • Anticoagulation: Fondaparinux/Enoxaparin/UFH
  • Beta-blockers
  • ACE inhibitors
  • Statins
  • Nitrates (symptom relief)

docs.title: Invasive Strategy
docs.text:
Early invasive (<24 hours): High-risk patients Delayed invasive (25-72 hours): Intermediate-risk Conservative: Low-risk, stable patients

NEET PG: NSTEMI = Medical therapy + Risk stratification → Invasive strategy if high-risk

docs.title: Early (<24 hours)
docs.text:
1. Arrhythmias (most common cause of death in 1st hour):
  2. VF/VT: Leading cause of sudden death
  3. Bradycardia: Inferior MI (vagal stimulation)

  4. AV blocks: Inferior MI (transient, usually resolves)

  5. Cardiogenic Shock: Pump failure (>40% LV damage)

  6. LV Failure: Pulmonary edema

docs.title: Subacute (1-7 days)
docs.text:
1. Free Wall Rupture (3-7 days):
  2. Sudden hemodynamic collapse, cardiac tamponade

  3. Treatment: Emergency surgery

  4. Ventricular Septal Rupture (3-7 days):

  5. Harsh pansystolic murmur, thrill
  6. Diagnosis: Echo showing VSD

  7. Treatment: Surgery

  8. Papillary Muscle Rupture (3-7 days):

  9. Sudden-onset severe MR
  10. Posteromedial papillary muscle (RCA) more common

  11. Treatment: Surgery

  12. Pericarditis (2-4 days): Fibrinous pericarditis

  13. Pleuritic chest pain, pericardial rub
  14. Treatment: Aspirin/NSAIDs

docs.title: Late (>1 week)
docs.text:
1. Dressler's Syndrome (2-10 weeks):
  2. Autoimmune pericarditis (antibodies against myocardium)
  3. Fever, pericardial rub, pleural effusion

  4. Treatment: Aspirin/NSAIDs, steroids if severe

  5. LV Aneurysm:

  6. Persistent ST elevation (weeks after MI)
  7. Echo: Dyskinetic wall motion
  8. Complications: Mural thrombus, arrhythmias, CHF

  9. Treatment: Anticoagulation, surgery if symptomatic

  10. Mural Thrombus: Risk of embolization

    • Treatment: Anticoagulation × 3-6 months

NEET PG High-Yield: - Most common cause of death in 1st hour: VF - Free wall rupture: Sudden death, tamponade (3-7 days) - VSD rupture: Pansystolic murmur, step-up in O2 (3-7 days) - Papillary muscle rupture: Acute severe MR (3-7 days) - Dressler's syndrome: Autoimmune, 2-10 weeks, fever + pericarditis

docs.title: Secondary Prevention (Post-MI)
docs.text:
Medications (Lifelong unless contraindicated): 1. Aspirin 75-150 mg 2. P2Y12 inhibitor (Ticagrelor/Clopidogrel) × 12 months 3. Beta-blocker (Metoprolol, Carvedilol) 4. ACE inhibitor (or ARB if intolerant) 5. Statin (Atorvastatin 80 mg)

Mnemonic: "**A**ll **P**ost-MI **B**aby **A**ce **S**tatin"

Lifestyle Modifications: - Smoking cessation - Cardiac rehabilitation - Diet (Mediterranean diet) - Exercise (30 min, 5 days/week) - Weight loss (BMI <25) - BP control (<130/80) - LDL goal (<70 mg/dL) - HbA1c <7% (if diabetic)

NEET PG: All post-MI patients: ABCDE (Aspirin + Beta-blocker + Clopidogrel + ACEI + Statin) + Lifestyle

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most important modifiable risk factor for IHD? Answer: Hypertension

  2. Gold standard investigation for CAD? Answer: Coronary angiography

  3. First drug to give in STEMI? Answer: Aspirin 300 mg (chew)

  4. Preferred reperfusion in STEMI? Answer: Primary PCI (if available within 120 min)

  5. Most common cause of death in first hour of MI? Answer: Ventricular fibrillation

  6. Difference between UA and NSTEMI? Answer: Troponin (negative in UA, positive in NSTEMI)

  7. Most common complication in first 48 hours post-MI? Answer: Arrhythmias

  8. Dressler's syndrome occurs when? Answer: 2-10 weeks post-MI (autoimmune)

docs.title: STEMI Management
docs.text: "MONA GREETS HER PATIENT" - **M**orphine - **O**xygen - **N**itroglycerin - **A**spirin - **G**lycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitors (if PCI) - **R**eperfusion (PCI/Thrombolysis) - **E**noxaparin (anticoagulation) - **H**eparin - **P**rasugrel/Ticagrelor (P2Y12 inhibitor) - **A**CE inhibitor - **T**icagrelor - **S**tatin - **B**eta-blocker

docs.title: Complications of MI (Timeline)
docs.text: "Very Bad Faulty Valve Pump Died" - **V**F/VT (hours) - **B**radycardia (hours) - **F**ree wall rupture (3-7 days) - **V**SD (3-7 days) - **P**apillary rupture (3-7 days) - **D**ressler's (2-10 weeks)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know all risk factors for IHD
  • Understand stable vs unstable angina
  • Learn ECG localization of MI
  • Memorize cardiac biomarker timeline
  • Know indications for PCI vs CABG
  • Understand STEMI management algorithm
  • Learn all MI complications and timeline
  • Know secondary prevention medications
  • Practice ECG interpretation
  • Solve 50+ MCQs on IHD/ACS

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 6-8 questions)

docs.title: HEART FAILURE - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text: Heart Failure (HF): Clinical syndrome resulting from structural or functional cardiac disorder that impairs ability of ventricle to fill or eject blood.

docs.title: By Ejection Fraction
docs.text:
Type EF Characteristics HFrEF <40% Systolic dysfunction, dilated LV HFmrEF 40-49% Mid-range HFpEF ≥50% Diastolic dysfunction, preserved EF

NEET PG: HFrEF = Systolic dysfunction, HFpEF = Diastolic dysfunction

docs.title: By Time Course
docs.text:
• Acute HF: Rapid onset (hours-days)
  • Chronic HF: Gradual onset (months-years)

docs.title: By Side Affected
docs.text:
• Left HF: Pulmonary congestion
  • Right HF: Systemic congestion
  • Biventricular: Both

docs.title: NYHA Functional Classification
docs.text:
Class Symptoms I No limitation, ordinary activity does not cause symptoms II Slight limitation, comfortable at rest, ordinary activity causes symptoms III Marked limitation, less than ordinary activity causes symptoms IV Symptoms at rest, unable to carry out any activity

NEET PG: NYHA classification used for symptom assessment and treatment decisions

docs.title: Left Heart Failure (Most Common)
docs.text:
1. Ischemic heart disease (60-70%) - MOST COMMON
  2. Hypertension
  3. Valvular disease (AS, AR, MR)
  4. Dilated cardiomyopathy
  5. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy

docs.title: Right Heart Failure
docs.text:
1. Left heart failure (most common cause)
  2. Chronic lung disease (Cor pulmonale)
  3. Pulmonary HTN
  4. Tricuspid/Pulmonary valve disease
  5. RV infarction

NEET PG: IHD is most common cause of HF worldwide

docs.title: Frank-Starling Mechanism
docs.text:
↓ Cardiac Output
     ↓
Compensatory Mechanisms:
  ↓
1. ↑ Preload (↑ EDV) ──→ Initially ↑ SV
2. ↑ Heart rate         (Frank-Starling)
3. ↑ Contractility         ↓
4. Ventricular hypertrophy  Eventually maladaptive
5. RAAS activation      ↓
6. SNS activation    Decompensation
     ↓
Fluid retention, Vasoconstriction
     ↓
HEART FAILURE

docs.title: Neurohormonal Activation
docs.text:
RAAS: - ↑ Angiotensin II → Vasoconstriction, ↑ Afterload - ↑ Aldosterone → Na+ & water retention, fibrosis

Sympathetic: - ↑ Norepinephrine → ↑ HR, vasoconstriction, myocyte toxicity

ADH: - Water retention → Hyponatremia

Natriuretic Peptides (Compensatory): - BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide) → Vasodilation, natriuresis - ANP (Atrial natriuretic peptide)

NEET PG: BNP released in response to ventricular stretch

docs.title: Left Heart Failure - Pulmonary Congestion
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Dyspnea (exertional → at rest) - Orthopnea: Dyspnea when lying flat (ask: how many pillows?) - Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND): Sudden nighttime dyspnea - Fatigue, weakness - Cough (dry or frothy pink sputum) - Nocturia

Signs: - S3 gallop (ventricular gallop) - Hallmark - S4 (if diastolic dysfunction) - Displaced apex (laterally and downward) - Tachycardia - Bibasilar crackles (pulmonary edema) - Pleural effusion

NEET PG High-Yield: - S3 gallop: Most specific sign of HF - Orthopnea + PND: Classic for LVF - Laterally displaced apex: LV dilatation

docs.title: Right Heart Failure - Systemic Congestion
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Peripheral edema (ankle/leg) - Abdominal distension (ascites) - Anorexia, nausea - RUQ pain (hepatic congestion)

Signs: - ↑ JVP (>4 cm above sternal angle) - Hepatojugular reflux (positive) - Hepatomegaly (tender, pulsatile) - Ascites - Peripheral pitting edema - Parasternal heave (RV hypertrophy)

NEET PG: ↑ JVP + hepatomegaly + edema = RHF triad

docs.title: ECG
docs.text:
• Not diagnostic but identifies underlying cause
  • LVH, Q waves (prior MI), arrhythmias (AF)

docs.title: Chest X-ray
docs.text:
Findings in Pulmonary Edema (mnemonic: ABCDE): - A**lveolar edema (bat's wing appearance) - **B-lines (Kerley B lines - interstitial edema) - **C**ardiomegaly (CTR >0.5) - **D**ilated upper lobe vessels (cephalization) - **E**ffusion (pleural)

NEET PG: Cardiomegaly = CTR >0.5 on PA chest X-ray

docs.title: Echocardiography - MOST IMPORTANT
docs.text:
Provides: - Ejection fraction (diagnostic, prognostic) - Chamber size, wall thickness - Regional wall motion abnormalities - Valvular function - Diastolic function

Normal EF: 55-65% HFrEF: EF <40% HFpEF: EF ≥50%

docs.title: BNP (B-type Natriuretic Peptide)
docs.text:
Use: Diagnosis + Prognosis

BNP Level Interpretation <100 pg/mL HF unlikely 100-400 pg/mL Possible HF >400 pg/mL HF likely

NT-proBNP (alternative): - <300 pg/mL: HF unlikely - >450 pg/mL (<50 years) or >900 pg/mL (≥50 years): HF likely

NEET PG: - BNP >100 pg/mL: Suggests HF - Elevated in: HF, renal failure, PE, AF - Low in: Obesity (false negative)

docs.title: Other Tests
docs.text:
• CBC: Anemia (exacerbates HF)
  • Renal function: Baseline before ACE-I/diuretics
  • Electrolytes: Hyponatremia (poor prognosis)
  • Liver function: Hepatic congestion
  • TSH: Rule out thyroid dysfunction
  • Troponin: If ACS suspected
  • Coronary angiography: If IHD suspected

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
1. Improve symptoms
  2. Reduce hospitalizations
  3. Improve survival

docs.title: Lifestyle Modifications
docs.text:
• Salt restriction: <2g/day
  • Fluid restriction: 1.5-2 L/day (if severe HF)
  • Weight monitoring: Daily (report gain >2kg in 3 days)
  • Exercise (cardiac rehabilitation)
  • Avoid NSAIDs, alcohol
  • Smoking cessation

docs.title: Drugs that IMPROVE SURVIVAL (Use in ALL patients)
docs.text:
1. ACE Inhibitors (1st line)

Examples: Enalapril, Ramipril, Lisinopril

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality (20-30%) - ↓ Hospitalizations - ↓ LV remodeling

Mechanism: Block RAAS

Dose: Start low, titrate up

Adverse Effects: - Cough (10-15%) - Due to bradykinin accumulation - Hypotension - Hyperkalemia - Renal dysfunction - Angioedema (rare)

Contraindications: - Bilateral renal artery stenosis - Pregnancy - Angioedema history - K+ >5.5 mEq/L

NEET PG: If ACE-I intolerant (due to cough) → ARB (Valsartan, Losartan)

2. Beta-blockers (Start AFTER ACE-I)

Agents: Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate, Bisoprolol

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality (30-35%) - ↓ Sudden death - ↑ EF

Mechanism: Block sympathetic overactivity

Important: - Start at low dose, titrate slowly - May worsen symptoms initially (start when stable) - Do NOT stop abruptly (rebound)

Contraindications: - Bradycardia (<50 bpm) - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Severe asthma - Cardiogenic shock

NEET PG: Beta-blockers reduce mortality in HFrEF (proven: Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate, Bisoprolol)

3. Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonists (MRA)

Agents: Spironolactone, Eplerenone

Indications: - EF ≤35% - NYHA II-IV despite ACE-I + Beta-blocker

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality (30%) - ↓ Hospitalization - Reduce fibrosis

Adverse Effects: - Hyperkalemia (monitor K+) - Gynecomastia (spironolactone > eplerenone) - Renal dysfunction

Contraindications: - K+ >5 mEq/L - Creatinine >2.5 mg/dL

NEET PG: Spironolactone causes gynecomastia (anti-androgen effect); Eplerenone does not

4. ARNI (Angiotensin Receptor-Neprilysin Inhibitor)

Agent: Sacubitril/Valsartan (Entresto)

Mechanism: - Valsartan: ARB (blocks AT1 receptor) - Sacubitril: Neprilysin inhibitor (↑ natriuretic peptides)

Indication: HFrEF (EF ≤40%), NYHA II-IV, instead of ACE-I/ARB

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality (better than ACE-I alone) - ↓ Hospitalization

Important: - Do NOT use with ACE-I (risk of angioedema) - Wash-out period: 36 hours after stopping ACE-I

NEET PG: ARNI superior to ACE-I in HFrEF, but cannot combine with ACE-I

5. SGLT2 Inhibitors

Agents: Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality - ↓ HF hospitalization - Works in diabetics AND non-diabetics

Mechanism: Inhibit SGLT2 in kidney → Glycosuria, natriuresis

NEET PG: SGLT2i beneficial in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status

docs.title: Drugs that IMPROVE SYMPTOMS (Add as needed)
docs.text:
1. Diuretics (Loop > Thiazide)

Agents: Furosemide, Bumetanide, Torsemide

Indication: Fluid overload (edema, dyspnea)

Benefits: ↓ Symptoms, ↓ Hospitalizations (NO mortality benefit)

Monitoring: Daily weight, electrolytes, renal function

Adverse Effects: - Hypokalemia - Hypomagnesemia - Hyponatremia - Renal dysfunction - Ototoxicity (high doses)

NEET PG: Diuretics improve symptoms but NOT mortality

2. Digoxin

Mechanism: ↑ Contractility (inhibits Na-K ATPase)

Indications: - HFrEF with atrial fibrillation (rate control) - Persistent symptoms despite optimal therapy

Benefits: ↓ Symptoms, ↓ Hospitalization (NO mortality benefit)

Therapeutic level: 0.5-0.9 ng/mL (lower target in HF)

Toxicity: Nausea, visual changes (yellow halos), arrhythmias

NEET PG: Digoxin does NOT improve survival in HF

3. Hydralazine + Isosorbide Dinitrate

Indication: - African-American patients (with standard therapy) - ACE-I/ARB/ARNI intolerant

Benefits: ↓ Mortality (in African-Americans)

4. Ivabradine

Mechanism: Inhibits If channel in SA node → ↓ HR

Indication: Sinus rhythm, HR >70 bpm despite beta-blocker

NEET PG: Ivabradine for HR control if HR remains >70 despite max beta-blocker

docs.title: Device Therapy
docs.text:
1. ICD (Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator)

Indication: - EF ≤35% despite optimal medical therapy - Prior cardiac arrest/sustained VT - Primary prevention of sudden cardiac death

Benefits: ↓ Sudden cardiac death

NEET PG: ICD indicated if EF ≤35% (NYHA II-III) despite 3 months optimal therapy

2. CRT (Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy)

Indication: - EF ≤35% - NYHA III-IV despite optimal therapy - LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms (sinus rhythm)

Mechanism: Biventricular pacing → Synchronizes contraction

Benefits: ↑ EF, ↓ Symptoms, ↓ Mortality

NEET PG: CRT indicated in HFrEF + LBBB (wide QRS ≥150 ms)

docs.title: Surgical Options
docs.text:
1. Coronary Revascularization (PCI/CABG) - If IHD underlying cause + viable myocardium

2. Valve Repair/Replacement - If significant valvular disease

3. LV Assist Device (LVAD) - Bridge to transplant - Destination therapy (not transplant candidate)

4. Heart Transplantation - End-stage HF refractory to medical therapy - <65 years, no contraindications

docs.title: Management of HFpEF (Preserved EF)
docs.text:
No proven therapies to reduce mortality

Goals: Control symptoms, manage comorbidities

Treatment: 1. Diuretics: For congestion 2. BP control: ACE-I/ARB, CCB 3. Rate control: If AF (beta-blocker, digoxin) 4. SGLT2 inhibitors: Emerging evidence (dapagliflozin) 5. Treat comorbidities: HTN, AF, DM, obesity, sleep apnea

Avoid: Beta-blockers (may worsen diastolic dysfunction), high-dose diuretics

NEET PG: HFpEF management = Symptom control + Treat comorbidities (no mortality benefit proven)

docs.title: Clinical Presentation
docs.text:
• Acute dyspnea at rest
  • Pulmonary edema
  • ↑ JVP
  • Hypoxia

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Immediate: 1. Position: Sit upright (↓ preload) 2. Oxygen: Maintain SpO2 >90% 3. IV loop diuretics: Furosemide 20-40 mg IV (↑ dose if already on oral diuretics) 4. Nitroglycerin: IV infusion (vasodilator, ↓ preload/afterload)

If severe/pulmonary edema: 5. Non-invasive ventilation: BiPAP/CPAP 6. Morphine: 2-4 mg IV (if severe dyspnea) - Use cautiously

NEET PG: Acute HF = Upright position + O2 + IV diuretics + Nitroglycerin

Inotropes (if cardiogenic shock, hypotension): - Dobutamine: β1 agonist (↑ contractility) - Milrinone: Phosphodiesterase inhibitor - Norepinephrine: If persistent hypotension

Ultrafiltration: If refractory to diuretics

docs.title: Complications of Heart Failure
docs.text:
1. Cardiogenic shock
  2. Arrhythmias: AF (most common), VT/VF
  3. Thromboembolic events: Stroke, PE (anticoagulate if AF or LV thrombus)
  4. Renal dysfunction (Cardiorenal syndrome)
  5. Hepatic dysfunction (Cardiac cirrhosis)
  6. Sudden cardiac death

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Poor prognostic factors: - NYHA IV - Low EF (<20%) - Hyponatremia (Na <135) - High BNP - Renal dysfunction - Cachexia - Frequent hospitalizations

NEET PG: Hyponatremia is poor prognostic marker in HF

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most common cause of HF worldwide? Answer: Ischemic heart disease

  2. Most specific sign of heart failure? Answer: S3 gallop

  3. Investigation of choice for HF? Answer: Echocardiography (assess EF)

  4. BNP level diagnostic of HF? Answer: >100 pg/mL (>400 highly suggestive)

  5. Drugs that reduce mortality in HFrEF? Answer: ACE-I, Beta-blockers, MRA, ARNI, SGLT2i

  6. Which beta-blocker proven in HF? Answer: Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate, Bisoprolol

  7. Indication for ICD in HF? Answer: EF ≤35% despite 3 months optimal therapy

  8. Drug causing gynecomastia in HF? Answer: Spironolactone

docs.title: HFrEF Drugs (Mortality Benefit)
docs.text: "ACE BETA MARS" - ACE inhibitor - BETA blocker - **M**ineralocorticoid antagonist (MRA) - **A**RNI - **S**GLT2 inhibitor

docs.title: Chest X-ray in Pulmonary Edema
docs.text: "ABCDE" - A**lveolar edema - **B-lines (Kerley) - **C**ardiomegaly - **D**ilated upper lobe vessels - **E**ffusion

docs.title: Acute HF Management
docs.text: "LMNOP" - **L**oop diuretics - **M**orphine - **N**itrates - **O**xygen - **P**osition (upright)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know difference between HFrEF and HFpEF
  • Learn NYHA classification
  • Understand pathophysiology of HF
  • Know clinical features of LVF vs RVF
  • Learn all drugs that reduce mortality
  • Understand indications for ICD and CRT
  • Know management of acute HF
  • Memorize BNP levels
  • Learn chest X-ray findings
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on heart failure

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-7 questions)

docs.title: Arrhythmias - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Atrial Fibrillation (AF)
  3. Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)
  4. Heart Blocks
  5. Other Important Arrhythmias
  6. Anti-Arrhythmic Drugs
  7. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  8. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Arrhythmias are disorders of cardiac rhythm caused by abnormalities in impulse formation, impulse conduction, or both.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
By Heart Rate: - Tachyarrhythmias: Heart rate >100 bpm - Bradyarrhythmias: Heart rate <60 bpm

By Origin: - Supraventricular: Atria or AV node - Ventricular: Ventricles

By Mechanism: - Re-entry: Most common (AF, VT, AVNRT) - Abnormal Automaticity: Ectopic pacemaker - Triggered Activity: EADs (Torsades), DADs (Digoxin toxicity)

docs.title: Mechanisms - "RAT"
docs.text:
R - Re-entry (Most common)
A - Automaticity
T - Triggered activity

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia characterized by rapid, irregular atrial activation without discrete P waves.

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Prevalence: 1-2% general population
  • Increases with age: 10% in >80 years
  • Risk of stroke: 5x increased

docs.title: Etiology - "PIRATES"
docs.text:
P - Pulmonary disease (COPD, PE)
I - Ischemic heart disease (MI, CAD)
R - Rheumatic heart disease (Mitral stenosis)
A - Alcohol (Holiday heart syndrome)
T - Thyrotoxicosis
E - Embolism (Pulmonary)
S - Sepsis, Surgery

Additional Causes: - Hypertension (Most common in developed countries) - Mitral valve disease (Most common worldwide) - Cardiomyopathies - Congenital heart disease (ASD) - Pericarditis - Sick sinus syndrome

docs.title: 1. Based on Duration
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   AF CLASSIFICATION                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ First detected      → First episode documented          │
│ Paroxysmal          → Terminates within 7 days          │
│ Persistent          → Lasts >7 days                     │
│ Long-standing       → Lasts >12 months                  │
│ Permanent           → Accepted, no cardioversion        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 2. Lone AF
docs.text:
• AF in patients <60 years
  • No structural heart disease
  • Good prognosis

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Mechanism: Multiple micro-re-entry circuits in atria

Key Changes: 1. Atrial rate: 300-600 bpm (irregular) 2. Ventricular rate: 120-180 bpm (irregularly irregular) 3. Loss of atrial kick: ↓ Cardiac output by 20-30% 4. Atrial stasis: Thrombus formation (LAA - Left Atrial Appendage)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Palpitations (Most common) - Dyspnea, fatigue - Chest discomfort - Dizziness, syncope - Some patients asymptomatic (detected incidentally)

Signs: - Irregularly irregular pulse (Hallmark) - Pulse deficit (Radial pulse < Apical rate) - Varying intensity of S1 - Absent 'a' wave in JVP

Complications: 1. Thromboembolism (Stroke - most serious) 2. Heart failure (Tachycardia-induced) 3. Hypotension 4. Cardiomyopathy

docs.title: ECG - Diagnostic
docs.text:
Key Features: 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AF ON ECG                               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. Absent P waves                                    │
│ 2. Fibrillatory waves (f waves)                      │
│ 3. Irregularly irregular QRS complexes               │
│ 4. Narrow QRS (unless bundle branch block)           │
│ 5. Ventricular rate: Usually 120-180 bpm             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

AF with RVR (Rapid Ventricular Response): Rate >100 bpm

docs.title: Other Investigations
docs.text:
• Echocardiography:
  • LA size (>4 cm - high risk)
  • LV function
  • Valvular disease

  • Thrombus detection (TEE more sensitive)

  • Thyroid function tests: Rule out thyrotoxicosis

  • Holter monitoring: Paroxysmal AF
  • Chest X-ray: Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion

docs.title: 1. Rate Control vs Rhythm Control
docs.text:
Rate Control (Preferred in most patients): - Target: Resting HR <110 bpm (lenient) or <80 bpm (strict) - First-line drugs: - Beta-blockers (Metoprolol, Bisoprolol) - DOC - Calcium channel blockers (Diltiazem, Verapamil) - Digoxin (Only in sedentary/HF patients)

Rhythm Control (Cardioversion): - Indications: - Symptomatic despite rate control - First episode - Young patients - AF precipitated by reversible cause

  • Methods:
  • Electrical cardioversion: 200-360 J (synchronized)
  • Pharmacological: Flecainide, Propafenone, Amiodarone

<48 hours rule: - AF duration <48 hours: Cardioversion safe (low thrombus risk) - AF duration >48 hours or unknown: - Anticoagulate 3 weeks before cardioversion - Or TEE to rule out thrombus - Continue anticoagulation 4 weeks after

docs.title: 2. Anticoagulation - MOST IMPORTANT
docs.text:
CHA₂DS₂-VASc Score (Stroke Risk):

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CHA₂DS₂-VASc SCORE                       │
├────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────┤
│ C - CHF/LV dysfunction         │      1 point     │
│ H - Hypertension               │      1 point     │
│ A₂ - Age ≥75 years             │      2 points    │
│ D - Diabetes mellitus          │      1 point     │
│ S₂ - Stroke/TIA/TE history     │      2 points    │
│ V - Vascular disease           │      1 point     │
│ A - Age 65-74 years            │      1 point     │
│ Sc - Sex category (Female)     │      1 point     │
├────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────┤
│ Maximum Score: 9                                  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Interpretation:
Score 0 (Male) or 1 (Female): Low risk → No anticoagulation
Score 1 (Male): Consider anticoagulation
Score ≥2: Anticoagulation recommended

HAS-BLED Score (Bleeding Risk):

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           HAS-BLED SCORE                        │
├──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────┤
│ H - Hypertension (>160 mmHg)     │   1 point    │
│ A - Abnormal renal/liver function│   1-2 points │
│ S - Stroke history               │   1 point    │
│ B - Bleeding history             │   1 point    │
│ L - Labile INR                   │   1 point    │
│ E - Elderly (>65 years)          │   1 point    │
│ D - Drugs/Alcohol                │   1-2 points │
├──────────────────────────────────┴──────────────┤
│ Score ≥3: High bleeding risk (CAUTION)          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Anticoagulation Options:

1. Vitamin K Antagonists: - Warfarin: Target INR 2-3 - Requires regular monitoring

2. DOACs (Direct Oral Anticoagulants) - Preferred: - Dabigatran: Direct thrombin inhibitor - Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban: Factor Xa inhibitors - Advantages: No monitoring, fixed dose, fewer drug interactions - Disadvantages: No reversal agent (except Idarucizumab for Dabigatran), cost

DOAC Contraindications: - Severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 ml/min) - Mechanical heart valves - Mitral stenosis (Warfarin preferred)

docs.title: 3. Special Situations
docs.text:
Hemodynamically Unstable AF: - Immediate DC cardioversion - No waiting for anticoagulation

Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) with AF: - AVOID: Digoxin, Beta-blockers, CCBs (may cause VF) - Use: Procainamide, Ibutilide, or DC cardioversion

docs.title: 4. Catheter Ablation
docs.text:
• Indications:
  • Symptomatic paroxysmal AF refractory to drugs
  • Patient preference

  • Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy

  • Procedure: Pulmonary vein isolation (PVI)

  • Success rate: 60-80%

docs.title: 5. Surgical Options
docs.text:
• MAZE procedure: Creates scar tissue to block abnormal circuits
  • LAA closure: Watchman device (alternative to anticoagulation)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: ≥3 consecutive ventricular beats at rate >100 bpm

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
By Duration: - Non-sustained VT (NSVT): <30 seconds - Sustained VT: ≥30 seconds or requires termination

By Morphology: - Monomorphic VT: Same QRS morphology (single focus) - Polymorphic VT: Varying QRS morphology - Torsades de Pointes: Special type with twisting QRS

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
With Structural Heart Disease (90%): - Ischemic heart disease (Post-MI scar - most common) - Cardiomyopathies (Dilated, Hypertrophic, ARVC) - Valvular heart disease - Congenital heart disease

Without Structural Heart Disease (10%): - Idiopathic VT: - RVOT VT (Right Ventricular Outflow Tract) - Fascicular VT - Channelopathies: - Long QT syndrome - Brugada syndrome - Catecholaminergic polymorphic VT

Reversible Causes - "HIKE" 

H - Hypoxia
I - Ischemia/Infarction
K - K+ abnormalities (Hypo/Hyperkalemia)
E - Electrolyte imbalance, Drugs

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text: Mechanism: - Re-entry (Most common) - around MI scar - Abnormal automaticity - Triggered activity

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Palpitations - Dizziness, syncope - Chest pain - Dyspnea - Sudden cardiac death (if sustained)

Signs: - Tachycardia (>100 bpm, usually 120-250) - Hypotension - Signs of poor perfusion - Cannon 'a' waves in JVP (AV dissociation) - Varying S1 intensity

Hemodynamic Stability: - Stable: Conscious, BP maintained - Unstable: Hypotension, altered consciousness, pulmonary edema

docs.title: ECG Features of VT
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VT ON ECG                               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. Wide QRS complex (>120 ms)                        │
│ 2. Rate >100 bpm (usually 120-250)                   │
│ 3. Regular or slightly irregular                     │
│ 4. AV dissociation (P and QRS independent)           │
│ 5. Capture beats (occasional normal QRS)             │
│ 6. Fusion beats (QRS intermediate morphology)        │
│ 7. Positive or negative concordance in chest leads   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Brugada Criteria for VT vs SVT with Aberrancy:

  1. Absence of RS complex in all chest leads → VT
  2. R to S interval >100 ms in any chest lead → VT
  3. AV dissociation → VT
  4. Morphology criteria:
  5. RBBB pattern: R in V1 or QS/QR → VT
  6. LBBB pattern: R in V1 >30ms, Slurred S downstroke → VT

VT is more likely if: - History of MI/structural heart disease - Age >35 years - Wide QRS (>140 ms)

docs.title: Other Investigations
docs.text:
• Echocardiography: LV function, structural disease
  • Coronary angiography: CAD
  • Cardiac MRI: Scar, infiltrative disease, ARVC
  • Electrophysiology study: Inducibility, ablation

docs.title: Acute Management
docs.text:
Algorithm: 

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         VT ACUTE MANAGEMENT                        │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                    │
│  UNSTABLE (Hypotension/AMS/Chest pain/Pulm edema) │
│               ↓                                    │
│      DC Cardioversion (Synchronized)               │
│      Energy: 100-200 J (Biphasic)                  │
│                                                    │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                    │
│  STABLE                                            │
│               ↓                                    │
│      Correct K+, Mg2+ (Target: K>4, Mg>2)          │
│               ↓                                    │
│      Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min              │
│      (or Procainamide, Sotalol)                    │
│               ↓                                    │
│      If fails → DC Cardioversion                   │
│                                                    │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

NEVER use: - Verapamil in VT → May cause cardiovascular collapse - Adenosine → Only for VT vs SVT differentiation (if truly uncertain)

docs.title: Long-term Management
docs.text:
1. ICD (Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator) - MOST IMPORTANT

Indications: - Secondary prevention: - Survived cardiac arrest (VT/VF) - Sustained VT with hemodynamic compromise

  • Primary prevention:
  • Post-MI with EF ≤30% (>40 days post-MI)
  • Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF ≤35%
  • Genetic conditions (Brugada, LQTS, HCM)

2. Anti-arrhythmic Drugs - Amiodarone: Most effective - Sotalol: Beta-blocker + Class III - Beta-blockers: Post-MI (reduce VT)

3. Catheter Ablation - Monomorphic VT with identifiable scar - Recurrent VT despite ICD/drugs - Idiopathic VT (curative)

4. Treat Underlying Cause - Revascularization for ischemia - Optimize heart failure management - Correct electrolytes

docs.title: Torsades de Pointes (TdP)
docs.text:
Special type of polymorphic VT with prolonged QT

ECG Features: - "Twisting of the points" - QRS axis rotates around baseline - Ventricular rate: 200-250 bpm - Prolonged QT interval (>500 ms)

Causes - "ABCDE" 

A - Anti-arrhythmics (Quinidine, Sotalol, Amiodarone)
B - Bradycardia (Heart block)
C - Congenital (Long QT syndrome)
D - Diuretics (Hypokalemia, Hypomagnesemia)
E - Erythromycin, other drugs (Antipsychotics, TCAs)

Management: 1. Magnesium sulfate 2g IV (Even if Mg2+ normal) - DRUG OF CHOICE 2. Correct electrolytes: K+ >4.5, Mg2+ >2 3. Stop offending drugs 4. Temporary pacing (to increase HR and shorten QT) 5. Isoproterenol (if pacing not available) 6. DC cardioversion (if hemodynamically unstable)

AVOID: Standard anti-arrhythmics (may prolong QT further)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Definition: Impaired conduction from atria to ventricles through AV node/His-Purkinje system

Classification: 1. First-degree AV block 2. Second-degree AV block - Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach) - Mobitz Type II 3. Third-degree (Complete) AV block

docs.title: First-Degree AV Block
docs.text:
Definition: Prolonged AV conduction without dropped beats

ECG Features: 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      1° AV BLOCK ECG                         │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • PR interval >200 ms (>5 small squares)     │
│ • Every P wave followed by QRS               │
│ • PR interval constant                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Causes: - Increased vagal tone (Athletes) - Drugs (Beta-blockers, CCBs, Digoxin) - Inferior MI - Myocarditis, Lyme disease - Degenerative conduction disease

Management: - Usually no treatment required - Asymptomatic - Monitor if drugs/ischemia involved - No pacemaker needed

docs.title: Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach)
docs.text:
ECG Features: 

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      MOBITZ TYPE I (WENCKEBACH)                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Progressive PR prolongation                   │
│ • Until one P wave not conducted (dropped QRS)  │
│ • Then cycle repeats                            │
│ • "Progressively longer, until dropped, repeat" │
│ • RR interval progressively shortens            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Characteristics: - Site: AV node (above His bundle) - Usually benign - Common in inferior MI (RCA occlusion) - Athletes, during sleep

Management: - Usually no treatment if asymptomatic - May resolve spontaneously - Temporary pacing if symptomatic (rare) - Treat underlying cause (e.g., inferior MI)

docs.title: Mobitz Type II
docs.text:
ECG Features: 

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      MOBITZ TYPE II                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Constant PR interval                          │
│ • Sudden dropped QRS (without PR prolongation)  │
│ • "Constant, constant, dropped"                 │
│ • Wide QRS if infra-Hisian                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Characteristics: - Site: Below AV node (His-Purkinje system) - More serious - may progress to complete heart block - Common in anterior MI (LAD occlusion) - Wide QRS (bundle branch involvement)

Management: - Permanent pacemaker indicated (high risk of progression) - Temporary pacing if acute (e.g., MI) - Do NOT use Atropine (may worsen block)

docs.title: Third-Degree (Complete) AV Block
docs.text:
Definition: Complete dissociation between atria and ventricles

ECG Features: 

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      COMPLETE HEART BLOCK                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • P waves regular                               │
│ • QRS complexes regular                         │
│ • No relationship between P and QRS             │
│ • "P and QRS march independently"               │
│ • Ventricular rate slower (20-40 bpm)           │
│ • Wide QRS if ventricular escape                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Escape Rhythms: - Junctional escape: 40-60 bpm, narrow QRS (if block at AV node) - Ventricular escape: 20-40 bpm, wide QRS (if block below AV node)

Causes: - Congenital: Maternal SLE (anti-Ro antibodies) - Acquired: - Degenerative (Lev's disease, Lenègre's disease) - Ischemic: MI (inferior or anterior) - Drugs: Beta-blockers, CCBs, Digoxin - Infiltrative: Sarcoidosis, amyloidosis - Infections: Lyme disease, endocarditis - Post-cardiac surgery

Clinical Features: - Bradycardia (20-40 bpm) - Syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks) - Fatigue, dyspnea - Heart failure - Sudden cardiac death

Management:

Acute: 1. Atropine 0.5 mg IV (if narrow QRS/junctional escape) 2. Temporary pacing (transcutaneous or transvenous) 3. Isoproterenol infusion (if pacing delayed)

Chronic: - Permanent pacemaker (Definitive treatment) - Indications: - Symptomatic complete heart block - Asymptomatic with pauses >3 seconds - HR <40 bpm while awake - Congenital CHB with wide QRS, symptoms, or ventricular dysfunction

docs.title: Atrial Flutter
docs.text:
Definition: Regular atrial rhythm at 250-350 bpm with "sawtooth" pattern

ECG Features: - Sawtooth F waves (best in II, III, aVF) - Atrial rate: 300 bpm (typically) - Variable AV block: 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 - 2:1 block → Ventricular rate 150 bpm - 3:1 block → Ventricular rate 100 bpm

Management: - Similar to AF - Rate control: Beta-blockers, CCBs - Rhythm control: Cardioversion (lower energy needed, ~50-100 J) - Anticoagulation: Same as AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) - Catheter ablation: Very high success rate (>90%) - CURATIVE

docs.title: Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT)
docs.text:
Types: 1. AVNRT (AV Nodal Reentrant Tachycardia) - Most common (60%) 2. AVRT (AV Reentrant Tachycardia) - Accessory pathway (WPW) 3. Atrial tachycardia

AVNRT: - Mechanism: Re-entry within AV node (slow-fast pathway) - ECG: Narrow QRS, regular, rate 150-250, pseudo-r' in V1, pseudo-s in II/III/aVF - Acute management: - Vagal maneuvers (Carotid massage, Valsalva) - First - Adenosine 6 mg IV rapid bolus → 12 mg → 12 mg - Verapamil/Diltiazem if adenosine fails - DC cardioversion if unstable - Long-term: Catheter ablation (curative)

Adenosine: - Mechanism: Blocks AV node transiently - Dose: 6 mg → 12 mg → 12 mg (rapid IV push) - Side effects: Transient asystole, flushing, chest discomfort - Contraindications: Asthma (bronchospasm), WPW with AF - Use: Diagnostic (unmasks atrial activity) and therapeutic (terminates SVT)

docs.title: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) Syndrome
docs.text:
Definition: Pre-excitation syndrome with accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent)

ECG Features (Sinus Rhythm): 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      WPW ECG                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Short PR interval (<120 ms)                │
│ • Delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke)          │
│ • Wide QRS (>120 ms)                         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Complications: - AVRT (Orthodromic - narrow QRS; Antidromic - wide QRS) - AF with rapid ventricular response → May degenerate to VF

Management: - Acute AVRT: - Vagal maneuvers, Adenosine (if narrow QRS) - DC cardioversion if unstable

  • AF with WPW:
  • Procainamide or Ibutilide (slow accessory pathway)
  • DC cardioversion if unstable

  • AVOID: Digoxin, Beta-blockers, CCBs, Adenosine (may accelerate conduction through accessory pathway → VF)

  • Long-term: Catheter ablation of accessory pathway (Curative)

docs.title: Premature Beats
docs.text:
PACs (Premature Atrial Contractions): - Early P wave (different morphology) - Followed by QRS (usually narrow) - Benign - no treatment needed

PVCs (Premature Ventricular Contractions): - Wide QRS, no preceding P wave - Compensatory pause (full pause) - Benign in structurally normal heart - Concerning if: - Frequent (>10/hour) - Multifocal - R-on-T phenomenon - Associated with structural heart disease - Management: Treat underlying cause, Beta-blockers

docs.title: Key Drug Details
docs.text:
Amiodarone: - Most effective anti-arrhythmic - Uses: VT, AF, SVT - Side effects: "ABCDEFG" - A - Arrhythmias (Bradycardia, TdP) - B - Blue-gray skin (photosensitivity) - C - Corneal deposits - D - Deranged LFTs - E - Everything else (Neuropathy, myopathy) - F - Fibrosis (Pulmonary - most serious) - G - Goiter (Hypo/Hyperthyroidism) - Monitoring: TFTs, LFTs, PFTs, Chest X-ray

Sotalol: - Beta-blocker + Class III - Proarrhythmic: May cause TdP (dose-dependent) - Contraindicated: CrCl <40 ml/min, QT >450 ms

Digoxin: - Use: AF with heart failure (rate control) - Narrow therapeutic window - Toxicity: "Yellow vision," Nausea, Arrhythmias (bidirectional VT) - Precipitants: Hypokalemia, Hypercalcemia, Hypomagnesemia, Renal failure - Treatment of toxicity: Digoxin-specific Fab fragments

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. AF is the most common sustained arrhythmia

  2. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score:

  3. ≥2: Anticoagulation mandatory

  4. Score 0 (male) or 1 (female): No anticoagulation

  5. DOACs preferred over Warfarin (except mechanical valves, mitral stenosis)

  6. AF <48 hours: Cardioversion safe without prior anticoagulation

  7. VT vs SVT with aberrancy: VT more likely if history of MI, age >35, wide QRS >140 ms

  8. AV dissociation, Capture/Fusion beats → VT

  9. Torsades de Pointes treatment: Magnesium sulfate (DOC)

  10. Mobitz II requires permanent pacemaker (unlike Mobitz I)

  11. Complete heart block: Permanent pacemaker indicated

  12. WPW with AF: AVOID Digoxin, Beta-blockers, CCBs → Use Procainamide

  13. ICD indications:

    • Post-MI EF ≤30%
    • Survived cardiac arrest
    • Sustained VT with hemodynamic compromise

  14. Adenosine:

    • Dose: 6 mg → 12 mg → 12 mg (rapid IV)
    • Contraindicated in asthma, WPW with AF

  15. Amiodarone most common side effect: Pulmonary fibrosis (most serious), Thyroid dysfunction (most common)

  16. Flecainide: Only in structurally normal heart (CAST study - ↑ mortality post-MI)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Irregularly irregular pulse → AF

Scenario 2: Wide QRS tachycardia in post-MI patient → VT (until proven otherwise)

Scenario 3: Young patient with palpitations, delta wave on ECG → WPW

Scenario 4: Progressive PR prolongation, then dropped QRS in inferior MI → Mobitz I (Wenckebach)

Scenario 5: Constant PR with sudden dropped QRS in anterior MI → Mobitz II → Needs pacemaker

Scenario 6: Patient on Sotalol develops "twisting" QRS tachycardia → Torsades → Give Magnesium

Scenario 7: AF patient, CHA₂DS₂-VASc = 3 → Anticoagulation mandatory

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• AF classification: Paroxysmal, Persistent, Permanent
  • CHA₂DS₂-VASc and HAS-BLED scores
  • DOACs: Dabigatran, Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban
  • VT ECG features: Wide QRS, AV dissociation, Capture/Fusion beats
  • Brugada criteria for VT vs SVT
  • Torsades management: Magnesium sulfate
  • Heart blocks: 1°, Mobitz I, Mobitz II, Complete
  • Pacemaker indications: Mobitz II, Complete heart block
  • WPW: Delta wave, avoid AV nodal blockers in AF
  • ICD indications: Post-MI EF ≤30%, cardiac arrest survivor
  • Anti-arrhythmic classification: Vaughan Williams
  • Amiodarone side effects: Pulmonary, thyroid
  • Adenosine: 6-12-12 mg, contraindicated in asthma

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 65-year-old male presents with palpitations. ECG shows irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves and narrow QRS complexes at a rate of 140 bpm. He has diabetes and hypertension. What is his CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and management?

A) Score 2, Aspirin B) Score 3, Anticoagulation with DOAC C) Score 1, No anticoagulation D) Score 4, Warfarin only

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Score 3, Anticoagulation with DOAC** **Explanation:** - Diagnosis: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, no P waves) - CHA₂DS₂-VASc calculation: - C (CHF): 0 - H (Hypertension): 1 - A₂ (Age ≥75): 0 - D (Diabetes): 1 - S₂ (Stroke): 0 - V (Vascular): 0 - A (Age 65-74): 1 - Sc (Female): 0 - **Total: 3** - Score ≥2 → Anticoagulation required - DOACs preferred over Warfarin (unless contraindicated)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 55-year-old male with previous MI presents with wide QRS tachycardia at 180 bpm. ECG shows AV dissociation and capture beats. He is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate initial management?

A) Verapamil IV B) Adenosine 6 mg IV C) Amiodarone 150 mg IV D) Immediate DC cardioversion

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Amiodarone 150 mg IV** **Explanation:** - Diagnosis: **Ventricular Tachycardia** (wide QRS, AV dissociation, capture beats, post-MI) - Patient is **stable** → Pharmacological management first - **Amiodarone 150 mg IV** is drug of choice for stable VT - Verapamil is **contraindicated** in VT (may cause cardiovascular collapse) - Adenosine not appropriate for VT - DC cardioversion reserved for unstable patients or failed medical management

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 70-year-old female with inferior MI on day 2 develops bradycardia. ECG shows progressive PR interval prolongation followed by a dropped QRS, then the cycle repeats. Management?

A) Permanent pacemaker B) Temporary pacing C) Observation only D) Atropine infusion

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Observation only** **Explanation:** - Diagnosis: **Mobitz Type I (Wenckebach)** - progressive PR prolongation until dropped QRS - Context: **Inferior MI** (RCA occlusion affects AV node) - Mobitz I is usually **benign** and often **resolves spontaneously** - No treatment needed if asymptomatic - If symptomatic (rare): Consider temporary pacing - **No permanent pacemaker** needed (unlike Mobitz II) - Atropine may help if symptomatic, but not as infusion

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A patient on Sotalol develops palpitations. ECG shows polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with QRS complexes that appear to "twist" around the baseline. QTc is 580 ms. What is the treatment?

A) Amiodarone IV B) Magnesium sulfate 2g IV C) Lidocaine IV D) Increase Sotalol dose

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Magnesium sulfate 2g IV** **Explanation:** - Diagnosis: **Torsades de Pointes** (polymorphic VT with "twisting" QRS, prolonged QT) - Cause: Sotalol (Class III anti-arrhythmic - prolongs QT) - **Magnesium sulfate** is the **drug of choice** for Torsades - Give even if serum Mg2+ is normal - Also: - **Stop Sotalol** - Correct K+ (>4.5) and Mg2+ (>2) - Consider temporary pacing to increase HR (shortens QT) - **Avoid:** Standard anti-arrhythmics (may worsen by prolonging QT further)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 30-year-old athlete has routine ECG showing PR interval of 220 ms with normal QRS complexes. Every P wave is followed by a QRS. He is asymptomatic. Management?

A) Permanent pacemaker B) EP study C) No treatment, reassurance D) Beta-blocker therapy

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) No treatment, reassurance** **Explanation:** - Diagnosis: **First-degree AV block** (PR >200 ms, every P followed by QRS) - **Common in athletes** (increased vagal tone) - **Asymptomatic** and **benign** - **No treatment required** - No pacemaker needed - Monitor if taking AV nodal blocking drugs or if ischemia present

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient with WPW syndrome develops AF with rapid ventricular rate of 220 bpm. He is hypotensive. What should be avoided?

A) DC cardioversion B) Procainamide C) Ibutilide D) Verapamil

Click to reveal answer **Answer: D) Verapamil** **Explanation:** - **WPW with AF** is a medical emergency (risk of VF) - **AVOID:** Digoxin, Beta-blockers, CCBs (Verapamil, Diltiazem), Adenosine - These block AV node → More conduction through accessory pathway → ↑ Ventricular rate → VF - **Appropriate treatments:** - **DC cardioversion** (if hemodynamically unstable) - **CORRECT ANSWER HERE** - **Procainamide or Ibutilide** (slow accessory pathway conduction) - This patient is **unstable (hypotensive)** → **Immediate DC cardioversion** is the treatment - Verapamil could be **fatal** in this scenario

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 60-year-old male with dilated cardiomyopathy and EF 25% presents after syncope. Holter shows non-sustained VT. What is the most appropriate long-term management?

A) Amiodarone only B) ICD implantation C) EP study with catheter ablation D) Beta-blocker only

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) ICD implantation** **Explanation:** - **Primary prevention ICD indication:** - Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy (dilated CM) with **EF ≤35%** - Symptomatic (syncope) with NSVT - ICD reduces risk of sudden cardiac death - **Amiodarone/Beta-blockers** are adjuncts but **ICD is definitive** for primary prevention - Catheter ablation has limited role in non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with diffuse disease

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A patient presents with palpitations. ECG shows narrow complex regular tachycardia at 180 bpm. Carotid sinus massage terminates the arrhythmia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A) Atrial fibrillation B) Atrial flutter with 2:1 block C) AVNRT D) Ventricular tachycardia

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) AVNRT** **Explanation:** - **AVNRT** (AV Nodal Reentrant Tachycardia): - **Most common SVT** (60%) - **Narrow QRS, regular** tachycardia - **Terminates with vagal maneuvers** (carotid massage, Valsalva) - Mechanism: Re-entry within AV node - AF is **irregular** - Atrial flutter has **sawtooth** pattern and usually doesn't terminate with vagal maneuvers (may slow temporarily) - VT has **wide QRS** and doesn't respond to vagal maneuvers

End of Arrhythmias Study Material

Next Topics: - Hypertension - Infective Endocarditis - Rheumatic Heart Disease - Valvular Heart Disease

Cross-References: - Ischemic Heart Disease → Medicine/02_Ischemic_Heart_Disease.md - Heart Failure → Medicine/03_Heart_Failure.md - Cardiac Cycle → Cardiac_Cycle_Study_Material.md

Study Tips: 1. Master ECG recognition of AF, VT, heart blocks 2. Memorize CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (comes in every exam) 3. Know when to use pacemaker (Mobitz II, Complete heart block) 4. Remember Torsades = Magnesium 5. Understand WPW danger - NEVER give AV nodal blockers in AF 6. Practice wide QRS tachycardia algorithm (VT vs SVT with aberrancy)

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 4-5 hours Revision Time: 1-2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Hypertension - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (⅘) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition & Classification
  2. Etiology
  3. Pathophysiology
  4. Clinical Features
  5. Investigations
  6. Management
  7. Hypertensive Emergencies
  8. Special Situations
  9. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  10. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Persistent elevation of blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg (based on average of ≥2 readings on ≥2 occasions)

docs.title: Special Definitions
docs.text:
Isolated Systolic Hypertension: - SBP ≥140 mmHg with DBP <90 mmHg - Common in elderly (loss of arterial compliance) - Strong predictor of cardiovascular risk

White Coat Hypertension: - Elevated BP in clinic but normal at home - Confirm with ABPM (Ambulatory BP Monitoring) - Lower cardiovascular risk than sustained HTN

Masked Hypertension: - Normal BP in clinic but elevated at home - Higher cardiovascular risk - Confirm with home BP monitoring

docs.title: Primary (Essential) Hypertension - 90-95%
docs.text:
Definition: Hypertension without identifiable cause

Risk Factors - "ABCDEFGH" 

A - Age (>55 men, >65 women)
B - Black race
C - Cigarette smoking
D - Diabetes mellitus
E - Elevated cholesterol
F - Family history
G - Gender (Males > Females before menopause)
H - High sodium intake, High BMI

Pathophysiology: - Multifactorial - Genetic predisposition + Environmental factors - ↑ Sympathetic activity - ↑ Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS) - ↑ Sodium retention - Endothelial dysfunction

docs.title: Secondary Hypertension - 5-10%
docs.text:
Suspect if: - Age <30 or >55 years at onset - Resistant hypertension (uncontrolled on 3 drugs including diuretic) - Sudden onset or sudden worsening - Severe hypertension (>180/110) - Hypokalemia (unprovoked)

Causes - "ABCDE"

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         SECONDARY HYPERTENSION CAUSES                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ A - ADRENAL                                              │
│     • Primary aldosteronism (Conn's syndrome) - MC       │
│     • Pheochromocytoma                                   │
│     • Cushing's syndrome                                 │
│     • Congenital adrenal hyperplasia                     │
│                                                          │
│ B - BAD KIDNEYS (Renal) - Most common overall           │
│     • Chronic kidney disease                             │
│     • Renovascular disease (Renal artery stenosis)       │
│     • Polycystic kidney disease                          │
│     • Glomerulonephritis                                 │
│                                                          │
│ C - COARCTATION of Aorta                                │
│                                                          │
│ D - DRUGS                                                │
│     • NSAIDs, Steroids                                   │
│     • OCP, Decongestants                                 │
│     • Cocaine, Amphetamines                              │
│     • Erythropoietin, Cyclosporine                       │
│                                                          │
│ E - ENDOCRINE                                            │
│     • Hyperthyroidism, Hypothyroidism                    │
│     • Hyperparathyroidism                                │
│     • Acromegaly                                         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Key Secondary Causes:
docs.text:
1. Renal Artery Stenosis - Most common: Atherosclerosis (older, males, smokers) - Young females: Fibromuscular dysplasia - Clue: Abdominal bruit, ↑ Creatinine with ACE inhibitors - Investigation: Renal Doppler, CT/MR angiography - Treatment: Angioplasty ± stenting (if bilateral or solitary kidney with worsening renal function)

2. Primary Aldosteronism (Conn's Syndrome) - Most common endocrine cause of secondary HTN - Causes: - Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (60%) - Most common - Aldosterone-producing adenoma (35%) - Clue: Hypertension + Hypokalemia (unprovoked) - Screening: Aldosterone-to-Renin Ratio (ARR) - ARR >20-30 suggests primary aldosteronism - Confirmation: Saline suppression test, Oral sodium loading - Localization: Adrenal CT, Adrenal vein sampling - Treatment: - Adenoma → Adrenalectomy (curative) - Bilateral hyperplasia → Spironolactone or Eplerenone

3.
