Pheochromocytoma - "10% tumor": 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% familial - Clue: Episodic headaches, sweating, palpitations (paroxysmal HTN) - Investigation: - 24-hour urinary metanephrines (best screening test) - Plasma free metanephrines - Imaging: Adrenal CT/MRI, MIBG scan - Treatment: - Preoperative: α-blocker (Phenoxybenzamine) FIRST, then β-blocker - Surgical resection (curative) - Never give β-blocker first (unopposed α-stimulation → hypertensive crisis)

4. Cushing's Syndrome - Clue: Central obesity, moon face, purple striae, proximal myopathy - Screening: 24-hour urinary cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test - Treatment: Treat underlying cause

5. Coarctation of Aorta - Clue: Upper limb HTN + Lower limb hypotension, Radio-femoral delay - Investigation: Echocardiography, CT/MR angiography - Treatment: Balloon angioplasty ± stenting, surgical repair

docs.title: Hemodynamics
docs.text:
BP = Cardiac Output (CO) × Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR)

Mechanisms: 1. ↑ Cardiac Output: - ↑ Sodium retention → ↑ Blood volume - ↑ Sympathetic activity

  1. ↑ SVR:
  2. Vasoconstriction (Angiotensin II, Catecholamines)
  3. Vascular remodeling (thickening)

docs.title: Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS)
docs.text:
Renin (Kidney)
             ↓
     Angiotensinogen → Angiotensin I
                            ↓ ACE
                      Angiotensin II
                       ↙         ↘
              Vasoconstriction   Aldosterone
              (↑ SVR)            (↑ Na+ retention)
                                 (↑ CO)

docs.title: End-Organ Damage
docs.text:
Target Organs - "HBKRE" 

H - Heart (LVH, CHF, IHD)
B - Brain (Stroke, Encephalopathy)
K - Kidneys (CKD, Proteinuria)
R - Retina (Hypertensive retinopathy)
E - Eyes (Vision loss)

Chronic Hypertension Effects: - Heart: LVH (most common), Diastolic dysfunction, HFpEF, IHD - Brain: Stroke (hemorrhagic/ischemic), Vascular dementia - Kidneys: Hypertensive nephrosclerosis, CKD - Eyes: Hypertensive retinopathy, Vision loss - Vessels: Atherosclerosis, AAA, Peripheral arterial disease

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Essential Hypertension: - Usually ASYMPTOMATIC ("Silent killer") - Detected incidentally on routine check-up

Symptoms (if present): - Headache (occipital, early morning) - Dizziness - Blurred vision - Epistaxis (nosebleeds) - Tinnitus

Symptoms of Complications: - Chest pain (IHD, MI) - Dyspnea (Heart failure) - Hemiparesis (Stroke) - Altered consciousness (Hypertensive encephalopathy)

docs.title: Physical Examination
docs.text:
General: - Measure BP properly: - Rest 5 minutes - Appropriate cuff size (bladder covers 80% of arm) - Arm at heart level - Average of 2-3 readings

Cardiovascular: - S4 gallop (LVH with diastolic dysfunction) - Displaced apex (LVH) - Loud A2 (Hypertension) - Murmurs (Aortic stenosis, regurgitation)

Signs of Secondary Causes: - Abdominal bruit (Renal artery stenosis) - Radio-femoral delay (Coarctation) - Cushingoid features (Cushing's) - Enlarged kidneys (Polycystic kidney disease)

Signs of End-Organ Damage: - Fundoscopy: Hypertensive retinopathy - Neurological deficits: Previous stroke - Peripheral pulses: Peripheral arterial disease

docs.title: Initial Evaluation
docs.text: Objectives: 1. Assess for secondary causes 2. Evaluate end-organ damage 3. Identify cardiovascular risk factors

docs.title: Basic Investigations
docs.text:
Mandatory: 1. ECG: - LVH (Sokolow-Lyon criteria: SV1 + RV5/V6 >35 mm) - Ischemic changes - Arrhythmias (AF)

  1. Urinalysis:
  2. Proteinuria (renal damage)

  3. Hematuria (glomerulonephritis)

  4. Serum Creatinine & eGFR:

  5. Assess renal function

  6. CKD staging

  7. Serum Electrolytes:

  8. Hypokalemia: Primary aldosteronism, diuretic use

  9. Hyperkalemia: CKD, RAS inhibitors

  10. Fasting Blood Glucose & HbA1c:

  11. Diabetes screening

  12. Lipid Profile:

  13. Cardiovascular risk assessment

  14. Complete Blood Count:

  15. Anemia (CKD)
  16. Polycythemia (secondary causes)

Additional Investigations (if indicated):

  1. Echocardiography:
  2. LVH (more sensitive than ECG)
  3. LV systolic/diastolic function

  4. Valvular disease

  5. Fundoscopy:

  6. Hypertensive retinopathy grading

  7. 24-hour ABPM or Home BP monitoring:

    • Confirm diagnosis
    • White coat/Masked hypertension

docs.title: Screening for Secondary Hypertension
docs.text:
When to Screen: - Age <30 or >55 years - Resistant hypertension (uncontrolled on 3 drugs including diuretic) - Sudden onset/worsening - Severe HTN (>180/110) - Unprovoked hypokalemia

Investigations: - Renal artery stenosis: Renal Doppler, CT/MR angiography - Primary aldosteronism: Aldosterone-Renin ratio, Saline suppression test - Pheochromocytoma: 24-hour urinary metanephrines - Cushing's: 24-hour urinary cortisol, Dexamethasone suppression test - Coarctation: Echocardiography, CT/MR angiography - Thyroid: TSH, Free T4

docs.title: Goals of Treatment
docs.text:
Target BP: - General population: <140/90 mmHg - Diabetes/CKD: <130/80 mmHg - Elderly (>65 years): <150/90 mmHg (more lenient)

2017 ACC/AHA Guidelines: - General: <130/80 mmHg (more aggressive)

docs.title: Non-Pharmacological (Lifestyle Modifications)
docs.text:
"LEAP DS" 

L - Limit alcohol (<2 drinks/day men, <1 drink/day women)
E - Exercise (30 min/day, 5 days/week)
A - Avoid smoking
P - Potassium-rich diet (fruits, vegetables)

D - DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension)
    • ↑ Fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy
    • ↓ Saturated fat, cholesterol
    • Effect: ↓ 8-14 mmHg

S - Sodium restriction (<2.4 g/day)
    • Effect: ↓ 5-6 mmHg
    • Weight loss (if overweight)

Weight Loss: - Most effective non-pharmacological measure - Every 1 kg loss → ↓ 1-2 mmHg BP

docs.title: When to Start Drugs:
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│     WHEN TO START ANTI-HYPERTENSIVE DRUGS             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Stage 1 HTN (130-139/80-89):                           │
│   • With CVD/10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% → Start drugs    │
│   • No CVD/low risk → Lifestyle modification           │
│                                                        │
│ Stage 2 HTN (≥140/90):                                 │
│   • Start drugs + lifestyle modification               │
│                                                        │
│ Hypertensive crisis (≥180/120):                        │
│   • Immediate treatment (may need IV)                  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 1. ACE Inhibitors (ACEIs)
docs.text:
Examples: Ramipril, Enalapril, Lisinopril, Perindopril

Mechanism: Inhibit ACE → ↓ Angiotensin II → ↓ Vasoconstriction, ↓ Aldosterone

Indications (Compelling): - Diabetes with proteinuria - CKD with proteinuria - Heart failure (HFrEF) - Post-MI - LVH

Side Effects: - Dry cough (10-15%) - due to ↑ Bradykinin (most common) - Hyperkalemia - Angioedema (rare but serious) - ↑ Creatinine (acceptable if <30% increase) - Teratogenic (Contraindicated in pregnancy)

Contraindications: - Bilateral renal artery stenosis (may cause acute renal failure) - Pregnancy - History of angioedema - Hyperkalemia (K+ >5.5)

docs.title: 2. Angiotensin Receptor Blockers (ARBs)
docs.text:
Examples: Losartan, Valsartan, Telmisartan, Irbesartan

Mechanism: Block AT1 receptors → ↓ Effects of Angiotensin II

Indications: - Same as ACEIs - Alternative if ACEI causes cough (ARBs don't increase bradykinin)

Side Effects: - Similar to ACEIs BUT NO COUGH - Hyperkalemia - ↑ Creatinine - Teratogenic

Contraindications: Same as ACEIs

ACEI + ARB Combination: - NOT recommended (↑ Risk of hyperkalemia, AKI, hypotension) - Exception: Nephrotic syndrome (under specialist guidance)

docs.title: 3. Calcium Channel Blockers (CCBs)
docs.text:
Types: - Dihydropyridines (DHP): Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Felodipine - Non-Dihydropyridines: Verapamil, Diltiazem

Mechanism: Block Ca2+ channels → Vasodilation (DHP) or ↓ HR + ↓ Contractility (Non-DHP)

Indications: - Elderly (Isolated systolic HTN) - Black patients - Angina - Raynaud's phenomenon

Side Effects: - Peripheral edema (Amlodipine) - most common - Flushing, headache - Gingival hyperplasia - Constipation (Verapamil) - Bradycardia, AV block (Non-DHP)

Contraindications: - Non-DHP: Heart failure (HFrEF), AV block, Sick sinus syndrome - DHP: Severe aortic stenosis

Note: Verapamil/Diltiazem NOT with β-blockers (risk of bradycardia/heart block)

docs.title: 4. Diuretics
docs.text:
Thiazides: Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ), Chlorthalidone, Indapamide

Mechanism: ↓ Na+ reabsorption in DCT → Natriuresis → ↓ Blood volume

Indications: - Elderly - Black patients - Heart failure - Osteoporosis (↓ Ca2+ excretion)

Side Effects - "HYPO GLUC" 

HYPO:
  • Hyponatremia
  • Hypokalemia (most common)
  • Hypocalcemia (rare - thiazides ↓ Ca excretion)
  • Hypomagnesemia

GLUC:
  • Glucose elevation (hyperglycemia)
  • Lipid elevation (hyperlipidemia)
  • Uric acid elevation (hyperuricemia → Gout)
  • Calcium retention

Contraindications: - Gout (relative) - Severe renal impairment (ineffective if GFR <30)

Loop Diuretics (Furosemide, Torsemide): - Not first-line for HTN - Use if CKD (GFR <30) or Heart failure

Potassium-Sparing Diuretics: - Spironolactone: Aldosterone antagonist - Use: Resistant HTN, Primary aldosteronism - Side effects: Hyperkalemia, Gynecomastia - Amiloride: ENaC blocker - No anti-androgen effects (unlike Spironolactone)

docs.title: 5. Beta-Blockers
docs.text:
Examples: Metoprolol, Atenolol, Bisoprolol, Carvedilol

Mechanism: Block β-receptors → ↓ HR, ↓ Contractility, ↓ Renin

Indications (Compelling): - Post-MI - Heart failure (Bisoprolol, Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate) - Angina - Arrhythmias (AF, SVT) - Young patients with hyperkinetic circulation

Side Effects: - Bradycardia - Fatigue, Depression - Bronchospasm (Non-selective) - Mask hypoglycemia (in diabetics) - Cold extremities - Erectile dysfunction - Worsen lipid profile

Contraindications: - Asthma/COPD (relative - use cardioselective β1) - Heart block (2°/3°) - Severe peripheral vascular disease - Cocaine use (unopposed α → vasoconstriction)

Note: No longer first-line for uncomplicated hypertension (less effective in preventing stroke)

docs.title: Combination Therapy
docs.text:
Preferred Combinations: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      PREFERRED DRUG COMBINATIONS                       │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. ACEI/ARB + CCB                                      │
│ 2. ACEI/ARB + Diuretic                                 │
│ 3. CCB + Diuretic                                      │
│                                                        │
│ Triple therapy: ACEI/ARB + CCB + Diuretic              │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

AVOID: - ACEI + ARB (↑ Adverse effects, no benefit) - β-blocker + Non-DHP CCB (↑ Bradycardia, AV block)

docs.title: Algorithm for Hypertension Treatment
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         HYPERTENSION TREATMENT ALGORITHM                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                          │
│  Stage 1 HTN (130-139/80-89)                             │
│         ↓                                                │
│  Lifestyle modification                                  │
│  + Drug if CVD/High risk                                 │
│         ↓                                                │
│  ACEI/ARB or CCB or Thiazide                             │
│                                                          │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────────        │
│                                                          │
│  Stage 2 HTN (≥140/90)                                   │
│         ↓                                                │
│  Start 2 drugs (combination pill if possible)            │
│         ↓                                                │
│  ACEI/ARB + CCB  OR  ACEI/ARB + Thiazide                 │
│         ↓                                                │
│  If not controlled:                                      │
│  Add 3rd drug (Triple: ACEI/ARB + CCB + Thiazide)        │
│         ↓                                                │
│  If still not controlled (Resistant HTN):                │
│  Add Spironolactone (25-50 mg)                           │
│  Or consider other causes                                │
│                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Race-Based Approach: - Black patients: Start with CCB or Thiazide (less responsive to ACEI/ARB monotherapy) - White/Asian patients: Start with ACEI/ARB

Age-Based Approach: - <55 years: ACEI/ARB (higher renin) - >55 years or Black: CCB or Thiazide (lower renin, salt-sensitive)

docs.title: Resistant Hypertension
docs.text:
Definition: BP uncontrolled on 3 drugs (including diuretic) at optimal doses

Causes - "ABCDEFGH" 

A - Adherence (Poor compliance) - Most common
B - Blood pressure measurement error (White coat)
C - Chronic kidney disease
D - Drugs (NSAIDs, Steroids, OCP, Decongestants)
E - Excessive alcohol
F - Fluid overload (inadequate diuretic)
G - "G"Secondary causes (Aldosteronism, RAS, Pheo)
H - High sodium intake

Management: 1. Confirm true resistance: - Check adherence - ABPM (rule out white coat) - Proper BP technique

  1. Optimize therapy:
  2. Ensure diuretic is adequate
  3. Switch HCTZ to Chlorthalidone (more potent)

  4. Add Spironolactone 25-50 mg (most effective 4th agent)

  5. Screen for secondary causes

  6. Consider:

  7. α-blockers (Doxazosin)
  8. Direct vasodilators (Hydralazine, Minoxidil)
  9. Central α-agonists (Clonidine, Methyldopa)
  10. Referral to hypertension specialist

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Hypertensive Crisis: Severe elevation of BP (>180/120 mmHg)

Two Types:

  1. Hypertensive Emergency:
  2. BP >180/120 mmHg WITH acute end-organ damage
  3. Requires immediate IV therapy (within 1 hour)

  4. Target: Reduce BP by 25% in first hour, then to 160/100 over next 2-6 hours

  5. Hypertensive Urgency:

  6. BP >180/120 mmHg WITHOUT acute end-organ damage
  7. Can use oral agents
  8. Reduce BP over 24-48 hours

docs.title: Hypertensive Emergency - End-Organ Damage
docs.text: Manifestations: - Hypertensive encephalopathy (Headache, confusion, seizures, vision changes) - Acute stroke (Ischemic or hemorrhagic) - Acute MI - Acute pulmonary edema - Acute kidney injury - Aortic dissection - Eclampsia - Retinopathy Grade IV (Papilledema)

docs.title: Management of Hypertensive Emergency
docs.text:
General Principles: 1. IV anti-hypertensives 2. ICU monitoring 3. Gradual BP reduction (too rapid → ischemia) - First hour: Reduce MAP by 25% - Next 2-6 hours: Reduce to 160/100 - Next 24-48 hours: Normalize

NEVER reduce BP too rapidly (Risk of stroke, MI, renal failure)

IV Anti-Hypertensive Drugs:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      IV ANTI-HYPERTENSIVES FOR EMERGENCIES                  │
├──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ DRUG             │  INDICATION                              │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Labetalol        │ Most situations (α+β blocker)            │
│                  │ Pregnancy (DOC)                          │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Nicardipine      │ Most situations (CCB)                    │
│                  │ Stroke                                   │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Nitroprusside    │ Aortic dissection (with β-blocker)       │
│                  │ Acute pulmonary edema                    │
│                  │ (Caution: Cyanide toxicity if >48-72 h)  │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Nitroglycerin    │ Acute coronary syndrome                  │
│                  │ Acute pulmonary edema                    │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Esmolol          │ Aortic dissection (DOC)                  │
│                  │ Perioperative HTN                        │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Hydralazine      │ Eclampsia/Pre-eclampsia                  │
│                  │ (Avoid if aortic dissection)             │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Enalaprilat      │ Acute LV failure                         │
│ (IV ACEI)        │ (Avoid in ACS)                           │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Special Situations:

Aortic Dissection: - Target: SBP <120 mmHg, HR <60 bpm (within 20 min) - Drugs: β-blocker (Esmolol) FIRST, then Nitroprusside - Never vasodilate before β-blockade (↑ dP/dt → propagate dissection)

Acute Ischemic Stroke: - Permissive hypertension (Allow BP to remain elevated initially) - Only lower if BP >220/120 mmHg (or >185/110 if thrombolysis candidate) - Use Labetalol or Nicardipine

Intracerebral Hemorrhage: - Target: SBP 140-160 mmHg (balance perfusion vs bleeding)

Acute Coronary Syndrome: - Nitroglycerin (↓ Preload, ↓ Myocardial O₂ demand) - Avoid pure vasodilators (reflex tachycardia)

Acute Pulmonary Edema: - Nitroglycerin or Nitroprusside (↓ Preload + afterload) - Loop diuretics (Furosemide)

Pregnancy (Eclampsia/Severe Pre-eclampsia): - Labetalol IV (DOC) or Hydralazine - Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis - Avoid: ACEIs, ARBs (teratogenic)

docs.title: Hypertensive Urgency
docs.text:
No acute end-organ damage

Management: - Oral agents (Resume/Intensify home medications) - Outpatient management (unless very high risk) - Reduce BP over 24-48 hours - Do NOT use short-acting nifedipine (risk of precipitous drop)

Oral Drugs: - Captopril 25 mg (Oral, fast-acting) - Labetalol 200-400 mg PO - Clonidine 0.1-0.2 mg PO - Amlodipine 5-10 mg PO

docs.title: Hypertension in Pregnancy
docs.text:
Classification: 1. Chronic HTN: Pre-existing (before 20 weeks) 2. Gestational HTN: New-onset after 20 weeks (no proteinuria) 3. Pre-eclampsia: HTN + Proteinuria after 20 weeks 4. Eclampsia: Pre-eclampsia + Seizures

Treatment: - Target: <140/90 mmHg - Safe drugs: Methyldopa (DOC), Labetalol, Nifedipine - AVOID: ACEIs, ARBs (teratogenic), Atenolol (IUGR)

Detailed in: ObsGyn/01_Hypertensive_Disorders_of_Pregnancy.md

docs.title: Hypertension in Diabetes
docs.text:
Target: <130/80 mmHg

First-line: ACEI or ARB (renoprotective)

Benefits: - ↓ Proteinuria - ↓ Progression to ESRD - ↓ Cardiovascular events

docs.title: Hypertension in CKD
docs.text:
Target: <130/80 mmHg (if proteinuria >1 g/day)

First-line: ACEI or ARB

Monitoring: - Check K+ and Creatinine after starting ACEI/ARB - ↑ Creatinine <30% acceptable - If ↑ Creatinine >30% → Suspect bilateral RAS

docs.title: Hypertension Post-MI
docs.text: Preferred: - β-blocker (↓ Recurrent MI, mortality) - ACEI (↓ Remodeling, mortality)

docs.title: Hypertension with Heart Failure
docs.text:
HFrEF: - ACEI/ARB + β-blocker + Aldosterone antagonist + Loop diuretic - (See Heart Failure topic for details)

HFpEF: - Diuretics for volume overload - Treat underlying HTN

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Definition: BP ≥140/90 mmHg (WHO/ISH), ≥130/80 (ACC/AHA 2017)

  2. Essential (Primary) HTN: 90-95% of cases

  3. Most common cause of secondary HTN:

  4. Overall: Renal disease (CKD, Renovascular)

  5. Endocrine: Primary aldosteronism

  6. Primary aldosteronism clue: HTN + Unprovoked hypokalemia

  7. Most common cause: Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia
  8. Screening: Aldosterone-Renin Ratio (ARR)

  9. Treatment: Adenoma → Surgery; Hyperplasia → Spironolactone

  10. Pheochromocytoma clue: Episodic headaches, sweating, palpitations

  11. Screening: 24-hour urinary metanephrines

  12. Treatment: α-blocker FIRST, then β-blocker, then surgery

  13. Renal artery stenosis:

  14. Elderly males: Atherosclerosis
  15. Young females: Fibromuscular dysplasia

  16. Clue: ↑ Creatinine with ACEIs

  17. First-line drugs - "ABCD":

  18. A: ACEIs/ARBs (Diabetes, CKD, HF, Post-MI, LVH)
  19. B: β-blockers (Post-MI, HF, Angina, Arrhythmias)
  20. C: CCBs (Elderly, Black, ISH, Angina)

  21. D: Diuretics (Thiazides - Elderly, Black, HF)

  22. ACEI side effects:

  23. Cough (10-15%) - most common
  24. Angioedema (rare)
  25. Hyperkalemia

  26. Teratogenic

  27. ARBs: Same as ACEIs but NO COUGH

  28. ACEI + ARB: NOT recommended (↑ Adverse effects)

  29. CCB side effects: Peripheral edema (most common - Amlodipine)

  30. Thiazide side effects - "HYPO GLUC":

    • Hypokalemia, Hyponatremia
    • Hyperglycemia, Hyperlipidemia, Hyperuricemia (Gout)

  31. Race/Age approach:

    • <55 years or White: ACEI/ARB
    • >55 years or Black: CCB or Thiazide

  32. Resistant HTN: Uncontrolled on 3 drugs (including diuretic)

    • Add: Spironolactone (most effective 4th agent)

  33. Hypertensive Emergency:

    • BP >180/120 WITH end-organ damage
    • IV drugs, ICU
    • Reduce MAP by 25% in 1st hour

  34. Hypertensive Urgency:

    • BP >180/120 WITHOUT end-organ damage
    • Oral drugs, outpatient

  35. Aortic dissection:

    • Target: SBP <120 mmHg, HR <60 bpm
    • Drugs: β-blocker (Esmolol) FIRST, then Nitroprusside

  36. Pregnancy HTN:

    • Safe: Methyldopa, Labetalol, Nifedipine
    • AVOID: ACEIs, ARBs (teratogenic)

  37. Diabetes/CKD: ACEI/ARB first-line (renoprotective)

  38. Post-MI: β-blocker + ACEI (↓ Mortality)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Young female with HTN, hypokalemia, low renin → Primary aldosteronism

Scenario 2: Episodic HTN with headaches, sweating, palpitations → Pheochromocytoma (24-hr urine metanephrines)

Scenario 3: HTN + Abdominal bruit + ↑ Creatinine with ACEI → Renal artery stenosis

Scenario 4: HTN + Upper limb BP > Lower limb BP + Radio-femoral delay → Coarctation of aorta

Scenario 5: Patient on ACEI develops dry cough → Switch to ARB

Scenario 6: Black patient with HTN, no comorbidities → Start CCB or Thiazide

Scenario 7: Diabetic with HTN and microalbuminuria → Start ACEI or ARB

Scenario 8: HTN uncontrolled on ACEI + CCB + Thiazide → Add Spironolactone

Scenario 9: Pregnant woman with HTN → Use Methyldopa or Labetalol, AVOID ACEIs

Scenario 10: Aortic dissection with BP 200/110 → Give β-blocker (Esmolol) FIRST, then Nitroprusside

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• BP classification: Normal, Stage 1, Stage 2
  • Essential vs Secondary HTN (90-95% vs 5-10%)
  • Secondary causes: Renal (most common), Aldosteronism, Pheo, Cushing's, Coarctation
  • Primary aldosteronism: HTN + Hypokalemia, ARR, Spironolactone
  • Pheochromocytoma: Triad (Headache, Sweating, Palpitations), α-blocker first
  • First-line drugs: ACEI/ARB, CCB, Thiazide, β-blocker (with indications)
  • ACEI: Cough, Angioedema, Hyperkalemia, Teratogenic
  • ARB: No cough
  • CCB: Peripheral edema
  • Thiazide: HYPO GLUC
  • Combination therapy: ACEI+CCB, ACEI+Thiazide, CCB+Thiazide
  • Resistant HTN: 3 drugs, add Spironolactone
  • Hypertensive emergency: IV drugs, reduce MAP 25% in 1 hour
  • Aortic dissection: β-blocker FIRST
  • Pregnancy: Methyldopa, Labetalol, avoid ACEIs/ARBs

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 35-year-old female presents with hypertension. Labs show K+ 2.8 mEq/L, HCO3- 30 mEq/L. Plasma aldosterone is elevated and renin is suppressed. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A) Pheochromocytoma B) Primary aldosteronism C) Cushing's syndrome D) Renal artery stenosis

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Primary aldosteronism** **Explanation:** - **Hypokalemia + Metabolic alkalosis** (↑ HCO3-) - **Elevated aldosterone + Suppressed renin** → Primary (autonomous) aldosterone secretion - Classic presentation of **Conn's syndrome** - Screen with **Aldosterone-Renin Ratio** (elevated) - **Most common cause:** Bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (60%) - **Treatment:** Adenoma → Surgery; Hyperplasia → Spironolactone **Why not others:** - Pheo: Episodic symptoms, no typical electrolyte changes - Cushing's: Cushingoid features, ↑ cortisol - RAS: ↑ Renin (not suppressed)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 45-year-old male with hypertension is started on an ACEI. After 2 weeks, he develops a persistent dry cough. What is the most appropriate next step?

A) Continue ACEI and add cough suppressant B) Switch to ARB C) Switch to CCB D) Discontinue and observe

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Switch to ARB** **Explanation:** - **Dry cough** is a common side effect of ACEIs (10-15%) - Mechanism: ↑ Bradykinin (ACE normally breaks down bradykinin) - **ARBs** do NOT cause cough (don't affect bradykinin) - ARBs have **same indications and benefits** as ACEIs - **Switch to ARB** if patient has compelling indication for RAAS blockade (e.g., diabetes, CKD, HF) - CCB is alternative but loses renoprotective benefit if diabetic/CKD

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 60-year-old male with HTN on ACEI, amlodipine, and hydrochlorothiazide has BP 160/95 mmHg. What is the best 4th agent to add?

A) Atenolol B) Spironolactone C) Doxazosin D) Hydralazine

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Spironolactone** **Explanation:** - Patient has **resistant hypertension** (uncontrolled on 3 drugs including diuretic) - **Spironolactone 25-50 mg** is the **most effective 4th-line agent** - Blocks aldosterone → Addresses aldosterone breakthrough - **PATHWAY trial** showed Spironolactone superior to Doxazosin and Bisoprolol - Monitor for **hyperkalemia** (especially with ACEI) **Why not others:** - β-blockers: Less effective, not recommended as routine add-on - Doxazosin: Less effective than Spironolactone - Hydralazine: Side effects, less effective

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A patient presents with severe headache and BP 210/130 mmHg. CT shows intracerebral hemorrhage. What is the target BP?

A) <120/80 mmHg immediately B) <140-160/90-100 mmHg C) <180/105 mmHg D) No BP lowering (permissive hypertension)

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) <140-160/90-100 mmHg** **Explanation:** - **Intracerebral hemorrhage** with severe HTN - **Target SBP:** 140-160 mmHg (balance between preventing hematoma expansion and maintaining cerebral perfusion) - **INTERACT-2 and ATACH-2 trials:** Intensive BP lowering (SBP <140) may be safe and prevent hematoma growth - **Avoid excessive lowering** (risk of perihematomal ischemia) - Use **IV Labetalol or Nicardipine** **Why not others:** - Too aggressive lowering (option A) may worsen ischemia - Too high (option C) risks hematoma expansion - Permissive hypertension (option D) used in ischemic stroke, NOT hemorrhagic

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A pregnant woman at 32 weeks gestation has BP 165/105 mmHg with 2+ proteinuria. Which drug is contraindicated?

A) Methyldopa B) Labetalol C) Nifedipine D) Enalapril

Click to reveal answer **Answer: D) Enalapril** **Explanation:** - Diagnosis: **Severe pre-eclampsia** (BP ≥160/110 + Proteinuria at >20 weeks) - **ACEIs (Enalapril) and ARBs are CONTRAINDICATED** in pregnancy - **Teratogenic** effects: - 1st trimester: Cardiac/CNS malformations - 2nd/3rd trimester: Renal dysgenesis, Oligohydramnios, IUGR, Fetal death - **Safe anti-hypertensives in pregnancy:** - **Methyldopa** (DOC for chronic HTN) - **Labetalol** (DOC for acute severe HTN/IV use) - **Nifedipine** (alternative) - **Hydralazine** (IV for emergencies) **Management of severe pre-eclampsia:** - IV Labetalol or Hydralazine - Magnesium sulfate (seizure prophylaxis) - Plan delivery (definitive treatment)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient with aortic dissection presents with BP 200/110 mmHg and HR 110 bpm. What is the first drug to give?

A) Nitroprusside B) Esmolol (β-blocker) C) Hydralazine D) Nitroglycerin

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Esmolol (β-blocker)** **Explanation:** - **Aortic dissection:** Medical emergency - **Goals:** - **HR <60 bpm** (↓ dP/dt - force of LV contraction) - **SBP <120 mmHg** - Achieve within **20 minutes** - **Treatment sequence:** 1. **β-blocker FIRST** (Esmolol IV) - ↓ HR, ↓ dP/dt 2. **Then** add Nitroprusside (if BP still high) - **NEVER give vasodilator first** (Nitroprusside, Hydralazine, Nitroglycerin) - Causes reflex tachycardia → ↑ dP/dt → Propagates dissection **Why Esmolol:** - **Ultra-short acting** IV β-blocker - Easy to titrate - Rapid onset and offset

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 28-year-old male has HTN with BP in upper limbs 170/90 mmHg and lower limbs 110/70 mmHg. Femoral pulses are delayed compared to radial. Most likely diagnosis?

A) Aortic regurgitation B) Coarctation of aorta C) Pheochromocytoma D) Renal artery stenosis

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Coarctation of aorta** **Explanation:** - **Classic triad:** 1. **Upper limb HTN + Lower limb hypotension** 2. **Radio-femoral delay** 3. **Weak/absent femoral pulses** - **Coarctation:** Narrowing of aorta (usually at ductus arteriosus insertion - distal to left subclavian artery) - **Associated findings:** - **Rib notching** on CXR (collateral vessels erode ribs) - **Bicuspid aortic valve** (50%) - **Berry aneurysms** (CNS) - **Turner syndrome** (15%) - **Investigation:** Echocardiography, CT/MR angiography - **Treatment:** Balloon angioplasty ± stenting or surgical repair **Why not others:** - AR: Wide pulse pressure, water-hammer pulse, no differential BP - Pheo: Episodic symptoms - RAS: No differential BP between limbs

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A diabetic patient with HTN and microalbuminuria (urine albumin 100 mg/day) should be started on which drug class?

A) Thiazide diuretic B) CCB C) ACEI or ARB D) Beta-blocker

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) ACEI or ARB** **Explanation:** - **Diabetes + Microalbuminuria** → Strong indication for RAAS blockade - **ACEIs/ARBs are FIRST-LINE** in diabetic nephropathy **Benefits:** - **Renoprotection:** - ↓ Intraglomerular pressure (dilate efferent arteriole) - ↓ Proteinuria - Slow progression to ESRD - **Cardiovascular protection:** - ↓ MI, Stroke, CV death **Landmark Trials:** - **HOPE trial:** Ramipril ↓ CV events in diabetics - **RENAAL, IDNT trials:** ARBs slow diabetic nephropathy **Target BP in diabetes:** <130/80 mmHg **Monitoring:** - Check K+ and Creatinine after starting - ↑ Creatinine <30% acceptable

End of Hypertension Study Material

Next Topics: - Infective Endocarditis - Rheumatic Heart Disease - Valvular Heart Disease

Cross-References: - Ischemic Heart Disease → Medicine/02_Ischemic_Heart_Disease.md - Heart Failure → Medicine/03_Heart_Failure.md - Arrhythmias → Medicine/04_Arrhythmias.md

Study Tips: 1. Master secondary HTN causes and their clues 2. Know first-line drugs and compelling indications 3. Understand drug side effects (very high-yield) 4. Remember pregnancy-safe drugs (Methyldopa, Labetalol) 5. Know hypertensive emergency management (especially aortic dissection) 6. Practice algorithm for drug selection based on age, race, comorbidities

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (High) Estimated Study Time: 3-4 hours Revision Time: 1 hour

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Infective Endocarditis - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition & Overview
  2. Etiology & Risk Factors
  3. Microbiology
  4. Pathophysiology
  5. Clinical Features
  6. Investigations
  7. Diagnosis - Duke Criteria
  8. Management
  9. Complications
  10. Prophylaxis
  11. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  12. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Infective Endocarditis (IE) is an infection of the endocardial surface of the heart, primarily affecting heart valves, characterized by formation of vegetations (infected platelet-fibrin thrombi).

docs.title: By Onset:
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         CLASSIFICATION BY ONSET                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Acute IE:                                                │
│   • Rapid onset (<6 weeks)                               │
│   • Virulent organisms (S. aureus)                       │
│   • Normal valves                                        │
│   • High mortality                                       │
│                                                          │
│ Subacute IE:                                             │
│   • Insidious onset (weeks to months)                    │
│   • Less virulent organisms (S. viridans)                │
│   • Abnormal valves                                      │
│   • Better prognosis                                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: By Valve Type:
docs.text:
• Native Valve Endocarditis (NVE): Most common
  • Prosthetic Valve Endocarditis (PVE):
  • Early PVE: <12 months post-surgery (nosocomial organisms)
  • Late PVE: >12 months post-surgery (similar to NVE)
  • IV Drug User Endocarditis (IVDU-IE): Affects tricuspid valve (right-sided)

docs.title: By Culture:
docs.text:
• Culture-positive: Organism identified (85%)
  • Culture-negative: No organism identified (10-15%)

docs.title: Predisposing Cardiac Conditions
docs.text:
High Risk - "PIVR" 

P - Prosthetic heart valves (Highest risk)
I - IV drug abuse
V - Valvular heart disease:
    • Rheumatic heart disease (Most common in India)
    • Mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation
    • Bicuspid aortic valve
    • Degenerative valve disease
R - Repaired/Congenital heart defects:
    • VSD, PDA, Coarctation
    • Previous IE (Very high risk)

Valves Affected (NVE): - Mitral valve: 40% (Most common in developing countries - RHD) - Aortic valve: 35% (Most common in developed countries) - Tricuspid valve: 10-15% (IVDU) - Pulmonic valve: <5% (Rare)

Right-sided IE (Tricuspid/Pulmonary): - IV drug users (Most common cause) - Pacemaker/Central line infections - Congenital heart disease (ASD, VSD)

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
"IVDU CHAMP" 

I - IV drug use (S. aureus, Candida)
V - Valvular disease (RHD, MVP)
D - Diabetes mellitus
U - Urinary tract procedures

C - Cardiac devices (Pacemaker, ICD)
H - Hemodialysis, Healthcare exposure
A - Alcoholism, Advanced age
M - Mitral valve prolapse
P - Prosthetic valves, Prior IE

docs.title: Most Common Organisms
docs.text:
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         ORGANISMS IN INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS               │
├─────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┤
│ ORGANISM                │  CLINICAL SETTING               │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Staphylococcus aureus   │ • Acute IE (Most common)        │
│ (30-40%)                │ • IVDU (tricuspid valve)        │
│                         │ • Prosthetic valves (early PVE) │
│                         │ • Most virulent                 │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Viridans streptococci   │ • Subacute IE (Most common)     │
│ (30-40%)                │ • Dental procedures             │
│ (S. mutans, S. sanguis) │ • Poor dental hygiene           │
│                         │ • Previously most common overall│
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Enterococcus            │ • GU/GI procedures              │
│ (E. faecalis > faecium) │ • Elderly males                 │
│ (10-15%)                │ • Nosocomial                    │
│                         │ • Resistant to many antibiotics │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Staph. epidermidis      │ • Prosthetic valves (Early PVE) │
│ (Coagulase-negative)    │ • Indwelling catheters          │
│                         │ • Nosocomial                    │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Strep. bovis            │ • Associated with colon cancer  │
│ (now S. gallolyticus)   │ • Colonoscopy recommended       │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ HACEK group             │ • Fastidious gram-negative      │
│ (Haemophilus, Aggrega-  │ • Prolonged culture (>7 days)   │
│ tibacter, Cardiobac-    │ • Dental/periodontal disease    │
│ terium, Eikenella,      │ • Large vegetations             │
│ Kingella)               │                                 │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
│ Candida, Aspergillus    │ • IVDU                          │
│ (Fungal)                │ • Immunocompromised             │
│                         │ • Prosthetic valves             │
│                         │ • Poor prognosis                │
└─────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Culture-Negative Endocarditis (10-15%)
docs.text:
Causes - "HACEK + Others" 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      CULTURE-NEGATIVE IE CAUSES                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. Prior antibiotic use (Most common cause)            │
│                                                        │
│ 2. Fastidious organisms:                               │
│    • HACEK group                                       │
│    • Brucella                                          │
│    • Legionella                                        │
│    • Mycoplasma                                        │
│                                                        │
│ 3. Intracellular organisms:                            │
│    • Coxiella burnetii (Q fever) - Most common        │
│    • Bartonella (Culture-negative in homeless/cats)   │
│    • Chlamydia                                         │
│    • Tropheryma whipplei                               │
│                                                        │
│ 4. Fungi: Candida, Aspergillus                         │
│                                                        │
│ 5. Right-sided IE (lower bacterial count)             │
│                                                        │
│ 6. Non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE)       │
│    (Marantic - malignancy, SLE)                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Diagnosis: - Serology (Coxiella, Bartonella) - PCR from blood/valve tissue - Prolonged culture (>7 days for HACEK)

docs.title: Mechanism of IE Development
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         PATHOGENESIS OF IE                             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                        │
│ 1. ENDOTHELIAL DAMAGE                                  │
│    • Turbulent flow (valve disease)                    │
│    • High-pressure jets (VSD, AR)                      │
│    • Foreign material (prosthetic valve)               │
│              ↓                                         │
│ 2. STERILE VEGETATION (NBTE)                           │
│    • Platelet-fibrin thrombus                          │
│    • On damaged endothelium                            │
│              ↓                                         │
│ 3. BACTEREMIA                                          │
│    • Dental procedure, GI/GU procedure                 │
│    • IV drug use                                       │
│    • Spontaneous (dental caries, UTI)                  │
│              ↓                                         │
│ 4. BACTERIAL ADHERENCE                                 │
│    • Bacteria adhere to NBTE                           │
│    • Facilitated by surface adhesins                   │
│              ↓                                         │
│ 5. VEGETATION FORMATION                                │
│    • Bacterial proliferation                           │
│    • More platelet-fibrin deposition                   │
│    • Biofilm formation                                 │
│              ↓                                         │
│ 6. COMPLICATIONS                                       │
│    • Valve destruction                                 │
│    • Emboli                                            │
│    • Immune complex deposition                         │
│                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Vegetations: - Friable masses of platelets, fibrin, bacteria - Located on atrial side of AV valves or ventricular side of semilunar valves - Size: mm to cm (HACEK → large vegetations)

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Classic Triad (Only 10-15%): 1. Fever (85%) - Most common symptom 2. New heart murmur (85%) 3. Embolic phenomena (30%)

General Symptoms: - Fever: Low-grade (subacute) or high-grade (acute) - Chills, rigors - Fatigue, malaise - Weight loss (subacute IE) - Night sweats - Arthralgia, myalgia

Cardiac Symptoms: - Dyspnea (Heart failure - most common cause of death) - Chest pain (MI from coronary embolism) - Palpitations

Embolic Symptoms: - Stroke (CNS emboli - most common neurological) - Renal infarct: Flank pain, hematuria - Splenic infarct: LUQ pain - Pulmonary emboli: Right-sided IE (dyspnea, hemoptysis)

docs.title: Signs
docs.text:
Cardiovascular: - New or changing murmur (80-85%) - Mitral regurgitation: Holosystolic, apex → axilla - Aortic regurgitation: Early diastolic, left sternal border - Heart failure signs (50-60%) - Tachycardia

Peripheral Stigmata (Rare now - <10%):

"OLD JROS" - Classic Signs 

O - Osler nodes (Painful, red nodules on fingers/toes)
L - Libman-Sacks endocarditis (SLE - sterile)

D - Dark-field microscopy (for spirochetes - not IE)

J - Janeway lesions (Painless, hemorrhagic macules on palms/soles)
R - Roth spots (Retinal hemorrhages with white center)
O - Osler nodes (Already mentioned)
S - Splinter hemorrhages (Linear hemorrhages under nails)

Better Mnemonic - "FROM JANE" 

F - Fever (Most common)
R - Roth spots (Retinal)
O - Osler nodes (Painful - fingers/toes)
M - Murmur (New/changed)

J - Janeway lesions (Painless - palms/soles)
A - Anemia
N - Nail findings (Splinter hemorrhages)
E - Embolic phenomena

Key Differences: - Osler nodes: PAINFUL, tips of fingers/toes, immune complex deposition - Janeway lesions: PAINLESS, palms/soles, septic emboli - Roth spots: Retinal hemorrhages with pale center (cotton-wool spots) - Splinter hemorrhages: Linear, under nails

Splenomegaly: 30-40% (subacute IE)

Clubbing: Rare, subacute IE

docs.title: Microbiological
docs.text:
1. Blood Cultures - MOST IMPORTANT

Diagnostic Criteria: - 3 sets of blood cultures from different sites - Before antibiotics (if stable) - Interval: 1 hour apart (or 30 min if unstable) - Aerobic and anaerobic bottles - Prolonged incubation: 7-21 days (HACEK, Brucella)

Culture-Positive: - ≥2 positive cultures with same organism → Major criterion - Continuously positive cultures (12 hours apart) → Major criterion

Typical Organisms (Major criterion): - S. viridans, S. bovis, HACEK - S. aureus, Enterococcus (community-acquired, no other focus)

2. Serology (if culture-negative): - Coxiella burnetii (Q fever) - IgG Phase I >1:800 - Bartonella - Brucella, Legionella

3. PCR/Molecular testing (valve tissue)

docs.title: Cardiac Imaging
docs.text:
1. Echocardiography - MANDATORY

Transthoracic Echocardiography (TTE): - First-line imaging - Sensitivity: 60-70% (NVE), 40-50% (PVE) - Findings: - Vegetation: Mobile, oscillating mass on valve - Valve perforation - Abscess: Thickened area with echolucency - Dehiscence (prosthetic valve) - New regurgitation

Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE): - More sensitive: 90-95% (NVE), 85-90% (PVE) - Indications: - Prosthetic valves - Negative TTE with high clinical suspicion - Complications: Abscess, perforation, fistula - Pre-operative assessment - Better for: - Small vegetations (<5 mm) - Perivalvular complications - Aortic valve

Vegetation Characteristics: - Size >10 mm → ↑ Embolic risk - Mobile → ↑ Embolic risk - Anterior mitral leaflet → ↑ Embolic risk

2. Cardiac CT/MRI: - Alternative if TEE contraindicated or non-diagnostic - Assess for abscess, fistula

docs.title: Other Investigations
docs.text:
Hematology: - CBC: - Anemia (normocytic, normochromic - anemia of chronic disease) - Leukocytosis (acute IE) - Normal WBC (subacute IE) - ESR/CRP: Elevated (non-specific)

Immunology: - Rheumatoid factor: Positive (50% - subacute IE) - Complement (C3, C4): Low (immune complex) - Cryoglobulins: May be positive

Renal: - Urinalysis: Microscopic hematuria, proteinuria (glomerulonephritis) - Creatinine: Elevated (renal failure - immune complex GN or septic emboli)

ECG: - PR prolongation/AV block: Suggests perivalvular abscess (aortic root extension) - Conduction abnormalities

Imaging (for emboli): - CT brain: Stroke, mycotic aneurysm - CT chest: Pulmonary emboli (right-sided IE) - CT abdomen: Splenic/renal infarct

docs.title: Modified Duke Criteria (Most Widely Used)
docs.text:
Definite IE: - 2 Major criteria, OR - 1 Major + 3 Minor criteria, OR - 5 Minor criteria

Possible IE: - 1 Major + 1 Minor, OR - 3 Minor criteria

Rejected: - Alternative diagnosis, OR - Resolution with <4 days antibiotics, OR - Does not meet "Possible" criteria

docs.title: MAJOR CRITERIA
docs.text:
1. Positive Blood Cultures - Typical organism in ≥2 separate cultures: - S. viridans, S. bovis, HACEK group - S. aureus, Enterococcus (community-acquired, no other focus)

  • Persistently positive cultures:
  • ≥2 positive cultures >12 hours apart, OR

  • All of 3, or majority of ≥4 cultures positive

  • Single positive culture for Coxiella burnetii or IgG Phase I >1:800

2. Evidence of Endocardial Involvement (Echocardiography) - Vegetation: Oscillating intracardiac mass on valve/supporting structures - Abscess: Perivalvular abscess - Dehiscence: New partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve - New valvular regurgitation (not pre-existing)

docs.title: MINOR CRITERIA
docs.text:
1. Predisposing Condition: - Predisposing heart condition (valve disease, prosthetic valve) - IV drug use

2. Fever: - Temperature >38°C (100.4°F)

3. Vascular Phenomena: - Major arterial emboli - Septic pulmonary infarcts (right-sided IE) - Mycotic aneurysm - Intracranial hemorrhage - Janeway lesions - Conjunctival hemorrhages

4. Immunologic Phenomena: - Glomerulonephritis - Osler nodes - Roth spots - Rheumatoid factor positive

5. Microbiological Evidence: - Positive blood culture not meeting major criterion, OR - Serological evidence of infection with organism consistent with IE

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text:
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           DUKE CRITERIA SUMMARY                           │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ MAJOR CRITERIA:                                           │
│   1. Blood cultures positive (typical organism x2, OR     │
│      persistently positive, OR Coxiella)                  │
│   2. Echocardiogram positive (vegetation, abscess,        │
│      new regurgitation, dehiscence)                       │
│                                                           │
│ MINOR CRITERIA:                                           │
│   1. Predisposing condition (valve disease, IVDU)         │
│   2. Fever >38°C                                          │
│   3. Vascular phenomena (emboli, Janeway)                 │
│   4. Immunologic phenomena (GN, Osler, Roth, RF)          │
│   5. Microbiological evidence (not major)                 │
│                                                           │
│ DEFINITE IE:                                              │
│   • 2 Major, OR                                           │
│   • 1 Major + 3 Minor, OR                                 │
│   • 5 Minor                                               │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text:
"ABCDE" 

A - Admit (hospitalize all cases)
B - Blood cultures (x3, before antibiotics)
C - Cardiology/Infectious disease consult
D - Determine organism (culture-directed therapy)
E - Echocardiography (TTE ± TEE)

docs.title: Empiric Therapy (Before culture results)
docs.text:
Native Valve, Community-Acquired: - Ampicillin-Sulbactam 12 g/day IV + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV - OR Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV q12h + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day - OR Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + Gentamicin

Prosthetic Valve (or Nosocomial/Healthcare-associated): - Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV q12h + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day + Rifampin 300 mg PO q8h - (Covers MRSA, Coagulase-negative Staph, Enterococcus)

IV Drug User (Right-sided): - Vancomycin + Gentamicin (covers MRSA) - If MSSA confirmed → Nafcillin or Oxacillin

docs.title: Targeted Therapy (Culture-directed)
docs.text:
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         ORGANISM-SPECIFIC THERAPY                             │
├───────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┤
│ ORGANISM                  │  TREATMENT (Duration)             │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ Viridans Strep (PCN-S)    │ Penicillin G 12-18 MU/day (4 wks)│
│ (MIC <0.12 µg/ml)         │ + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg (2 wks)      │
│                           │ OR Ceftriaxone 2g daily (4 wks)   │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ Viridans Strep (PCN-R)    │ Penicillin G 24 MU/day (4-6 wks)  │
│ (MIC >0.12 µg/ml)         │ + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg (4-6 wks)    │
│                           │ OR Vancomycin (if PCN allergy)    │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ MSSA (Staph aureus)       │ Nafcillin/Oxacillin 12 g/day      │
│ Native valve              │ (4-6 weeks)                       │
│                           │ + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg (3-5 days)   │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ MRSA                      │ Vancomycin 15 mg/kg q12h (6 wks)  │
│ OR Prosthetic valve MSSA  │ + Rifampin 300 mg q8h (PVE)       │
│                           │ + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg (2 wks-PVE)  │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ Enterococcus (PCN-S)      │ Ampicillin 12 g/day (4-6 wks)     │
│                           │ + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg (4-6 wks)    │
│                           │ OR Vancomycin (if PCN allergy)    │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ Enterococcus (VRE)        │ Linezolid 600 mg q12h (8 wks)     │
│ OR High-level gent resist │ OR Daptomycin 10-12 mg/kg         │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ HACEK group               │ Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily (4 wks)   │
│                           │ OR Ampicillin-Sulbactam           │
├───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
│ Fungal (Candida)          │ Amphotericin B (6-10 wks)         │
│                           │ + Valve replacement (MANDATORY)   │
│                           │ Then Fluconazole lifelong         │
└───────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

Duration: - Native valve: 4-6 weeks - Prosthetic valve: 6 weeks minimum - Right-sided (IVDU, uncomplicated): 2 weeks (if sensitive S. aureus)

Monitoring: - Repeat blood cultures at 48-72 hours (should be negative) - Weekly blood cultures until negative - Gentamicin levels (trough <1 µg/ml, peak 3-4 µg/ml) - Vancomycin levels (trough 15-20 µg/ml)

docs.title: Surgical Management
docs.text:
Indications for Surgery - "SHAVE"

S - Severe heart failure (Acute regurgitation, refractory)
H - Heart block (Perivalvular abscess)
A - Abscess (Perivalvular, myocardial)
V - Vegetation (Large >10 mm, mobile, recurrent emboli)
E - Emboli (Recurrent despite antibiotics)

Additional:
F - Fungal IE
P - Prosthetic valve dehiscence
R - Resistant organisms (S. aureus PVE, multi-drug resistant)

docs.title: Detailed Indications:
docs.text:
1. Heart Failure (Most common indication): - Acute severe AR/MR with pulmonary edema - Refractory heart failure despite medical therapy - Fistula formation (valve-to-chamber) - Chordal rupture (flail leaflet)

2. Uncontrolled Infection: - Persistent bacteremia >5-7 days despite appropriate antibiotics - Perivalvular abscess, pseudoaneurysm, fistula - Fungal IE (Candida, Aspergillus) - Difficult-to-treat organisms: S. lugdunensis, Multi-drug resistant - Prosthetic valve endocarditis with S. aureus or fungal

3. Prevention of Embolism: - Large vegetation >10 mm (especially anterior mitral leaflet) - Recurrent emboli despite appropriate therapy - Mobile vegetation >10 mm + other indications

4. Conduction Abnormalities: - New AV block, bundle branch block → Suggests abscess

Timing: - Emergent (<24 hours): Severe refractory heart failure - Urgent (few days): Perivalvular abscess, heart block - Elective (1-2 weeks): After completing antibiotics (if stable)

Contraindications to Surgery: - Recent large stroke with hemorrhagic transformation (delay 3-4 weeks) - Severe neurological damage - Terminal malignancy

docs.title: Cardiac (Most Common)
docs.text:
1. Heart Failure (50-60%) - Most common cause of death in IE - Mechanism: Acute severe valve regurgitation (destruction) - Mortality: 20-40%

2. Perivalvular Abscess (10-15%) - More common: Aortic valve, Prosthetic valves - Clue: New AV block (extension to septum) - Diagnosis: TEE - Treatment: Surgery (drainage + valve replacement)

3. Myocardial Abscess - Extension from valve - High mortality

4. Myocarditis, Pericarditis - Rare

5. Valve Perforation - Leads to acute regurgitation

docs.title: Embolic (20-50%)
docs.text:
Left-Sided IE: - CNS (65%): - Ischemic stroke (Most common neurological) - Hemorrhagic stroke (mycotic aneurysm rupture) - Brain abscess - Meningitis - Spleen (44%): Splenic infarct/abscess - Kidney (30%): Renal infarct - Coronary: MI (rare) - Liver, bowel

Right-Sided IE: - Pulmonary emboli (multiple, septic) - Lung abscesses - Empyema

Mycotic Aneurysm: - Not fungal (misnomer - "mycotic" = fungus-like appearance) - Infected arterial aneurysm - Sites: Middle cerebral artery (most common), Aorta, Visceral arteries - Complication: Rupture → Intracerebral hemorrhage (20% mortality) - Imaging: CT/MR angiography - Treatment: Antibiotics ± Endovascular/surgical repair

docs.title: Renal (10-30%)
docs.text:
1. Glomerulonephritis (Immune complex) - Diffuse proliferative GN (most common) - Hematuria, proteinuria, ↑ Creatinine - Treatment: Antibiotics (resolves with IE treatment)

2. Renal Infarction (Emboli)

3. Interstitial Nephritis (Drug-induced)

docs.title: Neurological (20-40%)
docs.text:
1. Stroke (ischemic > hemorrhagic) 2. Intracerebral hemorrhage (mycotic aneurysm rupture) 3. Brain abscess 4. Meningitis 5. Seizures 6. Encephalopathy

Thrombolysis in IE-related stroke: - Generally contraindicated (risk of hemorrhagic transformation from mycotic aneurysm)

docs.title: Splenic
docs.text: 1. Splenic Infarction/Abscess - LUQ pain, splenomegaly - May require splenectomy

docs.title: Sepsis/Septic Shock
docs.text: • More common with S. aureus IE

docs.title: Current Guidelines (AHA 2007, ESC 2015)
docs.text:
MAJOR CHANGE: Prophylaxis ONLY for HIGH-RISK patients undergoing HIGH-RISK procedures

Previously: Given liberally (discontinued due to lack of evidence, risk of antibiotic resistance)

docs.title: High-Risk Cardiac Conditions (Prophylaxis Indicated)
docs.text:
"PPHC" - Only 4 Indications 

P - Prosthetic cardiac valve
P - Previous infective endocarditis
H - Heart transplant with valvulopathy
C - Congenital heart disease:
    • Unrepaired cyanotic CHD
    • Repaired CHD with residual defect
    • Repaired CHD <6 months post-repair

NO longer indicated for: - Mitral valve prolapse - Bicuspid aortic valve - Acquired valve disease (AS, AR, MS, MR) - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy - ASD, VSD, PDA (repaired >6 months)

docs.title: High-Risk Procedures (Prophylaxis Indicated)
docs.text:
1. Dental Procedures: - ONLY if manipulation of gingival tissue or periapical region of teeth or perforation of oral mucosa - Examples: Tooth extraction, periodontal procedures, dental implants

NOT required for: - Routine cleaning, fluoride treatment - Local anesthetic injections - X-rays - Orthodontic appliance adjustment - Shedding of deciduous teeth

2. Respiratory Tract: - NO longer recommended (Previously: Bronchoscopy with biopsy)

3. GI/GU Procedures: - NO longer routinely recommended - May consider (not routine) if: High-risk cardiac + GU procedure with active infection

docs.title: Prophylactic Regimens
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         IE PROPHYLAXIS REGIMENS (Dental)                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Standard regimen:                                          │
│   Amoxicillin 2 g PO (1 hour before procedure)             │
│                                                            │
│ Penicillin allergy:                                        │
│   Clindamycin 600 mg PO, OR                                │
│   Azithromycin/Clarithromycin 500 mg PO, OR                │
│   Cephalexin 2 g PO (if no anaphylaxis to PCN)             │
│                                                            │
│ Unable to take oral:                                       │
│   Ampicillin 2 g IV/IM, OR                                 │
│   Cefazolin 1 g IV/IM, OR                                  │
│   Clindamycin 600 mg IV                                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Timing: Single dose 30-60 minutes before procedure

Note: If dose inadvertently not given before, can give up to 2 hours after

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Most common organism overall: S. aureus (acute IE, IVDU)

  2. Most common organism in subacute IE: S. viridans (dental)

  3. IVDU IE:

  4. Organism: S. aureus (most common)
  5. Valve: Tricuspid (right-sided)

  6. Complication: Septic pulmonary emboli

  7. Prosthetic valve IE (Early <12 months): S. epidermidis (Coagulase-negative Staph)

  8. S. bovis (S. gallolyticus) IE: Associated with colon cancer → Screen with colonoscopy

  9. Culture-negative IE:

  10. Most common cause: Prior antibiotics
  11. Most common organism: Coxiella burnetii (Q fever)

  12. HACEK group: Fastidious gram-negative, prolonged culture

  13. Duke Criteria:

  14. 2 Major OR 1 Major + 3 Minor OR 5 Minor = Definite IE
  15. Major: (1) Positive blood cultures (typical organism x2), (2) Echo positive

  16. Minor: (1) Predisposing, (2) Fever, (3) Vascular, (4) Immunologic, (5) Micro evidence

  17. Osler nodes: PAINFUL, fingers/toes (immune complex)

  18. Janeway lesions: PAINLESS, palms/soles (septic emboli)

  19. Roth spots: Retinal hemorrhages with white center

  20. Most common complication: Heart failure (most common cause of death)

  21. New AV block in IE: Suggests perivalvular abscess

  22. Most common embolic site: CNS (stroke)

  23. Right-sided IE emboli: Pulmonary (septic emboli)

  24. Mycotic aneurysm: Infected aneurysm (not fungal), middle cerebral artery

  25. Renal involvement: Immune complex glomerulonephritis (diffuse proliferative GN)

  26. Fungal IE: Candida (IVDU, immunocompromised), Surgery mandatory

  27. Surgical indications - "SHAVE":

    • **S**evere heart failure
    • **H**eart block (abscess)
    • **A**bscess
    • **V**egetation (large >10 mm, recurrent emboli)
    • **E**mboli (recurrent)

  28. Prophylaxis indications (HIGH-RISK only):

    • **P**rosthetic valve
    • **P**revious IE
    • **H**eart transplant valvulopathy
    • **C**yanotic CHD (unrepaired or repaired <6 months)

  29. Prophylaxis NOT indicated: MVP, Bicuspid AV, AS/AR, VSD/ASD (repaired >6 months)

  30. Prophylactic drug: Amoxicillin 2g PO (1 hour before dental procedure)

  31. Empiric therapy (native valve): Ampicillin-Sulbactam + Gentamicin OR Vancomycin + Gentamicin

  32. MSSA treatment: Nafcillin/Oxacillin (4-6 weeks)

  33. MRSA treatment: Vancomycin (6 weeks)

  34. Enterococcus treatment: Ampicillin + Gentamicin (both for 4-6 weeks - synergy)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: IVDU with fever, multiple pulmonary nodules → Right-sided (tricuspid) IE with septic pulmonary emboli

Scenario 2: Dental extraction, 2 weeks later fever + new murmur → Subacute IE (S. viridans)

Scenario 3: IE with S. bovis bacteremia → Screen for colon cancer

Scenario 4: IE with new PR prolongation/AV block → Perivalvular abscess → Need surgery

Scenario 5: IE with painless hemorrhagic lesions on palms → Janeway lesions (septic emboli)

Scenario 6: IE with painful nodules on fingertips → Osler nodes (immune complex)

Scenario 7: IE with hematuria, proteinuria, RBC casts → Immune complex glomerulonephritis

Scenario 8: IE with large anterior mitral vegetation (15 mm) → High embolic risk → Consider surgery

Scenario 9: Prosthetic valve patient undergoing dental extraction → Amoxicillin 2g PO prophylaxis

Scenario 10: Culture-negative IE with IgG Phase I antibody >1:800 → Coxiella burnetii (Q fever)

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Organisms: S. aureus (acute/IVDU), S. viridans (subacute), Enterococcus (GU/GI), S. epidermidis (PVE)
  • IVDU: S. aureus, tricuspid valve, pulmonary emboli
  • S. bovis → Colon cancer screening
  • Culture-negative: Prior antibiotics, Coxiella, HACEK, Bartonella
  • Duke Criteria: 2 Major OR 1 Major + 3 Minor OR 5 Minor
  • Major: (1) Blood cultures, (2) Echo
  • Minor: (1) Predisposing, (2) Fever, (3) Vascular, (4) Immunologic, (5) Micro
  • Osler (painful) vs Janeway (painless)
  • Roth spots: Retinal hemorrhages with white center
  • Complications: Heart failure (most common), Emboli (CNS), Abscess, GN
  • New AV block → Abscess
  • Surgical indications: SHAVE
  • Prophylaxis: PPHC only (Prosthetic, Previous IE, Heart transplant, Cyanotic CHD)
  • Prophylaxis drug: Amoxicillin 2g PO
  • Treatment: Nafcillin (MSSA), Vancomycin (MRSA), Ampicillin+Gent (Enterococcus)
  • Fungal IE → Surgery mandatory

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 30-year-old IV drug user presents with fever and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules on chest X-ray. Blood cultures grow S. aureus. Which valve is most likely affected?

A) Mitral B) Aortic C) Tricuspid D) Pulmonic

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Tricuspid** **Explanation:** - **IVDU IE** most commonly affects **right-sided valves** - **Tricuspid valve** (90% of IVDU IE) - **Organism:** S. aureus (most common in IVDU) - **Pulmonary nodules:** Septic pulmonary emboli from right-sided IE - **Other features:** Dyspnea, hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain **Left-sided valves** (Mitral, Aortic) affected in non-IVDU IE

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A patient with IE has painless erythematous macules on palms and soles. What is the most likely diagnosis of these lesions?

A) Osler nodes B) Janeway lesions C) Roth spots D) Splinter hemorrhages

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Janeway lesions** **Explanation:** - **Janeway lesions:** **PAINLESS**, hemorrhagic macules on **palms/soles** - Mechanism: **Septic emboli** - More common in **acute IE** (S. aureus) **Differentials:** - **Osler nodes:** **PAINFUL**, nodules on **finger/toe tips** (immune complex) - **Roth spots:** **Retinal** hemorrhages with white center - **Splinter hemorrhages:** Linear, under **nails** **Mnemonic:** - **J**aneway = **J**ust doesn't hurt (Painless) - **O**sler = **O**uch! (Painful)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with IE develops new first-degree AV block. What complication should be suspected?

A) Large vegetation B) Perivalvular abscess C) Myocarditis D) Septic pulmonary emboli

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Perivalvular abscess** **Explanation:** - **New conduction abnormalities** (AV block, BBB) in IE → Suspect **perivalvular abscess** - **Mechanism:** Abscess extends into **interventricular septum** (conduction system) - **Most common:** Aortic valve IE (proximity to septum) **Diagnosis:** - **TEE** (more sensitive than TTE for abscess) **Management:** - **Surgical drainage + valve replacement** (URGENT) - High mortality if untreated **Other surgical indications:** - Heart failure, fungal IE, persistent bacteremia, large mobile vegetations

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A patient with S. bovis endocarditis should be screened for which malignancy?

A) Pancreatic cancer B) Colon cancer C) Renal cell carcinoma D) Hepatocellular carcinoma

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Colon cancer** **Explanation:** - **S. bovis** (now reclassified as **S. gallolyticus**) IE has **strong association** with **colon cancer** - **Mechanism:** S. bovis colonizes colorectal tumors/polyps → Transient bacteremia - **Up to 60%** of S. bovis IE patients have colonic neoplasia **Management:** - **Colonoscopy** mandatory in all S. bovis IE cases - Also consider other GI pathology (diverticulosis, inflammatory bowel disease) **Other GI-associated organisms:** - **Enterococcus:** GI/GU procedures - **Clostridium septicum:** Colon cancer (septicemia, not IE typically)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Which of the following patients requires IE prophylaxis before dental extraction?

A) Mitral valve prolapse without regurgitation B) Bicuspid aortic valve C) Previous infective endocarditis D) Repaired VSD (2 years ago, no residual defect)

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Previous infective endocarditis** **Explanation:** - **AHA 2007 Guidelines:** Prophylaxis ONLY for **HIGH-RISK** patients **HIGH-RISK (Prophylaxis indicated) - "PPHC":** 1. **P**rosthetic cardiac valve 2. **P**revious infective endocarditis ✓ 3. **H**eart transplant with valvulopathy 4. **C**yanotic congenital heart disease (unrepaired or repaired <6 months with residual defect) **NO longer indicated for:** - MVP (with or without regurgitation) - Bicuspid aortic valve - Acquired valve disease (AS, AR, MS, MR) - **Repaired CHD >6 months without residual defect** (VSD, ASD, PDA) **Regimen:** **Amoxicillin 2g PO** (30-60 min before procedure)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient with culture-negative IE has IgG Phase I antibody titer of 1:1024 against Coxiella burnetii. This is a major criterion for which classification system?

A) Jones criteria B) Duke criteria C) SIRS criteria D) Framingham criteria

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Duke criteria** **Explanation:** - **Modified Duke Criteria** used for IE diagnosis **Major Criterion:** - **Single positive blood culture for Coxiella burnetii** OR - **IgG Phase I antibody titer >1:800** **Coxiella burnetii (Q fever):** - **Most common cause** of culture-negative IE (intracellular organism) - Diagnosis: **Serology** (not blood culture) - **Chronic Q fever** → IE - Treatment: **Doxycycline + Hydroxychloroquine** (18 months minimum) **Other Criteria:** - **Jones:** Rheumatic fever - **SIRS:** Sepsis - **Framingham:** Heart failure

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A patient with mitral valve IE has microscopic hematuria and RBC casts. What is the most likely renal complication?

A) Renal infarction B) Acute tubular necrosis C) Immune complex glomerulonephritis D) Interstitial nephritis

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Immune complex glomerulonephritis** **Explanation:** - **RBC casts** are pathognomonic of **glomerulonephritis** - IE-associated GN: **Immune complex-mediated** (Type III hypersensitivity) **Pathology:** - **Diffuse proliferative GN** (most common) - Less common: Focal proliferative, Membranoproliferative, Crescentic **Presentation:** - Microscopic hematuria - Proteinuria (usually non-nephrotic) - ↑ Creatinine (variable) - Hypocomplementemia (low C3, C4) **Management:** - **Treat IE** (antibiotics) → GN usually resolves - No need for immunosuppression (unlike primary GN) **Differentials:** - **Renal infarction:** Flank pain, hematuria (no RBC casts) - **ATN:** Muddy brown casts - **Interstitial nephritis:** WBC casts, eosinophiluria

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen for suspected native valve IE in a community-acquired case?

A) Vancomycin alone B) Ceftriaxone + Gentamicin C) Ampicillin-Sulbactam + Gentamicin D) Nafcillin + Rifampin

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Ampicillin-Sulbactam + Gentamicin** **Explanation:** - **Empiric therapy** (before culture results) must cover **most likely organisms** **Native Valve, Community-Acquired:** - **Cover:** S. viridans, S. aureus (MSSA), Enterococcus - **Regimen:** **Ampicillin-Sulbactam 12 g/day IV + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day** - Ampicillin-Sulbactam: Covers Strep, MSSA, Enterococcus - Gentamicin: Synergy for Strep and Enterococcus **Alternatives:** - **Vancomycin + Gentamicin** (if PCN allergy or risk of MRSA) - **Ceftriaxone + Gentamicin** (alternative) **Prosthetic Valve or Nosocomial:** - **Vancomycin + Gentamicin + Rifampin** (cover MRSA, Coag-neg Staph) **Targeted therapy** after culture results

End of Infective Endocarditis Study Material

Next Topics: - Rheumatic Heart Disease - Valvular Heart Disease - Cardiomyopathies

Cross-References: - Heart Failure → Medicine/03_Heart_Failure.md - Arrhythmias → Medicine/04_Arrhythmias.md - Hypertension → Medicine/05_Hypertension.md

Study Tips: 1. Master Duke Criteria (very high-yield) 2. Know organism-procedure-valve associations 3. Understand prophylaxis guidelines (only 4 indications now) 4. Remember Osler vs Janeway (comes in every exam) 5. Know surgical indications - SHAVE 6. Practice antibiotic regimens for different organisms

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 4-5 hours Revision Time: 1.5-2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (⅘) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition & Overview
  2. Etiology & Pathogenesis
  3. Acute Rheumatic Fever
  4. Chronic Rheumatic Heart Disease
  5. Clinical Features
  6. Diagnosis - Jones Criteria
  7. Investigations
  8. Management
  9. Prophylaxis
  10. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  11. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) is chronic valvular heart disease resulting from Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF), a delayed autoimmune complication of Group A Streptococcal (GAS) pharyngitis.

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Most common cause of acquired heart disease in children and young adults (worldwide)
  • Most common cause of mitral stenosis (developing countries)
  • Prevalence: Low in developed countries (improved hygiene, antibiotics)
  • Prevalence: High in developing countries (India, Sub-Saharan Africa, South America)

docs.title: Spectrum
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         RHEUMATIC DISEASE SPECTRUM                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│  GAS Pharyngitis (Group A Streptococcus)                   │
│         ↓                                                  │
│  (2-4 weeks later)                                         │
│         ↓                                                  │
│  Acute Rheumatic Fever (ARF)                               │
│  • Carditis, Polyarthritis, Chorea, Erythema, Nodules     │
│         ↓                                                  │
│  (Years later - if recurrent ARF)                          │
│         ↓                                                  │
│  Chronic Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD)                     │
│  • Mitral Stenosis (most common)                           │
│  • Mitral Regurgitation                                    │
│  • Aortic Regurgitation                                    │
│  • Combined lesions                                        │
│                                                            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Group A β-Hemolytic Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes)

Key Points: - Only pharyngitis leads to ARF (NOT skin infections/pyoderma) - M-protein serotypes: M1, M3, M5, M18 (rheumatogenic strains) - Not all strains cause ARF

docs.title: Pathogenesis
docs.text:
Mechanism: Molecular mimicry (Autoimmune)

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         PATHOGENESIS OF ARF                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                        │
│  1. GAS Pharyngitis                                    │
│     ↓                                                  │
│  2. Immune Response to GAS antigens                    │
│     (M-protein, Hyaluronic acid capsule)               │
│     ↓                                                  │
│  3. MOLECULAR MIMICRY:                                 │
│     • GAS antigens resemble human proteins             │
│       - M-protein ≈ Cardiac myosin                     │
│       - M-protein ≈ Joint proteins                     │
│       - M-protein ≈ Basal ganglia                      │
│     ↓                                                  │
│  4. Cross-Reactive Antibodies                          │
│     • Attack heart, joints, brain                      │
│     ↓                                                  │
│  5. Autoimmune Inflammation                            │
│     ↓                                                  │
│  6. Acute Rheumatic Fever                              │
│                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Latent Period: 2-4 weeks (average 19 days) after GAS pharyngitis

Risk Factors for ARF: - Age: 5-15 years (peak incidence) - Genetic predisposition: HLA-DR7, HLA-DR4 - Poverty, crowding - Poor hygiene - Recurrent GAS infections

docs.title: Clinical Manifestations - "JONES" Criteria
docs.text:
5 Major + 4 Minor Criteria (Discussed in Diagnosis section)

Major Manifestations: 1. Carditis (Pancarditis) 2. Polyarthritis (Migratory) 3. Chorea (Sydenham's chorea) 4. Erythema Marginatum 5. Subcutaneous Nodules

docs.title: 1. Carditis (50-60% of ARF)
docs.text:
Most serious manifestation (only one causing permanent damage)

Pancarditis: Involves all 3 layers - Endocarditis: Valvulitis (Mitral > Aortic) - Myocarditis: Dilated heart, heart failure - Pericarditis: Pericardial effusion, friction rub

Valvular Involvement: 

Mitral valve:     75-80% (Most common)
Aortic valve:     30% (Usually with mitral)
Tricuspid:        10% (Rare alone)
Pulmonary:        <5% (Very rare)

Acute Valvular Lesions: - Verrucae: Small vegetations along line of closure (1-2 mm) - Regurgitation (acute phase - valve edema, inflammation) - Mitral regurgitation - Aortic regurgitation - Stenosis: Develops LATER (chronic RHD) due to scarring

Clinical Features of Carditis: - Tachycardia (out of proportion to fever) - New murmur: - Carey Coombs murmur: Mid-diastolic rumble (mitral valvulitis) - Mitral regurgitation: Holosystolic, apex → axilla - Aortic regurgitation: Early diastolic, left sternal border - Cardiomegaly - Heart failure (severe carditis) - Pericardial friction rub (pericarditis) - S3 gallop

Aschoff Bodies (Pathognomonic): - Granulomas in myocardium - Contains Anitschkow cells (macrophages with ribbon-like chromatin - "caterpillar cells") - Aschoff giant cells (multinucleated)

docs.title: 2. Polyarthritis (70-75% of ARF)
docs.text:
Most common manifestation (but does NOT cause permanent damage)

Characteristics: - Large joints: Knees, ankles, elbows, wrists - Migratory: Moves from one joint to another (1-2 days per joint) - Symmetric - Additive: New joints affected while old ones still symptomatic - Exquisitely painful - Red, hot, swollen, tender - Dramatic response to aspirin (within 24-48 hours) - Diagnostic clue

Fleeting: Resolves completely without deformity (unlike rheumatoid arthritis)

Duration: 2-3 weeks

Note: Small joints, axial joints (spine) NOT typically involved

docs.title: 3. Sydenham's Chorea (10-15% of ARF)
docs.text:
St. Vitus' Dance - Involuntary, purposeless, rapid movements

Characteristics: - Delayed onset: 1-6 months after GAS infection (longest latency) - Affects: Face, tongue, upper limbs (more than lower limbs) - Exacerbated by stress, disappear during sleep - Associated: Emotional lability, muscle weakness, hypotonia - More common: Females

Signs: - Milkmaid's grip: Intermittent grip strength - Pronator sign: Hands pronate when held above head - Choreiform movements: Grimacing, tongue protrusion

Prognosis: - Self-limited: Resolves in 3-6 months - May recur during pregnancy (Chorea gravidarum), OCP use

Treatment: - Haloperidol or Valproic acid (if severe) - Benzathine penicillin prophylaxis

docs.title: 4. Erythema Marginatum (<5% of ARF)
docs.text:
Characteristics: - Evanescent, pink/red rash - Serpiginous (snake-like), annular (ring-shaped) with central clearing - Trunk and proximal limbs (NOT face) - Non-pruritic, non-painful - Blanches with pressure - Migratory, transient (comes and goes) - Heat-sensitive (appears with warmth)

Pathognomonic for ARF (if present)

docs.title: 5. Subcutaneous Nodules (1-5% of ARF)
docs.text:
Characteristics: - Firm, painless, pea-sized (0.5-2 cm) - Over bony prominences, extensor surfaces - Elbows, knees, wrists, ankles - Occiput, spine - Symmetric - Mobile under skin - Last 1-2 weeks

Associated with severe carditis

Rare in current era

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
• Results from recurrent episodes of ARF
  • Progressive fibrosis and scarring of valves
  • Develops years to decades after initial ARF

docs.title: Valvular Lesions
docs.text:
Mitral Valve (Most Common):

1. Mitral Stenosis (MS) - Most common RHD lesion - Mechanism: - Commissural fusion - Leaflet thickening, calcification - Chordal fusion and shortening - Fish-mouth or buttonhole orifice - Normal mitral valve area: 4-6 cm² - Severe MS: <1 cm²

2. Mitral Regurgitation (MR) - Leaflet thickening, retraction - Chordal shortening - Incomplete coaptation

3. Combined MS + MR

Aortic Valve:

1. Aortic Regurgitation (AR) - More common than AS - Leaflet thickening, retraction - Incomplete coaptation - Aortic root dilation

2. Aortic Stenosis (AS) - Commissural fusion - Leaflet calcification

3. Combined AS + AR

Combined Lesions: - Mitral + Aortic: Common (30% of RHD) - Mitral + Aortic + Tricuspid: 10%

Tricuspid Valve: - Usually regurgitation (functional due to RV dilation) - Rarely: Tricuspid stenosis (always with mitral disease)

Pulmonary Valve: - Very rare

docs.title: Pathology
docs.text:
Gross: - Thickened, fibrosed valves - Fused commissures - Shortened, thickened chordae - Fish-mouth orifice (MS) - Calcification (chronic)

Microscopic: - Aschoff bodies (acute phase) - Pathognomonic - Anitschkow cells (macrophages) - Fibrosis (chronic)

MacCallum's patch: - Left atrial endocardial thickening - Due to regurgitant jet in MR

docs.title: Acute Rheumatic Fever
docs.text:
General: - Fever: Low to moderate (101-103°F) - Malaise, fatigue - Preceding pharyngitis (2-4 weeks before) - Age: 5-15 years (typically)

Major Manifestations: - Carditis: Murmurs, tachycardia, cardiomegaly, HF - Polyarthritis: Migratory, large joints, exquisitely painful - Chorea: Involuntary movements (delayed onset) - Erythema marginatum: Annular rash, trunk/limbs - Subcutaneous nodules: Bony prominences

Minor Manifestations: - Fever - Arthralgia (if arthritis not present as major) - Elevated acute phase reactants (ESR, CRP) - Prolonged PR interval (first-degree AV block)

docs.title: Chronic RHD
docs.text:
