Symptoms depend on valve lesion: - Mitral stenosis: Dyspnea, orthopnea, PND, hemoptysis, AF - Mitral regurgitation: Dyspnea, fatigue - Aortic regurgitation: Dyspnea, angina, wide pulse pressure - Aortic stenosis: Syncope, angina, dyspnea

Detailed in: Valvular Heart Disease topic

docs.title: Modified Jones Criteria (2015 AHA/ACC)
docs.text:
For Diagnosis of ACUTE RHEUMATIC FEVER:

Requirements: 

Evidence of GAS infection
        +
2 Major criteria, OR
1 Major + 2 Minor criteria

docs.title: Evidence of Preceding GAS Infection (MANDATORY)
docs.text:
One of: 1. Positive throat culture or Rapid Strep antigen test 2. Elevated/Rising anti-streptococcal antibodies: - ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) - Most common - Anti-DNase B - Anti-Hyaluronidase - Streptozyme (multiple antibodies)

Note: - ASO peaks at 3-5 weeks, remains elevated for months - Anti-DNase B more specific (remains elevated longer) - Negative ASO does NOT rule out ARF (20% of cases)

docs.title: Major Criteria
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         MAJOR CRITERIA ("JONES")                     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ J - Joints (Polyarthritis - migratory, large)        │
│ O - ❤️ (Carditis - pancarditis)                      │
│ N - Nodules (Subcutaneous)                           │
│ E - Erythema marginatum                              │
│ S - Sydenham's chorea                                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Note: 2015 revision has different criteria for low-risk vs moderate/high-risk populations:

Low-Risk Populations (e.g., USA, Western Europe): - Polyarthritis (only)

Moderate/High-Risk (e.g., India, Africa): - Polyarthritis OR Monoarthritis OR Polyarthralgia

docs.title: Minor Criteria
docs.text:
Clinical: 1. Fever: ≥38.5°C (low-risk) or ≥38°C (moderate/high-risk) 2. Arthralgia: (If arthritis NOT present as major) - Polyarthralgia (low-risk) - Monoarthralgia (moderate/high-risk)

Laboratory: 3. Elevated acute phase reactants: - ESR ≥60 mm/hr (low-risk) or ≥30 mm/hr (moderate/high-risk) - CRP ≥3 mg/dL (low-risk) or ≥1 mg/dL (moderate/high-risk)

  1. Prolonged PR interval (first-degree AV block) on ECG
  2. Age-specific values

docs.title: Special Situations
docs.text:
1. Recurrent ARF (Patient with history of ARF/RHD): - Lower threshold: 2 Minor criteria + Evidence of GAS infection

2. Chorea alone: - May be sufficient for diagnosis (long latency - other criteria resolved)

3. Indolent Carditis: - May present alone (especially in chronic RHD reactivation)

docs.title: Diagnosis Algorithm
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ARF DIAGNOSIS FLOWCHART                           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                        │
│  Evidence of GAS infection?                            │
│  (Throat culture, ASO, Anti-DNase B)                   │
│         │                                              │
│         ├─ YES → Proceed                               │
│         └─ NO  → ARF unlikely                          │
│                                                        │
│  Count Major and Minor Criteria:                       │
│         │                                              │
│         ├─ 2 Major → ARF                               │
│         ├─ 1 Major + 2 Minor → ARF                     │
│         └─ Otherwise → ARF unlikely                    │
│                                                        │
│  Special:                                              │
│  • Chorea alone (with GAS) → ARF                       │
│  • Recurrent ARF: 2 Minor + GAS → ARF                  │
│                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Microbiological
docs.text:
1. Throat Culture: - Gold standard for current GAS infection - Sensitivity: 90-95% (if done properly) - May be negative by time of ARF (2-4 weeks later)

2. Rapid Strep Antigen Test: - Quick (5-10 minutes) - Lower sensitivity than culture - High specificity

3. Anti-Streptococcal Antibodies: - ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O): - Most widely used - Peaks at 3-5 weeks - Elevated: >200 Todd units (adults), >333 (children) - Caveat: NOT elevated after skin infections (streptolysin O inactivated by skin lipids)

  • Anti-DNase B:
  • More specific than ASO
  • Elevated after both pharyngitis AND skin infections
  • Remains elevated longer

  • Elevated: >480 units (children), >240 (adults)

  • Streptozyme:

  • Detects multiple antibodies (ASO, DNase B, Hyaluronidase, Streptokinase)
  • Screening test

Interpretation: - Rising titer (2-fold increase) more significant than single elevated value - Negative ASO does NOT rule out ARF (20%)

docs.title: Acute Phase Reactants
docs.text:
1. ESR (Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate): - Elevated in ARF (usually >60 mm/hr) - Non-specific - May be normal in chorea, mild carditis

2. CRP (C-Reactive Protein): - Elevated in ARF - More sensitive than ESR - Returns to normal faster than ESR

Note: - Normal ESR/CRP makes ARF unlikely (except isolated chorea) - Not elevated in chorea (long latency)

docs.title: Cardiac Investigations
docs.text:
1. ECG: - PR prolongation (first-degree AV block) - Most common (40-50%) - Minor criterion (if not higher-degree block) - Second/Third-degree AV block (less common) - ST-T changes (myocarditis, pericarditis) - Arrhythmias: Atrial fibrillation (chronic MS)

2. Echocardiography: - Mandatory in all suspected ARF - Subclinical carditis: Echo-detected valvulitis without clinical signs - Findings: - Valve regurgitation (Mitral > Aortic) - Valve thickening - Restricted motion - Pericardial effusion - LV dysfunction

3. Chest X-ray: - Cardiomegaly (severe carditis, chronic RHD) - Pulmonary edema (heart failure) - Normal (mild carditis)

docs.title: Other Investigations
docs.text:
CBC: - Leukocytosis (neutrophilia) - Anemia (chronic disease - chronic RHD)

Serum Protein Electrophoresis: - ↑ α2-globulin, γ-globulin

docs.title: 1. Eradicate GAS Infection
docs.text:
Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM (single dose) - DOC for GAS eradication - OR Oral Penicillin V 250 mg QID × 10 days

Penicillin Allergy: - Azithromycin 500 mg daily × 5 days, OR - Erythromycin 250 mg QID × 10 days

Note: Given even if throat culture negative (ARF occurs 2-4 weeks later)

docs.title: 2. Anti-Inflammatory Therapy
docs.text:
For Arthritis/Arthralgia:

Aspirin (High-dose): - Dose: 80-100 mg/kg/day (divided q4-6h) - Duration: 4-8 weeks (taper gradually) - Response: Dramatic within 24-48 hours (diagnostic clue) - Monitoring: Salicylate levels (20-25 mg/dL therapeutic) - Side effects: Tinnitus, GI upset, hepatotoxicity

For Carditis:

Mild Carditis (no cardiomegaly, HF): - Aspirin (same as arthritis)

Moderate to Severe Carditis (cardiomegaly, HF): - Prednisolone: - Dose: 2 mg/kg/day (max 60-80 mg) - Duration: 2-3 weeks, then taper over 2-3 weeks - Overlap aspirin during steroid taper - Benefit: Reduces inflammation, may prevent chronic damage (controversial)

Heart Failure Management: - Bed rest - Diuretics: Furosemide - ACE inhibitors - Digoxin: If AF, severe MR - Avoid β-blockers initially (may worsen AV block)

docs.title: 3. Symptomatic Treatment
docs.text:
Chorea: - Mild: Reassurance, avoid stress - Severe/Disabling: - Haloperidol 0.5-2 mg BID/TID - Valproic acid 15-20 mg/kg/day (alternative) - Carbamazepine (alternative)

General: - Bed rest (during acute phase, especially carditis) - Fever: Antipyretics (paracetamol)

docs.title: 4. Secondary Prophylaxis (Most Important)
docs.text:
Prevent recurrent ARF → Prevent chronic RHD

See Prophylaxis section below

docs.title: Chronic RHD
docs.text:
Medical Management: - Heart failure: Diuretics, ACE inhibitors, Digoxin - Atrial fibrillation: Rate control, anticoagulation (Warfarin) - Infective endocarditis prophylaxis (if indicated) - Continued secondary prophylaxis

Surgical Management: - Indicated for significant valve dysfunction - Mitral stenosis: - PBMV (Percutaneous Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty): If suitable anatomy - Open mitral commissurotomy: If not suitable for PBMV - Mitral valve replacement: If severe calcification, MR, unsuitable anatomy - Mitral/Aortic regurgitation: Valve repair/replacement - Aortic stenosis: Valve replacement

Detailed in: Valvular Heart Disease topic

docs.title: Primary Prophylaxis
docs.text:
Goal: Prevent ARF by treating GAS pharyngitis

Indications: - All GAS pharyngitis (especially in children, endemic areas)

Regimen: - Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM (single dose) - Best - OR Oral Penicillin V 250 mg TID × 10 days

Penicillin Allergy: - Azithromycin 500 mg daily × 5 days - Erythromycin 250 mg QID × 10 days - First-generation cephalosporin (if no anaphylaxis to PCN)

Important: Complete 10-day course (oral) - adherence critical

docs.title: Secondary Prophylaxis (Most Important)
docs.text:
Goal: Prevent recurrent ARF → Prevent/limit chronic RHD

Indications: - ALL patients with history of ARF (with or without carditis)

Regimen:

Benzathine Penicillin G: - Dose: 1.2 million units IM - Frequency: - Every 4 weeks (standard) - Every 3 weeks (high-risk areas, previous recurrence) - Route: IM (better adherence than oral)

Oral Alternatives (if IM not feasible): - Penicillin V 250 mg PO BID, OR - Sulfadiazine 0.5 g (if <27 kg) or 1 g (if >27 kg) PO daily

Penicillin Allergy: - Azithromycin 250 mg PO daily, OR - Erythromycin 250 mg PO BID

docs.title: Duration of Secondary Prophylaxis
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      DURATION OF SECONDARY PROPHYLAXIS                   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                          │
│ ARF WITHOUT carditis:                                    │
│   → 5 years OR until age 21 (whichever longer)           │
│                                                          │
│ ARF WITH carditis but NO residual heart disease:         │
│   → 10 years OR until age 21 (whichever longer)          │
│                                                          │
│ ARF WITH carditis AND residual heart disease (RHD):      │
│   → 10 years OR until age 40 (whichever longer)          │
│   → Some recommend LIFELONG (especially severe RHD)      │
│                                                          │
│ Post-valve surgery:                                      │
│   → LIFELONG                                             │
│                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

High-Risk Patients (consider longer/lifelong): - Healthcare workers - Teachers (exposure to children) - Parents of young children - Living in endemic areas

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. ARF is autoimmune sequela of Group A Streptococcal (GAS) pharyngitis
  2. Only pharyngitis (NOT skin infections) causes ARF

  3. Latent period: 2-4 weeks

  4. Mechanism: Molecular mimicry (GAS antigens resemble cardiac myosin, joints, basal ganglia)

  5. Age: 5-15 years (peak)

  6. Jones Criteria for ARF Diagnosis:

  7. Evidence of GAS infection (MANDATORY)

  8. PLUS: 2 Major OR 1 Major + 2 Minor

  9. Major Criteria - "JONES":

  10. **J**oints (Polyarthritis - migratory, large)
  11. O (Heart - Carditis)
  12. **N**odules (Subcutaneous)
  13. **E**rythema marginatum

  14. **S**ydenham's chorea

  15. Minor Criteria:

  16. Fever, Arthralgia, ↑ ESR/CRP, Prolonged PR

  17. Polyarthritis:

  18. Most common manifestation (70-75%)
  19. Large joints: Knees, ankles, elbows, wrists
  20. Migratory, additive
  21. Exquisitely painful
  22. Dramatic response to aspirin (24-48 hours)

  23. No permanent damage

  24. Carditis:

  25. Most serious manifestation (only one causing permanent damage)
  26. Pancarditis: Endocarditis + Myocarditis + Pericarditis
  27. Valve: Mitral > Aortic
  28. Acute: Regurgitation (MR, AR)
  29. Carey Coombs murmur: Mid-diastolic rumble (mitral valvulitis)

  30. Aschoff bodies: Pathognomonic granulomas in myocardium (Anitschkow cells)

  31. Sydenham's Chorea:

  32. Delayed onset: 1-6 months (longest latency)
  33. Involuntary, purposeless movements
  34. Females > Males
  35. Self-limited: 3-6 months

  36. May be sole manifestation (other criteria resolved)

  37. Erythema Marginatum:

    • Annular, serpiginous, pink rash
    • Trunk/proximal limbs (NOT face)
    • Central clearing
    • Pathognomonic for ARF

  38. ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O):

    • Most common anti-streptococcal antibody test
    • Peaks: 3-5 weeks post-infection
    • Negative in 20% of ARF cases

  39. Anti-DNase B:

    • More specific than ASO
    • Elevated after pharyngitis AND skin infections (unlike ASO)

  40. ECG: PR prolongation (first-degree AV block) - most common (40-50%)

  41. Treatment of ARF:

    • Eradicate GAS: Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM
    • Arthritis: Aspirin 80-100 mg/kg/day (dramatic response)
    • Severe carditis: Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day
    • Chorea: Haloperidol, Valproic acid

  42. Secondary Prophylaxis (MOST IMPORTANT):

    • Regimen: Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks
    • Duration:
    • No carditis: 5 years or age 21
    • Carditis no RHD: 10 years or age 21
    • RHD: 10 years or age 40 (or lifelong)

  43. Chronic RHD:

    • Results from recurrent ARF
    • Most common lesion: Mitral stenosis
    • Valve involvement: Mitral (75%) > Aortic (30%) > Tricuspid (10%)

  44. Pathology:

    • Aschoff bodies: Pathognomonic (granulomas with Anitschkow cells)
    • Commissural fusion, leaflet thickening, chordal fusion
    • Fish-mouth orifice (MS)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Child with sore throat 3 weeks ago, now fever + migratory joint pain (knees, ankles) → ARF with polyarthritis

Scenario 2: ARF patient with dramatic response to aspirin within 24 hours → Confirms polyarthritis

Scenario 3: ARF with involuntary movements, emotional lability starting 3 months after pharyngitis → Sydenham's chorea

Scenario 4: ARF with annular rash on trunk, central clearing → Erythema marginatum

Scenario 5: ARF with mid-diastolic rumble at apex → Carey Coombs murmur (mitral valvulitis)

Scenario 6: ARF patient with elevated ASO, normal ESR/CRP → Likely isolated chorea (long latency)

Scenario 7: Young adult with mitral stenosis (fish-mouth orifice on echo) → Chronic RHD

Scenario 8: ARF patient → Give Benzathine Penicillin G IM every 3-4 weeks for secondary prophylaxis

Scenario 9: ARF without carditis → Continue prophylaxis for 5 years or until age 21

Scenario 10: ARF with carditis and RHD → Continue prophylaxis for 10 years or until age 40

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• ARF: Autoimmune, post-GAS pharyngitis, 2-4 weeks latency
  • Molecular mimicry mechanism
  • Jones Criteria: GAS evidence + (2 Major OR 1 Major + 2 Minor)
  • Major: JONES (Joints, O-heart, Nodules, Erythema, Sydenham's)
  • Minor: Fever, Arthralgia, ↑ ESR/CRP, PR prolongation
  • Polyarthritis: Migratory, large joints, aspirin response
  • Carditis: Pancarditis, MR/AR (acute), Carey Coombs murmur
  • Aschoff bodies: Pathognomonic
  • Chorea: Delayed (1-6 months), self-limited
  • Erythema marginatum: Annular, trunk, pathognomonic
  • ASO peaks 3-5 weeks, negative in 20%
  • Treatment: Benzathine PCN (eradicate), Aspirin (arthritis), Prednisolone (severe carditis)
  • Secondary prophylaxis: Benzathine PCN IM q3-4 weeks
  • Duration: 5y/age 21 (no carditis), 10y/age 21 (carditis no RHD), 10y/age 40 (RHD)
  • Chronic RHD: Mitral stenosis most common

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 10-year-old boy presents with fever and migratory joint pain affecting knees and ankles for 1 week. He had sore throat 3 weeks ago. On examination, joints are swollen and tender. Which feature would MOST support the diagnosis of ARF?

A) Joint pain persisting for >1 month B) Dramatic improvement within 24 hours of aspirin C) Small joint involvement (fingers, toes) D) Residual joint deformity

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Dramatic improvement within 24 hours of aspirin** **Explanation:** - **ARF polyarthritis** has **characteristic dramatic response to aspirin** within 24-48 hours - This rapid response is a **diagnostic clue** for ARF **ARF Polyarthritis Features:** - **Large joints** (knees, ankles, elbows, wrists) - **Migratory** (moves from joint to joint) - **Exquisitely painful** - **Fleeting** (2-3 weeks total) - **NO residual deformity** (unlike rheumatoid arthritis) **Why not others:** - Prolonged duration (>1 month) suggests other diagnosis - Small joints NOT typically involved in ARF - NO residual deformity in ARF (complete resolution)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 12-year-old girl diagnosed with ARF has involuntary, purposeless movements of face and hands that worsen with stress and disappear during sleep. These symptoms appeared 4 months after sore throat. Her ESR and CRP are normal. What is the most likely manifestation?

A) Polyarthritis B) Sydenham's chorea C) Carditis D) Erythema marginatum

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Sydenham's chorea** **Explanation:** - **Sydenham's chorea** (St. Vitus' Dance) - **Characteristic features:** - **Delayed onset:** 1-6 months after GAS infection (LONGEST latency) - **Involuntary, purposeless movements** - **Worsen with stress, disappear during sleep** - **Emotional lability** - **Normal ESR/CRP** (due to long latency - acute inflammation resolved) - **May be SOLE manifestation** of ARF (other criteria resolved by then) **Treatment:** - Self-limited (3-6 months) - If severe: Haloperidol or Valproic acid **Still requires:** - Benzathine Penicillin for GAS eradication - Secondary prophylaxis

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Which histopathological finding is PATHOGNOMONIC for rheumatic carditis?

A) Caseating granulomas B) Aschoff bodies C) Langerhans giant cells D) Reed-Sternberg cells

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Aschoff bodies** **Explanation:** - **Aschoff bodies** are **PATHOGNOMONIC** for rheumatic carditis - **Granulomas in myocardium** **Components:** - **Anitschkow cells** (macrophages with ribbon-like chromatin - "caterpillar cells") - **Aschoff giant cells** (multinucleated) - Central area of fibrinoid necrosis - Lymphocytes **Other findings:** - **Caseating granulomas:** Tuberculosis - **Langerhans giant cells:** Tuberculosis, sarcoidosis - **Reed-Sternberg cells:** Hodgkin's lymphoma

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 15-year-old diagnosed with ARF with carditis but NO residual heart disease on echo at discharge. What is the recommended duration of secondary prophylaxis?

A) 5 years or until age 21, whichever is longer B) 10 years or until age 21, whichever is longer C) 10 years or until age 40, whichever is longer D) Lifelong

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) 10 years or until age 21, whichever is longer** **Explanation:** - **Duration of secondary prophylaxis:** 
ARF WITHOUT carditis:
  → 5 years OR until age 21 (whichever longer)

ARF WITH carditis but NO residual heart disease:
  → 10 years OR until age 21 (whichever longer) ✓

ARF WITH carditis AND residual heart disease (RHD):
  → 10 years OR until age 40 (whichever longer)
  → Some recommend lifelong

Post-valve surgery:
  → LIFELONG
**Regimen:** - **Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks** **Goal:** Prevent recurrent ARF → Prevent chronic RHD

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Which valve lesion is MOST common in chronic rheumatic heart disease?

A) Mitral regurgitation B) Mitral stenosis C) Aortic stenosis D) Aortic regurgitation

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Mitral stenosis** **Explanation:** - **Mitral stenosis** is the **MOST COMMON** lesion in chronic RHD - **RHD is the MOST COMMON cause** of mitral stenosis (>99%) **Chronic RHD Valve Involvement:** - **Mitral valve:** 75-80% (Most common) - **Mitral stenosis** (most common lesion) - Mitral regurgitation - Combined MS + MR - **Aortic valve:** 30% (usually with mitral) - **Aortic regurgitation** > Aortic stenosis - **Tricuspid:** 10% (rarely alone) - **Pulmonary:** <5% (very rare) **Pathology:** - Commissural fusion - Leaflet thickening, fibrosis - Chordal fusion and shortening - **Fish-mouth orifice** **Note:** - **Acute ARF** causes **regurgitation** (MR, AR) - valvulitis, edema - **Chronic RHD** causes **stenosis** (MS, AS) - scarring, fibrosis

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient with suspected ARF has elevated ASO titer. Which of the following is TRUE about ASO?

A) Peaks at 1 week post-infection B) Always elevated in ARF C) Elevated after streptococcal skin infections D) More specific than Anti-DNase B

Click to reveal answer **Answer: None of the options are correct, but if forced to choose, D is "least wrong" though still incorrect** **Explanation:** Let me correct each option: **ASO (Anti-Streptolysin O) Facts:** - **Peaks:** 3-5 weeks post-infection (NOT 1 week) - A is wrong - **Negative in 20% of ARF** (NOT always elevated) - B is wrong - **NOT elevated after skin infections** (streptolysin O inactivated by skin lipids) - C is wrong - **LESS specific** than Anti-DNase B (NOT more specific) - D is wrong **Anti-DNase B:** - **More specific** than ASO - Elevated after BOTH pharyngitis AND skin infections - Remains elevated longer **Best Answer for Question:** - If this was an actual exam, the question might be flawed - **Anti-DNase B is more specific than ASO** - But none of the options correctly describe ASO **Key Points:** - ASO peaks 3-5 weeks - 20% of ARF have normal ASO - Only elevated after pharyngitis (not skin) - Less specific than Anti-DNase B

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A child with ARF has a pink, non-pruritic rash on trunk with serpiginous borders and central clearing. What is this rash called?

A) Osler nodes B) Janeway lesions C) Erythema marginatum D) Erythema nodosum

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Erythema marginatum** **Explanation:** - **Erythema marginatum** is a **major criterion** for ARF **Characteristics:** - **Pink/red rash** - **Serpiginous** (snake-like), **annular** (ring-shaped) - **Central clearing** - **Trunk and proximal limbs** (NOT face) - **Non-pruritic, non-painful** - **Blanches with pressure** - **Evanescent** (comes and goes) - **Heat-sensitive** (appears with warmth) **PATHOGNOMONIC for ARF** (if present) **Rare** in current era (<5% of ARF) **Differentials:** - **Osler nodes:** Painful nodules on fingertips (Infective endocarditis) - **Janeway lesions:** Painless hemorrhagic macules on palms/soles (IE) - **Erythema nodosum:** Tender nodules on shins (Sarcoidosis, TB, IBD)

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
What is the MOST appropriate regimen for secondary prophylaxis in a patient with history of ARF?

A) Oral Penicillin V 250 mg once daily B) Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks C) Amoxicillin 500 mg TID for 10 days every month D) Aspirin 325 mg daily

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM every 3-4 weeks** **Explanation:** - **Secondary prophylaxis** prevents recurrent ARF → prevents chronic RHD **Preferred Regimen:** - **Benzathine Penicillin G 1.2 million units IM** - **Frequency:** Every 4 weeks (standard) or Every 3 weeks (high-risk) - **IM route preferred** (better adherence than oral) **Oral Alternatives** (if IM not feasible): - **Penicillin V 250 mg PO BID** (NOT once daily) - Sulfadiazine 1 g PO daily **Penicillin Allergy:** - Azithromycin 250 mg PO daily - Erythromycin 250 mg PO BID **Why not others:** - Oral Penicillin V: BID, not once daily (poor adherence) - Amoxicillin intermittent: Not standard regimen - Aspirin: For acute arthritis, NOT prophylaxis **Duration:** Based on carditis and RHD status (see Q4)

End of Rheumatic Heart Disease Study Material

Next Topics: - Valvular Heart Disease - Cardiomyopathies - Pericardial Diseases

Cross-References: - Infective Endocarditis → Medicine/06_Infective_Endocarditis.md - Heart Failure → Medicine/03_Heart_Failure.md

Study Tips: 1. Master Jones Criteria (very high-yield - comes in every exam) 2. Know differences between manifestations (Polyarthritis vs Chorea vs Erythema) 3. Understand Aschoff bodies (pathognomonic) 4. Memorize secondary prophylaxis duration (commonly asked) 5. Remember chronic RHD valve lesions (MS most common) 6. Know ASO vs Anti-DNase B differences

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (High) Estimated Study Time: 3-4 hours Revision Time: 1-1.5 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Valvular Heart Disease - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Mitral Stenosis
  3. Mitral Regurgitation
  4. Aortic Stenosis
  5. Aortic Regurgitation
  6. Tricuspid & Pulmonary Valve Disease
  7. Prosthetic Valves
  8. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  9. Practice Questions

docs.title: Normal Valve Areas
docs.text:
Mitral valve:      4-6 cm²
Aortic valve:      3-4 cm²
Tricuspid valve:   7-9 cm²
Pulmonary valve:   5-6 cm²

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
• Stenosis: Narrowed orifice → Obstruction to forward flow
  • Regurgitation (Insufficiency): Incompetent valve → Backward flow
  • Mixed: Stenosis + Regurgitation

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Narrowing of mitral valve orifice → Obstruction to LV filling

Severity: - Mild: Valve area 1.5-2.5 cm² - Moderate: 1.0-1.5 cm² - Severe: <1 cm² - Critical: <0.6 cm²

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) - 99% of cases (Most common cause)

Other Rare Causes: - Congenital (Parachute mitral valve) - Calcific (elderly) - Carcinoid - SLE (Libman-Sacks endocarditis) - Rheumatoid arthritis

Key Point: Almost all MS is rheumatic in origin

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Rheumatic Changes: 1. Commissural fusion 2. Leaflet thickening and fibrosis 3. Chordal fusion and shortening 4. Calcification (chronic)

Result: "Fish-mouth" or "buttonhole" orifice

Hemodynamic Consequences:

Normal MVA: 4-6 cm²
        ↓
Stenotic MVA: <2 cm²
        ↓
↑ LA Pressure (to maintain CO)
        ↓
↑ Pulmonary Venous Pressure
        ↓
↑ Pulmonary Capillary Pressure (>25 mmHg)
        ↓
PULMONARY EDEMA (dyspnea)
        ↓
Chronic ↑ Pulmonary Pressure
        ↓
PULMONARY HYPERTENSION
        ↓
RV Failure → Tricuspid Regurgitation

Critical Gradient: - Normal: Minimal gradient across MV - MS: LA-LV gradient ≥10 mmHg → Symptoms

Factors ↑ Gradient (Worsen Symptoms): - ↑ Heart rate (↓ diastolic filling time) - Most important - ↑ Cardiac output (Exercise, pregnancy, fever) - Atrial fibrillation (loss of atrial kick, rapid rate)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms:

Early (Mild MS): - Asymptomatic for years (latent period: 10-20 years after ARF)

Progressive: - Dyspnea on exertion (DOE) - Most common symptom - Orthopnea - Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND) - Fatigue

Advanced (Severe MS): - Hemoptysis (3 types): - Pulmonary edema: Pink frothy sputum - Pulmonary venous rupture: Frank hemoptysis (rupture of bronchial veins) - Pulmonary infarction: From AF embolism - Hoarseness (Ortner's syndrome - LA compression of left recurrent laryngeal nerve) - Dysphagia (LA compression of esophagus) - Right heart failure: Edema, ascites, hepatomegaly

Complications: - Atrial fibrillation (40-80%) - LA dilation - Systemic embolism (10-20%) - LA thrombus (especially with AF) - Infective endocarditis (Rare in pure MS) - Pulmonary hypertension - RV failure

Signs:

General: - Mitral facies: Malar flush (pinkish-purple discoloration of cheeks) - Low-volume pulse (low CO)

Palpation: - Tapping apex beat (palpable S1) - Diastolic thrill at apex (if severe) - RV heave (if pulmonary HTN)

Auscultation - KEY FINDINGS:

Murmur: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      MITRAL STENOSIS MURMUR                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • LOW-PITCHED, RUMBLING, MID-DIASTOLIC             │
│ • Best heard at APEX                               │
│ • With BELL of stethoscope                         │
│ • In LEFT LATERAL DECUBITUS position               │
│ • Accentuated by EXERCISE (post-exercise auscult.) │
│ • Preceded by OPENING SNAP (OS)                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Additional Sounds: - Loud S1 (increased closing force - pliable leaflets) - Soft S1: If heavily calcified (rigid leaflets) - Opening Snap (OS): High-pitched sound in early diastole - Timing: S2 - OS interval SHORT → Severe MS - S2 - OS >80 ms: Mild - S2 - OS <60 ms: Severe - Graham Steell murmur: High-pitched, early diastolic (Pulmonary regurgitation from severe pulmonary HTN)

Mnemonic for MS Auscultation: "Loud S1, Opening Snap, Rumbling Diastolic Murmur"

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - P mitrale: Broad, notched P wave in lead II (>120 ms) - LA enlargement - AF (40-80% in severe MS) - RVH (if pulmonary HTN) - R/S ratio >1 in V1

Chest X-ray: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      CHEST X-RAY IN MITRAL STENOSIS                │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ PA View:                                           │
│ • LA enlargement (Straightening of left heart      │
│   border, Double density, Splaying of carina >90°) │
│ • Pulmonary venous congestion (Upper lobe diversion)│
│ • Kerley B lines (Interstitial edema)              │
│ • Pulmonary edema (Bat's wing)                     │
│ • Pulmonary artery enlargement                     │
│                                                    │
│ Lateral View:                                      │
│ • LA pushes esophagus backwards (Barium swallow)   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Echocardiography - GOLD STANDARD:

2D Echo: - Valve area (Planimetry) - Mean gradient (Doppler) - LA size - Thrombus in LA/LAA (TEE more sensitive) - Pulmonary artery pressure - RV function

Wilkins Score (For PBMV suitability): - Assesses: Leaflet mobility, Thickening, Calcification, Subvalvular thickening - Score 0-4 for each (Total: 0-16) - Score ≤8: Suitable for PBMV - Score >8: Consider surgery

Cardiac Catheterization: - Rarely needed (echo sufficient) - If echo non-diagnostic or discrepant - Measure: LA-LV gradient, Valve area, Pulmonary pressures

docs.title: Medical:
docs.text:
1. Diuretics: - Furosemide - Reduce pulmonary congestion - First-line for symptoms

2. Rate Control (if AF): - Beta-blockers (Metoprolol, Bisoprolol) - DOC - Calcium channel blockers (Diltiazem, Verapamil) - Digoxin (if HF present) - Goal: Resting HR 60-80 bpm (prolong diastolic filling time)

3. Anticoagulation: - Warfarin (INR 2-3) if: - AF (paroxysmal or permanent) - MANDATORY - LA thrombus - Prior embolism - LA size >55 mm (consider) - DOACs NOT recommended in MS (especially severe or AF with MS)

4. Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis: - NOT routinely recommended (per AHA 2007 guidelines)

5. Rheumatic Fever Prophylaxis: - If history of ARF

docs.title: Interventional/Surgical:
docs.text:
Indications for Intervention: - Symptomatic (NYHA class II-IV) with: - Moderate-severe MS (MVA <1.5 cm² and/or mean gradient >5 mmHg)

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR MITRAL STENOSIS                 │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│ 1. PBMV (Percutaneous Balloon Mitral Valvuloplasty)        │
│    • DOC for suitable anatomy                              │
│    • Wilkins score ≤8                                      │
│    • No/Minimal MR                                         │
│    • No LA thrombus (TEE to rule out)                      │
│    • Non-calcified valve                                   │
│    • Success: MVA increases to 1.5-2 cm²                   │
│    • Complications: MR (severe - 2-10%), Tamponade, Stroke │
│                                                            │
│ 2. Open Mitral Commissurotomy (OMC)                        │
│    • If unsuitable for PBMV                                │
│    • Wilkins score >8 but pliable valve                    │
│    • Young patient, no calcification                       │
│    • Best long-term results                                │
│                                                            │
│ 3. Mitral Valve Replacement (MVR)                          │
│    • Heavily calcified valve                               │
│    • Severe MR                                             │
│    • Failed PBMV/OMC                                       │
│    • Prosthetic valve: Mechanical or Bioprosthetic         │
│                                                            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Special Situations:

Pregnancy: - MS poorly tolerated (↑ CO, ↑ HR) - If symptomatic despite medical therapy → PBMV (safe in 2nd/3rd trimester)

Asymptomatic Severe MS: - Consider intervention if: - Pulmonary HTN (PA systolic >50 mmHg at rest or >60 mmHg with exercise) - New-onset AF - Planning pregnancy

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Incomplete closure of mitral valve → Backflow from LV to LA during systole

Severity (Regurgitant Fraction): - Mild: <30% - Moderate: 30-50% - Severe: >50%

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Acute MR: - Papillary muscle rupture (Post-MI) - Most common cause of acute MR - Chordae tendineae rupture (Trauma, IE, MVP) - Infective endocarditis (Leaflet perforation) - Acute rheumatic fever (Rare)

Chronic MR:

Primary (Organic) - Valve/Apparatus Abnormality: - Mitral valve prolapse (Most common in developed countries) - Rheumatic heart disease (Most common in developing countries) - Infective endocarditis - Degenerative (Myxomatous, Barlow's disease) - Ischemic (Papillary muscle dysfunction) - Congenital (Cleft mitral valve) - Connective tissue disorders (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos)

Secondary (Functional) - LV Dysfunction: - Dilated cardiomyopathy (LV dilation → Mitral annular dilation) - Ischemic cardiomyopathy

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Acute MR: 

Sudden MR
    ↓
↑ LA Pressure (normal-sized, non-compliant LA)
    ↓
↑ Pulmonary Venous/Capillary Pressure
    ↓
ACUTE PULMONARY EDEMA
    ↓
↓ Forward Cardiac Output
    ↓
CARDIOGENIC SHOCK
Medical Emergency - Requires urgent surgery

Chronic MR: 

Chronic MR
    ↓
↑ LA Volume (compensated - LA dilates)
    ↓
LV Volume Overload (↑ Preload)
    ↓
LV Eccentric Hypertrophy (LV dilation)
    ↓
Compensated Phase (Asymptomatic - years)
    ↓
LV Dysfunction (↓ EF)
    ↓
Decompensated HF, Pulmonary HTN, AF

Key Hemodynamics: - LV: Volume overload, ↑ Preload, ↓ Afterload (regurgitant pathway) - LA: Dilates (v wave prominent)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms:

Acute MR: - Sudden-onset severe dyspnea - Pulmonary edema - Cardiogenic shock

Chronic MR: - Asymptomatic for years (compensated phase) - Dyspnea (progressive) - Fatigue (low forward CO) - Palpitations (AF) - Orthopnea, PND (late)

Signs:

Palpation: - Hyperdynamic, displaced apex (LV dilation) - Systolic thrill at apex (severe MR)

Auscultation:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      MITRAL REGURGITATION MURMUR                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • HIGH-PITCHED, BLOWING, HOLOSYSTOLIC              │
│ • Best heard at APEX                               │
│ • Radiates to AXILLA (or left infrascapular area)  │
│ • Band-shaped (same intensity throughout systole)  │
│ • Soft S1 (incomplete coaptation)                  │
│ • Loud P2 (if pulmonary HTN)                       │
│ • S3 (LV volume overload)                          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Acute MR: May have soft murmur (acute LA pressure rise → small LA-LV gradient)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - P mitrale, AF (LA enlargement) - LVH (chronic MR)

Chest X-ray: - LA enlargement - LV enlargement (chronic) - Pulmonary edema (acute)

Echocardiography: - Severity: Color Doppler (regurgitant jet area, vena contracta) - Mechanism: Leaflet, chordae, annular abnormalities - LV size and function (EF, dimensions) - LA size

Cardiac MRI: - Quantify regurgitant volume/fraction

Cardiac Catheterization: - V wave in PCWP tracing (LA pressure)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Acute Severe MR: - Medical Emergency - ICU, intubation if pulmonary edema - Vasodilators: Nitroprusside (↓ Afterload → ↑ Forward flow) - Diuretics: Furosemide - Inotropes: Dobutamine (if cardiogenic shock) - IABP (Intraaortic balloon pump) - Bridge to surgery - Urgent surgical repair/replacement

Chronic MR:

Medical: - Asymptomatic, normal LV function: - Observation (serial echo every 6-12 months) - No role for prophylactic vasodilators (if normal BP, EF)

  • Symptomatic or LV dysfunction:
  • Diuretics (pulmonary congestion)
  • ACE inhibitors/ARBs (LV remodeling, afterload reduction)
  • Beta-blockers (if HF)
  • Anticoagulation (if AF)
  • Definitive: Surgery

Surgical:

Indications: 1. Symptomatic severe MR (NYHA ≥II) 2. Asymptomatic severe MR with: - LV dysfunction (EF ≤60% or LVESD ≥40 mm) - AF - Pulmonary HTN (PA systolic >50 mmHg)

Options: - Mitral valve repair (Preferred - better outcomes, no anticoagulation) - Annuloplasty, chordal repair, leaflet resection - Mitral valve replacement (If repair not feasible)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Narrowing of aortic valve orifice → Obstruction to LV outflow

Severity: - Mild: Valve area 1.5-2.0 cm², Mean gradient <25 mmHg - Moderate: 1.0-1.5 cm², 25-40 mmHg - Severe: <1.0 cm², >40 mmHg - Critical: <0.6 cm², >50 mmHg

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Age-Dependent:

<70 years: - Bicuspid aortic valve (Most common) - Congenital (1-2% population) - Premature degeneration/calcification

>70 years: - Senile degenerative calcific AS (Most common) - Age-related wear-and-tear, calcification

Any Age: - Rheumatic heart disease (Always with mitral involvement)

Rare: - Congenital (Unicuspid, Quadricuspid) - Familial hypercholesterolemia (early calcification)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Aortic Stenosis
    ↓
↑ LV Afterload (Pressure Overload)
    ↓
LV Concentric Hypertrophy (Thick walls, small cavity)
    ↓
Compensated Phase (Normal EF, Asymptomatic - years)
    ↓
↑ LV Wall Stress, ↑ O₂ Demand, ↓ Coronary Perfusion
    ↓
↓ Coronary Flow Reserve
    ↓
ANGINA (even without CAD)
    ↓
↓ Cardiac Output (Fixed)
    ↓
SYNCOPE (exertional - unable to ↑ CO)
    ↓
LV Systolic Dysfunction (Decompensation)
    ↓
HEART FAILURE (↓ EF)

Classic Triad (Late Findings): 1. Angina 2. Syncope (exertional) 3. Dyspnea (Heart failure)

Prognosis after Symptoms: - Angina: 50% mortality at 5 years - Syncope: 50% mortality at 3 years - Heart Failure: 50% mortality at 2 years - Sudden death: 5-15% (usually after symptoms)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Asymptomatic for decades (long latent period - 30-50 years) - Classic Triad: 1. Angina (exertional) - ↓ Coronary reserve 2. Syncope (exertional) - Fixed CO, vasodilation in muscles 3. Dyspnea (exertional → rest) - Heart failure

Signs:

Peripheral: - Pulsus parvus et tardus: Small, slow-rising pulse (delayed peak) - Narrow pulse pressure - Sustained, non-displaced apex (LVH)

Auscultation:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      AORTIC STENOSIS MURMUR                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • HARSH, CRESCENDO-DECRESCENDO (Ejection) SYSTOLIC │
│ • Best heard at RIGHT UPPER STERNAL BORDER (RUSB)  │
│   (2nd right intercostal space)                    │
│ • Radiates to CAROTIDS                             │
│ • Increases with leg raise, decreases with Valsalva│
│ • Soft/Absent A2 (immobile valve)                  │
│ • S4 (LVH with diastolic dysfunction)              │
│ • Paradoxical splitting of S2 (severe AS)          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Gallavardin phenomenon: High-frequency AS murmur radiates to apex (mimics MR)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - LVH with strain (ST depression, T inversion in I, aVL, V5-V6) - LAE (LV diastolic dysfunction) - LBBB (late)

Chest X-ray: - Normal cardiac silhouette (compensated) - LV enlargement (decompensated) - Aortic valve calcification - Post-stenotic dilation of ascending aorta

Echocardiography - GOLD STANDARD: - Valve area (Continuity equation) - Mean gradient - Peak velocity - LV wall thickness, EF - Valve morphology (Bicuspid, calcification)

Cardiac Catheterization: - Coronary angiography: Before surgery (if age >40 men, >50 women, or risk factors) - Measure gradient (if echo non-diagnostic)

Exercise Testing: - CONTRAINDICATED in symptomatic severe AS - Consider in asymptomatic severe AS (supervised, to assess symptoms)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Medical: - No medical therapy prevents progression or delays need for surgery - Avoid: Vasodilators (may cause hypotension - fixed CO) - Symptomatic HF: - Diuretics (cautious - need high filling pressures) - ACE inhibitors (low dose, cautious)

Surgical - DEFINITIVE:

Indications: 1. Symptomatic severe AS (Any symptom - angina, syncope, dyspnea) 2. Asymptomatic severe AS with: - EF <50% - Undergoing other cardiac surgery (CABG, other valve) - Abnormal exercise test (symptoms, hypotension) - Very severe AS (Vmax >5 m/s, mean gradient >60 mmHg)

Options: - Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement (SAVR): - Mechanical valve (Younger, no contraindication to anticoagulation) - Bioprosthetic valve (Elderly >65-70, contraindication to anticoagulation)

  • Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR):
  • Indications: High surgical risk, elderly (>75-80), severe comorbidities
  • Via femoral artery (or transapical)

Balloon Aortic Valvuloplasty (BAV): - Palliative only (high restenosis rate) - Bridge to SAVR/TAVR - Contraindication to surgery

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Incompetent aortic valve → Backflow from aorta to LV during diastole

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Acute AR: - Infective endocarditis (Leaflet perforation) - Most common - Aortic dissection (Type A) - Trauma

Chronic AR:

Valve Abnormality: - Bicuspid aortic valve - Rheumatic heart disease (Always with other valve involvement) - Infective endocarditis (Healed) - Degenerative (Senile) - Myxomatous (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos) - Rheumatoid arthritis, SLE

Aortic Root Abnormality: - Hypertension (Most common overall) - Marfan syndrome (Aortic root dilation) - Ankylosing spondylitis (Aortitis) - Takayasu arteritis, Giant cell arteritis - Syphilitic aortitis (Historical) - Aortic dissection (Chronic)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Acute AR: 

Sudden AR
    ↓
LV Volume Overload (normal-sized, non-compliant LV)
    ↓
↑ LVEDP
    ↓
↓ Coronary Perfusion (diastolic pressure low)
    ↓
ACUTE PULMONARY EDEMA + CARDIOGENIC SHOCK

Chronic AR: 

Chronic AR
    ↓
LV Volume Overload (Both ↑ Preload and ↑ Afterload)
    ↓
LV Eccentric Hypertrophy (Largest heart in cardiology)
    ↓
Compensated Phase (High stroke volume - wide pulse pressure)
    ↓
LV Dysfunction
    ↓
HEART FAILURE

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms:

Acute AR: - Sudden dyspnea, pulmonary edema - Cardiogenic shock

Chronic AR: - Asymptomatic for years (decades) - Exertional dyspnea - Orthopnea, PND - Angina (↓ Diastolic BP → ↓ Coronary perfusion) - Palpitations (Large stroke volume)

Signs:

Wide Pulse Pressure (High SBP, Low DBP): - SBP >160, DBP <50 mmHg (severe AR)

Eponymous Signs (Peripheral Signs of Chronic AR):

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      PERIPHERAL SIGNS OF AORTIC REGURGITATION          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Corrigan's pulse: Water-hammer pulse (bounding)      │
│ • de Musset's sign: Head bobbing with heartbeat        │
│ • Quincke's sign: Capillary pulsations (nail bed)      │
│ • Traube's sign: "Pistol shot" sounds over femoral    │
│ • Duroziez's sign: To-and-fro murmur over femoral     │
│                    (with compression)                   │
│ • Hill's sign: Popliteal SBP >20 mmHg higher than      │
│                brachial (severe AR)                     │
│ • Müller's sign: Uvula pulsations                      │
│ • Rosenbach's sign: Liver pulsations                   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Auscultation:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      AORTIC REGURGITATION MURMUR                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • HIGH-PITCHED, BLOWING, EARLY DIASTOLIC           │
│ • Best heard at LEFT STERNAL BORDER (3rd-4th ICS)  │
│ • Patient sitting, leaning forward, in expiration  │
│ • Radiates to apex                                 │
│ • Longer murmur → More severe AR                   │
│ • Austin Flint murmur: Mid-diastolic rumble at apex│
│   (Regurgitant jet hits anterior mitral leaflet)   │
│ • S3 (LV volume overload)                          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Acute AR: - Soft murmur (Early closure of mitral valve due to ↑ LVEDP) - Soft S1

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - LVH with strain (chronic AR)

Chest X-ray: - LV enlargement ("Boot-shaped" heart) - Aortic root dilation

Echocardiography: - Severity: Color Doppler (jet width, vena contracta) - LV size and function - Aortic root size - Early mitral valve closure (acute AR)

Cardiac MRI: - Quantify regurgitant volume

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Acute Severe AR: - Medical Emergency - Vasodilators: Nitroprusside (↓ Afterload → ↓ Regurgitant volume) - Inotropes: Dobutamine - AVOID β-blockers (prolong diastole → ↑ Regurgitation) - Urgent surgical replacement

Chronic AR:

Medical: - Asymptomatic, normal LV function: - Vasodilators (Nifedipine, ACE inhibitors) - May delay progression - Serial echo every 6-12 months

  • Symptomatic or LV dysfunction:
  • Diuretics, ACE inhibitors (HF)
  • Definitive: Surgery

Surgical:

Indications: 1. Symptomatic severe AR 2. Asymptomatic severe AR with: - EF <50% - LVESD >50 mm or LVEDD >70 mm - Undergoing other cardiac surgery

Options: - Aortic valve replacement (Mechanical or Bioprosthetic) - Valve repair (If feasible - bicuspid, prolapse) - Aortic root replacement (If root dilation >50-55 mm)

docs.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation (TR)
docs.text:
Causes: - Functional (Secondary to RV dilation) - Most common - Pulmonary HTN from left heart disease (MS, LV failure) - Organic: - Rheumatic (with mitral/aortic) - Infective endocarditis (IVDU) - Carcinoid, Ebstein's anomaly

Clinical Features: - Pulsatile liver (systolic pulsations in JVP - cv wave) - Prominent v wave in JVP - Holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border (increases with inspiration - Carvallo's sign) - S3 (RV) - Peripheral edema, ascites

Management: - Treat underlying cause (Pulmonary HTN, RV failure) - Diuretics - Surgery: If severe and undergoing left-sided valve surgery

docs.title: Tricuspid Stenosis (TS)
docs.text:
Causes: - Rheumatic (Always with mitral stenosis)

Clinical Features: - Giant a wave in JVP - Mid-diastolic murmur at left lower sternal border (increases with inspiration) - Hepatomegaly, ascites (worse than MS alone)

Management: - Balloon valvuloplasty or Surgical repair/replacement

docs.title: Pulmonary Stenosis (PS)
docs.text:
Causes: - Congenital (Most common) - Carcinoid, Rheumatic (rare)

Clinical Features: - Ejection systolic murmur at left upper sternal border - Wide splitting of S2 (delayed P2) - RVH

Management: - Balloon valvuloplasty (DOC)

docs.title: Pulmonary Regurgitation (PR)
docs.text:
Causes: - Idiopathic dilation of pulmonary artery - Pulmonary HTN (Graham Steell murmur) - Post-valvuloplasty, Tetralogy of Fallot repair

Clinical Features: - Early diastolic murmur at left upper sternal border (increases with inspiration)

Management: - Usually well tolerated - Surgery if severe with RV dysfunction

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
Mechanical Valves: - Bileaflet (St. Jude, Carbomedics) - Most common - Tilting disc (Bjork-Shiley) - Ball-and-cage (Starr-Edwards) - Obsolete

Advantages: - Durable (Lasts 20-30 years)

Disadvantages: - Requires lifelong anticoagulation (Warfarin, INR 2.5-3.5) - Thromboembolism risk - Prosthetic valve endocarditis - Hemolysis

Bioprosthetic Valves: - Porcine (pig valve) - Bovine pericardial

Advantages: - No anticoagulation (or only 3-6 months)

Disadvantages: - Limited durability (10-15 years) - Structural deterioration (calcification)

docs.title: Indications for Mechanical vs Bioprosthetic
docs.text:
Mechanical: - Age <60-65 years (aortic), <65-70 (mitral) - Already on anticoagulation (AF, prior VTE) - No contraindication to anticoagulation

Bioprosthetic: - Age >65-70 years - Contraindication to anticoagulation - Desire to avoid anticoagulation (Women of childbearing age - Warfarin teratogenic) - Limited life expectancy

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
1. Thromboembolism: - Mechanical > Bioprosthetic - Mitral > Aortic - Prevention: Warfarin (INR 2.5-3.5 for mechanical)

2. Prosthetic Valve Endocarditis: - Early (<12 months): S. epidermidis (Coagulase-negative Staph) - Late (>12 months): Similar to native valve (S. viridans, S. aureus)

3. Paravalvular Leak: - Regurgitation around valve (not through) - Causes: Infection, suture dehiscence - → Hemolysis, HF

4. Structural Deterioration: - Bioprosthetic (calcification, tear) - Usually after 10-15 years

5. Hemolysis: - Mechanical valves - Usually mild (↑ LDH, ↓ Haptoglobin)

6. Pannus Formation: - Fibrous tissue overgrowth → Stenosis

docs.title: Anticoagulation
docs.text:
Mechanical Valves: - Warfarin (lifelong) - INR target: - 2.5-3.5: Mechanical mitral, Older generation valves - 2.0-3.0: Mechanical aortic (bileaflet, no other risk factors)

Bioprosthetic Valves: - Warfarin for 3-6 months (until endothelialization) - Then aspirin (or stop if no other indication)

Pregnancy: - Warfarin teratogenic (1st trimester) - Options: - Switch to UFH or LMWH in 1st trimester - Warfarin in 2nd/3rd trimester (safer) - or UFH/LMWH throughout (less effective - ↑ valve thrombosis risk)

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Mitral Stenosis:
  2. 99% Rheumatic in origin
  3. Most common RHD lesion
  4. Murmur: Low-pitched, rumbling, mid-diastolic at apex
  5. Loud S1 + Opening Snap
  6. S2-OS interval SHORT → Severe MS

  7. Treatment: PBMV (DOC if Wilkins ≤8), OMC, MVR

  8. Mitral Regurgitation:

  9. Acute: Papillary muscle rupture (post-MI) - Emergency
  10. Chronic: MVP (developed), RHD (developing)
  11. Murmur: High-pitched, blowing, holosystolic at apex → Axilla

  12. Treatment: Repair > Replacement

  13. Aortic Stenosis:

  14. <70y: Bicuspid AV, >70y: Senile calcific
  15. Classic Triad: Angina, Syncope, Dyspnea
  16. Prognosis: Angina (5y), Syncope (3y), HF (2y) - 50% mortality
  17. Murmur: Harsh, crescendo-decrescendo systolic at RUSB → Carotids
  18. Pulse: Parvus et tardus (small, slow-rising)

  19. Treatment: SAVR or TAVR (if symptomatic or EF <50%)

  20. Aortic Regurgitation:

  21. Acute: IE, Aortic dissection - Emergency
  22. Chronic: HTN (most common), Bicuspid AV, Marfan
  23. Murmur: High-pitched, blowing, early diastolic at left sternal border
  24. Austin Flint murmur: Mid-diastolic rumble at apex
  25. Wide pulse pressure (High SBP, Low DBP)
  26. Peripheral signs: Corrigan's, de Musset's, Quincke's, etc.

  27. Treatment: Vasodilators (chronic), Surgery if symptomatic/EF <50%/LVESD >50mm

  28. Prosthetic Valves:

  29. Mechanical: Lifelong anticoagulation (Warfarin INR 2.5-3.5)
  30. Bioprosthetic: No anticoagulation (or 3-6 months)
  31. Age <65: Mechanical, >65: Bioprosthetic

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 40-year-old female with dyspnea on exertion has a low-pitched, rumbling, mid-diastolic murmur at the apex with a loud S1 and an opening snap. Chest X-ray shows LA enlargement and Kerley B lines. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A) Mitral regurgitation B) Mitral stenosis C) Aortic stenosis D) Tricuspid stenosis

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Mitral stenosis** **Explanation:** - **Classic MS findings:** - **Low-pitched, rumbling, mid-diastolic murmur at apex** (Hallmark) - **Loud S1** (increased closing force - pliable leaflets) - **Opening snap** (High-pitched sound in early diastole) - **LA enlargement** (on CXR) - **Kerley B lines** (Interstitial edema from pulmonary venous congestion) - **99% of MS is Rheumatic** in origin **Other findings:** - Orthopnea, PND, hemoptysis - AF, systemic embolism - Right heart failure (late)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 60-year-old male presents with exertional syncope and chest pain. On examination, pulse is small and slow-rising. A harsh, crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur is heard at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids. What is the most appropriate management if he is symptomatic with severe stenosis?

A) Medical therapy with vasodilators B) Percutaneous balloon valvuloplasty C) Aortic valve replacement D) Observation with serial echocardiography

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Aortic valve replacement** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis - **Classic triad:** Angina + Syncope + Dyspnea - **Pulse:** Parvus et tardus (small, slow-rising) - **Murmur:** Harsh, systolic at RUSB → Carotids - **Symptomatic severe AS** is an **absolute indication for surgery** - **Aortic valve replacement** (SAVR or TAVR) **Prognosis without surgery:** - **50% mortality** at 2-5 years after symptoms develop **Why not others:** - **Vasodilators:** Contraindicated (may cause hypotension) - **Balloon valvuloplasty:** High restenosis, only palliative - **Observation:** WRONG - symptomatic severe AS requires surgery

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with chronic aortic regurgitation has a high-pitched, blowing, early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border and a mid-diastolic rumble at the apex. What is the apical murmur called?

A) Carey Coombs murmur B) Graham Steell murmur C) Austin Flint murmur D) Still's murmur

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Austin Flint murmur** **Explanation:** - **Austin Flint murmur:** Mid-diastolic rumble at apex in AR - **Mechanism:** Regurgitant jet from AR hits anterior mitral leaflet → Functional MS **Differentials:** - **Carey Coombs murmur:** Mid-diastolic rumble in acute rheumatic fever (mitral valvulitis) - **Graham Steell murmur:** Early diastolic murmur at LUSB (Pulmonary regurgitation from severe pulmonary HTN) - **Still's murmur:** Innocent systolic murmur in children **AR findings:** - Early diastolic murmur at left sternal border - Wide pulse pressure - Peripheral signs (Corrigan's, Quincke's, etc.) - Austin Flint murmur (if severe)

End of Valvular Heart Disease Study Material

Next Topics: - Cardiomyopathies - Pericardial Diseases - (Then Respiratory topics)

Study Tips: 1. Master murmur characteristics (Timing, location, radiation) 2. Know severity criteria for each lesion 3. Understand surgical indications (very high-yield) 4. Practice differentiating similar murmurs 5. Remember eponymous signs for AR 6. Know prosthetic valve anticoagulation

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 5-6 hours Revision Time: 2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Cardiomyopathies - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview & Classification
  2. Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
  3. Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
  4. Restrictive Cardiomyopathy (RCM)
  5. Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC)
  6. Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy
  7. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  8. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Cardiomyopathies are diseases of the myocardium associated with mechanical and/or electrical dysfunction, usually with inappropriate ventricular hypertrophy or dilation, and often leading to cardiovascular death or heart failure.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Primary Cardiomyopathies: - Genetic: - Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) - Arrhythmogenic RV Cardiomyopathy (ARVC) - Left Ventricular Non-compaction - Mitochondrial myopathies - Acquired: - Takotsubo (Stress) cardiomyopathy - Peripartum cardiomyopathy - Myocarditis

Secondary Cardiomyopathies: - Ischemic (CAD) - Most common cause of DCM - Hypertensive - Valvular - Toxic (Alcohol, Chemotherapy - Doxorubicin) - Infiltrative (Amyloidosis, Sarcoidosis, Hemochromatosis) - Endocrine (Diabetes, Thyroid, Acromegaly) - Nutritional (Thiamine deficiency - Beriberi, Selenium)

docs.title: Functional Classification
docs.text:
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      FUNCTIONAL TYPES OF CARDIOMYOPATHY                   │
├──────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. DILATED (DCM) │ • Dilated ventricles (LV > RV)         │
│                  │ • ↓ Systolic function (↓ EF <40%)      │
│                  │ • Thin walls                           │
│                  │ • Heart failure (HFrEF)                │
├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 2. HYPERTROPHIC  │ • Thickened LV walls (>15 mm)          │
│    (HCM)         │ • Small LV cavity                      │
│                  │ • ↓ Diastolic function (HFpEF)         │
│                  │ • +/- LV outflow obstruction           │
├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 3. RESTRICTIVE   │ • Normal/Mildly thickened walls        │
│    (RCM)         │ • ↓ Diastolic compliance (stiff)       │
│                  │ • Normal/Near-normal EF                │
│                  │ • Dilated atria                        │
├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 4. ARVC          │ • RV involvement (fibrofatty replace)  │
│                  │ • Arrhythmias (VT)                     │
│                  │ • Sudden cardiac death                 │
└──────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: LV/biventricular dilation and systolic dysfunction (EF <40%) without CAD, HTN, valvular, or congenital disease.

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
"ABCDEFGHI" 

A - Alcohol (Most common reversible cause)
B - Beriberi (Thiamine deficiency)
C - Chagas disease, Cocaine, Chemotherapy (Doxorubicin)
D - Diabetes
E - Endocrine (Thyroid, Acromegaly, Pheochromocytoma)
F - Familial/Genetic (20-35%)
G - Giant cell myocarditis
H - Hemochromatosis
I - Idiopathic (25-30%) - Most common
   Ischemic (Post-MI) - Most common secondary cause
   Inflammatory (Myocarditis - Viral, Autoimmune)

Genetic DCM: - 20-35% of cases - Autosomal dominant (most common) - Genes: TTN (Titin) - most common, LMNA, MYH7

Peripartum Cardiomyopathy: - Last month of pregnancy or first 5 months postpartum - Risk factors: Multiparity, Advanced age, African descent - May recover (50%)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Myocardial Damage
        ↓
↓ Contractility
        ↓
↓ Stroke Volume, ↓ Ejection Fraction
        ↓
↑ End-Systolic Volume (incomplete emptying)
        ↓
LV Dilation (Eccentric hypertrophy)
        ↓
Mitral Annular Dilation → Functional MR
        ↓
↑ Wall Stress (Laplace: Stress = Pressure × Radius)
        ↓
Further ↓ Contractility, Neurohormonal activation
        ↓
HEART FAILURE (HFrEF)

Complications: - Heart failure (systolic - HFrEF) - Arrhythmias (AF, VT) - Thromboembolism (LV thrombus) - Sudden cardiac death

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Dyspnea (exertional → rest) - Orthopnea, PND - Fatigue, weakness - Peripheral edema - Chest pain (rare - unlike ischemic)

Signs: - Displaced apex (laterally, downward) - S3 gallop (volume overload) - Mitral regurgitation murmur (functional) - Elevated JVP - Peripheral edema, ascites - Low pulse pressure (low CO)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - LVH, LBBB - AF, PVCs, NSVT - Q waves (if ischemic cause)

Chest X-ray: - Cardiomegaly (Cardiothoracic ratio >50%) - Pulmonary edema - Pleural effusions

Echocardiography - DIAGNOSTIC: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECHO FINDINGS IN DCM                          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Dilated LV (LVEDD >55-60 mm)                     │
│ • ↓ Ejection Fraction (<40%)                       │
│ • Global hypokinesis                               │
│ • Thin walls (unlike HCM)                          │
│ • Dilated atria                                    │
│ • Functional MR (mitral annular dilation)          │
│ • LV thrombus (if present)                         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Cardiac MRI: - Best for assessment of myocardium - Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE): - Mid-wall pattern: Non-ischemic DCM - Subendocardial pattern: Ischemic cardiomyopathy - Quantify EF, volumes

Coronary Angiography: - Rule out ischemic cause (most important) - Recommended in all patients with new DCM (age >40 or risk factors)

Lab Tests: - BNP/NT-proBNP: Elevated (heart failure severity) - Thyroid function: Rule out thyroid disease - Ferritin, Transferrin saturation: Hemochromatosis - Autoantibodies: Autoimmune myocarditis (Anti-Ro, Anti-La) - Genetic testing: If family history

Endomyocardial Biopsy: - Rarely needed - If suspected: Giant cell myocarditis, Sarcoidosis, Amyloidosis

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Treat Underlying Cause: - Alcohol: Abstinence - Thyroid disease: Correct hypo/hyperthyroidism - Hemochromatosis: Phlebotomy, Chelation - Myocarditis: Immunosuppression (if giant cell or autoimmune)

Heart Failure Management (Standard HFrEF Therapy):

"ABCD" 

A - ACE inhibitor/ARB (or ARNI - Sacubitril/Valsartan)
    • ↓ Mortality, ↓ Hospitalization
    • Titrate to target doses

B - Beta-blocker (Carvedilol, Bisoprolol, Metoprolol succinate)
    • ↓ Mortality
    • Start low, titrate slowly

C - "C"oagulation (Anticoagulation if AF, LV thrombus)

D - Diuretics (Furosemide - for volume overload)
    • Does NOT reduce mortality

Additional:
- Aldosterone antagonist (Spironolactone, Eplerenone) if EF ≤35%
- SGLT2 inhibitor (Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin) - New standard
- Ivabradine (if HR >70 on max β-blocker)
- Hydralazine + Isosorbide dinitrate (African Americans or if ACEI/ARB intolerant)

Device Therapy:

ICD (Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator): - Primary prevention: EF ≤35% despite 3 months optimal medical therapy - Secondary prevention: Survived cardiac arrest, Sustained VT

CRT (Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy - Biventricular Pacing): - Indications: - EF ≤35% - LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms - NYHA class II-IV despite optimal therapy - Improves symptoms, ↓ Mortality

Advanced Therapies: - LV Assist Device (LVAD): Bridge to transplant or destination therapy - Heart Transplant: End-stage refractory HF

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Inappropriate LV hypertrophy (wall thickness ≥15 mm) in the absence of loading conditions (HTN, AS) that would account for it.

Most common genetic cardiovascular disease (1 in 500)

docs.title: Genetics
docs.text:
• Autosomal dominant (most cases)
  • Mutations in sarcomeric protein genes:
  • β-Myosin heavy chain (MYH7) - Most common
  • Myosin binding protein C (MYBPC3)
  • Troponin T, I
  • α-Tropomyosin

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Asymmetric Septal Hypertrophy (Most common - 70%): - Septal thickness >> posterior wall - ± LV outflow tract obstruction (LVOTO)

Mechanism of LVOTO: 

Hypertrophied septum
        +
Systolic Anterior Motion (SAM) of mitral valve
        ↓
Narrowing of LVOT
        ↓
Dynamic Obstruction (worsens during systole)
        ↓
↑ Gradient across LVOT (>30 mmHg)

Factors ↑ LVOTO (Worsening Gradient): - ↓ Preload: Dehydration, Valsalva, Standing - ↓ Afterload: Vasodilators - ↑ Contractility: Exercise, Inotropes

Factors ↓ LVOTO (Improving Gradient): - ↑ Preload: Fluids, Leg raise - ↑ Afterload: Squatting, Handgrip - ↓ Contractility: β-blockers

Diastolic Dysfunction: - Stiff, non-compliant LV (thick walls) - Impaired relaxation - ↑ Filling pressures → Dyspnea

Myocardial Ischemia: - Imbalance: ↑ O₂ demand (massive hypertrophy), ↓ O₂ supply (small vessel disease, compression during systole) - → Angina (even without CAD)

Arrhythmias: - AF (common - 20-25%) - VT/VF → Sudden Cardiac Death (SCD)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Often Asymptomatic (discovered incidentally or family screening)

Symptoms: - Dyspnea (exertional) - Most common symptom - Due to diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF) - Angina (exertional) - ↓ Coronary flow reserve - Syncope/Presyncope (exertional) - LVOTO worsens with exercise - Arrhythmias - Palpitations (AF, VT) - Sudden cardiac death (Often 1st presentation - young athletes)

Signs:

Palpation: - Double/Triple apical impulse (Forceful atrial contraction) - Systolic thrill

Auscultation:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      HCM WITH LVOTO MURMUR                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • HARSH, CRESCENDO-DECRESCENDO SYSTOLIC            │
│ • Best heard at LEFT STERNAL BORDER (LLSB)         │
│ • DOES NOT radiate to carotids (unlike AS)         │
│ • ↑ With Valsalva, Standing (↓ preload)            │
│ • ↓ With Squatting, Handgrip (↑ preload/afterload)│
│ • S4 (atrial kick against stiff LV)                │
│ • Mitral regurgitation (from SAM)                  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Differentiating Feature: - HCM murmur ↑ with Valsalva (LVOTO worsens) - AS murmur ↓ with Valsalva (↓ Stroke volume)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECG IN HCM                                    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • LVH with strain (Most common)                    │
│ • DEEP Q waves (Septal depolarization)             │
│   - Inferolateral leads (II, III, aVF, V5-V6)      │
│   - Pseudo-infarction pattern                      │
│ • Giant negative T waves (Apical HCM - Yamaguchi)  │
│ • Left atrial enlargement                          │
│ • AF, PVCs                                         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Chest X-ray: - Often normal (LV not dilated) - May show LA enlargement

Echocardiography - DIAGNOSTIC:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECHO FINDINGS IN HCM                          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • LV wall thickness ≥15 mm (any segment)           │
│ • Asymmetric septal hypertrophy (Septum/PW >1.3)   │
│ • Small LV cavity                                  │
│ • Normal or ↑ EF (hyperdynamic)                    │
│ • Systolic Anterior Motion (SAM) of mitral valve   │
│ • LVOT gradient (Doppler):                         │
│   - <30 mmHg: Non-obstructive                      │
│   - ≥30 mmHg: Obstructive                          │
│ • Mitral regurgitation (from SAM)                  │
│ • Diastolic dysfunction                            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Provocation: - Valsalva or Amyl Nitrite → ↑ Gradient (if dynamic obstruction)

Cardiac MRI: - Gold standard for wall thickness measurement - Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE): Myocardial fibrosis (↑ SCD risk)

Genetic Testing: - Recommended in all HCM patients - Cascade screening of family members if mutation identified

Holter Monitoring: - Detect NSVT (↑ SCD risk)

Exercise Testing: - Assess symptoms, BP response - Abnormal BP response (Failure to rise or drop) → ↑ SCD risk

docs.title: Risk Stratification for Sudden Cardiac Death
docs.text:
Major Risk Factors: 1. Cardiac arrest or Sustained VT (previous) 2. Family history of SCD (1st degree relative, <40 years) 3. Unexplained syncope (Recent, especially if exertional) 4. LV wall thickness ≥30 mm 5. NSVT on Holter (Non-sustained VT) 6. Abnormal BP response to exercise (Failure to rise ≥20 mmHg or drop)

≥1 Major Risk Factor → Consider ICD

HCM Risk-SCD Score: - Calculator incorporating multiple factors - Estimates 5-year SCD risk - ≥6% risk → ICD recommended

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Goals: 1. Relieve symptoms (dyspnea, angina) 2. Prevent sudden cardiac death

General Measures: - Avoid dehydration - Avoid competitive sports (Sudden death risk) - Family screening (Echo, ECG, Genetic testing)

Medical Therapy:

For Symptoms (Dyspnea, Angina):

β-Blockers (First-line): - Metoprolol, Atenolol, Propranolol - ↓ HR, ↓ Contractility → ↓ LVOTO, ↑ Diastolic filling time - Improve dyspnea, angina

Calcium Channel Blockers (Verapamil, Diltiazem): - Alternative if β-blockers contraindicated - Improve diastolic filling - Caution: May cause vasodilation → ↑ LVOTO (Use if non-obstructive)

Disopyramide: - Negative inotrope - ↓ LVOTO - Used as add-on if symptoms persist

AVOID: - Vasodilators (↓ Afterload → ↑ LVOTO) - Diuretics (↓ Preload → ↑ LVOTO) - Use cautiously if needed - Digoxin (↑ Contractility → ↑ LVOTO) - Nitrates (↓ Preload → ↑ LVOTO)

For Atrial Fibrillation: - Rate control: β-blockers, Verapamil - Anticoagulation: MANDATORY (High stroke risk)

Interventional Therapy (For Refractory Symptoms with LVOTO ≥50 mmHg):

1. Septal Myectomy (Surgical): - Gold standard - Resect hypertrophied septum - Excellent long-term results

2. Alcohol Septal Ablation: - Percutaneous - Inject alcohol into septal perforator artery → Controlled MI → Septal thinning - Alternative if high surgical risk

ICD (For SCD Prevention): - Indications: - Secondary prevention: Survived cardiac arrest, Sustained VT - Primary prevention: ≥1 Major risk factor OR HCM Risk-SCD ≥6%

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Impaired ventricular filling due to ↓ compliance (stiff myocardium) with normal/near-normal systolic function and normal wall thickness.

Least common cardiomyopathy

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Infiltrative (Most Common): - Amyloidosis (Most common cause of RCM) - AL (Light chain) - Primary - AA (Serum A) - Secondary to chronic inflammation - ATTR (Transthyretin) - Senile, Familial - Sarcoidosis - Hemochromatosis (Iron deposition) - Glycogen storage diseases (Fabry, Gaucher)

Non-Infiltrative: - Idiopathic - Scleroderma - Post-radiation - Diabetic cardiomyopathy

Endomyocardial: - Endomyocardial fibrosis (Tropical - Africa, India) - Hypereosinophilic syndrome (Loeffler endocarditis) - Carcinoid heart disease

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Infiltration / Fibrosis
        ↓
↓ Ventricular Compliance (Stiff myocardium)
        ↓
↓ Diastolic Filling
        ↓
↑ Filling Pressures (LA, RA)
        ↓
Atrial Dilation
        ↓
Pulmonary/Systemic Venous Congestion
        ↓
HEART FAILURE (HFpEF)

Hemodynamics: - "Dip and Plateau" (Square root sign) on ventricular pressure tracing - Early rapid filling, then abrupt cessation

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Dyspnea (exertional) - Fatigue - Right heart failure (Peripheral edema, ascites, hepatomegaly) - Often more prominent than left heart failure

Signs: - Elevated JVP with prominent x and y descents - Kussmaul's sign: ↑ JVP with inspiration (impaired RV filling) - S3, S4 gallops - Hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral edema

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - Low voltage (infiltration - Amyloidosis) - Pseudo-infarction pattern (Q waves) - AF, AV block

Chest X-ray: - Normal/Mildly enlarged heart - Dilated atria - Pulmonary congestion

Echocardiography:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECHO IN RCM                                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Normal/Mildly thickened LV walls                 │
│ • Normal/Near-normal EF                            │
│ • Dilated atria (LA >> LV)                         │
│ • Restrictive filling pattern (Doppler)            │
│   - ↑ E/A ratio (>2)                               │
│   - Short deceleration time (<150 ms)              │
│ • Granular sparkling myocardium (Amyloidosis)      │
│ • Thickened valves, IVS (Amyloidosis)              │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Cardiac MRI: - Late gadolinium enhancement: Diffuse subendocardial (Amyloidosis)

Cardiac Catheterization: - Dip-and-plateau (Square root sign) - Equalization of pressures: RA ≈ RV diastolic ≈ LA ≈ LV diastolic

Endomyocardial Biopsy: - Definitive diagnosis for infiltrative causes - Amyloid: Congo red stain (Apple-green birefringence under polarized light) - Sarcoidosis: Non-caseating granulomas - Hemochromatosis: Prussian blue stain (iron)

Specific Tests: - Amyloidosis: - Serum/Urine protein electrophoresis (SPEP/UPEP) - Serum free light chains - Bone marrow biopsy (AL amyloid) - Technetium-99m pyrophosphate scan (ATTR amyloid) - Hemochromatosis: ↑ Ferritin, ↑ Transferrin saturation, Genetic testing (HFE gene) - Sarcoidosis: ↑ ACE, Tissue biopsy

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Treat Underlying Cause: - AL Amyloidosis: Chemotherapy (Melphalan + Dexamethasone), Stem cell transplant - ATTR Amyloidosis: Tafamidis (TTR stabilizer) - Hemochromatosis: Phlebotomy, Iron chelation (Deferasirox) - Sarcoidosis: Corticosteroids

Heart Failure Management: - Diuretics: Cautious (need high filling pressures) - Digoxin: Contraindicated in amyloidosis (binds to amyloid fibrils → toxicity) - β-blockers, ACE inhibitors: Often poorly tolerated (↓ Preload → ↓ CO)

Anticoagulation: - If AF (high thromboembolic risk - especially amyloidosis)

Advanced Therapy: - Heart transplant (for selected patients)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Fibrofatty replacement of RV myocardium → RV dysfunction + Ventricular arrhythmias

Genetic disorder (Autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance)

docs.title: Genetics
docs.text:
• Desmosomal protein mutations:
  • Plakophilin-2 (PKP2) - Most common
  • Desmoglein-2, Desmocollin-2
  • "Disease of desmosomes"

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Desmosome dysfunction
        ↓
Loss of cell-cell adhesion
        ↓
Myocyte apoptosis
        ↓
Fibrofatty replacement (RV >> LV)
        ↓
RV Dilation, Dysfunction
        ↓
Arrhythmogenic substrate
        ↓
VT (LBBB morphology), VF
        ↓
SUDDEN CARDIAC DEATH (Young athletes)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Often Asymptomatic until arrhythmia

Symptoms: - Palpitations (VT) - Syncope/Presyncope (VT/VF) - Sudden cardiac death (First presentation - young athletes) - Heart failure (Late - RV dysfunction)

Arrhythmias: - VT with LBBB morphology (Origin from RV) - VF

Triggers: - Exercise (↑ Catecholamines)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECG IN ARVC                                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Epsilon wave (Terminal notch in QRS - V1-V3)     │
│   (Hallmark - Delayed RV activation)               │
│ • T wave inversion in V1-V3 (RV strain)            │
│ • Prolonged S wave upstroke (>55 ms in V1-V3)      │
│ • RBBB                                             │
│ • VT with LBBB morphology (RV origin)              │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Echocardiography: - RV dilation, dysfunction - RV wall motion abnormalities (akinesia, dyskinesia) - Aneurysms (RV free wall, Apex, Infundibulum)

Cardiac MRI - GOLD STANDARD: - Fibrofatty replacement of RV - RV dilation, dysfunction - Wall thinning

Endomyocardial Biopsy: - <50% myocytes replaced by fibrofatty tissue (diagnostic) - Risk of perforation (RV thin)

Genetic Testing: - Confirm diagnosis - Family screening

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Lifestyle Modifications: - Avoid competitive sports, strenuous exercise (Trigger for VT/SCD)

Medical: - β-blockers: ↓ Arrhythmias, ↓ Disease progression - Antiarrhythmics: Sotalol, Amiodarone (if recurrent VT)

ICD: - Indicated for: - Cardiac arrest survivor (Secondary prevention) - Sustained VT (Secondary prevention) - High risk (Extensive RV involvement, LV involvement, Syncope, NSVT, Family history of SCD)

Catheter Ablation: - For recurrent VT despite ICD/drugs

Heart Transplant: - End-stage biventricular failure

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Transient LV dysfunction (apical ballooning) following emotional or physical stress, mimicking acute MI but without obstructive CAD.

"Broken Heart Syndrome" or "Stress Cardiomyopathy"

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Postmenopausal women (90%)
  • Triggered by emotional stress (Death of loved one, natural disaster) or physical stress (Surgery, illness)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Severe Stress (Emotional/Physical)
        ↓
↑ Catecholamines (Surge)
        ↓
Catecholamine-induced myocardial stunning
        ↓
Apical akinesia/dyskinesia (Apical ballooning)
        ↓
Basal hyperkinesis
        ↓
Transient LV dysfunction (↓ EF)
        ↓
Recovery (days to weeks)

Mechanism: Direct catecholamine toxicity, Microvascular dysfunction, Coronary spasm

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Presentation: - Chest pain, dyspnea (Mimics acute MI) - ECG changes (ST elevation, T inversion) - ↑ Troponin (Mild elevation) - Recent stressor (emotional/physical)

Often Indistinguishable from STEMI at presentation

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - ST elevation (Anterior leads) or T wave inversion - QTc prolongation

Cardiac Biomarkers: - Troponin: Mildly elevated (usually <10x ULN) - BNP: Elevated

Echocardiography:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECHO IN TAKOTSUBO                             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Apical akinesia/dyskinesia (Apical ballooning)   │
│   "Octopus pot" shape (Takotsubo = octopus trap)   │
│ • Basal hyperkinesis                               │
│ • ↓ EF (usually 20-40%)                            │
│ • Resolves within weeks                            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Coronary Angiography - DIAGNOSTIC: - Normal or Non-obstructive coronary arteries - Differentiates from STEMI (which has occluded artery)

Ventriculography: - Apical ballooning (Takotsubo shape)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Acute: - Supportive care - ACE inhibitors, β-blockers (standard HF therapy) - Diuretics if pulmonary edema - Anticoagulation (if LV thrombus)

Complications: - Cardiogenic shock (Rare - 5-10%) - LV thrombus (Apical akinesia) - Mitral regurgitation (Functional) - LVOT obstruction (Basal hyperkinesis)

Prognosis: - Excellent - Full recovery in days to weeks (most patients) - Mortality: 1-2% (in-hospital) - Recurrence: 5-10%

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM):
  2. Most common cardiomyopathy (Overall)
  3. Causes: Idiopathic (most common), Ischemic, Alcohol, Viral myocarditis
  4. Echo: Dilated LV, ↓ EF (<40%), Thin walls

  5. Treatment: Standard HFrEF (ACEI/ARB, β-blocker, Diuretic, Spironolactone), ICD if EF ≤35%

  6. Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM):

  7. Most common genetic cardiovascular disease (1 in 500)
  8. Autosomal dominant (β-Myosin heavy chain gene)
  9. Asymmetric septal hypertrophy (Most common type)
  10. LV wall ≥15 mm
  11. LVOTO: ↑ with Valsalva, Standing; ↓ with Squatting, Handgrip
  12. Murmur: ↑ with Valsalva (unlike AS)
  13. ECG: LVH, Deep Q waves (pseudo-infarction)
  14. Echo: SAM (Systolic Anterior Motion), LVOT gradient
  15. SCD risk factors: Cardiac arrest, Family history SCD, Syncope, LV ≥30 mm, NSVT

  16. Treatment: β-blockers (1st-line), Avoid vasodilators/diuretics, ICD if high risk

  17. Restrictive Cardiomyopathy (RCM):

  18. Least common cardiomyopathy
  19. Most common cause: Amyloidosis
  20. Echo: Normal/Mildly thick walls, Normal EF, Dilated atria, Granular sparkling (amyloid)
  21. Catheterization: Dip-and-plateau (Square root sign)
