  22. Biopsy: Congo red → Apple-green birefringence (Amyloidosis)

  23. ARVC:

  24. Fibrofatty replacement of RV
  25. Genetic: Desmosomal proteins (PKP2)
  26. ECG: Epsilon wave (Hallmark), T inversion V1-V3
  27. VT with LBBB morphology (RV origin)
  28. SCD in young athletes

  29. Treatment: β-blockers, Avoid sports, ICD

  30. Takotsubo:

  31. Postmenopausal women after emotional stress
  32. Mimics STEMI (Chest pain, ST elevation, ↑ Troponin)
  33. Angiography: Normal coronaries (Differentiates from MI)
  34. Echo: Apical ballooning (Octopus pot shape)
  35. Excellent prognosis - Full recovery

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 25-year-old athlete presents with syncope during exercise. Echocardiography shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy with LV wall thickness of 22 mm and SAM of the mitral valve. What maneuver will INCREASE the intensity of the murmur?

A) Squatting B) Handgrip C) Valsalva maneuver D) Leg raise

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Valsalva maneuver** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM) with LVOT obstruction - **Murmur increases with ↓ preload or ↓ afterload:** - **Valsalva** (↓ venous return → ↓ LV volume → ↑ LVOTO) - **Standing** (↓ venous return) - **Nitrates** (vasodilation) - **Murmur decreases with ↑ preload or ↑ afterload:** - **Squatting** (↑ venous return + ↑ afterload) - **Handgrip** (↑ afterload) - **Leg raise** (↑ venous return) **Key Differentiator:** - **HCM murmur ↑ with Valsalva** (LVOTO worsens) - **AS murmur ↓ with Valsalva** (↓ Stroke volume)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 60-year-old male with history of chronic alcohol use presents with dyspnea. Echo shows dilated LV with EF 25%. Coronary angiography is normal. What is the most appropriate long-term therapy?

A) Alcohol septal ablation B) ACE inhibitor + Beta-blocker + Diuretic C) Calcium channel blocker + Disopyramide D) Observation only

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) ACE inhibitor + Beta-blocker + Diuretic** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** Dilated Cardiomyopathy (Alcoholic) - HFrEF - **Treatment:** Standard HFrEF therapy **"ABCD" for HFrEF:** - **A:** ACE inhibitor/ARB (or ARNI) - **B:** Beta-blocker - **D:** Diuretic (for volume overload) - **Additional:** Aldosterone antagonist (Spironolactone) if EF ≤35%, SGLT2 inhibitor **Device Therapy:** - **ICD** if EF ≤35% despite 3 months optimal medical therapy **Why not others:** - Alcohol septal ablation: For HCM with LVOTO - CCB + Disopyramide: For HCM - Observation: WRONG - needs treatment

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Which finding on ECG is MOST specific for Arrhythmogenic RV Cardiomyopathy (ARVC)?

A) LBBB B) Epsilon wave in V1-V3 C) Delta wave D) U wave

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Epsilon wave in V1-V3** **Explanation:** - **Epsilon wave** is **HALLMARK** of ARVC - **Terminal notch** in QRS complex in V1-V3 - Represents **delayed RV activation** (Fibrofatty replacement) **Other ECG findings in ARVC:** - **T wave inversion** in V1-V3 (RV strain) - Prolonged S wave upstroke (>55 ms in V1-V3) - VT with **LBBB morphology** (RV origin) **Why not others:** - LBBB: Non-specific (DCM, HCM, etc.) - Delta wave: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome - U wave: Hypokalemia, Hypothermia

End of Cardiomyopathies Study Material

Next Topic: - Pericardial Diseases - (Then Respiratory topics)

Study Tips: 1. Compare DCM vs HCM vs RCM (Table very high-yield) 2. Know HCM maneuvers (Valsalva ↑ murmur) 3. Understand SCD risk stratification (HCM, ARVC) 4. Remember Epsilon wave (ARVC hallmark) 5. Know Takotsubo presentation (Mimics MI)

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 4-5 hours Revision Time: 1.5-2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Pericardial Diseases - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (⅘) | NEET PG Weightage: HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Cardiology

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Anatomy & Overview
  2. Acute Pericarditis
  3. Pericardial Effusion
  4. Cardiac Tamponade
  5. Constrictive Pericarditis
  6. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  7. Practice Questions

docs.title: Pericardium
docs.text: Two-layered sac surrounding the heart: - Visceral pericardium (Epicardium) - Adherent to heart - Parietal pericardium - Outer fibrous layer - Pericardial cavity: Space between layers (15-50 ml fluid normally)

docs.title: Functions
docs.text:
• Mechanical protection
  • Prevents excessive cardiac dilation
  • Reduces friction (pericardial fluid)
  • Barrier to infection

docs.title: Pericardial Diseases Classification
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      TYPES OF PERICARDIAL DISEASES                 │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. ACUTE PERICARDITIS                              │
│    • Inflammation of pericardium                   │
│                                                    │
│ 2. PERICARDIAL EFFUSION                            │
│    • Excess fluid in pericardial cavity            │
│                                                    │
│ 3. CARDIAC TAMPONADE                               │
│    • Hemodynamic compromise from effusion          │
│                                                    │
│ 4. CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS                       │
│    • Chronic fibrosis, impaired filling            │
│                                                    │
│ 5. EFFUSIVE-CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS              │
│    • Effusion + Constriction                       │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Inflammation of the pericardium characterized by chest pain, pericardial friction rub, and ECG changes.

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
"CARDIAC" 

C - Collagen vascular disease (SLE, Rheumatoid arthritis, Scleroderma)
A - Autoimmune/Post-cardiac injury (Dressler's syndrome, Post-pericardiotomy)
R - Renal failure (Uremic pericarditis)
D - Drugs (Hydralazine, Procainamide, Isoniazid)
I - Idiopathic/Viral (Most common - 80-90%)
A - Aortic dissection (with hemopericardium)
C - Cancer (Lung, Breast, Lymphoma)

Additional:
- Infection (Bacterial - TB, Viral - Coxsackie, HIV)
- Myocardial infarction (Acute - 1-3 days, Dressler's - 2-10 weeks)
- Radiation
- Trauma

Most Common Causes: - Idiopathic/Viral: 80-90% (Coxsackie B, Echovirus, Adenovirus) - Tuberculosis: Most common in developing countries (India, Africa) - Post-MI: Acute (1-3 days), Dressler's syndrome (2-10 weeks) - Uremia: Chronic kidney disease

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text: Classic Triad: 1. Chest pain 2. Pericardial friction rub 3. ECG changes

docs.title: 1. Chest Pain - HALLMARK
docs.text:
Characteristics: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      PERICARDITIC CHEST PAIN                       │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • SHARP, PLEURITIC (worse with breathing)          │
│ • RETROSTERNAL or Left precordial                  │
│ • Radiates to TRAPEZIUS ridge (Pathognomonic)      │
│ • Worse with:                                      │
│   - Lying supine                                   │
│   - Inspiration                                    │
│   - Coughing                                       │
│ • Better with:                                     │
│   - SITTING UP and LEANING FORWARD (Hallmark)      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Differentiates from MI: - Pleuritic (worse with breathing) - Positional (better sitting forward) - Radiates to trapezius (Phrenic nerve irritation)

docs.title: 2. Pericardial Friction Rub - PATHOGNOMONIC
docs.text:
Characteristics: - High-pitched, scratchy, grating sound - Best heard: Left lower sternal border - Patient position: Sitting, leaning forward, in expiration - Triphasic: 3 components (Atrial systole, Ventricular systole, Ventricular diastole) - Most common: Biphasic or Monophasic - Transient: May come and go - Present in 33-85% of cases

DIAGNOSTIC if present (Specific for pericarditis)

docs.title: 3. Other Symptoms
docs.text:
• Fever (Low-grade)
  • Dyspnea (if large effusion)
  • Cough
  • Myalgia, malaise

docs.title: ECG - KEY DIAGNOSTIC TOOL
docs.text:
4 Sequential Stages:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECG STAGES IN ACUTE PERICARDITIS                  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                        │
│ STAGE 1 (Hours to days):                              │
│ • Diffuse ST elevation (concave upward)                │
│ • PR depression (most specific - atrial injury)        │
│ • Spares aVR, V1 (ST depression in aVR)                │
│                                                        │
│ STAGE 2 (Days):                                        │
│ • ST returns to baseline                               │
│ • T waves flatten                                      │
│                                                        │
│ STAGE 3 (Weeks):                                       │
│ • T wave inversion (diffuse)                           │
│                                                        │
│ STAGE 4 (Weeks to months):                             │
│ • ECG normalizes OR                                    │
│ • Persistent T wave inversion                          │
│                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Features: - Diffuse ST elevation (All leads except aVR, V1) - Concave upward (vs convex in MI) - PR depression (Most specific for pericarditis - 80%) - No reciprocal ST depression (unlike MI) - No Q waves (unlike MI)

Differentiating from MI: 

┌────────────────┬──────────────────┬───────────────────┐
│                │  PERICARDITIS    │  MI (STEMI)       │
├────────────────┼──────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ ST elevation   │ Diffuse, Concave │ Regional, Convex  │
├────────────────┼──────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ PR segment     │ DEPRESSION ✓     │ Normal            │
├────────────────┼──────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Reciprocal ST↓ │ NO               │ YES               │
├────────────────┼──────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Q waves        │ NO               │ YES (later)       │
├────────────────┼──────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ T inversion    │ After ST normal  │ While ST elevated │
└────────────────┴──────────────────┴───────────────────┘

docs.title: Cardiac Biomarkers
docs.text:
• Troponin: May be mildly elevated (15-30%) - Myopericarditis
  • CRP, ESR: Elevated (inflammation)
  • WBC: May be elevated

docs.title: Chest X-ray
docs.text:
• Usually normal (acute pericarditis without effusion)
  • "Water bottle" heart: If large pericardial effusion (>250 ml)
  • Cardiomegaly

docs.title: Echocardiography
docs.text:
• Pericardial effusion (if present)
  • Rule out tamponade
  • LV function (normal in pure pericarditis)
  • Exclude other cardiac pathology

docs.title: Additional Tests (Based on Suspected Etiology)
docs.text:
• Tuberculosis: Sputum AFB, Mantoux test, Pericardial fluid ADA (>40 U/L)
  • Uremia: BUN, Creatinine
  • Autoimmune: ANA, Anti-dsDNA (SLE), RF (Rheumatoid)
  • Malignancy: Cytology of pericardial fluid

docs.title: Acute Pericarditis (Without Effusion)
docs.text:
First-Line: NSAIDs + Colchicine

1. NSAIDs: - Ibuprofen 600-800 mg TID (Preferred - fewer side effects) - Indomethacin 25-50 mg TID (Alternative) - Aspirin 750-1000 mg TID (Especially post-MI) - Duration: Until symptom resolution + CRP normalization (Usually 1-2 weeks), then taper

2. Colchicine: - Dose: 0.5-0.6 mg BD (if >70 kg) or OD (if <70 kg) - Duration: 3 months - Benefits: - ↓ Symptom duration - ↓ Recurrence risk (COPE, ICAP trials) - Side effect: Diarrhea (common)

3. Corticosteroids: - Reserved for: - Refractory to NSAIDs + Colchicine - Contraindication to NSAIDs (Active GI ulcer) - Specific indications: Autoimmune, Uremic pericarditis - Dose: Prednisone 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day - Avoid as first-line: ↑ Recurrence risk

4. Avoid: - Anticoagulation (if possible - risk of hemopericardium) - If essential (e.g., AF, prosthetic valve), monitor closely

docs.title: Activity Restriction
docs.text:
• Athletes: Avoid competitive sports until:
  • Symptom resolution
  • CRP normalization
  • No effusion
  • Minimum 3 months

docs.title: Treat Underlying Cause
docs.text:
• Uremic: Dialysis (Most effective)
  • TB: Anti-tuberculous therapy (RIPE)
  • Bacterial: Antibiotics
  • Autoimmune: Corticosteroids, Immunosuppressants
  • Post-MI (Dressler's): NSAIDs + Colchicine (Avoid corticosteroids if possible)

docs.title: Recurrent Pericarditis (15-30%)
docs.text: Management: - NSAIDs + Colchicine (Higher dose, longer duration - 6 months) - Corticosteroids (if refractory - slow taper over months) - Azathioprine, Methotrexate (Steroid-sparing agents) - IVIG, Anakinra (IL-1 inhibitor) (Refractory cases) - Pericardiectomy (Last resort - chronic refractory)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Excess fluid in the pericardial cavity (>50 ml)

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text: Same as acute pericarditis + Additional causes: - Hypothyroidism (Myxedema) - Congestive heart failure - Nephrotic syndrome - Cirrhosis

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
By Speed of Accumulation: - Acute: Rapid accumulation → Small volume causes tamponade (e.g., Trauma, Aortic dissection) - Chronic: Slow accumulation → Large volume tolerated (e.g., Hypothyroidism, Malignancy)

By Size: - Small: <10 mm (Echo-free space) - Moderate: 10-20 mm - Large: >20 mm - Very Large: >20 mm + Compression of cardiac chambers

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Small Effusion: Often asymptomatic

Large Effusion: - Dyspnea - Chest discomfort - Cough (Compression of bronchus) - Dysphagia (Compression of esophagus) - Hoarseness (Compression of recurrent laryngeal nerve) - Hiccups (Phrenic nerve irritation)

Signs: - Muffled heart sounds (if large) - Ewart's sign: Dullness at left lung base (Pericardial effusion compressing left lower lobe)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - Low voltage (QRS <5 mm in limb leads) - Electrical alternans: Beat-to-beat variation in QRS amplitude (Swinging heart) - SPECIFIC for large effusion/tamponade

Chest X-ray: - "Water bottle" heart: Flask-shaped cardiac silhouette (if >250 ml) - Cardiomegaly

Echocardiography - DIAGNOSTIC: - Echo-free space around heart - Quantify: Small, Moderate, Large - Distribution: Circumferential vs Loculated - Signs of tamponade (See below)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Treat Underlying Cause: - Hypothyroidism: Thyroid hormone replacement - Uremia: Dialysis - TB: Anti-tuberculous therapy

Pericardiocentesis: - Indications: - Cardiac tamponade (Therapeutic) - Large effusion with symptoms - Diagnostic: Unknown etiology (send fluid for analysis)

Pericardial Fluid Analysis: - Appearance: Straw-colored (Transudate), Bloody (Malignancy, Trauma), Purulent (Bacterial) - Cell count, Differential - Protein, LDH, Glucose - Gram stain, Culture, AFB, TB culture - Cytology (Malignancy) - ADA (Adenosine deaminase >40 U/L → TB)

Pericardial Window/Pericardiectomy: - Recurrent effusions - Malignant effusions (Sclerosing agents)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Life-threatening compression of the heart by pericardial fluid → Impaired cardiac filling → ↓ Cardiac output

MEDICAL EMERGENCY

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Acute (Rapid Accumulation): - Trauma (Penetrating, Blunt) - Aortic dissection (Type A with hemopericardium) - Cardiac rupture (Post-MI - free wall rupture) - Iatrogenic: Post-cardiac catheterization, Pacemaker insertion

Subacute/Chronic: - Malignancy (Lung, Breast, Lymphoma) - Tuberculosis - Uremia - Acute pericarditis (any cause)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Pericardial Fluid Accumulation
        ↓
↑ Intrapericardial Pressure
        ↓
Compression of Cardiac Chambers (RA, RV first)
        ↓
↓ Diastolic Filling
        ↓
↓ Stroke Volume, ↓ Cardiac Output
        ↓
Compensatory ↑ HR (Tachycardia)
        ↓
If Severe: CARDIOGENIC SHOCK

Key Concept: Pericardium is non-compliant - small acute accumulation (100-150 ml) can cause tamponade, but large chronic accumulation (1-2 L) may be tolerated.

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Beck's Triad (Classic - 33%): 1. Hypotension (↓ Cardiac output) 2. Elevated JVP (↑ RA pressure) 3. Muffled heart sounds (Fluid around heart)

Additional Signs:

Pulsus Paradoxus - HALLMARK: - Exaggeration of normal (>10 mmHg drop in SBP with inspiration) - Normal: <10 mmHg drop - Tamponade: >10 mmHg (often >20 mmHg) - Mechanism: During inspiration → ↑ RV filling → Septal shift → ↓ LV filling → ↓ SBP

How to Measure: 1. Inflate BP cuff above systolic 2. Deflate slowly 3. Note SBP when Korotkoff sounds heard only during expiration 4. Continue deflating - Note SBP when sounds heard throughout respiratory cycle 5. Difference = Pulsus paradoxus

Other Signs: - Tachycardia (Compensatory) - Tachypnea, Dyspnea - Cool extremities (Poor perfusion) - Kussmaul's sign: Paradoxical ↑ JVP with inspiration (Less common in tamponade - more in constriction)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - Low voltage - Electrical alternans (Swinging heart) - SPECIFIC - Sinus tachycardia

Chest X-ray: - Cardiomegaly ("Water bottle" heart)

Echocardiography - DIAGNOSTIC:

Diagnostic Signs: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECHO FINDINGS IN TAMPONADE                    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Pericardial effusion (Circumferential)           │
│ • RA/RV diastolic collapse (MOST SENSITIVE)        │
│   - RA collapse >1/3 of cardiac cycle              │
│   - RV collapse in early diastole                  │
│ • IVC plethora (Dilated IVC >2 cm, <50% collapse)  │
│ • Respiratory variation in flow velocities:        │
│   - Mitral inflow ↓ >25% with inspiration          │
│   - Tricuspid inflow ↑ >40% with inspiration       │
│ • Swinging heart (in large effusion)               │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Cardiac Catheterization: - Equalization of diastolic pressures: RA ≈ RV ≈ PA diastolic ≈ PCWP - Rarely done (Echo sufficient)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
EMERGENCY - REQUIRES IMMEDIATE DRAINAGE

Acute Stabilization: 1. IV Fluids: Aggressive fluid resuscitation (↑ Preload → ↑ Filling) 2. Avoid: - Diuretics (↓ Preload → Worsen tamponade) - Vasodilators (↓ Afterload → Hypotension) - Positive pressure ventilation (↑ Intrathoracic pressure → ↓ Venous return)

Definitive Treatment: Pericardiocentesis

Procedure: - Approach: Subxiphoid (Most common), Apical, Parasternal - Echo or Fluoroscopy-guided - Drain fluid: Immediate relief (Even small volume - 50-100 ml) - Leave catheter in place (for ongoing drainage)

Indications for Surgical Drainage: - Hemopericardium (Trauma, Aortic dissection, Post-MI rupture) - Pericardiocentesis may worsen bleeding - Surgical exploration + Repair - Purulent pericarditis (Bacterial) - Pericardial window or Pericardiectomy - Recurrent effusions

Post-Procedure: - Monitor for: - Re-accumulation - Complications: Bleeding, Pneumothorax, Arrhythmias, Coronary/Chamber laceration

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Chronic inflammation → Pericardial fibrosis and calcification → Impaired diastolic filling of ventricles

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Developing Countries: - Tuberculosis - Most common (50-70%)

Developed Countries: - Idiopathic/Viral (40-50%) - Post-cardiac surgery (Post-pericardiotomy, Post-radiation) - Post-pericarditis (Recurrent)

Other Causes: - Radiation therapy (Mediastinal - Hodgkin's lymphoma, Breast cancer) - Connective tissue disease (SLE, Rheumatoid) - Malignancy - Uremia - Post-trauma

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Chronic Pericardial Inflammation
        ↓
Fibrosis + Calcification (Thick, rigid pericardium)
        ↓
"Rigid Shell" around heart
        ↓
↓ Diastolic Compliance (Impaired filling)
        ↓
↑ Atrial Pressures (RA, LA)
        ↓
Systemic/Pulmonary Venous Congestion
        ↓
RIGHT HEART FAILURE (Predominant)

Hemodynamics: - Early rapid filling, then abrupt cessation ("Dip and plateau" - Square root sign) - Equalization of diastolic pressures - Ventricular interdependence (Respiratory variation in filling)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Insidious Onset (Months to years)

Symptoms: - Dyspnea (exertional) - Fatigue - Right heart failure symptoms: - Peripheral edema (Legs, ankles) - Ascites (Often massive, out of proportion to peripheral edema) - Abdominal distension - Orthopnea (Less prominent than left heart failure)

Signs:

Elevated JVP - HALLMARK: - Prominent x and y descents (Steep, rapid) - Kussmaul's sign: ↑ JVP (or failure to fall) with inspiration - CLASSIC - Normally JVP ↓ with inspiration - Constriction: Rigid pericardium prevents normal filling → JVP ↑

Other Signs: - Hepatomegaly (Pulsatile liver - if TR present) - Ascites (May be massive) - Peripheral edema - Pericardial knock: Early diastolic sound (Abrupt cessation of filling) - Earlier than S3, higher-pitched

"Pericardial knock" vs S3: - Pericardial knock: Earlier (0.09-0.12 s after A2), Higher-pitched - S3: Later (0.14-0.16 s after A2), Lower-pitched

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - Low voltage (Pericardial thickening) - Non-specific ST-T changes - AF (Atrial enlargement)

Chest X-ray: - Pericardial calcification (20-30%) - BEST SEEN on lateral view - DIAGNOSTIC if present (Specific for constrictive pericarditis) - Normal/Mildly enlarged heart (Unlike dilated CM) - Pleural effusions

Echocardiography:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      ECHO IN CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Thickened pericardium (>4 mm) - CT/MRI better    │
│ • Normal/Mildly enlarged atria                     │
│ • Normal ventricular size, EF                      │
│ • Septal bounce (Ventricular interdependence)      │
│ • Respiratory variation in flow velocities:        │
│   - Mitral inflow ↓ >25% with inspiration          │
│   - Tricuspid inflow ↑ >40% with inspiration       │
│ • IVC plethora (Dilated, <50% collapse)            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Cardiac CT/MRI - BEST for Pericardium: - Pericardial thickness >4 mm (Normal: 1-2 mm) - >6 mm: Highly suggestive - Pericardial calcification (CT best) - Conical/Tubular ventricles

Cardiac Catheterization:

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      CATHETERIZATION IN CONSTRICTION              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • "Dip and plateau" (Square root sign)             │
│   - Early rapid ↓ in pressure, then plateau        │
│ • Equalization of diastolic pressures:             │
│   - RA ≈ RV diastolic ≈ PA diastolic ≈ PCWP       │
│   (Within 5 mmHg of each other)                    │
│ • RVEDP/RVSP >1/3 (>0.33)                          │
│ • Discordant RV/LV systolic pressures with respir  │
│   (Ventricular interdependence)                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Differentiation: Constriction vs Restriction
docs.text:
Often challenging - Both have diastolic dysfunction

┌──────────────┬────────────────┬───────────────────┐
│              │ CONSTRICTION   │ RESTRICTION (RCM) │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Pericardium  │ Thick (>4 mm)  │ Normal            │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Myocardium   │ Normal         │ Thick/Infiltrated │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Calcification│ Common (20-30%)│ Rare              │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Kussmaul sign│ PRESENT ✓      │ ABSENT            │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Resp variation│ MARKED (>25%)  │ Minimal (<10%)    │
│ (Doppler)    │                │                   │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ BNP          │ Normal/Low     │ Elevated          │
├──────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤
│ Biopsy       │ Not needed     │ Diagnostic        │
└──────────────┴────────────────┴───────────────────┘

Key Differentiators: - Kussmaul's sign: Present in constriction, Absent in restriction - Respiratory variation: More marked in constriction - Pericardial calcification: Suggests constriction - BNP: Low in constriction, High in restriction

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Medical (Limited Role): - Diuretics: Cautious (Reduce ascites/edema, but may ↓ preload too much) - Treat underlying cause: TB (Anti-tuberculous therapy)

Definitive Treatment: Pericardiectomy

Indications: - Symptomatic (NYHA class III-IV) - Hemodynamic compromise

Procedure: - Surgical resection of pericardium (Anterior and lateral walls, phrenic-to-phrenic) - High-risk surgery: 5-10% mortality - Outcomes: - Improvement in 60-90% - May take months for full benefit (Epicardial constriction may persist)

Poor Prognostic Factors: - Radiation-induced (Myocardial fibrosis) - NYHA class IV - Low cardiac output - Severe calcification - RV dysfunction

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Acute Pericarditis - Classic Triad:
  2. Chest pain (Sharp, pleuritic, worse lying down, better sitting forward)
  3. Pericardial friction rub (Triphasic, scratchy, PATHOGNOMONIC)

  4. ECG changes (Diffuse ST elevation, PR depression)

  5. Pericarditis ECG:

  6. Stage 1: Diffuse ST elevation (concave), PR depression (Most specific)
  7. Diffuse (All leads except aVR, V1)
  8. No reciprocal ST depression (Unlike MI)

  9. No Q waves

  10. Pericarditis Management:

  11. NSAIDs + Colchicine (First-line)
  12. Colchicine 3 months (↓ Recurrence)

  13. Avoid corticosteroids as first-line (↑ Recurrence)

  14. Cardiac Tamponade - Beck's Triad:

  15. Hypotension
  16. Elevated JVP

  17. Muffled heart sounds

  18. Tamponade - Pulsus Paradoxus:

  19. >10 mmHg drop in SBP with inspiration (HALLMARK)

  20. Mechanism: Inspiration → ↑ RV filling → Septal shift → ↓ LV filling

  21. Tamponade Echo:

  22. RA/RV diastolic collapse (Most sensitive)
  23. IVC plethora (Dilated, <50% collapse)
  24. Respiratory variation in flow (Mitral ↓ >25%, Tricuspid ↑ >40%)

  25. Electrical alternans (ECG) - Swinging heart

  26. Tamponade Management:

  27. EMERGENCY: Pericardiocentesis
  28. IV fluids (↑ Preload)

  29. AVOID: Diuretics, Vasodilators, Positive pressure ventilation

  30. Constrictive Pericarditis - Most Common Cause:

  31. Developed: Idiopathic/Viral, Post-cardiac surgery

  32. Developing: Tuberculosis (India, Africa)

  33. Constrictive Pericarditis - Kussmaul's Sign:

  34. ↑ JVP (or failure to fall) with inspiration - CLASSIC

  35. Rigid pericardium prevents normal filling

  36. Constrictive Pericarditis - Pericardial Knock:

    • Early diastolic sound (Abrupt cessation of filling)
    • Earlier and higher-pitched than S3

  37. Constrictive Pericarditis - Investigations:

    • CXR: Pericardial calcification (20-30%) - DIAGNOSTIC
    • CT/MRI: Pericardial thickness >4 mm
    • Catheterization: Dip-and-plateau (Square root sign), Equalization of diastolic pressures

  38. Constrictive Pericarditis - Management:

    • Pericardiectomy (Definitive treatment)

  39. Constriction vs Restriction:

    • Kussmaul sign: Present in constriction, Absent in restriction
    • Pericardial calcification: Constriction
    • BNP: Low/Normal in constriction, High in restriction

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Sharp chest pain, worse lying down, better sitting forward, radiates to trapezius, friction rub → Acute pericarditis

Scenario 2: Diffuse ST elevation, PR depression on ECG → Acute pericarditis

Scenario 3: Hypotension, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, SBP drop >10 mmHg with inspiration → Cardiac tamponade (Beck's triad + Pulsus paradoxus)

Scenario 4: RA collapse on echo, IVC plethora → Cardiac tamponade → Emergency pericardiocentesis

Scenario 5: Chronic dyspnea, ascites, JVP ↑ with inspiration (Kussmaul), pericardial calcification on CXR → Constrictive pericarditis

Scenario 6: Dip-and-plateau on catheterization → Constrictive pericarditis (or Restrictive cardiomyopathy)

Scenario 7: Post-MI patient (2 weeks) with pleuritic chest pain, fever → Dressler's syndrome (Post-MI pericarditis)

Scenario 8: CKD patient on dialysis with pericarditis → Uremic pericarditis → Dialysis most effective treatment

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Acute pericarditis: Chest pain (sharp, positional), Friction rub, ECG (ST elevation, PR depression)
  • Pericarditis ECG: Diffuse ST↑, PR↓, No reciprocal ST↓, No Q waves
  • Pericarditis treatment: NSAIDs + Colchicine (3 months)
  • Tamponade: Beck's triad (Hypotension, ↑ JVP, Muffled sounds)
  • Pulsus paradoxus: >10 mmHg drop with inspiration
  • Tamponade echo: RA/RV collapse, IVC plethora
  • Tamponade treatment: Emergency pericardiocentesis, IV fluids
  • Constrictive: TB (developing), Kussmaul's sign, Pericardial knock
  • Constrictive CXR: Pericardial calcification
  • Constrictive catheterization: Dip-and-plateau, Equalization
  • Constriction vs Restriction: Kussmaul (present vs absent), BNP (low vs high)
  • Pericardiectomy for constrictive pericarditis

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 30-year-old male presents with sharp chest pain that worsens with lying down and improves when sitting forward. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. What is the most appropriate initial management?

A) Thrombolysis B) NSAIDs + Colchicine C) Corticosteroids D) Pericardiocentesis

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) NSAIDs + Colchicine** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** Acute pericarditis - **Chest pain:** Sharp, positional (worse lying, better sitting forward) - **ECG:** Diffuse ST elevation + PR depression **Management:** - **First-line:** **NSAIDs + Colchicine** - NSAIDs: Ibuprofen 600-800 mg TID or Aspirin 750-1000 mg TID - **Colchicine:** 0.5-0.6 mg BD for **3 months** (↓ Recurrence) **Why not others:** - **Thrombolysis:** For STEMI (Pericarditis mimics STEMI but is NOT MI) - **Corticosteroids:** Avoid as first-line (↑ Recurrence risk) - Reserved for refractory or specific indications - **Pericardiocentesis:** For tamponade or large symptomatic effusion (Not indicated in uncomplicated pericarditis)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A trauma patient has hypotension, elevated JVP, and muffled heart sounds. BP is 80/60 mmHg. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

A) IV fluids B) Diuretics C) Emergency pericardiocentesis D) Pericardiectomy

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Emergency pericardiocentesis** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** **Cardiac tamponade** (post-trauma - likely hemopericardium) - **Beck's Triad:** - Hypotension - Elevated JVP - Muffled heart sounds **Management:** - **EMERGENCY: Pericardiocentesis** - Definitive treatment - **Even small volume drainage** (50-100 ml) provides immediate relief **Supportive:** - **IV fluids** (↑ Preload) - Can give while preparing for pericardiocentesis **AVOID:** - **Diuretics** (↓ Preload → Worsen tamponade) **Note:** - **Hemopericardium (trauma)** may require **surgical exploration** (Pericardiocentesis can worsen bleeding) - But in acute tamponade with shock, **pericardiocentesis is life-saving first step**

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Which ECG finding is MOST SPECIFIC for acute pericarditis?

A) Diffuse ST elevation B) PR depression C) Electrical alternans D) Low voltage

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) PR depression** **Explanation:** - **PR depression** is the **MOST SPECIFIC** ECG finding for acute pericarditis (80%) - Mechanism: **Atrial injury** (Pericardium inflamed → Atrial epicardium involved) **Other ECG findings in pericarditis:** - **Diffuse ST elevation** (More sensitive but less specific) - **No reciprocal ST depression** (Unlike MI) - **Concave upward ST** (Unlike convex in MI) - **No Q waves** **Why not others:** - **Diffuse ST elevation:** Sensitive but can occur in other conditions - **Electrical alternans:** Specific for **large pericardial effusion/tamponade** (Swinging heart) - **Low voltage:** Pericardial effusion, Obesity, COPD, Amyloidosis

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A patient has dyspnea, ascites, and JVP that paradoxically rises with inspiration. Chest X-ray shows pericardial calcification. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A) Cardiac tamponade B) Constrictive pericarditis C) Restrictive cardiomyopathy D) Dilated cardiomyopathy

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Constrictive pericarditis** **Explanation:** - **Kussmaul's sign:** ↑ JVP with inspiration - **CLASSIC** for constrictive pericarditis - **Pericardial calcification** on CXR - **DIAGNOSTIC** (20-30% of constriction cases) **Other features:** - Ascites (often out of proportion to peripheral edema) - Right heart failure symptoms - Pericardial knock (Early diastolic sound) **Catheterization:** - Dip-and-plateau (Square root sign) - Equalization of diastolic pressures **Treatment:** - **Pericardiectomy** (Definitive) **Why not others:** - **Tamponade:** Would have hypotension, Kussmaul's sign LESS common - **Restrictive CM:** NO Kussmaul's sign, No pericardial calcification - **Dilated CM:** Systolic dysfunction, No Kussmaul, No calcification

End of Pericardial Diseases Study Material

Next Topics: - Respiratory topics (Asthma, COPD, Pneumonia, Tuberculosis, ILD, Bronchiectasis, Lung Cancer, Pleural Effusion, Pulmonary Embolism, Respiratory Failure)

Study Tips: 1. Master pericarditis ECG (Diffuse ST↑, PR↓) - Very high-yield 2. Know Beck's triad and Pulsus paradoxus (Tamponade) 3. Understand Kussmaul's sign (Constrictive pericarditis) 4. Differentiate Constriction vs Restriction (Table) 5. Remember pericardiocentesis indications

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (High) Estimated Study Time: 3-4 hours Revision Time: 1-1.5 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Asthma - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (⅘) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Respiratory

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition & Overview
  2. Pathophysiology
  3. Clinical Features
  4. Diagnosis
  5. Classification & Severity
  6. Management
  7. Acute Exacerbation
  8. Special Types
  9. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  10. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways characterized by reversible airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and recurrent episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and cough.

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Most common chronic disease in children
  • Prevalence: 5-10% worldwide
  • Often begins in childhood
  • Atopy (personal/family history of allergies) - Strong risk factor

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Reversible airflow obstruction (vs COPD - partially reversible)
  • Variable symptoms (day-to-day, seasonal)
  • Triggered by specific stimuli

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Genetic Predisposition + Environmental Triggers
              ↓
    Airway Inflammation
      (Eosinophilic)
              ↓
    ┌─────────────┬────────────────┐
    ↓             ↓                ↓
Bronchoconstriction  Airway Edema   Mucus Plugging
    ↓             ↓                ↓
      ↓           ↓                ↓
        AIRFLOW OBSTRUCTION
              ↓
   Bronchial Hyperresponsiveness
              ↓
        ASTHMA SYMPTOMS

docs.title: Inflammatory Cells
docs.text:
Type 2 (T2) Inflammation: - Eosinophils (Most important) - Mast cells (Release histamine, leukotrienes) - Th2 lymphocytes (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) - IgE-mediated (Allergic asthma)

Inflammatory Mediators: - Histamine, Leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) → Bronchoconstriction - IL-5 → Eosinophil activation - IL-4, IL-13 → IgE production

docs.title: Airway Remodeling (Chronic Asthma)
docs.text:
Long-term inflammation → Structural changes: - Basement membrane thickening - Smooth muscle hypertrophy - Goblet cell hyperplasia → ↑ Mucus - Subepithelial fibrosis

→ Irreversible airflow obstruction (if untreated)

docs.title: Triggers
docs.text:
"ASTHMA" Mnemonic: 

A - Allergens (Pollen, Dust mites, Animal dander)
S - Smoke (Tobacco, Air pollution)
T - Temperature (Cold air)
H - Hypersensitivity (Aspirin, NSAIDs, Beta-blockers)
M - Mold, Medications
A - Activity (Exercise-induced)

Additional:
- Respiratory infections (Viral - RSV, Rhinovirus)
- Emotional stress
- GERD (Gastroesophageal reflux)
- Occupational exposures

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Classic Triad: 1. Wheezing (Expiratory >> Inspiratory) 2. Dyspnea (Breathlessness) 3. Cough (Often nocturnal, dry or productive)

Additional: - Chest tightness - Symptoms worse at night/early morning - Variable, episodic - Triggered by specific stimuli

Cough-Variant Asthma: - Chronic cough as ONLY symptom - No wheezing or dyspnea - Responds to bronchodilators

docs.title: Physical Examination
docs.text:
Stable Asthma: - Often normal between exacerbations

During Exacerbation: - Tachypnea, Tachycardia - Prolonged expiratory phase - Wheeze (High-pitched, polyphonic, expiratory) - Bilateral - Intensity NOT related to severity (Severe: Silent chest - no air movement) - Use of accessory muscles - Hyperinflation (Barrel chest if chronic) - Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg - severe exacerbation)

docs.title: Clinical Diagnosis
docs.text:
Asthma Suspected if: - Recurrent respiratory symptoms (Wheezing, dyspnea, cough, chest tightness) - Variable (Day-to-day, seasonal) - Triggered by specific stimuli - Worse at night/early morning - Personal/Family history of atopy

Diagnosis Confirmed by: - Reversible airflow obstruction (Spirometry)

docs.title: Spirometry - GOLD STANDARD
docs.text:
Obstructive Pattern: - FEV₁/FVC ratio <0.70 (or <70%) - FEV₁: Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second - FVC: Forced Vital Capacity - ↓ FEV₁ (Severity)

Reversibility Testing (Bronchodilator Response):

Diagnostic Criteria: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      BRONCHODILATOR REVERSIBILITY                  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Post-bronchodilator improvement:                  │
│ • FEV₁ increase ≥12% AND ≥200 ml from baseline     │
│                                                    │
│ Confirms REVERSIBLE airflow obstruction            │
│ → Diagnostic for Asthma                            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

How to Perform: 1. Baseline spirometry 2. Administer bronchodilator (Salbutamol 400 mcg inhaled) 3. Repeat spirometry after 15-20 minutes 4. Calculate improvement in FEV₁

Interpretation: - ↑ FEV₁ ≥12% and ≥200 ml → Positive (Asthma) - No significant change → Negative (Does NOT rule out asthma - may need further testing)

docs.title: Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR)
docs.text:
Portable device for monitoring

Uses: - Home monitoring (Diurnal variation) - Assess severity of exacerbation - Guide treatment adjustments

Diurnal Variation: - >20% variation between morning and evening → Suggests asthma - PEFR lowest in early morning, highest in afternoon

Normal PEFR: - Depends on age, height, sex - Personal best is most useful reference

docs.title: Other Investigations
docs.text:
Chest X-ray: - Usually normal (during stable period) - Hyperinflation (Chronic asthma) - Rule out: Pneumonia, Pneumothorax, Foreign body - During acute exacerbation: To rule out complications

CBC: - Eosinophilia (if allergic asthma)

Serum IgE: - Elevated (Allergic asthma)

Allergy Testing: - Skin prick test or Specific IgE (RAST) - Identify triggers

Methacholine Challenge Test: - If spirometry normal but asthma suspected - Positive: PC20 <8 mg/ml (Provocative concentration causing 20% ↓ FEV₁) - Confirms bronchial hyperresponsiveness

Fractional Exhaled Nitric Oxide (FeNO): - ↑ in eosinophilic inflammation - >50 ppb: Suggests asthma - Useful: Difficult-to-diagnose cases, Guide steroid therapy

docs.title: Based on Symptoms & Lung Function (Before Treatment)
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         ASTHMA SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION                       │
├───────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ INTERMITTENT  │ • Symptoms ≤2 days/week                      │
│               │ • Nighttime awakenings ≤2x/month             │
│               │ • SABA use ≤2 days/week                      │
│               │ • No interference with normal activity       │
│               │ • FEV₁ >80% predicted                        │
├───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ MILD          │ • Symptoms >2 days/week but not daily        │
│ PERSISTENT    │ • Nighttime awakenings 3-4x/month            │
│               │ • SABA use >2 days/week but not daily        │
│               │ • Minor limitation                           │
│               │ • FEV₁ >80% predicted                        │
├───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ MODERATE      │ • Daily symptoms                             │
│ PERSISTENT    │ • Nighttime awakenings >1x/week but not      │
│               │   nightly                                    │
│               │ • Daily SABA use                             │
│               │ • Some limitation                            │
│               │ • FEV₁ 60-80% predicted                      │
├───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ SEVERE        │ • Symptoms throughout the day                │
│ PERSISTENT    │ • Nighttime awakenings often 7x/week         │
│               │ • SABA use several times per day             │
│               │ • Extremely limited                          │
│               │ • FEV₁ <60% predicted                        │
└───────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Asthma Control (On Treatment)
docs.text:
Goal: Achieve and maintain control

┌──────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ WELL-        │ • Symptoms ≤2 days/week                    │
│ CONTROLLED   │ • Nighttime awakenings ≤2x/month           │
│              │ • SABA use ≤2 days/week                    │
│              │ • No activity limitation                   │
│              │ • FEV₁/PEFR >80% predicted or personal best│
│              │ • No exacerbations                         │
├──────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ NOT WELL-    │ • Symptoms >2 days/week                    │
│ CONTROLLED   │ • Nighttime awakenings 1-3x/week           │
│              │ • SABA use >2 days/week                    │
│              │ • Some limitation                          │
│              │ • FEV₁/PEFR 60-80%                         │
│              │ • 2-3 exacerbations/year                   │
├──────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ VERY POORLY  │ • Throughout the day                       │
│ CONTROLLED   │ • ≥4x/week                                 │
│              │ • Several times/day                        │
│              │ • Extremely limited                        │
│              │ • FEV₁/PEFR <60%                           │
│              │ • ≥4 exacerbations/year                    │
└──────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
1. Control symptoms
  2. Prevent exacerbations
  3. Maintain normal lung function
  4. Minimize side effects

docs.title: General Measures
docs.text:
Avoid Triggers: - Allergens (Dust mites, Animal dander) - Smoke (Tobacco, Air pollution) - Cold air, Exercise (pre-medicate) - NSAIDs, Beta-blockers (if sensitive)

Environmental Control: - Dust mite-proof bedding - Remove carpets, soft toys - HEPA filters

Patient Education: - Proper inhaler technique - PEFR monitoring - Action plan for exacerbations

docs.title: Stepwise Pharmacological Management (GINA Guidelines)
docs.text:
"Step Up" if not controlled, "Step Down" if controlled ≥3 months

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         STEPWISE ASTHMA MANAGEMENT                         │
├──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 1   │ As-needed low-dose ICS-Formoterol               │
│          │ OR As-needed SABA (Not preferred)               │
├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 2   │ Daily low-dose ICS                              │
│          │ + As-needed SABA                                │
│          │ OR As-needed low-dose ICS-Formoterol            │
├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 3   │ Low-dose ICS-LABA (Maintenance)                 │
│          │ + As-needed SABA                                │
│          │ OR Low-dose ICS-Formoterol (Maintenance + Relief│
├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 4   │ Medium/High-dose ICS-LABA                       │
│          │ + As-needed SABA                                │
│          │ Consider add: Tiotropium, Leukotriene modifier  │
├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ STEP 5   │ High-dose ICS-LABA                              │
│          │ + Add-on: Anti-IgE (Omalizumab), Anti-IL5       │
│          │   (Mepolizumab), Anti-IL4R (Dupilumab)          │
│          │ OR Low-dose OCS (if needed)                     │
└──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ICS: Inhaled Corticosteroid LABA: Long-Acting Beta-2 Agonist SABA: Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonist OCS: Oral Corticosteroid

docs.title: 1. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) - CONTROLLER (Mainstay)
docs.text:
Examples: - Bud esonide, Fluticasone, Ciclesonide

Mechanism: ↓ Airway inflammation (↓ Eosinophils, ↓ Cytokines)

Indications: - All persistent asthma (Mild, Moderate, Severe) - First-line controller

Doses: - Low: Budesonide 200-400 mcg/day, Fluticasone 100-250 mcg/day - Medium: Budesonide 400-800 mcg/day, Fluticasone 250-500 mcg/day - High: Budesonide >800 mcg/day, Fluticasone >500 mcg/day

Side Effects: - Local: Oral candidiasis (Thrush), Dysphonia (Hoarseness) - Prevention: Rinse mouth after use, Use spacer - Systemic: Rare at low-moderate doses (Growth suppression, Adrenal suppression, Osteoporosis at high doses)

Key Point: Start ICS early (Even mild persistent asthma) - Prevents airway remodeling

docs.title: 2. Long-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (LABA) - CONTROLLER
docs.text:
Examples: - Formoterol, Salmeterol, Vilanterol

Mechanism: Bronchodilation (12-24 hours)

NEVER use as monotherapy (↑ Asthma-related deaths) ALWAYS use with ICS (Combination inhaler)

Combination Inhalers: - Budesonide/Formoterol (Symbicort) - Fluticasone/Salmeterol (Seretide) - Fluticasone/Vilanterol (Relvar)

Formoterol: - Fast-acting (Onset 5 minutes) - Can be used for Maintenance And Reliever Therapy (MART) - Single inhaler for both daily maintenance and as-needed relief

docs.title: 3. Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (SABA) - RELIEVER
docs.text:
Examples: - Salbutamol (Albuterol), Terbutaline, Levosalbutamol

Mechanism: Rapid bronchodilation (Onset 5 minutes, Duration 4-6 hours)

Indications: - As-needed relief for acute symptoms - Pre-exercise (Exercise-induced asthma)

Overuse (>2 canisters/month or >2 doses/week): - Suggests poor control → Need to step up controller

Side Effects: - Tremor, Tachycardia, Hypokalemia (High doses)

docs.title: 4. Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists (LTRA) - CONTROLLER
docs.text:
Examples: - Montelukast (Most common - 10 mg OD) - Zafirlukast

Mechanism: Block LTD4 receptors → ↓ Bronchoconstriction, ↓ Inflammation

Indications: - Add-on to ICS (Step 3-4) - Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) - Exercise-induced asthma - Allergic rhinitis coexisting

Advantages: - Oral (Good for patients with poor inhaler technique)

Side Effects: - Generally well tolerated - Neuropsychiatric effects (Rare - Mood changes, Suicidality) - FDA warning

docs.title: 5. Anticholinergics (Muscarinic Antagonists)
docs.text:
Long-Acting: - Tiotropium (Spiriva)

Mechanism: Block M3 muscarinic receptors → ↓ Bronchoconstriction

Indications: - Add-on to ICS-LABA (Step 4-5) if uncontrolled

Benefits: - ↓ Exacerbations - ↑ Lung function

docs.title: 6. Biologics (Monoclonal Antibodies) - SEVERE ASTHMA
docs.text:
Indications: - Severe eosinophilic or allergic asthma - Uncontrolled on Step 4-5 - Frequent exacerbations despite high-dose ICS-LABA

Options:

Anti-IgE: - Omalizumab (Xolair) - Indication: Allergic asthma (High IgE, Positive skin tests) - Dose: Based on IgE level and weight - SC injection every 2-4 weeks

Anti-IL-5: - Mepolizumab (Nucala), Benralizumab (Fasenra) - Indication: Severe eosinophilic asthma (Eosinophils >300/μl)

Anti-IL-4 Receptor: - Dupilumab (Dupixent) - Indication: Type 2 asthma (Eosinophilic or FeNO >25 ppb)

docs.title: 7. Oral Corticosteroids (OCS)
docs.text:
Indications: - Acute exacerbations (Short course: 5-7 days) - Severe asthma uncontrolled on all other therapies (Maintenance - LAST RESORT)

Dose: - Acute: Prednisolone 40-50 mg daily × 5-7 days - Maintenance: Lowest possible dose (Side effects)

Side Effects (Long-term): - Osteoporosis, Diabetes, HTN, Weight gain, Cataracts, Adrenal suppression, Immunosuppression

Steroid-Sparing Agents: - Biologics (Omalizumab, Mepolizumab, etc.) - Methotrexate, Azathioprine (Rarely used)

docs.title: Inhaler Devices
docs.text:
Metered-Dose Inhaler (MDI): - Most common - Requires coordination (Actuation + Inhalation) - Use with spacer (↑ Lung deposition, ↓ Oropharyngeal deposition)

Dry Powder Inhaler (DPI): - Breath-actuated (Easier for some patients) - Requires adequate inspiratory flow

Nebulizer: - Converts liquid to mist - For acute severe exacerbations, Young children, Elderly

Proper Technique: 1. Shake (MDI) 2. Exhale fully 3. Seal lips around mouthpiece 4. Actuate and inhale slowly and deeply (MDI) 5. Hold breath for 10 seconds 6. Exhale slowly 7. Rinse mouth (if ICS)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Acute or subacute worsening of symptoms beyond normal day-to-day variation

docs.title: Triggers
docs.text:
• Viral infections (Most common - RSV, Rhinovirus)
  • Allergen exposure
  • Air pollution
  • Poor medication adherence
  • Weather changes

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - ↑ Dyspnea, Wheezing, Cough, Chest tightness - ↓ Response to SABA

Signs: - Tachypnea (RR >30/min) - Tachycardia (HR >120/min) - Prolonged expiration - Wheeze (May be absent in severe - "Silent chest") - Use of accessory muscles - Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg) - Cyanosis (Severe) - Altered consciousness (Life-threatening)

docs.title: Severity Assessment
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      SEVERITY OF ACUTE ASTHMA EXACERBATION               │
├─────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ MILD-       │ • Talks in sentences                       │
│ MODERATE    │ • Prefers sitting to lying                 │
│             │ • Not agitated                             │
│             │ • RR <30/min                               │
│             │ • HR <120/min                              │
│             │ • PEFR >50% predicted/best                 │
│             │ • SpO₂ >90%                                │
├─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ SEVERE      │ • Talks in words                           │
│             │ • Sits hunched forward                     │
│             │ • Agitated                                 │
│             │ • RR >30/min                               │
│             │ • HR >120/min                              │
│             │ • PEFR 33-50% predicted/best               │
│             │ • SpO₂ 90-95%                              │
├─────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ LIFE-       │ • Cannot speak                             │
│ THREATENING │ • Exhausted, Confused, Drowsy              │
│             │ • Silent chest (No wheeze - no air movement│
│             │ • Cyanosis                                 │
│             │ • Bradycardia, Hypotension                 │
│             │ • PEFR <33% predicted/best                 │
│             │ • SpO₂ <90%                                │
│             │ • PaO₂ <60 mmHg, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg (or rising)│
└─────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Life-Threatening Features: - Silent chest (No wheeze - very little air movement) - Cyanosis - Bradycardia (<60/min) - Sign of impending arrest - Hypotension - Exhaustion, Confusion - PaCO₂ >45 mmHg or Rising (Respiratory failure)

Normal/Low PaCO₂ expected (Hyperventilation) Rising PaCO₂ = Respiratory muscle fatigue → Impending respiratory arrest

docs.title: Management of Acute Exacerbation
docs.text:
"ASTHMA" Management: 

A - Assess severity (PEFR, SpO₂, ABG)
S - Supplemental oxygen (Target SpO₂ 93-95%)
T - Treatment (Bronchodilators, Steroids)
H - Hospital admission if severe
M - Monitor response (PEFR, Clinical)
A - Adjust therapy based on response

docs.title: Immediate Treatment (All Severities):
docs.text:
1. Oxygen: - Target: SpO₂ 93-95% (Adults), 94-98% (Children) - High-flow if needed

2. Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonist (SABA): - Salbutamol: - MDI + Spacer: 4-8 puffs (100 mcg each) every 20 min × 1 hour - Nebulizer: 2.5-5 mg every 20 min × 1 hour (Or continuous) - Response: ↑ PEFR, ↓ Symptoms

3. Ipratropium Bromide (Anticholinergic): - Add to SABA if severe or poor response - Nebulizer: 0.5 mg every 20 min × 1 hour

4. Systemic Corticosteroids: - Prednisolone 40-50 mg PO (Or IV Hydrocortisone 100 mg if unable to take oral) - Within 1 hour of presentation - Continue 5-7 days (No need to taper if <2 weeks)

docs.title: Additional Treatment (Severe/Life-Threatening):
docs.text:
5. Magnesium Sulfate: - Indication: Severe exacerbation not responding to initial treatment - Dose: 2 g IV over 20 minutes (Single dose) - Mechanism: Bronchodilation (Ca²⁺ channel blocker) - Effective: ↓ Hospital admissions

6. IV Beta-2 Agonist: - If nebulized not effective or life-threatening - Salbutamol 5 mcg/kg IV bolus, then infusion

7. Heliox: - Helium-Oxygen mixture (80:20 or 70:30) - ↓ Airway resistance (Helium less dense than air) - Alternative if not responding

docs.title: Life-Threatening Asthma:
docs.text:
8. ICU Admission: - Intubation and Mechanical Ventilation (if respiratory arrest/failure) - Anesthetic agents: Ketamine, Halothane (Bronchodilators)

Indications for Intubation: - Respiratory arrest - Severe hypoxemia despite O₂ - Exhaustion, Altered consciousness - PaCO₂ >50 mmHg and rising

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
• PEFR: Every 15-30 min initially
  • SpO₂: Continuous
  • ABG: If SpO₂ <92% or severe/life-threatening
  • Clinical assessment: RR, HR, Work of breathing

Response to Treatment: - Good: PEFR >60-80% predicted within 1-2 hours → Consider discharge - Incomplete: PEFR 40-60% → Continue treatment, Admit - Poor: PEFR <40%, Worsening → ICU

docs.title: Discharge Criteria
docs.text:
Patient can be discharged if: - PEFR >60-80% predicted or personal best - Sustained improvement ≥1 hour after last treatment - Adequate inhaler technique - Stable on discharge medications

Discharge Medications: - Continue SABA as needed - ICS ± LABA (Controller - Start or increase dose) - Prednisolone 40-50 mg daily × 5-7 days (Oral steroids)

Follow-up: - Within 2-7 days - Review medications, Inhaler technique, Action plan

docs.title: 1. Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction (EIB)
docs.text:
Definition: Transient airflow obstruction triggered by exercise

Mechanism: - Airway cooling and drying during exercise (Hyperventilation) - Triggers mast cell degranulation → Bronchoconstriction

Clinical: - Wheezing, Dyspnea, Cough during or 5-15 min after exercise - Resolves spontaneously within 30-60 min - Refractory period: 2-3 hours (Less responsive if exercise repeated)

Diagnosis: - Exercise challenge test: ↓ FEV₁ ≥10% post-exercise

Management: - Pre-treatment: SABA 15 min before exercise (Salbutamol 2-4 puffs) - Long-term: ICS (If frequent EIB) - LTRA: Montelukast (Effective alternative) - Warm-up before exercise

docs.title: 2. Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease (AERD)
docs.text:
Samter's Triad: 1. Asthma 2. Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps 3. Aspirin/NSAID sensitivity

Mechanism: - COX-1 inhibition → ↑ Leukotriene production → Bronchoconstriction

Clinical: - Bronchospasm within 30 min - 3 hours after aspirin/NSAID - Severe exacerbation possible

Management: - Avoid aspirin, NSAIDs - Safe alternatives: Paracetamol, Selective COX-2 inhibitors (Celecoxib - Usually safe) - LTRA: Montelukast (Beneficial) - Aspirin desensitization: If aspirin needed (e.g., Cardiac disease)

docs.title: 3. Occupational Asthma
docs.text:
Definition: Asthma caused by exposure to workplace sensitizers

Common Agents: - High molecular weight: Flour (Baker's asthma), Animal proteins, Latex - Low molecular weight: Isocyanates (Paints), Wood dust, Epoxy resins

Diagnosis: - Work-related symptoms: Improve on weekends/vacations - Serial PEFR monitoring: At work vs away from work

Management: - Avoid exposure (Primary) - Change job if avoidance not possible - Pharmacotherapy (ICS, Bronchodilators)

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Asthma Definition: Reversible airflow obstruction + Bronchial hyperresponsiveness + Inflammation

  2. Key Feature: Reversibility (vs COPD - partially reversible)

  3. Diagnosis:

  4. FEV₁/FVC <0.70 (Obstructive)

  5. Bronchodilator reversibility: FEV₁ ↑ ≥12% AND ≥200 ml post-SABA

  6. Most Common Chronic Disease in Children

  7. ICS (Inhaled Corticosteroids):

  8. Mainstay of treatment (Controller)
  9. All persistent asthma (Mild, Moderate, Severe)

  10. Side effects: Oral candidiasis, Dysphonia (Rinse mouth, Use spacer)

  11. LABA: NEVER as monotherapy (Always with ICS)

  12. SABA: As-needed reliever (Overuse >2 canisters/month → Poor control)

  13. Stepwise Management:

  14. Step 1: Intermittent - As-needed SABA
  15. Step 2: Mild persistent - Daily low-dose ICS
  16. Step 3: Moderate - ICS-LABA

  17. Step 4-5: Severe - High-dose ICS-LABA + Add-ons (Tiotropium, LTRA, Biologics)

  18. Acute Exacerbation Management:

  19. Oxygen: Target SpO₂ 93-95%
  20. SABA: Salbutamol nebulizer 2.5-5 mg every 20 min
  21. Ipratropium: Add if severe
  22. Systemic steroids: Prednisolone 40-50 mg (Within 1 hour)

  23. Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV: If severe, not responding

  24. Life-Threatening Features:

    • Silent chest (No wheeze - no air movement)
    • Cyanosis, Bradycardia
    • Exhaustion, Confusion
    • PaCO₂ >45 mmHg or rising

  25. Exercise-Induced Asthma:

    • Pre-treatment: SABA 15 min before exercise
    • Long-term: LTRA (Montelukast)

  26. Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease (AERD):

    • Samter's Triad: Asthma + Nasal polyps + Aspirin sensitivity
    • Avoid: Aspirin, NSAIDs
    • Safe: Paracetamol, Selective COX-2 inhibitors

  27. Biologics:

    • Omalizumab: Anti-IgE (Allergic asthma)
    • Mepolizumab, Benralizumab: Anti-IL-5 (Eosinophilic asthma)
    • Dupilumab: Anti-IL-4R (Type 2 asthma)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Recurrent wheezing, worse at night, FEV₁/FVC <0.70, FEV₁ ↑ 15% post-SABA → Asthma

Scenario 2: Mild persistent asthma (Symptoms 3x/week) → Start Daily low-dose ICS

Scenario 3: Severe asthma exacerbation, SpO₂ 88%, PEFR 30% → Oxygen + Salbutamol nebulizer + Ipratropium + Prednisolone 40 mg + Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV

Scenario 4: Asthma patient with silent chest, bradycardia, PaCO₂ 50 mmHg → Life-threatening → ICU, Consider intubation

Scenario 5: Wheezing 10 min after exercise, resolves in 30 min → Exercise-induced asthma → Pre-treat with SABA

Scenario 6: Asthma + Nasal polyps + Bronchospasm after aspirin → AERD (Samter's Triad)

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Asthma: Reversible airflow obstruction
  • Diagnosis: FEV₁/FVC <0.70, Reversibility ≥12% and ≥200 ml
  • ICS: Mainstay, All persistent asthma
  • LABA: Never alone, Always with ICS
  • Stepwise management: Step 1-5
  • Acute exacerbation: Oxygen, SABA, Ipratropium, Steroids, Magnesium
  • Life-threatening: Silent chest, Cyanosis, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg
  • Exercise-induced: Pre-treat SABA, LTRA
  • AERD: Samter's triad, Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs
  • Biologics: Omalizumab (Anti-IgE), Mepolizumab (Anti-IL-5)

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 25-year-old presents with recurrent episodes of wheezing and dyspnea, worse at night. Spirometry shows FEV₁/FVC 0.65. Post-bronchodilator FEV₁ increases by 18% and 250 ml. What is the diagnosis?

A) COPD B) Asthma C) Bronchiectasis D) Interstitial lung disease

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Asthma** **Explanation:** - **Obstructive pattern:** FEV₁/FVC <0.70 - **Significant reversibility:** FEV₁ ↑ 18% AND 250 ml (Both criteria met: ≥12% AND ≥200 ml) - **Confirms REVERSIBLE airflow obstruction** → **Asthma** **Why not COPD:** - COPD has **partial reversibility** (<12% or <200 ml) - Usually in older patients with smoking history

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Which medication should NEVER be used as monotherapy in asthma?

A) Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) B) Long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) C) Short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA) D) Leukotriene receptor antagonist (LTRA)

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA)** **Explanation:** - **LABA as monotherapy** is associated with **increased asthma-related deaths** (SMART trial) - **ALWAYS use LABA with ICS** (Combination inhaler) **Mechanism:** - LABA provides bronchodilation but does NOT treat underlying inflammation - Masks symptoms while inflammation worsens **All other options can be used as monotherapy:** - ICS: Mainstay controller - SABA: As-needed reliever - LTRA: Alternative controller

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with severe asthma exacerbation is not responding to nebulized salbutamol and ipratropium. SpO₂ is 89%, PEFR 35%. What is the next most appropriate medication?

A) Oral prednisolone B) IV magnesium sulfate 2 g C) Oral montelukast D) IV aminophylline

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) IV magnesium sulfate 2 g** **Explanation:** - **Severe exacerbation** not responding to initial bronchodilators - **Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV** over 20 min is indicated - **Mechanism:** Bronchodilation (Ca²⁺ channel blocker) - **Evidence:** ↓ Hospital admissions in severe asthma **Note:** - **Oral prednisolone** should have already been given (Within 1 hour of presentation) - If not, give it NOW as well - Montelukast: Not for acute management - Aminophylline: Second-line (Not preferred - narrow therapeutic window, side effects)

End of Asthma Study Material

Next Topic: - COPD

Study Tips: 1. Master bronchodilator reversibility criteria (≥12% AND ≥200 ml) 2. Know stepwise management (GINA guidelines) 3. Remember ICS side effects and prevention 4. Understand acute exacerbation management (Very high-yield) 5. Learn life-threatening features (Silent chest, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg) 6. Know when to use magnesium sulfate

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (High) Estimated Study Time: 3-4 hours Revision Time: 1-1.5 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease) - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Respiratory

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition & Overview
  2. Pathophysiology
  3. Clinical Features
  4. Diagnosis
  5. Classification & Severity
  6. Management
  7. Acute Exacerbation
  8. Complications
  9. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  10. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) is a preventable and treatable disease characterized by persistent respiratory symptoms and airflow limitation that is not fully reversible, due to airway and/or alveolar abnormalities, usually caused by significant exposure to noxious particles or gases.

Key Features: - Chronic airflow limitation (FEV₁/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator) - Not fully reversible (vs Asthma - reversible) - Progressive - Preventable (Smoking cessation)

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• 4th leading cause of death worldwide
  • Smoking: 80-90% of cases
  • Prevalence increases with age (>40 years)
  • More common in men (Historically - gap narrowing due to ↑ women smoking)

docs.title: Umbrella Term
docs.text:
COPD includes: 1. Chronic Bronchitis (Clinical diagnosis) 2. Emphysema (Pathological diagnosis)

Most patients have BOTH

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
"COPD RISKS" 

C - Cigarette smoking (MOST IMPORTANT - 80-90%)
O - Occupational exposures (Coal dust, Silica, Cadmium)
P - Pollution (Biomass fuel, Air pollution)
D - Deficiency (α₁-Antitrypsin) - Genetic

R - Recurrent respiratory infections (Childhood)
I - Indoor air pollution (Biomass fuels - Developing countries)
S - Socioeconomic status (Poverty)
K - Kyphoscoliosis (Restrictive, but can coexist)
S - Second-hand smoke

Smoking: - Dose-dependent: Pack-years (Packs/day × Years) - Most important modifiable risk factor

docs.title: Two Main Components
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         COPD PATHOLOGY                              │
├──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┤
│ CHRONIC          │ • Airway inflammation            │
│ BRONCHITIS       │ • Mucus hypersecretion           │
│ (Clinical)       │ • Small airway narrowing         │
│                  │ • Bronchial wall thickening      │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│ EMPHYSEMA        │ • Alveolar destruction           │
│ (Pathological)   │ • Loss of elastic recoil         │
│                  │ • Air trapping                   │
│                  │ • Hyperinflation                 │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 1. Chronic Bronchitis
docs.text:
Clinical Definition: "Productive cough for ≥3 months per year for ≥2 consecutive years" (after excluding other causes)

Pathology: - Hypertrophy of mucus-secreting glands (Reid index >0.4) - Reid index = Gland thickness / Bronchial wall thickness (Normal <0.4) - Goblet cell hyperplasia - Chronic inflammation (Neutrophils, Macrophages) - Small airway narrowing, fibrosis

Phenotype: "Blue Bloater" - Cyanosis (Chronic hypoxemia) - Edema (Cor pulmonale) - Overweight - ↑ PaCO₂ (Hypercapnia)

docs.title: 2. Emphysema
docs.text:
Pathological Definition: "Abnormal permanent enlargement of airspaces distal to terminal bronchioles, with destruction of alveolar walls, without obvious fibrosis"

Types (Based on Location):

Centriacinar (Centrilobular): - Most common (95%) - Smoking-related - Upper lobes predominantly - Respiratory bronchioles affected

Panacinar (Panlobular): - α₁-Antitrypsin deficiency - Lower lobes predominantly - Entire acinus affected

Paraseptal: - Subpleural (Periphery) - Associated with pneumothorax (Bullae rupture)

Pathology: - Alveolar wall destruction (Protease-antiprotease imbalance) - Loss of elastic recoil → Air trapping - ↓ Diffusing capacity (DLCO)

Phenotype: "Pink Puffer" - Pink (Normal O₂ saturation - compensated) - Pursed-lip breathing (Prevent airway collapse) - Cachexia (Weight loss - ↑ Work of breathing) - Barrel chest (Hyperinflation) - Normal/Low PaCO₂ (Hyperventilation to maintain O₂)

docs.title: Protease-Antiprotease Imbalance (Emphysema)
docs.text:
Normal: - Balance between proteases (Elastase, Proteinase) and antiproteases (α₁-Antitrypsin)

Emphysema: 

Cigarette Smoke / α₁-AT Deficiency
        ↓
↑ Neutrophils, Macrophages
        ↓
↑ Elastase, Proteases
        ↓
Overwhelm antiproteases (↓ α₁-Antitrypsin)
        ↓
Alveolar Wall Destruction
        ↓
EMPHYSEMA

docs.title: α₁-Antitrypsin Deficiency
docs.text:
Genetics: - Autosomal codominant - Pi phenotype (Protease inhibitor) - PiMM: Normal (Most common) - PiZZ: Severe deficiency (<10-15% normal) - Homozygous - PiMZ: Heterozygous (Mild deficiency)

Clinical Features: - Early-onset emphysema (<40 years, especially smokers) - Panacinar pattern, Lower lobe predominance - Liver cirrhosis (Protein accumulation in hepatocytes)

Diagnosis: - Low serum α₁-Antitrypsin level (<50 mg/dL - severe) - Pi phenotyping or Genetic testing

Management: - Smoking cessation (MOST IMPORTANT) - α₁-Antitrypsin augmentation therapy (IV infusions) - If severe deficiency + established emphysema - Lung transplant (End-stage)

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Cardinal Symptoms: 1. Dyspnea (Progressive, Exertional → At rest) - Most common presenting symptom - Chronic, Progressive 2. Chronic cough (Productive - "Smoker's cough") - May precede dyspnea by years 3. Sputum production (Mucoid, Chronic) - Purulent during exacerbations

Additional: - Wheezing (Less prominent than asthma) - Chest tightness - Fatigue, Weight loss (Advanced disease)

Symptoms worse in: - Early morning (Mucus accumulation overnight) - Cold weather - Exacerbations

docs.title: Physical Examination
docs.text:
Inspection: - Barrel chest (Hyperinflation - AP = Transverse diameter) - Pursed-lip breathing (Prevent airway collapse) - Use of accessory muscles (Sternocleidomastoid, Scalene) - Tripod position (Sitting, leaning forward) - Cyanosis (Chronic hypoxemia - "Blue bloater") - Cachexia (Advanced disease - "Pink puffer") - Clubbing: NOT typical (If present, think lung cancer complication)

Palpation: - ↓ Chest expansion - ↓ Tactile fremitus (Hyperinflation)

Percussion: - Hyperresonant (Hyperinflation) - ↓ Liver dullness (Diaphragm pushed down)

Auscultation: - ↓ Breath sounds (Air trapping) - Prolonged expiration (Airflow obstruction) - Wheeze (Expiratory, polyphonic) - Crackles (If bronchitis or infection)

Cor Pulmonale (Right Heart Failure): - Elevated JVP - RV heave (Parasternal lift) - Loud P2 (Pulmonary hypertension) - Hepatomegaly, Peripheral edema, Ascites

docs.title: Spirometry - GOLD STANDARD
docs.text:
Essential for Diagnosis

Criteria: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      COPD DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (GOLD)               │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ POST-BRONCHODILATOR FEV₁/FVC <0.70                 │
│                                                    │
│ • Confirms PERSISTENT airflow limitation           │
│ • NOT fully reversible (vs Asthma)                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Point: Post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC <0.70 (Confirms irreversible obstruction)

Obstructive Pattern: - FEV₁/FVC <0.70 (Ratio reduced) - ↓ FEV₁ (Severity) - Normal or ↓ FVC

Bronchodilator Response: - Minimal reversibility (<12% or <200 ml FEV₁ improvement) - Differentiates from asthma (Asthma: ≥12% AND ≥200 ml)

docs.title: Other Pulmonary Function Tests
docs.text:
Lung Volumes: - ↑ Total Lung Capacity (TLC) (Hyperinflation) - ↑ Residual Volume (RV) (Air trapping) - ↑ Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) - ↑ RV/TLC ratio (>35%) - Air trapping

Diffusing Capacity (DLCO): - ↓ in Emphysema (Alveolar destruction) - Normal in Chronic Bronchitis

docs.title: Chest X-ray
docs.text:
Findings:

Hyperinflation: - Flattened diaphragm (<1.5 cm height) - ↑ Retrosternal airspace (Lateral view - >4.5 cm) - Narrow cardiac silhouette ("Tear-drop" heart) - ↓ Vascular markings (Emphysema)

Chronic Bronchitis: - Increased lung markings ("Dirty lungs") - Cardiomegaly (If cor pulmonale)

Bullae: Large airspaces (>1 cm) - Emphysema

Uses: - Rule out complications: Pneumonia, Pneumothorax, Lung cancer - Not diagnostic for COPD (Spirometry is)

docs.title: ABG (Arterial Blood Gas)
docs.text:
Indications: - Severe COPD (FEV₁ <50%) - Acute exacerbation - Suspected respiratory failure

Findings:

Chronic Bronchitis ("Blue Bloater"): - Hypoxemia: ↓ PaO₂ (<60 mmHg) - Hypercapnia: ↑ PaCO₂ (>45 mmHg) - Chronic CO₂ retention - Compensated respiratory acidosis: pH normal (↑ HCO₃⁻ - Renal compensation)

Emphysema ("Pink Puffer"): - Normal/Mild hypoxemia (Initially) - Normal/Low PaCO₂ (Hyperventilation)

Acute Exacerbation: - Uncompensated respiratory acidosis: ↓ pH, ↑ PaCO₂

docs.title: Additional Investigations
docs.text:
ECG: - Cor Pulmonale: RVH, P pulmonale (Tall P in II, III, aVF), RAD, RBBB

Echocardiography: - Pulmonary hypertension (PA systolic pressure) - RV function, Tricuspid regurgitation

6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT): - Assess functional capacity - Prognostic: <350 m → Poor prognosis

α₁-Antitrypsin Level: - If early-onset COPD (<40 years), Minimal smoking, or Family history

docs.title: GOLD ABCD Assessment Tool
docs.text:
Combines: 1. Symptoms (mMRC dyspnea scale or CAT score) 2. Exacerbation history

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      GOLD ABCD ASSESSMENT                              │
├───────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ GROUP │  CRITERIA                                      │
├───────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  A    │ • Low symptoms (mMRC 0-1 or CAT <10)           │
│       │ • Low exacerbation risk (0-1 exacerbations/yr, │
│       │   No hospitalizations)                         │
├───────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  B    │ • High symptoms (mMRC ≥2 or CAT ≥10)           │
│       │ • Low exacerbation risk (0-1 exacerbations/yr) │
├───────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  C    │ • Low symptoms (mMRC 0-1 or CAT <10)           │
│       │ • High exacerbation risk (≥2 exacerbations or  │
│       │   ≥1 hospitalization)                          │
├───────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  D    │ • High symptoms (mMRC ≥2 or CAT ≥10)           │
│       │ • High exacerbation risk (≥2 exacerbations or  │
│       │   ≥1 hospitalization)                          │
└───────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

mMRC (Modified Medical Research Council) Dyspnea Scale: - 0: Dyspnea only with strenuous exercise - 1: Dyspnea when hurrying or walking up a slight hill - 2: Walks slower than people of same age due to dyspnea, or stops for breath when walking at own pace - 3: Stops for breath after walking ~100 m - 4: Too dyspneic to leave house, or dyspneic with dressing

CAT (COPD Assessment Test): - Questionnaire (0-40 points) - <10: Low symptoms - ≥10: High symptoms

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
1. Relieve symptoms
  2. Prevent disease progression
  3. ↓ Exacerbations
  4. Improve exercise tolerance and quality of life
  5. Reduce mortality

docs.title: 1. Smoking Cessation - MOST IMPORTANT
docs.text:
ONLY intervention that slows disease progression

All smokers with COPD should be counseled to quit

Methods: - Behavioral counseling - Nicotine replacement therapy (NRT - Gum, Patch, Lozenge) - Pharmacotherapy: - Varenicline (Champix) - Partial nicotine receptor agonist (Most effective) - Bupropion (Antidepressant)

Benefits: - ↓ Rate of FEV₁ decline - ↓ Exacerbations - ↓ Mortality

docs.title: 2. Vaccinations
docs.text:
Influenza Vaccine: - Annual (All COPD patients) - ↓ Exacerbations, ↓ Hospitalizations

Pneumococcal Vaccine: - PCV13 (Conjugate) + PPSV23 (Polysaccharide) - All COPD patients ≥65 years or younger with significant comorbidities

COVID-19 Vaccine: - Recommended

docs.title: 3. Pulmonary Rehabilitation
docs.text:
Components: - Exercise training (Aerobic, Strength) - Education - Nutritional counseling - Psychosocial support

Indications: - All symptomatic COPD (Group B, C, D)

Benefits: - ↑ Exercise tolerance - ↓ Dyspnea, ↑ Quality of life - ↓ Hospitalizations - Does NOT improve FEV₁ or mortality

docs.title: 4. Oxygen Therapy (Long-Term Oxygen Therapy - LTOT)
docs.text:
Indications: - PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg (or SpO₂ ≤88%) at rest, OR - PaO₂ 56-59 mmHg with: - Evidence of cor pulmonale (Edema, P pulmonale on ECG) - OR Polycythemia (Hct >55%)

Prescription: - ≥15 hours/day (More is better) - Flow rate: To achieve SpO₂ 88-92%

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality (ONLY intervention proven in COPD besides smoking cessation) - ↓ Polycythemia - ↓ Pulmonary hypertension - ↑ Exercise tolerance

AVOID high-flow oxygen (Target SpO₂ 88-92%, NOT >92%): - Risk of CO₂ retention (Hypercapnia) - Loss of hypoxic drive

docs.title: 5. Surgical Options
docs.text:
Lung Volume Reduction Surgery (LVRS): - Resection of emphysematous tissue (Upper lobe) - Indications: - Severe emphysema (FEV₁ <45%) - Hyperinflation with poor exercise capacity - Upper lobe predominant disease - Benefits: ↑ FEV₁, ↑ Exercise tolerance, ↓ Mortality (Selected patients)

Lung Transplant: - End-stage COPD (FEV₁ <25%, Severe dyspnea) - Age <65 years

Bullectomy: - Large bullae compressing surrounding lung

docs.title: 1. Short-Acting Bronchodilators - RELIEVERS
docs.text:
Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (SABA): - Salbutamol, Levosalbutamol - Mechanism: Bronchodilation (Onset 5 min, Duration 4-6 hr) - Use: As-needed for acute symptoms

Short-Acting Muscarinic Antagonists (SAMA): - Ipratropium bromide - Mechanism: Block M3 receptors → Bronchodilation (Onset 30 min, Duration 6-8 hr) - Use: As-needed or regular (QID)

Combination: Salbutamol + Ipratropium (Combivent)

docs.title: 2. Long-Acting Bronchodilators - MAINTENANCE (Mainstay)
docs.text:
Long-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (LABA): - Formoterol, Salmeterol, Indacaterol, Olodaterol, Vilanterol - Duration: 12-24 hours - Benefits: ↑ FEV₁, ↓ Dyspnea, ↑ Quality of life, ↓ Exacerbations

Long-Acting Muscarinic Antagonists (LAMA): - Tiotropium, Glycopyrronium, Umeclidinium, Aclidinium - Duration: 12-24 hours - Benefits: ↑ FEV₁, ↓ Dyspnea, ↓ Exacerbations (More effective than LABA)

LAMA vs LABA: - LAMA slightly more effective in ↓ exacerbations - LAMA preferred as initial monotherapy (Group C)

Dual Bronchodilation (LABA + LAMA): - Examples: Indacaterol/Glycopyrronium, Vilanterol/Umeclidinium - Better than monotherapy (↑ FEV₁, ↓ Exacerbations) - First-line for Group B, D

docs.title: 3. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY
docs.text:
Examples: - Budesonide, Fluticasone, Beclomethasone

Indications: - NOT first-line in COPD (Unlike asthma) - Add to LABA in: - Frequent exacerbations despite LABA/LAMA - Eosinophilia (Blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL) - Asthma-COPD overlap

ICS/LABA Combinations: - Budesonide/Formoterol, Fluticasone/Salmeterol, Fluticasone/Vilanterol

Side Effects: - Pneumonia (↑ Risk - 30-70%) - Oral candidiasis, Dysphonia - Osteoporosis (Long-term)

Triple Therapy (ICS + LABA + LAMA): - Group D with eosinophils ≥100 - Severe COPD with frequent exacerbations - Single inhaler: Fluticasone/Umeclidinium/Vilanterol (Trelegy)

docs.title: 4. Phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE-4) Inhibitors
docs.text:
Roflumilast: - Oral anti-inflammatory - Indications: - Severe COPD (FEV₁ <50%) - Chronic bronchitis phenotype - Frequent exacerbations despite LABA/LAMA or ICS/LABA

Benefits: - ↓ Exacerbations (Moderate effect) - Small ↑ FEV₁

Side Effects: - Diarrhea, Nausea, Weight loss (Common) - Psychiatric effects (Depression)

docs.title: 5. Theophylline
docs.text:
Mechanism: Bronchodilation (PDE inhibitor, Adenosine antagonist)

Role in COPD: - Third-line (If inadequate response to LABA/LAMA) - Limited use (Narrow therapeutic window, Side effects, Drug interactions)

Side Effects: - Nausea, Arrhythmias, Seizures (Toxicity)

Monitoring: Serum levels (Therapeutic: 10-20 μg/ml)

docs.title: 6. Mucolytics
docs.text: N-Acetylcysteine, Carbocysteine: - Mechanism: Breaks disulfide bonds in mucus - Controversial benefit - May ↓ Exacerbations in selected patients (Chronic bronchitis, frequent exacerbations)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Acute worsening of respiratory symptoms beyond normal day-to-day variation, requiring change in medication.

docs.title: Triggers
docs.text:
"ICE" - Most Common: 

I - Infection (50-80%) - Viral >> Bacterial
    • Viral: Rhinovirus, Influenza, RSV
    • Bacterial: H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, M. catarrhalis
C - Cardiac (MI, CHF)
E - Environmental (Air pollution, Cold weather)

Other: - Non-adherence to medications - Pulmonary embolism - Pneumothorax

30%: No identifiable cause

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
