Symptoms: - ↑ Dyspnea (Cardinal symptom) - ↑ Sputum volume - ↑ Sputum purulence (Yellow/Green) - Cough - Wheeze, Chest tightness

Anthonisen Criteria (For Bacterial Infection): 1. ↑ Dyspnea 2. ↑ Sputum volume 3. ↑ Sputum purulence

Type I: All 3 → Antibiotics indicated Type II: 2 of 3 → Antibiotics usually given Type III: 1 of 3 + URTI → Antibiotics may not be needed

docs.title: Severity Assessment
docs.text:
Mild: - Managed outpatient - ↑ SABA/SAMA use

Moderate: - Outpatient or hospitalization - Antibiotics, Steroids

Severe: - Hospitalization - Respiratory failure, ICU

docs.title: Management of Acute Exacerbation
docs.text:
"ABCDEFG" 

A - Assess severity (ABG, RR, HR, Mentation)
B - Bronchodilators (SABA + SAMA)
C - Corticosteroids (Systemic)
D - Deliver oxygen (Controlled)
E - Antibiotics (if indicated)
F - Fluids, Nutrition
G - Get ready for ventilation (if needed)

docs.title: 1. Oxygen Therapy - CONTROLLED
docs.text:
Target: SpO₂ 88-92% (NOT >92%)

Why controlled: - Type 2 respiratory failure in COPD (Chronic CO₂ retainers) - Hypoxic drive: If give high O₂ → Loss of hypoxic respiratory drive → ↑ CO₂ retention → Respiratory acidosis, CO₂ narcosis

Delivery: - Venturi mask (Fixed FiO₂ - Preferred) - Start 24-28% O₂ - Nasal cannula: 1-2 L/min

Monitor: ABG (15-30 min after starting O₂)

docs.title: 2. Bronchodilators
docs.text:
Nebulized: - Salbutamol 2.5-5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg every 4-6 hours (Or more frequent if severe)

Better than MDI in acute setting (Ease of use, Delivery in distressed patient)

docs.title: 3. Systemic Corticosteroids
docs.text:
Indication: All exacerbations requiring hospitalization

Regimen: - Prednisolone 40 mg PO daily × 5 days - OR IV Methylprednisolone if unable to take oral

Benefits: - ↓ Recovery time - ↑ Lung function - ↓ Treatment failure - ↓ Length of stay

Duration: 5 days (Longer courses NO additional benefit)

docs.title: 4. Antibiotics
docs.text:
Indications: - Purulent sputum (Type I or II exacerbation - Anthonisen criteria) - Mechanically ventilated

Not needed if: Likely viral (Watery sputum, URTI symptoms)

Choice: - First-line: - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625 mg TID × 5-7 days - Doxycycline 100 mg BD × 5-7 days - Azithromycin 500 mg OD × 3-5 days

  • Second-line (Risk factors - Previous exacerbations, Severe COPD):
  • Respiratory fluoroquinolone (Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin)
  • Cephalosporins (Ceftriaxone)

Duration: 5-7 days

docs.title: 5. Ventilatory Support
docs.text:
Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV) - BiPAP:

Indications: - Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg) - Despite optimal medical therapy

Contraindications: - Respiratory arrest - Hemodynamic instability - Impaired consciousness (GCS <8) - Facial trauma - Unable to protect airway

Benefits: - ↓ Intubation rate - ↓ Mortality - ↓ Length of stay

Settings: IPAP 10-15 cm H₂O, EPAP 4-5 cm H₂O (Titrate)

Invasive Mechanical Ventilation:

Indications: - Respiratory arrest - Failed NIV - Severe acidosis (pH <7.25) - Severe hypoxemia despite O₂ - Impaired consciousness

Complications: Prolonged weaning, Ventilator-associated pneumonia

docs.title: 1. Respiratory Failure
docs.text: Type 2 (Hypercapnic) Respiratory Failure: - Most common in COPD - PaO₂ <60 mmHg, PaCO₂ >50 mmHg - Chronic CO₂ retention → Compensated (↑ HCO₃⁻) - Acute on chronic → Decompensated (↓ pH)

docs.title: 2. Cor Pulmonale (Right Heart Failure)
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: 

Chronic Hypoxemia
        ↓
Hypoxic Pulmonary Vasoconstriction
        ↓
Pulmonary Hypertension
        ↓
RV Pressure Overload
        ↓
RV Hypertrophy → RV Failure
        ↓
COR PULMONALE

Clinical Features: - Elevated JVP - RV heave (Parasternal lift) - Loud P2 (Pulmonary HTN) - Hepatomegaly (Pulsatile), Peripheral edema, Ascites - Tricuspid regurgitation murmur

Management: - LTOT (Most important) - Diuretics (Edema) - Treat underlying COPD

docs.title: 3. Polycythemia
docs.text:
Mechanism: Chronic hypoxemia → ↑ EPO → ↑ RBC production

Complications: ↑ Blood viscosity → Thrombosis

Management: LTOT

docs.title: 4. Pneumothorax
docs.text:
Spontaneous: Rupture of bullae (Emphysema)

High index of suspicion if sudden worsening of dyspnea

docs.title: 5. Pneumonia
docs.text: Common due to impaired clearance, Smoking

docs.title: 6. Lung Cancer
docs.text:
Smoking is common risk factor for both COPD and lung cancer

Higher incidence in COPD patients

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. COPD Definition: Persistent airflow limitation, NOT fully reversible, Progressive

  2. Diagnosis: Post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC <0.70

  3. Most Important Risk Factor: Cigarette smoking (80-90%)

  4. Most Important Intervention: Smoking cessation (Only one that slows progression)

  5. Chronic Bronchitis Definition: Productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 years

  6. Emphysema Definition: Alveolar destruction, Loss of elastic recoil

  7. Blue Bloater vs Pink Puffer:

  8. Blue Bloater: Chronic bronchitis, Cyanosis, Edema, ↑ PaCO₂

  9. Pink Puffer: Emphysema, Pursed-lip breathing, Cachexia, Normal/↓ PaCO₂

  10. α₁-Antitrypsin Deficiency:

  11. PiZZ genotype
  12. Early-onset emphysema (<40 years)
  13. Panacinar, Lower lobe predominance

  14. Liver cirrhosis

  15. GOLD Severity:

  16. GOLD 1: FEV₁ ≥80%
  17. GOLD 2: FEV₁ 50-79%
  18. GOLD 3: FEV₁ 30-49%

  19. GOLD 4: FEV₁ <30%

  20. Mainstay of Treatment: Long-acting bronchodilators (LABA, LAMA)

  21. ICS in COPD:

    • NOT first-line (Unlike asthma)
    • Add if frequent exacerbations or eosinophilia (≥300)
    • Side effect: ↑ Pneumonia risk

  22. LTOT (Long-Term Oxygen Therapy):

    • Indications: PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg OR PaO₂ 56-59 mmHg + Cor pulmonale/Polycythemia
    • ≥15 hours/day
    • ↓ Mortality (Only pharmacological intervention besides smoking cessation)

  23. Oxygen in COPD Exacerbation:

    • Controlled: Target SpO₂ 88-92% (NOT >92%)
    • Avoid high O₂: Risk of CO₂ retention

  24. Acute Exacerbation Management:

    • Oxygen (Controlled)
    • Bronchodilators: SABA + SAMA (Nebulized)
    • Steroids: Prednisolone 40 mg × 5 days
    • Antibiotics: If purulent sputum (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate, Doxycycline)

  25. NIV (BiPAP) Indications:

    • Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg)
    • ↓ Intubation, ↓ Mortality

  26. Cor Pulmonale: RV failure from chronic pulmonary HTN (Chronic hypoxemia)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Chronic smoker, productive cough, post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC 0.65 → COPD

Scenario 2: COPD patient, best intervention to slow progression → Smoking cessation

Scenario 3: COPD patient with PaO₂ 52 mmHg, cor pulmonale → Start LTOT (≥15 hr/day)

Scenario 4: COPD exacerbation, give oxygen, target SpO₂ → 88-92% (NOT >92%)

Scenario 5: COPD exacerbation, purulent sputum → Amoxicillin-Clavulanate + Prednisolone

Scenario 6: COPD exacerbation, pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 55 mmHg → NIV (BiPAP)

Scenario 7: Young patient (<40y), emphysema, lower lobes, liver cirrhosis → α₁-Antitrypsin deficiency

Scenario 8: COPD, Blue bloater phenotype → Chronic bronchitis (Cyanosis, Edema, ↑ PaCO₂)

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• COPD: Post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC <0.70
  • Smoking: Most important risk factor and intervention (cessation)
  • Chronic bronchitis: Productive cough ≥3 mo/yr × 2 yr
  • Blue bloater vs Pink puffer
  • α₁-Antitrypsin deficiency: Early, Panacinar, Lower lobe, Liver cirrhosis
  • GOLD grades: 1-4 based on FEV₁
  • Treatment: LABA/LAMA (Mainstay), ICS (NOT first-line)
  • LTOT: PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg, ≥15 hr/day, ↓ Mortality
  • Oxygen target: SpO₂ 88-92%
  • Exacerbation: Bronchodilators, Steroids (5 days), Antibiotics (if purulent)
  • NIV: pH <7.35, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg
  • Cor pulmonale: RV failure from pulmonary HTN

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 60-year-old chronic smoker has dyspnea and chronic productive cough. Spirometry shows FEV₁ 55% predicted, post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC 0.62. What is the diagnosis and GOLD grade?

A) Asthma, Moderate B) COPD, GOLD 2 C) COPD, GOLD 3 D) Bronchiectasis

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) COPD, GOLD 2** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** COPD (Post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC <0.70) - **GOLD Grade:** - FEV₁ 55% predicted - **GOLD 2 (Moderate):** 50% ≤ FEV₁ <80% **Why not others:** - Asthma: Would have reversibility (≥12% AND ≥200 ml) - GOLD 3: FEV₁ 30-49% - Bronchiectasis: Different clinical picture (purulent sputum, imaging findings)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
What is the ONLY intervention proven to slow disease progression in COPD?

A) Inhaled corticosteroids B) Long-acting bronchodilators C) Smoking cessation D) Long-term oxygen therapy

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Smoking cessation** **Explanation:** - **Smoking cessation** is the **ONLY intervention** that **slows the rate of FEV₁ decline** in COPD - **Most important** intervention (Both prevention and treatment) **Other interventions:** - ICS, LABA/LAMA: Improve symptoms, ↓ exacerbations (Do NOT slow disease progression) - LTOT: ↓ Mortality (Does NOT slow FEV₁ decline)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A COPD patient has PaO₂ 52 mmHg at rest with evidence of cor pulmonale. What is the most appropriate management?

A) Inhaled corticosteroids B) Long-term oxygen therapy ≥15 hours/day C) Pulmonary rehabilitation D) Lung transplant

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Long-term oxygen therapy ≥15 hours/day** **Explanation:** - **LTOT Indications:** - **PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg** at rest (Patient has 52 mmHg) ✓ - OR PaO₂ 56-59 mmHg + Cor pulmonale or Polycythemia ✓ - **Prescription:** ≥15 hours/day (More is better - Aim for 24 hr) - **Target:** SpO₂ 88-92% **Benefits:** - **↓ Mortality** (ONLY pharmacological intervention with this benefit besides smoking cessation) - ↓ Pulmonary HTN - ↓ Polycythemia

End of COPD Study Material

Next Topics: - Pneumonia - Tuberculosis - (Continue with remaining respiratory topics)

Study Tips: 1. Master post-bronchodilator FEV₁/FVC <0.70 (Diagnostic) 2. Understand Blue Bloater vs Pink Puffer 3. Know LTOT indications (Very high-yield) 4. Remember oxygen target 88-92% in COPD 5. Learn acute exacerbation management 6. Know α₁-Antitrypsin deficiency features

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 4-5 hours Revision Time: 1.5-2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Pneumonia - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Respiratory

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview & Classification
  2. Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)
  3. Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP)
  4. Aspiration Pneumonia
  5. Atypical Pneumonia
  6. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  7. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Pneumonia is acute infection of the pulmonary parenchyma (alveoli) characterized by consolidation.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
By Acquisition: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. COMMUNITY-ACQUIRED (CAP)                        │
│    • Most common                                   │
│    • Onset outside hospital or <48h admission      │
│                                                    │
│ 2. HOSPITAL-ACQUIRED (HAP)                         │
│    • ≥48 hours after admission                     │
│    • Includes Ventilator-Associated (VAP)          │
│                                                    │
│ 3. ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA                            │
│    • Inhalation of gastric/oropharyngeal contents  │
│                                                    │
│ 4. IMMUNOCOMPROMISED                               │
│    • HIV, Transplant, Chemotherapy                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

By Pathology: - Lobar: Entire lobe (S. pneumoniae) - Bronchopneumonia: Patchy, multiple lobes (S. aureus, Gram-negative) - Interstitial: Alveolar walls (Atypical - Mycoplasma, Viruses)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Pneumonia acquired outside hospital or <48 hours after admission.

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Leading infectious cause of death worldwide
  • All ages, more severe in elderly, immunocompromised

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Most Common Organism OVERALL: - Streptococcus pneumoniae (Pneumococcus) - 30-50%

Other Bacteria: - Haemophilus influenzae (COPD, Smokers) - Staphylococcus aureus (Post-influenza, IVDU) - Mycoplasma pneumoniae (Young adults, Atypical) - Chlamydophila pneumoniae (Elderly, Atypical) - Legionella pneumophila (Contaminated water, Atypical) - Klebsiella pneumoniae (Alcoholics, Diabetics - "Currant jelly" sputum) - Moraxella catarrhalis (COPD)

Viral: - Influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19)

Fungal/Other: - Pneumocystis jirovecii (HIV with CD4 <200)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Classic (Typical) Pneumonia:

Symptoms: - Fever, Rigors (High-grade fever, Shaking chills) - Productive cough (Purulent sputum) - "Rusty" sputum: S. pneumoniae - "Currant jelly" sputum: Klebsiella - Pleuritic chest pain (Sharp, worse with breathing) - Dyspnea

Signs: - Tachypnea, Tachycardia - Consolidation: - Inspection: ↓ Chest expansion (affected side) - Palpation: ↑ Tactile fremitus - Percussion: Dull - Auscultation: - Bronchial breath sounds (High-pitched, Tubular) - Crackles (Coarse, Inspiratory) - Bronchophony, Egophony, Whispered pectoriloquy - Cyanosis (Severe)

Atypical Pneumonia: (See separate section)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Clinical + Radiological + Microbiological

docs.title: 1. Chest X-ray - ESSENTIAL
docs.text:
Findings: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      CHEST X-RAY IN PNEUMONIA                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Lobar consolidation (Air-space opacity)          │
│   - Homogeneous opacity                            │
│   - Air bronchograms (Air-filled bronchi visible)  │
│ • Patchy/Multifocal opacities (Bronchopneumonia)   │
│ • Pleural effusion (Parapneumonic)                 │
│ • Cavitation (S. aureus, Klebsiella, Anaerobes)    │
│ • Interstitial pattern (Atypical)                  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Patterns by Organism: - S. pneumoniae: Lobar consolidation - Klebsiella: Upper lobe, Bulging fissure (Heavy consolidation) - S. aureus: Cavitation, Pneumatoceles - Mycoplasma: Interstitial, Reticulonodular

docs.title: 2. Sputum Examination
docs.text:
Gram Stain + Culture: - Quality: >25 PMNs, <10 epithelial cells per LPF (Valid sample) - S. pneumoniae: Gram-positive diplococci (Lancet-shaped) - H. influenzae: Gram-negative coccobacilli - S. aureus: Gram-positive cocci in clusters - Klebsiella: Gram-negative bacilli (Mucoid)

Low Yield if: - Already on antibiotics - Atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Legionella - don't grow on standard culture)

docs.title: 3. Blood Cultures
docs.text:
Indications: - Severe CAP (ICU, Septic shock) - Before antibiotics (If not delaying treatment)

Positive: 5-15% of CAP cases

docs.title: 4. Urine Antigen Tests
docs.text:
Streptococcus pneumoniae: - Binax NOW (Rapid antigen test) - High sensitivity/specificity - Positive even after antibiotics started

Legionella pneumophila: - Urine antigen (Detects serogroup 1 - 80% of cases)

docs.title: 5. Laboratory Tests
docs.text:
CBC: - Leukocytosis with left shift (Bacterial) - >15,000/μL: Suggests bacterial - <4,000 or >30,000: Poor prognosis - Normal/Low WBC: Viral, Atypical, Immunocompromised

Inflammatory Markers: - CRP: Elevated (↑ In bacterial, Less in viral/atypical) - Procalcitonin: ↑ In bacterial (Guide antibiotic therapy)

ABG: - Hypoxemia (PaO₂ <60 mmHg or SpO₂ <90%) - Assess severity

Renal, LFTs: Assess complications

docs.title: 6. Thoracentesis
docs.text: If significant pleural effusion (>10 mm on lateral decubitus) - Parapneumonic effusion (Exudate - meets Light's criteria) - Empyema: Pus (pH <7.2, Glucose <40 mg/dL, LDH >1000) - Complicated: Needs drainage

docs.title: CURB-65 Score (Outpatient vs Inpatient Decision)
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CURB-65 SCORE                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ C - Confusion (New onset)                     1    │
│ U - Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >20 mg/dL)            1    │
│ R - Respiratory rate ≥30/min                  1    │
│ B - Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)       1    │
│ 65 - Age ≥65 years                            1    │
│                                                    │
│ Total: 0-5                                         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

INTERPRETATION:
• Score 0-1: Outpatient treatment (Mortality <3%)
• Score 2: Consider hospitalization (Mortality 9%)
• Score ≥3: Hospitalization (Severe - Mortality 15-40%)
  - Consider ICU

Simplified CRB-65: (Without Urea - Outpatient setting) - Same, but exclude Urea - Score ≥1: Consider hospitalization

docs.title: Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI/PORT Score)
docs.text: More complex (20 variables - Age, Comorbidities, Vitals, Labs) - Classes I-V - Class I-II: Outpatient - Class III: Inpatient brief stay or observation - Class IV-V: Hospitalization

docs.title: SMART-COP (ICU Admission)
docs.text: Severe CAP requiring ICU if score ≥3: - **S**ystolic BP <90 mmHg (2 points) - **M**ultilobar involvement on CXR (1) - **A**lbumin <3.5 g/dL (1) - **R**espiratory rate: Age ≤50: ≥25/min, Age >50: ≥30/min (1) - **T**achycardia ≥125/min (1) - **C**onfusion (1) - **O**xygenation: Age ≤50: PaO₂ <70, Age >50: <60 (2) - **P**H <7.35 (2)

docs.title: General Measures
docs.text: Supportive: - Oxygen: Target SpO₂ ≥90% (92-96% if not COPD) - IV Fluids: Hydration - Antipyretics: Paracetamol - Chest physiotherapy

docs.title: Antibiotic Therapy
docs.text:
Principles: - Start empirically (Don't wait for culture) - Within 4-8 hours of diagnosis (↓ Mortality) - De-escalate based on culture results

Duration: 5-7 days (Minimum 5 days, Afebrile ≥48-72 hours)

OUTPATIENT (CURB-65 0-1):

Previously Healthy, No Antibiotics in 3 months: - Amoxicillin 1 g TID (First-line) - OR Doxycycline 100 mg BD - OR Azithromycin 500 mg OD × 3 days (Covers atypical)

Comorbidities OR Antibiotics in 3 months: - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625 mg TID (or 1g BD) - + Azithromycin 500 mg OD (Add if atypical suspected) - OR Respiratory fluoroquinolone (Levofloxacin 750 mg OD, Moxifloxacin 400 mg OD)

INPATIENT NON-ICU (CURB-65 2):

  • IV β-Lactam (Ampicillin-Sulbactam 1.5 g q6h, Ceftriaxone 1-2 g OD, Cefotaxime 1 g q8h)
  • + Azithromycin 500 mg OD (or Doxycycline)
  • OR Respiratory fluoroquinolone alone (Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin)

Switch to PO when clinically improving, tolerating oral

ICU (CURB-65 ≥3 or Severe):

  • IV β-Lactam (Ceftriaxone 2 g OD OR Cefotaxime 2 g q8h OR Ampicillin-Sulbactam 3 g q6h)
  • + Azithromycin 500 mg OD (or Levofloxacin)

If Pseudomonas Risk (Bronchiectasis, Severe COPD, Recent hospitalization, Prior Pseudomonas): - Antipseudomonal β-Lactam: - Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g q6h OR - Cefepime 2 g q8h OR - Meropenem 1 g q8h - + Fluoroquinolone (Levofloxacin) OR Aminoglycoside (Gentamicin)

If MRSA Risk (Recent hospitalization, IVDU, Severe influenza): - Add Vancomycin 15 mg/kg q12h (or Linezolid)

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
Pneumococcal Vaccine: - PCV13 (Conjugate) + PPSV23 (Polysaccharide) - All ≥65 years, Chronic diseases, Immunocompromised

Influenza Vaccine: - Annual (All adults, especially elderly, Chronic diseases)

Smoking Cessation

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
1. Pleural Effusion: - Parapneumonic: Exudate (Sterile initially) - Empyema: Pus (pH <7.2, Glucose <40, LDH >1000) - Treatment: Chest tube drainage + Antibiotics

2. Lung Abscess: - Cavitation with air-fluid level - Organisms: Anaerobes (Aspiration), S. aureus, Klebsiella - Treatment: Prolonged antibiotics (4-6 weeks), Drainage if large

3. Septic Shock: - Severe sepsis → Hypotension, Organ dysfunction - ICU, Vasopressors, Broad-spectrum antibiotics

4. Respiratory Failure: - ARDS (Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome) - Mechanical ventilation

5. Metastatic Infections: - Meningitis, Endocarditis, Septic arthritis (S. pneumoniae)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Pneumonia occurring ≥48 hours after hospital admission (Not incubating at admission)

Includes: - Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP): ≥48 hours after intubation

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• 2nd most common nosocomial infection (After UTI)
  • Highest mortality among nosocomial infections

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
"MIST-HAP" 

M - Mechanical ventilation (Biggest risk for VAP)
I - Intubation, ICU stay
S - Supine position (Head of bed <30°)
T - Tracheostomy

H - H2 blockers, PPI (↑ Gastric pH → Bacterial overgrowth)
A - Antibiotics (Prior use → Resistant organisms)
P - Poor hand hygiene

Other: - Age >70, Chronic lung disease, Immunosuppression, Malnutrition

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Different from CAP - More resistant organisms

Early HAP (<5 days): - S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae (Similar to CAP) - MSSA (Methicillin-sensitive S. aureus)

Late HAP (≥5 days) or Risk Factors: - Gram-negative bacilli: - Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Most common) - Klebsiella pneumoniae (ESBL-producing) - Acinetobacter baumannii - E. coli, Enterobacter - MRSA (Methicillin-resistant S. aureus) - Polymicrobial (Common)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Clinical: - New infiltrate on CXR - + ≥2 of: Fever, Leukocytosis/Leukopenia, Purulent sputum

Microbiological: - Tracheal aspirate (Intubated patients) - Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) or Protected specimen brush (PSB) (Gold standard) - Quantitative cultures: - BAL: ≥10⁴ CFU/mL - PSB: ≥10³ CFU/mL - Blood cultures

Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score (CPIS): - Helps diagnose VAP (Temperature, WBC, Tracheal secretions, Oxygenation, CXR, Culture) - Score >6: Likely pneumonia

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Empiric Antibiotics (Broad-spectrum):

Cover Pseudomonas + MRSA (If risk factors): 

Antipseudomonal β-Lactam:
  • Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g q6h, OR
  • Cefepime 2 g q8h, OR
  • Meropenem 1 g q8h
           +
Antipseudomonal Fluoroquinolone (Levofloxacin) or Aminoglycoside
           +
Anti-MRSA: Vancomycin or Linezolid

De-escalate based on culture (within 48-72 hours)

Duration: 7-8 days (Shorter if good response, Non-fermenting GNB may need 14 days)

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
VAP Bundle: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         VAP PREVENTION BUNDLE                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Elevate head of bed 30-45°                       │
│ • Daily sedation vacation + Spontaneous breathing  │
│   trial (Assess extubation readiness)              │
│ • Oral care with chlorhexidine                     │
│ • Subglottic suctioning (ETT with suction port)    │
│ • Avoid gastric overdistension                     │
│ • DVT prophylaxis                                  │
│ • Stress ulcer prophylaxis (Only if indicated)     │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Early Extubation (Most effective)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Pneumonia following aspiration of gastric or oropharyngeal contents

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
"ASPIRATION" 

A - Altered consciousness (Stroke, Seizure, Anesthesia, Alcohol)
S - Swallowing dysfunction (Dysphagia - Stroke, Parkinson's, Esophageal)
P - Poor dentition
I - Intubation (Prolonged)
R - Recumbent position
A - Achalasia, GERD
T - Tracheostomy
I - Impaired gag reflex
O - Obstruction (Esophageal tumor)
N - NG tube

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Two Syndromes:

1. Chemical Pneumonitis (Mendelson's Syndrome): - Aspiration of gastric acid (pH <2.5) - Acute inflammation (Not infection) - Rapid onset (Within hours) - Clinical: Dyspnea, Tachypnea, Hypoxemia, Bilateral infiltrates - Management: Supportive (Oxygen, Bronchodilators), Antibiotics NOT needed (Unless secondary bacterial infection)

2. Aspiration Pneumonia (Bacterial): - Aspiration of oropharyngeal bacteria - Anaerobes (Most common): Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium - Develops over days - Predilection: Right lower lobe (if upright), Superior segments of lower lobes or posterior segments of upper lobes (if supine)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Subacute onset (Days)
  • Fever, Productive cough (Foul-smelling sputum - Anaerobes)
  • CXR: Infiltrate in dependent areas (RLL if upright, Posterior/Superior segments if supine)
  • May progress to lung abscess (Cavitation with air-fluid level)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Aspiration Pneumonitis: - Supportive care (Oxygen, Fluids) - Bronchodilators - Antibiotics: Only if secondary bacterial infection suspected (Fever persists >48-72 hours)

Aspiration Pneumonia (Bacterial): - Antibiotics covering anaerobes: - Ampicillin-Sulbactam 3 g q6h (First-line) - OR Clindamycin 600 mg q8h + Cephalosporin - OR Moxifloxacin 400 mg OD (Covers anaerobes) - Duration: 7-14 days (Longer if lung abscess - 4-6 weeks)

Prevention: - Head of bed elevation (30-45°) - Swallowing assessment before oral feeding (Stroke patients) - Thickened liquids (if dysphagia) - Oral care

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Pneumonia caused by "atypical" organisms that don't respond to β-lactams and have distinct clinical features.

Organisms: 1. Mycoplasma pneumoniae 2. Chlamydophila pneumoniae 3. Legionella pneumophila 4. Viruses (Influenza, RSV, Adenovirus, SARS-CoV-2)

docs.title: Mycoplasma pneumoniae
docs.text:
Epidemiology: - Young adults (5-40 years) - "Walking pneumonia" - Outbreaks in closed communities (Schools, Military)

Clinical: - Gradual onset - Dry cough (Persistent, Paroxysmal) - Low-grade fever, Headache, Malaise - Extrapulmonary manifestations (20%): - Bullous myringitis (Ear - Hemorrhagic blisters on TM) - Hemolytic anemia (Cold agglutinins - IgM anti-I) - Erythema multiforme (Skin - Stevens-Johnson syndrome) - Encephalitis, Guillain-Barré syndrome (Rare)

CXR: - Interstitial/Reticulonodular pattern - Patchy infiltrates (Often looks worse than clinical symptoms - "Walking pneumonia")

Diagnosis: - Cold agglutinins: Positive (Agglutination at 4°C) - Non-specific - IgM antibodies (Serology - Acute infection) - PCR (Most sensitive)

Treatment: - Macrolides: Azithromycin 500 mg OD × 3-5 days (DOC) - OR Doxycycline 100 mg BD × 7-10 days - OR Respiratory fluoroquinolone

docs.title: Legionella pneumophila
docs.text:
Epidemiology: - Contaminated water sources (Air conditioning, Cooling towers, Hot tubs) - Elderly, Smokers, Immunocompromised

Clinical: - Severe pneumonia - High fever, Rigors - Dry cough (Later productive) - Extrapulmonary features: - GI: Diarrhea, Nausea, Vomiting (Common - 30-50%) - CNS: Confusion, Headache - Hyponatremia (SIADH) - ↑ Transaminases - Relative bradycardia (Fever-pulse dissociation)

CXR: - Patchy/Lobar consolidation - Pleural effusion (Common)

Diagnosis: - Urine antigen test (Detects L. pneumophila serogroup 1 - 80%) - Best initial test - Rapid, Sensitive, Remains positive even after antibiotics - Culture: Buffered charcoal yeast extract (BCYE) agar (Gold standard but slow) - PCR

Treatment: - Respiratory fluoroquinolone: Levofloxacin 750 mg OD × 7-10 days (DOC) - OR Azithromycin 500 mg OD × 5-7 days - Severe: Add Rifampin to fluoroquinolone

docs.title: Chlamydophila pneumoniae
docs.text:
Epidemiology: - Elderly - Sore throat preceding pneumonia (Common)

Clinical: - Mild atypical pneumonia - Dry cough, Hoarseness - Pharyngitis

Diagnosis: - Serology (IgM, IgG) - PCR

Treatment: - Macrolides (Azithromycin) or Doxycycline

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Most Common Cause of CAP: Streptococcus pneumoniae (30-50%)

  2. Rusty Sputum: S. pneumoniae

  3. Currant Jelly Sputum: Klebsiella (Alcoholics, Diabetics)

  4. CURB-65 Score:

  5. 0-1: Outpatient
  6. 2: Consider hospitalization

  7. ≥3: Hospitalize (ICU if needed)

  8. Empiric Antibiotics CAP (Outpatient):

  9. Healthy: Amoxicillin or Doxycycline

  10. Comorbid: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate + Azithromycin (or Respiratory FQ)

  11. Empiric Antibiotics CAP (Inpatient):

  12. β-Lactam + Azithromycin (or Respiratory FQ alone)

  13. Empiric Antibiotics CAP (ICU):

  14. Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin (or Levofloxacin)

  15. Add Vancomycin if MRSA risk

  16. HAP/VAP Organisms: Pseudomonas, MRSA (Most common)

  17. HAP/VAP Treatment: Antipseudomonal β-lactam + Antipseudomonal FQ/Aminoglycoside + Vancomycin

  18. VAP Prevention: Elevate head 30-45°, Oral care, Daily sedation vacation, Early extubation

  19. Aspiration Pneumonia:

    • Chemical pneumonitis: NO antibiotics (Supportive)
    • Bacterial: Anaerobes → Ampicillin-Sulbactam or Clindamycin
    • Predilection: Right lower lobe (upright), Posterior/Superior segments (supine)

  20. Mycoplasma pneumoniae:

    • Young adults, "Walking pneumonia"
    • Dry cough, Bullous myringitis, Cold agglutinins
    • Treatment: Azithromycin (DOC)

  21. Legionella:

    • Water source, Diarrhea, Hyponatremia, Relative bradycardia
    • Urine antigen test (Best initial)
    • Treatment: Levofloxacin (DOC) or Azithromycin

  22. Pneumococcal Vaccine: All ≥65 years (PCV13 + PPSV23)

  23. Empyema Criteria: pH <7.2, Glucose <40 mg/dL, LDH >1000 → Chest tube drainage

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Fever, rusty sputum, lobar consolidation → S. pneumoniae CAP

Scenario 2: Alcoholic, currant jelly sputum, upper lobe consolidation with bulging fissure → Klebsiella pneumoniae

Scenario 3: Post-influenza, cavitation on CXR → S. aureus pneumonia

Scenario 4: Young adult, dry cough, bullous myringitis, cold agglutinins → Mycoplasma

Scenario 5: Elderly, severe pneumonia, diarrhea, hyponatremia, relative bradycardia → Legionella

Scenario 6: ICU patient, ≥48 hours post-intubation, new infiltrate → VAP (Pseudomonas, MRSA)

Scenario 7: Stroke patient, aspiration, foul-smelling sputum, RLL infiltrate → Aspiration pneumonia (Anaerobes) → Ampicillin-Sulbactam

Scenario 8: Empyema with pH 7.1, glucose 30 → Chest tube drainage

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• S. pneumoniae: Most common CAP, Rusty sputum
  • CURB-65: 0-1 outpatient, 2 consider admission, ≥3 admit
  • CAP antibiotics: Amoxicillin (healthy), Amox-Clav + Azithro (comorbid), β-lactam + Azithro (inpatient)
  • HAP/VAP: Pseudomonas, MRSA
  • VAP prevention: Head elevation, Oral care, Early extubation
  • Aspiration: Anaerobes, Ampicillin-Sulbactam
  • Mycoplasma: Young, Dry cough, Bullous myringitis, Azithromycin
  • Legionella: Water, Diarrhea, Hyponatremia, Levofloxacin
  • Empyema: pH <7.2, Glucose <40 → Drain

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 65-year-old presents with fever, productive cough with rusty sputum, and pleuritic chest pain. CXR shows right lower lobe consolidation. CURB-65 score is 2. Most appropriate management?

A) Outpatient Amoxicillin B) Hospitalize, IV Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin C) Outpatient Azithromycin only D) ICU admission

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Hospitalize, IV Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin** **Explanation:** - **CURB-65 = 2:** Consider hospitalization (Mortality ~9%) - **Likely S. pneumoniae** (Rusty sputum, Lobar consolidation) - **Inpatient treatment:** - **IV β-Lactam** (Ceftriaxone, Ampicillin-Sulbactam) + **Azithromycin** - OR Respiratory FQ alone **Why not others:** - Outpatient: CURB-65 ≥2 → Consider admission - ICU: CURB-65 ≥3 or severe (This patient is moderate)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Which organism is most commonly associated with "currant jelly" sputum and upper lobe pneumonia with bulging fissure?

A) Streptococcus pneumoniae B) Klebsiella pneumoniae C) Staphylococcus aureus D) Mycoplasma pneumoniae

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Klebsiella pneumoniae** **Explanation:** - **Klebsiella pneumoniae** pneumonia: - **"Currant jelly" sputum** (Mucoid, Bloody) - **Upper lobe** predilection - **Bulging fissure** (Heavy, gelatinous consolidation) - **Risk factors:** Alcoholics, Diabetics, Aspiration - **Gram-negative bacilli** (Mucoid colonies) **Others:** - S. pneumoniae: Rusty sputum - S. aureus: Post-influenza, Cavitation - Mycoplasma: Atypical, Dry cough

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 25-year-old presents with gradual onset dry cough, low-grade fever, and bullous myringitis. CXR shows patchy interstitial infiltrates. Cold agglutinins are positive. What is the most appropriate treatment?

A) Amoxicillin B) Ceftriaxone C) Azithromycin D) Vancomycin

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Azithromycin** **Explanation:** - **Diagnosis:** **Mycoplasma pneumoniae** pneumonia - **Young adult**, **Gradual onset** - **Dry cough**, **Bullous myringitis** (Hemorrhagic blisters on TM) - **Cold agglutinins** positive - **CXR:** Interstitial/Patchy **Treatment:** - **Macrolides:** **Azithromycin 500 mg OD × 3-5 days** (DOC) - OR Doxycycline, Respiratory FQ **Why not β-lactams:** - Mycoplasma lacks cell wall → β-lactams (Amoxicillin, Ceftriaxone) ineffective

End of Pneumonia Study Material

Next Topic: - Tuberculosis

Study Tips: 1. Master CURB-65 (Very high-yield for management decisions) 2. Know organism-clinical associations (Rusty = Pneumococcus, Currant jelly = Klebsiella) 3. Understand empiric antibiotic choices for different settings 4. Remember atypical pneumonia features (Mycoplasma, Legionella) 5. Know HAP/VAP organisms and prevention

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 4-5 hours Revision Time: 1.5-2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: Tuberculosis - NEET PG Study Material
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5) | NEET PG Weightage: VERY HIGH | Subject: Medicine - Respiratory

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview & Epidemiology
  2. Pathogenesis
  3. Pulmonary Tuberculosis
  4. Extrapulmonary Tuberculosis
  5. Diagnosis
  6. Treatment
  7. Drug-Resistant TB
  8. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  9. Practice Questions

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Tuberculosis (TB) is a chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB), primarily affecting the lungs (Pulmonary TB - 80%) but can affect any organ (Extrapulmonary TB).

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Leading infectious cause of death worldwide (Before COVID-19)
  • India: Highest TB burden globally (~27% of global cases)
  • HIV-TB coinfection: Major problem (TB is leading cause of death in HIV)
  • One-third of world population has latent TB infection (LTBI)

docs.title: Causative Organism
docs.text:
Mycobacterium tuberculosis Complex: - M. tuberculosis (Most common - 95%) - M. bovis (Cattle - Milk-borne, Rare now) - M. africanum

Characteristics: - Acid-Fast Bacillus (AFB) - Ziehl-Neelsen (ZN) stain - Aerobic (Prefers upper lobes - high O₂) - Slow-growing (Doubling time 15-20 hours) - Intracellular (Survives in macrophages) - Cell wall: High lipid content (Mycolic acid) → Resistance to drying, Acid-fast

docs.title: Transmission
docs.text:
Airborne droplet nuclei (<5 μm) from infected person (Cough, Sneeze, Speak)

Risk of Transmission: - Sputum-positive pulmonary TB (Cavitary >> Non-cavitary) - Close contact, Prolonged exposure

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         NATURAL HISTORY OF TB                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                    │
│ INFECTION (Inhalation of MTB)                      │
│         ↓                                          │
│         ├─ 90% → PRIMARY TB (Usually asymptomatic) │
│         │         ↓                                │
│         │         ├─ 90% → Latent TB (LTBI)        │
│         │         │         • No symptoms          │
│         │         │         • Not infectious       │
│         │         │         • TST/IGRA positive    │
│         │         │         • 5-10% lifetime risk  │
│         │         │           of reactivation      │
│         │         │                                │
│         │         └─ 10% → Progressive Primary TB  │
│         │                   (Children, HIV)        │
│         │                                          │
│         └─ 10% → Direct progression to disease     │
│                                                    │
│ REACTIVATION (Post-Primary/Secondary TB)           │
│ • From latent TB (5-10% lifetime)                  │
│ • ↑ Risk: HIV, Immunosuppression, Malnutrition    │
│ • Usually PULMONARY (Upper lobes)                  │
│                                                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Pathology
docs.text:
Primary TB (Ghon Complex): 1. Ghon focus: Subpleural granuloma (Usually lower/middle lobe) 2. Lymphangitis 3. Hilar lymphadenopathy

Ghon Complex = Ghon focus + Hilar LN

Ranke Complex = Healed Ghon complex (Calcified)

Secondary (Reactivation) TB: - Upper lobes (Apical/Posterior segments) - High O₂ - Cavitation (Caseous necrosis → Liquefaction → Cavity) - Bronchogenic spread (To other lobes)

Granuloma: - Caseating granuloma (Hallmark of TB) - Central caseous necrosis (Cheese-like) - Epithelioid cells, Langerhans giant cells - Lymphocytes (Periphery)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Primary TB: - First infection with MTB - Usually asymptomatic (Children, Young adults) - Lower/Middle lobes - Hilar lymphadenopathy (Prominent) - Pleural effusion (Common in primary)

Post-Primary (Reactivation/Secondary) TB: - Reactivation of latent infection OR Reinfection - Symptomatic (Adults) - Upper lobes (Apical/Posterior) - Cavitation (Common) - Most contagious (Sputum-positive, Cavitary)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms:

Constitutional ("B Symptoms"): - Fever: Low-grade, Evening rise (Characteristic) - Night sweats (Drenching) - Weight loss (Significant) - Anorexia, Fatigue

Respiratory: - Chronic cough (>2-3 weeks) - Most common symptom - Initially dry, Later productive - Hemoptysis (Blood-streaked sputum) - Massive hemoptysis: Erosion into bronchial artery (Rasmussen aneurysm) - Dyspnea (Advanced disease) - Chest pain (Pleuritic - if pleural involvement)

Duration: Insidious onset, Chronic course (Weeks to months)

Signs: - Often minimal (Early disease) - Fever - Wasting, Cachexia - Crackles (Apical - Upper lobes) - Amphoric breath sounds (Large cavity) - Lymphadenopathy (Cervical - Scrofula)

docs.title: Chest X-ray
docs.text:
Primary TB: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      CXR IN PRIMARY TB                             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Hilar/Mediastinal lymphadenopathy (Prominent)    │
│ • Lower/Middle lobe infiltrate (Ghon focus)        │
│ • Pleural effusion (Common - especially children)  │
│ • Miliary pattern (If disseminated)                │
│ • Calcification (Ranke complex - Healed)           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Post-Primary (Reactivation) TB: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      CXR IN POST-PRIMARY TB                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Upper lobe infiltrates (Apical/Posterior)        │
│ • CAVITATION (Ring shadows with air-fluid level)   │
│   - Single or multiple                             │
│   - Thick-walled                                   │
│ • Fibrosis, Reticulonodular opacities              │
│ • Volume loss (Fibrosis → Upper lobe retraction)   │
│ • Bronchogenic spread (Tree-in-bud pattern)        │
│ • Calcification (Old TB)                           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Miliary TB: - Millet-seed sized nodules (1-3 mm) - Diffuse, Bilateral - Hematogenous dissemination

docs.title: Extrapulmonary Tuberculosis
docs.text: 15-20% of TB cases (↑ In HIV - up to 50%)

docs.title: 1. Tuberculous Lymphadenitis (Most Common EPTB)
docs.text:
Sites: Cervical (Most common - Scrofula)

Clinical: - Painless, Matted lymph nodes - Cold abscess (Fluctuant, Non-tender) - Sinus formation (Chronic)

Diagnosis: - FNAC: Caseating granulomas, AFB - Excision biopsy (If FNAC non-diagnostic)

docs.title: 2. Tuberculous Pleural Effusion
docs.text:
Pathogenesis: - Hypersensitivity reaction to TB antigens in pleura (NOT direct infection usually)

Clinical: - Pleuritic chest pain, Dyspnea - Fever

Pleural Fluid Analysis: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      TB PLEURAL EFFUSION CHARACTERISTICS           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Exudate (Light's criteria)                       │
│ • Straw-colored to bloody                          │
│ • Lymphocytic predominance (>50%, Usually >80%)    │
│ • Protein >5 g/dL                                  │
│ • Glucose <60 mg/dL (Low)                          │
│ • LDH elevated                                     │
│ • ADA (Adenosine Deaminase) >40 U/L (Diagnostic)   │
│   - Sensitivity/Specificity >90%                   │
│ • AFB smear: Usually NEGATIVE (Low yield <10%)     │
│ • TB culture: Positive 20-40%                      │
│ • GeneXpert: Sensitivity 20-50% (Lower than sputum)│
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

ADA >40 U/L: HIGHLY SUGGESTIVE of TB pleural effusion

Pleural Biopsy: - Caseating granulomas (Definitive diagnosis) - Higher yield than fluid AFB/culture

docs.title: 3. Tuberculous Meningitis (TBM)
docs.text:
Most severe form of TB (High mortality/morbidity)

Clinical: 

THREE STAGES:
Stage I (Prodromal): 1-3 weeks
  • Low-grade fever, Headache, Malaise
  • Personality changes

Stage II (Meningitic): 2-3 weeks
  • Meningismus (Neck stiffness, Kernig's, Brudzinski's)
  • Cranial nerve palsies (CN VI most common)
  • Confusion, Focal deficits

Stage III (Paralytic): >3 weeks
  • Coma, Seizures
  • Hemiplegia, Paraplegia
  • Decerebrate rigidity

CSF Analysis: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      TB MENINGITIS CSF                             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Appearance: Clear to cloudy (Xanthochromic)      │
│ • Pressure: Elevated (>250 mm H₂O)                 │
│ • WBC: 100-500/μL (Lymphocytic predominance >50%)  │
│   - Early: PMN may predominate                     │
│ • Protein: Markedly elevated (>100 mg/dL, Often    │
│   100-500 mg/dL)                                   │
│ • Glucose: Low (<45 mg/dL or CSF/Blood <0.5)       │
│ • Chloride: <110 mEq/L (Old teaching - not used)   │
│ • AFB smear: Low sensitivity (10-20%)              │
│   - ↑ Yield: Large volume (6-10 mL), Centrifuge    │
│ • TB culture: Positive 50-80% (Takes 4-8 weeks)    │
│ • GeneXpert: Sensitivity 50-60% (Better than smear)│
│ • ADA: >10 U/L suggestive (Less specific than      │
│   pleural)                                         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Imaging: - CT/MRI: Basal meningeal enhancement, Hydrocephalus, Tuberculomas, Infarcts

Treatment: - ATT (4 drugs - HRZE) + Steroids (Dexamethasone or Prednisolone) - Steroids ↓ mortality/disability (Proven in TBM) - Treat for 12 months (Longer than pulmonary TB)

Complications: - Hydrocephalus (Basal exudate blocks CSF flow) - Vasculitis → Infarcts - Cranial nerve palsies (CN VI most common) - SIADH

docs.title: 4. Spinal TB (Pott's Disease)
docs.text:
Most common site of skeletal TB

Location: Lower thoracic/Lumbar spine (Most common)

Pathology: - Vertebral body destruction (Anterior > Posterior) - Disc space narrowing (Unlike pyogenic - usually preserves disc) - Paravertebral abscess (Cold abscess) - Gibbus deformity (Angular kyphosis)

Clinical: - Back pain (Insidious, Progressive) - Neurological deficits: Paraparesis, Paraplegia (Cord compression) - Gibbus (Visible angular deformity)

Imaging: - MRI: Gold standard - Vertebral body destruction - Disc involvement - Paravertebral abscess - Cord compression

Diagnosis: - CT-guided biopsy: AFB, Culture, Histology

Treatment: - ATT (Standard 6-9 months, Longer if extensive) - Surgery: If neurological deficit, Spinal instability, Large abscess

docs.title: 5. Abdominal TB
docs.text:
Sites: - Intestinal: Terminal ileum, Ileocecal region (Most common) - Peritoneal: Ascites - Lymph nodes: Mesenteric

Clinical: - Chronic abdominal pain - Fever, Weight loss - Diarrhea/Constipation - Abdominal mass (Ileocecal) - Ascites (Peritoneal TB)

Ascitic Fluid: - Exudate - Lymphocytic (>250 cells/μL) - SAAG <1.1 g/dL (Exudate) - ADA >40 U/L (Suggestive) - AFB smear: Low yield

Laparoscopy with Biopsy: Definitive (Caseating granulomas, AFB)

docs.title: 6. Genitourinary TB
docs.text:
Renal TB: - Sterile pyuria (WBC in urine, Negative bacterial culture) - Hematuria - Dysuria, Frequency - IVP: "Moth-eaten" calyces, Hydronephrosis

Genital TB: - Females: Infertility (Endometrial TB), Menstrual irregularities - Males: Epididymo-orchitis (Beaded vas)

docs.title: 7. Miliary TB
docs.text:
Hematogenous dissemination → Multiple organs

Clinical: - Fever, Night sweats, Weight loss (Severe) - Hepatosplenomegaly - Choroidal tubercles (Ophthalmoscopy - Pathognomonic)

CXR: - Miliary pattern (1-3 mm nodules, Diffuse, Bilateral)

Diagnosis: - Sputum AFB: Often negative - Blood culture for MTB (BACTEC) - Liver/Bone marrow biopsy: Granulomas

docs.title: 1. Sputum Smear Microscopy (Ziehl-Neelsen Stain)
docs.text:
Technique: - Acid-fast stain (AFB - Pink/Red rods on blue background) - Fluorescent stain (Auramine-rhodamine)

Criteria: - ≥3 sputum samples (Spot-Morning-Spot or 2 consecutive morning samples)

Grading: - 1+: 10-99 AFB in 100 fields - 2+: 1-10 AFB per field (50 fields) - 3+: >10 AFB per field (20 fields)

Sensitivity: 50-60% (Depends on bacillary load - Cavitary >> Non-cavitary)

Specificity: High (But can't distinguish MTB from NTM)

Advantages: Fast (2 hours), Cheap

Limitations: - Low sensitivity (Needs 5,000-10,000 bacilli/mL) - Cannot distinguish MTB from NTM (Non-tuberculous mycobacteria) - Cannot detect drug resistance

docs.title: 2. Sputum Culture
docs.text:
Media: - Liquid: BACTEC MGIT (Mycobacteria Growth Indicator Tube) - Faster (1-3 weeks) - Solid: Lowenstein-Jensen (LJ) medium - Slower (4-8 weeks)

Gold Standard for diagnosis

Advantages: - Most sensitive (100-1000 bacilli/mL) - Confirms MTB - Drug Sensitivity Testing (DST) possible

Limitations: - Slow (Weeks) - Requires biosafety lab

docs.title: 3. GeneXpert MTB/RIF (Molecular - Cartridge-Based PCR)
docs.text:
Revolutionized TB diagnosis

Technique: - Real-time PCR detects MTB DNA + Rifampicin resistance (rpoB gene mutations)

Advantages: - Rapid: 2 hours - High sensitivity: 90-95% (Smear-positive), 70-75% (Smear-negative) - Detects Rifampicin resistance (Proxy for MDR-TB) - WHO-recommended initial test (Replacing smear)

Specimen: Sputum, CSF, Pleural fluid, Lymph node aspirate

Limitations: - Cost (Higher than smear) - Cannot detect Isoniazid resistance alone - Lower sensitivity in extrapulmonary TB (CSF ~50-60%)

Indications (WHO): - All presumptive TB cases - HIV-TB (Initial test) - Suspected MDR-TB - Extrapulmonary TB

docs.title: 4. Line Probe Assay (LPA)
docs.text:
Molecular test for drug resistance

Types: - First-line LPA: Detects resistance to Rifampicin + Isoniazid (MDR-TB) - Second-line LPA: Fluoroquinolones + Injectable agents (XDR-TB)

Advantages: - Faster than culture DST (1-2 days)

Specimen: Sputum (Smear-positive) or Culture isolate

docs.title: 1. Tuberculin Skin Test (TST / Mantoux Test)
docs.text:
Technique: - Intradermal injection of 0.1 mL PPD (Purified Protein Derivative - 5 TU) - Volar aspect of forearm - Read at 48-72 hours (Measure induration, NOT erythema)

Interpretation: 

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      TST (MANTOUX) INTERPRETATION                  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ≥5 mm: Positive if                                 │
│   • HIV-positive                                   │
│   • Recent TB contact                              │
│   • CXR suggestive of old TB                       │
│   • Immunosuppressed (Steroids, Transplant)        │
│                                                    │
│ ≥10 mm: Positive if                                │
│   • Healthcare workers                             │
│   • IVDU, Homeless                                 │
│   • Immigrants from high-burden countries          │
│   • Chronic diseases (DM, CKD, Malignancy)         │
│                                                    │
│ ≥15 mm: Positive in                                │
│   • Healthy individuals (Low risk)                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Indicates: TB infection (LTBI or Active TB) - Does NOT distinguish

False Positive: - BCG vaccination (Wanes over time - Usually <15 mm) - NTM infection

False Negative: - Anergy (Severe TB, HIV, Malnutrition, Steroids) - Recent infection (<8 weeks) - Very young (<6 months) or very old

docs.title: 2. Interferon-Gamma Release Assay (IGRA)
docs.text:
Blood test measuring IFN-γ release by T cells when exposed to TB antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10)

Types: - QuantiFERON-TB Gold - T-SPOT.TB

Advantages over TST: - NOT affected by BCG (More specific) - Single visit (No return for reading) - Less subjective

Limitations: - Cost (Higher than TST) - Cannot distinguish LTBI from active TB - False negative in immunosuppressed

Indications: - Latent TB screening (Especially BCG-vaccinated) - Contact tracing

docs.title: Radiological
docs.text:
Chest X-ray: Described above

CT Chest: - Tree-in-bud pattern (Bronchogenic spread) - Cavitation, Consolidation

docs.title: Histopathology
docs.text:
Biopsy (Lymph node, Pleura, Liver, Bone marrow)

Caseating granuloma: - Central caseous necrosis - Epithelioid cells, Langerhans giant cells - Lymphocytes

Highly suggestive of TB (But can occur in others - Fungal)

Confirm with AFB stain, Culture

docs.title: Anti-Tubercular Therapy (ATT)
docs.text: Goals: - Cure TB - Prevent relapse - Prevent transmission - Prevent drug resistance

docs.title: First-Line Drugs ("HRZE-S")
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      FIRST-LINE ANTI-TB DRUGS                      │
├────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ DRUG   │  KEY POINTS                               │
├────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ H      │ • Isoniazid (H) - Bactericidal            │
│ (INH)  │ • Dose: 5 mg/kg (Max 300 mg)              │
│        │ • Side effects: Hepatitis (Most common),  │
│        │   Peripheral neuropathy (Give Pyridoxine  │
│        │   10 mg/day), Seizures (High dose)        │
├────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ R      │ • Rifampicin (R) - Bactericidal (Most     │
│ (RIF)  │   potent)                                 │
│        │ • Dose: 10 mg/kg (Max 600 mg)             │
│        │ • Side effects: Orange discoloration      │
│        │   (Urine, Tears, Sweat), Hepatitis,       │
│        │   Flu-like (Intermittent), Enzyme inducer │
│        │   (↓ OCP, Warfarin)                       │
├────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Z      │ • Pyrazinamide (Z) - Bactericidal         │
│ (PZA)  │   (Sterilizing - kills intracellular)     │
│        │ • Dose: 25 mg/kg (Max 2000 mg)            │
│        │ • Side effects: Hepatitis,                │
│        │   Hyperuricemia (Gout - Common), Arthralgia│
├────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ E      │ • Ethambutol (E) - Bacteriostatic         │
│ (EMB)  │ • Dose: 15-20 mg/kg                       │
│        │ • Side effects: Optic neuritis            │
│        │   (Retrobulbar - Dose/Duration dependent) │
│        │   • Red-green color blindness             │
│        │   • Check visual acuity monthly           │
│        │ • AVOID in children <5 years (Can't assess│
│        │   vision)                                 │
├────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ S      │ • Streptomycin (S) - Bactericidal         │
│ (SM)   │   (Injectable)                            │
│        │ • Dose: 15 mg/kg IM (Max 1 g)             │
│        │ • Side effects: Ototoxicity (8th nerve),  │
│        │   Nephrotoxicity                          │
│        │ • Rarely used now (Replaced by others)    │
└────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: New Drug-Sensitive Pulmonary TB
docs.text:
WHO 2021 Recommendation: - 6 months regimen: 2HRZE / 4HR - Intensive Phase: 2 months of HRZE (4 drugs daily) - Continuation Phase: 4 months of HR (2 drugs daily)

India (NTEP - National TB Elimination Programme): - Daily regimen (Preferred): 2HRZE / 4HR (6 months total) - Weight-based dosing (FDC - Fixed Dose Combination tablets)

Monitoring: - Sputum smear/GeneXpert: End of intensive phase (2 months), End of treatment - LFTs: Baseline, Monthly (if symptomatic)

Treatment Success: Cure + Treatment completed (>85% target)

docs.title: Extrapulmonary TB
docs.text:
Most Sites: - Same as pulmonary: 2HRZE / 4HR (6 months)

Exceptions (Longer duration): - TB Meningitis: 2HRZE / 10HR (12 months total) - Add Steroids: Dexamethasone (taper over 6-8 weeks) or Prednisolone - Steroids ↓ mortality/disability (Proven) - Spinal TB (Pott's): 2HRZE / 7HR (9 months) to 12 months - Bone/Joint TB: 9-12 months

docs.title: Retreatment TB (Previously Treated)
docs.text: If relapse/failure: - DO NOT use Category II (2HRZES/1HRZE/5HRE) - Abandoned due to high failure/MDR - Treat as Drug-Resistant TB until DST available - GeneXpert + Culture DST (Mandatory)

docs.title: Directly Observed Therapy (DOT)
docs.text: DOTS (Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course): - Healthcare worker watches patient swallow pills - Improves adherence - ↓ Drug resistance - Cornerstone of TB control

docs.title: Monitoring & Adverse Effects
docs.text:
Hepatotoxicity (Most common serious ADR): - Drugs: H, R, Z (All three) - Monitor: LFTs (Baseline, then if symptoms) - Action: - Transaminases >3x ULN + Symptoms OR >5x ULN: STOP all hepatotoxic drugs - Restart sequentially when LFTs normalize (R first, then H, then Z)

Peripheral Neuropathy: - Isoniazid - Prevention: Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) 10 mg/day (Give to all patients - cheap, safe)

Optic Neuritis: - Ethambutol - Monitor: Visual acuity, Color vision (Monthly - especially >2 months) - Action: STOP Ethambutol immediately if symptoms

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      DRUG-RESISTANT TB DEFINITIONS                 │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ • Mono-resistant: Resistance to 1 drug             │
│                                                    │
│ • Poly-resistant: Resistance to >1 drug (NOT HR)   │
│                                                    │
│ • MDR-TB: Resistance to H + R (± others)           │
│   (MOST IMPORTANT DEFINITION)                      │
│                                                    │
│ • Pre-XDR TB: MDR-TB + Resistance to any           │
│   Fluoroquinolone                                  │
│                                                    │
│ • XDR-TB: Pre-XDR-TB + Resistance to ≥1 Group A   │
│   drug (Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin, Bedaquiline,   │
│   Linezolid)                                       │
│                                                    │
│ • RR-TB: Rifampicin-resistant (Detected by        │
│   GeneXpert - Treat as MDR)                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

MDR-TB = Resistance to at least Isoniazid + Rifampicin

docs.title: Risk Factors for MDR-TB
docs.text:
"PRIOR TB" 

P - Previous TB treatment (Biggest risk - Relapse, Failure)
R - Rifampicin resistance on GeneXpert
I - Immunocompromised (HIV)
O - Occupational exposure (Healthcare workers)
R - Residence in high MDR area

T - Treatment failure
B - Bad adherence (Non-compliance)

Suspect MDR if: - Previously treated (Relapse, Failure, LTFU) - Treatment failure at 4-5 months - Contact of MDR-TB patient - HIV-positive

docs.title: Diagnosis of DR-TB
docs.text:
1. GeneXpert MTB/RIF: - Detects Rifampicin resistance (rpoB mutations) - RR-TB = Proxy for MDR-TB (95% of RR-TB is MDR)

2. Culture + Drug Sensitivity Testing (DST): - Gold standard - Phenotypic DST: Tests drug susceptibility (Takes weeks) - LPA (Line Probe Assay): Faster (1-2 days) - First-line LPA: H + R resistance - Second-line LPA: Fluoroquinolones + Injectables

docs.title: Treatment of MDR-TB
docs.text:
WHO 2021 Guidelines:

Shorter Regimen (9-12 months) - BPaLM: - Bedaquiline (B) + Pretomanid (Pa) + Linezolid (L) + Moxifloxacin (M) - 9-11 months for non-severe, non-extensive disease - All-oral, Shorter duration

Longer Regimen (18-24 months): - ≥4 effective drugs for 18-24 months - Group A (Include all 3 if susceptible): - Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin (Fluoroquinolone) - Bedaquiline - Linezolid - Group B (Add 1-2): - Clofazimine, Cycloserine - Group C (If needed): - Ethambutol, Delamanid, Pyrazinamide, etc.

Monitoring: - Monthly sputum culture - QTc interval (Bedaquiline, Fluoroquinolones prolong QT) - Audiometry (Aminoglycosides - if used) - Visual acuity (Linezolid - optic neuropathy)

Treatment Success: Lower than DS-TB (~60-70%)

docs.title: 🔥 MUST KNOW
docs.text:
1. Most Common Site: Lungs (Pulmonary TB - 80%)

  2. Transmission: Airborne droplets (<5 μm)

  3. Organism: Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Acid-fast bacillus)

  4. Primary TB:

  5. Ghon complex: Ghon focus + Hilar lymphadenopathy
  6. Ranke complex: Healed (Calcified) Ghon complex

  7. Lower/Middle lobes, Pleural effusion common

  8. Reactivation (Secondary) TB:

  9. Upper lobes (Apical/Posterior) - High O₂
  10. Cavitation common

  11. Most infectious (Sputum-positive, Cavitary)

  12. Most Common Symptom: Chronic cough (>2-3 weeks)

  13. Classical Features: Fever (Evening rise), Night sweats, Weight loss

  14. Diagnosis:

  15. Sputum smear (AFB - ZN stain): Fast, Cheap, Low sensitivity (50-60%)
  16. Sputum culture: Gold standard, Slow (Weeks)

  17. GeneXpert MTB/RIF: Rapid (2 hours), Detects MTB + Rifampicin resistance (WHO-recommended initial test)

  18. Treatment (New Drug-Sensitive Pulmonary TB):

  19. 2HRZE / 4HR (6 months total)
  20. Intensive: 2 months HRZE (4 drugs daily)

  21. Continuation: 4 months HR (2 drugs daily)

  22. First-Line Drugs - "HRZE-S":

    • H: Isoniazid - Hepatitis, Peripheral neuropathy (Give Pyridoxine)
    • R: Rifampicin - Orange discoloration, Hepatitis, Enzyme inducer
    • Z: Pyrazinamide - Hepatitis, Hyperuricemia (Gout)
    • E: Ethambutol - Optic neuritis (Red-green color blindness)
    • S: Streptomycin - Ototoxicity, Nephrotoxicity

  23. Extrapulmonary TB:

    • Most common: Lymph node (Cervical - Scrofula)
    • TB Meningitis: 12 months treatment + Steroids (Proven benefit)
    • Pott's Disease: Spinal TB, Gibbus deformity, Paraplegia

  24. TB Pleural Effusion:

    • Lymphocytic exudate
    • ADA >40 U/L (Highly suggestive)
    • AFB smear: Low yield (<10%)

  25. TB Meningitis CSF:

    • ↑ Protein (>100 mg/dL), ↓ Glucose, ↓ Chloride
    • Lymphocytic (>50%)
    • AFB smear: Low yield (10-20% - ↑ Yield with large volume)

  26. MDR-TB Definition: Resistance to H + R (± others)

  27. GeneXpert: Detects Rifampicin resistance → RR-TB = Treat as MDR-TB

  28. Latent TB (LTBI):

    • No symptoms, Not infectious
    • TST or IGRA positive
    • 5-10% lifetime risk of reactivation
    • Treatment: Isoniazid 300 mg daily × 6-9 months (or Rifampicin 4 months)

  29. DOTS: Directly Observed Therapy, Short-course (Improves adherence, ↓ Resistance)

  30. Most Common ADR: Hepatotoxicity (H, R, Z)

  31. Miliary TB: Hematogenous dissemination, Millet-seed nodules (1-3 mm) on CXR, Choroidal tubercles

  32. Tuberculoma: CNS mass lesion, Ring-enhancing (Target/Bull's eye on MRI)

docs.title: Clinical Scenarios
docs.text:
Scenario 1: Chronic cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss, upper lobe cavitation on CXR → Pulmonary TB (Reactivation)

Scenario 2: AFB-positive sputum, start ATT → 2HRZE / 4HR (6 months)

Scenario 3: Painless cervical lymph node, matted, cold abscess → TB lymphadenitis (Scrofula)

Scenario 4: Lymphocytic pleural effusion, ADA >40 U/L → TB pleural effusion

Scenario 5: Headache, neck stiffness, cranial nerve palsy, CSF: ↑ Protein, ↓ Glucose, Lymphocytic → TB meningitis → Treat 12 months + Steroids

Scenario 6: Back pain, paraplegia, gibbus deformity, vertebral destruction on MRI → Pott's Disease (Spinal TB)

Scenario 7: GeneXpert shows Rifampicin resistance → RR-TB → Treat as MDR-TB

Scenario 8: Previously treated TB, relapse → Suspect MDR-TB → GeneXpert + Culture DST

Scenario 9: On ATT, develops red-green color blindness → Ethambutol-induced optic neuritis → STOP Ethambutol

Scenario 10: On ATT, orange discoloration of urine → Rifampicin (Normal, Reassure)

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• TB: Airborne, Acid-fast bacillus, Lungs (80%)
  • Primary TB: Ghon complex, Lower lobes, Pleural effusion
  • Reactivation TB: Upper lobes, Cavitation, Most infectious
  • Chronic cough >2-3 weeks, Fever (Evening), Night sweats, Weight loss
  • Diagnosis: Smear (Fast, Low sensitivity), Culture (Gold standard), GeneXpert (Rapid, Detects RIF-R)
  • Treatment: 2HRZE / 4HR (6 months)
  • HRZE: H-Hepatitis/Neuropathy, R-Orange/Hepatitis, Z-Gout/Hepatitis, E-Optic neuritis
  • EPTB: Lymph node (Most common), TBM (12 mo + Steroids), Pott's (Gibbus)
  • TB pleural: ADA >40 U/L
  • TBM CSF: ↑ Protein, ↓ Glucose, Lymphocytic
  • MDR-TB: H + R resistance
  • LTBI: TST/IGRA+, No symptoms, INH 6-9 months

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 30-year-old presents with chronic cough, hemoptysis, and weight loss for 2 months. CXR shows right upper lobe cavitation. Sputum is AFB-positive. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

A) 2HRZE / 4HR B) 2HRZES / 1HRZE / 5HRE C) Bedaquiline + Pretomanid + Linezolid + Moxifloxacin D) Isoniazid alone for 9 months

Click to reveal answer **Answer: A) 2HRZE / 4HR** **Explanation:** - **New drug-sensitive pulmonary TB** - **Standard 6-month regimen:** - **Intensive Phase:** 2 months of HRZE (4 drugs daily) - **Continuation Phase:** 4 months of HR (2 drugs daily) **Why not others:** - **Category II (2HRZES/1HRZE/5HRE):** Abandoned (High failure/MDR rates) - **BPaLM:** For MDR-TB (No indication here) - **Isoniazid alone:** For Latent TB, NOT active TB

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A patient with TB pleural effusion has pleural fluid ADA level of 55 U/L. What does this indicate?

A) Pyogenic infection B) Malignant effusion C) Highly suggestive of TB D) Heart failure

Click to reveal answer **Answer: C) Highly suggestive of TB** **Explanation:** - **ADA (Adenosine Deaminase) >40 U/L** is **HIGHLY SUGGESTIVE** of TB pleural effusion - **Sensitivity/Specificity >90%** **TB Pleural Effusion:** - **Lymphocytic exudate** - **ADA >40 U/L** - **AFB smear:** Low yield (<10%) - **Pleural biopsy:** Caseating granulomas (Definitive) **Other causes of ↑ ADA:** - **Empyema** (But usually neutrophilic, Not lymphocytic) - **Lymphoma** (But usually <40 U/L)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with TB meningitis is started on ATT. Which additional medication has PROVEN mortality/disability benefit?

A) Pyridoxine B) Corticosteroids C) Aspirin D) Mannitol

Click to reveal answer **Answer: B) Corticosteroids** **Explanation:** - **TB Meningitis:** **Steroids ↓ mortality and disability** (PROVEN in multiple RCTs) - **Regimen:** - **Dexamethasone** (Taper over 6-8 weeks), OR - **Prednisolone** (Taper) **Mechanism:** - ↓ Inflammation - ↓ Basal exudate → ↓ Hydrocephalus - ↓ Vasculitis → ↓ Infarcts **Treatment:** - **2HRZE / 10HR** (12 months total) + **Steroids** **Why not others:** - Pyridoxine: Prevents peripheral neuropathy (Isoniazid), Does NOT ↓ mortality - Aspirin, Mannitol: No proven benefit in TBM

End of Tuberculosis Study Material

Next Topics: - Interstitial Lung Disease - Bronchiectasis - (Continue remaining respiratory topics)

Study Tips: 1. Master first-line drugs and side effects (Very high-yield) 2. Know treatment regimens (6 months standard, 12 months TBM) 3. Understand MDR-TB definition (H + R resistance) 4. Remember ADA >40 U/L for TB pleural effusion 5. Know TBM CSF findings and steroid benefit 6. Learn GeneXpert indications (WHO-recommended initial test)

Difficulty Rating: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) Estimated Study Time: 5-6 hours Revision Time: 2 hours

Last Updated: 2026-05-16

docs.title: INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE (ILD)
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5 - High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 6-8 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Definition & Classification
  2. Epidemiology
  3. Pathophysiology
  4. Clinical Features
  5. Diagnosis
  6. Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF)
  7. Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis
  8. Sarcoidosis
  9. Connective Tissue Disease-ILD
  10. Drug-Induced ILD
  11. Management
  12. Complications
  13. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  14. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Interstitial Lung Diseases (ILDs) are a heterogeneous group of disorders characterized by: - Inflammation and/or fibrosis of the pulmonary interstitium - Restrictive pattern on pulmonary function tests - Impaired gas exchange - Progressive dyspnea and dry cough

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASES
│
├─ KNOWN CAUSE (35%)
│  ├─ Drugs (Amiodarone, Bleomycin, Methotrexate, Nitrofurantoin)
│  ├─ Environmental/Occupational (Asbestosis, Silicosis, Coal worker's)
│  ├─ Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (Farmer's lung, Bird fancier's)
│  └─ Connective Tissue Disease (RA, SLE, SSc, PM/DM, Sjögren's)
│
├─ IDIOPATHIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIAS (35%)
│  ├─ Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) - MOST COMMON
│  ├─ Non-Specific Interstitial Pneumonia (NSIP)
│  ├─ Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia (COP)
│  ├─ Acute Interstitial Pneumonia (AIP)
│  ├─ Respiratory Bronchiolitis-ILD (RB-ILD)
│  └─ Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia (DIP)
│
└─ GRANULOMATOUS ILD (20%)
   ├─ Sarcoidosis - MOST COMMON GRANULOMATOUS ILD
   ├─ Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (Chronic)
   └─ Berylliosis

docs.title: Mnemonic: "GRODE" for ILD Classification
docs.text:
• G - Granulomatous (Sarcoidosis, HP)
  • R - Rheumatologic (CTD-ILD)
  • O - Occupational/Environmental (Pneumoconiosis)
  • D - Drug-induced
  • E - idiopathic (IPF, NSIP, COP)

docs.title: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF)
docs.text:
• Most common idiopathic ILD (~30% of all ILDs)
  • Age: >60 years (rare <50 years)
  • Sex: Male > Female (2:1)
  • Risk factors: Smoking, genetic factors (TERT, TERC mutations), GERD

docs.title: Sarcoidosis
docs.text:
• Most common ILD overall in young adults
  • Age: 20-40 years (bimodal: 20-40 and 50-60)
  • Sex: Female > Male
  • Race: African Americans (10-17x higher), Scandinavians
  • Löfgren syndrome: Acute sarcoidosis with excellent prognosis

docs.title: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis
docs.text:
• Occurs at any age
  • Related to occupational/environmental exposure
  • Farmer's lung (moldy hay), Bird fancier's lung (avian proteins)

docs.title: Common Pathway
docs.text:
INITIAL INJURY (Drugs/Environment/Autoimmune/Unknown)
           ↓
    ALVEOLAR INJURY
           ↓
    INFLAMMATION (Alveolitis)
    • Lymphocytes, Macrophages
    • Cytokine release (TGF-β, PDGF)
           ↓
    FIBROBLAST PROLIFERATION
           ↓
    COLLAGEN DEPOSITION
           ↓
    INTERSTITIAL FIBROSIS
           ↓
    ARCHITECTURAL DISTORTION
    • Honeycombing
    • Loss of alveolar-capillary units
           ↓
    RESTRICTIVE LUNG DISEASE
    • ↓ Lung volumes
    • ↓ Compliance
    • ↓ Diffusion capacity
           ↓
    PULMONARY HYPERTENSION (Late)
           ↓
    COR PULMONALE

docs.title: Key Pathologic Patterns
docs.text: Pattern Characteristic Feature Associated Disease UIP (Usual Interstitial Pneumonia) Honeycombing, subpleural/basal, heterogeneous, fibroblast foci IPF (definite), CTD-ILD NSIP (Non-Specific Interstitial Pneumonia) Homogeneous, temporally uniform, cellular or fibrotic CTD-ILD, Idiopathic NSIP OP (Organizing Pneumonia) Masson bodies (plugs in alveoli/bronchioles), patchy COP, Secondary OP DAD (Diffuse Alveolar Damage) Hyaline membranes, acute onset AIP, ARDS RB (Respiratory Bronchiolitis) Smoker's macrophages in bronchioles RB-ILD DIP (Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia) Diffuse alveolar macrophage accumulation DIP (smoking-related) Granulomatous Non-caseating granulomas Sarcoidosis, HP

docs.title: Common Symptoms (Across All ILDs)
docs.text:
1. Progressive Dyspnea on Exertion (MOST COMMON)
  2. Insidious onset over months-years

  3. Progressive breathlessness

  4. Dry Cough (Non-productive)

  5. Persistent, irritating

  6. May be the presenting symptom

  7. Constitutional Symptoms

  8. Fatigue, weight loss

  9. Low-grade fever (HP, Sarcoidosis)

  10. Chest Discomfort

  11. Vague chest tightness

docs.title: Physical Examination
docs.text:
1. Respiratory Examination
  2. Inspiratory Bibasilar Crackles ("Velcro crackles")
    • Most sensitive sign (90% of IPF)
    • Fine, dry crackles at lung bases

  3. Reduced chest expansion

  4. Digital Clubbing

  5. Present in 50-70% of IPF (HIGHLY SPECIFIC)

  6. Rare in Sarcoidosis (helps differentiate)

  7. Signs of Pulmonary Hypertension (Advanced disease)

  8. Loud P2, RV heave, JVP elevation

  9. Pedal edema, ascites

  10. Extrapulmonary Manifestations (Disease-specific)

  11. Sarcoidosis: Skin lesions, uveitis, lymphadenopathy
  12. CTD-ILD: Joint deformities, skin changes
  13. HP: None typically

docs.title: 1. Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs)
docs.text:
Classic Finding: RESTRICTIVE PATTERN with ↓ DLCO

Parameter Finding Explanation FVC ↓ Decreased Loss of lung volume FEV₁ ↓ Decreased Proportional to FVC FEV₁/FVC Normal or ↑ ≥0.70 (RESTRICTIVE) TLC ↓ Decreased ↓ Lung compliance RV ↓ Decreased Cannot fully expand DLCO ↓↓ Markedly Decreased Loss of alveolar-capillary surface A-a gradient ↑ Increased Impaired gas exchange

Key Point: ↓ DLCO is the most sensitive early marker of ILD.

docs.title: 2. Chest X-Ray
docs.text:
Classic Findings: - Bilateral reticular opacities (interstitial pattern) - Lower lobe predominance (IPF, NSIP, CTD-ILD) - Upper lobe predominance (Sarcoidosis, HP, Silicosis) - Honeycombing (Advanced IPF) - Reduced lung volumes

Limitations: Normal CXR in 10-15% of early ILD

docs.title: 3. High-Resolution CT (HRCT) Chest
docs.text:
GOLD STANDARD for ILD diagnosis and characterization

HRCT PATTERNS IN ILD
│
├─ UIP PATTERN (IPF)
│  • Subpleural, basal predominance
│  • Honeycombing (definite UIP)
│  • Reticular opacities
│  • Traction bronchiectasis
│  • Minimal ground-glass opacities
│  • Heterogeneous (patchy) distribution
│
├─ NSIP PATTERN
│  • Subpleural sparing (unlike UIP)
│  • Ground-glass opacities > reticular
│  • Homogeneous distribution
│  • Lower lobe predominance
│  • Minimal honeycombing
│
├─ ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA (OP)
│  • Patchy consolidation
│  • Peribronchovascular distribution
│  • Ground-glass opacities
│  • Subpleural/peribronchial
│  • "Reverse halo sign" (Atoll sign)
│
├─ HYPERSENSITIVITY PNEUMONITIS
│  • Ground-glass opacities (Acute/Subacute)
│  • Centrilobular nodules
│  • Mosaic attenuation (Air trapping)
│  • Upper/mid lobe predominance
│  • Fibrosis (Chronic HP)
│
└─ SARCOIDOSIS
   • Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL)
   • Perilymphatic nodules
   • Upper lobe predominance
   • Bronchocentric distribution
   • Fibrosis (Stage IV)

docs.title: 4. Laboratory Tests
docs.text: Test Purpose Finding CBC Anemia, eosinophilia Eosinophilia (Drug-induced ILD, CEP) ESR/CRP Inflammation ↑ in Sarcoidosis, CTD-ILD ANA Connective tissue disease Positive in SLE, SSc, Sjögren's RF Rheumatoid arthritis Positive in RA-ILD Anti-Scl-70 Systemic sclerosis SSc-ILD Anti-Jo-1 Myositis PM/DM-ILD ACE level Sarcoidosis ↑ in 60% (not specific) Serum calcium Sarcoidosis Hypercalcemia (10%) Serum precipitins Hypersensitivity pneumonitis Positive (exposure history)

docs.title: 5. Arterial Blood Gas (ABG)
docs.text: Early: Hypoxemia on exertion Advanced: Hypoxemia at rest A-a gradient: ↑ Increased (V/Q mismatch, diffusion limitation) Pattern: Type 1 Respiratory Failure (↓ PaO₂, Normal/↓ PaCO₂)

docs.title: 6. 6-Minute Walk Test (6MWT)
docs.text:
• Assess exercise capacity and desaturation
  • >4% drop in SpO₂ or <250 meters distance → Poor prognosis
  • Serial monitoring for disease progression

docs.title: 7. Bronchoscopy with BAL/Biopsy
docs.text:
Indications: - Uncertain diagnosis after HRCT - Rule out infection/malignancy - Specific diagnoses (Sarcoidosis, HP, Eosinophilic pneumonia)

Disease BAL Findings Sarcoidosis ↑ Lymphocytes, CD4/CD8 ratio >3.5 Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis ↑↑ Lymphocytes (>50%), CD4/CD8 ratio <1 Eosinophilic Pneumonia ↑↑ Eosinophils (>25%) Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis Milky fluid, PAS-positive material Hemorrhage Hemosiderin-laden macrophages IPF Neutrophils, eosinophils (non-specific)

Transbronchial Biopsy: - Good yield: Sarcoidosis (granulomas in 80%), Organizing pneumonia - Low yield: IPF, NSIP (need surgical biopsy)

docs.title: 8. Surgical Lung Biopsy
docs.text:
Indications: - Uncertain diagnosis after non-invasive tests - IPF diagnosis (if HRCT not typical) - Young patients requiring definitive diagnosis

Methods: - Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS) - Preferred - Open lung biopsy (if VATS not feasible)

Sites: Multiple lobes (heterogeneity in ILD), avoid lingula/middle lobe

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Most common and most lethal idiopathic interstitial pneumonia Histopathologic pattern: UIP (Usual Interstitial Pneumonia)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Age: >60 years (rare <50) Sex: Male > Female (2:1) Risk Factors: Smoking, GERD, family history, viral infections

Symptoms: - Progressive dyspnea over 6-24 months - Dry cough (persistent) - Fatigue, weight loss

Signs: - Bibasilar inspiratory crackles (Velcro crackles) - 90% - Clubbing (50-70%) - Cyanosis, pulmonary hypertension (advanced)

docs.title: HRCT Criteria for UIP Pattern
docs.text:
Feature Definite UIP Probable UIP Indeterminate Alternative Dx Honeycombing Present Absent Absent Absent Reticular opacities Subpleural/basal Subpleural/basal Subpleural/basal Variable Distribution Heterogeneous Heterogeneous Heterogeneous Diffuse Ground-glass Minimal Minimal Extensive Predominant Consolidation Absent Absent May be present Present Nodules/cysts Absent Absent May be present Present

IPF Diagnosis Algorithm:

HRCT → Definite UIP pattern + Clinical context
        ↓
        IPF DIAGNOSIS (No biopsy needed)

HRCT → Probable UIP/Indeterminate
        ↓
        SURGICAL LUNG BIOPSY (VATS)
        ↓
        UIP histology → IPF

docs.title: Histopathology (UIP Pattern)
docs.text: Key Features: 1. Heterogeneous involvement (patchy) 2. Temporal heterogeneity (old and new fibrosis) 3. Fibroblast foci (active fibrosis) 4. Honeycombing (end-stage) 5. Subpleural and paraseptal distribution 6. Minimal inflammation

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Median Survival: 2.5-3.5 years from diagnosis Poor Prognostic Factors: - Age >70 years - Male sex - Severe dyspnea (NYHA III/IV) - ↓↓ FVC (<50%), ↓↓ DLCO (<35%) - ↓ 6MWT distance (<250 m) or desaturation - Pulmonary hypertension - Acute exacerbation

GAP Index (Gender, Age, Physiology):

Variable Points Gender (Male) 1 Age (>60 years) 1 FVC 50-75% predicted 1 FVC <50% predicted 2 DLCO 36-55% predicted 1 DLCO ≤35% predicted 2
  • 0-3 points: Stage I (Mortality 6% at 1 year)
  • 4-5 points: Stage II (Mortality 16% at 1 year)
  • 6-8 points: Stage III (Mortality 39% at 1 year)

docs.title: Antifibrotic Therapy
docs.text:
Approved Drugs (Slow disease progression):

  1. Pirfenidone
  2. Mechanism: Anti-fibrotic, anti-inflammatory
  3. Dose: 801 mg TID (with food)
  4. Side effects: Photosensitivity, GI upset, elevated LFTs
  5. Monitoring: LFTs every month × 6, then q3 months

  6. Sun protection mandatory

  7. Nintedanib

  8. Mechanism: Tyrosine kinase inhibitor (PDGFR, FGFR, VEGFR)
  9. Dose: 150 mg BID
  10. Side effects: Diarrhea (60%), nausea, elevated LFTs
  11. Monitoring: LFTs, pregnancy test (teratogenic)

Evidence: Both drugs ↓ FVC decline by ~50%, no mortality benefit proven

docs.title: Supportive Care
docs.text:
• Oxygen therapy: For hypoxemia (SpO₂ <88%)
  • Pulmonary rehabilitation: Improves QOL
  • GERD treatment: PPIs (↓ microaspiration)
  • Vaccination: Influenza, pneumococcal
  • Smoking cessation: Mandatory

docs.title: Lung Transplantation
docs.text:
Indications: - Progressive disease despite antifibrotics - FVC <80% or DLCO <40% - Significant hypoxemia - 6MWT <250 m or desaturation

Timing: Early referral (GAP Stage II or III)

Contraindications: - Age >65-70 years (relative) - Severe comorbidities - Active malignancy

docs.title: Acute Exacerbation of IPF
docs.text:
Definition: Acute worsening of dyspnea (<1 month) without identifiable cause

Diagnostic Criteria: 1. Previous/concurrent IPF diagnosis 2. Acute worsening (<1 month) 3. HRCT: New bilateral ground-glass/consolidation 4. Not explained by infection, PE, heart failure, pneumothorax

Management: - High-dose corticosteroids: Methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV × 3 days - Supportive care: Oxygen, mechanical ventilation - Empiric antibiotics: If infection cannot be excluded - Consider lung transplant evaluation

Prognosis: In-hospital mortality 50-80%

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Immunologically mediated lung disease from repeated inhalation of organic antigens in sensitized individuals.

Also called: Extrinsic Allergic Alveolitis

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text: Disease Antigen Source Antigen Farmer's Lung Moldy hay, grain Thermophilic actinomycetes Bird Fancier's Lung Avian droppings, feathers Avian proteins Humidifier Lung Contaminated water Bacteria, fungi, amoebae Hot Tub Lung Hot tub water Mycobacterium avium complex Cheese Worker's Lung Cheese mold Penicillium casei Mushroom Worker's Lung Mushroom compost Thermophilic actinomycetes Bagassosis Moldy sugarcane Thermophilic actinomycetes Malt Worker's Lung Moldy barley Aspergillus clavatus

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Type III (Immune Complex) + Type IV (Cell-Mediated) Hypersensitivity

ANTIGEN INHALATION (in sensitized individual)
           ↓
    IMMUNE RESPONSE
    • Type III: Immune complex formation (4-8 hours)
    • Type IV: T-cell mediated (48-72 hours)
           ↓
    ACUTE HP: Alveolitis, PMNs
           ↓
    SUBACUTE HP: Granulomas, Lymphocytes
           ↓
    CHRONIC HP: Fibrosis, Honeycombing

docs.title: Acute HP
docs.text:
Timing: 4-8 hours after exposure

Symptoms: - Sudden onset dyspnea, cough - Fever, chills (flu-like illness) - Chest tightness

Signs: - Tachypnea, crackles - Fever

Recovery: Symptoms resolve in 24-48 hours if exposure stopped

Key Point: Recurrent acute episodes with re-exposure

docs.title: Subacute HP
docs.text:
Timing: Days to weeks of exposure

Symptoms: - Progressive dyspnea, cough - Fatigue, weight loss - Low-grade fever

Signs: - Bibasilar crackles - No clubbing

docs.title: Chronic HP
docs.text:
Timing: Months to years of low-level exposure

Symptoms: - Insidious dyspnea, cough - Weight loss, fatigue - No fever (unlike acute)

Signs: - Crackles - Clubbing (rare, <10%) - Pulmonary hypertension (advanced)

Key Point: Mimics IPF (difficult to differentiate)

docs.title: 1. History
docs.text: CRITICAL: Detailed exposure history - Occupation, hobbies (birds, farming) - Home environment (mold, humidifiers, hot tubs) - Temporal relationship (symptoms improve away from exposure)

docs.title: 2. HRCT Chest
docs.text:
Stage HRCT Findings Acute Diffuse ground-glass opacities, poorly defined centrilobular nodules Subacute Centrilobular nodules, ground-glass, mosaic attenuation (air trapping), upper/mid lobe Chronic Fibrosis (UIP-like pattern), traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing, upper lobe

Mosaic Attenuation on Expiratory CT: Highly suggestive of HP (air trapping)

docs.title: 3. Pulmonary Function Tests
docs.text:
• Restrictive pattern (↓ FVC, ↓ TLC)
  • ↓↓ DLCO (prominent)
