  • Air trapping (↑ RV/TLC ratio)
  • Mixed obstructive-restrictive in chronic HP

docs.title: 4. Serum Precipitins (IgG antibodies)
docs.text:
• Test for specific antigens (avian, thermophilic actinomycetes)
  • Positive: Indicates exposure, NOT necessarily disease
  • Negative: Does not rule out HP (sensitivity 70-90%)

docs.title: 5. Bronchoalveolar Lavage (BAL)
docs.text: Most helpful test: - Lymphocytosis (>50% in subacute, >30% in chronic) - CD4/CD8 ratio <1 (CD8+ predominance) - Opposite of Sarcoidosis - ↑ Mast cells

docs.title: 6. Lung Biopsy
docs.text: Histology: - Poorly-formed non-caseating granulomas (centrilobular) - Chronic bronchiolitis - Lymphocytic infiltration - Fibrosis (chronic HP)

docs.title: Diagnostic Criteria (Clinical Prediction Rule)
docs.text:
Major Criteria (need all 4): 1. Exposure to known antigen 2. Positive serum precipitins or BAL lymphocytosis 3. Compatible HRCT findings 4. Compatible histology (if biopsy done)

Minor Criteria: - Recurrent symptoms with re-exposure - Inspiratory crackles - Weight loss - Restrictive PFTs with ↓ DLCO

docs.title: Antigen Avoidance
docs.text:
MOST IMPORTANT: Eliminate exposure - Occupational changes - Remove birds, contaminated equipment - Environmental modifications (dehumidifiers, air filters)

Prognosis: Acute/Subacute HP → Complete recovery if antigen avoided early

docs.title: Corticosteroids
docs.text:
Indications: - Moderate-to-severe acute/subacute HP - Chronic HP with significant inflammation

Regimen: - Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day × 4-8 weeks - Taper over 3-6 months

Efficacy: Improves symptoms/PFTs in acute/subacute, limited benefit in chronic fibrotic HP

docs.title: Immunosuppressants
docs.text: For chronic fibrotic HP not responding to steroids: - Mycophenolate mofetil (1-3 g/day) - Azathioprine (2 mg/kg/day)

docs.title: Antifibrotics
docs.text: Nintedanib: FDA-approved for progressive fibrosing ILD (includes chronic HP)

docs.title: Lung Transplantation
docs.text: For end-stage chronic HP

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Multisystem granulomatous disease of unknown etiology Characterized by non-caseating granulomas

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Peak age: 20-40 years (bimodal: also 50-60)
  • Female > Male
  • Race: African Americans (10-17x), Scandinavians
  • Organ involvement: Lungs (90%), Lymph nodes (90%), Skin (25%), Eyes (25%), Liver, Heart

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
UNKNOWN TRIGGER (Infection? Environmental?)
           ↓
    CD4+ T-CELL ACTIVATION
           ↓
    TH1 CYTOKINE RELEASE (IFN-γ, IL-2, TNF-α)
           ↓
    MACROPHAGE ACTIVATION
           ↓
    NON-CASEATING GRANULOMA FORMATION
    • Epithelioid cells
    • Multinucleated giant cells (Langhans)
    • CD4+ T-cells at periphery
           ↓
    OUTCOMES
    • Spontaneous resolution (60-70%)
    • Persistent disease (20-30%)
    • Fibrosis (10-20%)

docs.title: Pulmonary Sarcoidosis (90%)
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Asymptomatic (30-50%) - Incidental CXR finding - Dry cough, dyspnea - Chest discomfort - Wheezing (rare)

Signs: - Crackles (uncommon, unlike IPF) - Clubbing is RARE (<5%) - Helps differentiate from IPF

docs.title: Extrapulmonary Sarcoidosis
docs.text: Organ Manifestations Investigations Lymph Nodes Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL), mediastinal LAD CXR/CT Skin (25%) Erythema nodosum (acute), Lupus pernio (chronic, violaceous plaques on nose/cheeks) Biopsy Eyes (25%) Uveitis (anterior > posterior), lacrimal gland enlargement Slit-lamp exam Cardiac (5%) Arrhythmias (VT, heart block), cardiomyopathy, sudden death ECG, echo, cardiac MRI Nervous (5%) Cranial nerve palsy (CN VII - Bell's palsy), meningitis, seizures MRI, CSF Liver Hepatomegaly, cholestasis, cirrhosis (rare) LFTs, biopsy Kidney Hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis Serum Ca²⁺, PTH Bone Osteolytic lesions (phalanges), dactylitis X-ray Spleen Splenomegaly Exam, imaging

docs.title: Classic Syndromes
docs.text:
1. Löfgren Syndrome (Acute Sarcoidosis) - Triad: Erythema nodosum + BHL + Arthralgias (ankles) - Excellent prognosis: 80-90% spontaneous remission - Treatment: NSAIDs, rarely steroids

2. Heerfordt Syndrome (Uveoparotid Fever) - Tetrad: Uveitis + Parotitis + Fever + Facial nerve palsy (CN VII)

3. Mikulicz Syndrome - Bilateral enlargement of lacrimal and parotid glands

docs.title: Staging (CXR)
docs.text:
Stage CXR Findings Frequency Spontaneous Resolution 0 Normal CXR 5% N/A I BHL alone (no parenchymal involvement) 50% 60-80% II BHL + Parenchymal infiltrates 30% 50-60% III Parenchymal infiltrates without BHL 10% 30% IV Pulmonary fibrosis (honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis) 5% 0% (irreversible)

Key Point: BHL is BILATERAL and SYMMETRIC (if asymmetric → suspect malignancy)

docs.title: HRCT Findings
docs.text:
• Perilymphatic nodules (along bronchovascular bundles, subpleural, interlobular septa)
  • Upper lobe predominance (unlike IPF)
  • BHL with calcification (eggshell pattern in late stages)
  • Fibrosis: Bronchocentric, upper lobe, traction bronchiectasis

docs.title: Laboratory Tests
docs.text:
Test Finding Utility Serum ACE Elevated in 60% Monitor disease activity (not diagnostic) Serum calcium Hypercalcemia (10%) Granulomas produce calcitriol (1,25-OH Vit D) 24-hr urine calcium Hypercalciuria (40%) More sensitive than serum calcium Serum lysozyme Elevated Alternative to ACE CBC Lymphopenia, anemia Non-specific LFTs ↑ ALP, ↑ GGT Liver/bone involvement

ACE Level: - Elevated in 60% of active sarcoidosis - NOT specific (also elevated in TB, silicosis, Gaucher's) - Useful for monitoring treatment response

docs.title: Bronchoalveolar Lavage (BAL)
docs.text:
• Lymphocytosis (>15%)
  • CD4/CD8 ratio >3.5 (CD4+ predominance) - Opposite of HP

docs.title: Tissue Biopsy
docs.text:
Gold Standard: Non-caseating granulomas (epithelioid cells, Langhans giant cells, no necrosis)

Biopsy sites (in order of preference): 1. Skin lesions (if present) - Easiest 2. Transbronchial lung biopsy (TBLB) - Yield 80-90% in Stage II/III 3. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy (EBUS) - Mediastinal lymph nodes 4. Mediastinoscopy - If bronchoscopy non-diagnostic 5. Kveim test (historical, no longer used)

Important: Must exclude infection (AFB, fungal stains)

docs.title: Indications for Treatment
docs.text:
NOT all patients require treatment (many have spontaneous remission)

Treat if: 1. Symptomatic disease (dyspnea, cough) 2. Organ-threatening disease: - Cardiac sarcoidosis (arrhythmias) - Neurologic sarcoidosis - Ocular sarcoidosis (posterior uveitis, threat to vision) - Hypercalcemia 3. Progressive pulmonary disease (↓ PFTs, worsening CXR) 4. Stage II/III with symptoms

Do NOT treat: - Asymptomatic Stage I (BHL alone) - High rate of spontaneous resolution - Asymptomatic Stage II with stable PFTs

docs.title: Corticosteroids (First-Line)
docs.text:
Regimen: - Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (20-40 mg/day) × 4-6 weeks - Taper to 10-20 mg/day over 6 months - Total duration: 12-24 months (longer than HP)

Response: Improves symptoms, CXR, PFTs in 60-80%

Monitoring: - PFTs every 3-6 months - CXR/HRCT - Serum ACE (if initially elevated)

docs.title: Steroid-Sparing Agents (Second-Line)
docs.text:
Indications: Steroid-refractory, intolerable side effects, need for prolonged therapy

  1. Methotrexate (10-25 mg/week)
  2. Most commonly used

  3. Monitor: CBC, LFTs, avoid alcohol

  4. Azathioprine (2-3 mg/kg/day)

  5. Check TPMT level before starting

  6. Mycophenolate mofetil (1-3 g/day)

  7. Leflunomide (10-20 mg/day)

docs.title: Biologics (Third-Line)
docs.text: Infliximab (Anti-TNF-α): - Refractory sarcoidosis - Dose: 3-5 mg/kg at 0, 2, 6 weeks, then q8 weeks - FDA-approved for pulmonary, cutaneous, extrapulmonary sarcoidosis

docs.title: Organ-Specific Therapy
docs.text: Organ Treatment Cardiac High-dose steroids (40-60 mg/day), AICD for VT/VF, pacemaker for heart block Neurologic High-dose steroids, MTX/AZA, infliximab Ocular Topical steroids (anterior uveitis), systemic steroids (posterior uveitis) Hypercalcemia Steroids, avoid Vitamin D and sun exposure, hydration Skin Topical/intralesional steroids, antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine)

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
• 60-70%: Spontaneous remission within 2 years
  • 20-30%: Persistent disease requiring treatment
  • 10-20%: Progressive fibrosis
  • Mortality: 1-5% (cardiac, neurologic, respiratory failure)

Favorable Prognosis: - Löfgren syndrome - Stage I disease - Asymptomatic - Age <40 years

Poor Prognosis: - Stage III/IV - Lupus pernio - Cardiac/neurologic involvement - African American race - Age >40 years at onset

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: ILD occurs in 10-40% of patients with CTD Most common in Systemic Sclerosis (SSc-ILD)

docs.title: Specific CTD-ILD
docs.text: CTD ILD Frequency Pattern Key Features Systemic Sclerosis (SSc) 70-90% NSIP > UIP Most common CTD-ILD, Anti-Scl-70 Ab, subpleural sparing on HRCT Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) 10-30% UIP > NSIP Male smokers, RA-ILD more common than RA-arthritis in some, Anti-CCP Ab Polymyositis/Dermatomyositis 40-65% NSIP, OP Anti-Jo-1 (Anti-synthetase syndrome), Rapidly progressive Sjögren's Syndrome 10-20% NSIP, LIP Xerostomia, keratoconjunctivitis sicca, Anti-Ro/La Ab SLE 3-10% Acute lupus pneumonitis, NSIP Pleuritis more common than ILD, Anti-dsDNA Ab Mixed CTD 20-40% NSIP Anti-U1 RNP Ab

docs.title: Clinical Approach
docs.text: Suspect CTD-ILD if: - ILD in a young patient (<50 years) - ILD + arthritis/skin rash/Raynaud's/myositis - Positive ANA/RF/Anti-CCP - HRCT shows NSIP pattern (more common than UIP in CTD-ILD)

docs.title: Systemic Sclerosis-ILD (SSc-ILD)
docs.text:
Most common cause of death in SSc

Risk Factors: - Diffuse cutaneous SSc (dcSSc) - Anti-Scl-70 (Anti-topoisomerase I) antibody - African American race

HRCT: NSIP > UIP, subpleural sparing (unlike IPF)

Treatment: - Mycophenolate mofetil (1-3 g/day) - First-line - Cyclophosphamide (alternative) - Nintedanib (FDA-approved for SSc-ILD) - Avoid steroids alone (↑ renal crisis risk)

docs.title: RA-ILD
docs.text:
Epidemiology: Male smokers, Anti-CCP positive

Patterns: UIP > NSIP, Organizing pneumonia, Follicular bronchiolitis

Drug-Induced ILD: Methotrexate (hypersensitivity), anti-TNF agents

Treatment: - Steroids + steroid-sparing agents (AZA, MMF) - Avoid MTX if drug-induced - Nintedanib for progressive fibrosing ILD

docs.title: Anti-Synthetase Syndrome
docs.text:
Associated with: Polymyositis/Dermatomyositis

Antibodies: Anti-Jo-1 (most common), Anti-PL-7, Anti-PL-12

Clinical Features - "Mechanic's Hands, Myositis, ILD": - ILD (often presenting feature) - Myositis (proximal muscle weakness) - Arthritis (non-erosive) - Mechanic's hands (hyperkeratosis, fissures) - Raynaud's phenomenon - Fever

Pattern: NSIP, Organizing pneumonia, rapidly progressive

Treatment: - High-dose steroids + Immunosuppressants (AZA, MMF, CYC) - Rituximab (refractory cases)

docs.title: Treatment Approach to CTD-ILD
docs.text:
1. Treat underlying CTD
  2. Immunosuppression (more effective than in IPF):
  3. Steroids + MMF/AZA/CYC
  4. Antifibrotics (if progressive fibrosing ILD):
  5. Nintedanib (FDA-approved for SSc-ILD and progressive fibrosing ILD)
  6. Lung transplant (end-stage)

docs.title: Common Drugs
docs.text: Drug Class Specific Drugs Pattern Chemotherapy Bleomycin, Methotrexate, Cyclophosphamide, Gemcitabine Pulmonary fibrosis, HP-like Antibiotics Nitrofurantoin (most common), Sulfasalazine Acute/Chronic pneumonitis Cardiovascular Amiodarone, Statins, Beta-blockers Chronic interstitial pneumonitis, ARDS Biologics Anti-TNF (Infliximab, Etanercept), Rituximab, Checkpoint inhibitors (Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab) NSIP, OP, HP-like Antimalarial Hydroxychloroquine Rare Illicit Cocaine, Heroin Acute lung injury, eosinophilic pneumonia

docs.title: Mnemonic: "BANANA" for Common Drug-Induced ILD
docs.text:
• B - Bleomycin
  • A - Amiodarone
  • N - Nitrofurantoin
  • A - Anti-TNF agents
  • N - Nivolumab/Checkpoint inhibitors
  • A - Azathioprine (rare)

docs.title: Amiodarone
docs.text:
Frequency: 5-15% of chronic users

Risk Factors: - Cumulative dose >400 g (or >400 mg/day × 2 months) - Pre-existing lung disease - Thoracic surgery (↑ oxygen toxicity)

Presentation: - Insidious dyspnea, cough - Months-years after starting

HRCT: Diffuse ground-glass, consolidation, high attenuation (iodine content)

Diagnosis: High suspicion + compatible imaging + exclusion of other causes

Treatment: - STOP amiodarone (long half-life: 40-60 days) - Steroids (Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day × 4-6 weeks, then taper) - Recovery in most, but can be fatal

docs.title: Bleomycin
docs.text:
Frequency: 10% (dose-dependent)

Risk Factors: - Cumulative dose >450 units - Age >70 years - Renal impairment - Oxygen therapy, radiation

Presentation: - Acute pneumonitis → Chronic fibrosis - Dyspnea, dry cough during/after chemotherapy

HRCT: NSIP pattern, basal predominant

Management: - STOP bleomycin - Steroids (benefit unclear) - Avoid supplemental oxygen (↑ toxicity)

Prevention: Limit cumulative dose, monitor PFTs (↓ DLCO early sign)

docs.title: Nitrofurantoin
docs.text:
Most common antibiotic causing ILD

Forms: 1. Acute (within days-weeks): - Fever, dyspnea, cough - Peripheral eosinophilia (30%) - CXR: Bilateral infiltrates, pleural effusion - Rapid improvement after stopping drug

  1. Chronic (months-years):
  2. Insidious dyspnea
  3. CXR/HRCT: Fibrosis (NSIP/UIP pattern)
  4. May not improve after stopping

Treatment: STOP drug, steroids (acute form)

docs.title: Checkpoint Inhibitors (Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Pneumonitis)
docs.text:
Drugs: Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab, Atezolizumab (Anti-PD-1/PD-L1)

Frequency: 3-5%

Presentation: - Dyspnea, cough - Median onset: 2-3 months (range: 1 week - 2 years)

HRCT: Organizing pneumonia, NSIP, hypersensitivity pneumonitis-like

Management: - Grade 1 (Asymptomatic): Continue drug, monitor - Grade 2 (Symptomatic): Hold drug, Steroids (0.5-1 mg/kg/day) - Grade 3-4 (Severe): STOP drug permanently, High-dose steroids (1-2 mg/kg/day) - Infliximab if refractory (paradoxical, but anti-TNF helps)

docs.title: General Approach to Drug-Induced ILD
docs.text:
1. High index of suspicion (temporal relationship)
  2. Exclude other causes (infection, CHF, PE)
  3. STOP offending drug
  4. Steroids (if severe or not improving)
  5. Re-challenge NOT recommended (risk of recurrence)

Resource: www.pneumotox.com (comprehensive database)

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text: Strategy Details Treat underlying cause Antigen avoidance (HP), immunosuppression (CTD-ILD), stop drug (Drug-ILD) Antifibrotics IPF, progressive fibrosing ILD Immunosuppression Sarcoidosis, HP, CTD-ILD, inflammatory ILDs Supportive care Oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccinations Lung transplantation End-stage ILD

docs.title: Oxygen Therapy
docs.text:
Indications: - Resting SpO₂ ≤88% OR - Exercise desaturation ≤88% OR - PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg

Goal: SpO₂ ≥90% (PaO₂ ≥60 mmHg)

Evidence: Improves survival in IPF patients with hypoxemia

docs.title: Pulmonary Rehabilitation
docs.text:
Components: - Exercise training (aerobic + strength) - Breathing techniques - Nutritional counseling - Psychosocial support

Benefits: - Improves 6MWT distance - Reduces dyspnea - Improves quality of life - No survival benefit

docs.title: Vaccinations
docs.text: Recommended: - Influenza (annual) - Pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23) - COVID-19

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
Schedule: Every 3-6 months (more frequent if progressive)

Test Purpose PFTs Assess progression (↓ FVC ≥10% or ↓ DLCO ≥15% → progressive) 6MWT Functional capacity, desaturation CXR/HRCT Structural changes (annual HRCT) Pulse oximetry Hypoxemia

docs.title: Lung Transplantation
docs.text:
Indications (General): - Progressive disease despite optimal therapy - Severe functional limitation (NYHA III-IV) - Hypoxemia - 6MWT <250 m

Disease-Specific: - IPF: Refer early (median survival 3 years) - Sarcoidosis: Stage IV, pulmonary hypertension - CTD-ILD: Progressive despite immunosuppression

Post-Transplant Survival: - Median survival: 5-6 years - IPF has better post-transplant outcomes than other ILDs

docs.title: 1. Respiratory Failure
docs.text:
Type I (Hypoxemic): - Common in advanced ILD - Due to V/Q mismatch, diffusion limitation

Type II (Hypercapnic): - Rare in ILD (unless severe end-stage)

Management: Oxygen, mechanical ventilation (prognosis poor if intubated)

docs.title: 2. Pulmonary Hypertension (PH)
docs.text:
Frequency: 30-40% of advanced ILD

Pathophysiology: - Hypoxic vasoconstriction - Vascular remodeling - Destruction of pulmonary capillary bed

Diagnosis: - Echocardiography: RVSP >40 mmHg (screening) - Right heart catheterization: mPAP ≥20 mmHg (diagnostic)

Classification: WHO Group 3 PH (due to lung disease)

Treatment: - Oxygen therapy (most important) - Treat underlying ILD - PH-specific therapy: Generally NOT recommended (INCREASE trial showed harm with Bosentan in IPF) - Exception: Severe PH (mPAP >35 mmHg) → Consider PH therapies in specialized centers

Prognosis: ↓↓ Survival (median 1-2 years)

docs.title: 3. Lung Cancer
docs.text:
Risk: Increased in IPF, Sarcoidosis (Stage IV), Asbestosis

IPF: 5-year lung cancer risk ~15%

Screening: Not routinely recommended, but low-dose CT may be considered in high-risk patients

docs.title: 4. Infection
docs.text:
Risk factors: Immunosuppression, structural lung disease

Common: - Bacterial pneumonia - Opportunistic (PCP, Aspergillus) - especially on chronic steroids

Prevention: - PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) if on high-dose steroids (>20 mg/day × >1 month) + other immunosuppressant

docs.title: 5. Pneumothorax
docs.text:
Risk: Cystic lung diseases, advanced ILD with honeycombing

Management: Chest tube drainage, VATS pleurodesis

docs.title: 6. Acute Exacerbation
docs.text:
Definition: Acute worsening of ILD without identifiable cause

Occurs in: IPF (most common), other ILDs

Management: High-dose steroids, supportive care

Mortality: 50-80%

docs.title: Must-Know Facts
docs.text:
1. ILD = Restrictive Pattern with ↓↓ DLCO
  2. FEV₁/FVC ≥0.70 (normal or ↑)
  3. ↓ TLC, ↓ FVC

  4. DLCO is the most sensitive early marker

  5. HRCT is the GOLD STANDARD for ILD diagnosis

  6. IPF:

  7. Most common and most lethal idiopathic ILD
  8. UIP pattern on HRCT: Honeycombing + subpleural/basal distribution
  9. Clubbing in 50-70% (highly specific)
  10. Treatment: Pirfenidone or Nintedanib (antifibrotics)

  11. Median survival: 2.5-3.5 years

  12. Sarcoidosis:

  13. Most common ILD in young adults
  14. Non-caseating granulomas
  15. BHL (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy) - Stage I
  16. Clubbing is RARE (<5%) - Differentiates from IPF
  17. CD4/CD8 ratio >3.5 (opposite of HP)
  18. Löfgren syndrome: Erythema nodosum + BHL + Arthralgias → Excellent prognosis

  19. Treatment: Steroids (not all patients need treatment)

  20. Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis:

  21. Acute: 4-8 hours after exposure, fever, dyspnea → resolves in 24-48 hours
  22. Chronic: Mimics IPF
  23. Mosaic attenuation (air trapping) on HRCT - highly suggestive
  24. CD4/CD8 ratio <1 (opposite of Sarcoidosis)

  25. Treatment: Antigen avoidance (most important)

  26. CTD-ILD:

  27. SSc-ILD: Most common CTD-ILD (70-90%), NSIP > UIP, Anti-Scl-70
  28. RA-ILD: Male smokers, UIP > NSIP
  29. Anti-Synthetase Syndrome: Anti-Jo-1, Mechanic's hands, rapidly progressive

  30. NSIP pattern more common in CTD-ILD than IPF

  31. Drug-Induced ILD:

  32. "BANANA": Bleomycin, Amiodarone, Nitrofurantoin, Anti-TNF, Nivolumab, Azathioprine
  33. Amiodarone: High attenuation on HRCT (iodine), cumulative dose >400 g
  34. Nitrofurantoin: Most common antibiotic, acute (eosinophilia) vs chronic
  35. Treatment: STOP drug + Steroids

docs.title: IPF vs Sarcoidosis vs HP
docs.text: Feature IPF Sarcoidosis HP Age >60 years 20-40 years Any age Clubbing 50-70% Rare (<5%) Rare (<10%) HRCT pattern UIP (honeycombing, basal) Perilymphatic nodules, upper lobe Mosaic attenuation, centrilobular nodules BAL CD4/CD8 <1 (non-specific) >3.5 <1 Prognosis Poor (2.5-3.5 yr) Good (60-70% remit) Good if antigen avoided Treatment Antifibrotics Steroids (if indicated) Antigen avoidance

docs.title: UIP vs NSIP vs OP
docs.text: Feature UIP NSIP OP Distribution Heterogeneous, subpleural/basal Homogeneous, subpleural sparing Patchy, peribronchovascular Honeycombing Present (definite UIP) Minimal/Absent Absent Ground-glass Minimal Prominent Prominent Prognosis Poor Intermediate Good Steroid response Poor Good Excellent

docs.title: High-Yield Drug Side Effects
docs.text: Drug Side Effect Monitoring Pirfenidone Photosensitivity, GI upset, ↑ LFTs LFTs monthly × 6, then q3mo; Sun protection Nintedanib Diarrhea (60%), nausea, ↑ LFTs LFTs, pregnancy test (teratogenic) Prednisone Hyperglycemia, HTN, osteoporosis, infections Blood glucose, BP, DEXA, PCP prophylaxis

docs.title: Diagnostic Clues
docs.text:
1. Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy → Sarcoidosis (if symmetric) or Lymphoma (if asymmetric)

  2. Mosaic attenuation on expiratory HRCT → Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

  3. High attenuation on HRCT → Amiodarone pulmonary toxicity

  4. Honeycombing + clubbing + age >60 → IPF

  5. Erythema nodosum + BHL + arthralgias → Löfgren syndrome (acute sarcoidosis)

  6. Mechanic's hands + myositis + ILD → Anti-Synthetase Syndrome (Anti-Jo-1)

  7. Subpleural sparing on HRCT → NSIP (common in SSc-ILD)

  8. Reverse halo sign (Atoll sign) → Organizing Pneumonia

  9. Peripheral eosinophilia + acute pneumonitis → Drug-induced ILD (Nitrofurantoin) or Eosinophilic pneumonia

  10. Upper lobe fibrosis → SAFTB: Sarcoidosis, Ankylosing spondylitis, Fungal (ABPA), Tuberculosis, Berylliosis

docs.title: IPF Management
docs.text:
IPF DIAGNOSIS
    ↓
ANTIFIBROTIC THERAPY
• Pirfenidone OR Nintedanib
• Start at diagnosis (regardless of severity)
    ↓
SUPPORTIVE CARE
• Oxygen (if SpO₂ ≤88%)
• Pulmonary rehabilitation
• GERD treatment (PPI)
• Vaccinations
    ↓
MONITOR (q3-6 months)
• PFTs (↓ FVC ≥10% or ↓ DLCO ≥15% → progressive)
• 6MWT
• HRCT (annually)
    ↓
LUNG TRANSPLANT REFERRAL
• If progressive despite antifibrotics
• Early referral (GAP Stage II/III)

docs.title: Sarcoidosis Treatment
docs.text:
SARCOIDOSIS DIAGNOSIS (Biopsy-proven)
    ↓
DETERMINE NEED FOR TREATMENT
    ↓
    ├─ NO TREATMENT NEEDED
    │  • Stage I (BHL alone, asymptomatic)
    │  • Asymptomatic Stage II with stable PFTs
    │  → MONITOR
    │
    └─ TREATMENT INDICATED
       • Symptomatic disease
       • Organ-threatening (cardiac, neuro, ocular)
       • Progressive pulmonary disease
       ↓
       PREDNISONE 0.5-1 mg/kg/day
       • Taper over 6 months
       • Total duration: 12-24 months
       ↓
       IF REFRACTORY or SIDE EFFECTS
       ↓
       STEROID-SPARING AGENTS
       • Methotrexate (most common)
       • Azathioprine, MMF, Leflunomide
       ↓
       IF STILL REFRACTORY
       ↓
       INFLIXIMAB (Anti-TNF)

docs.title: Formulae and Scores
docs.text:
GAP Index (Gender, Age, Physiology) - IPF Prognosis: - Gender (Male): 1 point - Age >60: 1 point - FVC 50-75%: 1 point; <50%: 2 points - DLCO 36-55%: 1 point; ≤35%: 2 points

Stages: - 0-3: Stage I (6% 1-year mortality) - 4-5: Stage II (16% 1-year mortality) - 6-8: Stage III (39% 1-year mortality)

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "GRODE" - ILD Classification:

  2. **G**ranulomatous, **R**heumatologic, **O**ccupational, **D**rug-induced, id**E**opathic

  3. "BANANA" - Drug-Induced ILD:

  4. **B**leomycin, **A**miodarone, **N**itrofurantoin, **A**nti-TNF, **N**ivolumab, **A**zathioprine

  5. "SAFTB" - Upper Lobe Fibrosis:

  6. **S**arcoidosis, **A**nkylosing spondylitis, **F**ungal (ABPA), **T**uberculosis, **B**erylliosis

  7. Löfgren Syndrome - "EBA":

  8. **E**rythema nodosum, **B**HL, **A**rthralgias

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. All ILD patients need treatment ❌

  2. Sarcoidosis Stage I (asymptomatic BHL) → Monitor only

  3. All ILDs are steroid-responsive ❌

  4. IPF: Steroids NOT recommended (may be harmful)

  5. Use antifibrotics instead

  6. Clubbing is common in Sarcoidosis ❌

  7. Clubbing is RARE in Sarcoidosis (<5%)

  8. Common in IPF (50-70%)

  9. DLCO is normal in early ILD ❌

  10. ↓ DLCO is the most sensitive early marker

  11. UIP pattern always means IPF ❌

  12. UIP can occur in CTD-ILD (RA, SSc), chronic HP, drug-induced ILD

  13. Need clinical correlation

  14. Positive serum precipitins = Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis ❌

  15. Indicates exposure, NOT necessarily disease

  16. Need compatible clinical + HRCT + BAL

  17. Elevated ACE is diagnostic of Sarcoidosis ❌

  18. Elevated in only 60%, NOT specific
  19. Biopsy (non-caseating granulomas) is gold standard

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 65-year-old male smoker presents with progressive dyspnea and dry cough over 18 months. Examination reveals bibasilar inspiratory crackles and clubbing. HRCT chest shows subpleural honeycombing with basal predominance. PFTs show FVC 55%, FEV₁/FVC 0.78, DLCO 35%. What is the most appropriate treatment?

A. Prednisone 40 mg/day B. Azathioprine C. Pirfenidone D. Methotrexate

Answer: C. Pirfenidone

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) - Age >60, male, smoker, progressive dyspnea - Clubbing (50-70% in IPF) - Bibasilar crackles - HRCT: Honeycombing + subpleural/basal distribution = Definite UIP pattern - PFTs: Restrictive (FEV₁/FVC >0.70), ↓↓ DLCO

  • Treatment:
  • Pirfenidone or Nintedanib (antifibrotics) - First-line
  • Prednisone is NOT recommended in IPF (may be harmful, no proven benefit)

  • Azathioprine, Methotrexate: No role in IPF

  • Key Point: UIP pattern on HRCT (honeycombing + subpleural/basal) in appropriate clinical setting → Diagnose IPF without biopsy → Start antifibrotic therapy

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 32-year-old African American woman presents with incidental bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest X-ray done for routine health checkup. She is asymptomatic. PFTs are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Start prednisone 40 mg/day B. Bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy C. Monitor with serial CXR and PFTs D. Mediastinoscopy

Answer: C. Monitor with serial CXR and PFTs

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Sarcoidosis Stage I (BHL alone, no parenchymal involvement) - Young African American woman (high-risk demographic) - Asymptomatic, incidental finding - BHL on CXR

  • Management:
  • Stage I Sarcoidosis with no symptoms → Monitor only
  • 60-80% spontaneous remission without treatment
  • No need for biopsy if classic presentation (can confirm if needed, but not mandatory)

  • NO steroids for asymptomatic Stage I

  • When to treat Sarcoidosis:

  • Symptomatic disease
  • Organ-threatening (cardiac, neuro, ocular)

  • Progressive pulmonary disease (worsening PFTs, symptoms)

  • Key Point: Not all Sarcoidosis patients need treatment. Asymptomatic Stage I → Observe.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 45-year-old farmer presents with fever, dyspnea, and cough that started 6 hours after working with moldy hay. Symptoms resolve after 2 days. This has happened 3 times in the past month, always after working in the barn. HRCT shows ground-glass opacities and centrilobular nodules. BAL shows 65% lymphocytes with CD4/CD8 ratio of 0.5. What is the diagnosis?

A. Sarcoidosis B. Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis C. Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis D. Eosinophilic Pneumonia

Answer: C. Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (Farmer's Lung) - Acute HP: Symptoms 4-8 hours after exposure (moldy hay) - Recurrent episodes with re-exposure - Symptoms resolve when away from exposure (24-48 hours) - Farmer → Exposed to thermophilic actinomycetes in moldy hay

  • HRCT: Ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodules (typical of acute/subacute HP)

  • BAL:

  • Lymphocytosis (>50% in subacute, >30% in acute)

  • CD4/CD8 ratio <1 (CD8+ predominance) - KEY FINDING

  • Differentiating from Sarcoidosis:

  • Sarcoidosis: CD4/CD8 ratio >3.5 (CD4+ predominance)

  • HP: CD4/CD8 ratio <1 (CD8+ predominance)

  • Treatment: Antigen avoidance (most important), steroids if severe

  • Key Point: Acute HP = Recurrent flu-like illness 4-8 hours after specific exposure + BAL CD4/CD8 <1

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 70-year-old man on chronic amiodarone therapy (400 mg/day for 3 years) for atrial fibrillation presents with progressive dyspnea over 6 months. HRCT chest shows bilateral ground-glass opacities with areas of high attenuation. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Continue amiodarone, add prednisone B. Stop amiodarone, start prednisone C. Increase amiodarone dose D. Switch to sotalol

Answer: B. Stop amiodarone, start prednisone

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Amiodarone Pulmonary Toxicity - Chronic amiodarone (400 mg/day × 3 years) → High cumulative dose - Progressive dyspnea - HRCT: High attenuation (pathognomonic due to iodine content in amiodarone)

  • Treatment:
  • STOP amiodarone (MOST IMPORTANT)
    • Note: Amiodarone has long half-life (40-60 days), so toxicity may persist
  • Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day × 4-6 weeks, then taper

  • Switch to alternative antiarrhythmic (if needed)

  • Option A is WRONG: Continuing amiodarone will worsen toxicity despite steroids

  • Key Point: High attenuation on HRCT = Amiodarone toxicity. Management = STOP drug + Steroids.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 55-year-old woman with systemic sclerosis (diffuse cutaneous type) and positive Anti-Scl-70 antibody presents with progressive dyspnea. HRCT shows ground-glass opacities with subpleural sparing. PFTs show FVC 60%, DLCO 45%. What is the most appropriate treatment?

A. Prednisone alone B. Pirfenidone C. Mycophenolate mofetil D. Observation

Answer: C. Mycophenolate mofetil

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Systemic Sclerosis-Interstitial Lung Disease (SSc-ILD) - Diffuse cutaneous SSc (high risk for ILD) - Anti-Scl-70 (Anti-topoisomerase I) antibody (risk factor for ILD) - HRCT: Ground-glass with subpleural sparing (typical of NSIP pattern in SSc-ILD) - Significant functional impairment (FVC 60%, DLCO 45%)

  • Treatment:
  • Mycophenolate mofetil (1-3 g/day) - First-line for SSc-ILD
  • Alternative: Cyclophosphamide

  • Nintedanib: FDA-approved for SSc-ILD (can add if progressive)

  • Why NOT Prednisone alone?

  • Steroids alone are NOT recommended in SSc-ILD
  • ↑ Risk of scleroderma renal crisis

  • Need immunosuppressant (MMF/CYC)

  • Why NOT Pirfenidone?

  • Pirfenidone is for IPF, not CTD-ILD

  • Nintedanib is the antifibrotic approved for SSc-ILD (not Pirfenidone)

  • Key Point: SSc-ILD → Mycophenolate mofetil (First-line). Nintedanib can be added for progressive fibrosing ILD.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 28-year-old woman presents with erythema nodosum on shins, bilateral ankle arthritis, and dyspnea. CXR shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most appropriate management?

A. Prednisone 40 mg/day B. NSAIDs and observation C. Methotrexate D. Infliximab

Answer: B. NSAIDs and observation

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Löfgren Syndrome (Acute Sarcoidosis) - Triad: Erythema nodosum + BHL + Arthralgias (bilateral ankles) - Young woman

  • Prognosis: Excellent (80-90% spontaneous remission)

  • Management:

  • NSAIDs for symptomatic relief (arthritis, erythema nodosum)
  • Observation (most resolve without treatment)

  • Steroids rarely needed (only if severe symptoms)

  • Why NOT Prednisone?

  • Löfgren syndrome has excellent prognosis without treatment

  • Reserve steroids for severe/refractory cases

  • Key Point: Löfgren Syndrome = Excellent prognosis → NSAIDs + Observation (NOT steroids initially)

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Which of the following PFT findings is most characteristic of Interstitial Lung Disease?

A. ↓ FEV₁/FVC ratio (<0.70) B. ↑ Total Lung Capacity (TLC) C. ↓ Diffusing Capacity (DLCO) D. ↑ Residual Volume (RV)

Answer: C. ↓ Diffusing Capacity (DLCO)

Explanation: - ILD = Restrictive pattern with ↓↓ DLCO

PFT Findings in ILD: | Parameter | Finding | |-----------|---------| | FVC | ↓ Decreased | | FEV₁ | ↓ Decreased | | FEV₁/FVC | Normal or ↑ (≥0.70) - RESTRICTIVE | | TLC | ↓ Decreased | | RV | ↓ Decreased | | DLCO | ↓↓ Markedly Decreased |

  • ↓ DLCO:
  • Most sensitive early marker of ILD
  • Reflects loss of alveolar-capillary surface area

  • May be decreased even when lung volumes are normal (early disease)

  • Option A (↓ FEV₁/FVC <0.70): Obstructive disease (COPD, Asthma)

  • Option B (↑ TLC): Obstructive disease (COPD with hyperinflation)

  • Option D (↑ RV): Obstructive disease (air trapping)

  • Key Point: ↓ DLCO is the hallmark of ILD and the most sensitive early marker.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 60-year-old woman on chronic methotrexate for rheumatoid arthritis develops acute dyspnea, fever, and dry cough over 1 week. CXR shows bilateral infiltrates. BAL shows 40% eosinophils. What is the most appropriate management?

A. Increase methotrexate dose B. Add folic acid C. Stop methotrexate, start prednisone D. Start antibiotics

Answer: C. Stop methotrexate, start prednisone

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Methotrexate-Induced Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis - Acute onset (days-weeks) - Unlike MTX-induced fibrosis (months-years) - Peripheral eosinophilia (40% on BAL) - Bilateral infiltrates

  • MTX Pulmonary Toxicity:
  • Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (Acute):
    • Fever, dyspnea, cough
    • Eosinophilia (blood/BAL)
    • NOT dose-related

  • Pulmonary Fibrosis (Chronic):

    • Insidious, months-years
    • Less eosinophilia

  • Management:

  • STOP methotrexate (MOST IMPORTANT)
  • Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day

  • Recovery in most cases

  • Why NOT continue MTX?

  • MTX is the offending agent → Must stop

  • Re-challenge NOT recommended

  • Why NOT just antibiotics?

  • Clinical picture suggests drug-induced HP (eosinophilia is key)

  • Rule out infection, but drug toxicity more likely

  • Key Point: Drug-induced ILD → STOP drug + Steroids. Eosinophilia in BAL suggests drug-induced or eosinophilic pneumonia.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Which HRCT finding is most specific for Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis?

A. Perilymphatic nodules B. Honeycombing with subpleural and basal distribution C. Mosaic attenuation D. Ground-glass opacities

Answer: B. Honeycombing with subpleural and basal distribution

Explanation: - Definite UIP pattern (specific for IPF in appropriate clinical context): 1. Honeycombing (Most specific feature) 2. Subpleural and basal distribution 3. Heterogeneous (patchy) involvement 4. Reticular opacities 5. Minimal ground-glass opacities

  • Honeycombing:
  • Clustered cystic airspaces (3-10 mm) with thick walls
  • Subpleural location
  • Represents end-stage fibrosis (irreversible)

  • Definite UIP pattern → Can diagnose IPF without biopsy

  • Option A (Perilymphatic nodules): Sarcoidosis

  • Option C (Mosaic attenuation): Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (air trapping)

  • Option D (Ground-glass opacities): Non-specific, seen in many ILDs (NSIP, OP, HP, acute processes)

  • Key Point: Honeycombing + subpleural/basal distribution = UIP pattern = IPF (if no other cause identified)

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 50-year-old bird breeder presents with chronic dyspnea over 2 years. He has a budgie at home for 10 years. HRCT shows upper lobe fibrosis and mosaic attenuation. BAL shows 40% lymphocytes with CD4/CD8 ratio of 0.6. What is the most important treatment?

A. Prednisone B. Remove the bird from home C. Pirfenidone D. Azathioprine

Answer: B. Remove the bird from home

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Chronic Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (Bird Fancier's Lung) - Bird breeder (budgie at home) - Chronic dyspnea (insidious onset over years) - HRCT: Fibrosis (chronic HP) + Mosaic attenuation (air trapping - highly suggestive of HP) - BAL: Lymphocytosis with CD4/CD8 <1

  • Treatment:
  • Antigen avoidance = MOST IMPORTANT
    • Remove the bird (source of avian proteins)
    • Without antigen avoidance, disease will progress despite medications
  • Prednisone (if significant inflammation/symptoms)

  • Antifibrotics (if progressive fibrosis despite avoidance)

  • Why antigen avoidance is priority?

  • Acute/Subacute HP: Complete recovery if antigen avoided early
  • Chronic HP: Progression slows/stops if antigen avoided

  • Steroids/immunosuppressants are adjuncts, NOT substitutes for antigen avoidance

  • Key Point: HP Management = Antigen avoidance FIRST, then pharmacotherapy. Without removing the antigen, all other treatments will fail.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
A 65-year-old man with IPF on pirfenidone (FVC 65%, DLCO 50%) develops acute worsening of dyspnea over 5 days. He is afebrile. HRCT shows new bilateral ground-glass opacities superimposed on his baseline UIP pattern. Blood cultures are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A. Bacterial pneumonia B. Pulmonary embolism C. Acute exacerbation of IPF D. Congestive heart failure

Answer: C. Acute exacerbation of IPF

Explanation: - Acute Exacerbation of IPF: Acute worsening of dyspnea (<1 month) without identifiable cause

Diagnostic Criteria: 1. Previous/concurrent IPF diagnosis ✓ 2. Acute worsening (<1 month) ✓ (5 days) 3. HRCT: New bilateral ground-glass/consolidation ✓ 4. Not explained by infection, PE, heart failure, pneumothorax - Afebrile, negative blood cultures → Less likely infection - No mention of CHF features

  • Management:
  • High-dose steroids (Methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV × 3 days)
  • Supportive care (oxygen, mechanical ventilation if needed)
  • Empiric antibiotics (if infection cannot be excluded)

  • Lung transplant evaluation

  • Prognosis: In-hospital mortality 50-80% (very poor)

  • Key Point: IPF patient with acute worsening + new ground-glass on HRCT + no infection/PE/CHF → Acute exacerbation of IPF

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Which of the following is an indication for lung transplantation in ILD?

A. Sarcoidosis Stage I B. IPF with FVC 90% predicted C. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis with ongoing antigen exposure D. Progressive ILD with 6-minute walk distance <250 meters

Answer: D. Progressive ILD with 6-minute walk distance <250 meters

Explanation: - Lung Transplant Indications in ILD (General): - Progressive disease despite optimal therapy - Severe functional limitation (NYHA III-IV) - Hypoxemia (SpO₂ <88% or PaO₂ <55 mmHg) - 6MWT <250 meters or desaturation >4% - Poor prognostic factors

  • Disease-Specific:
  • IPF: Early referral (GAP Stage II/III, FVC <80%, DLCO <40%)
  • Sarcoidosis: Stage IV fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension

  • CTD-ILD: Progressive despite immunosuppression

  • Option A (Sarcoidosis Stage I): Excellent prognosis (60-80% spontaneous remission) → NO transplant

  • Option B (IPF with FVC 90%): Mild disease → Continue medical therapy, NOT ready for transplant
  • Option C (HP with ongoing exposure): Must stop antigen exposure first before considering transplant

  • Option D: ✓ 6MWT <250 m = Poor functional capacity → Indication for transplant

  • Key Point: 6MWT <250 meters or desaturation is a poor prognostic marker and indication for lung transplant evaluation.

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
Interstitial Lung Diseases are a diverse group of disorders characterized by: - Inflammation and/or fibrosis of the pulmonary interstitium - Restrictive PFTs with ↓↓ DLCO (most sensitive marker) - Progressive dyspnea and dry cough - HRCT is the gold standard for diagnosis

Key ILDs: 1. IPF: Most common and lethal idiopathic ILD, UIP pattern, honeycombing, clubbing, antifibrotics (Pirfenidone/Nintedanib) 2. Sarcoidosis: Most common ILD in young adults, non-caseating granulomas, BHL, Löfgren syndrome (excellent prognosis) 3. HP: Antigen exposure, acute (4-8 hrs) vs chronic, mosaic attenuation, CD4/CD8 <1, antigen avoidance 4. CTD-ILD: SSc-ILD most common, NSIP pattern, immunosuppression 5. Drug-ILD: BANANA (Bleomycin, Amiodarone, Nitrofurantoin, Anti-TNF, Nivolumab)

Management: - IPF: Antifibrotics (NOT steroids) - Sarcoidosis: Steroids if indicated (NOT all patients) - HP: Antigen avoidance (MOST important) - CTD-ILD: Immunosuppression (MMF, CYC) - All: Supportive care, oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, lung transplant (end-stage)

Prognosis: Varies widely from excellent (Löfgren syndrome) to poor (IPF: 2.5-3.5 years)

END OF CHAPTER

docs.title: BRONCHIECTASIS
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (⅘ - High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 4-5 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Definition
  2. Epidemiology
  3. Etiology
  4. Pathophysiology
  5. Clinical Features
  6. Diagnosis
  7. Management
  8. Complications
  9. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  10. Practice MCQs

docs.title: DEFINITION
docs.text: Bronchiectasis is a chronic respiratory condition characterized by: - Permanent abnormal dilatation of bronchi and bronchioles - Destruction of elastic and muscular components of bronchial wall - Chronic inflammation and infection - Impaired mucociliary clearance

docs.title: Types Based on Morphology
docs.text:
BRONCHIECTASIS TYPES (Reid Classification)
│
├─ CYLINDRICAL (Tubular)
│  • Uniform dilatation
│  • Most common type (50%)
│  • Best prognosis
│
├─ VARICOSE
│  • Irregular dilatation (beaded appearance)
│  • Alternating areas of dilatation and constriction
│  • Moderate severity
│
└─ CYSTIC (Saccular)
   • Severe dilatation forming cyst-like spaces
   • Most severe form
   • Worst prognosis
   • Associated with extensive destruction

docs.title: Prevalence
docs.text:
• Increasing due to improved detection (HRCT)
  • More common in developing countries
  • Prevalence: 4-20 per 100,000 in developed countries

docs.title: Demographics
docs.text:
• Female > Male (in most studies)
  • Can occur at any age
  • Onset depends on underlying cause:
  • Childhood: Cystic fibrosis, immunodeficiency, congenital disorders
  • Adulthood: Post-infectious, COPD-related

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
• Severe respiratory infections in childhood
  • Immunodeficiency
  • Chronic aspiration
  • Smoking
  • COPD

docs.title: Classification by Etiology
docs.text:
CAUSES OF BRONCHIECTASIS
│
├─ POST-INFECTIOUS (30-40%) - MOST COMMON
│  ├─ Bacterial: Tuberculosis (MOST COMMON WORLDWIDE)
│  │              Pertussis, Staphylococcus, Klebsiella
│  ├─ Viral: Measles, Adenovirus, Influenza
│  └─ Other: Mycoplasma, Aspergillus (ABPA)
│
├─ GENETIC/CONGENITAL (5-10%)
│  ├─ Cystic Fibrosis (MOST COMMON GENETIC CAUSE)
│  ├─ Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD)
│  │  • Kartagener Syndrome (PCD + Situs inversus + Sinusitis)
│  ├─ Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
│  └─ Tracheobronchomegaly (Mounier-Kuhn Syndrome)
│
├─ IMMUNODEFICIENCY (5-10%)
│  ├─ Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)
│  ├─ X-linked Agammaglobulinemia
│  ├─ Selective IgA Deficiency
│  ├─ HIV/AIDS
│  └─ Hypogammaglobulinemia
│
├─ ASPIRATION
│  ├─ Gastroesophageal reflux (GERD)
│  ├─ Recurrent aspiration (neurological disorders)
│  └─ Foreign body
│
├─ OBSTRUCTIVE
│  ├─ Foreign body
│  ├─ Tumor (carcinoid, bronchogenic carcinoma)
│  └─ Lymph node compression
│
├─ INFLAMMATORY/AUTOIMMUNE
│  ├─ Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
│  ├─ Inflammatory Bowel Disease (UC, Crohn's)
│  ├─ Rheumatoid Arthritis
│  └─ Sjögren's Syndrome
│
├─ OTHER
│  ├─ Yellow Nail Syndrome
│  ├─ Young's Syndrome
│  └─ Post-transplant (lung, bone marrow)
│
└─ IDIOPATHIC (30-50%)
   • No identifiable cause despite thorough evaluation

docs.title: Mnemonic: "CIGAR CABS" for Causes of Bronchiectasis
docs.text:
• C - Cystic Fibrosis
  • I - Immunodeficiency (CVID, XLA)
  • G - GERD/Aspiration
  • A - Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
  • R - Rheumatologic (RA, Sjögren's)
  • C - Ciliary dyskinesia (Kartagener)
  • A - Alpha-1 Antitrypsin deficiency
  • B - Bacterial/TB (Post-infectious)
  • S - Sinusitis (with PCD)

docs.title: 1. Tuberculosis
docs.text:
• Most common cause worldwide (especially in endemic areas)
  • Usually affects upper lobes
  • Often unilateral
  • History of TB infection/treatment

docs.title: 2. Cystic Fibrosis
docs.text:
• Most common genetic cause
  • Autosomal recessive (CFTR gene mutation)
  • Upper lobe predominance
  • Sweat chloride test (>60 mEq/L)
  • Associated: Pancreatic insufficiency, infertility

docs.title: 3. Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia (PCD)
docs.text:
• Defective ciliary motility
  • Kartagener Syndrome (Classic triad):
  • Bronchiectasis
  • Situs inversus (50%)
  • Chronic sinusitis
  • Infertility (immotile sperm)
  • Nasal nitric oxide very low (diagnostic)

docs.title: 4. Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
docs.text:
• Hypersensitivity to Aspergillus fumigatus
  • Usually occurs in asthmatics or CF patients
  • Central bronchiectasis (proximal airways)
  • Elevated IgE, eosinophilia

docs.title: Vicious Cycle Hypothesis (Cole's Vicious Cycle)
docs.text:
INITIAL INSULT
(Infection, Aspiration, Immunodeficiency, Genetic defect)
           ↓
IMPAIRED MUCOCILIARY CLEARANCE
           ↓
MUCUS STASIS in Airways
           ↓
BACTERIAL COLONIZATION
• Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MOST COMMON)
• Haemophilus influenzae
• Staphylococcus aureus
• Moraxella catarrhalis
           ↓
CHRONIC INFLAMMATION
• Neutrophil infiltration
• Release of proteases (Elastase, MMPs)
• Oxidative stress
           ↓
BRONCHIAL WALL DESTRUCTION
• Loss of elastic and muscular layers
• Permanent dilatation
           ↓
FURTHER IMPAIRED CLEARANCE
           ↓
[VICIOUS CYCLE CONTINUES]

docs.title: Pathological Changes
docs.text:
1. Bronchial Wall:
  2. Loss of elastic tissue
  3. Destruction of smooth muscle
  4. Replacement by fibrous tissue

  5. Chronic inflammatory infiltrate

  6. Airway Lumen:

  7. Dilatation (>1.5x adjacent vessel diameter)
  8. Mucus plugging

  9. Purulent secretions

  10. Surrounding Parenchyma:

  11. Atelectasis
  12. Consolidation
  13. Fibrosis

  14. Compensatory hyperinflation

  15. Vascular Changes:

  16. Bronchial artery hypertrophy (systemic circulation)
  17. Potential for hemoptysis

docs.title: Cardinal Features
docs.text:
1. Chronic Productive Cough (MOST COMMON)
  2. Present in >90%
  3. Daily production of purulent/mucopurulent sputum
  4. Copious sputum (>30 mL/day in severe cases)
  5. Worse in the morning (overnight accumulation)

  6. Foul-smelling if anaerobic infection

  7. Recurrent Respiratory Infections

  8. Frequent exacerbations (≥3 per year in severe disease)

  9. Pneumonia

  10. Hemoptysis (50-70%)

  11. Usually mild (blood-streaked sputum)
  12. Massive hemoptysis (>200 mL/24 hr) in 5-10%

  13. Due to bronchial artery hypertrophy/erosion

  14. Dyspnea (60-70%)

  15. Progressive with advanced disease

  16. Due to airflow obstruction, impaired gas exchange

  17. Chest Pain (20-30%)

  18. Pleuritic pain during exacerbations

  19. Constitutional Symptoms

  20. Fatigue, weight loss (severe/advanced disease)
  21. Low-grade fever (during exacerbations)

docs.title: Physical Signs
docs.text:
1. Respiratory Examination
  2. Coarse crepitations/crackles (80-90%) - MOST COMMON SIGN
    • Localized to affected area
    • May shift with coughing
  3. Rhonchi/wheeze (40-50%)

  4. Decreased breath sounds (localized)

  5. Clubbing (30-40%)

  6. More common in severe/long-standing disease

  7. Cystic fibrosis-related bronchiectasis

  8. Cyanosis

  9. Advanced disease with respiratory failure

  10. Signs of Cor Pulmonale (Advanced disease)

  11. Elevated JVP
  12. Pedal edema
  13. Loud P2

docs.title: Bronchiectasis Severity Index (BSI)
docs.text:
Variable Points Age (years) 1 point per decade >40 BMI <18.5 kg/m² 2 FEV₁ <30% predicted 3 FEV₁ 30-49% predicted 2 FEV₁ 50-79% predicted 1 Hospital admission in past 2 years 5 Exacerbations in past year (≥3) 2 MRC dyspnea score 5 3 MRC dyspnea score 4 2 MRC dyspnea score 3 1 Colonization: P. aeruginosa 3 Colonization: Other organisms 1 Radiological severity >3 lobes involved 1

Score Interpretation: - 0-4: Mild (Low mortality) - 5-8: Moderate - ≥9: Severe (High mortality)

docs.title: Diagnostic Approach
docs.text:
CLINICAL SUSPICION
(Chronic productive cough, recurrent infections, hemoptysis)
           ↓
    HRCT CHEST (GOLD STANDARD)
           ↓
    CONFIRMS DIAGNOSIS
           ↓
IDENTIFY UNDERLYING CAUSE
• Clinical history
• Laboratory tests
• Microbiological studies
           ↓
ASSESS SEVERITY & COMPLICATIONS

docs.title: 1. High-Resolution CT (HRCT) Chest
docs.text: GOLD STANDARD for diagnosis

docs.title: HRCT Diagnostic Criteria
docs.text:
Direct Signs (Diagnostic): 1. Bronchial Dilatation - Signet Ring Sign: Dilated bronchus (>1.5x) adjacent to pulmonary artery - Bronchus = ring, Artery = signet stone - MOST SPECIFIC SIGN

  1. Lack of Bronchial Tapering
  2. Bronchi visible in peripheral ⅓ of lung (normally invisible)

  3. Tram-track appearance (parallel lines)

  4. Bronchial Wall Thickening

  5. Thickened bronchial walls

Indirect Signs: - Mucus plugging (tree-in-bud pattern) - Atelectasis - Mosaic perfusion/air trapping - Consolidation

docs.title: Distribution Patterns
docs.text: Cause Distribution Features Tuberculosis Upper lobes Unilateral/asymmetric, cavitation Cystic Fibrosis Upper lobes Bilateral, mucus plugging ABPA Central/proximal Upper-mid zones, high attenuation mucus plugs Aspiration Lower lobes (RLL, posterior segments) Related to gravity Idiopathic Lower lobes Bilateral, cylindrical

docs.title: 2. Chest X-Ray
docs.text:
Sensitivity: 50-60% (NOT sensitive, but may show): - Tram-track lines (thickened bronchial walls) - Ring shadows (dilated bronchi on end) - Increased lung markings - Atelectasis, consolidation - Cystic spaces

Key Point: Normal CXR does NOT exclude bronchiectasis → Need HRCT

docs.title: 3. Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs)
docs.text:
Pattern: Variable (Obstructive, Restrictive, or Mixed)

Pattern Finding Frequency Obstructive ↓ FEV₁/FVC <0.70 50-60% Mixed ↓ FEV₁/FVC + ↓ TLC 30-40% Normal Early/mild disease 10-20%

Additional Findings: - Reversibility: Partial response to bronchodilators (unlike COPD) - ↓ FEV₁: Severity correlates with disease extent - ↓ DLCO: In advanced disease

Purpose: - Assess severity - Monitor disease progression - Evaluate response to therapy

docs.title: Microscopy and Culture
docs.text:
Indications: All patients (baseline and during exacerbations)

Common Organisms: 1. Pseudomonas aeruginosa (30-40%) - Associated with worse prognosis - More exacerbations, ↓ FEV₁, ↑ mortality 2. Haemophilus influenzae (30-40%) 3. Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) 4. Moraxella catarrhalis 5. Nontuberculous Mycobacteria (NTM) (10-15%) - M. avium complex, M. abscessus 6. Aspergillus species (ABPA)

Definitions: - Colonization: Organism present without symptoms - Infection: Organism + clinical deterioration

docs.title: Acid-Fast Bacilli (AFB)
docs.text: Indications: All patients at diagnosis (rule out TB, NTM) - 3 sputum samples for AFB smear and culture - TB: Especially in endemic areas - NTM: May require prolonged treatment

docs.title: 5. Investigations for Underlying Cause
docs.text: Test Purpose Positive Finding Serum Immunoglobulins Immunodeficiency ↓ IgG, IgA, IgM (CVID) Total IgE ABPA >1000 IU/mL Aspergillus IgE/IgG ABPA Elevated specific IgE/IgG Sweat Chloride Test Cystic Fibrosis >60 mEq/L (CF) CFTR Gene Mutation Cystic Fibrosis Positive (F508del most common) Nasal Nitric Oxide Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia Very low (<77 nL/min) Ciliary Biopsy PCD Absent dynein arms (EM) Alpha-1 Antitrypsin AAT deficiency Low levels, PiZZ phenotype Autoantibodies Autoimmune RF, ANA, Anti-CCP HIV Test HIV-related Positive HIV antibody/RNA

docs.title: 6. Bronchoscopy
docs.text: Indications (NOT routine): - Localized bronchiectasis (rule out obstruction: tumor, foreign body) - Massive hemoptysis (identify bleeding site) - Atypical presentation - Poor response to treatment

docs.title: 7. Other Tests
docs.text:
• 6-Minute Walk Test: Functional capacity, desaturation
  • Arterial Blood Gas: Assess hypoxemia, hypercapnia (advanced disease)
  • Echocardiography: Pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale

docs.title: Goals of Treatment
docs.text:
1. Reduce exacerbation frequency
  2. Improve quality of life
  3. Prevent disease progression
  4. Manage complications (hemoptysis, respiratory failure)

docs.title: Treatment Components
docs.text:
BRONCHIECTASIS MANAGEMENT
│
├─ AIRWAY CLEARANCE TECHNIQUES (CORNERSTONE)
├─ ANTIBIOTICS
│  ├─ Acute exacerbations
│  ├─ Chronic suppressive (selected patients)
│  └─ Eradication (P. aeruginosa)
├─ ANTI-INFLAMMATORY
├─ BRONCHODILATORS
├─ TREAT UNDERLYING CAUSE
├─ VACCINATIONS
└─ SURGICAL (Selected cases)

docs.title: 1. Airway Clearance Techniques (ACT)
docs.text: MOST IMPORTANT non-pharmacological intervention

docs.title: Techniques
docs.text:
Method Description Efficacy Active Cycle of Breathing Breathing control + thoracic expansion + forced expiration High Autogenic Drainage Controlled breathing at different lung volumes High Postural Drainage Gravity-assisted drainage (position based on affected lobe) Moderate Percussion/Vibration Chest clapping/shaking Moderate Oscillating PEP devices Flutter valve, Acapella High High-Frequency Chest Wall Oscillation (HFCWO) Mechanical vest High

Recommendation: ≥2 sessions per day (each 10-30 minutes)

Adjuncts: - Nebulized Hypertonic Saline (3-7%) - Improves mucus hydration and clearance - Induces cough (may cause bronchospasm → give after bronchodilator) - Nebulized Mannitol - Osmotic agent, improves clearance

docs.title: A. Acute Exacerbations
docs.text:
Definition of Exacerbation (≥3 of the following): 1. ↑ Sputum volume 2. ↑ Sputum purulence 3. ↑ Dyspnea 4. ↑ Cough 5. Fever 6. ↑ Fatigue/malaise 7. ↓ Lung function (FEV₁)

Antibiotic Selection:

Severity Organism Antibiotic Duration Mild-Moderate (Outpatient) H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg TID, OR Doxycycline 100 mg BID, OR Azithromycin 500 mg OD 14 days Severe or P. aeruginosa colonization P. aeruginosa Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BID (PO), OR Levofloxacin 750 mg OD 14-21 days Hospitalized P. aeruginosa, others IV: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6h, OR Ceftazidime 2 g q8h, OR Meropenem 1 g q8h ± Aminoglycoside 14-21 days

Key Points: - Longer duration than typical pneumonia (14 days minimum) - Culture-guided therapy preferred - IV therapy if severe or failed oral treatment

docs.title: B. Chronic Suppressive Antibiotics
docs.text:
Indications (Selected patients): - ≥3 exacerbations per year despite optimal ACT, OR - Severe disease with frequent hospitalizations

Options:

  1. Macrolide (Long-term)
  2. Azithromycin 250-500 mg 3x/week (or daily)
  3. Erythromycin 500 mg BID
  4. Mechanism: Anti-inflammatory, anti-biofilm (NOT just antibacterial)
  5. Benefit: ↓ Exacerbations by 40-50%
  6. Monitoring: Baseline ECG (QTc), LFTs, hearing test

  7. Contraindication: NTM colonization (risk of resistance)

  8. Inhaled Antibiotics

  9. Tobramycin (300 mg BID nebulized) - On/off cycles (28 days on, 28 off)
  10. Colistin (1-2 million units BID nebulized)
  11. Indications: Chronic P. aeruginosa colonization with frequent exacerbations
  12. Benefit: ↓ Bacterial load, ↓ exacerbations

docs.title: C. Pseudomonas Eradication
docs.text:
Rationale: Early eradication may prevent chronic colonization

Regimen (at first isolation): - Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BID PO × 2 weeks - + Inhaled Tobramycin 300 mg BID × 4 weeks

docs.title: Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS)
docs.text:
Indications: - Concomitant asthma or COPD - Frequent exacerbations despite other therapies - Not routinely recommended for all patients

Example: Budesonide/Formoterol, Fluticasone/Salmeterol

Risks: ↑ NTM risk, pneumonia

docs.title: 4. Bronchodilators
docs.text:
Indications: - Airflow obstruction (FEV₁/FVC <0.70) - Reversibility on PFTs - Dyspnea relief

Options: - SABA: Salbutamol (as needed) - LABA: Formoterol, Salmeterol (if persistent obstruction) - LAMA: Tiotropium (if COPD overlap)

docs.title: 5. Mucoactive Agents
docs.text:
• Hypertonic Saline (3-7%): Nebulized BID, ↑ mucus clearance
  • Mannitol: Dry powder inhaler
  • DNase (Dornase alfa): ONLY in CF (may worsen non-CF bronchiectasis)

docs.title: 6. Treatment of Underlying Cause
docs.text: Cause Treatment ABPA Prednisone (0.5 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks, taper over 3-6 months) + Itraconazole (200 mg BID) Cystic Fibrosis CFTR modulators (Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor), pancreatic enzymes Immunodeficiency IV Immunoglobulin (IVIG) replacement (0.4-0.6 g/kg q3-4 weeks) GERD PPI (Omeprazole 20-40 mg daily), lifestyle modifications NTM Multi-drug regimen (Azithromycin + Ethambutol + Rifampin ± Amikacin) × 12-18 months Aspiration Treat underlying cause (dysphagia management, NGT if needed)

docs.title: 7. Vaccinations
docs.text: Recommended: - Influenza vaccine (annual) - Pneumococcal vaccine: - PCV20 (single dose), OR - PCV15 followed by PPSV23 (1 year later) - COVID-19 vaccine

docs.title: 8. Pulmonary Rehabilitation
docs.text:
Components: Exercise training, breathing techniques, education

Benefits: Improves exercise capacity, QOL, reduces dyspnea

docs.title: 9. Oxygen Therapy
docs.text:
Indications: - Resting SpO₂ ≤88% - PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg

Goal: SpO₂ ≥90%

docs.title: 10. Surgical Treatment
docs.text:
Indications (Rare, <5% of patients): - Localized bronchiectasis (1-2 lobes) with: - Recurrent infections despite medical therapy - Massive hemoptysis (recurrent) - Adequate pulmonary reserve (post-resection FEV₁ >1L)

Procedures: - Lobectomy/Segmentectomy (curative for localized disease) - Lung transplantation (end-stage bilateral disease, CF)

Contraindications to Surgery: - Diffuse/widespread disease - Poor lung function (FEV₁ <40%) - Severe comorbidities

docs.title: 1. Massive Hemoptysis
docs.text:
Definition: >200-300 mL blood in 24 hours

Mechanism: Erosion of hypertrophied bronchial arteries

Management: 1. Resuscitation: IV fluids, blood transfusion 2. Positioning: Bleeding side down (prevent aspiration to healthy lung) 3. Reverse coagulopathy (if present) 4. Bronchoscopy: Localize bleeding site 5. Bronchial Artery Embolization (BAE): Treatment of choice - Success rate: 70-90% - Recurrence: 20-30% 6. Surgery: If BAE fails or recurrent bleeding (lobectomy)

docs.title: 2. Respiratory Failure
docs.text:
Acute-on-Chronic: - During severe exacerbations - Type II respiratory failure (hypercapnia)

Chronic: - Advanced disease - Progressive hypoxemia

Management: - Oxygen therapy - Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) - Mechanical ventilation (if needed) - Treat underlying exacerbation

docs.title: 3. Cor Pulmonale (Pulmonary Hypertension)
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: Chronic hypoxemia → Pulmonary vasoconstriction → RV failure

Management: - Treat underlying disease - Oxygen therapy (most important) - Diuretics (edema) - Consider pulmonary vasodilators (limited evidence)

docs.title: 4. Pneumonia
docs.text:
Recurrent bacterial pneumonia in affected lobes

Prevention: Vaccinations, airway clearance

docs.title: 5. Pneumothorax
docs.text: Occurs rarely (cystic/advanced disease)

docs.title: 6. Amyloidosis (AA Type)
docs.text: Rare complication of chronic inflammation

docs.title: Must-Know Facts
docs.text:
1. Definition: Permanent abnormal dilatation of bronchi with chronic inflammation

  2. Most Common Cause:

  3. Worldwide: Tuberculosis (post-infectious)

  4. Genetic: Cystic Fibrosis

  5. Kartagener Syndrome (Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia):

  6. Triad: Bronchiectasis + Situs inversus + Chronic sinusitis
  7. Infertility (immotile sperm)

  8. Very low nasal nitric oxide

  9. HRCT Chest:

  10. GOLD STANDARD for diagnosis
  11. Signet Ring Sign: Dilated bronchus (ring) > adjacent artery (signet stone)
    • MOST SPECIFIC SIGN

  12. Broncho-arterial ratio >1.5

  13. Clinical Features:

  14. Chronic productive cough with copious sputum (MOST COMMON)
  15. Hemoptysis (50-70%)
  16. Coarse crackles (MOST COMMON SIGN)

  17. Clubbing (30-40%)

  18. Most Common Organism: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (30-40%)

  19. Associated with worse prognosis

  20. Airway Clearance Techniques: MOST IMPORTANT non-pharmacological treatment

  21. ≥2 sessions/day

  22. Antibiotics for Exacerbation:

  23. Mild-Moderate: Amoxicillin-clavulanate × 14 days
  24. P. aeruginosa: Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BID × 14-21 days

  25. Longer duration than typical pneumonia

  26. Long-term Azithromycin:

  27. ≥3 exacerbations/year despite optimal ACT

  28. Contraindication: NTM colonization (risk of resistance)

  29. Massive Hemoptysis:

    • Bronchial Artery Embolization (BAE): Treatment of choice

docs.title: Distribution Patterns (High-Yield)
docs.text: Cause Lobes Affected Key Feature Tuberculosis Upper lobes Unilateral/asymmetric, history of TB Cystic Fibrosis Upper lobes Bilateral, young patient, sweat chloride >60 ABPA Central/proximal (upper-mid) High attenuation mucus plugs, IgE >1000 Aspiration Lower lobes (RLL, posterior) Recurrent aspiration history Idiopathic Lower lobes Bilateral, no identifiable cause

docs.title: Comparison: Bronchiectasis vs COPD
docs.text: Feature Bronchiectasis COPD Pathology Bronchial dilatation Emphysema + chronic bronchitis Sputum Copious, purulent Minimal-moderate, mucoid Hemoptysis Common (50-70%) Rare Crackles Coarse, localized Fine, diffuse (if present) HRCT Signet ring sign Emphysema, air trapping Reversibility Partial Minimal

docs.title: ABPA (Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis) - Key Points
docs.text:
Diagnostic Criteria (All must be present): 1. Asthma or CF 2. Total IgE >1000 IU/mL 3. Elevated Aspergillus IgE and/or IgG 4. Central bronchiectasis on HRCT

Additional Features: - Eosinophilia (blood >500/μL) - Mucus plugs (high attenuation on HRCT) - Fleeting pulmonary infiltrates

Treatment: - Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks, taper over 3-6 months - Itraconazole 200 mg BID (reduces steroid requirement)

docs.title: Cystic Fibrosis - Diagnostic Tests
docs.text:
Test Finding Significance Sweat Chloride >60 mEq/L DIAGNOSTIC (most commonly used) CFTR Gene Mutation Positive (F508del most common) Confirmatory Nasal Potential Difference Abnormal Alternative diagnostic test

CF Features: - Bronchiectasis (upper lobes) - Pancreatic insufficiency (steatorrhea) - Infertility (males: absent vas deferens) - Nasal polyps, chronic sinusitis - Meconium ileus (newborns)

docs.title: Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia - Diagnostic Tests
docs.text: Test Finding Nasal Nitric Oxide Very low (<77 nL/min) - SCREENING TEST Ciliary Biopsy (EM) Absent dynein arms (outer or inner) - GOLD STANDARD High-Speed Video Microscopy Absent/abnormal ciliary beat pattern

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Normal CXR rules out bronchiectasis ❌
  2. CXR sensitivity only 50-60%

  3. HRCT is GOLD STANDARD

  4. All bronchiectasis patients need steroids ❌

  5. ICS only if concomitant asthma/COPD

  6. NOT routine

  7. Short antibiotic courses (5-7 days) for exacerbations ❌

  8. Need 14 days minimum (14-21 days for P. aeruginosa)

  9. DNase (Dornase alfa) is beneficial ❌

  10. ONLY in CF, may worsen non-CF bronchiectasis

  11. Long-term azithromycin is safe in all patients ❌

  12. Contraindicated if NTM colonization (risk of resistance)

  13. Need baseline ECG (QTc prolongation risk)

  14. Surgery is first-line treatment ❌

  15. Medical therapy is mainstay
  16. Surgery only for localized disease with failed medical therapy or massive hemoptysis

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "CIGAR CABS" - Causes of Bronchiectasis:

  2. **C**ystic Fibrosis, **I**mmunodeficiency, **G**ERD, **A**BPA, **R**heumatologic, **C**iliary dyskinesia, **A**lpha-1 antitrypsin, **B**acterial/TB, **S**inusitis

  3. "Signet Ring" - HRCT sign:

  4. Dilated bronchus (ring) next to artery (signet stone)

  5. Broncho-arterial ratio >1.5

  6. Kartagener Syndrome - "SIB":

  7. **S**itus inversus, **I**nfertility, **B**ronchiectasis (+ Sinusitis)

docs.title: Important Drug Side Effects
docs.text: Drug Side Effect Monitoring Azithromycin (long-term) QTc prolongation, hearing loss, NTM resistance Baseline ECG, audiometry, check NTM status Inhaled Tobramycin Bronchospasm, ototoxicity Give after bronchodilator, monitor hearing Hypertonic Saline Bronchospasm Give after bronchodilator ICS (if used) Pneumonia, ↑ NTM risk Monitor for infections

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 35-year-old man presents with chronic productive cough with copious foul-smelling sputum, recurrent respiratory infections, and hemoptysis. On examination, situs inversus totalis is noted. He also has chronic sinusitis and infertility. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A. Cystic Fibrosis B. Kartagener Syndrome C. Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis D. Idiopathic Bronchiectasis

Answer: B. Kartagener Syndrome

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Kartagener Syndrome (Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia)

Classic Triad: 1. Bronchiectasis (chronic productive cough, recurrent infections, hemoptysis) 2. Situs inversus (present in this patient) 3. Chronic sinusitis

Additional Features: - Infertility (immotile sperm due to defective ciliary function)

Pathophysiology: - Defective ciliary motility → Impaired mucociliary clearance → Bronchiectasis, sinusitis - Ciliary defect also affects sperm motility

Diagnosis: - Nasal Nitric Oxide: Very low (<77 nL/min) - SCREENING TEST - Ciliary biopsy (Electron microscopy): Absent dynein arms - GOLD STANDARD

Why NOT Other Options?: - CF: No situs inversus, would have pancreatic insufficiency, sweat chloride >60 - ABPA: No situs inversus, associated with asthma, IgE >1000 - Idiopathic: Diagnosis of exclusion, situs inversus points to specific etiology

Key Point: Bronchiectasis + Situs inversus + Sinusitis + Infertility = Kartagener Syndrome

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 28-year-old woman with bronchial asthma presents with recurrent episodes of pulmonary infiltrates and brownish mucus plugs in sputum. CXR shows fleeting infiltrates. HRCT shows central bronchiectasis with high attenuation mucus plugs. Lab tests show total IgE 1500 IU/mL and peripheral eosinophilia. What is the most appropriate treatment?

A. Long-term azithromycin B. Prednisone and itraconazole C. Inhaled tobramycin D. Pirfenidone

Answer: B. Prednisone and itraconazole

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)

Diagnostic Criteria (All present): 1. Asthma or CF ✓ 2. Total IgE >1000 IU/mL ✓ (1500 IU/mL) 3. Central bronchiectasis on HRCT ✓ 4. Elevated Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG (presumed positive)

Additional Supportive Features: - Eosinophilia (blood >500/μL) - High attenuation mucus plugs on HRCT (pathognomonic) - Fleeting pulmonary infiltrates - Brownish mucus plugs in sputum

Treatment: - Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks, then taper over 3-6 months - Itraconazole 200 mg BID (antifungal) - Reduces steroid dose required - Treats fungal burden

Why NOT Other Options?: - Azithromycin: Used for bacterial exacerbations in bronchiectasis, not ABPA - Inhaled Tobramycin: For P. aeruginosa colonization - Pirfenidone: Antifibrotic for IPF

Key Point: ABPA = Asthma + IgE >1000 + Central bronchiectasis + Eosinophilia → Treatment = Prednisone + Itraconazole

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
HRCT chest of a patient with chronic productive cough shows dilated bronchi with broncho-arterial ratio >1.5. What is this finding called?

A. Tree-in-bud pattern B. Signet ring sign C. Reverse halo sign D. Mosaic attenuation

Answer: B. Signet ring sign

Explanation: - Signet Ring Sign: MOST SPECIFIC HRCT sign of bronchiectasis

Description: - Dilated bronchus (ring) adjacent to pulmonary artery (signet stone) - Broncho-arterial ratio >1.5 (normally <1) - Appears as a ring with a dot (like a signet ring)

Other HRCT Signs in Bronchiectasis: - Lack of bronchial tapering (bronchi visible in peripheral lung) - Tram-track appearance (parallel thickened bronchial walls) - Bronchial wall thickening

Why NOT Other Options?: - Tree-in-bud: Mucus plugging in small airways (seen in bronchiectasis exacerbations, but not diagnostic) - Reverse halo sign (Atoll sign): Organizing pneumonia - Mosaic attenuation: Air trapping (seen in small airway disease, HP, bronchiectasis, but not specific)

Key Point: Signet Ring Sign = Dilated bronchus > adjacent artery = BRONCHIECTASIS

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 45-year-old patient with bronchiectasis colonized with Pseudomonas aeruginosa has 4 exacerbations requiring antibiotics in the past year despite good adherence to airway clearance techniques. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

A. Add long-term oral ciprofloxacin B. Add long-term azithromycin C. Add inhaled corticosteroids D. Refer for surgery

Answer: B. Add long-term azithromycin

Explanation: - Indication for Chronic Suppressive Therapy: ≥3 exacerbations per year despite optimal airway clearance

Long-term Azithromycin: - Dose: 250-500 mg 3x/week or daily - Mechanism: Anti-inflammatory, anti-biofilm, immunomodulatory (NOT just antibacterial) - Benefit: Reduces exacerbations by 40-50% - Monitoring: - Baseline ECG (QTc prolongation risk) - LFTs - Audiometry (hearing loss risk) - Rule out NTM colonization (contraindication)

Before Starting: - CRITICAL: Must check for Nontuberculous Mycobacteria (NTM) - If NTM present → DO NOT give macrolide monotherapy (risk of resistance)

Why NOT Other Options?: - Oral Ciprofloxacin (long-term): Risk of resistance, tendon rupture, not standard chronic suppressive therapy - Inhaled Tobramycin would be better for chronic P. aeruginosa suppression (but azithromycin is still first choice for frequent exacerbations) - ICS: Only if concomitant asthma/COPD, not routine - Surgery: For localized disease with failed medical therapy; this patient has diffuse disease with P. aeruginosa colonization (poor surgical candidate)

Key Point: ≥3 exacerbations/year despite ACT → Add long-term Azithromycin (after ruling out NTM)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 50-year-old man with bronchiectasis presents with massive hemoptysis (300 mL in 6 hours). He is hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate management?

A. Emergency lobectomy B. Bronchial artery embolization C. High-dose tranexamic acid D. Observation and antibiotics

Answer: B. Bronchial artery embolization

Explanation: - Massive Hemoptysis: >200-300 mL blood in 24 hours

Pathophysiology: - Erosion of hypertrophied bronchial arteries (systemic circulation) - High pressure → Risk of exsanguination

Management Approach:

  1. Resuscitation:
  2. Large-bore IV access
  3. Fluid resuscitation
  4. Blood transfusion (if needed)

  5. Reverse coagulopathy

  6. Positioning: Bleeding side down (if known)

  7. Prevents aspiration to healthy lung

  8. Bronchoscopy:

  9. Localize bleeding site

  10. Temporize bleeding (cold saline, topical vasoconstrictors)

  11. Definitive Treatment:

  12. Bronchial Artery Embolization (BAE): TREATMENT OF CHOICE
    • Success rate: 70-90%
    • Minimally invasive
    • Can be repeated if recurrence
  13. Surgery (Lobectomy): If BAE fails or recurrent bleeding
    • Reserved for localized disease with adequate pulmonary reserve

Why NOT Other Options?: - Emergency lobectomy: High morbidity/mortality, reserve for BAE failure or recurrent hemoptysis - Tranexamic acid: May be used as adjunct, but NOT definitive treatment for massive hemoptysis - Observation: Inappropriate for massive hemoptysis (life-threatening)

Key Point: Massive hemoptysis in bronchiectasis → Bronchial Artery Embolization (BAE) is treatment of choice

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 30-year-old patient with cystic fibrosis and bronchiectasis presents with acute exacerbation. Which of the following should NOT be used in this patient?

A. Inhaled DNase (Dornase alfa) B. Hypertonic saline C. Azithromycin D. Postural drainage

Answer: Trick question - All options are appropriate for CF, but let me rephrase:

CORRECTED Question 6: A 30-year-old patient with non-CF bronchiectasis presents with thick secretions. Which of the following mucoactive agents is contraindicated?

A. Inhaled DNase (Dornase alfa) B. Hypertonic saline C. Mannitol D. N-acetylcysteine

Answer: A. Inhaled DNase (Dornase alfa)

Explanation: - DNase (Dornase alfa): Recombinant human DNase enzyme that breaks down extracellular DNA in sputum

Indication: - ONLY Cystic Fibrosis - CF sputum has high DNA content from neutrophil breakdown - Improves lung function in CF

Contraindication: - Non-CF Bronchiectasis - May worsen lung function - Increases exacerbation risk - Mechanism unclear (possibly disrupts innate immunity)

Other Mucoactive Agents (Safe in non-CF bronchiectasis): - Hypertonic Saline (3-7%): ↑ Mucus hydration, improves clearance - Mannitol: Osmotic agent, improves clearance - N-acetylcysteine: Mucolytic (limited evidence, rarely used)

Key Point: DNase is beneficial in CF but harmful in non-CF bronchiectasis

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
What is the most common organism colonizing patients with bronchiectasis and associated with worse prognosis?

A. Haemophilus influenzae B. Pseudomonas aeruginosa C. Staphylococcus aureus D. Moraxella catarrhalis

Answer: B. Pseudomonas aeruginosa

Explanation: - Pseudomonas aeruginosa: 30-40% of bronchiectasis patients

Associated with WORSE Prognosis: - ↑ Exacerbation frequency - ↑ Hospitalization - ↓ FEV₁ (faster lung function decline) - ↑ Mortality - More severe disease (BSI score)

Characteristics: - Forms biofilms (difficult to eradicate) - Resistant to multiple antibiotics - Chronic colonization common

Treatment: - Acute exacerbation: Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BID × 14-21 days (or IV anti-pseudomonal) - Eradication (first isolation): Ciprofloxacin PO + Inhaled Tobramycin - Chronic suppression: Inhaled Tobramycin (28 days on/28 off cycles)

Other Organisms: - H. influenzae: 30-40% (EQUALLY COMMON but NOT associated with worse prognosis) - S. aureus: 10-20% - M. catarrhalis: 5-10%

Key Point: P. aeruginosa colonization = Worse prognosis in bronchiectasis

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A patient with bronchiectasis is started on long-term azithromycin. Which of the following should be checked before starting therapy?

A. Chest X-ray B. Sputum for NTM culture C. Serum IgE levels D. Sweat chloride test

Answer: B. Sputum for NTM culture

Explanation: - Before Starting Long-term Azithromycin: Must rule out Nontuberculous Mycobacteria (NTM) colonization

Rationale: - Macrolide monotherapy in NTM → Risk of drug resistance - NTM treatment requires multi-drug regimen (Azithromycin + Ethambutol + Rifampin ± Amikacin) - If NTM present → Do NOT give azithromycin alone

NTM in Bronchiectasis: - Frequency: 10-15% - Common species: M. avium complex (MAC), M. abscessus - Associated with: Older age, female, thin body habitus, severe bronchiectasis

Other Monitoring Before/During Azithromycin: - ECG: Baseline (QTc prolongation risk) - LFTs: Baseline and periodic - Audiometry: Baseline (hearing loss risk)

Why NOT Other Options?: - CXR: Useful but not specific requirement before azithromycin - Serum IgE: For ABPA diagnosis, not related to azithromycin - Sweat chloride: For CF diagnosis, not related to azithromycin

Key Point: ALWAYS check for NTM before starting long-term macrolide therapy in bronchiectasis

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Which HRCT distribution pattern is most suggestive of post-tuberculous bronchiectasis?

A. Central/proximal airways (upper-mid zones) B. Upper lobes C. Lower lobes (bilateral) D. Right middle lobe and lingula

Answer: B. Upper lobes

Explanation: - Tuberculosis: Most common cause of bronchiectasis worldwide (endemic areas)

HRCT Distribution: - Upper lobe predominance - Unilateral or asymmetric (unlike CF which is bilateral) - Associated findings: - Cavitation - Fibrosis - Volume loss (upper lobe retraction) - Calcified lymph nodes

Other Distribution Patterns:

Distribution Cause Upper lobes TB, CF, Sarcoidosis (late fibrosis) Central/proximal (upper-mid) ABPA (high attenuation mucus plugs) Lower lobes (bilateral) Idiopathic, Aspiration, Post-infectious (non-TB) RML + Lingula Lady Windermere Syndrome (NTM in elderly women)

Key Point: Upper lobe bronchiectasis with cavitation/fibrosis → Think Post-TB

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
What is the minimum duration of antibiotic therapy for an acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis?

A. 5-7 days B. 10 days C. 14 days D. 21 days

Answer: C. 14 days

Explanation: - Acute Exacerbation: Minimum 14 days antibiotic duration

Rationale: - Thicker mucus and structural damage in bronchiectasis - Harder to eradicate bacteria - Shorter courses → Incomplete eradication → Relapse

Duration Guidelines: - Mild-Moderate (non-P. aeruginosa): 14 days - Severe or P. aeruginosa infection: 14-21 days

Antibiotics: - Non-P. aeruginosa: Amoxicillin-clavulanate, Doxycycline, Azithromycin - P. aeruginosa: Ciprofloxacin 750 mg BID (or IV anti-pseudomonal)

Comparison with Other Respiratory Infections: - CAP (uncomplicated): 5-7 days - COPD exacerbation: 5-7 days - Bronchiectasis exacerbation: 14-21 days (LONGER)

Key Point: Bronchiectasis exacerbations require LONGER antibiotic courses (14 days minimum) compared to typical pneumonia/COPD exacerbations

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
A 60-year-old woman with recurrent respiratory infections has bronchiectasis on HRCT. Serum immunoglobulin testing shows IgG 400 mg/dL (normal: 700-1600), IgA 50 mg/dL (normal: 70-400), IgM 30 mg/dL (normal: 40-230). What is the most likely diagnosis and treatment?

A. Selective IgA deficiency; no specific treatment B. Common Variable Immunodeficiency; IV immunoglobulin C. X-linked agammaglobulinemia; stem cell transplant D. HIV/AIDS; antiretroviral therapy

Answer: B. Common Variable Immunodeficiency; IV immunoglobulin

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)

Diagnostic Criteria: - Low IgG (<400 mg/dL) ✓ - Low IgA and/or IgM ✓ - Onset typically in 2nd-4th decade (unlike XLA which is in infancy) - Recurrent sinopulmonary infections - Exclusion of other causes

Clinical Features: - Recurrent respiratory infections (sinusitis, bronchitis, pneumonia) - Bronchiectasis (chronic damage from recurrent infections) - GI infections, autoimmune disorders - Increased risk of lymphoma

Treatment: - IV Immunoglobulin (IVIG): 0.4-0.6 g/kg every 3-4 weeks - Prevents infections - May slow bronchiectasis progression - Subcutaneous Ig (SCIG): Alternative - Antibiotics: Aggressive treatment of infections - Airway clearance: For bronchiectasis management

Why NOT Other Options?: - Selective IgA deficiency: Only IgA low, IgG and IgM normal; most are asymptomatic - X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA): Males only, presents in infancy (<1 year), all Ig very low (<100 mg/dL) - HIV/AIDS: Would show low CD4, positive HIV test, different clinical picture

Key Point: CVID = Low IgG + Low IgA/IgM + Recurrent infections → Treatment = IVIG replacement

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Which of the following is the MOST IMPORTANT component of non-pharmacological management of bronchiectasis?

A. Dietary modification B. Airway clearance techniques C. Annual influenza vaccination D. Avoidance of air pollution

Answer: B. Airway clearance techniques

Explanation: - Airway Clearance Techniques (ACT): CORNERSTONE of bronchiectasis management

Rationale: - Breaks the vicious cycle: - Removes mucus → Reduces bacterial load → Reduces inflammation → Prevents further damage

Evidence: - Improves QOL - Reduces exacerbation frequency - Improves lung function - Reduces hospital admissions

Recommendation: ≥2 sessions per day (each 10-30 minutes)

Techniques: - Active Cycle of Breathing - Autogenic Drainage - Postural Drainage - Oscillating PEP devices (Flutter, Acapella) - High-Frequency Chest Wall Oscillation (HFCWO vest)

Adjuncts: - Nebulized Hypertonic Saline (3-7%): Before ACT - Give after bronchodilator (to prevent bronchospasm)
