Why NOT Other Options?: - Dietary modification: No specific role in bronchiectasis (unless associated condition like CF) - Influenza vaccination: Important for prevention, but NOT the MOST important - Avoidance of air pollution: Helpful, but NOT the MOST important

Key Point: Airway Clearance Techniques are the MOST IMPORTANT non-pharmacological intervention in bronchiectasis

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
Bronchiectasis is characterized by: - Permanent abnormal bronchial dilatation with chronic inflammation - Most common cause: TB (worldwide), CF (genetic) - Cardinal symptom: Chronic productive cough with copious sputum - Gold standard diagnosis: HRCT (Signet Ring Sign) - Most common organism: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (worse prognosis)

Management: 1. Airway Clearance Techniques: CORNERSTONE (≥2 sessions/day) 2. Antibiotics: 14 days for exacerbations; long-term azithromycin if ≥3 exacerbations/year (rule out NTM first) 3. Treat underlying cause: ABPA (steroids + itraconazole), CVID (IVIG), CF (CFTR modulators) 4. Massive hemoptysis: Bronchial Artery Embolization (BAE)

Key Syndromes: - Kartagener: Bronchiectasis + Situs inversus + Sinusitis + Infertility - ABPA: Asthma + IgE >1000 + Central bronchiectasis + Eosinophilia - CVID: Low IgG + IgA/IgM + Recurrent infections → IVIG

Prognosis: Variable; worse with P. aeruginosa, frequent exacerbations, low FEV₁

END OF CHAPTER

Next Topic: Lung Cancer (Medicine/17_Lung_Cancer.md)

Cross-References: - Tuberculosis (Medicine/14_Tuberculosis.md) - Asthma (Medicine/11_Asthma.md) - COPD (Medicine/12_COPD.md) - Cystic Fibrosis (Pediatrics - planned)

docs.title: LUNG CANCER
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5 - Extremely High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 8-10 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Definition & Classification
  2. Epidemiology
  3. Risk Factors
  4. Pathophysiology
  5. Clinical Features
  6. Diagnosis
  7. Staging
  8. Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)
  9. Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC)
  10. Treatment
  11. Paraneoplastic Syndromes
  12. Screening
  13. Prognosis
  14. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  15. Practice MCQs

docs.title: DEFINITION & CLASSIFICATION
docs.text: Lung Cancer: Malignant tumor arising from the respiratory epithelium (bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
LUNG CANCER
│
├─ NON-SMALL CELL LUNG CANCER (NSCLC) - 85%
│  │
│  ├─ ADENOCARCINOMA (40%) - MOST COMMON OVERALL
│  │  • Peripheral location
│  │  • Non-smokers, women
│  │  • Glandular differentiation
│  │  • Lepidic, acinar, papillary, solid subtypes
│  │
│  ├─ SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (SCC) (25-30%)
│  │  • Central location (hilar)
│  │  • STRONG association with smoking
│  │  • Keratin pearls, intercellular bridges
│  │  • Cavitation common
│  │  • Hypercalcemia (PTHrP)
│  │
│  └─ LARGE CELL CARCINOMA (10-15%)
│     • Poorly differentiated
│     • Large cells, prominent nucleoli
│     • Diagnosis of exclusion
│
└─ SMALL CELL LUNG CANCER (SCLC) - 15%
   • Central location
   • STRONGEST association with smoking
   • Neuroendocrine origin
   • Rapid growth, early metastasis
   • Paraneoplastic syndromes (SIADH, Cushing's, LEMS)
   • Small cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding
   • "Oat cell" appearance

docs.title: Mnemonic: "SALL" for Lung Cancer Types
docs.text:
• S - Small Cell (15%, worst prognosis)
  • A - Adenocarcinoma (40%, MOST COMMON)
  • L - Large Cell (10-15%)
  • L - squamous (Large) cell (25-30%, was most common before, now 2nd)

docs.title: Global Burden
docs.text:
• Most common cancer worldwide (both sexes combined)
  • Leading cause of cancer death worldwide
  • Accounts for ~18% of all cancer deaths

docs.title: India
docs.text:
• Most common cancer in males (age-adjusted)
  • 3rd-4th most common in females (after breast, cervix)
  • Increasing incidence due to smoking, pollution

docs.title: Demographics
docs.text: Parameter Details Age Peak incidence: 60-70 years (rare <40 years) Sex Male > Female (historically 3:1, now narrowing to 2:1 due to ↑ smoking in women) Smoking 85-90% of lung cancers are smoking-related

docs.title: Trends
docs.text:
• Adenocarcinoma incidence increasing (now MOST COMMON)
  • More common in non-smokers, women
  • Associated with "light" cigarettes (deeper inhalation → peripheral deposition)
  • Squamous cell incidence decreasing
  • Previously most common
  • Strongly linked to heavy smoking

docs.title: 1. Cigarette Smoking
docs.text:
MOST IMPORTANT risk factor (85-90% of cases)

Parameter Risk Pack-years (Packs per day) × (Years smoked) Risk increase 20-fold in heavy smokers (>20 pack-years) Dose-response ↑ Duration and intensity → ↑ Risk Type Squamous cell and SCLC - STRONGEST association Adenocarcinoma - Weaker but still strong association Secondhand smoke 20-30% ↑ risk Cessation Risk decreases over 10-15 years but never returns to baseline

Pack-Year Calculation: - 1 pack-year = 1 pack/day × 1 year - Example: 2 packs/day × 20 years = 40 pack-years

docs.title: 2. Radon Exposure
docs.text:
• 2nd most common cause of lung cancer (after smoking)
  • Radioactive gas from uranium decay
  • Accumulates in basements, mines
  • Synergistic with smoking (multiplicative risk)

docs.title: 3. Occupational Exposures
docs.text:
Carcinogen Occupation Associated Type Asbestos Shipbuilding, construction, insulation Adenocarcinoma, Mesothelioma Arsenic Pesticides, smelting All types Chromium Welding, electroplating Squamous, SCLC Nickel Refining, electroplating Squamous, SCLC Silica Mining, sandblasting All types Bis(chloromethyl) ether Chemical industry SCLC

Asbestos + Smoking: Multiplicative risk (50-90x ↑ vs non-smokers)

docs.title: 4. Other Risk Factors
docs.text:
• Air pollution: Particulate matter (PM2.5)
  • Radiation: Thoracic radiation (breast cancer, lymphoma treatment)
  • Lung diseases: COPD, pulmonary fibrosis, TB (scar carcinoma)
  • Family history: First-degree relative with lung cancer (2x risk)
  • Genetic: Polymorphisms in CYP genes, DNA repair genes

docs.title: Non-Smoking-Related Lung Cancer
docs.text:
10-15% of lung cancers occur in never-smokers

Risk Factors: - Female sex - Asian ethnicity - EGFR mutations (somatic, not inherited) - Environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) - Radon, occupational exposures - Cooking fumes (indoor air pollution)

Type: Predominantly Adenocarcinoma (>80%)

docs.title: Carcinogenesis Pathway
docs.text:
CARCINOGEN EXPOSURE (Tobacco smoke, Radon, Asbestos)
           ↓
    GENETIC MUTATIONS
    • Oncogene activation: KRAS, EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF
    • Tumor suppressor inactivation: TP53, RB, CDKN2A
           ↓
    DYSPLASIA → CARCINOMA IN SITU
           ↓
    INVASIVE CARCINOMA
           ↓
    LOCAL INVASION
    • Chest wall, mediastinum, diaphragm
    • Pleural effusion (malignant)
           ↓
    LYMPHATIC SPREAD
    • Hilar → Mediastinal → Supraclavicular nodes
           ↓
    HEMATOGENOUS SPREAD
    • Brain, Bone, Liver, Adrenal glands

docs.title: Molecular Subtypes (NSCLC)
docs.text:
Targetable Driver Mutations (Predominantly in Adenocarcinoma, non-smokers):

Mutation Frequency (Adenoca) Target Therapy Notes EGFR 15-30% (Asia: 50%) Erlotinib, Gefitinib, Osimertinib Most common in Asian non-smokers, women ALK 3-5% Crizotinib, Alectinib, Brigatinib Young, minimal/never-smokers, signet ring cells ROS1 1-2% Crizotinib Similar to ALK BRAF (V600E) 1-3% Dabrafenib + Trametinib Melanoma drug repurposed MET (exon 14 skipping) 3-4% Capmatinib, Tepotinib Elderly, sarcomatoid histology KRAS (G12C) 25-30% (mostly smokers) Sotorasib, Adagrasib Historically "undruggable", now targetable RET 1-2% Selpercatinib, Pralsetinib Also in MTC NTRK <1% Larotrectinib, Entrectinib Pan-cancer target

Key Point: EGFR and ALK are the MOST CLINICALLY IMPORTANT due to frequency and efficacy of targeted therapy

PD-L1 Expression (Immunotherapy Biomarker): - High (≥50%): First-line pembrolizumab monotherapy - 1-49%: Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy - <1%: Chemotherapy alone (or other immunotherapy combinations)

docs.title: Local/Thoracic Symptoms (50-60% at presentation)
docs.text:
1. Persistent Cough (50-75%)
  2. Most common symptom
  3. New cough in smoker or change in chronic cough

  4. Dry or productive

  5. Hemoptysis (25-50%)

  6. Blood-streaked sputum

  7. Massive hemoptysis is rare (<5%)

  8. Dyspnea (25-40%)

  9. Airway obstruction, atelectasis, pleural effusion

  10. Chest Pain (20-40%)

  11. Dull, aching (tumor invasion)

  12. Pleuritic (pleural involvement)

  13. Wheezing/Stridor

  14. Endobronchial obstruction

docs.title: Constitutional Symptoms (30-40%)
docs.text:
• Weight loss (SCLC > NSCLC)
  • Fatigue, anorexia
  • Fever (post-obstructive pneumonia, paraneoplastic)

docs.title: Symptoms from Intrathoracic Extension
docs.text: Structure Involved Syndrome Clinical Features Recurrent laryngeal nerve (Left more common) Vocal cord paralysis Hoarseness (ask patient to say "E") Phrenic nerve Hemidiaphragm paralysis Dyspnea, ↑ elevated hemidiaphragm on CXR Esophagus Dysphagia Difficulty swallowing, odynophagia Sympathetic chain (C8-T2) Horner Syndrome Ptosis, Miosis, Anhidrosis (ipsilateral face) Brachial plexus (C8-T1) Pancoast Syndrome Shoulder pain → arm pain (ulnar distribution), Horner syndrome, rib erosion Superior vena cava (SVC) SVC Syndrome Facial/upper extremity edema, distended neck veins, dyspnea, cyanosis, collateral veins on chest Pericardium Malignant pericardial effusion Dyspnea, tamponade (rare) Pleura Malignant pleural effusion Dyspnea, dullness on percussion

docs.title: Metastatic Symptoms (30-40% at presentation)
docs.text:
Site Frequency Symptoms Brain 20-40% Headache, seizures, focal neurological deficits, personality changes Bone 30-40% Bone pain (back, hip, rib), pathological fractures Liver 30-50% RUQ pain, hepatomegaly (usually asymptomatic) Adrenal 20-30% Usually asymptomatic (bilateral involvement → adrenal insufficiency) Lung (contralateral) 10-20% May be synchronous primary or metastasis

SCLC: 60-70% have metastases at presentation (vs 40% in NSCLC)

docs.title: Asymptomatic Presentation
docs.text:
• 10-15% detected incidentally on imaging (CXR, CT)
  • Increasing with screening programs

docs.title: Physical Examination
docs.text: Finding Significance Clubbing Chronic hypoxemia, HPOA Lymphadenopathy Supraclavicular (especially left = Virchow's node), cervical, axillary Wheezing/Stridor Endobronchial obstruction Pleural effusion Dullness, ↓ breath sounds, ↓ vocal fremitus SVC syndrome Facial plethora, distended neck/chest veins, edema Horner syndrome Ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis (Pancoast tumor) Hepatomegaly Liver metastases (hard, nodular) Bone tenderness Bone metastases Focal neurological signs Brain metastases

docs.title: Diagnostic Approach
docs.text:
CLINICAL SUSPICION
(Persistent cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, smoking history)
           ↓
    IMAGING (Chest X-Ray → CT Chest)
           ↓
    TISSUE DIAGNOSIS (Biopsy)
    • Bronchoscopy (central lesions)
    • CT-guided biopsy (peripheral lesions)
    • Lymph node biopsy (mediastinal staging)
           ↓
    STAGING (TNM)
    • CT Chest/Abdomen
    • PET-CT
    • Brain MRI
    • Bone scan (if symptomatic)
           ↓
    MOLECULAR TESTING (NSCLC, non-squamous)
    • EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, PD-L1

docs.title: 1. Chest X-Ray
docs.text:
Initial test in most patients

Findings: - Lung nodule/mass (>3 cm = mass) - Solitary pulmonary nodule (SPN): Single, round, <3 cm - Hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy - Atelectasis/collapse (obstruction) - Pleural effusion - Cavitation (Squamous > others) - Rib erosion (Pancoast tumor)

Limitations: - Small nodules (<1 cm) may be missed - Central lesions hidden by mediastinum/heart

docs.title: 2. CT Chest (Contrast-Enhanced)
docs.text:
Gold standard for detection and characterization

Provides: - Size, location, characteristics of primary tumor - Lymph node involvement (hilar, mediastinal) - Chest wall/mediastinal invasion - Pleural involvement - Additional nodules (multifocal disease)

Nodule Characteristics Suggestive of Malignancy: - Size >8-10 mm - Spiculated/irregular margins (corona radiata sign) - Growth on serial imaging (doubling time 30-400 days) - Upper lobe location - Solid (vs ground-glass)

docs.title: 3. PET-CT (Fluorodeoxyglucose-FDG)
docs.text:
Indications: - Staging (detect metastases) - Differentiate benign vs malignant nodules (if indeterminate on CT) - Assess treatment response

Principle: Cancer cells have ↑ glucose uptake (FDG avid)

Sensitivity: 85-95% for lesions >1 cm Specificity: 70-85% (False positives: Infection, inflammation, granulomas)

False Negatives: - Bronchoalveolar carcinoma (lepidic-predominant adenocarcinoma) - Small lesions (<8 mm) - Well-differentiated tumors - Hyperglycemia (↓ tumor uptake)

docs.title: A. Bronchoscopy
docs.text:
Indications: - Central lesions (endobronchial) - Accessible hilar/mediastinal lymph nodes (EBUS)

Techniques: 1. Endobronchial Biopsy: Direct visualization and biopsy 2. Transbronchial Biopsy: Through bronchial wall (peripheral lesions) 3. Bronchial Brushing/Washing: Cytology 4. Endobronchial Ultrasound (EBUS): Guide biopsy of mediastinal lymph nodes (N staging)

Yield: - Central lesions: 80-90% - Peripheral lesions: 50-70%

docs.title: B. CT-Guided Transthoracic Needle Biopsy (TTNA)
docs.text:
Indications: - Peripheral lesions not accessible by bronchoscopy - Staging (adrenal, liver, bone metastases)

Yield: 80-95%

Risk: Pneumothorax (15-30%, <5% require chest tube)

docs.title: C. Surgical Biopsy
docs.text:
Indications: - Non-diagnostic bronchoscopy/TTNA - Diagnostic uncertainty - Concomitant treatment (e.g., resection of SPN)

Methods: - Video-Assisted Thoracoscopic Surgery (VATS): Wedge resection - Mediastinoscopy: Mediastinal lymph node biopsy (paratracheal, subcarinal)

docs.title: D. Other Biopsy Sites
docs.text:
• Supraclavicular lymph node: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) or excisional biopsy
  • Pleural fluid: Thoracentesis (cytology) ± Pleural biopsy
  • Bone marrow: If SCLC with cytopenias

docs.title: 5. Molecular/Genetic Testing
docs.text:
WHO: All non-squamous NSCLC (especially adenocarcinoma) WHEN: At diagnosis (stage IIIB/IV, recurrent disease)

Essential Tests: 1. EGFR mutations (Exons 18-21) - Activating mutations: Exon 19 deletion, L858R (Exon 21) - Best response to TKIs - Resistance mutation: T790M (Exon 20) - Use 3rd-gen TKI (Osimertinib)

  1. ALK rearrangement
  2. IHC or FISH

  3. EML4-ALK fusion (most common)

  4. ROS1 rearrangement

  5. IHC or FISH

  6. PD-L1 expression (Immunohistochemistry)

  7. Tumor Proportion Score (TPS): % of tumor cells expressing PD-L1
  8. ≥50%: High expression → Pembrolizumab monotherapy
  9. 1-49%: Combination therapy
  10. <1%: Chemotherapy

Additional Tests (if negative for above or specific indications): - BRAF V600E mutation - MET exon 14 skipping - RET rearrangement - NTRK fusion - KRAS G12C mutation

Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS): Can test multiple genes simultaneously

docs.title: 6. Staging Investigations
docs.text: Test Purpose CT Chest + Abdomen (Contrast) Lung, mediastinum, liver, adrenal metastases PET-CT Distant metastases (especially bone), mediastinal lymph nodes Brain MRI (Contrast) ALL small cell, Stage III/IV NSCLC (brain mets common) Bone scan If bone pain or elevated ALP (less sensitive than PET-CT) Mediastinoscopy/EBUS Pathological confirmation of mediastinal nodes (if PET-positive)

docs.title: 7. Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs)
docs.text:
Indication: Preoperative assessment (determine operability)

Criteria for Operability: - Post-operative predicted FEV₁ >40% (or >1 L) - Post-operative predicted DLCO >40%

If marginal lung function: - Cardiopulmonary exercise testing (VO₂ max >15 mL/kg/min)

docs.title: T (Primary Tumor)
docs.text: Stage Descriptor TX Cannot be assessed, or tumor proven by cytology but not seen on imaging/bronchoscopy T0 No evidence of primary tumor Tis Carcinoma in situ (Squamous or Adenocarcinoma in situ) T1 ≤3 cm, surrounded by lung/visceral pleura, no bronchoscopic invasion proximal to lobar bronchus T1a ≤1 cm (minimally invasive adenocarcinoma if ≤3 cm with ≤5 mm invasion) T1b >1 to ≤2 cm T1c >2 to ≤3 cm T2 >3 to ≤5 cm, OR involves main bronchus (not carina), OR invades visceral pleura, OR atelectasis/pneumonitis extending to hilum T2a >3 to ≤4 cm T2b >4 to ≤5 cm T3 >5 to ≤7 cm, OR separate nodule(s) in same lobe, OR invades chest wall, phrenic nerve, parietal pericardium T4 >7 cm, OR invades diaphragm, mediastinum, heart, great vessels, trachea, RLN, esophagus, vertebral body, carina, OR separate nodule(s) in different ipsilateral lobe

docs.title: N (Regional Lymph Nodes)
docs.text: Stage Descriptor NX Cannot be assessed N0 No regional lymph node metastasis N1 Ipsilateral peribronchial and/or hilar nodes, intrapulmonary nodes N2 Ipsilateral mediastinal and/or subcarinal nodes N3 Contralateral mediastinal/hilar nodes, ipsilateral/contralateral scalene/supraclavicular nodes

docs.title: M (Distant Metastasis)
docs.text: Stage Descriptor M0 No distant metastasis M1a Separate nodule(s) in contralateral lobe, OR pleural/pericardial nodules, OR malignant pleural/pericardial effusion M1b Single extrathoracic metastasis (in single organ) M1c Multiple extrathoracic metastases (single or multiple organs)

docs.title: Stage Grouping (NSCLC)
docs.text: Stage TNM 5-Year Survival IA1 T1a N0 M0 90% IA2 T1b N0 M0 85% IA3 T1c N0 M0 80% IB T2a N0 M0 73% IIA T2b N0 M0 65% IIB T1-2 N1 M0, T3 N0 M0 56% IIIA T1-2 N2 M0, T3-4 N1 M0, T4 N0 M0 36% IIIB T3-4 N2 M0 26% IIIC T1-4 N3 M0 13% IVA Any T, Any N, M1a/M1b 10% IVB Any T, Any N, M1c 0%

docs.title: SCLC Staging (Simplified)
docs.text:
Traditional (Still used clinically):

Stage Definition Treatment Limited Stage Confined to one hemithorax + regional lymph nodes (can fit in one radiation port) Chemo + Radiation (Curative intent) Extensive Stage Beyond one hemithorax (contralateral lung, distant metastases, malignant pleural/pericardial effusion) Chemotherapy (Palliative)

TNM Staging (Now preferred): Same as NSCLC (for uniform reporting)

docs.title: Adenocarcinoma
docs.text: Most common lung cancer (40%)

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Location Peripheral (subpleural) Smoking association Moderate (but most common in never-smokers) Demographics Women, non-smokers, younger age Growth Slower than SCLC Metastasis Moderate rate Molecular mutations EGFR (15-30%, Asia 50%), ALK (3-5%), ROS1 (1-2%) Histology Glandular differentiation, mucin production Subtypes Lepidic, Acinar, Papillary, Solid, Mucinous

docs.title: Special Subtypes
docs.text:
1. Lepidic-Predominant Adenocarcinoma (formerly Bronchoalveolar Carcinoma) - Growth along alveolar walls (lepidic pattern) - Ground-glass opacity on CT - Low FDG uptake on PET (may be PET-negative) - Better prognosis

2. Invasive Mucinous Adenocarcinoma - Abundant mucin production - Consolidation on imaging (mimics pneumonia) - KRAS mutations common

docs.title: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)
docs.text: Second most common (25-30%)

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Location Central (proximal bronchi, hilar) Smoking association STRONGEST (rare in never-smokers) Demographics Men, heavy smokers Growth Moderate Metastasis Later than adenocarcinoma Molecular mutations Rare (EGFR, ALK uncommon) Histology Keratin pearls, intercellular bridges, keratinization Cavitation Common (25-30%) - Tumor outgrows blood supply → Necrosis

docs.title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes
docs.text:
• Hypercalcemia: PTHrP (Parathyroid Hormone-related Peptide) - MOST COMMON
  • Hypertrophic Pulmonary Osteoarthropathy (HPOA)

docs.title: Large Cell Carcinoma
docs.text: Least common NSCLC (10-15%)

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• Poorly differentiated
  • Diagnosis of exclusion: No glandular (adeno) or squamous differentiation on light microscopy
  • Large cells with prominent nucleoli
  • Peripheral location
  • Rapid growth, early metastasis
  • Worst prognosis among NSCLC

docs.title: Subtypes
docs.text: • Large Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma (LCNEC): Aggressive, neuroendocrine markers positive

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text: Feature Details Frequency 15% of lung cancers Location Central (hilar, mediastinal) Smoking association STRONGEST (>95% are smokers) Cell origin Neuroendocrine (Kulchitsky cells) Growth rate VERY RAPID (doubling time 30 days) Metastasis EARLY and WIDESPREAD (60-70% have metastases at diagnosis) Histology Small cells with scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, "salt-and-pepper" chromatin, "Oat cell" appearance Immunohistochemistry Chromogranin+, Synaptophysin+, CD56+, TTF-1+ Prognosis WORST (Median survival 6-12 months if untreated)

docs.title: Staging
docs.text:
Limited Stage (30%): - Confined to one hemithorax + regional lymph nodes - Can fit in one radiation port - Median survival: 15-20 months (with treatment)

Extensive Stage (70%): - Beyond one hemithorax - Distant metastases (Brain, liver, bone, adrenal, bone marrow) - Malignant pleural/pericardial effusion - Median survival: 8-12 months (with treatment)

docs.title: Common Metastatic Sites (at Diagnosis)
docs.text:
• Liver (50%)
  • Brain (40%) - Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI) given after response to prevent brain mets
  • Bone (30%)
  • Adrenal (20%)
  • Bone marrow (20%)

docs.title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes (SCLC >> NSCLC)
docs.text: Syndrome Mechanism Clinical Features Diagnostic Test SIADH (10-15%) - MOST COMMON Ectopic ADH production Hyponatremia (<130 mEq/L), euvolemic, urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg Serum Na+, Urine osmolality Cushing Syndrome (2-5%) Ectopic ACTH production Hyperglycemia, hypokalemia, alkalosis, muscle weakness, edema (NO cushingoid features due to rapid onset) ↑ Cortisol, ↑ ACTH, no dexamethasone suppression Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS) (1-3%) Autoantibodies to presynaptic Ca²⁺ channels (VGCC) at NMJ Proximal muscle weakness (legs > arms), IMPROVES with repetitive use, ↓ DTRs, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, erectile dysfunction) Anti-VGCC antibodies, incremental response on EMG (opposite of myasthenia)

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text: Treatment depends on: 1. Histology (NSCLC vs SCLC) 2. Stage (I-IV for NSCLC; Limited vs Extensive for SCLC) 3. Molecular markers (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1 for NSCLC) 4. Performance Status (ECOG 0-4) 5. Lung function (FEV₁, DLCO for surgery)

docs.title: NSCLC Treatment by Stage
docs.text:
NSCLC TREATMENT ALGORITHM
│
├─ STAGE I-II (T1-2, N0-1)
│  └─ SURGICAL RESECTION (Lobectomy preferred, Pneumonectomy if needed)
│     ± Adjuvant Chemotherapy (if IB-II, tumor >4 cm)
│
├─ STAGE III
│  ├─ IIIA (Resectable)
│  │  └─ Neoadjuvant Chemo/Chemoradiation → Surgery → Adjuvant therapy
│  │
│  ├─ IIIA (Unresectable) / IIIB / IIIC
│  │  └─ Concurrent Chemoradiation → Consolidation Immunotherapy (Durvalumab)
│  │
│  └─ Pancoast Tumor (T3-4 N0-1)
│     └─ Neoadjuvant Chemoradiation → Surgery
│
└─ STAGE IV (Metastatic)
   ├─ DRIVER MUTATION POSITIVE (EGFR/ALK/ROS1/BRAF/MET/RET/NTRK)
   │  └─ TARGETED THERAPY (First-line)
   │     • EGFR+: Osimertinib (1st-line)
   │     • ALK+: Alectinib, Brigatinib
   │     • ROS1+: Crizotinib, Entrectinib
   │     • BRAF V600E+: Dabrafenib + Trametinib
   │
   ├─ DRIVER MUTATION NEGATIVE, PD-L1 ≥50%
   │  └─ Pembrolizumab (Immunotherapy monotherapy)
   │
   └─ DRIVER MUTATION NEGATIVE, PD-L1 <50%
      └─ Chemotherapy ± Immunotherapy
         • Platinum doublet (Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Pemetrexed/Paclitaxel)
         • + Pembrolizumab (if PD-L1 ≥1%)

docs.title: Surgical Resection (NSCLC)
docs.text:
Indications: Stage I-II, selected IIIA

Criteria: - Resectable tumor (No T4 invasion, N2 resectable) - Adequate lung function (Post-op FEV₁ >40% or >1 L) - No distant metastases (M0) - Good performance status (ECOG 0-1)

Procedures: - Lobectomy: Standard of care (removes entire lobe) - Lowest recurrence rate - Segmentectomy/Wedge resection: For small tumors (<2 cm) or poor lung function - Pneumonectomy: If tumor involves main bronchus/central location (higher morbidity/mortality)

Adjuvant Chemotherapy (After Surgery): - Stage IB (>4 cm), II, IIIA: Platinum-based doublet × 4 cycles - Improves 5-year survival by 5-10%

docs.title: Radiation Therapy (NSCLC)
docs.text:
Indications: 1. Curative Intent: - Stage I-II (medically inoperable): Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy (SBRT) / Cyberknife - High-dose, few fractions (3-5), excellent local control - Stage III (unresectable): Concurrent chemoradiation 2. Palliative: - Bone metastases (pain relief) - Brain metastases - SVC obstruction - Hemoptysis, bronchial obstruction

Consolidation Immunotherapy (After Chemoradiation for Stage III): - Durvalumab (Anti-PD-L1) × 12 months - Improves progression-free survival

docs.title: Chemotherapy (NSCLC)
docs.text:
Regimens (Platinum-based doublet):

Histology Preferred Regimen Non-squamous Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Pemetrexed Squamous Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine

Note: Pemetrexed contraindicated in squamous cell carcinoma (ineffective)

Duration: 4-6 cycles

docs.title: Targeted Therapy (NSCLC)
docs.text:
ONLY if driver mutation positive

Mutation First-Line TKI Mechanism Notes EGFR (Exon 19 del, L858R) Osimertinib (3rd-gen) EGFR TKI ↓ Brain mets, overcomes T790M resistance Erlotinib, Gefitinib (1st-gen) Used if osimertinib unavailable EGFR T790M (resistance) Osimertinib After progression on 1st/2nd-gen TKIs ALK Alectinib, Brigatinib ALK TKI Better CNS penetration than crizotinib Crizotinib (1st-gen) ROS1 Crizotinib, Entrectinib ROS1 TKI BRAF V600E Dabrafenib + Trametinib BRAF + MEK inhibitors Combination required MET exon 14 Capmatinib, Tepotinib MET TKI RET Selpercatinib, Pralsetinib RET TKI KRAS G12C Sotorasib, Adagrasib KRAS G12C inhibitor First KRAS-targeted therapy

Advantages over Chemotherapy: - Higher response rates (60-80% vs 30-40%) - Longer progression-free survival - Better quality of life (oral, fewer side effects)

Resistance: Develops after 9-18 months (mechanisms: T790M, MET amplification, histologic transformation to SCLC)

docs.title: Immunotherapy (NSCLC)
docs.text:
Checkpoint Inhibitors (Block PD-1/PD-L1 or CTLA-4 → Restore T-cell activity)

Drug Target Indication Pembrolizumab Anti-PD-1 First-line (PD-L1 ≥50% monotherapy, or ≥1% with chemo) Nivolumab Anti-PD-1 Second-line (after platinum chemo) Atezolizumab Anti-PD-L1 First-line (with chemo) or second-line Durvalumab Anti-PD-L1 Consolidation after chemoradiation (Stage III)

Selection Criteria: - PD-L1 ≥50%: Pembrolizumab monotherapy (first-line) - PD-L1 1-49%: Chemotherapy + Pembrolizumab - PD-L1 <1%: Chemotherapy (consider immunotherapy combinations)

Contraindication: EGFR/ALK-positive (use TKIs first, immunotherapy after resistance)

Side Effects: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) - Pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis, hypophysitis, dermatitis - Managed with corticosteroids

docs.title: SCLC Treatment
docs.text:
SCLC TREATMENT ALGORITHM
│
├─ LIMITED STAGE
│  └─ CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION
│     • Chemotherapy: Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Etoposide × 4-6 cycles
│     • Radiation: Concurrent (given with 1st-2nd cycle)
│     ↓
│     IF RESPONSE → Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI)
│     • Reduces brain metastases by 50%
│     • Improves 3-year survival from 15% → 21%
│
└─ EXTENSIVE STAGE
   └─ CHEMOTHERAPY (Palliative)
      • Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Etoposide × 4-6 cycles
      • ± Immunotherapy (Atezolizumab/Durvalumab - added to chemo, improves survival)
      ↓
      IF RESPONSE → Consider PCI (controversial, no clear benefit in extensive stage)

Prognosis: - Limited Stage: Median survival 15-20 months (with treatment) - Extensive Stage: Median survival 8-12 months (with treatment)

Relapse: Common (80-90%) - Sensitive relapse (≥90 days after treatment): Re-challenge with same chemo - Refractory relapse (<90 days): Second-line chemo (Topotecan, Irinotecan)

Surgery: Rarely indicated (only very early stage I, <5% of patients)

docs.title: Endocrine/Metabolic
docs.text: Syndrome Mechanism Tumor Type Clinical Features Treatment Hypercalcemia PTHrP (Parathyroid Hormone-related Peptide) Squamous Cell (MOST COMMON) Polyuria, polydipsia, confusion, constipation, muscle weakness Fluids, Bisphosphonates (Zoledronic acid), Calcitonin SIADH Ectopic ADH SCLC (MOST COMMON) Hyponatremia (<130), euvolemic, confusion, seizures (if severe) Fluid restriction, Demeclocycline, Vaptans, Treat tumor Cushing Syndrome Ectopic ACTH SCLC Hyperglycemia, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, muscle weakness, edema (NO cushingoid features) Treat tumor, Ketoconazole, Metyrapone

docs.title: Neurological
docs.text: Syndrome Mechanism Tumor Type Clinical Features Diagnosis Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS) Anti-VGCC (Voltage-Gated Calcium Channel) antibodies SCLC (60%) Proximal muscle weakness, IMPROVES with use, ↓ DTRs, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, impotence) Anti-VGCC antibodies, Incremental response on EMG (↑ amplitude with rapid stimulation) Cerebellar Degeneration Anti-Yo, Anti-Hu antibodies SCLC Ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus Anti-neuronal antibodies, MRI (cerebellar atrophy) Encephalomyelitis Anti-Hu antibodies SCLC Seizures, dementia, sensory/motor deficits Anti-Hu antibodies Sensory Neuropathy Anti-Hu antibodies SCLC Asymmetric sensory loss, pain Anti-Hu antibodies

docs.title: Hematologic
docs.text: Syndrome Mechanism Clinical Features Anemia of Chronic Disease Inflammatory cytokines Normocytic anemia Leukemoid Reaction G-CSF production WBC >50,000/μL (mimics leukemia) Thrombocytosis IL-6, Thrombopoietin Platelets >450,000/μL Hypercoagulability (Trousseau Syndrome) Tissue factor, mucin Migratory thrombophlebitis, DVT/PE (especially adenocarcinoma) Marantic Endocarditis Hypercoagulability Non-bacterial vegetations on heart valves

docs.title: Dermatologic
docs.text: Syndrome Clinical Features Tumor Type Hypertrophic Pulmonary Osteoarthropathy (HPOA) Clubbing, periostitis (long bones), arthritis Adenocarcinoma, Squamous Acanthosis Nigricans Hyperpigmented, velvety plaques (axilla, neck) Adenocarcinoma Dermatomyositis Heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, proximal muscle weakness All types

docs.title: U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) Recommendations
docs.text:
Who to Screen: - Age 50-80 years (updated 2021, was 55-80) - ≥20 pack-year smoking history - Current smoker OR quit within past 15 years - Good overall health (life expectancy >10 years)

Screening Test: Low-Dose CT Chest (LDCT) annually

Benefits: - 20% reduction in lung cancer mortality (National Lung Screening Trial) - Detects cancers at earlier stage (more resectable)

Harms: - False positives (95% of nodules are benign) → Anxiety, unnecessary procedures - Radiation exposure (low, but cumulative) - Overdiagnosis (indolent cancers that would never harm)

Note: CXR and sputum cytology are NOT effective for screening

docs.title: Nodule Management (Fleischner Society Guidelines)
docs.text:
For nodules detected on CT:

Size Solid Nodule Ground-Glass Nodule <6 mm No routine follow-up (unless high risk) No routine follow-up 6-8 mm CT at 6-12 months, then annually × 1-2 years CT at 6-12 months, then annually × 2-4 years >8 mm CT at 3 months, PET-CT, or biopsy CT at 3 months, then annually until 5 years

Spiculated nodule >8 mm: Consider PET-CT or biopsy (high suspicion for malignancy)

docs.title: 5-Year Survival by Stage (NSCLC)
docs.text: Stage 5-Year Survival IA 85-90% IB 70-75% IIA 60-65% IIB 50-56% IIIA 30-36% IIIB 20-26% IV 5-10%

docs.title: Prognostic Factors
docs.text:
Favorable: - Early stage (I-II) - Female sex - Adenocarcinoma histology - Good performance status (ECOG 0-1) - Driver mutation positive (EGFR, ALK) with targeted therapy available - Complete surgical resection

Unfavorable: - Advanced stage (IV) - SCLC - Poor performance status (ECOG 3-4) - Weight loss >10% - Elevated LDH - Hypercalcemia - Metastases (brain, liver, bone)

docs.title: SCLC Prognosis
docs.text:
Stage Median Survival (with treatment) 2-Year Survival Limited 15-20 months 20-40% Extensive 8-12 months <5%

Without treatment: Median survival 2-4 months

docs.title: Must-Know Facts
docs.text:
1. Most Common Lung Cancer: Adenocarcinoma (40%)
  2. Peripheral location

  3. Most common in never-smokers, women

  4. Strongest Smoking Association: Squamous Cell and SCLC

  5. Squamous: Central, cavitation common, PTHrP → Hypercalcemia

  6. SCLC: Central, rapid growth, early metastasis, paraneoplastic syndromes

  7. SCLC Characteristics:

  8. Neuroendocrine origin (Chromogranin+, Synaptophysin+)
  9. "Oat cell" appearance
  10. 60-70% have metastases at presentation
  11. Worst prognosis

  12. Paraneoplastic: SIADH (most common), Ectopic ACTH, LEMS

  13. HRCT Diagnosis: Signet Ring Sign for bronchiectasis, NOT lung cancer

  14. Lung cancer: Spiculated nodule >8 mm → High suspicion

  15. Molecular Markers (NSCLC, especially Adenocarcinoma):

  16. EGFR: 15-30% (Asia 50%), Asian non-smokers, women
    • Treatment: Osimertinib (1st-line TKI)
  17. ALK: 3-5%, young, never-smokers
    • Treatment: Alectinib, Brigatinib

  18. PD-L1 ≥50%: Pembrolizumab monotherapy (1st-line)

  19. Pancoast Tumor (Superior Sulcus Tumor):

  20. Horner Syndrome (Ptosis, Miosis, Anhidrosis)
  21. Brachial plexus invasion (C8-T1) → Shoulder/arm pain (ulnar distribution)
  22. Rib erosion

  23. Treatment: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation → Surgery

  24. SVC Syndrome:

  25. Facial/upper extremity edema, distended neck veins, collateral veins on chest
  26. Most common cause: Lung cancer (SCLC > NSCLC)

  27. Emergency treatment: Radiation (if SCLC), Stent (if obstruction)

  28. Screening: Low-Dose CT Chest (LDCT)

  29. Age 50-80 years, ≥20 pack-years, current/quit within 15 years

  30. 20% reduction in mortality

  31. Surgical Criteria:

  32. Post-op FEV₁ >40% or >1 L

  33. Lobectomy preferred (standard of care)

  34. Chemotherapy:

    • Non-squamous: Platinum + Pemetrexed
    • Squamous: Platinum + Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine
    • DO NOT use Pemetrexed in squamous (ineffective)

  35. SCLC Treatment:

    • Limited Stage: Concurrent chemoradiation → PCI (Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation)
    • Extensive Stage: Chemotherapy (Platinum + Etoposide) ± Immunotherapy

  36. Paraneoplastic Syndromes:

    • Hypercalcemia: Squamous (PTHrP)
    • SIADH: SCLC (Ectopic ADH) → Hyponatremia
    • Cushing: SCLC (Ectopic ACTH) → Hypokalemia, alkalosis, NO cushingoid features
    • LEMS: SCLC (Anti-VGCC) → Proximal weakness, IMPROVES with use, Incremental response on EMG

docs.title: NSCLC vs SCLC
docs.text: Feature NSCLC SCLC Frequency 85% 15% Location Peripheral (Adeno) or Central (Squamous) Central Smoking Moderate-strong STRONGEST (>95% smokers) Growth Slower VERY RAPID Metastasis Moderate EARLY, WIDESPREAD (60-70% at diagnosis) Histology Glandular (Adeno), Keratin pearls (Squamous) Small cells, nuclear molding, "Oat cell" Markers EGFR, ALK, ROS1 (Adeno) Chromogranin+, Synaptophysin+ (Neuroendocrine) Paraneoplastic Hypercalcemia (Squamous), HPOA SIADH, Ectopic ACTH, LEMS Treatment Surgery (early), TKI (mutation+), Chemo/Immuno Chemoradiation (Limited), Chemo (Extensive) Prognosis Stage I: 70-90%, Stage IV: 5-10% Limited: 15-20 mo, Extensive: 8-12 mo

docs.title: Adenocarcinoma vs Squamous Cell
docs.text: Feature Adenocarcinoma Squamous Cell Frequency MOST COMMON (40%) 2<sup>nd</sup> most common (25-30%) Location Peripheral Central Smoking Moderate (but most common in never-smokers) STRONGEST Demographics Women, non-smokers, younger Men, heavy smokers Histology Glandular, mucin Keratin pearls, intercellular bridges Cavitation Uncommon Common (25-30%) Molecular EGFR, ALK, ROS1 (targetable) Rare mutations Paraneoplastic HPOA, Trousseau syndrome Hypercalcemia (PTHrP), HPOA Chemotherapy Pemetrexed allowed Pemetrexed contraindicated

docs.title: High-Yield Drug Targets
docs.text: Mutation Frequency Target Therapy Notes EGFR (Exon 19 del, L858R) 15-30% (Asia 50%) Osimertinib (1<sup>st</sup>-line) Asian, non-smokers, women, adenocarcinoma ALK 3-5% Alectinib, Brigatinib Young, never-smokers, signet ring cells EGFR T790M (resistance) After 1<sup>st</sup>/2<sup>nd</sup>-gen TKI Osimertinib Resistance mutation PD-L1 ≥50% ~30% Pembrolizumab monotherapy Immunotherapy biomarker

docs.title: Important Syndromes
docs.text:
1. Pancoast Syndrome (Superior Sulcus Tumor): - Tumor location: Apex of lung - Features: Shoulder/arm pain (C8-T1), Horner syndrome, rib erosion - Treatment: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation → Surgery

2. SVC Syndrome: - Cause: Lung cancer (SCLC > NSCLC), lymphoma - Features: Facial/arm edema, distended neck veins, dyspnea - Emergency: Radiation (SCLC), Stent

3. Horner Syndrome: - Features: Ptosis (drooping eyelid), Miosis (constricted pupil), Anhidrosis (no sweating) - ipsilateral face - Cause: Sympathetic chain (C8-T2) involvement

4. Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS): - Tumor: SCLC (60%) - Mechanism: Anti-VGCC antibodies (presynaptic Ca²⁺ channels) - Features: Proximal weakness, IMPROVES with repetitive use, ↓ DTRs, autonomic dysfunction - Diagnosis: Anti-VGCC antibodies, Incremental response on EMG (↑ amplitude with rapid stimulation) - Treatment: Treat tumor, 3,4-DAP (3,4-Diaminopyridine), IVIG

docs.title: Staging High-Yield
docs.text:
TNM Shortcuts: - T1: ≤3 cm - T2: >3 to ≤5 cm - T3: >5 to ≤7 cm, OR same lobe nodules - T4: >7 cm, OR mediastinal invasion, OR different ipsilateral lobe nodules

N: - N1: Ipsilateral hilar - N2: Ipsilateral mediastinal - N3: Contralateral or supraclavicular

M1a: Contralateral lung, pleural effusion/nodules M1b: Single distant metastasis M1c: Multiple distant metastases

Resectable: Stage I-II, selected IIIA

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Using Pemetrexed in Squamous Cell Carcinoma ❌
  2. Pemetrexed is ONLY for non-squamous NSCLC

  3. Use Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine for squamous

  4. Surgery as first-line for SCLC ❌

  5. Chemoradiation is standard (surgery rarely indicated, only very early stage)

  6. Immunotherapy before TKI in EGFR/ALK+ patients ❌

  7. TKI first in driver mutation+ patients

  8. Immunotherapy after resistance

  9. Forgetting PCI in Limited-Stage SCLC ❌

  10. Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation reduces brain mets, improves survival

  11. Adenocarcinoma always in smokers ❌

  12. Adenocarcinoma is most common in never-smokers (though still smoking-related in many)

  13. All lung cancer patients get brain MRI ❌

  14. Brain MRI for SCLC (all) and Stage III/IV NSCLC
  15. NOT routinely for Stage I-II NSCLC

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "SALL" - Lung Cancer Types:

  2. S**mall Cell, **A**denocarcinoma, **L**arge Cell, squamous (**L)

  3. "PTHrP" - Squamous Cell Paraneoplastic:

  4. **P**arathyroid Hormone-related **P**eptide → Hypercalcemia

  5. Horner Syndrome - "PAM":

  6. **P**tosis, **A**nhidrosis, **M**iosis

  7. SCLC Paraneoplastic - "SAL":

  8. **S**IADH, **A**CTH (Cushing), **L**EMS

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 60-year-old male smoker (40 pack-years) presents with persistent cough, hemoptysis, and weight loss. CT chest shows a 5 cm central mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Biopsy shows small cells with scant cytoplasm and nuclear molding. Immunohistochemistry is positive for chromogranin and synaptophysin. What is the most appropriate next step in staging?

A. PET-CT only B. Brain MRI + CT abdomen C. Mediastinoscopy D. Bone scan

Answer: B. Brain MRI + CT abdomen

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) - Small cells, scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding - Chromogranin+ and Synaptophysin+ (neuroendocrine markers)

Staging SCLC: - Brain MRI (MANDATORY in all SCLC) - 40% have brain metastases at diagnosis - Asymptomatic brain mets common - CT Chest/Abdomen (detect liver, adrenal mets) - Bone scan or PET-CT (detect bone mets)

Limited vs Extensive Stage: - Limited: One hemithorax + regional nodes (fit in one radiation port) - Extensive: Beyond one hemithorax, distant mets

Why NOT Other Options?: - PET-CT only: Misses some brain mets (MRI more sensitive for brain) - Mediastinoscopy: Not needed for SCLC (rarely surgical, chemoradiation/chemo is treatment) - Bone scan: Less sensitive than PET-CT (but PET or bone scan can be done, but brain MRI is must)

Key Point: ALL SCLC patients get Brain MRI + CT Chest/Abdomen (or PET-CT) for staging

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 55-year-old non-smoker woman with adenocarcinoma of the lung (Stage IV) has EGFR exon 19 deletion on molecular testing. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

A. Cisplatin + Pemetrexed B. Pembrolizumab monotherapy C. Osimertinib D. Carboplatin + Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab

Answer: C. Osimertinib

Explanation: - Diagnosis: EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma - Exon 19 deletion (activating mutation, TKI-sensitive)

Treatment Paradigm: - EGFR-mutant (Exon 19 del or L858R) → Osimertinib (3rd-gen EGFR TKI) - FIRST-LINE - Alternatives: Erlotinib, Gefitinib (1st-gen), Afatinib (2nd-gen) - Osimertinib preferred: Better CNS penetration, overcomes T790M resistance

Response: - 60-80% response rate (vs 30-40% with chemotherapy) - Longer PFS (18-20 months vs 5-6 months with chemo)

Why NOT Other Options?: - Chemotherapy (A, D): NOT first-line if targetable mutation present - Use after TKI resistance - Pembrolizumab (B): Immunotherapy is LESS effective in EGFR+ patients - TKI first, immunotherapy after resistance

Key Point: EGFR-mutant NSCLC → Osimertinib (Targeted therapy, NOT chemotherapy or immunotherapy first)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with squamous cell carcinoma of the lung presents with serum calcium 13 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.5), phosphate 2.0 mg/dL, and PTH <10 pg/mL (low). What is the mechanism of hypercalcemia?

A. Bone metastases B. Ectopic PTH production C. PTH-related peptide (PTHrP) D. Vitamin D excess

Answer: C. PTH-related peptide (PTHrP)

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Humoral Hypercalcemia of Malignancy due to PTHrP

Mechanism: - Squamous Cell Carcinoma → Produces PTHrP - PTHrP mimics PTH action: - ↑ Bone resorption (osteoclast activation) - ↑ Renal calcium reabsorption - ↓ Renal phosphate reabsorption

Laboratory Findings: - ↑ Calcium (13 mg/dL) - ↓ Phosphate (PTHrP → Renal phosphate wasting) - ↓ PTH (Suppressed by hypercalcemia) - ↑ PTHrP (if measured)

Why NOT Other Options?: - Bone metastases (A): Would have ↑ calcium, but mechanism is local osteolysis (PTHrP is humoral/systemic) - Ectopic PTH (B): Very rare (PTHrP is more common) - Vitamin D excess (D): Would have ↑ calcium, ↑ phosphate, ↓ PTH (not seen here)

Treatment: - IV fluids (0.9% saline 200-300 mL/hr) - Bisphosphonates (Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV) - Calcitonin (rapid onset, short duration) - Treat tumor (most important)

Key Point: Squamous Cell Carcinoma + Hypercalcemia + ↓ Phosphate + ↓ PTH = PTHrP-mediated

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 65-year-old man with SCLC (Limited Stage) completes concurrent chemoradiation with good response. What is the most appropriate next step to improve survival?

A. Adjuvant chemotherapy B. Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI) C. Maintenance immunotherapy D. Observation

Answer: B. Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI)

Explanation: - Limited-Stage SCLC Treatment: 1. Concurrent chemoradiation (Cisplatin + Etoposide + Thoracic radiation) 2. If response → Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI)

PCI Rationale: - Brain is common metastatic site (40% eventually develop brain mets) - SCLC has high propensity for CNS spread - PCI reduces brain metastases by 50% - Improves 3-year survival from 15% → 21%

Dose: 25 Gy in 10 fractions

Side Effects: - Cognitive decline (especially in older patients) - Fatigue, alopecia

Controversial in Extensive Stage: PCI may ↓ brain mets but no clear survival benefit

Why NOT Other Options?: - Adjuvant chemotherapy (A): Chemo already given concurrently (4-6 cycles) - Maintenance immunotherapy (C): Under investigation, not standard (though Durvalumab shows promise) - Observation (D): Misses opportunity to prevent brain mets

Key Point: Limited-Stage SCLC + Good response to chemoradiation → PCI (Reduces brain mets, improves survival)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 50-year-old woman (never-smoker) has stage IV lung adenocarcinoma with PD-L1 expression of 60%. Molecular testing shows no EGFR, ALK, or ROS1 mutations. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

A. Cisplatin + Pemetrexed B. Pembrolizumab monotherapy C. Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy D. Osimertinib

Answer: B. Pembrolizumab monotherapy

Explanation: - PD-L1 ≥50% + No driver mutations (EGFR/ALK/ROS1 negative) → Pembrolizumab monotherapy

Evidence: - KEYNOTE-024 Trial: PD-L1 ≥50%, Pembrolizumab vs Chemotherapy - Higher response rate, Longer PFS, Better OS - Pembrolizumab monotherapy is superior to chemotherapy

Treatment Algorithm for Stage IV NSCLC: 1. Test for driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, etc.) - If positive → Targeted therapy (TKI) 2. If negative → Check PD-L1: - PD-L1 ≥50% → Pembrolizumab monotherapy - PD-L1 1-49% → Chemotherapy + Pembrolizumab - PD-L1 <1% → Chemotherapy ± Immunotherapy combinations

Why NOT Other Options?: - Chemotherapy alone (A): Inferior to Pembrolizumab monotherapy when PD-L1 ≥50% - Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy (C): No added benefit over pembrolizumab alone when PD-L1 ≥50% (but can be used) - Osimertinib (D): Only for EGFR-mutant patients (this patient is EGFR-negative)

Key Point: PD-L1 ≥50% + No driver mutations → Pembrolizumab monotherapy (First-line)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient with lung cancer presents with proximal muscle weakness that improves with repetitive use, decreased deep tendon reflexes, and dry mouth. EMG shows incremental response to rapid repetitive stimulation. What is the diagnosis and associated tumor type?

A. Myasthenia Gravis; Thymoma B. Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome; Small Cell Lung Cancer C. Dermatomyositis; Adenocarcinoma D. Guillain-Barré Syndrome; Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Answer: B. Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome; Small Cell Lung Cancer

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS)

Clinical Features: - Proximal muscle weakness (legs > arms) - IMPROVES with repetitive use/exercise (KEY DIFFERENTIATOR) - ↓ Deep tendon reflexes - Autonomic dysfunction: Dry mouth, erectile dysfunction, constipation

EMG Findings: - Incremental (facilitated) response: ↑ Amplitude with rapid repetitive stimulation (>10 Hz) - Opposite of Myasthenia Gravis (decremental response)

Mechanism: - Autoantibodies to presynaptic Voltage-Gated Calcium Channels (VGCC) - ↓ ACh release at NMJ - Repetitive use → ↑ Intracellular Ca²⁺ → Partially compensates

Associated Tumor: - Small Cell Lung Cancer (60% of LEMS cases) - Other: Lymphoma, ovarian cancer

Diagnosis: - Anti-VGCC antibodies (P/Q-type calcium channels) - EMG: Incremental response

Treatment: - Treat underlying tumor (most important) - 3,4-Diaminopyridine (3,4-DAP): ↑ ACh release (blocks K+ channels) - IVIG, Plasmapheresis (if severe) - Pyridostigmine (AChE inhibitor, less effective than in MG)

Comparison with Myasthenia Gravis:

Feature LEMS Myasthenia Gravis Muscle weakness Proximal (legs > arms) Ocular, bulbar, then limb Effect of use IMPROVES WORSENS DTRs Decreased Normal Autonomic Yes (dry mouth, etc.) No Antibodies Anti-VGCC Anti-AChR, Anti-MuSK EMG Incremental response Decremental response Associated tumor SCLC Thymoma Edrophonium test Minimal response Positive

Key Point: Weakness that IMPROVES with use + SCLC = Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
What is the most common cause of SVC (Superior Vena Cava) syndrome?

A. Lung cancer B. Lymphoma C. Mediastinal fibrosis D. Thrombosis from central venous catheter

Answer: A. Lung cancer

Explanation: - Most common cause of SVC syndrome: Lung cancer (60-80%) - SCLC > NSCLC (SCLC is central, rapid growth) - Right-sided tumors more common (SVC on right side)

Other Causes: - Lymphoma (10-15%) - 2nd most common - Metastatic cancer - Mediastinal fibrosis (histoplasmosis, radiation) - Thrombosis (central lines, pacemakers)

Clinical Features: - Facial and upper extremity edema (bilateral) - Distended neck and chest wall veins (collaterals) - Dyspnea, cough - Facial plethora, cyanosis - Headache (worse when bending forward, lying down)

Diagnosis: - CT Chest with Contrast: Identifies obstruction, underlying cause - Tissue diagnosis (biopsy) before treatment (UNLESS life-threatening)

Treatment:

Cause Treatment SCLC Chemotherapy ± Radiation (Very responsive, rapid relief in days) NSCLC Radiation therapy, Stent (palliative) Lymphoma Chemotherapy (Very responsive) Thrombosis Anticoagulation, Thrombolysis, Stent

Emergency Management (Life-threatening symptoms: stridor, altered mental status): - Head elevation - Oxygen - Corticosteroids (↓ edema) - Radiation (if malignancy) - Endovascular stent (immediate relief)

Key Point: Lung cancer (especially SCLC) is the most common cause of SVC syndrome

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 70-year-old smoker undergoes Low-Dose CT screening and is found to have a 1.2 cm solid pulmonary nodule in the right upper lobe with spiculated margins. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Repeat CT in 12 months B. PET-CT scan C. CT-guided biopsy D. Observation

Answer: B. PET-CT scan

Explanation: - Solid nodule >8-10 mm with spiculated margins → High suspicion for malignancy

Features Suggesting Malignancy: - Size >8-10 mm ✓ (1.2 cm = 12 mm) - Spiculated (irregular) margins ✓ (corona radiata sign) - Upper lobe location - Solid (vs ground-glass) - Smoking history ✓

Management Approach:

Nodule Size Risk Features Management <6 mm Low risk No routine follow-up (unless high-risk patient) 6-8 mm Low risk CT at 6-12 months, then annually >8 mm High risk (spiculated, smoker, upper lobe) PET-CT or Biopsy

PET-CT (in this case): - Differentiate benign vs malignant (FDG-avid = malignant) - Staging (if malignant, detect metastases)

Next Steps: - PET-positive → Biopsy (tissue diagnosis) → Staging → Treatment - PET-negative → Follow-up CT (may still be early cancer or lepidic adenocarcinoma)

Why NOT Other Options?: - Repeat CT in 12 months (A): Too long a delay for high-risk nodule - CT-guided biopsy (C): Can be done, but PET-CT helps assess malignant probability and stage before invasive procedure - Observation (D): Inappropriate for high-risk nodule

Key Point: Solid nodule >8 mm with spiculated margins → PET-CT (assess malignancy, stage) → Biopsy

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Which chemotherapy agent should NOT be used in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung?

A. Cisplatin B. Carboplatin C. Pemetrexed D. Paclitaxel

Answer: C. Pemetrexed

Explanation: - Pemetrexed is CONTRAINDICATED in Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Mechanism: - Pemetrexed: Antifolate agent (inhibits thymidylate synthase, DHFR, GAART) - Squamous cells have HIGH thymidylate synthase expression → Resistant to pemetrexed

Clinical Trial Evidence: - Non-squamous NSCLC: Pemetrexed shows benefit - Squamous NSCLC: Pemetrexed shows NO benefit and may be inferior

Chemotherapy Regimens:

Histology Preferred Regimen Non-squamous (Adenocarcinoma, Large Cell) Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Pemetrexed Squamous Cell Cisplatin/Carboplatin + Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine

Maintenance Therapy: - Non-squamous: Pemetrexed maintenance (after induction) - Squamous: NO pemetrexed (use other agents or observation)

Why Other Options Are Used: - Cisplatin/Carboplatin (A, B): Platinum backbone for BOTH squamous and non-squamous - Paclitaxel (D): Used in squamous cell carcinoma (with platinum)

Key Point: DO NOT use Pemetrexed in Squamous Cell Carcinoma (ineffective, contraindicated)

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 62-year-old man with stage IA (T1bN0M0) adenocarcinoma of the lung undergoes lobectomy with complete resection. Tumor size is 1.8 cm. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Adjuvant chemotherapy B. Adjuvant radiation C. Adjuvant immunotherapy D. Observation

Answer: D. Observation

Explanation: - Stage IA1-IA3 (T1a-c N0 M0, tumor ≤3 cm) → Observation after complete resection

Adjuvant Chemotherapy Indications (NSCLC): - Stage IB (>4 cm tumor) - Stage II (T3 N0 or any T N1) - Stage III (any T N2)

This Patient: - Stage IA2 (T1b: >1 to ≤2 cm, N0, M0) - Tumor 1.8 cm (<2 cm, <4 cm) - NO adjuvant chemotherapy indicated

Adjuvant Therapy Summary:

Stage Tumor Size Adjuvant Therapy IA ≤3 cm, N0 Observation IB >3-4 cm, N0 Observation OR Chemotherapy (controversial) IB >4 cm, N0 Adjuvant Chemotherapy (4 cycles platinum doublet) II-IIIA Any size, N1-2 Adjuvant Chemotherapy (± Immunotherapy if N1-2)

Adjuvant Radiation: Generally NOT recommended after complete resection (except N2 disease, incomplete resection)

Adjuvant Immunotherapy (Atezolizumab): Approved for Stage II-IIIA with PD-L1 ≥1% (after chemotherapy)

Key Point: Stage IA NSCLC (≤3 cm, N0) after complete resection → Observation (NO adjuvant therapy)

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
Lung Cancer is the leading cause of cancer death worldwide: - Most common type: Adenocarcinoma (40%, peripheral, non-smokers) - Strongest smoking association: Squamous and SCLC - Worst prognosis: SCLC (rapid growth, early metastasis)

Diagnosis: - HRCT → Biopsy (bronchoscopy/CT-guided) → Staging (PET-CT, Brain MRI for SCLC/advanced NSCLC) - Molecular testing (NSCLC): EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1

Treatment: - NSCLC: - Early (I-II): Surgery ± Adjuvant chemo - Locally Advanced (III): Chemoradiation → Durvalumab - Metastatic (IV): - Driver mutation+ → Targeted therapy (EGFR: Osimertinib, ALK: Alectinib) - PD-L1 ≥50% → Pembrolizumab - Otherwise → Chemo ± Immunotherapy - SCLC: - Limited: Chemoradiation → PCI - Extensive: Chemotherapy

Paraneoplastic Syndromes: - Squamous: Hypercalcemia (PTHrP) - SCLC: SIADH, Ectopic ACTH, LEMS

Screening: Low-Dose CT for age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, current/quit within 15 years

END OF CHAPTER

Next Topic: Pleural Effusion (Medicine/18_Pleural_Effusion.md)

Cross-References: - COPD (Medicine/12_COPD.md) - Tuberculosis (Medicine/14_Tuberculosis.md) - Bronchiectasis (Medicine/16_Bronchiectasis.md)

docs.title: PLEURAL EFFUSION
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (⅘ - High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 5-6 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Definition & Anatomy
  2. Classification
  3. Etiology
  4. Pathophysiology
  5. Clinical Features
  6. Diagnosis
  7. Specific Causes
  8. Management
  9. Complications
  10. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  11. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Pleural Effusion: Abnormal accumulation of fluid in the pleural space (between visceral and parietal pleura)

docs.title: Normal Pleural Space
docs.text:
Anatomy: - Parietal pleura: Lines chest wall, diaphragm, mediastinum - Visceral pleura: Covers lung surface - Pleural space: Potential space (normally 10-20 μm)

Normal Pleural Fluid: - Volume: 0.1-0.2 mL/kg (~10-20 mL total) - Production: Parietal pleura (systemic circulation) - Absorption: Visceral pleura (pulmonary circulation) + Lymphatics - Protein: <1.5 g/dL - pH: 7.60-7.64 - Glucose: Equal to serum - LDH: <50% of serum - WBC: <1000/μL (mostly mesothelial cells, macrophages)

docs.title: Starling Forces
docs.text:
FLUID PRODUCTION (Parietal Pleura)
    ↓
Capillary hydrostatic pressure (30 mmHg)
+ Pleural space oncotic pressure (6 mmHg)
MINUS
Capillary oncotic pressure (34 mmHg)
+ Pleural space hydrostatic pressure (-5 mmHg)
    ↓
NET FILTRATION = +7 mmHg → Fluid enters pleural space
    ↓
FLUID ABSORPTION (Visceral Pleura + Lymphatics)
    ↓
BALANCE: 10-20 mL maintained

Lymphatic Drainage: ~500 mL/day capacity (can increase 10-20x)

docs.title: By Mechanism
docs.text:
PLEURAL EFFUSION
│
├─ TRANSUDATIVE (↑ Hydrostatic or ↓ Oncotic pressure)
│  • CHF (MOST COMMON)
│  • Cirrhosis
│  • Nephrotic syndrome
│  • Hypoalbuminemia
│  • Peritoneal dialysis
│
└─ EXUDATIVE (↑ Capillary permeability or ↓ Lymphatic drainage)
   • Infection (Parapneumonic, TB, Empyema)
   • Malignancy
   • Pulmonary embolism
   • Connective tissue disease
   • Pancreatitis
   • Post-CABG

docs.title: Mnemonic: "CHF NEPAL" for Transudative Causes
docs.text:
• C - CHF (Congestive Heart Failure)
  • H - Hepatic cirrhosis
  • F - Failure (renal - Nephrotic syndrome)
  • N - Nephrotic syndrome
  • E - starvation (hypoalbuminEmic)
  • P - Peritoneal dialysis
  • A - Atelectasis
  • L - constrictive pericarditis (Less common)

docs.title: Mnemonic: "CITE MePPP" for Exudative Causes
docs.text:
• C - Cancer/malignancy
  • I - Infection (parapneumonic, TB, empyema)
  • T - Trauma (hemothorax)
  • E - Embolism (PE)
  • Me - Mesothelioma
  • P - Pancreatitis
  • P - Post-CABG syndrome
  • P - autoimmune (RA, SLE - "rheumatic Pleural")

docs.title: Frequency of Causes
docs.text: Cause Frequency (%) Type Congestive Heart Failure 40% - MOST COMMON Transudate Pneumonia (Parapneumonic) 20-25% Exudate Malignancy 15% Exudate Pulmonary Embolism 10% Exudate (75%) or Transudate Cirrhosis 5% Transudate Tuberculosis 3-5% (Higher in endemic areas) Exudate Other <5% Variable

docs.title: 1. Congestive Heart Failure (CHF)
docs.text:
• MOST COMMON cause overall
  • Bilateral (70%), Right > Left (if unilateral)
  • ↑ Hydrostatic pressure (pulmonary venous congestion)

docs.title: 2. Hepatic Cirrhosis
docs.text:
• Right-sided effusion (70%)
  • Ascites usually present
  • Mechanism: ↓ Oncotic pressure + ascitic fluid movement through diaphragm

docs.title: 3. Nephrotic Syndrome
docs.text:
• Bilateral
  • ↓ Oncotic pressure (hypoalbuminemia)

docs.title: 4. Hypoalbuminemia
docs.text:
• Malnutrition, protein-losing enteropathy
  • ↓ Oncotic pressure

docs.title: 1. Parapneumonic Effusion
docs.text:
• Most common exudative cause
  • Accompanies bacterial pneumonia (40-60% of pneumonias)
  • Can be uncomplicated or complicated/empyema

docs.title: 2. Malignancy
docs.text:
• Lung cancer (35%) - MOST COMMON
  • Breast cancer (25%)
  • Lymphoma (10%)
  • Ovarian cancer (5%)
  • Mesothelioma

docs.title: 3. Tuberculosis
docs.text:
• Most common cause in developing countries
  • Lymphocytic predominant
  • High ADA levels

docs.title: 4. Pulmonary Embolism
docs.text:
• 30-50% of PE patients have effusion
  • Usually small, unilateral
  • Exudate (75%) or Transudate (25%)

docs.title: 5. Connective Tissue Diseases
docs.text:
• Rheumatoid Arthritis (5% of RA patients)
  • Low glucose (<30 mg/dL)
  • Low pH (<7.20)
  • SLE (30-50% of SLE patients)
  • Sjögren's syndrome

docs.title: 6. Pancreatitis
docs.text:
• Left-sided effusion (70%)
  • High amylase in pleural fluid

docs.title: 7. Post-Cardiac Injury Syndrome
docs.text:
• Post-CABG, post-MI (Dressler syndrome)
  • Weeks to months after cardiac surgery/MI

docs.title: Mechanisms of Effusion Formation
docs.text: Mechanism Example ↑ Hydrostatic pressure CHF, SVC obstruction ↓ Oncotic pressure Hypoalbuminemia (cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition) ↑ Capillary permeability Infection, inflammation, malignancy ↓ Lymphatic drainage Malignancy (lymphatic obstruction), lymphangitis carcinomatosa Movement from peritoneum Cirrhosis (transdiaphragmatic), pancreatitis Rupture of vascular structure Hemothorax (trauma, aortic dissection) Decreased pleural pressure Atelectasis (lung collapse → ↓ pressure → fluid accumulation)

docs.title: Parapneumonic Effusion Progression
docs.text:
BACTERIAL PNEUMONIA
    ↓
STAGE 1: EXUDATIVE (0-3 days)
• Sterile fluid, ↑ capillary permeability
• Clear, free-flowing
• pH >7.20, Glucose normal, LDH <1000
    ↓
STAGE 2: FIBRINOPURULENT (3-7 days)
• Bacterial invasion
• Turbid, fibrin deposition, loculations
• pH <7.20, Glucose <60 mg/dL, LDH >1000
• Gram stain/culture may be positive
    ↓
STAGE 3: ORGANIZING (7-28 days)
• Thick peel formation (fibrous)
• Trapped lung, empyema
• Requires drainage ± decortication

docs.title: Small Effusion (<300 mL)
docs.text:
• Often asymptomatic
  • Detected incidentally on imaging

docs.title: Moderate-Large Effusion (>500 mL)
docs.text:
1. Dyspnea (MOST COMMON)
  2. Exertional → Progressive to rest

  3. Severity correlates with effusion size

  4. Pleuritic Chest Pain

  5. Sharp, worse with inspiration/cough

  6. Suggests pleural inflammation (infection, PE, malignancy)

  7. Dry Cough

  8. Non-productive

  9. Due to bronchial compression

  10. Orthopnea (if massive effusion)

docs.title: Systemic Symptoms (Suggest Underlying Cause)
docs.text:
• Fever: Infection (parapneumonic, TB, empyema)
  • Weight loss: Malignancy, TB
  • Night sweats: TB, lymphoma
  • Chest trauma: Hemothorax
  • Abdominal pain: Pancreatitis, subphrenic abscess

docs.title: Classical Triad of Pleural Effusion
docs.text:
1. Dullness to Percussion
  2. MOST SENSITIVE sign
  3. Stony dull

  4. Requires >300-500 mL

  5. Decreased Breath Sounds

  6. Over effusion area

  7. Requires >300-500 mL

  8. Decreased Tactile Fremitus

  9. Fluid dampens vibrations

docs.title: Additional Signs
docs.text: Sign Description Aegophony (E→A change) Above effusion level (compressed lung) Tracheal shift Away from effusion (if massive, >1000 mL) Decreased chest expansion Ipsilateral side Ellis-Damoiseau sign Curved upper border of dullness (meniscus)

docs.title: Signs of Underlying Cause
docs.text:
• CHF: JVP elevation, S3 gallop, pedal edema, hepatomegaly
  • Cirrhosis: Ascites, jaundice, spider angiomas, palmar erythema
  • Malignancy: Cachexia, lymphadenopathy, clubbing
  • Empyema: Fever, toxicity

docs.title: Diagnostic Approach
docs.text:
CLINICAL SUSPICION (Dyspnea, Dullness, ↓ Breath sounds)
    ↓
CHEST X-RAY (Confirm effusion)
    ↓
THORACENTESIS (Diagnostic ± Therapeutic)
    ↓
PLEURAL FLUID ANALYSIS
    ↓
Light's Criteria (Transudate vs Exudate)
    ↓
FURTHER TESTS (Based on exudate type)
• Cell count & differential
• Glucose, pH, LDH, ADA
• Gram stain, Culture (aerobic/anaerobic/AFB)
• Cytology (if malignancy suspected)
    ↓
IMAGING (If diagnosis unclear or effusion persists)
• Ultrasound (loculations, septations)
• CT Chest (underlying lung pathology)
    ↓
PLEURAL BIOPSY (If TB or malignancy suspected, fluid non-diagnostic)

docs.title: 1. Chest X-Ray
docs.text:
Findings:

Volume CXR Finding <50 mL Not visible on standard PA view 50-175 mL Blunting of costophrenic angle on lateral view 175-500 mL Blunting of costophrenic angle on PA view >500 mL Meniscus sign, homogeneous opacity >1000 mL Complete opacification of hemithorax, mediastinal shift away

Views: - PA & Lateral: Standard - Lateral Decubitus: Detects small effusions, confirms free-flowing vs loculated - Fluid layers out if free-flowing (≥10 mm layer confirms drainable effusion)

Classic Appearance: - Meniscus sign (Ellis line): Curved upper border (concave upward) - Homogeneous opacity obscuring costophrenic angle

Subpulmonic Effusion: Fluid under lung base, mimics elevated hemidiaphragm

docs.title: 2. Ultrasound
docs.text:
Advantages: - More sensitive than CXR (detects 5-50 mL) - Differentiates fluid vs consolidation - Detects loculations, septations, pleural thickening - Guides thoracentesis (safer, higher yield)

Findings: - Anechoic (simple transudate) - Echogenic/complex: Septations (exudate, empyema, hemothorax)

docs.title: 3. CT Chest (Contrast-Enhanced)
docs.text:
Indications: - Persistent/recurrent effusion despite treatment - Suspected malignancy (evaluate lung parenchyma, pleural nodules, lymph nodes) - Empyema (loculations, pleural thickening) - Hemothorax (high attenuation) - Differentiate pleural vs parenchymal disease

Findings: - Pleural thickening/nodularity: Malignancy, empyema - Pleural enhancement (split pleura sign): Empyema (contrast-enhanced parietal + visceral pleura separated by fluid) - Loculations: Organized effusion - Underlying lung lesion: Primary tumor, pneumonia

docs.title: 4. Thoracentesis
docs.text: ESSENTIAL for diagnosis (except clear CHF responding to diuretics)

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
Diagnostic: - All new effusions of unknown etiology - Effusion ≥10 mm on lateral decubitus or ultrasound

Therapeutic: - Large effusion causing dyspnea

docs.title: Contraindications (Relative)
docs.text:
• Coagulopathy: INR >1.5, Platelets <50,000/μL
  • Correct before procedure OR use ultrasound guidance (safer)
  • Small effusion (<10 mm)
  • Mechanical ventilation (↑ pneumothorax risk)

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
Site: 5th-6th intercostal space, midaxillary or posterior axillary line - Just above the rib (neurovascular bundle runs below rib)

Technique: - Ultrasound-guided (preferred, ↓ complications) - Aspiration: 1-1.5 L maximum (↓ re-expansion pulmonary edema risk)

Complications: - Pneumothorax (5-10%, <2% need chest tube) - MOST COMMON - Hemothorax, infection - Re-expansion pulmonary edema (if >1.5 L removed rapidly) - Vasovagal reaction

docs.title: Gross Appearance
docs.text:
Appearance Causes Clear, straw-colored Transudate, early exudate Turbid/cloudy Exudate (high cell/protein), empyema, chylothorax Bloody Malignancy, PE, trauma (hemothorax), TB Purulent Empyema (pus) Milky Chylothorax (lymphatic leak, triglycerides >110 mg/dL) Food particles Esophageal rupture Foul odor Anaerobic empyema

Bloody Effusion (Differentiate from Traumatic Tap): - Hematocrit: If pleural fluid Hct/blood Hct >50% → Hemothorax - Traumatic tap: Blood clots, clears with continued aspiration

docs.title: Light's Criteria (Transudate vs Exudate)
docs.text:
GOLD STANDARD

Exudate if ANY ONE of the following:

Criterion Cutoff Pleural protein / Serum protein >0.5 Pleural LDH / Serum LDH >0.6 Pleural LDH >⅔ upper limit of normal serum LDH

Transudate: NONE of the above criteria met

Sensitivity: 98% (rarely misses exudate) Specificity: 80% (25% of transudates misclassified as exudates, especially CHF on diuretics)

Mnemonic: "PL-PL-PL" (Protein-LDH-LDH, 0.5-0.6-⅔)

docs.title: Additional Tests
docs.text: Test Transudate Exudate Significance Protein <3 g/dL >3 g/dL Light's criteria LDH Low (<200 U/L) High (>200 U/L) Light's criteria Glucose Normal (>60 mg/dL) <60 mg/dL Complicated parapneumonic, empyema, RA, malignancy, TB pH >7.40 <7.30 Complicated parapneumonic, empyema, esophageal rupture Cell count <1000/μL >1000/μL Inflammatory/infectious RBC <10,000/μL >10,000/μL (bloody) Malignancy, PE, trauma, TB WBC differential - PMN >50%: Acute (parapneumonic, PE), Lymphocytes >50%: Chronic (TB, malignancy) Amylase Normal Elevated Pancreatitis (>serum), esophageal rupture Triglycerides <50 mg/dL >110 mg/dL Chylothorax (milky fluid) ADA (Adenosine Deaminase) <40 U/L >40 U/L TB (Sensitivity 90%, Specificity 85% in endemic areas) Cholesterol <45 mg/dL >45 mg/dL Exudate (less specific than Light's)

docs.title: Microbiological Tests
docs.text: Test Indication Yield Gram stain Suspected infection 40-60% (empyema) Bacterial culture (aerobic + anaerobic) Suspected infection 60% (parapneumonic), 20% (empyema with antibiotics) AFB smear Suspected TB <10% (LOW yield) Mycobacterial culture Suspected TB 20-40% GeneXpert MTB/RIF Suspected TB 40-50% (better than smear/culture) Fungal culture Immunocompromised Low yield

docs.title: Cytology
docs.text:
Indication: Suspected malignancy

Yield: - 60% sensitivity (1st sample) - 80% (2nd sample) - 90% (3rd sample)

If negative → Pleural biopsy (VATS or image-guided)

docs.title: 6. Pleural Biopsy
docs.text:
Indications: - Suspected TB (fluid AFB negative) - Suspected malignancy (fluid cytology negative) - Undiagnosed exudative effusion

Methods:

Method Yield (TB) Yield (Malignancy) Closed pleural biopsy (Abrams/Cope needle) 60-70% 40-50% Image-guided biopsy (CT/US) - 80-90% (if pleural nodules visible) Thoracoscopy (VATS) 90-95% 90-95% (GOLD STANDARD)

Advantages of Thoracoscopy: - Direct visualization - Targeted biopsy - Therapeutic (pleurodesis, drainage)

docs.title: 1. Parapneumonic Effusion & Empyema
docs.text: Definitions: - Parapneumonic effusion: Any effusion associated with pneumonia - Uncomplicated parapneumonic: Sterile, will resolve with antibiotics alone - Complicated parapneumonic: Bacterial invasion, requires drainage - Empyema: Pus in pleural space (purulent fluid, positive Gram stain/culture)

docs.title: Classification (Based on Pleural Fluid)
docs.text: Type pH Glucose LDH Gram Stain/Culture Treatment Uncomplicated >7.20 >60 mg/dL <1000 U/L Negative Antibiotics alone Complicated <7.20 <60 mg/dL >1000 U/L Negative or Positive Antibiotics + Chest tube drainage Empyema <7.00 <40 mg/dL >1000 U/L Positive (or frank pus) Antibiotics + Chest tube ± Surgery

docs.title: Indications for Chest Tube Drainage
docs.text:
Absolute: - Frank pus (empyema) - Positive Gram stain or culture

Relative (Complicated parapneumonic): - pH <7.20 - Glucose <60 mg/dL - LDH >1000 U/L - Loculated effusion

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Antibiotics: - Cover common organisms: S. pneumoniae, S. aureus, K. pneumoniae, anaerobes - Regimen: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole OR Pip-Tazo - Duration: 2-4 weeks (longer for empyema)

Drainage: - Chest tube (24-28 Fr) + Suction - Fibrinolytics (tPA + DNase) - For loculated empyema - Improves drainage, ↓ need for surgery - Regimen: tPA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg via chest tube BID × 3 days

Surgery (If medical management fails): - VATS decortication: Remove fibrous peel - Open thoracotomy: If VATS not feasible

docs.title: 2. Tuberculous Pleural Effusion
docs.text: Characteristics: - Most common in young adults (20-40 years) - Unilateral (95%) - Exudate - Lymphocytic predominant (>50%, often >80%) - Pleural fluid AFB smear: <10% positive (LOW yield) - Pleural fluid culture: 20-40% positive (slow, 6-8 weeks)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
High-Yield Tests:

Test Sensitivity Specificity ADA (Adenosine Deaminase) >40 U/L 90% Pleural biopsy (Histology: Granulomas + AFB culture) 90% 95% (BEST) GeneXpert MTB/RIF (Pleural fluid) 40-50% 98% Pleural fluid AFB smear <10% 98%

ADA >40 U/L: Highly suggestive in TB-endemic areas

False Positives for ADA: Empyema, lymphoma, RA (but usually ADA <100)

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Same as pulmonary TB: - 2HRZE / 4HR (6 months total) - Steroids: Controversial, may ↓ residual pleural thickening - Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day × 4-6 weeks, then taper

Prognosis: Excellent with treatment, but 10% develop active TB later

docs.title: 3. Malignant Pleural Effusion
docs.text:
Causes: 1. Lung cancer (35%) - MOST COMMON 2. Breast cancer (25%) 3. Lymphoma (10%) 4. Ovarian cancer (5%) 5. Mesothelioma (rare, but always malignant)

Characteristics: - Exudate - Bloody (50%) - Large volume, recurrent - Low glucose (<60 mg/dL) in 30%

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Pleural fluid cytology: - 60% (1st tap), 80% (2nd), 90% (3rd)

If negative: - Pleural biopsy (image-guided if nodules, or VATS)

Tumor markers (Low sensitivity, NOT diagnostic): - CEA, CA 15-3, CA 125

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Goals: Symptom relief (dyspnea), prevent recurrence

Options:

Method Indication Success Rate Therapeutic thoracentesis Symptomatic relief Temporary (recurs in days-weeks) Tunneled pleural catheter (IPC) Recurrent effusion, trapped lung, poor prognosis 70-90% symptom control Pleurodesis Recurrent effusion, good lung expansion Talc: 90%, Doxycycline/Bleomycin: 60-70% Systemic chemotherapy Chemosensitive tumors (lymphoma, SCLC, breast) Variable

Pleurodesis: - Talc (powder or slurry) - MOST EFFECTIVE - Induces pleural inflammation → Adhesions between visceral and parietal pleura - Contraindication: Trapped lung (lung cannot expand)

Prognosis: Median survival 3-12 months (depends on primary tumor)

docs.title: 4. Chylothorax
docs.text:
Definition: Accumulation of lymphatic fluid (chyle) in pleural space

Causes: - Trauma/Surgery (thoracic duct injury): Esophagectomy, cardiac surgery - Malignancy: Lymphoma (MOST COMMON non-traumatic cause) - Congenital (lymphangiomatosis)

Characteristics: - Milky/opalescent appearance - Triglycerides >110 mg/dL (DIAGNOSTIC) - Chylomicrons present (lipoprotein electrophoresis) - Lymphocyte-predominant

Pseudochylothorax: Chronic effusion with high cholesterol (shimmering, not milky), low triglycerides

Treatment: - Conservative (50% traumatic cases resolve): - Low-fat diet or NPO + TPN (↓ chyle production) - Chest tube drainage - Surgical (if persistent >2 weeks): - Thoracic duct ligation - Pleuroperitoneal shunt

docs.title: 5. Hemothorax
docs.text:
Definition: Blood in pleural space (Pleural fluid Hct / Blood Hct >50%)

Causes: - Trauma (blunt/penetrating) - MOST COMMON - Iatrogenic (central line, thoracentesis) - Aortic dissection - Anticoagulation - Malignancy

Treatment: - Chest tube drainage (32-36 Fr, large bore) - Remove blood, ↓ infection risk, monitor ongoing bleeding - Surgery (VATS or thoracotomy) if: - Initial output >1500 mL - Ongoing bleeding >200 mL/hr × 2-4 hours - Retained hemothorax (organize clot → empyema risk)

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text: Goals: 1. Treat underlying cause 2. Relieve symptoms (dyspnea) 3. Prevent complications (empyema, trapped lung)

docs.title: Management by Cause
docs.text: Cause Management CHF Diuretics (Furosemide), afterload reduction (ACEi, ARB), thoracentesis if severe dyspnea Parapneumonic (Uncomplicated) Antibiotics alone Parapneumonic (Complicated) / Empyema Antibiotics + Chest tube ± Fibrinolytics ± Surgery Tuberculosis Anti-TB therapy (2HRZE/4HR) ± Steroids Malignancy Pleurodesis (Talc) OR Tunneled catheter (IPC), chemotherapy Cirrhosis Diuretics (Spironolactone + Furosemide), salt restriction, consider TIPS if refractory Pulmonary Embolism Anticoagulation (effusion usually resolves) Chylothorax Low-fat diet / TPN, chest tube, surgery if persistent Hemothorax Chest tube, surgery if ongoing bleeding

docs.title: Chest Tube Insertion
docs.text:
Indications: - Empyema (frank pus, positive culture) - Complicated parapneumonic effusion (pH <7.20, Glucose <60, LDH >1000) - Hemothorax - Large recurrent effusion (post-pleurodesis)

Site: 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line (triangle of safety)

Size: - Small bore (10-14 Fr): Simple effusion - Large bore (28-32 Fr): Empyema, hemothorax

Removal: When drainage <100-150 mL/day

docs.title: Pleurodesis
docs.text:
Indications: - Recurrent malignant effusion - Recurrent pneumothorax

Contraindications: - Trapped lung (lung cannot expand → pleurodesis will fail) - Life expectancy <3 months (IPC preferred)

Agents: - Talc (powder or slurry) - MOST EFFECTIVE (90% success) - Doxycycline, Bleomycin (60-70% success)

Procedure: - Chest tube drainage until <100-150 mL/day - Instill sclerosant via chest tube - Clamp tube × 2-4 hours (patient changes position to distribute) - Resume drainage

Complications: Pain, fever, ARDS (rare, with talc)

docs.title: 1. Trapped Lung
docs.text:
Definition: Lung unable to expand fully due to fibrous peel on visceral pleura

Causes: - Chronic effusion (malignant, TB, hemothorax) - Empyema

Diagnosis: - Persistent pneumothorax after thoracentesis - Non-expandable lung on imaging

Management: - Tunneled pleural catheter (IPC) - Palliative drainage - Decortication (surgical removal of peel) - If symptomatic and good prognosis

docs.title: 2. Empyema
docs.text: See above (Parapneumonic effusion section)

docs.title: 3. Fibrothorax
docs.text:
Chronic fibrous thickening of pleura → Restrictive lung disease

Causes: Hemothorax, empyema, TB

Management: Decortication (if symptomatic)

docs.title: 4. Re-expansion Pulmonary Edema
docs.text:
Mechanism: Rapid removal of large effusion (>1.5 L) → Capillary leak in re-expanded lung

Presentation: Dyspnea, cough, hypoxemia, unilateral infiltrates (post-thoracentesis)

Prevention: Limit drainage to 1-1.5 L per session

Treatment: Supportive (oxygen, diuretics if needed)

docs.title: Must-Know Facts
docs.text:
1. Most Common Cause of Pleural Effusion: CHF (40%)
  2. Bilateral, Right > Left if unilateral

  3. Transudate

  4. Most Common Exudative Cause: Parapneumonic effusion (20-25%)

  5. Light's Criteria (Exudate if ANY ONE):

  6. Pleural protein / Serum protein >0.5
  7. Pleural LDH / Serum LDH >0.6
  8. Pleural LDH >⅔ upper limit of normal serum LDH

  9. Mnemonic: "0.5 - 0.6 - ⅔"

  10. Physical Exam Triad:

  11. Dullness to percussion (MOST sensitive)
  12. Decreased breath sounds
  13. Decreased tactile fremitus

  14. Requires >300-500 mL

  15. CXR Findings:

  16. 175-500 mL: Blunting of costophrenic angle (PA view)
  17. >500 mL: Meniscus sign

  18. Lateral decubitus: Most sensitive (detects ≥10 mm layering)

  19. Complicated Parapneumonic / Empyema Indicators:

  20. pH <7.20 (MOST IMPORTANT)
  21. Glucose <60 mg/dL
  22. LDH >1000 U/L
  23. Positive Gram stain/culture OR Frank pus

  24. Treatment: Antibiotics + Chest tube drainage

  25. TB Pleural Effusion:

  26. Lymphocytic (>50%)
  27. ADA >40 U/L (90% sensitivity)
  28. Pleural biopsy: BEST test (90% sensitivity) - Granulomas + AFB culture

  29. AFB smear of fluid: <10% yield (LOW)

  30. Low Glucose in Pleural Fluid (<60 mg/dL):

  31. Complicated parapneumonic / Empyema
  32. Rheumatoid arthritis (very low, <30)
  33. Malignancy
  34. TB

  35. Mnemonic: "CREMT" - Complicated parapneumonic, RA, Empyema, Malignancy, TB

  36. High Amylase in Pleural Fluid:

  37. Pancreatitis (LEFT-sided effusion, 70%)

  38. Esophageal rupture

  39. Chylothorax:

    • Milky appearance
    • Triglycerides >110 mg/dL (DIAGNOSTIC)
    • Causes: Trauma (thoracic duct injury), Lymphoma (most common non-traumatic)

  40. Malignant Effusion:

    • Lung cancer (35%) - Most common cause
    • Bloody (50%)
    • Cytology: 60% (1st), 80% (2nd), 90% (3rd)
    • Treatment: Talc pleurodesis (90% success) OR Tunneled catheter (IPC)

  41. Hemothorax:

    • Pleural fluid Hct / Blood Hct >50%
    • Chest tube (large bore, 32-36 Fr)
    • Surgery if initial >1500 mL or ongoing >200 mL/hr

docs.title: Transudate vs Exudate
docs.text: Parameter Transudate Exudate Mechanism ↑ Hydrostatic or ↓ Oncotic ↑ Permeability or ↓ Lymphatic drainage Common causes CHF, Cirrhosis, Nephrotic Pneumonia, Malignancy, TB, PE Protein (P/S) ≤0.5 >0.5 LDH (P/S) ≤0.6 >0.6 Pleural LDH <⅔ ULN >⅔ ULN Glucose Normal (>60) May be low (<60) pH >7.40 May be low (<7.30) Cell count <1000/μL >1000/μL Appearance Clear Cloudy, bloody, purulent

docs.title: Uncomplicated vs Complicated Parapneumonic vs Empyema
docs.text: Feature Uncomplicated Complicated Empyema pH >7.20 <7.20 <7.00 Glucose >60 mg/dL <60 mg/dL <40 mg/dL LDH <1000 U/L >1000 U/L >1000 U/L Gram stain/Culture Negative Negative or Positive Positive Appearance Clear Cloudy Purulent (pus) Treatment Antibiotics alone Antibiotics + Chest tube Antibiotics + Chest tube ± Surgery

docs.title: High-Yield Diagnostic Clues
docs.text: Finding Diagnosis Bilateral effusion, CHF history, JVP↑ CHF Unilateral, fever, pneumonia Parapneumonic pH <7.20, Glucose <60, LDH >1000 Complicated parapneumonic / Empyema Lymphocytic, ADA >40 U/L TB Bloody, recurrent, cytology positive Malignancy Milky, Triglycerides >110 Chylothorax High Amylase, LEFT-sided Pancreatitis Glucose <30, pH <7.20, RA patient Rheumatoid pleuritis Hct (pleural)/Hct (blood) >50% Hemothorax Foul odor, anaerobic culture+ Anaerobic empyema

docs.title: Important Cutoffs to Memorize
docs.text: Test Cutoff Significance Light's Criteria 0.5, 0.6, ⅔ Exudate pH <7.20 Complicated parapneumonic, needs drainage Glucose <60 mg/dL Complicated parapneumonic, RA, malignancy, TB ADA >40 U/L TB Triglycerides >110 mg/dL Chylothorax LDH >1000 U/L Complicated parapneumonic Pleural Hct/Blood Hct >50% Hemothorax

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Diagnosing transudate by protein alone ❌

  2. Must use Light's Criteria (3 parameters)

  3. Treating all parapneumonic effusions with chest tube ❌

  4. Uncomplicated (pH >7.20, Glucose >60) → Antibiotics alone

  5. Complicated (pH <7.20) → Antibiotics + Chest tube

  6. Relying on AFB smear for TB pleural effusion ❌

  7. AFB smear yield <10% (very low)

  8. Use ADA >40 U/L (90% sensitivity) or Pleural biopsy (BEST)

  9. Removing >1.5 L of fluid at once ❌
