  10. Risk of re-expansion pulmonary edema

  11. Limit to 1-1.5 L per session

  12. Pleurodesis in trapped lung ❌

  13. Trapped lung (non-expandable) → Pleurodesis will FAIL

  14. Use Tunneled catheter (IPC) instead

  15. Forgetting to check glucose and pH in parapneumonic effusion ❌

  16. pH and Glucose determine need for drainage (NOT just Gram stain/culture)

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "PL-PL-PL" - Light's Criteria:

  2. **P**rotein > 0.5, **L**DH > 0.6, **PL**eural LDH > ⅔ upper limit

  3. "CHF NEPAL" - Transudative Causes:

  4. C**HF, **H**epatic cirrhosis, renal **F**ailure, **N**ephrotic, starvation (**E), P**eritoneal dialysis, **A**telectasis, constrictive pericarditis (**L)

  5. "CREMT" - Low Glucose Pleural Fluid:

  6. **C**omplicated parapneumonic, **R**A, **E**mpyema, **M**alignancy, **T**B

  7. "Left Pancreatitis" - Pancreatitis causes LEFT-sided effusion

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 65-year-old man with CHF presents with bilateral pleural effusion. Thoracentesis shows: Pleural protein 2.5 g/dL, Serum protein 6.0 g/dL, Pleural LDH 150 U/L, Serum LDH 300 U/L. What type of effusion is this?

A. Transudate B. Exudate C. Need more tests D. Empyema

Answer: A. Transudate

Explanation: - Apply Light's Criteria (Exudate if ANY ONE of 3 criteria met):

Criterion This Patient Cutoff Met? Pleural protein / Serum protein 2.5 / 6.0 = 0.42 >0.5 ❌ Pleural LDH / Serum LDH 150 / 300 = 0.5 >0.6 ❌ Pleural LDH 150 U/L >200 U/L (⅔ of 300) ❌
  • NONE of the criteria met → Transudate

Clinical Correlation: - CHF is most common cause of transudate - Bilateral effusion consistent with CHF

Key Point: Must apply ALL 3 Light's criteria; if NONE met → Transudate

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 45-year-old man with pneumonia has pleural effusion. Pleural fluid analysis: pH 7.10, Glucose 30 mg/dL, LDH 1500 U/L, Gram stain negative. What is the most appropriate management?

A. Antibiotics alone B. Antibiotics + Chest tube drainage C. Antibiotics + Repeat thoracentesis in 48 hours D. Pleurodesis

Answer: B. Antibiotics + Chest tube drainage

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Complicated Parapneumonic Effusion

Parapneumonic Effusion Classification:

Type pH Glucose LDH Treatment Uncomplicated >7.20 >60 <1000 Antibiotics alone Complicated <7.20 <60 >1000 Antibiotics + Chest tube Empyema <7.00 <40 >1000 Antibiotics + Chest tube ± Surgery

This Patient: - pH 7.10 (<7.20) ✓ - Glucose 30 (<60) ✓ - LDH 1500 (>1000) ✓ - ALL criteria for complicated parapneumonic met

Why Chest Tube Needed: - Without drainage, will progress to empyema - Antibiotics alone will NOT clear organized/complicated effusion

Gram Stain Negative: Does NOT rule out complicated effusion - pH <7.20 is MOST IMPORTANT criterion for drainage decision

Key Point: pH <7.20 in parapneumonic effusion → Chest tube drainage MANDATORY (regardless of Gram stain)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 30-year-old woman presents with unilateral pleural effusion. Pleural fluid analysis shows lymphocytic predominance (85% lymphocytes). ADA level is 65 U/L. AFB smear is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A. Parapneumonic effusion B. Tuberculous pleural effusion C. Malignant pleural effusion D. Rheumatoid pleuritis

Answer: B. Tuberculous pleural effusion

Explanation: - Classic Features of TB Pleural Effusion: - Unilateral ✓ - Lymphocytic predominant (>50%, often >80%) ✓ (85%) - ADA >40 U/L ✓ (65 U/L)

ADA (Adenosine Deaminase): - >40 U/L: 90% sensitivity, 85% specificity for TB - Very high ADA (>100 U/L): Strongly suggests TB

AFB Smear Negative: - Expected - AFB smear of pleural fluid has <10% yield (very low) - Does NOT rule out TB

Diagnostic Approach for TB Pleural Effusion: 1. ADA >40 U/L → Highly suggestive (start treatment in endemic areas) 2. Pleural biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain): - Granulomas on histology - AFB culture of biopsy tissue (20-40% of fluid culture, 70-80% of biopsy culture positive)

Why NOT Other Options: - Parapneumonic (A): PMN predominant (acute), ADA not typically >40 - Malignancy (C): Possible (lymphocytic), but ADA usually <40 (unless lymphoma) - RA pleuritis (D): Low glucose (<30), low pH, not typically high ADA

Treatment: 2HRZE / 4HR (same as pulmonary TB)

Key Point: Lymphocytic effusion + ADA >40 U/L = TB (in endemic areas)

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A patient with lung cancer has recurrent large pleural effusion causing dyspnea. Cytology is positive for adenocarcinoma. What is the most appropriate definitive management?

A. Repeat therapeutic thoracentesis as needed B. Tunneled pleural catheter (IPC) C. Talc pleurodesis D. Decortication

Answer: C. Talc pleurodesis

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Malignant pleural effusion (cytology-proven)

Management Options for Malignant Effusion:

Method Indication Success Pros/Cons Therapeutic thoracentesis Temporary relief N/A Recurs in days-weeks, NOT definitive Talc pleurodesis Recurrent effusion, good lung expansion 90% MOST EFFECTIVE, requires hospitalization Tunneled catheter (IPC) Trapped lung, poor prognosis 70-90% Outpatient drainage, good for trapped lung Chemotherapy Chemosensitive tumors Variable Adjunct, not for immediate symptom control

This Patient: - Recurrent, symptomatic → Needs definitive management - Lung presumably expandable (no mention of trapped lung) - Best option: Talc pleurodesis (90% success, prevents recurrence)

Pleurodesis Contraindications: - Trapped lung (lung cannot expand → pleurodesis will fail) - Life expectancy <3 months (IPC preferred)

Why NOT Other Options: - Repeat thoracentesis (A): NOT definitive, requires frequent procedures - IPC (B): Good option, but pleurodesis more effective if lung expandable - Decortication (D): For trapped lung/empyema, NOT routine malignant effusion

Key Point: Recurrent malignant effusion + expandable lung → Talc pleurodesis (MOST effective)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 50-year-old alcoholic presents with severe abdominal pain radiating to back. CXR shows LEFT-sided pleural effusion. Pleural fluid amylase is 800 U/L (serum 300 U/L). What is the diagnosis?

A. Parapneumonic effusion B. Pancreatitis-associated effusion C. Tuberculous effusion D. Esophageal rupture

Answer: B. Pancreatitis-associated effusion

Explanation: - Classic Triad: - Abdominal pain (pancreatitis) ✓ - LEFT-sided effusion (70% in pancreatitis) ✓ - High pleural fluid amylase (>serum) ✓

Mechanism: - Pancreatic enzymes cross diaphragm via lymphatics or direct communication - Left-sided because pancreatic tail is on left

Pleural Fluid Amylase: - Elevated (typically >serum) - Pleural/Serum amylase ratio >1

Other Causes of High Pleural Fluid Amylase: - Esophageal rupture (but would have food particles, low pH <6.0, history of vomiting/instrumentation) - Malignancy (lung, ovarian)

Why Pancreatitis (not Esophageal Rupture): - Clinical presentation: Abdominal pain (pancreatitis) vs vomiting/chest pain (rupture) - Alcoholic → High risk for pancreatitis - No mention of vomiting, instrumentation

Management: - Treat pancreatitis (NPO, IV fluids, pain control) - Effusion typically resolves with treatment - Thoracentesis if large and symptomatic

Key Point: LEFT-sided effusion + High amylase = Pancreatitis (most common) or Esophageal rupture

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient has pleural effusion with milky appearance. Triglyceride level is 150 mg/dL. What is the diagnosis?

A. Empyema B. Chylothorax C. Pseudochylothorax D. Tuberculous effusion

Answer: B. Chylothorax

Explanation: - Chylothorax: Lymphatic fluid (chyle) in pleural space

Diagnostic Criteria: - Milky/opalescent appearance ✓ - Triglycerides >110 mg/dL ✓ (150 mg/dL) - Chylomicrons present (lipoprotein electrophoresis)

Triglyceride Levels: - >110 mg/dL: Diagnostic of chylothorax - 50-110 mg/dL: Indeterminate (check for chylomicrons) - <50 mg/dL: Rules out chylothorax

Causes: - Trauma/Surgery (thoracic duct injury): Esophagectomy, cardiac surgery - Malignancy: Lymphoma (MOST COMMON non-traumatic) - Congenital (lymphangiomatosis)

Pseudochylothorax: - Cholesterol crystals (not chylomicrons) - Shimmering appearance (not truly milky) - Low triglycerides (<50 mg/dL) - High cholesterol (>200 mg/dL) - Chronic effusion (TB, RA)

Empyema: - Purulent (not milky) - PMN predominant, positive Gram stain/culture

Treatment of Chylothorax: - Conservative (50% resolve): - Low-fat diet or NPO + TPN (↓ chyle production) - Chest tube drainage - Surgical (if persistent >2 weeks): - Thoracic duct ligation

Key Point: Milky fluid + Triglycerides >110 mg/dL = Chylothorax

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A patient with empyema has chest tube draining purulent fluid. Despite 7 days of antibiotics and chest tube, fever persists and CXR shows loculated effusion. What is the next best step?

A. Continue current management B. Add intrapleural fibrinolytics (tPA + DNase) C. Remove chest tube D. Start antifungal therapy

Answer: B. Add intrapleural fibrinolytics (tPA + DNase)

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Loculated empyema not responding to antibiotics + chest tube alone

Empyema Stages: 1. Exudative (0-3 days): Sterile, free-flowing → Antibiotics 2. Fibrinopurulent (3-7 days): Bacterial invasion, loculations → Chest tube 3. Organizing (>7 days): Thick peel → May need surgery

This Patient: Stage 2-3 (loculated, not improving)

Intrapleural Fibrinolytics: - tPA (tissue Plasminogen Activator) + DNase (Deoxyribonuclease) - Breaks down loculations, improves drainage - MIST-2 Trial: ↓ Need for surgery, ↓ hospital stay

Regimen: - tPA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg via chest tube - BID × 3 days (6 total doses) - Clamp tube × 1 hour after instillation, then drain

Evidence: Superior to either agent alone

If Fibrinolytics Fail: - VATS decortication (remove fibrous peel) - Open thoracotomy (if VATS not feasible)

Why NOT Other Options: - Continue current (A): Not improving, need escalation - Remove chest tube (C): Incorrect, still has infection - Antifungal (D): No indication (bacterial empyema)

Key Point: Loculated empyema not improving with chest tube → Add intrapleural fibrinolytics (tPA + DNase)

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
What volume of pleural fluid is required to cause blunting of costophrenic angle on PA chest X-ray?

A. 50-75 mL B. 175-500 mL C. 500-1000 mL D. >1000 mL

Answer: B. 175-500 mL

Explanation: - CXR Detection of Pleural Effusion:

Volume Finding <50 mL Not visible on standard PA 50-175 mL Blunting of costophrenic angle on lateral view 175-500 mL Blunting of costophrenic angle on PA view >500 mL Meniscus sign, homogeneous opacity >1000 mL Complete opacification, mediastinal shift

Most Sensitive CXR View: Lateral decubitus - Detects as little as 5-10 mL (if layers out) - Confirms free-flowing vs loculated

Clinical Significance: - <300-500 mL: Usually asymptomatic, may not be palpable - >500 mL: Symptomatic (dyspnea), detectable on exam (dullness)

Key Point: 175-500 mL needed for blunting of costophrenic angle on PA CXR

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A patient with RA has pleural effusion with pH 7.10, Glucose 20 mg/dL, and negative cultures. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A. Parapneumonic effusion B. Rheumatoid pleuritis C. Tuberculous effusion D. Empyema

Answer: B. Rheumatoid pleuritis

Explanation: - Rheumatoid Pleuritis: Occurs in 5% of RA patients

Characteristic Pleural Fluid Findings: - Very low glucose (<30 mg/dL, often <20) ✓ (20 mg/dL) - Very low pH (<7.20, often <7.10) ✓ (7.10) - Negative cultures ✓ - Low complement (C3, C4) - Cholesterol crystals - Lymphocytic or PMN predominance

Mechanism: Immune complex deposition, complement consumption → Low glucose

Other Causes of Low Glucose Pleural Fluid (<60 mg/dL): - Complicated parapneumonic / Empyema - Rheumatoid pleuritis (LOWEST glucose, <30) - Malignancy - TB - Lupus pleuritis

Why NOT Other Options: - Parapneumonic (A): Would have clinical features of pneumonia (fever, productive cough, infiltrate on CXR) - TB (C): Low glucose possible, but usually not this low; ADA would be elevated - Empyema (D): Would have purulent fluid, positive Gram stain/culture

Clinical Correlation: RA patient + Very low glucose (<30) + Very low pH + Negative cultures = Rheumatoid pleuritis

Treatment: - NSAIDs, Corticosteroids - Treat underlying RA

Key Point: RA + Very low glucose (<30) + Low pH + Negative cultures = Rheumatoid pleuritis

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A trauma patient has chest tube draining 1800 mL of blood initially. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Continue chest tube drainage and observe B. Urgent thoracotomy C. Remove chest tube D. Add intrapleural fibrinolytics

Answer: B. Urgent thoracotomy

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Hemothorax (Pleural fluid Hct/Blood Hct >50%)

Indications for Urgent Thoracotomy (Surgery):

Absolute: - Initial chest tube output >1500 mL ✓ (1800 mL in this case) - Ongoing bleeding >200 mL/hr × 2-4 hours - Hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation

Rationale: - Initial drainage >1500 mL suggests major vascular injury - Requires surgical exploration and repair - Continued observation → Risk of exsanguination

Chest Tube in Hemothorax: - Large bore (32-36 Fr) - Purposes: - Remove blood (prevent organized hemothorax) - Monitor ongoing bleeding - Re-expand lung

If Output <1500 mL Initially and <200 mL/hr ongoing: - Continue chest tube drainage + observation - Surgery if bleeding persists or increases

Why NOT Other Options: - Observe (A): Incorrect - massive initial output requires surgery - Remove tube (C): Incorrect - would re-accumulate blood - Fibrinolytics (D): For empyema/loculations, NOT acute hemothorax

Key Point: Hemothorax with initial drainage >1500 mL → Urgent thoracotomy

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
Pleural Effusion is abnormal fluid accumulation in the pleural space: - Most common cause: CHF (transudate, bilateral) - Most common exudative cause: Parapneumonic effusion

Diagnosis: - Light's Criteria (Exudate if ANY ONE: Protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, Pleural LDH >⅔ ULN) - Physical exam: Dullness, ↓ breath sounds, ↓ fremitus (need >300-500 mL) - CXR: Blunting (175-500 mL on PA), Lateral decubitus most sensitive - Thoracentesis: MANDATORY for unknown etiology

Key Fluid Findings: - pH <7.20, Glucose <60: Complicated parapneumonic → Chest tube - Lymphocytic, ADA >40: TB → Anti-TB therapy - Bloody, cytology+: Malignancy → Pleurodesis or IPC - Milky, Triglycerides >110: Chylothorax - Very low glucose (<30), RA: Rheumatoid pleuritis - High amylase, LEFT-sided: Pancreatitis

Management: - Transudate: Treat underlying cause (diuretics for CHF) - Complicated parapneumonic: Antibiotics + Chest tube ± Fibrinolytics - Malignant: Talc pleurodesis (90% success) or IPC - Hemothorax: Chest tube; surgery if >1500 mL initially or >200 mL/hr ongoing

END OF CHAPTER

Next Topic: Pulmonary Embolism (Medicine/19_Pulmonary_Embolism.md)

Cross-References: - CHF (Medicine - Cardiology) - Pneumonia (Medicine/13_Pneumonia.md) - Tuberculosis (Medicine/14_Tuberculosis.md) - Lung Cancer (Medicine/17_Lung_Cancer.md)

docs.title: PULMONARY EMBOLISM (PE)
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5 - Extremely High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 7-8 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Definition
  2. Epidemiology
  3. Risk Factors
  4. Pathophysiology
  5. Clinical Features
  6. Diagnosis
  7. Treatment
  8. Special Situations
  9. Complications
  10. Prevention
  11. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  12. Practice MCQs

docs.title: DEFINITION
docs.text:
Pulmonary Embolism (PE): Obstruction of pulmonary arteries by thrombus (blood clot), fat, air, tumor, or amniotic fluid

Most Common: Thromboembolic PE (~95%) - Origin: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) of lower extremities (90%) - Venous Thromboembolism (VTE): DVT + PE (spectrum of same disease)

docs.title: Types by Source
docs.text:
PULMONARY EMBOLISM
│
├─ THROMBOEMBOLISM (95%) - MOST COMMON
│  • DVT (lower extremity) - 90%
│  • Pelvic veins
│  • Upper extremity DVT (central lines, pacemakers)
│
├─ FAT EMBOLISM
│  • Long bone fractures, orthopedic surgery
│  • 24-72 hours post-trauma
│  • Triad: Respiratory distress, Petechiae, CNS changes
│
├─ AIR EMBOLISM
│  • Central line insertion/removal
│  • Thoracic surgery, trauma
│
├─ AMNIOTIC FLUID EMBOLISM
│  • Childbirth, C-section
│  • DIC, shock, respiratory failure
│
└─ SEPTIC EMBOLISM
   • Tricuspid endocarditis (IV drug users)
   • Multiple peripheral infiltrates ("feeding vessel" sign)

docs.title: Incidence
docs.text:
• 60-70 per 100,000 population annually
  • 3rd most common acute cardiovascular syndrome (after MI, Stroke)
  • Increasing incidence with age (>60 years)

docs.title: Mortality
docs.text:
• Untreated: 25-30% mortality
  • Treated: 2-8% mortality
  • Massive PE: 30-50% mortality (despite treatment)

docs.title: Distribution
docs.text:
• Lower extremity DVT: 90% of PE source
  • Bilateral PE: 60%
  • Lower lobes: More commonly affected (↑ blood flow)

docs.title: Virchow's Triad (Pathogenesis of Thrombosis)
docs.text:
VIRCHOW'S TRIAD
│
├─ STASIS (Decreased blood flow)
│  • Immobilization, bed rest, prolonged travel
│  • CHF, stroke with paralysis
│  • Obesity
│
├─ HYPERCOAGULABILITY
│  • Inherited thrombophilias
│  • Malignancy, pregnancy, OCP/HRT
│  • Antiphospholipid syndrome
│
└─ ENDOTHELIAL INJURY
   • Surgery, trauma, fractures
   • Central venous catheters
   • Vasculitis

docs.title: Strong Risk Factors (OR >10)
docs.text: Risk Factor Relative Risk Major surgery (especially orthopedic: Hip/Knee replacement) 10-50x Hip/Pelvic fracture 10-50x Spinal cord injury 10-50x Prior VTE 10-20x

docs.title: Moderate Risk Factors (OR 2-10)
docs.text: Risk Factor Relative Risk Immobilization (>3 days bed rest) 2-10x Malignancy (active, especially pancreatic, brain, ovarian) 5-7x Pregnancy / Postpartum (6-fold higher risk) 5-10x Oral contraceptives (OCP) / HRT 3-5x Long-distance travel (>4-6 hours) 2-4x CHF, COPD 2-3x Age (each decade >60 years) 1.5-2x per decade

docs.title: Weak Risk Factors (OR <2)
docs.text:
• Obesity (BMI >30)
  • Varicose veins
  • Smoking

docs.title: Inherited Thrombophilias
docs.text:
Condition Prevalence VTE Risk Factor V Leiden (Activated Protein C resistance) 5% (Caucasians) - MOST COMMON 5-7x (heterozygous), 50-80x (homozygous) Prothrombin G20210A mutation 2-3% 3-5x Protein C deficiency 0.2-0.4% 10x Protein S deficiency 0.03-0.13% 10x Antithrombin deficiency 0.02-0.2% 25-50x (HIGHEST risk)

Screening Indications (Thrombophilia): - VTE at age <50 years - Unprovoked VTE - Recurrent VTE - VTE in unusual sites (cerebral, mesenteric, portal vein) - Family history of VTE - VTE during pregnancy/OCP use

docs.title: Sequence of Events
docs.text:
DVT FORMATION (Lower extremity deep veins)
    ↓
EMBOLIZATION (Clot breaks off)
    ↓
MIGRATION via IVC → Right Atrium → Right Ventricle
    ↓
LODGES IN PULMONARY ARTERIES
    ↓
ACUTE EFFECTS
│
├─ RESPIRATORY
│  • ↑ Alveolar dead space (V/Q mismatch)
│  • Hypoxemia (shunt, V/Q mismatch, ↓ CO)
│  • Hyperventilation (↓ PaCO₂)
│  • Bronchoconstriction (serotonin, histamine release)
│
├─ HEMODYNAMIC
│  • ↑ Pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR)
│  • ↑ RV afterload
│  • RV dilatation, dysfunction
│  • ↓ LV preload (↓ CO)
│  • Shock (if massive PE, >50% obstruction)
│
└─ INFARCTION (10-15% of PE)
   • More common with underlying cardiopulmonary disease
   • Peripheral, wedge-shaped consolidation
   • Hemoptysis, pleuritic pain, pleural effusion

docs.title: Hemodynamic Classification
docs.text:
Type Definition RV Dysfunction Shock Mortality Massive PE Hemodynamically unstable (SBP <90 mmHg or ↓ >40 mmHg × >15 min) Yes Yes 30-50% Submassive (Intermediate) Hemodynamically stable + RV dysfunction/myocardial injury Yes No 5-15% Low-risk (Minor) Hemodynamically stable, No RV dysfunction No No <1%

RV Dysfunction Indicators: - Echo: RV dilatation (RV/LV ratio >0.9) - CT: RV/LV ratio >1.0 - BNP/NT-proBNP: Elevated - Troponin: Elevated (myocardial strain)

docs.title: Symptoms (HIGHLY VARIABLE)
docs.text: Classic Triad (Present in <20%): 1. Dyspnea (sudden onset) 2. Pleuritic chest pain 3. Hemoptysis

docs.title: Common Symptoms
docs.text:
Symptom Frequency Dyspnea (sudden or progressive) 80-90% - MOST COMMON Pleuritic chest pain 40-50% Cough 40-50% Hemoptysis 10-15% (suggests infarction) Syncope 10-15% (suggests massive PE) Apprehension, anxiety 40-50%

Massive PE Presentation: - Shock (SBP <90 mmHg) - Syncope (↓ cardiac output) - Cardiac arrest (PEA - Pulseless Electrical Activity)

Small PE: - May be asymptomatic or minimal dyspnea

docs.title: Respiratory
docs.text: Sign Frequency Significance Tachypnea (RR >20/min) 70-90% - MOST COMMON SIGN Non-specific Crackles 50% Infarction, atelectasis Decreased breath sounds 20% Pleural effusion Wheezing 10-20% Bronchoconstriction Pleural friction rub Rare Infarction

docs.title: Cardiovascular
docs.text: Sign Frequency Significance Tachycardia (HR >100/min) 40-60% Compensatory Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) 5-10% Massive PE Loud P2 (accentuated pulmonic component) Variable Pulmonary hypertension RV heave Rare RV strain JVP elevation 30% RV failure

docs.title: DVT Signs
docs.text:
Sign Frequency Leg swelling, tenderness, erythema, warmth 30-40% Homan's sign (dorsiflexion pain) Unreliable, NOT recommended

Key Point: Normal physical exam does NOT exclude PE

docs.title: Diagnostic Approach
docs.text:
CLINICAL SUSPICION
    ↓
ASSESS PRE-TEST PROBABILITY (Wells Score or Revised Geneva Score)
    ↓
    ├─ LOW/INTERMEDIATE Probability
    │      ↓
    │  D-DIMER
    │      ↓
    │      ├─ NEGATIVE → PE EXCLUDED (98% NPV)
    │      └─ POSITIVE → IMAGING (CTPA or V/Q scan)
    │
    └─ HIGH Probability
           ↓
       IMAGING DIRECTLY (CTPA or V/Q scan)
       (Skip D-dimer - low specificity)

docs.title: Wells Score for PE
docs.text:
Clinical Feature Points Clinical signs of DVT (leg swelling, pain) 3.0 PE as likely or more likely than alternative diagnosis 3.0 Heart rate >100/min 1.5 Immobilization ≥3 days or surgery in past 4 weeks 1.5 Previous PE or DVT 1.5 Hemoptysis 1.0 Malignancy (treatment ongoing or within 6 months) 1.0

Interpretation: - <2: Low probability (risk ~10%) - 2-6: Moderate probability (risk ~30%) - >6: High probability (risk ~65%)

Alternative (Two-Level): - ≤4: PE unlikely → D-dimer - >4: PE likely → Imaging

docs.title: Revised Geneva Score
docs.text:
Variable Points Age >65 years 1 Previous DVT or PE 3 Surgery or fracture within past month 2 Active malignancy 2 Unilateral lower limb pain 3 Hemoptysis 2 Heart rate 75-94/min 3 Heart rate ≥95/min 5 Pain on deep palpation and unilateral edema 4

Interpretation: - 0-3: Low (8% probability) - 4-10: Intermediate (28%) - ≥11: High (74%)

docs.title: 2. D-Dimer
docs.text:
What it is: Fibrin degradation product (marker of fibrinolysis)

Indications: - Low or Intermediate clinical probability (Wells ≤6 or Revised Geneva <11) - NOT indicated in High probability (go straight to imaging)

Cutoff: <500 ng/mL (negative)

Performance: - Sensitivity: 95-98% (High) - Specificity: 40-60% (Low) - Negative Predictive Value: >95% (Excellent - rules out PE) - Positive Predictive Value: ~30% (Poor - many false positives)

Interpretation: - D-dimer <500 + Low/Intermediate probability → PE EXCLUDED (no imaging needed) - D-dimer ≥500 → Proceed to imaging (CTPA or V/Q)

Age-Adjusted D-Dimer (↑ specificity in elderly): - Cutoff (age >50): Age × 10 ng/mL - Example: 70-year-old → Cutoff = 700 ng/mL

False Positives (Elevated D-dimer WITHOUT PE): - Pregnancy, postpartum - Malignancy - Recent surgery, trauma - Infection, sepsis - CHF, MI - DIC - Liver disease - Advanced age (>70 years)

Key Point: D-dimer is a rule-out test (negative = excludes PE), NOT a rule-in test

docs.title: 3. Arterial Blood Gas (ABG)
docs.text:
Classic Findings (Present in only 50-60%): - Hypoxemia: PaO₂ <80 mmHg - Hypocapnia: PaCO₂ <35 mmHg (due to hyperventilation) - Respiratory alkalosis: pH >7.45 - ↑ A-a gradient: (A-a) gradient >20 mmHg

Formula: A-a gradient = PAO₂ - PaO₂ - PAO₂ = (FiO₂ × (Patm - PH₂O)) - (PaCO₂ / 0.8) - Normal = 5-15 mmHg (increases with age)

Limitations: - Normal ABG does NOT exclude PE (30-40% have normal PaO₂) - Non-specific (many causes of hypoxemia)

Utility: Assess severity, guide oxygen therapy

docs.title: 4. Chest X-Ray
docs.text:
Purpose: Exclude alternative diagnoses (pneumonia, pneumothorax, rib fracture, CHF)

Findings in PE (Present in only 10-20%, NON-SPECIFIC): - Normal CXR (50-70%) - MOST COMMON - Atelectasis (band-like opacities) - Pleural effusion (30-50%, usually small, unilateral) - Infiltrate (consolidation, infarction) - Hampton's hump: Wedge-shaped peripheral opacity (infarction) - Rare - Westermark sign: Focal oligemia (↓ vascularity) distal to embolus - Rare - Palla's sign: Enlarged right descending pulmonary artery - Rare

Key Point: CXR is usually normal or non-specific; does NOT diagnose or exclude PE

docs.title: 5. ECG
docs.text:
Purpose: Exclude MI, assess RV strain

Findings (Present in 70%, but NON-SPECIFIC): - Sinus tachycardia (40-50%) - MOST COMMON - Non-specific ST-T changes (30-40%) - S1Q3T3 pattern (10-20%): - S wave in lead I (deep) - Q wave in lead III (present) - T wave inversion in lead III - Suggests RV strain (seen in massive PE) - Right bundle branch block (RBBB) (6-10%) - Right axis deviation - P pulmonale (tall P in II, III, aVF) - T wave inversion V1-V4 (RV strain)

S1Q3T3 Pattern: Classic but insensitive (only 10-20%) and non-specific

Key Point: ECG is usually non-specific; normal ECG does NOT exclude PE

docs.title: 6. CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA)
docs.text:
GOLD STANDARD (First-line imaging)

Sensitivity: 85-95% Specificity: 90-95%

Findings: - Filling defect in pulmonary artery (partial or complete occlusion) - Abrupt vessel cutoff - RV dilatation (RV/LV ratio >1.0) - Indicates RV dysfunction

Advantages: - High sensitivity/specificity - Fast (minutes) - Visualizes alternative diagnoses (aortic dissection, pneumonia, tumor) - Assesses RV/LV ratio (prognostic)

Disadvantages: - Contrast (allergy, renal insufficiency) - Radiation (pregnancy, young patients) - Overdiagnosis of small subsegmental PE (clinical significance unclear)

Contraindications: - Severe contrast allergy (consider V/Q scan) - Severe renal insufficiency (GFR <30) - Use V/Q scan or adjust contrast dose

docs.title: 7. Ventilation-Perfusion (V/Q) Scan
docs.text:
Indications: - Contraindication to CTPA (renal failure, contrast allergy) - Pregnancy (lower radiation to fetus than CTPA) - Normal CXR (better interpretation)

Technique: - Perfusion: IV Tc-99m macroaggregated albumin → Shows blood flow - Ventilation: Inhaled Xe-133 or Tc-99m DTPA → Shows airflow

Interpretation (PIOPED II Criteria):

Result Criteria PE Probability Normal No perfusion defects <2% - PE EXCLUDED Low Probability Small perfusion defects, matched V/Q defects 10-20% Intermediate Multiple small V/Q mismatches, difficult to categorize 20-40% High Probability ≥2 large segmental V/Q mismatches OR ≥1 large + ≥2 moderate mismatches 85-90%

Mismatch: Perfusion defect (no blood flow) with normal ventilation (air flow present) - Suggests PE (ventilation OK, but no perfusion due to embolus)

Matched defect: Both perfusion and ventilation absent (suggests parenchymal disease like pneumonia, NOT PE)

Limitations: - Non-diagnostic in 40-50% (intermediate probability) - Requires normal CXR for interpretation - Less available than CTPA

Key Point: High probability V/Q scan = PE diagnosed, Normal V/Q = PE excluded, Intermediate = Further testing

docs.title: 8. Echocardiography
docs.text:
NOT for diagnosis, but for risk stratification

Indications: - Suspected massive PE (bedside, unstable patients) - Risk stratification (detect RV dysfunction) - Guided thrombolysis decision (submassive PE)

Findings Suggesting PE: - RV dilatation (RV/LV ratio >0.9) - RV hypokinesis (McConnell sign: RV free wall hypokinesis with apical sparing) - Interventricular septal flattening (D-shaped LV) - Tricuspid regurgitation (↑ RVSP) - Dilated IVC (↑ RA pressure)

Direct Visualization of Thrombus: Rare (<5%), only if large central clot

Limitations: Cannot rule out PE (50% have normal echo)

docs.title: 9. Compression Ultrasonography (Leg Veins)
docs.text:
Indications: - Clinical suspicion of DVT - If CTPA non-diagnostic or contraindicated

Findings: - Non-compressibility of vein (thrombus present) - Absence of flow on Doppler

Sensitivity: 95% for proximal DVT (femoral, popliteal), 50-70% for distal DVT (calf)

If DVT confirmed → Treat as VTE (same as PE), no need for CTPA (unless suspect PE)

docs.title: 10. Pulmonary Angiography
docs.text:
Invasive, RARELY used (replaced by CTPA)

Gold standard (historical), but high risk (1-2% mortality, arrhythmias, bleeding)

Current use: Catheter-directed thrombolysis in massive PE (therapeutic, not diagnostic)

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
1. Prevent death (hemodynamic collapse)
  2. Prevent recurrence
  3. Prevent post-thrombotic syndrome / chronic thromboembolic PH

docs.title: Treatment Algorithm
docs.text:
PE CONFIRMED
    ↓
ASSESS HEMODYNAMIC STATUS
    ↓
    ├─ MASSIVE PE (Shock, SBP <90)
    │      ↓
    │  • Resuscitation (IV fluids, O₂, vasopressors if needed)
    │  • SYSTEMIC THROMBOLYSIS (Alteplase)
    │  • Consider: Catheter-directed thrombolysis, Embolectomy (surgical/percutaneous)
    │  • Anticoagulation (Heparin)
    │
    ├─ SUBMASSIVE PE (RV dysfunction, NO shock)
    │      ↓
    │  • Anticoagulation (Heparin → Warfarin or DOAC)
    │  • Consider: Thrombolysis (if high risk of decompensation)
    │  • Monitoring (ICU/telemetry)
    │
    └─ LOW-RISK PE (No RV dysfunction, hemodynamically stable)
           ↓
       • Anticoagulation (DOAC or LMWH → Warfarin)
       • Outpatient management (if appropriate)

docs.title: 1. Anticoagulation (CORNERSTONE)
docs.text: ALL patients with confirmed PE (unless absolute contraindication)

docs.title: A. Initial Anticoagulation (First 5-10 days)
docs.text:
Options:

Drug Dose Monitoring Notes Unfractionated Heparin (UFH) IV Bolus 80 units/kg, then 18 units/kg/hr infusion aPTT (target 1.5-2.5× control) q6h Preferred if: Massive PE, renal failure (CrCl <30), high bleeding risk (reversible with protamine) Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH) Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC BID OR 1.5 mg/kg SC OD NO routine monitoring (anti-Xa if obesity/renal failure) Preferred for stable patients (outpatient option) Fondaparinux <50 kg: 5 mg, 50-100 kg: 7.5 mg, >100 kg: 10 mg SC OD NO monitoring Alternative if HIT Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs) See below NO monitoring Rivaroxaban, Apixaban can be started immediately (no parenteral bridge needed)

Duration: Minimum 5 days + INR ≥2 × 48 hours (if warfarin) OR 5-10 days (if DOAC)

docs.title: B. Long-Term Anticoagulation
docs.text:
Options:

Drug Dose Monitoring Notes Warfarin Start 5-10 mg OD, adjust based on INR INR (target 2-3) Requires parenteral bridge (UFH/LMWH × 5 days), Drug/food interactions Rivaroxaban (Xa inhibitor) 15 mg BID × 21 days, then 20 mg OD NO monitoring NO parenteral bridge needed Apixaban (Xa inhibitor) 10 mg BID × 7 days, then 5 mg BID NO monitoring NO parenteral bridge needed Edoxaban (Xa inhibitor) 60 mg OD (30 mg if CrCl 30-50, weight <60 kg) NO monitoring Requires parenteral bridge (LMWH × 5 days) Dabigatran (Thrombin inhibitor) 150 mg BID (110 mg if age >80, high bleeding risk) NO monitoring Requires parenteral bridge

Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs): - Advantages: No monitoring, rapid onset, fewer drug interactions, fixed dosing - Disadvantages: Cost, adherence issues (no INR to monitor), contraindicated if CrCl <15-30 (varies by drug) - Preferred in most patients (unless contraindication)

Warfarin: - Advantages: Reversible (Vitamin K, FFP, PCC), cost, safe in severe renal failure - Disadvantages: Slow onset, requires bridge, frequent monitoring (INR), drug/food interactions

Reversal Agents: - Warfarin: Vitamin K (slow, 12-24 hr), FFP, Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) (rapid) - Dabigatran: Idarucizumab (specific reversal) - Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban: Andexanet alfa (specific reversal, expensive) - UFH: Protamine sulfate - LMWH: Protamine (partial reversal)

docs.title: C. Duration of Anticoagulation
docs.text:
Scenario Duration Rationale Provoked PE (Surgery, trauma, temporary risk factor) 3 months Low recurrence risk (<5% per year) First unprovoked PE + Low bleeding risk ≥3 months (consider indefinite) Recurrence risk 10% per year Recurrent VTE (≥2 episodes) Indefinite (lifelong) High recurrence risk (>20% per year) Active malignancy Indefinite (as long as malignancy active) Use LMWH (superior to warfarin in cancer) Thrombophilia (e.g., Antithrombin deficiency) Indefinite High recurrence risk

Re-evaluation at 3 months: Assess bleeding risk vs recurrence risk → Decide continue vs stop

Bleeding Risk Assessment (HAS-BLED Score): - Hypertension, Abnormal renal/liver function, Stroke history, Bleeding history, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol - Score ≥3: High bleeding risk

docs.title: 2. Thrombolysis
docs.text: Indication: Massive PE (hemodynamic instability, shock, cardiac arrest) Controversial: Submassive PE (RV dysfunction without shock)

docs.title: Systemic Thrombolysis
docs.text:
Drugs: - Alteplase (tPA): 100 mg IV over 2 hours (PREFERRED) - Accelerated: 0.6 mg/kg over 15 min (max 50 mg) - for cardiac arrest - Streptokinase: 250,000 IU bolus, then 100,000 IU/hr × 24 hr (less used) - Urokinase: 4400 IU/kg bolus, then 4400 IU/kg/hr × 12-24 hr (less used)

Mechanism: Converts plasminogen → plasmin → Dissolves thrombus

Efficacy: ↓ Clot burden, ↓ Pulmonary artery pressure, ↓ RV dysfunction within 12-24 hours

Benefits: - ↓ Mortality in massive PE (50% → 30%) - Rapid hemodynamic improvement

Risks: - Major bleeding: 10-20% - Intracranial hemorrhage: 1-3% (HIGHEST risk) - Stroke, GI bleed

Absolute Contraindications: - Active bleeding - Recent stroke (<3 months) - Recent intracranial/spinal surgery (<2 months) - Intracranial malignancy, AVM - Suspected aortic dissection

Relative Contraindications: - Major surgery <10 days - Recent trauma, CPR - Uncontrolled hypertension (SBP >180) - Bleeding diathesis, thrombocytopenia (<100,000) - Pregnancy

Contraindication to Systemic Thrombolysis → Consider Catheter-directed thrombolysis or Embolectomy

docs.title: Catheter-Directed Thrombolysis
docs.text:
Indications: - Massive PE with contraindication to systemic thrombolysis - Persistent hypotension despite systemic thrombolysis

Technique: Catheter placed into pulmonary artery → Local tPA infusion

Advantages: Lower tPA dose → ↓ Bleeding risk

docs.title: Surgical/Percutaneous Embolectomy
docs.text:
Indications: - Massive PE with: - Failed thrombolysis - Contraindication to thrombolysis - Shock requiring CPR

Surgical Embolectomy: Open surgery, cardiopulmonary bypass - High mortality (20-30%), but life-saving if other options fail

Percutaneous Embolectomy: Catheter-based clot removal (AngioVac, FlowTriever) - Less invasive than surgery

docs.title: 3. Supportive Care
docs.text:
Oxygen: Target SpO₂ ≥90%

IV Fluids: Small boluses (250-500 mL) if hypotension - Avoid large volumes (may worsen RV failure by ↑ RV preload)

Vasopressors (if shock despite fluids): - Norepinephrine (preferred) - Dobutamine (if RV dysfunction without shock)

Mechanical Ventilation (if respiratory failure): - Avoid high PEEP (↑ RV afterload) - Permissive hypercapnia (avoid alkalosis → ↑ pulmonary vasoconstriction)

docs.title: 4. IVC Filter
docs.text:
NOT routine (does NOT prevent death, may ↑ recurrent DVT)

Indications: - Absolute contraindication to anticoagulation (active bleeding) - Recurrent VTE despite adequate anticoagulation - Massive PE with limited cardiopulmonary reserve (cannot tolerate recurrent PE)

Types: - Permanent: If lifelong contraindication - Retrievable: Place during acute contraindication, remove when safe to anticoagulate (within 2-4 weeks)

Complications: IVC thrombosis, filter migration, perforation

docs.title: 1. Pregnancy
docs.text:
Risk: 5-10x ↑ risk of VTE - Hypercoagulable state (↑ Factors VII, VIII, X, fibrinogen, ↓ Protein S) - Venous stasis (uterine compression of IVC) - Highest risk: Postpartum (6 weeks after delivery)

Diagnosis: - D-dimer: Not useful (↑ in normal pregnancy) - Compression ultrasound (first-line): If DVT confirmed → Treat (no further imaging needed) - V/Q scan (preferred over CTPA): Lower fetal radiation - CTPA: If V/Q non-diagnostic (acceptable, maternal benefit > fetal risk)

Treatment: - LMWH (Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID) - PREFERRED - Does not cross placenta - Continue throughout pregnancy + 6 weeks postpartum (minimum 3 months total) - UFH (if renal failure) - Warfarin: CONTRAINDICATED (teratogenic, especially 1st trimester) - DOACs: CONTRAINDICATED (no safety data)

Thrombolysis: If massive PE (life-threatening), can be used

docs.title: 2. Malignancy
docs.text:
Risk: 5-7x ↑ risk, highest in pancreatic, brain, ovarian, lung cancers

Trousseau Syndrome: Migratory thrombophlebitis + malignancy

Treatment: - LMWH (Enoxaparin, Dalteparin) - PREFERRED over warfarin - CLOT Trial: LMWH superior to warfarin in cancer-associated VTE (↓ recurrence) - DOACs: Edoxaban, Rivaroxaban - Emerging as alternatives (HOKUSAI-VTE, SELECT-D trials) - Caution in GI/GU malignancies (↑ bleeding) - Duration: Indefinite (as long as malignancy active)

docs.title: 3. Catheter-Associated Upper Extremity DVT
docs.text:
Risk: Central lines, pacemakers, PICC lines

Treatment: - Remove catheter (if no longer needed) - Anticoagulation × 3 months (same as lower extremity DVT) - If catheter needed → Continue anticoagulation while catheter in place

docs.title: 4. Subsegmental PE
docs.text:
Definition: PE in subsegmental arteries (branches of segmental arteries)

Management (CONTROVERSIAL): - If concurrent proximal DVT OR High recurrence risk → Anticoagulate - If isolated subsegmental PE + Low risk + NO DVT → Surveillance (serial leg ultrasounds) vs Anticoagulation - AACP 2019: Suggests surveillance over anticoagulation (if low risk)

Rationale: Clinical significance unclear, may represent overdiagnosis with modern CTPA

docs.title: 5. Chronic Thromboembolic Pulmonary Hypertension (CTEPH)
docs.text:
Definition: Persistent PH due to organized thrombi (>3 months post-PE)

Frequency: 0.5-4% of PE patients

Presentation: Progressive dyspnea, RV failure

Diagnosis: - Echo: Pulmonary hypertension (RVSP >40 mmHg) - V/Q scan: Mismatched perfusion defects (more sensitive than CTPA) - Right heart catheterization: mPAP ≥25 mmHg (gold standard)

Treatment: - Pulmonary Thromboendarterectomy (PTE): Curative (surgical removal of organized clots) - Medical: Riociguat (if inoperable), balloon pulmonary angioplasty - Lifelong anticoagulation

Prognosis: Poor if untreated (median survival 3-5 years); PTE improves survival

docs.title: 1. Death
docs.text:
• Massive PE: 30-50% mortality (despite treatment)
  • Low-risk PE: <1% mortality

docs.title: 2. Recurrent VTE
docs.text:
• Provoked: <5% per year (after stopping anticoagulation)
  • Unprovoked: 10% per year (after stopping anticoagulation)
  • Recurrent VTE: >20% per year

docs.title: 3. Chronic Thromboembolic Pulmonary Hypertension (CTEPH)
docs.text: • See above

docs.title: 4. Post-Thrombotic Syndrome (from DVT)
docs.text:
• Chronic leg pain, swelling, skin changes (hyperpigmentation, ulcers)
  • Occurs in 20-50% of DVT patients
  • Prevention: Compression stockings (controversial), adequate anticoagulation

docs.title: 5. Bleeding (from anticoagulation)
docs.text:
• Major bleeding: 1-3% per year (warfarin), 1-2% (DOACs)
  • ICH: 0.5% per year

docs.title: Risk Stratification
docs.text:
ALL hospitalized patients should be assessed for VTE risk

Risk Level Criteria Prophylaxis Low Mobile, age <40, minor surgery Early mobilization Moderate Age 40-60, major surgery, prolonged immobility Pharmacologic (LMWH, UFH, Fondaparinux) OR Mechanical (SCDs, GCS) High Age >60, malignancy, prior VTE, thrombophilia, major surgery Pharmacologic (preferred) Very High Hip/knee replacement, hip fracture, spinal cord injury Pharmacologic (extended duration, 10-35 days) + Mechanical

docs.title: Pharmacologic Prophylaxis
docs.text:
Drug Dose Notes Enoxaparin (LMWH) 40 mg SC OD (general surgery), 30 mg SC BID (orthopedic) PREFERRED UFH 5000 units SC TID If renal failure (CrCl <30) Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC OD Alternative

Duration: - General surgery: Until discharge (7-10 days) - Hip/Knee replacement: 10-35 days (extended prophylaxis) - Medical patients: Duration of hospitalization + 7-14 days (if high risk)

Contraindications: Active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia (<50,000), recent neurosurgery/spinal anesthesia

docs.title: Mechanical Prophylaxis
docs.text:
Indications: Contraindication to anticoagulation, or adjunct to pharmacologic (very high risk)

Methods: - Graduated Compression Stockings (GCS): 15-30 mmHg - Intermittent Pneumatic Compression (IPC) / Sequential Compression Devices (SCDs): Inflate/deflate → ↑ venous flow

Efficacy: ↓ VTE by 50-60% (less effective than pharmacologic alone)

docs.title: Long-Distance Travel
docs.text:
Risk: Flights >4-6 hours → 2-4x ↑ risk

Prevention (General population): - Calf exercises, walk every 1-2 hours - Adequate hydration - Avoid excessive alcohol

High-risk travelers (prior VTE, thrombophilia, obesity, pregnancy): - Compression stockings (knee-high, 15-30 mmHg) - Prophylactic LMWH (consider if very high risk)

docs.title: Must-Know Facts
docs.text:
1. Most Common Source: DVT of lower extremity (90%)

  2. Risk Factors:

  3. Strong (OR >10): Major surgery (hip/knee), fractures, spinal cord injury, prior VTE
  4. Moderate: Immobilization, malignancy, pregnancy, OCP/HRT

  5. Inherited: Factor V Leiden (most common), Prothrombin G20210A, Protein C/S deficiency, Antithrombin deficiency

  6. Clinical Features:

  7. MOST COMMON SYMPTOM: Dyspnea (80-90%)
  8. MOST COMMON SIGN: Tachypnea (70-90%)
  9. Classic triad (dyspnea, pleuritic pain, hemoptysis): <20%

  10. Normal exam does NOT exclude PE

  11. Wells Score for PE:

  12. Clinical DVT signs: 3 points
  13. PE as likely as alternative: 3 points
  14. ≤4: PE unlikely → D-dimer

  15. >4: PE likely → Imaging

  16. D-Dimer:

  17. High sensitivity (95-98%), Low specificity (40-60%)
  18. <500 ng/mL: EXCLUDES PE (if low/intermediate probability)
  19. Use for LOW/INTERMEDIATE probability ONLY (NOT high probability)

  20. Age-adjusted: Age × 10 ng/mL (if >50 years)

  21. Diagnostic Imaging:

  22. CTPA: GOLD STANDARD (85-95% sensitivity/specificity)

  23. V/Q scan: If CTPA contraindicated (renal failure, contrast allergy, pregnancy)

    • High probability: ≥2 large segmental mismatches → PE confirmed
    • Normal: PE excluded

  24. ABG Findings:

  25. Hypoxemia (PaO₂ <80), Hypocapnia (PaCO₂ <35), ↑ A-a gradient

  26. Normal ABG does NOT exclude PE (30-40% have normal PaO₂)

  27. ECG:

  28. Sinus tachycardia (most common)
  29. S1Q3T3: Deep S in I, Q in III, Inverted T in III (RV strain, only 10-20%)

  30. Normal ECG does NOT exclude PE

  31. Classification:

  32. Massive: Shock (SBP <90), high mortality (30-50%)
  33. Submassive: RV dysfunction WITHOUT shock

  34. Low-risk: No RV dysfunction, hemodynamically stable

  35. Anticoagulation:

    • Initial: UFH (if massive PE, renal failure) OR LMWH (if stable) OR DOAC (Rivaroxaban/Apixaban - no bridge needed)
    • Long-term: DOAC (preferred) OR Warfarin (INR 2-3)
    • Duration:
    • Provoked: 3 months
    • Unprovoked: ≥3 months, consider indefinite
    • Recurrent VTE, Active malignancy, Thrombophilia: Indefinite

  36. Thrombolysis:

    • Indication: Massive PE (shock)
    • Drug: Alteplase (tPA) 100 mg IV over 2 hours
    • Contraindication: Active bleeding, recent stroke (<3 mo), recent surgery, ICH

  37. IVC Filter:

    • NOT routine
    • Indication: Absolute contraindication to anticoagulation OR Recurrent VTE despite adequate anticoagulation

  38. Pregnancy:

    • LMWH throughout pregnancy + 6 weeks postpartum (minimum 3 months)
    • Warfarin, DOACs CONTRAINDICATED

  39. Malignancy:

    • LMWH preferred over warfarin (↓ recurrence)
    • Duration: Indefinite (as long as cancer active)

docs.title: Wells Score Simplified
docs.text:
Feature Points Clinical DVT signs 3 PE > Alternative diagnosis 3 HR >100 1.5 Immobilization/Surgery 1.5 Prior VTE 1.5 Hemoptysis 1 Malignancy 1

≤4: D-dimer, >4: Imaging

docs.title: D-Dimer Interpretation
docs.text: D-Dimer Wells Score Action <500 ≤4 PE EXCLUDED (no imaging) ≥500 ≤4 Imaging (CTPA or V/Q) Any value >4 (High probability) Imaging directly (skip D-dimer)

docs.title: CTPA vs V/Q Scan
docs.text: Feature CTPA V/Q Scan Sensitivity 85-95% 85-90% (high prob result) Specificity 90-95% 90-95% (high prob result) Advantages Fast, visualizes alternatives, RV assessment No contrast, lower fetal radiation Disadvantages Contrast (renal/allergy), radiation Non-diagnostic (40-50%), needs normal CXR Preferred First-line (most patients) Pregnancy, renal failure, contrast allergy

docs.title: Anticoagulation Options
docs.text: Drug Parenteral Bridge Monitoring Reversibility Use in Renal Failure Warfarin YES (UFH/LMWH × 5 days) INR (target 2-3) Yes (Vit K, PCC) Yes Rivaroxaban NO NO Andexanet alfa Avoid if CrCl <30 Apixaban NO NO Andexanet alfa Avoid if CrCl <25 Edoxaban YES NO Andexanet alfa Avoid if CrCl <30 Dabigatran YES NO Idarucizumab Avoid if CrCl <30 LMWH N/A (is parenteral) NO (anti-Xa if needed) Partial (Protamine) Avoid if CrCl <30 (use UFH)

docs.title: High-Yield Diagnostic Clues
docs.text: Scenario Diagnosis/Action Sudden dyspnea + pleuritic pain + recent surgery High suspicion PE → Imaging (CTPA) Wells ≤4 + D-dimer <500 PE EXCLUDED (no imaging needed) Wells >4 Imaging directly (skip D-dimer) V/Q: ≥2 large segmental mismatches High probability → PE confirmed S1Q3T3 on ECG RV strain (suggests massive PE, but only 10-20% sensitive) RV/LV ratio >1 on CTPA RV dysfunction (submassive/massive PE) SBP <90 mmHg Massive PE → Consider thrombolysis Recurrent VTE despite warfarin INR 2-3 Consider IVC filter, workup for malignancy/thrombophilia

docs.title: Important Cutoffs
docs.text: Parameter Cutoff Significance D-dimer <500 ng/mL Excludes PE (if low/intermediate probability) Wells Score ≤4 vs >4 PE unlikely vs PE likely SBP <90 mmHg Massive PE (thrombolysis indication) INR (Warfarin) 2-3 Therapeutic target RV/LV ratio (CTPA) >1.0 RV dysfunction RV/LV ratio (Echo) >0.9 RV dysfunction

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Using D-dimer in high-probability patients ❌
  2. D-dimer only for low/intermediate probability

  3. High probability → Imaging directly

  4. Excluding PE based on normal CXR or ECG ❌

  5. Normal CXR/ECG does NOT exclude PE

  6. Need CTPA or V/Q scan

  7. Excluding PE based on normal PaO₂ ❌

  8. 30-40% of PE have normal PaO₂

  9. Thrombolysis in submassive PE (RV dysfunction without shock) ❌

  10. Controversial, not routine
  11. Only if high risk of decompensation

  12. Massive PE (shock) → Clear indication

  13. Warfarin without parenteral bridge ❌

  14. Warfarin takes 5-7 days to reach therapeutic level

  15. MUST bridge with UFH/LMWH × 5 days + INR ≥2 × 48 hours

  16. Routine IVC filter placement ❌

  17. NOT routine (does NOT prevent death, may ↑ DVT)

  18. Only if absolute contraindication to anticoagulation

  19. DOACs in severe renal failure (CrCl <30) ❌

  20. Contraindicated (accumulation, ↑ bleeding)
  21. Use Warfarin or UFH

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "PEP" - PE Presentation:

  2. **P**leuritic pain, **E**xertional dyspnea (sudden), hemo**P**tysis

  3. "PERC" Rule (PE Rule-out Criteria) - LOW RISK if ALL negative:

  4. Age <50, HR <100, SpO₂ ≥95%, No hemoptysis, No estrogen, No prior VTE, No leg swelling, No surgery/trauma

  5. If ALL negative + Clinical suspicion low → No testing needed

  6. "S1Q3T3" - ECG RV strain:

  7. S in lead I, Q in lead III, inverted T in lead III

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 55-year-old woman presents 5 days after hip replacement surgery with sudden dyspnea. Wells score is 7 (high probability). What is the most appropriate next step?

A. D-dimer B. Chest X-ray C. CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) D. V/Q scan

Answer: C. CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA)

Explanation: - Wells Score 7 = High probability (>6 points)

High Probability → Imaging DIRECTLY (skip D-dimer)

Why CTPA: - GOLD STANDARD for PE diagnosis - Fast, high sensitivity/specificity - No contraindications mentioned

Why NOT D-dimer: - D-dimer is ONLY for low/intermediate probability (Wells ≤6) - In high probability, D-dimer has poor specificity (many false positives) - Would delay diagnosis

Algorithm: - Wells ≤4 (PE unlikely) → D-dimer first - Wells >4 (PE likely) → Imaging directly

Key Point: High clinical probability (Wells >6) → CTPA directly (do NOT check D-dimer)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 40-year-old man with calf pain has Wells score of 3 (low probability). D-dimer is 350 ng/mL. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. CT pulmonary angiography B. V/Q scan C. Start anticoagulation D. No further testing, PE excluded

Answer: D. No further testing, PE excluded

Explanation: - Wells ≤4 (Low/Intermediate probability) + D-dimer <500 ng/mL → PE EXCLUDED

D-dimer Performance: - Negative Predictive Value >95%: D-dimer <500 + Low/Intermediate probability → Safely excludes PE - No imaging needed

Algorithm: 

Wells ≤4 → D-dimer
    ├─ <500 ng/mL → **PE EXCLUDED** (stop)
    └─ ≥500 ng/mL → Imaging (CTPA or V/Q)

Why NOT Imaging: - Unnecessary (D-dimer <500 rules out PE) - Would expose patient to radiation, contrast, cost

Key Point: Wells ≤4 + D-dimer <500 → PE EXCLUDED (no further testing)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with confirmed massive PE (SBP 80 mmHg) has no contraindications to thrombolysis. What is the most appropriate treatment?

A. Warfarin B. Rivaroxaban C. Alteplase (tPA) D. IVC filter

Answer: C. Alteplase (tPA)

Explanation: - Massive PE (SBP <90 mmHg) → SYSTEMIC THROMBOLYSIS

Thrombolysis Indication: - Massive PE (hemodynamic instability, shock, cardiac arrest) - Alteplase (tPA) 100 mg IV over 2 hours - PREFERRED

Benefits: - Rapid clot dissolution (12-24 hours) - ↓ Mortality (50% → 30%) - Improves hemodynamics

Why NOT Anticoagulation Alone (A, B): - Warfarin/Rivaroxaban are for stable patients - Massive PE requires urgent intervention (anticoagulation too slow) - Start heparin + thrombolysis together

Why NOT IVC Filter (D): - Does NOT treat acute PE (prevents recurrence only) - NOT indicated unless contraindication to anticoagulation

Contraindications to Thrombolysis: - Active bleeding, recent stroke, recent surgery, ICH - If contraindicated → Embolectomy (surgical or catheter-directed)

Key Point: Massive PE (shock) → Systemic thrombolysis (Alteplase) + Anticoagulation

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 60-year-old woman with unprovoked PE is treated with anticoagulation. After 3 months, she has recovered fully with low bleeding risk. What is the most appropriate duration of anticoagulation?

A. Stop now (completed 3 months) B. Continue for 6 months total, then stop C. Continue indefinitely (lifelong) D. Continue for 1 year total, then stop

Answer: C. Continue indefinitely (lifelong)

Explanation: - First unprovoked PE + Low bleeding risk → Consider INDEFINITE anticoagulation

Anticoagulation Duration:

Scenario Duration Recurrence Risk (off anticoagulation) Provoked (surgery, trauma) 3 months <5% per year First unprovoked ≥3 months, consider indefinite 10% per year Recurrent VTE (≥2 episodes) Indefinite >20% per year

Rationale: - Unprovoked PE = High recurrence risk (10% per year) - Benefit of continued anticoagulation > Bleeding risk (if low bleeding risk)

Re-evaluation at 3 months: - Assess bleeding risk (HAS-BLED score) - Assess recurrence risk - Shared decision-making with patient

If High Bleeding Risk: May stop after 3 months (accept recurrence risk)

Key Point: Unprovoked PE + Low bleeding risk → Consider indefinite anticoagulation (recurrence risk 10% per year)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation presents with suspected PE. Compression ultrasound of legs is negative. What is the most appropriate next imaging test?

A. CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) B. V/Q scan C. Pulmonary angiography D. No imaging, start anticoagulation empirically

Answer: B. V/Q scan

Explanation: - Pregnancy + Suspected PE + Negative leg ultrasound → V/Q scan

Why V/Q Scan Preferred in Pregnancy: - Lower fetal radiation than CTPA - V/Q: 0.1-0.5 mGy to fetus - CTPA: 0.3-0.6 mGy to fetus - No contrast (avoid iodinated contrast if possible)

CTPA: - Acceptable if V/Q non-diagnostic - Lower maternal breast radiation than V/Q - Use if high suspicion and need definitive diagnosis

Algorithm in Pregnancy: 1. Compression ultrasound (legs) first - If positive (DVT) → Treat (no further imaging needed) 2. If negative → V/Q scan (preferred) or CTPA

Treatment if PE Confirmed: - LMWH (Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID) - Continue throughout pregnancy + 6 weeks postpartum (minimum 3 months total) - Warfarin, DOACs CONTRAINDICATED

Key Point: Pregnancy + Suspected PE → V/Q scan preferred (lower fetal radiation)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to thrombolysis in massive PE?

A. Age >75 years B. Recent stroke 2 months ago C. Uncontrolled hypertension (BP 190/110) D. Thrombocytopenia (platelets 80,000/μL)

Answer: B. Recent stroke 2 months ago

Explanation: - Recent stroke (<3 months) = ABSOLUTE contraindication to thrombolysis

Absolute Contraindications: - Active bleeding - Recent stroke (<3 months) - Recent intracranial/spinal surgery (<2 months) - Intracranial malignancy, AVM - Suspected aortic dissection

Relative Contraindications (Consider case-by-case): - Age >75 years (A) - Relative - Uncontrolled hypertension (C) - Relative (SBP >180 mmHg) - Thrombocytopenia (D) - Relative (<100,000/μL) - Major surgery <10 days - Recent trauma, CPR - Pregnancy

If Absolute Contraindication: - Catheter-directed thrombolysis (lower dose → ↓ bleeding risk) - Embolectomy (surgical or percutaneous)

Key Point: Recent stroke (<3 months) = Absolute contraindication to thrombolysis

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A patient with PE is started on warfarin and LMWH. INR is 2.3 on day 3. When can LMWH be stopped?

A. Now (INR >2) B. After INR >2 for 24 hours C. After INR >2 for 48 hours D. After 5 days minimum, AND INR >2 for 48 hours

Answer: D. After 5 days minimum, AND INR >2 for 48 hours

Explanation: - LMWH bridge with warfarin: Continue until BOTH criteria met: 1. Minimum 5 days of overlap, AND 2. INR ≥2 for 48 hours (2 consecutive days)

Rationale: - Warfarin takes 5-7 days to deplete Vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (especially Factor II, which has longest half-life ~60 hours) - INR reflects Factor VII (shortest half-life ~6 hours) → INR may become therapeutic before full anticoagulation - Need 48 hours of INR ≥2 to ensure all factors depleted

This Patient: - Day 3, INR 2.3 - Continue LMWH until day 5 + INR ≥2 × 48 hours - Cannot stop yet (only day 3)

Key Point: LMWH bridge → Stop after ≥5 days AND INR ≥2 × 48 hours (NOT just INR ≥2 alone)

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 50-year-old man with PE has creatinine clearance of 20 mL/min. Which anticoagulant is most appropriate for long-term treatment?

A. Rivaroxaban B. Apixaban C. Dabigatran D. Warfarin

Answer: D. Warfarin

Explanation: - Severe renal failure (CrCl <30 mL/min) → Warfarin or UFH (NOT DOACs)

DOACs in Renal Failure:

Drug Contraindication (CrCl) Rivaroxaban <30 mL/min Apixaban <25 mL/min Edoxaban <30 mL/min Dabigatran <30 mL/min

Rationale: DOACs are renally excreted → Accumulation → ↑ Bleeding risk

Safe Options in Severe Renal Failure: - Warfarin (hepatic metabolism, no renal excretion) - UFH (can use, adjust based on aPTT)

LMWH: Avoid in CrCl <30 (accumulation, ↑ bleeding)

Key Point: Severe renal failure (CrCl <30) → Warfarin (DOACs contraindicated)

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
V/Q scan shows 2 large segmental mismatched perfusion defects with normal ventilation. What is the interpretation?

A. Low probability B. Intermediate probability C. High probability D. Normal

Answer: C. High probability

Explanation: - ≥2 large segmental V/Q mismatches = High probability for PE

V/Q Scan Interpretation:

Result Criteria PE Probability Normal No perfusion defects <2% - PE excluded Low Small defects, matched V/Q 10-20% Intermediate Multiple small mismatches, difficult to categorize 20-40% High ≥2 large segmental mismatches OR 1 large + ≥2 moderate mismatches 85-90% - PE confirmed

This Patient: - 2 large segmental mismatches → High probability → PE diagnosis confirmed

Mismatch: Perfusion defect + Normal ventilation - Suggests PE (ventilation OK, but no perfusion due to embolus)

Matched defect: Both perfusion + ventilation absent → Parenchymal disease (NOT PE)

Next Step: Start anticoagulation (high probability V/Q = PE confirmed)

Key Point: ≥2 large segmental mismatches on V/Q → High probability → PE confirmed

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A patient with active pancreatic cancer develops PE. What is the most appropriate long-term anticoagulation?

A. Warfarin B. LMWH (Enoxaparin) C. Rivaroxaban D. IVC filter

Answer: B. LMWH (Enoxaparin)

Explanation: - Cancer-associated VTE → LMWH preferred over warfarin

Evidence: - CLOT Trial: LMWH vs Warfarin in cancer patients with VTE - LMWH superior: ↓ Recurrent VTE (9% vs 17%), no ↑ bleeding - Mechanism: LMWH has anti-inflammatory, anti-angiogenic effects

Regimen: - Enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID OR 1.5 mg/kg OD - Duration: Indefinite (as long as malignancy active)

DOACs (C): - Edoxaban, Rivaroxaban: Emerging alternatives (HOKUSAI-VTE, SELECT-D trials) - Caution in GI/GU malignancies (↑ bleeding risk) - Can be used if patient prefers (oral), but LMWH still preferred

Warfarin (A): - Less effective than LMWH in cancer patients - Drug/food interactions, difficult to maintain INR

IVC Filter (D): - NOT routine (only if contraindication to anticoagulation)

Key Point: Cancer-associated VTE → LMWH preferred over warfarin (↓ recurrent VTE)

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
Pulmonary Embolism (PE) is obstruction of pulmonary arteries, most commonly by thrombus from DVT: - Spectrum of VTE: DVT → PE - Risk: Virchow's Triad (Stasis, Hypercoagulability, Endothelial injury)

Diagnosis: - Pre-test probability: Wells Score (≤4 → D-dimer, >4 → Imaging) - D-dimer: <500 → Excludes PE (if low/intermediate probability) - CTPA: GOLD STANDARD (85-95% sensitivity/specificity) - V/Q scan: If CTPA contraindicated (pregnancy, renal failure)

Treatment: - Anticoagulation: ALL patients (DOAC preferred, or Warfarin) - Duration: Provoked (3 mo), Unprovoked (≥3 mo, consider indefinite) - Thrombolysis: Massive PE (shock) - Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hr - Special: Pregnancy (LMWH), Cancer (LMWH > Warfarin), Severe renal failure (Warfarin)

Prevention: Risk stratification + Pharmacologic (LMWH 40 mg OD) or Mechanical (SCDs)

Prognosis: Untreated (25-30% mortality), Treated (2-8%), Massive (30-50%)

END OF CHAPTER

Next Topic: Respiratory Failure (Medicine/20_Respiratory_Failure.md)

Cross-References: - Pleural Effusion (Medicine/18_Pleural_Effusion.md) - COPD (Medicine/12_COPD.md) - Pneumonia (Medicine/13_Pneumonia.md)

docs.title: RESPIRATORY FAILURE
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5 - Extremely High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 6-7 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Definition & Classification
  2. Pathophysiology
  3. Type I Respiratory Failure (Hypoxemic)
  4. Type II Respiratory Failure (Hypercapnic)
  5. Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
  6. Oxygen Therapy
  7. Mechanical Ventilation
  8. Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV)
  9. Complications
  10. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  11. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Respiratory Failure: Inability of the respiratory system to maintain adequate gas exchange

Arterial Blood Gas Criteria (at Sea Level, Room Air): - PaO₂ <60 mmHg (Hypoxemia), OR - PaCO₂ >45 mmHg (Hypercapnia)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
RESPIRATORY FAILURE
│
├─ BY MECHANISM
│  ├─ TYPE I (Hypoxemic) - PaO₂ <60, PaCO₂ normal/low
│  │  • Pneumonia, Pulmonary edema, PE, ILD, ARDS
│  │  • V/Q mismatch, Shunt
│  │
│  └─ TYPE II (Hypercapnic) - PaCO₂ >45 (± Hypoxemia)
│     • COPD exacerbation, Neuromuscular disease, CNS depression
│     • Hypoventilation
│
├─ BY ONSET
│  ├─ ACUTE (Minutes to hours)
│  │  • ARDS, Pneumonia, PE, Pneumothorax
│  │
│  ├─ CHRONIC (Weeks to months)
│  │  • COPD, ILD, Neuromuscular disease
│  │
│  └─ ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC
│     • COPD exacerbation, Neuromuscular crisis
│
└─ BY LOCATION OF DEFECT
   ├─ PUMP FAILURE (Type II)
   │  • CNS: Drug overdose, stroke
   │  • Neuromuscular: MG, GBS, ALS
   │  • Chest wall: Kyphoscoliosis, flail chest
   │
   └─ LUNG PARENCHYMAL (Type I)
      • Alveolar: Pneumonia, pulmonary edema
      • Interstitial: ILD, ARDS
      • Vascular: PE, Pulmonary hypertension

docs.title: Mnemonic: "VENT" for Causes of Respiratory Failure
docs.text:
• V - Ventilation/Perfusion mismatch (Type I)
  • E - (hypo)vEntilation (Type II)
  • N - Neuromuscular/CNS (Type II)
  • T - parenchymal/inTerstitial disease (Type I)

docs.title: Normal Gas Exchange
docs.text:
Oxygen Transport: 1. Ventilation: Air movement in/out of alveoli 2. Diffusion: O₂ crosses alveolar-capillary membrane 3. Perfusion: Blood flow through pulmonary capillaries 4. V/Q Matching: Ventilation matches perfusion (V/Q ratio ~0.8-1.0)

Carbon Dioxide Elimination: - Depends primarily on alveolar ventilation - PaCO₂ ∝ CO₂ production / Alveolar ventilation

docs.title: Mechanisms of Hypoxemia
docs.text:
Mechanism PaO₂ Response to 100% O₂ A-a Gradient Example 1. Low FiO₂ ↑ Corrects Normal High altitude 2. Hypoventilation ↑ Corrects Normal Drug overdose, neuromuscular disease 3. V/Q Mismatch ↑ Partially corrects ↑ Increased COPD, Asthma, PE (MOST COMMON) 4. Shunt No correction ↑↑ Markedly increased ARDS, Pneumonia, Pulmonary edema 5. Diffusion Limitation ↑ Corrects ↑ Increased ILD (exercise), High altitude

A-a Gradient = PAO₂ - PaO₂ - Normal: 5-15 mmHg (↑ with age: Age/4 + 4) - PAO₂ = (FiO₂ × [Patm - PH₂O]) - (PaCO₂ / 0.8) - At room air (FiO₂ 0.21): PAO₂ ≈ 150 - (PaCO₂ × 1.25)

Key Point: Normal A-a gradient = Hypoventilation (extra-pulmonary), ↑ A-a gradient = Lung parenchymal disease

docs.title: Mechanisms of Hypercapnia
docs.text:
PaCO₂ = (VCO₂ × 0.863) / V̇A

Where: - VCO₂ = CO₂ production - V̇A = Alveolar ventilation

Causes: 1. ↑ CO₂ Production (rare alone, compensate by ↑ ventilation) - Fever, sepsis, overfeeding 2. ↓ Alveolar Ventilation (MOST COMMON) - CNS depression, neuromuscular disease, airway obstruction

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• PaO₂ <60 mmHg on room air
  • PaCO₂ Normal or ↓ (hyperventilation due to hypoxemia)

docs.title: Mechanisms
docs.text:
• V/Q Mismatch (MOST COMMON)
  • Shunt (intrapulmonary or intracardiac)
  • Diffusion limitation (exercise, ILD)

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text: Category Examples Alveolar Filling Pneumonia (MOST COMMON), Pulmonary edema (cardiogenic, non-cardiogenic), Hemorrhage Interstitial/Alveolar Injury ARDS, ILD, Radiation pneumonitis Vascular Pulmonary embolism, Pulmonary hypertension Airway/Alveolar Collapse Atelectasis, Bronchiectasis

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Dyspnea (MOST COMMON) - Tachypnea (RR >20/min) - Cyanosis (PaO₂ <55 mmHg) - Confusion, agitation (severe hypoxemia)

Signs: - Tachycardia - Accessory muscle use - Nasal flaring - Cyanosis (central: Tongue, lips)

ABG: - PaO₂ <60 mmHg - PaCO₂ Normal (<45 mmHg) or ↓ (hyperventilation) - pH Normal or ↑ (respiratory alkalosis if hyperventilating)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
1. Oxygen Therapy (Correct hypoxemia)
  2. Target SpO₂ 90-96% (94-98% if no COPD risk)

  3. Methods: Nasal cannula, Face mask, High-flow nasal oxygen

  4. Treat Underlying Cause

  5. Pneumonia: Antibiotics
  6. Pulmonary edema: Diuretics, Nitrates
  7. PE: Anticoagulation

  8. ARDS: Lung-protective ventilation

  9. Mechanical Ventilation (If severe)

  10. Indications: PaO₂ <60 despite O₂, Impending respiratory arrest, Work of breathing

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• PaCO₂ >45 mmHg
  • ± Hypoxemia (PaO₂ <60 mmHg)
  • Alveolar Hypoventilation

docs.title: Mechanisms
docs.text:
• ↓ Respiratory Drive (CNS depression)
  • ↓ Neuromuscular Function
  • ↑ Airway Resistance / ↓ Lung Compliance
  • ↑ Work of Breathing → Respiratory muscle fatigue

docs.title: Common Causes ("Can't Breathe")
docs.text:
TYPE II RESPIRATORY FAILURE CAUSES
│
├─ CNS DEPRESSION (↓ Respiratory drive)
│  • Drug overdose (Opioids, Benzodiazepines)
│  • Brainstem stroke, trauma
│  • Hypothyroidism (severe)
│  • Central sleep apnea
│
├─ NEUROMUSCULAR (↓ Respiratory muscle function)
│  • Myasthenia Gravis, GBS, ALS
│  • Phrenic nerve injury
│  • Cervical spinal cord injury (C3-C5)
│  • Muscular dystrophy, Polio
│
├─ CHEST WALL / PLEURA
│  • Kyphoscoliosis, Ankylosing spondylitis
│  • Flail chest, Rib fractures
│  • Massive pleural effusion, Pneumothorax
│  • Obesity hypoventilation syndrome
│
└─ AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION / PARENCHYMAL
   • **COPD exacerbation** (MOST COMMON)
   • Severe asthma (status asthmaticus)
   • Upper airway obstruction (foreign body, tumor)
   • End-stage ILD (rare)

docs.title: Mnemonic: "DOPE" for Type II Causes
docs.text:
• D - Drugs (CNS depression)
  • O - Obstruction (COPD, asthma)
  • P - Pump failure (neuromuscular, chest wall)
  • E - End-stage lung disease

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Dyspnea - Confusion, Somnolence (CO₂ narcosis) - Headache (vasodilation from hypercapnia) - Asterixis (flapping tremor)

Signs: - ↓ Respiratory rate (if CNS/neuromuscular) OR Tachypnea (if COPD) - Shallow breathing - Paradoxical breathing (diaphragmatic paralysis) - Bounding pulse (hypercapnia → vasodilation) - Papilledema (↑ ICP from CO₂ retention)

ABG: - PaCO₂ >45 mmHg (↑↑ if acute) - pH <7.35 (Acute respiratory acidosis) OR pH normal (Chronic, compensated) - HCO₃⁻: Normal (Acute) OR ↑ (Chronic compensation)

Acute vs Chronic: - Acute: pH ↓ 0.08 for every 10 mmHg ↑ in PaCO₂ - Chronic: pH ↓ 0.03 for every 10 mmHg ↑ in PaCO₂ (renal compensation → ↑ HCO₃⁻)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
1. Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV) - FIRST-LINE for COPD, Neuromuscular
  2. BiPAP (Bilevel Positive Airway Pressure)

  3. Reduces need for intubation, mortality

  4. Treat Underlying Cause

  5. COPD exacerbation: Bronchodilators, Steroids, Antibiotics (if infection)
  6. Drug overdose: Naloxone (opioids), Flumazenil (benzodiazepines)
  7. Myasthenia Gravis: Pyridostigmine, IVIG, Plasmapheresis

  8. GBS: IVIG, Plasmapheresis

  9. Oxygen Therapy (CAUTION in COPD)

  10. Controlled oxygen: Target SpO₂ 88-92% (avoid hyperoxia → ↑ CO₂)

  11. Venturi mask (delivers precise FiO₂)

  12. Mechanical Ventilation (If NIV fails or contraindicated)

  13. Indications: pH <7.25, Severe acidosis, Hemodynamic instability, Altered mental status

docs.title: Definition (Berlin Criteria, 2012)
docs.text:
ARDS: Acute diffuse inflammatory lung injury leading to ↑ pulmonary vascular permeability, ↑ lung weight, ↓ aerated lung tissue

Criteria (ALL must be present):

Component Criterion Timing Within 1 week of known clinical insult OR new/worsening respiratory symptoms Chest Imaging Bilateral opacities (CXR or CT) - NOT fully explained by effusions, collapse, nodules Origin of Edema NOT fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (need objective assessment - Echo if doubt) Oxygenation Defect PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio ≤300 mmHg (with PEEP or CPAP ≥5 cm H₂O)

docs.title: Severity Classification
docs.text:
Severity PaO₂/FiO₂ Ratio Mortality Mild 200-300 mmHg 27% Moderate 100-200 mmHg 32% Severe <100 mmHg 45%

PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O required for diagnosis

docs.title: Direct Lung Injury (Pulmonary ARDS)
docs.text: Cause Frequency Pneumonia (bacterial, viral, fungal) MOST COMMON (40-50%) Aspiration (gastric contents) 10-15% Inhalation injury (smoke, toxins) 5% Pulmonary contusion (chest trauma) 5% Near-drowning Rare

docs.title: Indirect Lung Injury (Extrapulmonary ARDS)
docs.text:
Cause Frequency Sepsis MOST COMMON (30-40%) Severe trauma / Polytrauma 10% Pancreatitis 5% Multiple transfusions (TRALI) 5% Drug overdose (opioids, cocaine) Rare Burns Rare

Mnemonic: "SPARTAN" for ARDS Causes: - S - Sepsis - P - Pneumonia, Pancreatitis - A - Aspiration - R - tRauma - T - Transfusions (TRALI) - A - inhalAtion injury - N - Near-drowning

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
INITIATING INJURY (Sepsis, Pneumonia, Trauma)
    ↓
INFLAMMATORY RESPONSE
• Cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6, IL-8)
• Neutrophil recruitment and activation
    ↓
ENDOTHELIAL & EPITHELIAL INJURY
    ↓
↑ ALVEOLAR-CAPILLARY PERMEABILITY
    ↓
PULMONARY EDEMA (Protein-rich fluid in alveoli)
    ↓
HYALINE MEMBRANE FORMATION
    ↓
IMPAIRED GAS EXCHANGE
• Shunt (blood bypasses non-ventilated alveoli)
• V/Q mismatch
    ↓
SEVERE HYPOXEMIA (Refractory to oxygen)
    ↓
FIBROSIS (if prolonged, organizing phase)

Three Phases: 1. Exudative (0-7 days): Alveolar edema, hyaline membranes, neutrophil infiltration 2. Proliferative (7-21 days): Type II pneumocyte proliferation, fibroblast activity 3. Fibrotic (>21 days): Collagen deposition, fibrosis (if survives)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms (Develop over hours-days): - Severe dyspnea - Tachypnea (RR >30/min) - Hypoxemia refractory to oxygen

Signs: - Diffuse crackles (bilateral) - Tachycardia - Cyanosis - Accessory muscle use

Chest X-Ray: - Bilateral diffuse infiltrates ("white-out lung") - Normal heart size (distinguishes from cardiogenic pulmonary edema)

ABG: - Severe hypoxemia (PaO₂ <60 mmHg despite high FiO₂) - Respiratory alkalosis (early, hyperventilation) - Respiratory acidosis (late, fatigue)

Pulmonary Artery Catheter (if performed): - PCWP ≤18 mmHg (normal LA pressure → excludes cardiogenic edema)

docs.title: Differential Diagnosis
docs.text:
ARDS vs Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema:

Feature ARDS Cardiogenic Edema Mechanism ↑ Permeability (non-cardiogenic) ↑ Hydrostatic pressure CXR Bilateral infiltrates, Normal heart size Infiltrates, Enlarged heart, Kerley B lines, Cephalization PCWP ≤18 mmHg >18 mmHg BNP Normal or mildly ↑ Markedly ↑ (>400-500 pg/mL) Response to diuretics Poor Good Protein (pleural fluid/edema) High (>0.5) Low (<0.5)

docs.title: 1. Lung-Protective Ventilation Strategy (CORNERSTONE)
docs.text:
Evidence: ARDSNet Trial (2000) - ↓ Mortality from 40% → 31%

Principles:

Parameter Target Rationale Tidal Volume (Vt) 6 mL/kg IBW (Ideal Body Weight) ↓ Volutrauma, barotrauma Plateau Pressure (Pplat) ≤30 cm H₂O ↓ Alveolar overdistension PEEP 5-15 cm H₂O (titrated to FiO₂) Recruit collapsed alveoli, ↓ FiO₂ requirement FiO₂ Lowest to maintain SpO₂ 88-95% (PaO₂ 55-80 mmHg) Avoid oxygen toxicity Respiratory Rate Adjust to maintain pH >7.20 Permissive hypercapnia acceptable

Ideal Body Weight (IBW): - Male: 50 + 0.91 × (Height in cm - 152.4) - Female: 45.5 + 0.91 × (Height in cm - 152.4)

Example: 170 cm tall male → IBW = 50 + 0.91 × (170-152.4) = 66 kg → Vt = 6 × 66 = 396 mL (~400 mL)

Permissive Hypercapnia: Accept PaCO₂ >45 mmHg (even 60-80 mmHg) to avoid high tidal volumes - Contraindication: ↑ ICP, severe acidosis (pH <7.15)

docs.title: 2. PEEP (Positive End-Expiratory Pressure)
docs.text:
Purpose: Recruit collapsed alveoli, improve oxygenation, ↓ FiO₂ requirement

Titration: - ARDSNet PEEP/FiO₂ table (higher PEEP for higher FiO₂) - Typical: 10-15 cm H₂O (severe ARDS may need higher)

Risks: Barotrauma, ↓ Cardiac output (↑ intrathoracic pressure → ↓ venous return)

docs.title: 3. Prone Positioning
docs.text:
Indication: Severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <150 mmHg)

Mechanism: - Improves V/Q matching (dorsal lung recruitment) - ↓ Shunt

Protocol: ≥16 hours/day prone positioning

Evidence: PROSEVA Trial - ↓ Mortality (32% → 16%)

Contraindications: Spinal instability, open chest/abdomen, pregnancy, severe hemodynamic instability

docs.title: 4. Neuromuscular Blockade
docs.text:
Indication: Severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <150 mmHg) with patient-ventilator dyssynchrony

Drug: Cisatracurium infusion × 48 hours

Evidence: ACURASYS Trial - ↓ Mortality in severe ARDS (if used early)

Recent: ROSE Trial (2019) - NO mortality benefit with routine paralysis (conflicting)

Current Recommendation: Use if patient-ventilator dyssynchrony despite sedation, NOT routine

docs.title: 5. Fluid Management
docs.text:
Strategy: Conservative fluid management (negative fluid balance)

Evidence: FACTT Trial - Conservative strategy: - ↓ Ventilator days - ↓ ICU stay - NO ↑ mortality or renal failure

Goal: CVP 4-6 mmHg (or lowest tolerated without hypotension)

Caution: Avoid hypovolemia (↓ organ perfusion)

docs.title: 6. Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO)
docs.text:
Indication: Refractory hypoxemia despite maximal ventilatory support - PaO₂/FiO₂ <50-80 mmHg on FiO₂ 1.0 + PEEP 15-20 - Plateau pressure >30 despite lung-protective ventilation

Mechanism: Bypass lungs for gas exchange → Allow lung rest

Evidence: EOLIA Trial (2018) - Trend toward ↓ mortality (not significant, but crossover)

Availability: Specialized centers only

docs.title: 7. Supportive Care
docs.text:
• Treat underlying cause: Antibiotics (pneumonia), source control (sepsis)
  • Sedation: Minimize (daily awakening trials), avoid over-sedation
  • Nutrition: Early enteral nutrition (within 24-48 hours)
  • DVT prophylaxis: LMWH or UFH
  • Stress ulcer prophylaxis: H2-blockers or PPIs
  • Avoid: Steroids (NO benefit, may harm), Nitric oxide (NO mortality benefit)

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Mortality: 30-45% overall (varies by severity) - Mild: 27% - Moderate: 32% - Severe: 45%

Survivors: Most recover lung function within 6-12 months, but many have persistent: - Reduced exercise capacity - Pulmonary fibrosis (if prolonged ARDS) - Neurocognitive impairment, PTSD (ICU-related)

Predictors of Mortality: - Advanced age, immunosuppression - Sepsis, multi-organ failure - Severity (PaO₂/FiO₂ <100) - Non-pulmonary organ dysfunction

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
Acute Hypoxemia: - PaO₂ <60 mmHg OR - SpO₂ <90% (88-92% if COPD risk)

Goal: SpO₂ 94-98% (90-96% if COPD/chronic hypoxemia)

docs.title: 1. Low-Flow Systems (Variable FiO₂)
docs.text:
FiO₂ depends on patient's inspiratory flow and respiratory pattern

Device Flow Rate FiO₂ Delivered When to Use Nasal Cannula 1-6 L/min 24-44% (~4% per L/min) Mild hypoxemia, stable patients Simple Face Mask 5-10 L/min 40-60% Moderate hypoxemia Non-Rebreather Mask (with reservoir) 10-15 L/min 60-90% (up to 100% if tight seal) Severe hypoxemia

Nasal Cannula: - Rule: Each 1 L/min ↑ FiO₂ by ~4% - 1 L/min → 24%, 2 L → 28%, 3 L → 32%, 4 L → 36%, 5 L → 40%, 6 L → 44%

docs.title: 2. High-Flow Systems (Fixed FiO₂)
docs.text:
FiO₂ independent of patient's respiratory pattern

Device Flow Rate FiO₂ Delivered When to Use Venturi Mask Variable (depends on adapter) 24%, 28%, 31%, 35%, 40%, 50% (precise) COPD (need controlled FiO₂), Type II RF High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC) 30-60 L/min 21-100% (adjustable) Hypoxemic respiratory failure, post-extubation

Venturi Mask: - Uses Venturi principle (fixed oxygen dilution with room air) - Most precise FiO₂ delivery - IDEAL for COPD (avoid CO₂ retention from excess O₂)

High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC): - Heated, humidified, high-flow oxygen - Advantages: Comfort, washout of dead space, PEEP effect, better oxygenation - Indications: Hypoxemic RF (Type I), immunocompromised, post-extubation

docs.title: 3. Reservoir Systems
docs.text: Non-Rebreather Mask: - Reservoir bag fills with 100% O₂ during exhalation - Patient breathes from reservoir → Higher FiO₂ - One-way valves prevent exhaled air entering reservoir

docs.title: COPD Patients (Risk of CO₂ Retention)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Chronic hypercapnia → Blunted central chemoreceptor response → Hypoxemia drives ventilation - High FiO₂ → ↑ PaO₂ → Loss of hypoxic drive → ↓ Ventilation → ↑ PaCO₂

Target: SpO₂ 88-92% (NOT 94-98%)

Devices: Venturi mask (28-31%) preferred

Monitoring: Serial ABG (watch for rising PaCO₂)

Myth: "Oxygen causes CO₂ retention" is partially true but NEVER withhold O₂ if hypoxemic - Give oxygen, monitor ABG, use NIV if CO₂ rises

docs.title: Long-Term Oxygen Therapy (LTOT)
docs.text:
Indications (COPD, ILD): - PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg, OR - PaO₂ 56-59 mmHg + Evidence of end-organ hypoxia: - Cor pulmonale (RV failure) - Polycythemia (Hct >55%) - Pulmonary hypertension

Duration: ≥15 hours/day (preferably 24 hours)

Evidence: NOTT Trial - LTOT ↑ survival in COPD with chronic hypoxemia

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Apnea, Cardiac arrest - Severe hypoxemia (PaO₂ <60 despite high FiO₂) - Severe acidosis (pH <7.25 with hypercapnia) - Impending respiratory arrest (exhaustion, altered mental status)

Relative: - Excessive work of breathing (accessory muscle use, diaphoresis) - Hemodynamic instability - Need for airway protection (↓ GCS, vomiting)

docs.title: 1. Volume-Controlled (VC)
docs.text:
Set Parameter: Tidal Volume (Vt), Respiratory Rate, FiO₂, PEEP Variable: Pressure (Pplat varies with lung compliance)

Advantage: Guaranteed minute ventilation Disadvantage: Risk of high pressures (barotrauma) if lung compliance ↓

Use: ARDS (with lung-protective strategy: Vt 6 mL/kg, Pplat ≤30)

docs.title: 2. Pressure-Controlled (PC)
docs.text:
Set Parameter: Inspiratory Pressure, Respiratory Rate, FiO₂, PEEP Variable: Tidal Volume (varies with lung compliance)

Advantage: Pressure-limited (↓ barotrauma risk) Disadvantage: Vt may vary (if compliance changes)

Use: ARDS, asthma (pressure-limited)

docs.title: 3. Assist-Control (AC)
docs.text:
Concept: Ventilator delivers full support for EVERY breath (patient-triggered or machine-triggered)

Use: Acute respiratory failure, full support needed

Risk: Respiratory alkalosis (if patient tachypneic → excessive minute ventilation)

docs.title: 4. Synchronized Intermittent Mandatory Ventilation (SIMV)
docs.text:
Concept: Ventilator delivers SET number of breaths; patient can take additional breaths (unsupported or with Pressure Support)

Use: Weaning

docs.title: 5. Pressure Support Ventilation (PSV)
docs.text:
Concept: Patient triggers ALL breaths, ventilator provides PRESET pressure support

Use: Weaning, Spontaneous breathing trial

Typical Setting: PS 5-20 cm H₂O (enough to overcome endotracheal tube resistance)

docs.title: Ventilator Settings (Initial)
docs.text: Parameter Typical Setting Notes Mode AC (Volume or Pressure Control) Full support initially Tidal Volume (Vt) 6 mL/kg IBW (ARDS), 8-10 mL/kg (others) Lung-protective if ARDS Respiratory Rate (RR) 12-20/min Adjust for pH/PaCO₂ target FiO₂ Start 100%, titrate down Target SpO₂ 88-95% (ARDS), 94-98% (others) PEEP 5-10 cm H₂O Higher (10-20) in ARDS I:E Ratio 1:2 to 1:3 (inspiration:expiration) Allow adequate exhalation (avoid air-trapping) Plateau Pressure (Pplat) ≤30 cm H₂O (ARDS) Pause at end-inspiration to measure

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
Hourly: - Tidal Volume, Peak Inspiratory Pressure (PIP), Plateau Pressure (Pplat) - SpO₂

Daily: - ABG (assess oxygenation, ventilation, acid-base) - CXR (tube position, pneumothorax, infiltrates)

Pressure Monitoring: - Peak Inspiratory Pressure (PIP): Total pressure (airway + alveolar + PEEP) - ↑ PIP: Airway obstruction (secretions, bronchospasm, kinked tube) - Plateau Pressure (Pplat): Alveolar pressure (measured with end-inspiratory hold) - ↑ Pplat: ↓ Lung compliance (pneumonia, ARDS, pneumothorax, pulmonary edema) - Driving Pressure = Pplat - PEEP - Should be <15 cm H₂O (predictor of mortality in ARDS)

docs.title: Weaning from Mechanical Ventilation
docs.text:
