When to Consider Weaning: - Underlying cause improved/resolved - Adequate oxygenation (PaO₂/FiO₂ >150-200, PEEP ≤8, FiO₂ ≤0.5) - Hemodynamically stable (no vasopressors or low-dose) - Awake, able to protect airway

Weaning Process:

  1. Daily Spontaneous Awakening Trial (SAT): ↓ Sedation → Assess readiness

  2. Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT): 30-120 minutes

  3. T-piece (no support) OR

  4. Pressure Support 5-7 cm H₂O + PEEP 5 cm H₂O

  5. Assess SBT Success:

  6. Passed: RR <30-35/min, SpO₂ >90%, HR change <20%, SBP change <20%, no distress

  7. Failed: Tachypnea, hypoxemia, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, agitation

  8. If SBT Passed → EXTUBATE

  9. If SBT Failed → Continue ventilation, reassess daily

Predictors of Successful Extubation: - Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI) = RR / Vt (in liters) - <105: High likelihood of success - >105: High risk of failure

Post-Extubation: - High-flow nasal cannula or non-invasive ventilation (especially high-risk patients) - Monitor closely × 24-48 hours

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Positive pressure ventilation delivered via face mask or nasal mask (NO endotracheal intubation)

docs.title: 1. CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure)
docs.text:
• Single pressure throughout respiratory cycle
  • Improves oxygenation (alveolar recruitment)
  • Use: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema, OSA

docs.title: 2. BiPAP (Bilevel Positive Airway Pressure)
docs.text:
• Two pressures: IPAP (Inspiratory) > EPAP (Expiratory)
  • IPAP: Augments tidal volume (↓ work of breathing, ↓ PaCO₂)
  • EPAP: Like PEEP (recruits alveoli, ↑ PaO₂)
  • Use: Type II respiratory failure (COPD, neuromuscular), Cardiogenic pulmonary edema

Typical Settings: - IPAP: 10-20 cm H₂O - EPAP: 4-10 cm H₂O - FiO₂: Titrate to SpO₂ goal

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
Strong Evidence (↓ Intubation, ↓ Mortality): 1. COPD Exacerbation (MOST COMMON) - pH 7.25-7.35, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg 2. Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema 3. Immunocompromised patients with acute respiratory failure 4. Post-extubation respiratory failure (prevention)

Moderate Evidence: - Obesity hypoventilation syndrome - Neuromuscular disease (chronic Type II RF) - Post-operative respiratory failure - Palliative care (avoid intubation)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Cardiac/respiratory arrest - Inability to protect airway (↓ GCS, excessive secretions, vomiting) - Severe hemodynamic instability - Severe upper GI bleed - Facial trauma/surgery (mask cannot be fit)

Relative: - Uncooperative patient - High aspiration risk - Excessive secretions - Recent upper GI surgery

docs.title: Monitoring & Success Criteria
docs.text:
Monitor: - ABG (1-2 hours after starting): ↓ PaCO₂, ↑ pH, ↑ PaO₂ - Clinical improvement: ↓ RR, ↓ accessory muscle use, ↑ alertness

Success (Continue NIV): - ↓ PaCO₂ by 5-10 mmHg - ↑ pH toward normal - ↓ RR <25-30/min - Improved mental status

Failure (Intubate): - Worsening acidosis (pH <7.25) - ↑ PaCO₂ despite NIV - Persistent tachypnea (RR >35/min) - Hemodynamic instability - Unable to tolerate mask

Trial Duration: 1-2 hours → Reassess → Continue vs Intubate

docs.title: 1. Barotrauma / Volutrauma
docs.text:
Definition: Lung injury from high pressure (barotrauma) or high volume (volutrauma)

Manifestations: - Pneumothorax (MOST COMMON) - Sudden ↑ PIP, hypotension, unilateral ↓ breath sounds - Pneumomediastinum - Subcutaneous emphysema

Prevention: Lung-protective ventilation (Vt 6 mL/kg, Pplat ≤30 cm H₂O)

docs.title: 2. Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP)
docs.text:
Definition: Pneumonia developing >48 hours after intubation

Risk Factors: Prolonged intubation, re-intubation, supine position, aspiration

Diagnosis: New infiltrate + fever + purulent secretions + ↑ WBC

Prevention: - Elevate head of bed 30-45° - Daily sedation vacation (assess extubation readiness) - Oral care with chlorhexidine - Subglottic suctioning (special ET tubes)

Treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (cover Pseudomonas, MRSA)

docs.title: 3. Ventilator-Induced Lung Injury (VILI)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Overdistension (high Vt/pressure), cyclic atelectasis, inflammation

Prevention: Lung-protective ventilation

docs.title: 4. Hemodynamic Effects
docs.text:
Positive Pressure → ↑ Intrathoracic pressure → ↓ Venous return → ↓ Cardiac output

Manifestations: Hypotension (especially with high PEEP)

Management: IV fluids, ↓ PEEP if tolerated

docs.title: 5. Oxygen Toxicity
docs.text:
Mechanism: High FiO₂ (>0.6) for prolonged periods → Free radical damage

Manifestations: Worsening ARDS, tracheobronchitis

Prevention: Use lowest FiO₂ to maintain SpO₂ 88-95%

docs.title: 6. Auto-PEEP (Intrinsic PEEP)
docs.text:
Definition: Incomplete exhalation → Air-trapping → Positive alveolar pressure at end-expiration

Causes: Short expiratory time (high RR, long I-time), Airflow obstruction (COPD, asthma)

Effects: ↓ Venous return → ↓ CO, barotrauma, trigger difficulty

Detection: Expiratory flow doesn't return to zero before next breath

Management: ↑ Expiratory time (↓ RR, ↓ I:E ratio), bronchodilators, ↓ Vt

docs.title: Non-Invasive Ventilation Complications
docs.text:
• Mask discomfort, skin breakdown (pressure ulcers)
  • Gastric distension (air swallowing)
  • Aspiration (if vomiting occurs)
  • Eye irritation (air leak)

docs.title: Must-Know Facts
docs.text:
1. Respiratory Failure Definition:

  2. PaO₂ <60 mmHg OR PaCO₂ >45 mmHg

  3. Type I vs Type II:

Feature Type I (Hypoxemic) Type II (Hypercapnic) PaO₂ <60 mmHg May be normal or ↓ PaCO₂ Normal or ↓ >45 mmHg A-a gradient ↑ Increased Normal (if pure hypoventilation) Mechanism V/Q mismatch, Shunt Hypoventilation Common causes Pneumonia, ARDS, PE COPD, Neuromuscular, CNS depression Treatment Oxygen NIV (BiPAP), Treat cause
  1. Mechanisms of Hypoxemia:
  2. V/Q Mismatch: MOST COMMON (COPD, Pneumonia) - Partially corrects with O₂
  3. Shunt: ARDS, Pneumonia - Does NOT correct with 100% O₂

  4. Hypoventilation: Drug overdose, neuromuscular - Corrects with O₂, NORMAL A-a gradient

  5. ARDS (Berlin Criteria):

  6. Timing: Within 1 week of insult
  7. Imaging: Bilateral opacities
  8. Edema: NOT cardiac (PCWP ≤18 mmHg, BNP normal)
  9. Oxygenation: PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤300 mmHg (with PEEP ≥5)

  10. Severity: Mild (200-300), Moderate (100-200), Severe (<100)

  11. ARDS Causes:

  12. MOST COMMON: Pneumonia (pulmonary), Sepsis (extrapulmonary)

  13. Mnemonic: "SPARTAN" - Sepsis, Pneumonia/Pancreatitis, Aspiration, tRauma, Transfusions, inhalAtion, Near-drowning

  14. ARDS Treatment (Lung-Protective Ventilation):

  15. Tidal Volume: 6 mL/kg IBW (NOT 8-10)
  16. Plateau Pressure: ≤30 cm H₂O
  17. PEEP: 5-15 cm H₂O (titrate to FiO₂)
  18. Prone positioning: ≥16 hrs/day (if PaO₂/FiO₂ <150) - ↓ Mortality

  19. Conservative fluid strategy: ↓ Ventilator days

  20. Oxygen Delivery:

  21. Nasal cannula: 1-6 L/min → 24-44% FiO₂ (4% per L/min)
  22. Non-rebreather: 10-15 L/min → 60-90% FiO₂

  23. Venturi mask: MOST PRECISE FiO₂ → Use in COPD

  24. COPD Oxygen Target: SpO₂ 88-92% (avoid hyperoxia → CO₂ retention)

  25. NIV Indications (↓ Intubation, ↓ Mortality):

  26. COPD exacerbation (pH 7.25-7.35, PaCO₂ >45)
  27. Cardiogenic pulmonary edema

  28. Immunocompromised patients

  29. NIV Contraindications:

    • Cardiac/respiratory arrest
    • Unable to protect airway (↓ GCS, vomiting, excessive secretions)
    • Severe hemodynamic instability

  30. Mechanical Ventilation Indications:

    • Severe hypoxemia (PaO₂ <60 despite O₂)
    • Severe acidosis (pH <7.25 with hypercapnia)
    • Impending arrest (exhaustion, ↓ GCS)

  31. Ventilator Complications:

    • Barotrauma: Pneumothorax (sudden ↑ PIP, hypotension)
    • VAP: Pneumonia >48 hours after intubation
    • Auto-PEEP: Air-trapping (COPD, asthma) → ↓ CO, barotrauma

docs.title: Type I vs Type II Respiratory Failure
docs.text: Feature Type I Type II Other name Hypoxemic Hypercapnic Primary defect Oxygenation Ventilation PaO₂ <60 mmHg Variable PaCO₂ Normal or ↓ >45 mmHg A-a gradient ↑ Increased Normal (if pure hypoventilation) Mechanism V/Q mismatch, Shunt, Diffusion Hypoventilation Causes Pneumonia, ARDS, PE, ILD COPD, Neuromuscular, CNS depression, Chest wall First-line Rx Oxygen NIV (BiPAP)

docs.title: ARDS vs Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema
docs.text: Feature ARDS Cardiogenic Edema Mechanism ↑ Permeability ↑ Hydrostatic pressure Heart size (CXR) Normal Enlarged Kerley B lines Absent Present PCWP ≤18 mmHg >18 mmHg BNP Normal/mildly ↑ Markedly ↑ (>500) Response to diuretics Poor Good Pleural fluid protein High (>0.5) Low (<0.5)

docs.title: Oxygen Delivery Devices
docs.text: Device Flow Rate FiO₂ Use Nasal cannula 1-6 L/min 24-44% Mild hypoxemia Simple mask 5-10 L/min 40-60% Moderate hypoxemia Non-rebreather 10-15 L/min 60-90% Severe hypoxemia Venturi mask Variable 24-50% (precise) COPD (controlled FiO₂) HFNC 30-60 L/min 21-100% Type I RF, post-extubation

docs.title: High-Yield Formulas
docs.text:
1. A-a Gradient: - A-a gradient = PAO₂ - PaO₂ - PAO₂ = 150 - (PaCO₂ × 1.25) [simplified, at room air] - Normal: 5-15 mmHg (or Age/4 + 4) - ↑ A-a gradient → Lung parenchymal disease

2. PaO₂/FiO₂ Ratio: - Normal: 400-500 mmHg - ARDS: ≤300 mmHg

3. Ideal Body Weight (IBW): - Male: 50 + 0.91 × (Height in cm - 152.4) - Female: 45.5 + 0.91 × (Height in cm - 152.4)

4. Tidal Volume (ARDS): - 6 mL/kg IBW

5. Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI): - RSBI = RR / Vt (in liters) - <105: Likely successful extubation - >105: High risk of failure

docs.title: Important Cutoffs
docs.text: Parameter Cutoff Significance PaO₂ <60 mmHg Respiratory failure PaCO₂ >45 mmHg Hypercapnia (Type II RF) pH <7.25 Severe acidosis (intubation threshold) PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤300 mmHg ARDS PaO₂/FiO₂ <100 mmHg Severe ARDS PCWP ≤18 mmHg Non-cardiogenic edema (ARDS) Plateau Pressure ≤30 cm H₂O Target in ARDS SpO₂ (COPD) 88-92% Target oxygen saturation SpO₂ (Others) 94-98% Target oxygen saturation

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Using high tidal volume (8-10 mL/kg) in ARDS ❌

  2. Use 6 mL/kg IBW (lung-protective ventilation)

  3. Targeting SpO₂ 94-98% in COPD patients ❌

  4. Target 88-92% (avoid CO₂ retention)

  5. Giving high FiO₂ (100%) for prolonged periods ❌

  6. Oxygen toxicity (FiO₂ >0.6 × >24-48 hours)

  7. Titrate to lowest FiO₂ for SpO₂ goal

  8. Diagnosing ARDS without PEEP ≥5 cm H₂O ❌

  9. PaO₂/FiO₂ must be measured with PEEP ≥5

  10. Using NIV in cardiac arrest or unable to protect airway ❌

  11. Contraindication → Intubate

  12. Forgetting prone positioning in severe ARDS ❌

  13. PaO₂/FiO₂ <150 → Prone ≥16 hours/day (↓ mortality)

  14. Not checking for auto-PEEP in COPD/asthma on ventilator ❌

  15. Can cause barotrauma, ↓ CO
  16. Manage: ↑ Expiratory time, ↓ RR, bronchodilators

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "VENT" - Respiratory Failure Mechanisms:
  2. V - V/Q mismatch (Type I)
  3. E - hypovEntilation (Type II)
  4. N - Neuromuscular (Type II)

  5. T - paren chymal disease (Type I)

  6. "DOPE" - Type II Causes:

  7. D - Drugs (CNS depression)
  8. O - Obstruction (COPD, asthma)
  9. P - Pump failure (neuromuscular, chest wall)

  10. E - End-stage lung

  11. "SPARTAN" - ARDS Causes:

  12. S - Sepsis
  13. P - Pneumonia, Pancreatitis
  14. A - Aspiration
  15. R - tRauma
  16. T - Transfusions (TRALI)
  17. A - inhalAtion injury

  18. N - Near-drowning

  19. "Sexy PEEP" - ARDS Management:

  20. S - Six mL/kg tidal volume
  21. E - (Plat)eau pressure ≤30
  22. X - FiO₂ (minimize)
  23. Y - pHYsiologic pH (permissive hypercapnia OK)
  24. P - PEEP (titrate)
  25. E - Extubate early (daily SBT)
  26. E - Eliminate fluids (conservative strategy)
  27. P - Prone (if severe)

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 70-year-old man with COPD exacerbation has ABG: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 68 mmHg, PaO₂ 55 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 30 mEq/L on room air. What is the most appropriate initial management?

A. Intubation and mechanical ventilation B. High-flow oxygen (Non-rebreather mask) C. BiPAP (Non-invasive ventilation) D. Venturi mask 24% oxygen

Answer: C. BiPAP (Non-invasive ventilation)

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Acute-on-chronic Type II respiratory failure (COPD exacerbation)

ABG Analysis: - pH 7.28 (Acidemia) - PaCO₂ 68 mmHg (Hypercapnia) - HCO₃⁻ 30 mEq/L (Baseline compensation for chronic CO₂ retention) - Acute respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35, ↑ PaCO₂)

NIV (BiPAP) Indications: - COPD exacerbation with pH 7.25-7.35, PaCO₂ >45 mmHg - First-line treatment (↓ intubation rate, ↓ mortality)

This Patient: pH 7.28 (>7.25) → Trial of NIV appropriate

BiPAP Benefits: - ↓ PaCO₂ (IPAP augments ventilation) - ↑ PaO₂ (EPAP recruits alveoli) - ↓ Work of breathing

Monitoring: ABG in 1-2 hours → If improving (↑ pH, ↓ PaCO₂) → Continue NIV If pH <7.25 or worsening → Intubation

Why NOT Other Options: - Intubation (A): NOT first-line if pH >7.25 (try NIV first) - Non-rebreather (B): High FiO₂ → Risk of worsening CO₂ retention in COPD (loss of hypoxic drive) - Venturi 24% (D): TOO LOW for PaO₂ 55 mmHg (need higher FiO₂, but controlled → BiPAP allows this)

Key Point: COPD exacerbation + pH 7.25-7.35 + PaCO₂ >45 → NIV (BiPAP) first-line

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A patient on mechanical ventilation for ARDS has the following settings: Vt 400 mL, RR 20/min, PEEP 10, FiO₂ 0.6. The patient weighs 100 kg and is 180 cm tall. Plateau pressure is 35 cm H₂O. What is the most appropriate change?

A. Increase PEEP to 15 cm H₂O B. Decrease tidal volume to 450 mL C. Decrease tidal volume to 330 mL D. Increase FiO₂ to 1.0

Answer: C. Decrease tidal volume to 330 mL

Explanation: - Problem: Plateau pressure >30 cm H₂O (35 cm H₂O) → Risk of barotrauma/VILI

ARDS Lung-Protective Ventilation: - Tidal Volume: 6 mL/kg IBW - Plateau Pressure: ≤30 cm H₂O

Calculate IBW (Male, 180 cm): - IBW = 50 + 0.91 × (180 - 152.4) = 50 + 25.1 = 75 kg

Target Tidal Volume: - 6 mL/kg × 75 kg = 450 mL

Current Tidal Volume: 400 mL (already low, but Pplat still >30)

Action: Further ↓ Tidal Volume to achieve Pplat ≤30 cm H₂O - Try 330 mL (4.4 mL/kg) → Recheck Pplat - Permissive hypercapnia acceptable (allow PaCO₂ to rise to protect lung)

Why NOT Other Options: - ↑ PEEP (A): May ↑ Pplat further (PEEP adds to total pressure) - ↓ Vt to 450 mL (B): Would be appropriate initial target, but Pplat already >30 at 400 mL → Need LOWER Vt - ↑ FiO₂ (D): Doesn't address high Pplat, ↑ oxygen toxicity risk

Key Point: ARDS ventilation → Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, Pplat ≤30 cm H₂O (may need Vt <6 if Pplat still high)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 45-year-old man with severe pneumonia develops ARDS. ABG on FiO₂ 1.0 and PEEP 15 cm H₂O shows PaO₂ 60 mmHg. What is the PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio and ARDS severity?

A. 60, Mild ARDS B. 60, Moderate ARDS C. 60, Severe ARDS D. Cannot calculate without knowing PaCO₂

Answer: C. 60, Severe ARDS

Explanation: - PaO₂/FiO₂ Ratio = PaO₂ / FiO₂ - PaO₂ = 60 mmHg - FiO₂ = 1.0 - PaO₂/FiO₂ = 60 / 1.0 = 60 mmHg

ARDS Severity (Berlin Criteria):

Severity PaO₂/FiO₂ This Patient Mild 200-300 ❌ Moderate 100-200 ❌ Severe <100 ✓ (60 < 100)

This Patient: PaO₂/FiO₂ = 60 mmHg → Severe ARDS

Implications: - 45% mortality - Consider prone positioning (≥16 hours/day) - Consider neuromuscular blockade (if patient-ventilator dyssynchrony) - May need ECMO if refractory hypoxemia

Key Point: PaO₂/FiO₂ <100 = Severe ARDS (45% mortality)

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A patient on nasal cannula at 3 L/min has SpO₂ 85%. The approximate FiO₂ being delivered is:

A. 24% B. 28% C. 32% D. 36%

Answer: C. 32%

Explanation: - Nasal Cannula FiO₂ = ~21% (room air) + 4% per L/min

Calculation: - 1 L/min → 24% (21 + 4 × 1) - 2 L/min → 28% (21 + 4 × 2) - 3 L/min → 32% (21 + 4 × 3) - 4 L/min → 36% - 5 L/min → 40% - 6 L/min → 44%

This Patient: 3 L/min → 32% FiO₂

Note: These are approximate (actual FiO₂ varies with patient's respiratory pattern)

Next Step for SpO₂ 85%: - Increase O₂ (increase flow rate or switch to higher FiO₂ device) - Target SpO₂ ≥90% (88-92% if COPD)

Key Point: Nasal cannula provides ~4% FiO₂ increase per L/min (1 L = 24%, 2 L = 28%, 3 L = 32%, etc.)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Which of the following indicates NIV failure and need for intubation in a COPD patient?

A. PaCO₂ decreased from 70 to 60 mmHg after 2 hours B. pH increased from 7.28 to 7.32 after 2 hours C. pH decreased from 7.28 to 7.22 after 2 hours D. Respiratory rate decreased from 32 to 26/min

Answer: C. pH decreased from 7.28 to 7.22 after 2 hours

Explanation: - NIV Trial: Reassess after 1-2 hours

NIV Success Criteria (Continue NIV): - ↓ PaCO₂ (by 5-10 mmHg) ✓ (Option A) - ↑ pH toward normal ✓ (Option B) - ↓ Respiratory rate <25-30/min ✓ (Option D) - Improved mental status

NIV Failure Criteria (Intubate): - Worsening acidosis (↓ pH) ✓ (Option C) - ↑ PaCO₂ despite NIV - Persistent tachypnea (RR >35/min) - Hemodynamic instability - Unable to tolerate mask - pH <7.25 (absolute threshold for intubation in most protocols)

This Patient (Option C): - pH decreased from 7.28 → 7.22 (worsening acidosis) - NIV FAILURE → Intubate

Key Point: Worsening pH despite NIV (or pH <7.25) → Intubation needed

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient with ARDS on mechanical ventilation (Vt 6 mL/kg, PEEP 10, FiO₂ 0.8) has PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio of 80 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step to improve oxygenation?

A. Increase tidal volume to 8 mL/kg B. Increase FiO₂ to 1.0 C. Prone positioning D. Decrease PEEP to 5 cm H₂O

Answer: C. Prone positioning

Explanation: - Severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <100 mmHg) - Already on lung-protective ventilation (Vt 6 mL/kg, high PEEP, high FiO₂)

Prone Positioning: - Indication: Severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <150 mmHg, especially <100) - Duration: ≥16 hours/day - Mechanism: Improves V/Q matching (recruits dorsal lung), ↑ PaO₂ - Evidence: PROSEVA Trial - ↓ Mortality (32% → 16%)

Why NOT Other Options: - ↑ Vt to 8 mL/kg (A): Violates lung-protective strategy (should be 6 mL/kg) → ↑ VILI risk - ↑ FiO₂ to 1.0 (B): Already at 0.8 (high), further ↑ → ↑ Oxygen toxicity risk, minimal benefit - ↓ PEEP (D): Worsens oxygenation (alveolar collapse)

Other Options for Refractory Hypoxemia: - Neuromuscular blockade (if patient-ventilator dyssynchrony) - ECMO (if PaO₂/FiO₂ <50-80 despite maximal support)

Key Point: Severe ARDS (PaO₂/FiO₂ <150) → Prone positioning (≥16 hrs/day) reduces mortality

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 55-year-old woman with pneumonia has ABG on room air: pH 7.48, PaCO₂ 30 mmHg, PaO₂ 55 mmHg. What type of respiratory failure is this?

A. Type I (Hypoxemic) respiratory failure B. Type II (Hypercapnic) respiratory failure C. Mixed Type I and Type II D. No respiratory failure

Answer: A. Type I (Hypoxemic) respiratory failure

Explanation: - ABG Analysis: - pH 7.48 (Alkalemia) - PaCO₂ 30 mmHg (Hypocapnia, LOW) - PaO₂ 55 mmHg (Hypoxemia, <60 mmHg)

Interpretation: - Type I Respiratory Failure: PaO₂ <60 mmHg ✓ - PaCO₂ Normal or ↓ ✓ (30 mmHg, hyperventilation in response to hypoxemia) - Respiratory alkalosis (↑ pH, ↓ PaCO₂)

Mechanism: - Pneumonia → V/Q mismatch, shunt → Hypoxemia - Hypoxemia → Stimulates breathing → Hyperventilation → ↓ PaCO₂

Type II would have: PaCO₂ >45 mmHg (NOT present here)

Management: - Oxygen therapy (correct hypoxemia) - Treat pneumonia (antibiotics)

Key Point: PaO₂ <60 + PaCO₂ normal/low = Type I respiratory failure (hypoxemic)

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
What is the most important intervention to prevent ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP)?

A. Prophylactic antibiotics B. Daily sedation vacation and assessment for extubation C. Supine positioning D. Nasogastric tube placement

Answer: B. Daily sedation vacation and assessment for extubation

Explanation: - VAP Prevention:

Intervention Evidence Impact Elevate head of bed 30-45° Strong ↓ Aspiration Daily sedation vacation + SBT Strong ↓ Duration of ventilation → ↓ VAP Oral care with chlorhexidine Moderate ↓ Oropharyngeal colonization Subglottic suctioning Moderate Remove secretions above cuff Avoid unnecessary re-intubation Strong ↓ Trauma, aspiration

Daily Sedation Vacation + Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SBT): - Most important → Shortens duration of mechanical ventilation - Shorter ventilation → ↓ VAP risk (VAP incidence ~1-3% per day of ventilation)

Why NOT Other Options: - Prophylactic antibiotics (A): NOT recommended (↑ resistance, no benefit) - Supine positioning (C): Increases VAP risk (should elevate head 30-45°) - NGT (D): May ↑ aspiration risk (if not needed, avoid)

Key Point: Daily sedation vacation + SBT → Shortest duration of ventilation → ↓ VAP

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A mechanically ventilated patient suddenly develops hypotension, tachycardia, and unilateral decreased breath sounds. Peak inspiratory pressure has increased from 25 to 40 cm H₂O. What is the most likely complication?

A. Pneumothorax B. Mucus plugging C. Ventilator-associated pneumonia D. Auto-PEEP

Answer: A. Pneumothorax

Explanation: - Classic Presentation of Tension Pneumothorax: - Sudden hypotension, tachycardia - Unilateral ↓ breath sounds - ↑ Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP)

Mechanism: Air accumulates in pleural space → Lung collapse → ↑ Airway resistance → ↑ PIP → Mediastinal shift → ↓ Venous return → Hypotension

Diagnosis: - Clinical (don't wait for CXR if unstable) - CXR: Absent lung markings, mediastinal shift

Management: - Emergency needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) - Chest tube insertion

Why NOT Other Options: - Mucus plugging (B): ↑ PIP, but bilateral ↓ breath sounds, no hypotension - VAP (C): Gradual onset (>48 hours), fever, infiltrate, NOT sudden - Auto-PEEP (D): ↓ CO possible, but NOT unilateral ↓ breath sounds

Differentiating High PIP Causes:

Cause PIP Plateau Pressure Unilateral Signs Pneumothorax ↑ ↑ Yes (↓ breath sounds on affected side) Mucus plug / bronchospasm ↑ Normal/mildly ↑ No (bilateral) ↓ Lung compliance (ARDS, edema) ↑ ↑ No (bilateral) Auto-PEEP ↑ ↑ No (bilateral)

Key Point: Sudden hypotension + ↑ PIP + Unilateral ↓ breath sounds on ventilator = Pneumothorax (emergency)

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 60-year-old man with COPD is on home oxygen. Which of the following indicates he needs long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT)?

A. PaO₂ 60 mmHg B. PaO₂ 57 mmHg with cor pulmonale C. SpO₂ 94% at rest D. PaO₂ 70 mmHg during exercise

Answer: B. PaO₂ 57 mmHg with cor pulmonale

Explanation: - Long-Term Oxygen Therapy (LTOT) Indications:

Criterion Evidence PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg at rest Absolute indication PaO₂ 56-59 mmHg + Cor pulmonale OR Polycythemia (Hct >55%) Absolute indication SpO₂ ≤88% at rest Corresponds to PaO₂ ~55 mmHg

Evidence: NOTT Trial (Nocturnal Oxygen Therapy Trial) - LTOT (≥15 hrs/day) ↑ survival in COPD with chronic hypoxemia - ONLY intervention (besides smoking cessation) that ↑ survival in COPD

Duration: ≥15 hours/day (preferably 24 hours)

This Patient: - PaO₂ 57 mmHg (56-59 range) + Cor pulmonale → Indication for LTOT ✓

Why NOT Other Options: - PaO₂ 60 mmHg (A): Above threshold (>59), no indication unless cor pulmonale/polycythemia - SpO₂ 94% (C): Normal, no indication - PaO₂ 70 mmHg during exercise (D): Exercise desaturation alone NOT indication for continuous LTOT (may use PRN during exercise)

Key Point: LTOT indicated if PaO₂ ≤55 mmHg OR PaO₂ 56-59 + Cor pulmonale/Polycythemia

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
Respiratory Failure is inability to maintain adequate gas exchange: - Type I (Hypoxemic): PaO₂ <60, PaCO₂ normal/low → V/Q mismatch, Shunt - Type II (Hypercapnic): PaCO₂ >45 → Hypoventilation

ARDS (Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome): - Berlin Criteria: Bilateral opacities, within 1 week, PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤300, NOT cardiac - Causes: Pneumonia (pulmonary), Sepsis (extrapulmonary) - Treatment: Lung-protective ventilation (Vt 6 mL/kg, Pplat ≤30, PEEP 5-15), Prone positioning (if severe), Conservative fluids

Oxygen Therapy: - Target SpO₂ 94-98% (88-92% if COPD) - Devices: Nasal cannula (24-44%), Venturi (precise, for COPD), Non-rebreather (60-90%)

NIV (BiPAP): - Indications: COPD exacerbation, Cardiogenic pulmonary edema - Contraindications: Cardiac arrest, unable to protect airway

Mechanical Ventilation: - Indications: Severe hypoxemia, severe acidosis, impending arrest - Complications: Pneumothorax, VAP, VILI, oxygen toxicity

Prognosis: Varies by etiology (ARDS 30-45% mortality)

END OF CHAPTER

🎉 WEEK 1 COMPLETE! 🎉

Topics Completed (17/17): 1. Arrhythmias 2. Hypertension 3. Infective Endocarditis 4. Rheumatic Heart Disease 5. Valvular Heart Disease 6. Cardiomyopathies 7. Pericardial Diseases 8. Asthma 9. COPD 10. Pneumonia 11. Tuberculosis 12. Interstitial Lung Disease 13. Bronchiectasis 14. Lung Cancer 15. Pleural Effusion 16. Pulmonary Embolism 17. Respiratory Failure ✅

Next Phase: Week 2 - Medicine GI & Nephrology (18 topics)

Cross-References: - COPD (Medicine/12_COPD.md) - Asthma (Medicine/11_Asthma.md) - Pneumonia (Medicine/13_Pneumonia.md) - ARDS is covered in detail within this chapter

docs.title: GERD & PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE
docs.text: Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (⅘ - High-Yield for NEET PG) Estimated Study Time: 6-7 hours Last Updated: 2026

docs.title: TABLE OF CONTENTS
docs.text:
1. Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)
  2. Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD)
  3. Helicobacter pylori Infection
  4. Complications
  5. Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome
  6. NEET PG High-Yield Points
  7. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
GERD: Chronic condition where gastric contents reflux into the esophagus, causing symptoms and/or complications

Physiologic Reflux: Brief episodes (<1 hour/day total), no symptoms Pathologic Reflux (GERD): Frequent/prolonged episodes causing symptoms/mucosal injury

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Prevalence: 10-20% of Western populations
  • Age: Can occur at any age, increases with age
  • Sex: Male = Female (esophagitis more common in men)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Normal Antireflux Mechanisms: 1. Lower Esophageal Sphincter (LES): Tonically contracted (10-30 mmHg) 2. Diaphragmatic crura: External compression 3. Angle of His: Acute angle at gastroesophageal junction 4. Esophageal clearance: Peristalsis, salivary bicarbonate 5. Mucosal resistance: Mucus, bicarbonate secretion, blood flow

Mechanisms of GERD:

PRIMARY MECHANISM
    ↓
TRANSIENT LES RELAXATIONS (TLESRs) - MOST COMMON (60-70%)
• Inappropriate relaxation NOT triggered by swallowing
• Vagally mediated
• Triggered by: Gastric distension, fatty meals
    ↓
REFLUX OF GASTRIC CONTENTS
• Acid, Pepsin, Bile (if duodenogastric reflux)
    ↓
ESOPHAGEAL MUCOSAL INJURY
• ↓ if: Prolonged contact time, ↓ esophageal clearance
    ↓
SYMPTOMS & COMPLICATIONS

Contributing Factors: - ↓ LES pressure: Smoking, alcohol, caffeine, chocolate, peppermint, calcium channel blockers, nitrates - Delayed gastric emptying: Gastroparesis - Hiatal hernia: Disrupts LES function - ↑ Intra-abdominal pressure: Obesity, pregnancy, tight clothing - Esophageal clearance defect: Scleroderma

docs.title: Typical Symptoms
docs.text:
Symptom Frequency Characteristics Heartburn 75-90% - MOST COMMON Burning retrosternal discomfort, worse after meals, lying down, bending Regurgitation 60% Effortless return of gastric contents to mouth (sour/bitter taste) Dysphagia 30% Suggests stricture or severe esophagitis

Heartburn: - Postprandial (30 min - 2 hours after meals) - Worse: Lying down, bending forward, large meals, fatty/spicy foods - Relief: Antacids, sitting upright

docs.title: Atypical/Extraesophageal Symptoms
docs.text:
Symptom Mechanism Chronic cough Microaspiration, vagal reflex Asthma (Reflux-induced) Microaspiration → bronchospasm Laryngitis (Hoarseness) Acid contact with larynx Dental erosions Acid exposure Globus sensation "Lump in throat" feeling Chest pain (non-cardiac) Esophageal spasm, acid sensitivity

Alarm Features (Suggest complications or malignancy): - Dysphagia (especially progressive) - Odynophagia (painful swallowing) - Unintentional weight loss (>5% body weight) - GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena) - Anemia - Persistent vomiting - Age >50 years with new-onset symptoms

docs.title: Clinical Diagnosis
docs.text: Typical GERD (Heartburn + Regurgitation): - Empiric PPI trial (4-8 weeks) → If symptoms resolve → Diagnosis confirmed - NO need for endoscopy if: - Age <50 years - No alarm features - Typical symptoms

docs.title: Endoscopy (Upper GI Endoscopy/EGD)
docs.text:
Indications: - Alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, anemia) - Age ≥50 years with new-onset GERD - Refractory symptoms (not responding to PPI) - Long-standing GERD (>5-10 years) → Screen for Barrett's esophagus - Severe symptoms suggesting erosive esophagitis

Findings: - Normal (60-70%) = Non-Erosive Reflux Disease (NERD) - Erosive esophagitis (30-40%) - Barrett's esophagus (10-15% of chronic GERD) - Stricture (<5%)

Los Angeles Classification (Erosive Esophagitis):

Grade Description A ≥1 mucosal break ≤5 mm, not extending between mucosal folds B ≥1 mucosal break >5 mm, not extending between mucosal folds C Mucosal breaks continuous between ≥2 mucosal folds, involving <75% of circumference D Mucosal breaks involving ≥75% of circumference

docs.title: 24-Hour pH Monitoring
docs.text:
Indications: - Diagnosis unclear (atypical symptoms, normal endoscopy) - PPI-refractory symptoms (to confirm acid reflux) - Pre-operative evaluation (before antireflux surgery)

Technique: - Wireless Bravo capsule (preferred, more comfortable) - Transnasal catheter (conventional)

Performed OFF PPI (stop 7 days before)

Interpretation: - DeMeester Score >14.7 OR - pH <4 for >4% of time → Abnormal reflux

docs.title: Impedance-pH Monitoring
docs.text: Advantage: Detects non-acid reflux (weakly acidic, alkaline) - Useful if patient on PPI (pH monitoring alone may miss non-acid reflux)

docs.title: Esophageal Manometry
docs.text:
NOT for GERD diagnosis (reflux is NOT a motility disorder)

Indications: - Pre-operative (before fundoplication) → Rule out achalasia, scleroderma - Dysphagia with normal endoscopy → Rule out motility disorders

docs.title: Lifestyle Modifications
docs.text:
Dietary: - Avoid: Fatty foods, chocolate, peppermint, caffeine, alcohol, citrus, tomatoes, spicy foods - Small, frequent meals (avoid large meals) - Avoid eating 2-3 hours before bedtime

Positional: - Elevate head of bed (6-8 inches) - Use blocks, NOT extra pillows - Left lateral sleeping position

Other: - Weight loss (if BMI >25) - Most effective - Smoking cessation - Avoid tight clothing

Medications to Avoid (↓ LES pressure): - Calcium channel blockers, Nitrates, Theophylline, Anticholinergics, Benzodiazepines

docs.title: 1. Antacids
docs.text:
Mechanism: Neutralize gastric acid

Examples: Calcium carbonate, Magnesium hydroxide, Aluminum hydroxide

Use: On-demand for mild/intermittent symptoms

Limitations: Short duration (1-2 hours), not for chronic GERD

docs.title: 2. H2-Receptor Antagonists (H2RAs)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Block histamine-stimulated acid secretion

Examples: - Ranitidine (withdrawn 2020 due to NDMA contamination) - Famotidine 20-40 mg BID - Cimetidine, Nizatidine

Efficacy: ↓ Symptoms in mild GERD, inferior to PPIs

Use: Mild/intermittent GERD, on-demand, or add to PPI (bedtime dose if nocturnal symptoms)

Tachyphylaxis: Develops within 2-4 weeks (↓ efficacy)

docs.title: 3. Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs) - FIRST-LINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Irreversibly inhibit H⁺/K⁺-ATPase (proton pump) in parietal cells

Examples:

Drug Dose Notes Omeprazole 20-40 mg OD First PPI Esomeprazole 20-40 mg OD S-isomer of omeprazole Pantoprazole 40 mg OD IV formulation available Lansoprazole 30 mg OD Rabeprazole 20 mg OD Faster onset

Dosing: - Standard: Once daily, 30 minutes before breakfast - Severe: BID dosing (before breakfast and dinner)

Efficacy: - Symptom relief: 80-90% - Erosive esophagitis healing: 80-90% at 8 weeks

Duration: - Mild-Moderate: 4-8 weeks - Erosive esophagitis: 8-12 weeks - Maintenance: Lowest effective dose (on-demand or daily)

PPI-Refractory GERD (15-30% of patients): - Optimize: Take 30 min before meals, ensure compliance - Increase dose: BID dosing - Switch PPI (different PPI may work) - Add H2RA at bedtime (nocturnal acid breakthrough) - Re-evaluate: 24-hr pH monitoring (confirm acid reflux), endoscopy (rule out other causes)

Side Effects (Long-term use): - Common: Headache, diarrhea, nausea - Rare but important: - ↑ Risk of fractures (hip, spine, wrist) - ↓ Calcium absorption - Hypomagnesemia (chronic use >1 year) - ↑ Risk of C. difficile infection - Interstitial nephritis - Fundic gland polyps (benign) - ↑ Risk of community-acquired pneumonia (controversial)

Contraindication: Co-administration with clopidogrel (↓ clopidogrel activation, especially omeprazole/esomeprazole)

docs.title: 4. Prokinetics
docs.text:
Mechanism: ↑ LES pressure, ↑ esophageal clearance, ↑ gastric emptying

Examples: - Metoclopramide 10 mg TID (before meals) - Domperidone (not available in US)

Efficacy: Limited (minimal benefit over PPI alone)

Use: Adjunct if gastroparesis component

Side Effects: - Metoclopramide: Tardive dyskinesia (limit use to <12 weeks), drowsiness, dystonia

Not Recommended as monotherapy

docs.title: 5. Baclofen
docs.text:
Mechanism: GABA-B agonist → ↓ TLESRs

Use: Refractory GERD, especially with regurgitation

Dose: 5-20 mg TID

Side Effects: Drowsiness, dizziness, weakness

docs.title: Surgical Treatment (Fundoplication)
docs.text:
Indications: - Severe symptoms despite maximal medical therapy - Patient preference (young patients not wanting lifelong PPI) - Large hiatal hernia with symptoms - Complications (stricture, Barrett's with dysplasia)

Contraindications: - Achalasia, scleroderma (esophageal dysmotility) - Poor surgical candidate

Procedure:

Nissen Fundoplication (360° wrap): - Gastric fundus wrapped completely around lower esophagus - Laparoscopic (preferred) or open

Efficacy: 85-90% symptom control at 5 years

Complications: - Dysphagia (10-20%, usually temporary) - Gas-bloat syndrome (difficulty belching/vomiting) - Diarrhea (vagal injury) - Wrap failure (5-10% long-term)

Alternatives: Partial fundoplication (Toupet 270°, Dor 180°) - ↓ Dysphagia, but ↑ recurrence

docs.title: Endoscopic Therapies
docs.text:
Stretta Procedure: Radiofrequency ablation of LES

Transoral Incisionless Fundoplication (TIF): Endoscopic fundoplication

Efficacy: Less than surgical fundoplication, NOT widely adopted

docs.title: 1. Esophageal Stricture
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: Chronic inflammation → Fibrosis → Narrowing

Clinical: Progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids)

Diagnosis: Endoscopy (smooth, tapered narrowing)

Treatment: - Endoscopic dilation (bougie or balloon) - High-dose PPI (to prevent recurrence) - May need repeated dilations

docs.title: 2. Barrett's Esophagus
docs.text:
Definition: Replacement of normal squamous epithelium with intestinal metaplasia (columnar epithelium with goblet cells)

Epidemiology: - 10-15% of chronic GERD patients - Male > Female (3:1) - Caucasian, obesity, smoking

Diagnosis: Endoscopy + Biopsy - Endoscopy: Salmon-pink mucosa extending ≥1 cm above gastroesophageal junction - Biopsy: Intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells on histology) - REQUIRED for diagnosis

Seattle Protocol: Biopsies every 1-2 cm in 4 quadrants

Cancer Risk: 0.5% per year progression to esophageal adenocarcinoma

Surveillance:

Dysplasia Grade Surveillance Interval No dysplasia Every 3-5 years Low-grade dysplasia (LGD) Every 6-12 months (or ablation) High-grade dysplasia (HGD) Endoscopic ablation OR Surveillance every 3 months

Treatment: - PPI therapy (does NOT reverse Barrett's, but may ↓ progression) - HGD/Early cancer: Endoscopic ablation (Radiofrequency ablation - RFA) ± Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR)

docs.title: 3. Esophagitis
docs.text:
See Los Angeles classification above

Treatment: PPI × 8-12 weeks

docs.title: 4. Respiratory Complications
docs.text:
• Chronic cough
  • Asthma exacerbation
  • Recurrent pneumonia (aspiration)
  • Laryngitis

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Peptic Ulcer: Mucosal break (≥5 mm) extending through muscularis mucosae

Locations: - Duodenal ulcer (DU): Most common (70%) - Gastric ulcer (GU): 25% - Both: 5%

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Prevalence: 5-10% lifetime risk
  • Declining due to ↓ H. pylori prevalence, ↑ PPI use
  • Age: Can occur at any age, peak 55-65 years

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
PEPTIC ULCER DISEASE CAUSES
│
├─ H. PYLORI INFECTION (50-70% of DU, 30-50% of GU)
│  • MOST COMMON cause overall
│  • Prevalence: 50% in developing countries, 20% in developed
│
├─ NSAIDs (20-30%)
│  • Aspirin, Ibuprofen, Naproxen, COX-2 inhibitors
│  • Dose-dependent risk
│  • Risk factors: Age >65, history of PUD/GI bleed, steroids, anticoagulants
│
├─ STRESS ULCERS (ICU patients)
│  • Mechanical ventilation >48 hours
│  • Coagulopathy
│  • Burns (Curling ulcer), Head trauma (Cushing ulcer)
│
├─ ZOLLINGER-ELLISON SYNDROME (Gastrinoma) - Rare (<1%)
│  • Multiple/refractory ulcers, diarrhea
│
└─ OTHER (Rare)
   • Crohn's disease
   • Infection (CMV, HSV - immunocompromised)
   • Malignancy (lymphoma, adenocarcinoma)
   • Cocaine use

Mnemonic: "HANZ" for PUD Causes: - H - H. pylori - A - Aspirin/NSAIDs - N - ↑ (N) acid states (Zollinger-Ellison) - Z - Zollinger-Ellison syndrome

Idiopathic (10-20%): No H. pylori, no NSAIDs (likely undetected NSAID use or false-negative H. pylori tests)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Balance: Aggressive factors (Acid, Pepsin, H. pylori, NSAIDs) vs Protective factors (Mucus, Bicarbonate, Blood flow, Prostaglandins)

Ulcer Formation: Imbalance → Mucosal injury

docs.title: H. pylori Mechanism
docs.text:
H. PYLORI COLONIZATION (Gastric antrum, duodenum)
    ↓
VIRULENCE FACTORS
• Urease (produces NH₃ → ↑ pH, allows survival)
• CagA, VacA toxins (mucosal damage)
    ↓
INFLAMMATORY RESPONSE
• Neutrophil infiltration, cytokines (IL-8, TNF-α)
    ↓
MUCOSAL INJURY
    ↓
DUODENAL ULCER (70% have H. pylori)
• ↑ Gastric acid secretion (antral gastritis → ↑ Gastrin)
• Gastric metaplasia in duodenum → H. pylori colonizes → ulcer
    ↓
GASTRIC ULCER (50% have H. pylori)
• Corpus gastritis → ↓ Acid secretion (but still enough for ulcer)
• Direct mucosal injury

docs.title: NSAID Mechanism
docs.text:
NSAIDs → Inhibit COX-1 → ↓ Prostaglandins → ↓ Mucus, ↓ Bicarbonate, ↓ Blood flow → Mucosal injury

COX-2 Selective Inhibitors (Celecoxib): ↓ GI risk vs non-selective NSAIDs, but NOT zero risk

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
Feature Duodenal Ulcer Gastric Ulcer Epigastric pain MOST COMMON (80-90%) MOST COMMON (70-80%) Timing 2-5 hours AFTER meals, Nocturnal (midnight-3 AM) Within 1 hour AFTER meals (food worsens) Relief Food, Antacids Variable (may worsen with food) Weight Unchanged or ↑ (eat more to relieve pain) ↓ Weight loss (avoid food due to pain)

Classic DU Pain Pattern: "Burning hunger pain" 2-5 hours after meals → Relieved by food → Recurs

Classic GU Pain Pattern: Pain shortly after eating → Fear of eating → Weight loss

Asymptomatic: 20-30% (especially elderly, NSAID users)

docs.title: Physical Examination
docs.text:
• Epigastric tenderness (50-70%)
  • Usually non-specific
  • Peritoneal signs (rigidity, rebound) → Perforation

docs.title: Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
docs.text:
GOLD STANDARD

Indications: - Suspected PUD - Alarm features (age >50, weight loss, dysphagia, bleeding, anemia) - Persistent symptoms despite treatment

Findings: - Duodenal ulcer: Usually D1 (bulb), round/oval, clean base (if healed) - Gastric ulcer: Lesser curvature (most common), biopsy REQUIRED (rule out malignancy)

Gastric Ulcer Biopsy: - ALWAYS biopsy gastric ulcers (5% are malignant - adenocarcinoma) - Repeat endoscopy at 8-12 weeks after treatment (confirm healing, rule out malignancy)

Duodenal Ulcer Biopsy: NOT required (malignancy rare)

docs.title: H. pylori Testing
docs.text:
Test for ALL patients with PUD

Methods:

Test Sensitivity Specificity Notes Rapid Urease Test (RUT) (Biopsy-based) 90-95% 95-100% During endoscopy, results in minutes, cheap Histology (Biopsy-based) 95-99% 95-99% GOLD STANDARD, H&E stain or special stains Urea Breath Test (UBT) (Non-invasive) 95-98% 95-98% Best non-invasive, check eradication Stool Antigen Test (Non-invasive) 90-95% 90-95% Alternative to UBT Serology (IgG antibody) (Non-invasive) 85-92% 79-90% NOT for active infection (stays + after eradication), NOT for test-of-cure

False Negatives (all except serology): - PPI use (stop 2 weeks before), Antibiotics (stop 4 weeks), Bismuth (stop 4 weeks) - Recent bleeding (RUT may be false negative)

Test-of-Cure (Confirm eradication): - When: ≥4 weeks after treatment - Which test: Urea Breath Test OR Stool Antigen (NOT serology) - Indications: ALL patients (especially if bleeding ulcer, persistent symptoms)

docs.title: Barium Upper GI Series
docs.text:
NOT first-line (replaced by endoscopy)

Finding: "Crater" or "Niche" sign (barium-filled ulcer)

Limitation: Cannot biopsy, less sensitive than endoscopy

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text:
1. Eradicate H. pylori (if positive)
  2. Stop NSAIDs (if possible)
  3. Acid suppression (PPI)
  4. Lifestyle modifications (avoid smoking, alcohol, NSAIDs)

docs.title: H. pylori Eradication
docs.text: Goal: Cure ulcer (↓ recurrence from 60-70% → <5%), ↓ bleeding risk, ↓ cancer risk

docs.title: First-Line Regimens
docs.text:
1. Clarithromycin-Based Triple Therapy (Standard, but ↓ efficacy due to resistance):

PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin × 14 days - PPI (Omeprazole 20 mg or equivalent) BID - Clarithromycin 500 mg BID - Amoxicillin 1 g BID

Efficacy: 70-85% (↓ if clarithromycin resistance >15%)

Use ONLY if: Low clarithromycin resistance area (<15%) AND no prior macrolide exposure

2. Bismuth Quadruple Therapy (Preferred if high clarithromycin resistance):

PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole × 10-14 days - PPI BID - Bismuth subsalicylate 525 mg QID OR Bismuth subcitrate 120 mg QID - Tetracycline 500 mg QID - Metronidazole 500 mg TID (or 250 mg QID)

Efficacy: 90-95% (High, NOT affected by clarithromycin resistance)

Single-capsule formulation: Pylera (Bismuth + Metronidazole + Tetracycline) - 3 capsules QID × 10 days + PPI

3. Concomitant Therapy (Alternative):

PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin + Metronidazole × 10-14 days

Efficacy: 85-90%

docs.title: Second-Line Regimens (If first-line fails)
docs.text:
Levofloxacin-Based Triple Therapy:

PPI + Levofloxacin + Amoxicillin × 10-14 days - PPI BID - Levofloxacin 500 mg OD - Amoxicillin 1 g BID

Efficacy: 75-90%

Alternative: Bismuth Quadruple (if not used first-line)

docs.title: Test-of-Cure
docs.text:
ALWAYS perform ≥4 weeks after treatment completion

Method: Urea Breath Test OR Stool Antigen

If Still Positive → Repeat treatment with different regimen + Check compliance

docs.title: Acid Suppression (PPI)
docs.text:
Duration:

Indication Duration Duodenal ulcer (uncomplicated) 4 weeks Gastric ulcer (uncomplicated) 8 weeks Complicated ulcer (bleeding, perforation) 8-12 weeks NSAID-associated (if continuing NSAID) Chronic PPI (as long as NSAID continued) Giant ulcer (>2 cm) 12 weeks

Dose: Standard (Omeprazole 20 mg OD or equivalent)

Follow-up Endoscopy: - Gastric ulcer: ALWAYS repeat at 8-12 weeks (confirm healing, rule out malignancy) - Duodenal ulcer: NOT needed (unless persistent symptoms)

docs.title: NSAID Management
docs.text:
If NSAID-induced ulcer:

  1. Stop NSAID (if possible) - MOST IMPORTANT
  2. Switch to acetaminophen, topical NSAIDs

  3. If pain control needed: COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) + PPI

  4. If NSAID must continue:

  5. PPI (standard dose) - MOST EFFECTIVE prophylaxis
  6. Alternative: Misoprostol 200 mcg QID (prostaglandin analog) - Side effects: Diarrhea, cramping

Prevention (High-risk patients needing chronic NSAID): - High risk: Age >65, prior PUD/GI bleed, concurrent steroids/anticoagulants - Strategy: COX-2 inhibitor + PPI (best protection)

docs.title: Refractory Ulcers
docs.text:
Definition: Ulcer not healed after 8-12 weeks of PPI

Causes: - Non-compliance (MOST COMMON) - Persistent H. pylori (inadequate eradication) - Continued NSAID use (often surreptitious) - Smoking - Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (if multiple/refractory ulcers) - Gastric cancer (if gastric ulcer) - Crohn's disease

Workup: - Repeat endoscopy with biopsy (rule out malignancy) - Test H. pylori status (urea breath test or biopsy) - Assess compliance, NSAID use, smoking - Fasting gastrin level (if ZES suspected)

docs.title: Microbiology
docs.text:
• Gram-negative, spiral, flagellated bacterium
  • Urease-positive (key for survival in acidic environment)

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Global prevalence: 50% (higher in developing countries ~70-90%, lower in developed ~20-30%)
  • Acquisition: Childhood (fecal-oral, oral-oral transmission)
  • Persistence: Lifelong if untreated

docs.title: Associated Diseases
docs.text: Disease Association Chronic gastritis 100% of infected Duodenal ulcer 70% have H. pylori Gastric ulcer 50% have H. pylori Gastric adenocarcinoma 70-90% have H. pylori (WHO Class I carcinogen) MALT lymphoma >90% have H. pylori (eradication can cure early-stage) Functional dyspepsia 50% (controversial benefit of eradication)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: See table above (Rapid urease, Histology, Urea breath test, Stool antigen, Serology)

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text: See H. pylori eradication section above

docs.title: Indications for H. pylori Testing and Treatment
docs.text:
Test and Treat: - Peptic ulcer disease (active or history) - Gastric MALT lymphoma - Early gastric cancer (post-resection) - Unexplained iron deficiency anemia - Chronic idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) - First-degree relatives of gastric cancer patients - Uninvestigated dyspepsia (age <60, no alarm features) - "Test and treat" strategy

Do NOT Test (No benefit): - Asymptomatic individuals (unless high gastric cancer risk - family history) - GERD (no clear benefit, may worsen symptoms in some)

docs.title: 1. Upper GI Bleeding
docs.text:
MOST COMMON complication (15-20% of PUD)

See GI Bleeding chapter for detailed management

Presentation: - Hematemesis (fresh blood or "coffee-ground") - Melena (black, tarry stools) - Hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum - if massive bleeding)

Management: 1. Resuscitation: IV fluids, blood transfusion (target Hgb 7-9 g/dL) 2. PPI: High-dose IV (Pantoprazole 80 mg bolus → 8 mg/hr infusion × 72 hours) 3. Endoscopy: Within 24 hours (preferably 12-24 hours) - Forrest Classification (predicts rebleeding risk) - Endoscopic therapy: Injection (epinephrine), thermal (heater probe, APC), clips 4. Post-endoscopy: PPI BID × 2 weeks → Daily 5. H. pylori eradication (if positive)

Forrest Classification:

Class Description Rebleeding Risk Ia Spurting arterial bleeding 90% Ib Oozing bleeding 50% IIa Visible vessel (non-bleeding) 50% IIb Adherent clot 25-30% IIc Flat pigmented spot 10% III Clean base (no stigmata) 5%

Endoscopic Therapy: Ia, Ib, IIa, IIb

Rebleeding: 10-20% (risk factors: age >60, shock, Hgb <8, Forrest Ia/Ib/IIa)

Angiography: If rebleeding despite endoscopic therapy (embolization)

Surgery: If failed endoscopic + angiographic therapy (oversewing ulcer, vagotomy/pyloroplasty)

docs.title: 2. Perforation
docs.text:
2nd most common complication (5-10%)

Presentation: - Sudden, severe abdominal pain - Peritoneal signs (board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness) - Free air under diaphragm on upright CXR (70-80%)

Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate more commonly (free perforation) Posterior duodenal ulcers bleed more commonly (erode into gastroduodenal artery)

Diagnosis: - Upright CXR: Pneumoperitoneum (free air under diaphragm) - CT abdomen: More sensitive (if CXR negative)

Management: 1. Resuscitation: IV fluids, NPO, NG tube decompression 2. Antibiotics: Broad-spectrum (Pip-Tazo or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole) 3. Surgery: Definitive treatment - Graham patch (omental patch closure) - Biopsy ulcer edge (rule out malignancy) - Vagotomy rarely done now (PPI era) 4. Post-op: High-dose PPI × 8-12 weeks, H. pylori eradication

Non-operative management (selected cases): - Small perforation, hemodynamically stable, no peritonitis - IV antibiotics, PPI, NPO - Close monitoring (if deteriorates → surgery)

docs.title: 3. Gastric Outlet Obstruction (GOO)
docs.text:
Rare (<5%)

Causes: - Pyloric channel/duodenal ulcer → Edema, scarring, fibrosis - Gastric cancer (must rule out)

Presentation: - Nausea, vomiting (large volume, food from prior day) - Early satiety, bloating - Weight loss - Succussion splash (sloshing sound on shaking abdomen)

Diagnosis: - Endoscopy: Narrowing at pylorus/duodenum, cannot pass scope - Barium study: Dilated stomach, delayed gastric emptying - Saline load test: Give 750 mL saline via NG → Aspirate after 30 min → >400 mL retained = GOO

Management: 1. Acute edema (recent ulcer): - NG decompression - High-dose PPI IV (Pantoprazole 80 mg BID) - Hydration, Electrolyte correction (hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis) - Trial 72-96 hours → If improves, continue medical Rx 2. Chronic scarring (fibrosis): - Endoscopic balloon dilation (may need repeated) - Surgery: Vagotomy + Antrectomy (with Billroth I or II), or Vagotomy + Gastrojejunostomy

Complications (Post-surgery): - Dumping syndrome: Rapid gastric emptying → Hypoglycemia, diarrhea - Afferent loop syndrome: Bile reflux - Alkaline reflux gastritis

docs.title: 4. Penetration
docs.text:
Ulcer erodes into adjacent organ (pancreas most common)

Presentation: - Change in pain pattern: Constant, radiates to back (pancreatic penetration) - ↑ Amylase/Lipase (pancreatitis)

Diagnosis: CT/MRI (ulcer extending beyond gastric/duodenal wall)

Management: High-dose PPI; Surgery if medical failure

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Gastrinoma: Gastrin-secreting tumor (usually in gastrinoma triangle: Duodenum > Pancreas > Lymph nodes)

Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (ZES): Gastrinoma → Hypergastrinemia → ↑↑ Gastric acid → Severe/multiple PUD, diarrhea

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Rare: <1% of PUD cases
  • Age: 30-50 years
  • MEN-1: 20-30% have gastrinoma

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Classic Triad (not always present): 1. Severe, refractory, or recurrent peptic ulcers 2. Diarrhea (50-70%) - Acid hypersecretion → Fat malabsorption 3. Gastrin-secreting tumor

Suggestive Features: - Multiple ulcers (especially distal duodenum, jejunum - unusual locations) - Giant ulcers (>2 cm) - Refractory to standard PPI therapy - Ulcers + Diarrhea - Family history of MEN-1 (hyperparathyroidism, pituitary adenoma, pancreatic tumor)

docs.title: 1. Fasting Serum Gastrin
docs.text:
When to Test: - Refractory/recurrent ulcers - Multiple ulcers - Ulcer + Diarrhea - Family history of MEN-1

Procedure: STOP PPI ≥1 week before test (PPIs ↑ gastrin)

Interpretation:

Gastrin Level Interpretation <100 pg/mL Normal 100-500 pg/mL Indeterminate (many causes) >1000 pg/mL + Gastric pH <2 Highly suggestive of ZES

Other Causes of Hypergastrinemia: - PPI use (MOST COMMON) - Stop PPI × 1 week - Atrophic gastritis (achlorhydria → ↑ gastrin, but pH >4) - H. pylori gastritis - Chronic kidney disease - Retained gastric antrum (post-surgery)

Key: Gastrin >1000 + Gastric pH <2 (↑ acid) → ZES

docs.title: 2. Gastric pH
docs.text:
Measure during endoscopy

ZES: pH <2 (hyperacidity) Atrophic gastritis: pH >4 (achlorhydria)

docs.title: 3. Secretin Stimulation Test
docs.text:
GOLD STANDARD (if gastrin 100-1000, diagnosis unclear)

Procedure: - Give secretin 2 units/kg IV - Measure gastrin at 0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20 minutes

Interpretation: - ↑ Gastrin >200 pg/mL above baseline → ZES - No change or ↓ gastrin → Not ZES

Mechanism: Secretin stimulates gastrin release in gastrinoma, but inhibits in normal tissue

docs.title: 4. Localization (Once diagnosis confirmed)
docs.text: Imaging: - CT/MRI abdomen: Detect tumor (sensitivity 50-70%) - Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS): More sensitive for small tumors - Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (Octreotide scan): Detect primary and metastases (sensitivity 80-90%) - Selective arterial secretin injection (SASI): Provocative test + angiography

docs.title: Medical (Acid Suppression)
docs.text:
High-dose PPI: - Omeprazole 60-120 mg/day (divided doses) - Goal: Basal acid output <10 mEq/hr (<5 mEq/hr if prior gastric surgery)

Lifelong therapy if tumor unresectable

docs.title: Surgical
docs.text:
Curative: If localized, non-metastatic

Procedure: Tumor resection (enucleation or pancreaticoduodenectomy)

MEN-1 patients: Often multiple tumors → Surgery more complex, may not be curative

Metastatic: Chemotherapy (streptozocin + 5-FU), Somatostatin analogs (octreotide) - ↓ Gastrin, ↓ Tumor growth

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
• Survival: 90% at 5 years (if localized), 50% at 5 years (if metastatic)
  • Malignant: 60-90% (slow-growing)
  • Metastases: Liver (most common)

docs.title: GERD
docs.text:
1. MOST COMMON Cause: Transient LES relaxations (TLESRs) (60-70%)

  2. Symptoms:

  3. Heartburn (MOST COMMON) - Burning retrosternal, worse after meals/lying down

  4. Regurgitation - Effortless return of gastric contents

  5. Diagnosis:

  6. Empiric PPI trial (no endoscopy if <50 years, no alarm features)

  7. Endoscopy: If alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, age ≥50, bleeding)

  8. Treatment:

  9. First-line: PPI (Omeprazole 20 mg OD × 4-8 weeks)
  10. Lifestyle: Elevate head of bed, weight loss, avoid triggers

  11. Surgery (Fundoplication): If refractory or patient preference

  12. Barrett's Esophagus:

  13. Squamous → Intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells on biopsy)
  14. Cancer risk: 0.5% per year → Esophageal adenocarcinoma
  15. Surveillance: No dysplasia (q3-5 years), LGD (q6-12 months), HGD (ablation or q3 months)

docs.title: Peptic Ulcer Disease
docs.text:
1. MOST COMMON Cause: H. pylori (50-70% DU, 30-50% GU)

  2. Duodenal vs Gastric Ulcer:

Feature Duodenal Ulcer Gastric Ulcer Pain timing 2-5 hours AFTER meals Within 1 hour Relief Food, Antacids Variable Weight Unchanged ↓ Weight loss Malignancy Rare (no biopsy needed) 5% malignant (ALWAYS biopsy)
  1. H. pylori Testing:
  2. Invasive: Rapid urease test (RUT), Histology (GOLD STANDARD)
  3. Non-invasive: Urea breath test (BEST non-invasive), Stool antigen
  4. NOT for test-of-cure: Serology (IgG stays positive after eradication)

  5. Test-of-cure: ≥4 weeks after treatment (Urea breath test or Stool antigen)

  6. H. pylori Eradication:

  7. First-line: Bismuth Quadruple (PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole × 10-14 days) - 90-95% efficacy
  8. Alternative: Clarithromycin triple (if low resistance area) - 70-85% efficacy

  9. Test-of-cure: ALWAYS (≥4 weeks after treatment)

  10. PPI Duration:

    • DU: 4 weeks
    • GU: 8 weeks (+ repeat endoscopy to confirm healing, rule out malignancy)

  11. Complications:

    • Bleeding (MOST COMMON, 15-20%): Hematemesis, melena
    • Forrest IIa (visible vessel): 50% rebleeding risk → Endoscopic therapy
    • Perforation: Free air under diaphragm on upright CXR → Surgery (Graham patch)
    • GOO: Succussion splash, vomiting food from prior day

docs.title: Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome
docs.text:
1. Triad (not always present):

    • Severe/multiple/refractory ulcers
    • Diarrhea
    • Gastrinoma

  2. Diagnosis:

    • Fasting gastrin >1000 pg/mL + Gastric pH <2 → Highly suggestive
    • Secretin stimulation test: ↑ Gastrin >200 pg/mL (GOLD STANDARD)

  3. Treatment: High-dose PPI (Omeprazole 60-120 mg/day), Surgery if localized

docs.title: GERD vs PUD
docs.text: Feature GERD PUD Primary symptom Heartburn (retrosternal burning) Epigastric pain Timing Postprandial, lying down DU: 2-5 hrs post-meal; GU: 0-1 hr Relief Antacids, sitting up DU: Food; GU: Variable Diagnosis Empiric PPI trial, Endoscopy if alarm features Endoscopy (GOLD STANDARD) Treatment PPI × 4-8 weeks PPI × 4-8 weeks + H. pylori eradication

docs.title: Duodenal vs Gastric Ulcer
docs.text: Feature Duodenal Ulcer Gastric Ulcer Frequency 70% (MOST COMMON) 25% H. pylori association 70% 50% Pain timing 2-5 hours after meals, Nocturnal Within 1 hour after meals Relief Food, Antacids Variable (may worsen) Weight Unchanged or ↑ ↓ Weight loss Location D1 (bulb) Lesser curvature Biopsy NOT needed (malignancy rare) ALWAYS biopsy (5% malignant) Repeat endoscopy NOT needed ALWAYS at 8-12 weeks (confirm healing)

docs.title: H. pylori Tests
docs.text: Test Type Sensitivity Specificity Use Notes Rapid Urease (RUT) Invasive 90-95% 95-100% During endoscopy Fast, cheap Histology Invasive 95-99% 95-99% GOLD STANDARD Requires biopsy Urea Breath Test Non-invasive 95-98% 95-98% Best non-invasive, Test-of-cure Stop PPI × 2 weeks Stool Antigen Non-invasive 90-95% 90-95% Alternative to UBT Serology (IgG) Non-invasive 85-92% 79-90% NOT for test-of-cure Stays + after eradication

docs.title: PPI
docs.text:
Drug Dose Notes Omeprazole 20-40 mg OD First PPI Esomeprazole 20-40 mg OD S-isomer Pantoprazole 40 mg OD IV available (bleeding)

Dosing: 30 minutes before breakfast

Side Effects (long-term): - ↑ Fracture risk (hip, spine), Hypomagnesemia, C. diff infection - Contraindication: Clopidogrel (especially omeprazole/esomeprazole)

docs.title: Important Cutoffs
docs.text: Parameter Cutoff Significance Gastrin >1000 pg/mL + pH <2 Highly suggestive of ZES Secretin stimulation ↑ Gastrin >200 pg/mL ZES (GOLD STANDARD test) Barrett's progression 0.5% per year Risk of adenocarcinoma Forrest IIa 50% rebleeding Endoscopic therapy needed

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text:
1. Not biopsying gastric ulcers ❌
  2. ALWAYS biopsy gastric ulcers (5% malignant)

  3. Repeat endoscopy at 8-12 weeks (confirm healing)

  4. Using serology for test-of-cure ❌

  5. Serology stays positive after eradication

  6. Use Urea breath test or Stool antigen (≥4 weeks after Rx)

  7. Not stopping PPI before H. pylori testing ❌

  8. False negative if on PPI

  9. Stop 2 weeks before (except serology)

  10. Short antibiotic duration for H. pylori ❌

  11. Need 10-14 days (NOT 7 days)

  12. Not checking gastric pH in hypergastrinemia workup ❌

  13. ZES: Gastrin ↑ + pH <2 (↑ acid)

  14. Atrophic gastritis: Gastrin ↑ + pH >4 (↓ acid)

  15. Starting PPI in young GERD patient without trial ❌

  16. Empiric PPI trial is diagnostic AND therapeutic (no endoscopy if <50, no alarm features)

docs.title: Mnemonics
docs.text:
1. "HANZ" - PUD Causes:
  2. H - H. pylori
  3. A - Aspirin/NSAIDs
  4. N - ↑ (N) acid states

  5. Z - Zollinger-Ellison

  6. "Triple Therapy" - H. pylori (Clarithromycin-based):

  7. P**PI + **C**larithromycin + **A**moxicillin ("**PCA")

  8. "Quadruple Therapy" - H. pylori (Bismuth-based):

  9. P**PI + **B**ismuth + **T**etracycline + **M**etronidazole ("**PBTM")

  10. Forrest Classification (Bleeding ulcer):

  11. I: Active bleeding (Ia = spurting, Ib = oozing)
  12. II: Recent bleeding (IIa = vessel, IIb = clot, IIc = spot)
  13. III: Clean base

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 35-year-old man with epigastric pain has endoscopy showing a duodenal ulcer. Biopsy is positive for H. pylori. What is the most appropriate treatment?

A. PPI alone for 4 weeks B. PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin for 14 days C. PPI + H2RA for 8 weeks D. Surgery (vagotomy)

Answer: B. PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin for 14 days

Explanation: - Diagnosis: H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer

Treatment: - H. pylori eradication + Acid suppression

H. pylori Eradication Regimens: 1. Clarithromycin Triple Therapy (if low resistance): - PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin × 14 days ✓ 2. Bismuth Quadruple (preferred if high resistance): - PPI + Bismuth + Tetracycline + Metronidazole × 10-14 days

This Patient: First-line treatment → Triple or Quadruple therapy acceptable (option B is correct)

Why NOT Other Options: - PPI alone (A): Does NOT eradicate H. pylori → Ulcer recurrence 60-70% - PPI + H2RA (C): No antibiotics for H. pylori - Surgery (D): NOT indicated (medical therapy is effective)

Additional: - PPI duration after eradication: Continue × 4 weeks (DU) or 8 weeks (GU) - Test-of-cure: Urea breath test ≥4 weeks after treatment

Key Point: H. pylori-positive PUD → Eradication (antibiotics) + PPI

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 60-year-old man on chronic NSAIDs for arthritis develops a gastric ulcer. He needs to continue NSAIDs. After ulcer healing, what is the best strategy to prevent recurrence?

A. PPI alone B. H2RA alone C. COX-2 inhibitor + PPI D. Stop NSAIDs permanently

Answer: C. COX-2 inhibitor + PPI

Explanation: - Problem: High-risk patient (age >60, prior ulcer) needing chronic NSAIDs

Prevention Strategy:

Option GI Risk Reduction COX-2 inhibitor alone 50% (vs non-selective NSAID) PPI alone (with non-selective NSAID) 50-70% COX-2 inhibitor + PPI >80% (BEST)

This Patient: COX-2 inhibitor (Celecoxib) + PPI → Maximum protection

Alternatives (if COX-2 not available): - Non-selective NSAID + PPI (next best) - Misoprostol 200 mcg QID (effective but poorly tolerated - diarrhea)

Why NOT Other Options: - PPI alone (A): Good, but combination better (especially high-risk) - H2RA (B): Inferior to PPI for ulcer prevention - Stop NSAIDs (D): Ideal, but patient needs NSAIDs for arthritis

High-Risk Factors: - Age >65 - Prior PUD or GI bleed - Concurrent steroids, anticoagulants, SSRIs - High-dose or multiple NSAIDs

Key Point: High-risk patient needing NSAIDs → COX-2 inhibitor + PPI (best protection)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A patient with GERD has failed 8 weeks of PPI therapy. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Switch to a different PPI B. Increase PPI to BID dosing C. Add H2RA at bedtime D. Repeat endoscopy

Answer: B. Increase PPI to BID dosing

Explanation: - PPI-Refractory GERD (15-30% of patients)

Stepwise Approach:

  1. Optimize current PPI:
  2. Take 30 min before meals (not at bedtime)
  3. Ensure compliance

  4. Increase to BID dosing (before breakfast and dinner) ✓

  5. Switch PPI (if no response to BID):

  6. Different PPI may work (individual variation in metabolism)

  7. Add H2RA at bedtime:

  8. For nocturnal acid breakthrough

  9. (Note: Tachyphylaxis develops in 2-4 weeks)

  10. Re-evaluate diagnosis:

  11. 24-hour pH monitoring (confirm acid reflux)
  12. Endoscopy (if not done, or rule out complications)
  13. Manometry (if dysphagia, rule out achalasia/motility disorder)

This Patient: First step → Increase PPI to BID (before switching or adding drugs)

Why NOT D (Endoscopy) First: - If GERD confirmed and no alarm features → Optimize medical therapy first - Endoscopy if: Alarm features, diagnostic uncertainty, or persistent symptoms despite BID PPI

Other Causes of PPI-Refractory Symptoms: - Non-compliance (MOST COMMON) - Functional heartburn (no acid reflux on pH monitoring) - Eosinophilic esophagitis (dysphagia, food impaction, normal pH monitoring) - Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying)

Key Point: PPI-refractory GERD → First step: Optimize (BID dosing), then consider alternatives

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 55-year-old man has a gastric ulcer on endoscopy. Biopsy shows H. pylori negative. What is the next most important step?

A. Start PPI for 8 weeks B. Detailed NSAID history C. Check fasting gastrin level D. Repeat biopsy for malignancy

Answer: B. Detailed NSAID history

Explanation: - H. pylori-negative gastric ulcer → Investigate other causes

Causes of H. pylori-negative PUD: 1. NSAIDs (MOST COMMON) - Often surreptitious use (aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen) 2. False-negative H. pylori test (PPI use, recent antibiotics, bleeding) 3. Malignancy (adenocarcinoma, lymphoma) 4. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (if multiple/refractory ulcers) 5. Crohn's disease 6. Idiopathic (10-20%)

This Patient: - MOST important: Detailed NSAID history (including over-the-counter, aspirin, topical) - NSAIDs are responsible for most H. pylori-negative ulcers

Additional Steps: - Repeat H. pylori testing (Urea breath test or Stool antigen) - Rule out false negative - Stop PPI × 2 weeks before test - Biopsy (already done) - Rule out malignancy (ALWAYS biopsy gastric ulcers) - If NSAID use → Stop NSAID, PPI × 8 weeks - If NO NSAID and confirmed H. pylori-negative → Consider fasting gastrin (ZES workup)

Why NOT Other Options: - PPI (A): Will treat, but must identify cause first (NSAIDs? Malignancy?) - Gastrin (C): Check if refractory/multiple ulcers, but NSAID history more important first - Repeat biopsy for malignancy (D): Already done at initial endoscopy (ALWAYS biopsy gastric ulcers)

Key Point: H. pylori-negative gastric ulcer → Investigate NSAID use (MOST COMMON cause)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A patient with H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer completed eradication therapy 6 weeks ago. Which test should be used to confirm eradication?

A. Serology (IgG antibody) B. Urea breath test C. Rapid urease test (endoscopic biopsy) D. Stool antigen test

Answer: B. Urea breath test (or D. Stool antigen test - both acceptable)

Explanation: - Test-of-Cure: Confirm H. pylori eradication ≥4 weeks after treatment

Which Test:

Test Use for Test-of-Cure? Reason Urea Breath Test YES (BEST non-invasive) Detects active infection Stool Antigen YES (Alternative) Detects active infection Serology (IgG) NO Stays positive after eradication (takes months-years to ↓) Rapid Urease (RUT) YES, but invasive Requires endoscopy (NOT needed if asymptomatic)

This Patient: - Urea breath test OR Stool antigen (both non-invasive, detect active infection) - Preferred: Urea breath test (slightly better sensitivity/specificity)

Timing: ≥4 weeks after completion of antibiotics (and ≥2 weeks off PPI)

Why NOT Serology (A): - IgG antibody remains positive for months to years after eradication - Cannot distinguish active infection from past infection

Indications for Test-of-Cure: - ALL patients (especially if bleeding ulcer, persistent symptoms, or first eradication attempt)

If Still Positive: - Repeat treatment with different regimen (e.g., if used triple, switch to quadruple) - Check compliance (did patient take full course?)

Key Point: Test-of-cure → Urea breath test or Stool antigen (NOT serology) ≥4 weeks after Rx

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A patient presents with sudden severe abdominal pain. Upright CXR shows free air under the diaphragm. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A. Acute pancreatitis B. Perforated peptic ulcer C. Acute cholecystitis D. Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm

Answer: B. Perforated peptic ulcer

Explanation: - Free air under diaphragm (Pneumoperitoneum) = Perforated viscus

Most Common Cause: Perforated peptic ulcer (60-70%) - Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate more commonly (free perforation into peritoneal cavity)

Clinical Features: - Sudden, severe abdominal pain ("like being stabbed") - Peritoneal signs: Board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds - History: Often prior dyspepsia, NSAID use

Diagnosis: - Upright CXR: Pneumoperitoneum (free air under diaphragm) - 70-80% - CT abdomen: More sensitive (if CXR negative)

Management: 1. Resuscitation: IV fluids, NPO, NG decompression 2. Antibiotics: Broad-spectrum (Pip-Tazo) 3. Surgery: Graham patch (omental patch closure) + Biopsy 4. Post-op: High-dose PPI × 8-12 weeks, H. pylori eradication

Other Causes of Pneumoperitoneum: - Perforated sigmoid diverticulitis - Perforated appendix - Iatrogenic (post-laparoscopy)

Why NOT Other Options: - Pancreatitis (A): ↑ Amylase/lipase, NO free air - Cholecystitis (B): RUQ pain, Murphy's sign, NO free air (unless perforation - rare) - AAA rupture (D): Hypotension, pulsatile mass, retroperitoneal bleed (NO free air)

Key Point: Free air under diaphragm → Perforated peptic ulcer (MOST COMMON cause)

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A patient with refractory peptic ulcers has fasting gastrin 1200 pg/mL. Gastric pH is 1.5. What is the most appropriate next step?

A. Start high-dose PPI B. Secretin stimulation test C. Check for H. pylori D. Repeat gastrin off PPI

Answer: A. Start high-dose PPI (or B. Secretin stimulation test - for definitive diagnosis)

Explanation: - Suspected Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome

Diagnosis: - Fasting gastrin >1000 pg/mL + Gastric pH <2 → Highly suggestive of ZES

This Patient: - Gastrin 1200 pg/mL (>1000) ✓ - pH 1.5 (<2, ↑ acid) ✓ - → Diagnosis of ZES is very likely

Next Steps:

Option A: Start high-dose PPI (Omeprazole 60-120 mg/day) - Urgent (control symptoms, prevent complications) - Can be started while awaiting confirmatory tests

Option B: Secretin stimulation test (GOLD STANDARD for ZES) - Definitive diagnosis - ↑ Gastrin >200 pg/mL after secretin → Confirms ZES - Can be done after starting PPI (but ideally off PPI)

Both A and B are appropriate (A is more urgent clinically, B confirms diagnosis)

Imaging (once diagnosis confirmed): - CT/MRI, Endoscopic ultrasound, Octreotide scan (localize tumor)

Why NOT Other Options: - Check H. pylori (C): Already tested (assumed), and ZES more likely with this gastrin level - Repeat gastrin off PPI (D): Patient likely already off PPI (fasting gastrin done off PPI), and gastrin >1000 + pH <2 is diagnostic

ZES Treatment: - Medical: High-dose PPI (Omeprazole 60-120 mg/day) - Lifelong if unresectable - Surgical: Tumor resection (if localized, non-metastatic)

Key Point: Gastrin >1000 + pH <2 → ZES → Start high-dose PPI (urgent) + Secretin test (confirm)

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 50-year-old man with long-standing GERD (15 years) has endoscopy showing salmon-pink mucosa extending 3 cm above the gastroesophageal junction. Biopsy shows intestinal metaplasia with no dysplasia. What is the most appropriate surveillance interval?

A. No surveillance needed B. Repeat endoscopy in 1 year C. Repeat endoscopy in 3-5 years D. Repeat endoscopy in 6 months

Answer: C. Repeat endoscopy in 3-5 years

Explanation: - Diagnosis: Barrett's Esophagus (intestinal metaplasia on biopsy, NO dysplasia)

Barrett's Esophagus: - Replacement of squamous epithelium with intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells) - Risk: 0.5% per year progression to esophageal adenocarcinoma

Surveillance:

Dysplasia Grade Surveillance Interval No dysplasia Every 3-5 years ✓ Low-grade dysplasia (LGD) Every 6-12 months (or ablation) High-grade dysplasia (HGD) Endoscopic ablation (RFA) OR Every 3 months

This Patient: No dysplasia → Surveillance every 3-5 years

Biopsy Protocol: Seattle Protocol (4-quadrant biopsies every 1-2 cm)

Treatment: - PPI therapy (high-dose) - Does NOT reverse Barrett's, but may ↓ progression - Surveillance (as above) - HGD: Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) ± Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR)

Key Point: Barrett's esophagus with NO dysplasia → Surveillance every 3-5 years

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A patient with bleeding duodenal ulcer undergoes endoscopy showing a visible vessel (Forrest IIa). After endoscopic therapy, what is the estimated rebleeding risk?

A. 5% B. 25% C. 50% D. 90%

Answer: C. 50%

Explanation: - Forrest Classification (Predicts rebleeding risk)

Forrest IIa: Visible vessel (non-bleeding, protuberant vessel in ulcer base)

Rebleeding Risk: 50% (HIGH) ✓

Forrest Classification:

Class Description Rebleeding Risk Endoscopic Therapy? Ia Spurting arterial bleeding 90% YES Ib Oozing bleeding 50% YES IIa Visible vessel (non-bleeding) 50% YES ✓ IIb Adherent clot 25-30% YES IIc Flat pigmented spot 10% NO (low risk) III Clean base 5% NO (low risk)

Endoscopic Therapy: - Indications: Forrest Ia, Ib, IIa, IIb (active bleeding or high-risk stigmata) - Methods: - Injection: Epinephrine (1:10,000) - Thermal: Heater probe, Argon plasma coagulation (APC), Bipolar cautery - Mechanical: Clips - Combination: Epinephrine + Thermal/Clips (most effective)

Management (Forrest IIa): 1. Endoscopic therapy (injection + thermal/clips) 2. High-dose PPI IV: Pantoprazole 80 mg bolus → 8 mg/hr infusion × 72 hours 3. PPI BID (after IV) × 2 weeks 4. H. pylori eradication (if positive)

Key Point: Forrest IIa (visible vessel) → 50% rebleeding risk → Endoscopic therapy needed

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Which of the following is an indication for endoscopy in a patient with GERD?

A. Heartburn for 2 weeks B. Age 30 years with typical symptoms C. Progressive dysphagia D. Symptoms responsive to PPI

Answer: C. Progressive dysphagia

Explanation: - Alarm Features → Endoscopy indicated (rule out complications, malignancy)

Alarm Features ("RED FLAGS"): - Dysphagia (especially progressive) ✓ - Suggests stricture or malignancy - Odynophagia (painful swallowing) - Unintentional weight loss (>5% body weight) - GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena, anemia) - Persistent vomiting - Age ≥50 years with new-onset GERD

This Patient: Progressive dysphagia → Endoscopy MANDATORY (rule out stricture, cancer)

When Endoscopy NOT Needed (Empiric PPI trial): - Age <50 years - NO alarm features - Typical symptoms (heartburn, regurgitation)

Why NOT Other Options: - Heartburn × 2 weeks (A): Short duration, trial PPI first - Age 30 with typical symptoms (B): Empiric PPI trial (no endoscopy needed) - Responsive to PPI (D): Good response confirms GERD (no endoscopy needed unless long-standing GERD >5-10 years → screen for Barrett's)

Long-Standing GERD (>5-10 years): Consider endoscopy to screen for Barrett's esophagus (even without alarm features)

Key Point: GERD with alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, age ≥50) → Endoscopy

docs.title: SUMMARY
docs.text:
GERD (Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease): - Mechanism: Transient LES relaxations (TLESRs) - MOST COMMON - Symptoms: Heartburn (postprandial, lying down), Regurgitation - Diagnosis: Empiric PPI trial (if <50, no alarm features); Endoscopy if alarm features - Treatment: PPI (Omeprazole 20 mg OD × 4-8 weeks), Lifestyle modifications - Complication: Barrett's esophagus (0.5%/year → adenocarcinoma) → Surveillance

Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD): - Causes: H. pylori (50-70% DU, 30-50% GU) - MOST COMMON, NSAIDs (20-30%) - DU vs GU: DU pain 2-5 hrs post-meal (food relieves), GU pain within 1 hr (food worsens, weight loss) - Diagnosis: Endoscopy (GOLD STANDARD), H. pylori testing (RUT, Histology, UBT) - Treatment: - H. pylori eradication: Bismuth Quadruple (90-95%) or Clarithromycin Triple (70-85%) - PPI × 4 weeks (DU) or 8 weeks (GU) - ALWAYS biopsy gastric ulcers (5% malignant), Repeat endoscopy at 8-12 weeks - Complications: Bleeding (Forrest IIa = 50% rebleeding), Perforation (free air), GOO

Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (ZES): - Gastrinoma → ↑ Gastrin → ↑ Acid → Multiple/refractory ulcers + Diarrhea - Diagnosis: Gastrin >1000 + pH <2 → Secretin stimulation test (GOLD STANDARD) - Treatment: High-dose PPI (Omeprazole 60-120 mg/day), Surgery if localized

END OF CHAPTER

Week 2 Progress: 1/18 topics complete

Next Topic: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) (Medicine/22_Inflammatory_Bowel_Disease.md)

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition and Classification
  2. Epidemiology
  3. Pathophysiology
  4. Clinical Features
  5. Diagnosis
  6. Management
  7. Complications
  8. Extraintestinal Manifestations
  9. High-Yield Points
  10. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) encompasses chronic, relapsing-remitting inflammatory conditions of the gastrointestinal tract:

  1. Crohn's Disease (CD)
  2. Can affect any part of GI tract (mouth to anus)
  3. Transmural inflammation
  4. Skip lesions (discontinuous)

  5. Non-caseating granulomas (30-50%)

  6. Ulcerative Colitis (UC)

  7. Colon and rectum ONLY
  8. Mucosal/submucosal inflammation
  9. Continuous from rectum proximally

  10. NO granulomas

  11. IBD-Unclassified (IBD-U)

  12. Features of both CD and UC
  13. ~10% of IBD cases

docs.title: Incidence and Prevalence
docs.text:
Geographic Distribution: - Highest: North America, Northern Europe, UK - Increasing: Asia, South America, Middle East

Peak Age of Onset: - Bimodal: 15-30 years (primary), 50-70 years (secondary) - CD: Slightly earlier than UC

Gender: - UC: Males = Females - CD: Females slightly > Males

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
Genetic: - NOD2/CARD15 (strongest CD risk gene) - IL23R (protective) - Family History: 10-20% have affected first-degree relative - Concordance: Monozygotic twins 50% (CD), 10% (UC)

Environmental: - Smoking: - INCREASES Crohn's disease risk (2-fold) - DECREASES UC risk (protective) - WORSENS CD course - Quitting may precipitate UC

  • Appendectomy: Protective against UC (if done for appendicitis before age 20)
  • Antibiotics: Early-life exposure increases risk
  • NSAIDs: May precipitate flares
  • Oral Contraceptives: Modest increased risk
  • Western Diet: High fat, processed foods

Protective Factors: - Breastfeeding - Mediterranean diet - Physical activity

docs.title: Immune Dysregulation
docs.text:
General Mechanism: 

Genetic Susceptibility + Environmental Triggers
              ↓
Altered Intestinal Barrier Function
              ↓
Inappropriate Immune Response to Commensal Bacteria
              ↓
Chronic Inflammation

docs.title: Crohn's Disease
docs.text:
Th1/Th17-Mediated: - IL-12, IL-23 drive Th1/Th17 differentiation - TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-17 promote inflammation - Impaired autophagy (NOD2 mutations) - Defective bacterial clearance

Transmural Inflammation: - All layers of bowel wall - Leads to: - Strictures (fibrosis) - Fistulas - Abscesses

docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis
docs.text:
Th2-Mediated: - IL-5, IL-13 drive inflammation - Epithelial barrier dysfunction - Mucosal ulceration

Mucosal Inflammation: - Limited to mucosa/submucosa - Leads to: - Pseudopolyps - Toxic megacolon - Dysplasia/cancer

docs.title: Crohn's Disease vs Ulcerative Colitis Comparison
docs.text: Feature Crohn's Disease Ulcerative Colitis Location Mouth to anus (Any part) Colon and rectum ONLY Most Common Site Terminal ileum + colon (ileocolitis 50%) Left-sided colitis (40-50%) Distribution Skip lesions (discontinuous) Continuous from rectum Inflammation Transmural (all layers) Mucosal/Submucosal only Rectal Involvement Often spared (50% no rectal disease) ALWAYS involved (95-100%) Granulomas 30-50% (non-caseating) NEVER Fistulas Common (30-50%) Rare Strictures Common Rare (in chronic cases) Bleeding Less common (occult) Prominent (hematochezia) Perianal Disease 40% (fissures, fistulas, abscess) Rare Smoking Effect Worsens disease Protective Malignancy Risk Slightly increased HIGH (long-standing, extensive) Surgery Not curative (recurs) Curative (total colectomy)

docs.title: Crohn's Disease Patterns:
docs.text:
1. Ileocolitis (50%): - Right lower quadrant pain - Diarrhea (non-bloody) - Weight loss - May mimic appendicitis

2. Ileitis (30%): - Similar to ileocolitis - Palpable RLQ mass - B12 deficiency (terminal ileum)

3. Colitis (20%): - Bloody diarrhea - Similar to UC - May have rectal sparing

4. Upper GI (5-15%): - Nausea, vomiting - Dysphagia - Epigastric pain

5. Perianal (40%): - Perianal fistulas (most common fistula type) - Skin tags - Fissures, abscesses

docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis Extent:
docs.text:
1. Proctitis (30-40%): - Rectal bleeding (small amounts) - Tenesmus (common) - Urgency - Constipation (rectal inflammation)

2. Left-sided/Distal Colitis (40-50%): - Bloody diarrhea - LLQ cramping - Weight loss (moderate)

3. Extensive/Pancolitis (20-30%): - Severe bloody diarrhea (>6 stools/day) - Abdominal pain - Significant weight loss - Fever, tachycardia - Highest cancer risk

docs.title: Symptom Severity Classification
docs.text:
Crohn's Disease Activity Index (CDAI): - <150: Remission - 150-220: Mild - 220-450: Moderate - >450: Severe

Key Components: - Number of liquid stools - Abdominal pain severity - General well-being - Extraintestinal manifestations - Antidiarrheal use - Abdominal mass - Hematocrit - Body weight

Ulcerative Colitis - Truelove and Witts Severity Index:

Parameter Mild Moderate Severe Stools/day <4 4-6 >6 Blood in stool Small amounts Moderate Large Fever None Minimal >37.8°C Heart Rate Normal Moderate >90 bpm Hemoglobin Normal Moderate anemia <10.5 g/dL ESR <30 30-60 >60 mm/hr
