Severe UC = Acute Severe UC (ASUC): - Truelove-Witts Criteria: ≥6 bloody stools/day + systemic toxicity - Medical emergency - Requires hospitalization - Risk of toxic megacolon

docs.title: Clinical Evaluation
docs.text:
History: - Chronic diarrhea (>4 weeks) - Rectal bleeding - Abdominal pain - Weight loss, fever - Family history - Smoking history - Recent travel, infections

Physical Examination: - Abdominal tenderness, mass - Perianal inspection (tags, fistulas, fissures) - Extraintestinal manifestations - Signs of malnutrition

docs.title: Laboratory Investigations
docs.text:
Inflammatory Markers: - C-Reactive Protein (CRP): - Better correlates with CD than UC - Elevated in active disease - Normal CRP doesn't exclude IBD

  • Fecal Calprotectin:
  • >250 μg/g: Suggests IBD (vs IBS)
  • Sensitive for mucosal inflammation
  • Used for:
    • Screening
    • Monitoring disease activity
    • Detecting relapse
  • NOT specific for IBD

Stool Studies: - Fecal occult blood - Stool culture, C. diff toxin: Rule out infection - Ova and parasites

Serology: - Anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies (ASCA): 50-70% in CD - Perinuclear anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (p-ANCA): 50-70% in UC - Limited utility: NOT diagnostic alone

Complete Blood Count: - Anemia (iron deficiency, chronic disease, B12/folate) - Leukocytosis - Thrombocytosis (active inflammation)

Nutritional Assessment: - Albumin (low) - Iron studies - Vitamin B12 (CD with ileal involvement) - Vitamin D, Folate

docs.title: Endoscopic Evaluation
docs.text: Colonoscopy with Ileoscopy: - Gold Standard for diagnosis - Biopsies: Multiple from all segments (even normal-appearing) - Required for: - Diagnosis - Extent assessment - Surveillance for dysplasia

docs.title: Crohn's Disease Endoscopic Findings:
docs.text:
Macroscopic: - Skip lesions (discontinuous) - Aphthous ulcers (earliest lesion) - Linear/serpentine ulcers - "Cobblestoning": Deep ulcers with intervening edematous mucosa - Strictures - Fistulous openings - Rectal sparing (common)

Microscopic: - Non-caseating granulomas (30-50%) - HALLMARK - Transmural inflammation - Focal cryptitis - Preserved goblet cells

docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis Endoscopic Findings:
docs.text:
Macroscopic: - Continuous inflammation from rectum - Loss of vascular pattern - Erythema, friability - Pseudopolyps (inflammatory polyps) - Diffuse superficial ulceration - NO skip lesions

Microscopic: - Crypt abscesses (neutrophils in crypts) - Crypt distortion, branching - Goblet cell depletion - Basal plasmacytosis - NO granulomas - Mucosal/submucosal inflammation ONLY

Mayo Endoscopic Score (UC): - 0: Normal - 1: Erythema, decreased vascular pattern - 2: Friability, erosions - 3: Spontaneous bleeding, ulceration

docs.title: Imaging
docs.text:
Cross-Sectional Imaging (for CD especially):

CT/MR Enterography (CTE/MRE): - First-line for small bowel CD - Assess: - Bowel wall thickening - Mural enhancement - Strictures - Fistulas, abscesses - Mesenteric changes

Findings: - "Comb sign": Engorged vasa recta (mesenteric inflammation) - Mural stratification: Submucosal edema - "Creeping fat": Mesenteric fat wrapping around bowel

MR Enterography Advantages: - No radiation - Preferred for young patients, surveillance - Better soft tissue contrast

Capsule Endoscopy: - Small bowel evaluation (if no stricture) - Contraindicated if known/suspected stricture (capsule retention risk) - Pre-screening with patency capsule or MRE

Ultrasound: - Point-of-care assessment - Bowel wall thickness - Vascularity (Doppler)

Plain Radiography: - Acute Severe UC: Rule out toxic megacolon, perforation - Toxic megacolon: Colon diameter >6 cm

docs.title: Goals of Therapy
docs.text:
1. Induce remission (clinical, biochemical, endoscopic)
  2. Maintain remission
  3. Mucosal healing (reduces complications, hospitalization, surgery)
  4. Improve quality of life
  5. Prevent complications

docs.title: Treatment Approach
docs.text: Tailored Based on: - Disease location (CD: ileal, colonic, perianal; UC: extent) - Severity (mild, moderate, severe) - Disease behavior (CD: inflammatory, stricturing, penetrating) - Prior treatment response

docs.title: 1. Aminosalicylates (5-ASA)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Anti-inflammatory (↓ prostaglandins, leukotrienes, NF-κB)

Indications: - UC: First-line for mild-moderate disease - Proctitis: Suppositories (Mesalamine 1 g PR daily) - Left-sided: Enema (Mesalamine 4 g PR daily) + oral - Extensive: Oral (2.4-4.8 g/day divided)

  • CD: Limited efficacy
  • May use for mild ileocolonic CD
  • NOT effective for maintaining remission

Formulations: - Sulfasalazine: 4-6 g/day (cheap, but side effects) - Mesalamine: 2.4-4.8 g/day (better tolerated) - pH-dependent release - Time-dependent release

Side Effects: - Sulfasalazine: Nausea, headache, rash, oligospermia (reversible), sulfa allergy - Mesalamine: Generally well-tolerated - Rare: Interstitial nephritis, pancreatitis, paradoxical worsening

Monitoring: - Baseline: CBC, renal function - Periodic: Renal function (especially sulfasalazine)

docs.title: 2. Corticosteroids
docs.text:
Mechanism: Broad anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive

Indications: - Moderate-severe UC or CD (induction ONLY) - NOT for maintenance (ineffective, steroid-dependent = escalate)

Regimens:

Oral: - Prednisone/Prednisolone: 40-60 mg daily - Taper over 8-12 weeks after response - If no response in 2 weeks → escalate

IV (Severe UC): - Hydrocortisone: 100 mg IV q6-8h OR - Methylprednisolone: 60 mg IV daily - Used in Acute Severe UC (ASUC)

Topical (Distal UC): - Budesonide foam/enema: 2 mg PR daily

Budesonide (for CD): - Ileal/Right colonic CD: Budesonide 9 mg daily × 8-12 weeks - First-pass metabolism → fewer systemic side effects - NOT for maintenance

Side Effects: - Short-term: Insomnia, mood changes, hyperglycemia, hypertension - Long-term: Osteoporosis, cataracts, weight gain, adrenal suppression, infections

Steroid-Refractory: - No response after 7-10 days IV steroids (ASUC) → Consider rescue therapy or colectomy - Rescue: Infliximab or Cyclosporine

Steroid-Dependent: - Requires steroids to maintain remission - Escalate to immunomodulators or biologics

docs.title: 3. Immunomodulators
docs.text:
Thiopurines:

Azathioprine (AZA): 2-2.5 mg/kg/day 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP): 1-1.5 mg/kg/day

Mechanism: Purine analog → immunosuppression

Indications: - Maintain remission (both UC and CD) - Steroid-sparing - Often combined with biologics (reduce immunogenicity)

Onset: 3-6 months (slow)

Pre-Treatment: - TPMT enzyme activity: Low = high toxicity risk - Low/absent: Avoid or reduce dose (0.5-1 mg/kg) - Heterozygous: Reduce dose - Normal: Standard dose

  • Hepatitis B, C, HIV, TB screening
  • Varicella immunity

Monitoring: - CBC, LFTs: Every 1-2 weeks × 2 months, then every 3 months - 6-TGN levels: Therapeutic 230-450 pmol/8×10⁸ RBC

Side Effects: - Myelosuppression (dose-related) - Hepatotoxicity - Pancreatitis (3-7%, idiosyncratic, discontinue permanently) - Infections - Lymphoma risk: Small increase (4/10,000 patient-years) - Non-melanoma skin cancer: Increased risk

Methotrexate:

Dose: - Induction (CD): 25 mg IM/SC weekly × 16 weeks - Maintenance: 15 mg IM/SC weekly

Indications: - Crohn's disease (alternative to thiopurines) - NOT effective in UC

Contraindications: - Pregnancy (Category X - teratogenic) - Liver disease - Alcohol abuse

Monitoring: - CBC, LFTs every 4-8 weeks - Baseline chest X-ray

Side Effects: - Nausea (give with folic acid 1 mg daily) - Hepatotoxicity - Myelosuppression - Pneumonitis (rare)

docs.title: 4. Biologic Therapy
docs.text:
Anti-TNF Agents:

Infliximab (IFX): - Chimeric (mouse-human) monoclonal antibody - Indications: Moderate-severe CD and UC - Dosing: - Induction: 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks - Maintenance: 5 mg/kg IV every 8 weeks - May increase to 10 mg/kg or q6weeks if loss of response

  • Special Uses:
  • Fistulizing CD: Higher dose often needed
  • Acute Severe UC: Rescue therapy (vs cyclosporine)

Adalimumab (ADA): - Fully human monoclonal antibody (less immunogenic) - SC injection (patient-administered) - Dosing: - Induction: 160 mg SC week 0, 80 mg week 2 - Maintenance: 40 mg SC every 2 weeks

Certolizumab Pegol: - PEGylated Fab fragment - CD only (not approved for UC) - Safe in pregnancy (doesn't cross placenta)

Golimumab: - UC only - SC injection monthly

Pre-Treatment Screening: - Tuberculosis: - TST or IGRA (QuantiFERON) - Chest X-ray - If latent TB: Treat with INH × 9 months (start ≥3 weeks before anti-TNF)

  • Hepatitis B: HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs

  • If positive: Consult hepatology, consider prophylaxis

  • Hepatitis C, HIV

  • Varicella immunity

Monitoring: - Infusion reactions (infliximab) - Tuberculosis reactivation - Opportunistic infections - Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM): - Infliximab trough: 5-10 μg/mL - Adalimumab trough: 8-12 μg/mL - Anti-drug antibodies: If low drug levels + high antibodies → switch

Side Effects: - Infections: TB, fungal, viral - Infusion/injection reactions - Hepatotoxicity - Heart failure exacerbation (contraindicated in NYHA III-IV) - Demyelinating disorders (caution/contraindicated) - Malignancy: Lymphoma (rare), skin cancer - Paradoxical psoriasis

Loss of Response: - Optimize dosing: Increase dose or shorten interval - Check drug levels: Low levels → increase dose; antibodies → switch - Switch within class (anti-TNF to anti-TNF) or out of class

Anti-Integrin Agents:

Vedolizumab (VDZ): - Gut-selective α4β7 integrin antagonist - Indications: Moderate-severe CD and UC - Dosing: - Induction: 300 mg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks - Maintenance: 300 mg IV every 8 weeks

  • Advantages:
  • Gut-selective (lower systemic infection risk)
  • No TB reactivation

  • Good for patients with contraindications to anti-TNF

  • Slower onset: May take 12-14 weeks (vs 4-6 weeks for anti-TNF)

Side Effects: - Infusion reactions (rare) - Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML): Very rare (vs natalizumab)

Anti-IL-12/23 Agents:

Ustekinumab (UST): - IL-12/23 inhibitor - Indications: Moderate-severe CD (also UC in some regions) - Dosing: - Induction: Weight-based IV (260-520 mg at week 0) - Maintenance: 90 mg SC every 8-12 weeks

  • Good option for anti-TNF failures

Side Effects: - Infections (lower risk than anti-TNF) - Injection site reactions

JAK Inhibitors:

Tofacitinib: - Oral small molecule - UC only (not CD) - Dosing: - Induction: 10 mg PO BID × 8 weeks - Maintenance: 5 mg PO BID

Advantages: Oral, rapid onset

Side Effects: - Infections (herpes zoster - vaccinate) - Thromboembolism (black box warning) - Malignancy - Lipid abnormalities

Monitoring: - CBC, lipids - Hepatitis, TB screening

docs.title: 5. Antibiotics
docs.text:
Limited Role in IBD:

Metronidazole + Ciprofloxacin: - Perianal Crohn's disease - Crohn's colitis (adjunct) - Pouchitis (post-colectomy UC)

NOT for induction/maintenance of luminal IBD

docs.title: Indications for Surgery:
docs.text: General: - Medical refractory disease - Strictures with obstruction - Fistulas, abscesses (CD) - Perforation - Toxic megacolon - Dysplasia/Cancer - Growth retardation (pediatric)

docs.title: Crohn's Disease:
docs.text:
NOT Curative (high recurrence rate): - 70-80% require surgery eventually - Recurrence: 50% at 10 years post-resection

Operations: 1. Ileocolic resection (most common) - For terminal ileal disease

  1. Stricturoplasty:
  2. Preserve bowel length

  3. For short strictures (<10 cm)

  4. Segmental resection:

  5. Limited resection (preserve length)

  6. Seton placement:

  7. For complex perianal fistulas

  8. Abscess drainage:

  9. Percutaneous or surgical

Post-Op: - High recurrence risk: Endoscopic recurrence 70-90% at 1 year - Prophylaxis: - Anti-TNF (most effective) - Metronidazole (3 months) - Thiopurines

  • Surveillance endoscopy: 6-12 months post-op

docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis:
docs.text:
CURATIVE (total proctocolectomy removes all diseased tissue):

Operations:

  1. Total Proctocolectomy with Ileal Pouch-Anal Anastomosis (IPAA):
  2. Most common (young patients desiring continence)
  3. J-pouch (most common), K-pouch, S-pouch
  4. Usually 2-3 stage procedure

  5. Complications:

    • Pouchitis (50% at 10 years)
    • Incontinence
    • Infertility (women)

  6. Total Proctocolectomy with End Ileostomy:

  7. Permanent ileostomy

  8. For elderly, poor sphincter function

  9. Subtotal Colectomy with Ileostomy:

  10. Emergency for toxic megacolon, perforation
  11. Rectum left in situ (definitive surgery later)

Indications: - Acute Severe UC (ASUC) refractory to medical therapy - Dysplasia/Cancer - Chronic refractory disease - Intolerable medication side effects

docs.title: Management of Acute Severe Ulcerative Colitis (ASUC)
docs.text:
Truelove-Witts Criteria: - ≥6 bloody stools/day + systemic toxicity (fever >37.8°C, HR >90, anemia, ESR >30)

Hospital Admission:

1. Initial Assessment: - IV access, NPO - Plain abdominal X-ray: Rule out toxic megacolon (colon >6 cm) - Stool culture, C. diff: Rule out infection - Bloods: CBC, CRP, albumin, electrolytes

2. Medical Management:

IV Corticosteroids: - Hydrocortisone 100 mg q6h OR Methylprednisolone 60 mg daily - Assess response at Day 3: - Day 3 stool frequency OR Oxford Index (stool freq + CRP)

If No Improvement by Day 3-5: - Rescue Therapy:

A. Infliximab: - 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks - Preferred if long-term biologic likely needed - Lower colectomy rate long-term

B. Cyclosporine: - 2-4 mg/kg/day IV continuous infusion - Target trough: 200-400 ng/mL - Bridge to thiopurines (start AZA immediately) - Avoid if: - Cholesterol <120 mg/dL (seizure risk) - Concurrent use with IFX (↑ infection) - Short-term only (3-6 months)

3. Supportive Care: - Fluid resuscitation, electrolyte correction - NG decompression if ileus - Nutritional support - VTE prophylaxis (high thrombosis risk) - Antibiotics ONLY if concern for infection/perforation

4. Surgery: - Indications: - No response to rescue therapy by day 7 - Toxic megacolon (colon >6 cm) - Perforation - Massive bleeding

  • Subtotal colectomy + end ileostomy (emergent)

docs.title: Crohn's Disease Complications
docs.text:
1. Strictures (Fibrostenotic): - Chronic inflammation → fibrosis → narrowing - Presentation: Obstructive symptoms (pain, distension, nausea, vomiting) - Diagnosis: CT/MR enterography - Management: - Endoscopic balloon dilation (short strictures <4 cm, no fistula) - Surgery: Stricturoplasty or resection

2. Fistulas (Penetrating): - Transmural inflammation → erosion into adjacent structures - Types: - Perianal (most common): Anorectal, rectovaginal - Enteroenteric: Small bowel to small bowel - Enterovesical: Bowel to bladder (pneumaturia, fecaluria, recurrent UTI) - Enterocutaneous: Bowel to skin - Enterovaginal: Bowel to vagina (feculent discharge)

  • Diagnosis:
  • MR Enterography/Pelvis (best for perianal)
  • CT Enterography

  • Exam under anesthesia (perianal)

  • Management:

  • Medical:

    • Anti-TNF (infliximab) - most effective
    • Antibiotics (metronidazole + ciprofloxacin) - adjunct
    • Immunomodulators

  • Surgical:

    • Seton drainage (perianal - non-cutting seton for drainage)
    • Fistulotomy (simple, low fistulas)
    • Resection (internal fistulas)

3. Abscesses: - Intra-abdominal, perianal - Diagnosis: CT/MRI - Management: - Drainage (percutaneous or surgical) - MUST drain before biologics - Antibiotics - After drainage: Start/optimize medical therapy

4. Malabsorption: - Bile salt malabsorption: Terminal ileal disease (diarrhea, steatorrhea) - Tx: Cholestyramine - Vitamin B12 deficiency: Terminal ileum (megaloblastic anemia) - Tx: B12 supplementation - Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) - Protein-losing enteropathy

5. Small Bowel Obstruction: - Strictures - Adhesions (post-surgical)

6. Perianal Disease: - Fissures, fistulas, abscesses, skin tags - May precede intestinal symptoms

docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis Complications
docs.text:
1. Toxic Megacolon: - Life-threatening complication - Definition: Colon diameter >6 cm + systemic toxicity - Precipitants: - Severe colitis - Opioids, anticholinergics, antidiarrheals (precipitate) - Hypokalemia, hypocalcemia - Barium enema

  • Clinical:
  • Severe abdominal pain, distension
  • Decreased bowel sounds
  • Fever, tachycardia, hypotension

  • ↓ Stool output (paradoxical)

  • Diagnosis: Plain abdominal X-ray (colon >6 cm)

  • Management:

  • ICU admission
  • NPO, NG suction
  • IV fluids, electrolyte correction (K⁺, Mg²⁺)
  • IV corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 100 mg q6h)
  • Broad-spectrum antibiotics
  • NO antidiarrheals, opioids, anticholinergics

  • Avoid colonoscopy (perforation risk)

  • If no improvement in 24-48 hours OR signs of perforation:

    • Emergency subtotal colectomy + end ileostomy

2. Perforation: - Free perforation (peritonitis) - Often with toxic megacolon - Emergency colectomy

3. Massive Hemorrhage: - Rare (1-4%) - Requires transfusion >6 units/24 hrs - If uncontrolled → colectomy

4. Colorectal Cancer: - Significantly increased risk - Risk factors: - Duration: 1% after 10 yrs, 2% after 20 yrs, 18% after 30 yrs - Extent: Pancolitis > left-sided > proctitis - Primary sclerosing cholangitis (5× increased risk) - Family history of CRC - Severe/continuous inflammation

  • Surveillance:
  • Start: 8 years after diagnosis for pancolitis, 15 years for left-sided

  • Frequency:

    • No PSC: Every 1-3 years (depends on risk factors)
    • PSC: Annually from diagnosis

  • Method: Colonoscopy with chromoendoscopy + random biopsies (q10 cm × 4 quadrants)

  • Dysplasia Management:

  • High-grade dysplasia: Colectomy (50% have cancer)
  • Low-grade dysplasia: Endoscopic resection (if visible) vs colectomy
  • Indefinite for dysplasia: Repeat in 3-6 months

5. Strictures: - Uncommon in UC (suspect malignancy) - Evaluate: Biopsy to rule out cancer

docs.title: Common to Both
docs.text:
Venous Thromboembolism (VTE): - 3× increased risk (hypercoagulable state) - DVT, PE, CVT (cerebral venous thrombosis) - Prophylaxis: - Hospitalized patients: Heparin - Active disease: Consider prophylaxis

Osteoporosis: - Chronic inflammation - Corticosteroid use - Malabsorption (vitamin D, calcium) - Screening: DEXA scan (all patients on prolonged steroids) - Prevention: Calcium + Vitamin D, bisphosphonates if indicated

docs.title: Extraintestinal Manifestations
docs.text: Prevalence: 25-40% of IBD patients

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
1. Related to Disease Activity: - Improve with IBD treatment

Peripheral Arthropathy (Type 1): - Large joints (knees, ankles, hips, wrists) - Asymmetric, migratory - Pauciarticular (<5 joints) - Non-erosive (no joint destruction) - Parallels IBD activity - Treatment: Treat underlying IBD

Erythema Nodosum: - Tender, raised, red nodules on shins - Most common skin manifestation - UC > CD - Parallels IBD activity - Treatment: Treat IBD, NSAIDs (if tolerated), steroids

Episcleritis: - Red, painful eye (less severe than uveitis) - Self-limited - Parallels IBD activity

Aphthous Stomatitis: - Oral ulcers - Common

2. Independent of Disease Activity: - Do NOT improve with IBD treatment

Axial Arthropathy: - Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS): - Chronic back pain (worse at night, improves with exercise) - Morning stiffness >30 minutes - Sacroiliitis (bilateral) - HLA-B27 positive (70-90%) - X-ray: Bamboo spine, syndesmophytes - Does NOT parallel IBD activity - Treatment: NSAIDs, anti-TNF (if severe)

  • Sacroiliitis:
  • Isolated (without full AS)
  • Asymptomatic or back pain
  • 15-20% of IBD patients

Pyoderma Gangrenosum: - Painful ulcers with purple, undermined borders - Usually lower legs - CD > UC - Does NOT parallel IBD activity - Associated with severe IBD - Treatment: - Topical/intralesional steroids - Systemic steroids - Anti-TNF (very effective) - Avoid debridement (worsens - pathergy)

Uveitis: - Painful, red eye with photophobia, blurred vision - Medical emergency (can cause blindness) - Does NOT parallel IBD - Treatment: Ophthalmology referral (topical steroids, cycloplegics)

Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC): - Chronic cholestatic liver disease - Fibrosing inflammation of bile ducts - UC >> CD (70-80% have UC) - 5% of UC patients have PSC - Does NOT improve with colectomy

  • Presentation:
  • Asymptomatic (incidental ↑ ALP)
  • Pruritis, jaundice (advanced)

  • Cholangitis (rare)

  • Diagnosis:

  • ↑ ALP (cholestatic pattern)
  • MRCP: Beading of bile ducts (strictures + dilations)

  • ERCP: Diagnostic (+ therapeutic if dominant stricture)

  • Complications:

  • Cholangiocarcinoma (10-20% lifetime risk)
  • Cirrhosis, portal hypertension

  • 5× increased CRC risk (even after colectomy)

  • Management:

  • Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA): May improve LFTs (survival benefit unclear)
  • ERCP + stenting for dominant strictures
  • Liver transplant (only curative)
  • Annual colonoscopy (↑ CRC risk)
  • Annual MRCP or ultrasound + CA 19-9 (cholangiocarcinoma surveillance)

docs.title: Extraintestinal Manifestation Summary Table
docs.text: Manifestation Type Parallels IBD? Treatment Peripheral Arthritis Pauciarticular, large joints YES Treat IBD Erythema Nodosum Tender nodules, shins YES Treat IBD, steroids Episcleritis Red eye (mild) YES Treat IBD Aphthous Ulcers Oral ulcers YES Treat IBD Ankylosing Spondylitis Axial, HLA-B27 NO NSAIDs, anti-TNF Sacroiliitis Isolated SI joint inflammation NO NSAIDs Pyoderma Gangrenosum Ulcers with purple borders NO Steroids, anti-TNF Uveitis Painful eye, photophobia NO Ophthalmology (steroids) PSC Cholestatic, beading on MRCP NO UDCA, transplant

docs.title: Must-Know Differentiating Features: CD vs UC
docs.text:
1. Crohn's:
  2. Any GI location, transmural, skip lesions, granulomas (30-50%)
  3. Perianal disease (40%)
  4. Fistulas common
  5. Surgery NOT curative

  6. Smoking WORSENS

  7. UC:

  8. Colon/rectum ONLY, mucosal, continuous, NO granulomas
  9. Rectal involvement (95-100%)
  10. Fistulas rare
  11. Surgery CURATIVE (total colectomy)
  12. Smoking PROTECTIVE (paradoxically)

docs.title: Key Endoscopic/Histologic Features
docs.text:
CD: - Cobblestoning, linear ulcers, skip lesions - Non-caseating granulomas (pathognomonic if present) - Transmural inflammation

UC: - Continuous from rectum, loss of vascular pattern, pseudopolyps - Crypt abscesses, goblet cell depletion, basal plasmacytosis - Mucosal inflammation only

docs.title: Fecal Calprotectin
docs.text:
• >250 μg/g: Suggests organic disease (IBD) vs functional (IBS)
  • Useful for:
  • Screening
  • Monitoring activity
  • Detecting relapse
  • NOT specific for IBD (any inflammation)

docs.title: Treatment Pearls
docs.text:
1. 5-ASA:
  2. First-line mild-moderate UC (NOT CD)

  3. Topical > oral for distal disease

  4. Steroids:

  5. Induction ONLY (never maintenance)

  6. Steroid-dependent = escalate to immunomodulators/biologics

  7. Thiopurines (AZA/6-MP):

  8. Check TPMT before starting
  9. Slow onset (3-6 months)
  10. Monitor CBC, LFTs regularly

  11. Pancreatitis = discontinue permanently

  12. Anti-TNF:

  13. Screen for TB, Hep B before starting
  14. Infliximab: Best for fistulizing CD, acute severe UC

  15. Therapeutic drug monitoring: Optimize dosing

  16. Vedolizumab:

  17. Gut-selective (safer in elderly, infection risk)
  18. Slower onset (12-14 weeks)
  19. No TB reactivation

docs.title: Acute Severe UC (ASUC)
docs.text:
• ≥6 bloody stools/day + systemic toxicity
  • IV steroids × 3-5 days
  • If no improvement: Rescue (Infliximab or Cyclosporine) vs Surgery
  • Toxic megacolon (colon >6 cm): Emergency colectomy

docs.title: Surgical Considerations
docs.text:
CD: - NOT curative (70-80% require surgery) - High recurrence (50% at 10 years) - Post-op prophylaxis: Anti-TNF most effective

UC: - CURATIVE (total proctocolectomy) - IPAA (ileal pouch): Young patients, continent - Pouchitis: 50% at 10 years

docs.title: Colorectal Cancer Surveillance (UC)
docs.text:
• Start: 8 years (pancolitis), 15 years (left-sided)
  • Frequency: 1-3 years (higher if PSC - annual)
  • PSC: 5× CRC risk + annual colonoscopy from diagnosis
  • High-grade dysplasia: Colectomy (50% have cancer)

docs.title: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
docs.text:
• UC >> CD (70-80% have UC, 5% UC have PSC)
  • Does NOT improve with IBD treatment or colectomy
  • Cholangiocarcinoma risk: 10-20%
  • ↑ CRC risk: 5× (annual colonoscopy)
  • MRCP: Beading of bile ducts
  • Treatment: UDCA, liver transplant (curative)

docs.title: Extraintestinal Manifestations Mnemonic: "PEAS"
docs.text:
Parallels IBD activity: - **P**eripheral arthritis (pauciarticular) - **E**rythema nodosum - **A**phthous ulcers - **S**kin/eye (episcleritis)

Does NOT parallel: - Axial arthropathy (AS, sacroiliitis) - Pyoderma gangrenosum - Uveitis - PSC

docs.title: Perianal Crohn's Disease
docs.text:
• 40% of CD patients
  • Types: Fistulas (most common), fissures, abscesses, skin tags
  • Treatment:
  • Medical: Anti-TNF + antibiotics (metro + cipro)
  • Surgical: Seton drainage (non-cutting), fistulotomy
  • Drain abscesses before starting biologics

docs.title: Fistulizing Crohn's Disease
docs.text:
• Most common: Perianal
  • Other types: Enteroenteric, enterovesical (pneumaturia), enterovaginal
  • Best medical therapy: Infliximab
  • Rule out abscess (CT/MRI) before anti-TNF

docs.title: Pregnancy and IBD
docs.text:
• Conception: Best during remission
  • Medications:
  • Safe: 5-ASA, steroids, AZA/6-MP, anti-TNF (except certolizumab - safest)
  • Avoid: Methotrexate (Category X - teratogenic), tofacitinib
  • Disease activity: Maintain remission (active disease worsens outcomes)

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 25-year-old woman presents with 6 months of abdominal pain, non-bloody diarrhea, and 5 kg weight loss. Examination reveals right lower quadrant tenderness and a palpable mass. Colonoscopy shows patchy inflammation with skip lesions, cobblestoning, and terminal ileal ulceration. Biopsy reveals non-caseating granulomas. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

A) Ulcerative colitis B) Crohn's disease C) Intestinal tuberculosis D) Colorectal cancer

Answer: B) Crohn's disease

Explanation: - Crohn's disease is characterized by: - Transmural inflammation (can present as palpable mass) - Skip lesions (discontinuous inflammation) - Terminal ileal involvement (most common site) - Cobblestoning (deep ulcers with intervening edema) - Non-caseating granulomas (30-50% - pathognomonic if present) - Non-bloody diarrhea (ileal disease)

  • UC would show:
  • Continuous inflammation from rectum
  • Bloody diarrhea
  • Rectal involvement (always)

  • NO granulomas

  • Intestinal TB can mimic CD but:

  • Caseating granulomas (not non-caseating)
  • AFB staining positive
  • Risk factors (endemic area, immunosuppression)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 30-year-old man with known Crohn's disease presents with pneumaturia and recurrent urinary tract infections. CT scan shows an abnormal connection between the sigmoid colon and bladder. What type of complication has developed?

A) Enterovaginal fistula B) Enterocutaneous fistula C) Enterovesical fistula D) Perianal fistula

Answer: C) Enterovesical fistula

Explanation: - Enterovesical fistula = Abnormal connection between bowel and bladder - Classic triad: - Pneumaturia (air in urine - pathognomonic) - Fecaluria (stool in urine) - Recurrent UTIs

  • Diagnosis: CT with oral contrast (contrast in bladder), cystoscopy
  • Treatment:
  • Medical: Anti-TNF + antibiotics

  • Surgical: Resection of involved bowel segment + bladder repair

  • Other fistula types:

  • Enterovaginal: Feculent vaginal discharge
  • Enterocutaneous: Bowel to skin (stool from wound)
  • Perianal: Most common in CD (40%)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 28-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis is admitted with 8 bloody stools per day, fever 38.5°C, heart rate 110 bpm, and hemoglobin 9 g/dL. She is started on IV hydrocortisone. On day 4, her stool frequency remains 7 per day. What is the MOST appropriate next step?

A) Continue IV steroids for another week B) Start cyclosporine or infliximab (rescue therapy) C) Immediate colectomy D) Add oral mesalamine

Answer: B) Start cyclosporine or infliximab (rescue therapy)

Explanation: - This is Acute Severe UC (ASUC): - ≥6 bloody stools/day + systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, anemia)

  • Management:
  • IV steroids (hydrocortisone 100 mg q6h or methylprednisolone 60 mg daily)

  • Assess response at Day 3-5:

    • If no improvement → Rescue therapy or colectomy

  • Rescue therapy options:

  • Infliximab (5 mg/kg IV):

    • Preferred if long-term biologic likely
    • Lower colectomy rate

  • Cyclosporine (2-4 mg/kg/day IV):

    • Bridge to thiopurines
    • Avoid if cholesterol <120 (seizure risk)
    • Target trough 200-400 ng/mL

  • Surgery (subtotal colectomy):

  • If no response to rescue therapy by day 7

  • Toxic megacolon, perforation, massive bleeding

  • Continuing steroids beyond 5-7 days without improvement increases complications

  • 5-ASA is ineffective in severe disease

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Which of the following extraintestinal manifestations of IBD does NOT parallel disease activity and will NOT improve with treatment of the underlying IBD?

A) Peripheral arthritis B) Erythema nodosum C) Pyoderma gangrenosum D) Episcleritis

Answer: C) Pyoderma gangrenosum

Explanation: - Extraintestinal manifestations are classified by relationship to IBD activity:

Parallel IBD activity (improve with IBD treatment): - Peripheral arthritis (pauciarticular, large joints) - Erythema nodosum (tender nodules on shins) - Episcleritis (red eye, mild) - Aphthous stomatitis (oral ulcers)

Independent of IBD activity (do NOT improve with IBD treatment): - Pyoderma gangrenosum: - Painful ulcers with purple, undermined borders - Usually lower legs - Treatment: Topical/systemic steroids, anti-TNF (very effective) - Avoid debridement (pathergy - worsens with trauma)

  • Ankylosing spondylitis/Sacroiliitis
  • Uveitis (painful eye, photophobia - ophthalmology emergency)
  • Primary sclerosing cholangitis

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 35-year-old man with ulcerative pancolitis for 12 years presents with progressive fatigue and pruritis. Labs show elevated alkaline phosphatase (450 U/L) with normal bilirubin. MRCP shows multifocal strictures and dilations of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts ("beading"). What is the diagnosis and MOST important surveillance implication?

A) Autoimmune hepatitis; monitor for cirrhosis B) Primary biliary cholangitis; annual liver biopsy C) Primary sclerosing cholangitis; annual colonoscopy for colorectal cancer D) Drug-induced hepatotoxicity; stop all IBD medications

Answer: C) Primary sclerosing cholangitis; annual colonoscopy for colorectal cancer

Explanation: - Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC): - Cholestatic liver disease (↑ ALP >> AST/ALT) - MRCP: "Beading" (strictures + dilations of bile ducts) - diagnostic - UC >> CD (70-80% of PSC have UC, 5% of UC have PSC)

  • Complications:
  • Cholangiocarcinoma: 10-20% lifetime risk
  • Colorectal cancer: 5× increased risk (even after colectomy)

  • Cirrhosis, portal hypertension

  • Surveillance:

  • Annual colonoscopy with chromoendoscopy (from diagnosis) - CRITICAL

  • Annual MRCP or ultrasound + CA 19-9 (cholangiocarcinoma)

  • Management:

  • UDCA: May improve LFTs (survival benefit unclear)
  • Dominant stricture: ERCP + balloon dilation/stenting
  • Liver transplant: Only curative option

  • Does NOT improve with IBD treatment or colectomy

  • PSC-specific colorectal cancer risk:

  • 5× higher than UC alone
  • Annual surveillance (not 1-3 years like UC without PSC)
  • Risk persists even after colectomy

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 40-year-old woman with Crohn's disease is being started on azathioprine. Before initiating therapy, which enzyme should be checked to assess for increased toxicity risk?

A) G6PD B) TPMT (Thiopurine methyltransferase) C) CYP2C19 D) Pseudocholinesterase

Answer: B) TPMT (Thiopurine methyltransferase)

Explanation: - Thiopurines (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine): - Metabolized by TPMT enzyme - Low/absent TPMT activity → severe myelosuppression (life-threatening)

  • TPMT testing (before starting):
  • Low/absent: Avoid or reduce dose to 0.5-1 mg/kg (↑ toxicity risk)
  • Heterozygous (intermediate): Reduce dose

  • Normal: Standard dose (AZA 2-2.5 mg/kg)

  • AZA Monitoring:

  • CBC, LFTs: Every 1-2 weeks × 2 months, then every 3 months

  • 6-TGN levels: Therapeutic 230-450 pmol/8×10⁸ RBC

  • Side Effects:

  • Myelosuppression (dose-related)
  • Pancreatitis (3-7%, idiosyncratic - discontinue permanently)
  • Hepatotoxicity
  • Lymphoma: Small increased risk

  • Non-melanoma skin cancer: Increased risk

  • Other enzyme deficiencies:

  • G6PD: Hemolysis with oxidative drugs (sulfasalazine can cause, but not as critical)
  • CYP2C19: Proton pump inhibitor metabolism
  • Pseudocholinesterase: Succinylcholine metabolism

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 26-year-old man with perianal Crohn's disease has persistent perianal drainage. MRI pelvis shows a complex perianal fistula with a small abscess. What is the MOST appropriate initial management?

A) Start infliximab immediately B) Drain abscess, then start infliximab + antibiotics C) Seton placement alone D) Fecal diversion

Answer: B) Drain abscess, then start infliximab + antibiotics

Explanation: - Perianal Crohn's disease (40% of CD): - Fistulas, abscesses, fissures, skin tags

  • Management principles:

  • Rule out/drain abscess FIRST (before biologics)

    • CT/MRI pelvic
    • Exam under anesthesia (EUA) - gold standard for perianal assessment
    • Antibiotics contraindicated if undrained abscess (can worsen)

  • Medical therapy:

    • Infliximab (anti-TNF) - most effective for fistula healing
    • Antibiotics: Metronidazole + Ciprofloxacin (adjunct)
    • Immunomodulators: AZA/6-MP (maintenance)

  • Surgical:

    • Non-cutting seton: Drainage, prevent abscess (long-term)
    • Fistulotomy: Simple, low fistulas
    • Fecal diversion: Refractory cases (stoma)

  • Starting anti-TNF with undrained abscess = contraindicated (sepsis risk)

  • Perianal fistula healing:

  • Infliximab: 30-50% complete healing
  • Often requires combination (anti-TNF + seton + antibiotics)
  • May need long-term maintenance

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Which of the following is the PRIMARY advantage of vedolizumab over anti-TNF agents in treating IBD?

A) Faster onset of action B) Oral administration C) Gut-selective with lower systemic infection risk D) Lower cost

Answer: C) Gut-selective with lower systemic infection risk

Explanation: - Vedolizumab (VDZ): - α4β7 integrin antagonist - Gut-selective (blocks lymphocyte trafficking to GI tract)

  • Advantages:
  • Lower systemic infection risk (vs anti-TNF)
  • No tuberculosis reactivation (vs anti-TNF)
  • Good safety profile in elderly, immunocompromised

  • Good option for anti-TNF contraindications (heart failure, demyelinating disorders, recurrent infections)

  • Disadvantages:

  • Slower onset: 12-14 weeks (vs 4-6 weeks for anti-TNF)
  • IV infusion (not oral)

  • Similar cost to anti-TNF

  • Dosing:

  • Induction: 300 mg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks

  • Maintenance: 300 mg IV every 8 weeks

  • Side Effects:

  • Generally well-tolerated
  • Infusion reactions (rare)
  • PML: Very rare (unlike natalizumab)

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A 45-year-old woman with a 20-year history of ulcerative pancolitis undergoes surveillance colonoscopy. Biopsies reveal high-grade dysplasia in the ascending colon. What is the MOST appropriate management?

A) Repeat colonoscopy in 3 months B) Endoscopic mucosal resection C) Total proctocolectomy D) Intensify medical therapy with biologics

Answer: C) Total proctocolectomy

Explanation: - Colorectal cancer risk in UC: - Significantly increased with duration and extent - Surveillance: Start 8 years (pancolitis), 15 years (left-sided)

  • Dysplasia management:

High-Grade Dysplasia (HGD): - Total proctocolectomy recommended - 50% have concurrent cancer (may be invisible) - Endoscopic resection only if: - Dysplasia in discrete, completely resectable lesion - No flat dysplasia - Close surveillance acceptable

Low-Grade Dysplasia (LGD): - Visible lesion: Endoscopic resection + surveillance - Flat/invisible: Consider colectomy vs intensive surveillance (every 3-6 months)

Indefinite for Dysplasia: - Repeat colonoscopy in 3-6 months - Treat inflammation (may improve grading)

  • This patient:
  • 20-year pancolitis (very high cancer risk)
  • High-grade dysplasia → Colectomy (curative)

  • IPAA (ileal pouch) if good candidate

  • UC with PSC:

  • Even higher CRC risk (5×)
  • Annual colonoscopy from diagnosis
  • Lower threshold for colectomy

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 32-year-old man with Crohn's disease undergoes ileocolic resection for stricturing disease. What is the MOST effective prophylaxis to prevent post-operative recurrence?

A) Mesalamine B) Metronidazole for 3 months C) Anti-TNF therapy (infliximab/adalimumab) D) No prophylaxis needed

Answer: C) Anti-TNF therapy (infliximab/adalimumab)

Explanation: - Crohn's disease recurrence after surgery: - Surgery NOT curative - Endoscopic recurrence: 70-90% at 1 year - Clinical recurrence: 50% at 10 years - Re-operation: 30-50% at 10 years

  • Post-operative prophylaxis (high-risk patients):

Risk factors for recurrence: - Smoking (most important modifiable) - Prior resection - Penetrating behavior (fistulas) - Perianal disease - Extensive resection (>50 cm)

Prophylaxis efficacy: - Anti-TNF (infliximab/adalimumab): MOST EFFECTIVE - Reduces endoscopic and clinical recurrence - Start within 4 weeks post-op

  • Thiopurines (AZA/6-MP): Moderate efficacy

  • Metronidazole: Modest benefit (3 months)

    • Side effects limit long-term use

  • Mesalamine: NOT effective for CD prophylaxis

  • Surveillance:

  • Ileocolonoscopy: 6-12 months post-op

  • Rutgeerts Score: Assess endoscopic recurrence

    • i0-i1: Low risk
    • i2-i4: High risk → escalate therapy

  • Smoking cessation: CRITICAL (doubles recurrence risk)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) encompasses Crohn's disease and Ulcerative colitis, chronic relapsing-remitting inflammatory GI conditions with distinct features:

Key Distinctions: - Crohn's: Any GI location, transmural, skip lesions, granulomas, fistulas common, NOT curative with surgery - UC: Colon/rectum only, mucosal, continuous from rectum, NO granulomas, CURATIVE with colectomy

Diagnosis: Colonoscopy with biopsies (gold standard), fecal calprotectin (screening), CTE/MRE (small bowel CD)

Treatment: - UC mild-moderate: 5-ASA first-line - Moderate-severe: Steroids (induction) → Immunomodulators/Biologics (maintenance) - Anti-TNF: Infliximab/Adalimumab (fistulizing CD, moderate-severe disease, ASUC rescue) - Vedolizumab: Gut-selective (safer, slower onset) - ASUC: IV steroids → Rescue (IFX/cyclosporine) vs colectomy

Complications: - CD: Strictures, fistulas, abscesses, malabsorption - UC: Toxic megacolon, perforation, massive bleeding, colorectal cancer (surveillance)

Extraintestinal Manifestations: Arthritis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis, PSC (5× CRC risk)

Surveillance: UC pancolitis - colonoscopy every 1-3 years starting 8 years; PSC - ANNUAL

End of Topic 22: Inflammatory Bowel Disease

Next Topic: Acute & Chronic Diarrhea (Medicine/23_Acute_and_Chronic_Diarrhea.md)

Week 2 Progress: 2/18 topics complete

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition and Classification
  2. Pathophysiology
  3. Acute Diarrhea
  4. Chronic Diarrhea
  5. Diagnostic Approach
  6. Management
  7. Special Situations
  8. High-Yield Points
  9. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Diarrhea: - Increase in stool frequency (≥3 stools/day) AND/OR - Decrease in stool consistency (loose/watery) AND/OR - Increase in stool weight (>200 g/day)

docs.title: Duration-Based Classification
docs.text:
Acute Diarrhea: - <14 days duration - Usually infectious - Self-limited in most cases

Persistent Diarrhea: - 14 days to 4 weeks - May be infectious or post-infectious

Chronic Diarrhea: - >4 weeks duration - Usually non-infectious etiology - Requires systematic evaluation

docs.title: 1. Secretory Diarrhea
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Active secretion of electrolytes (Cl⁻) into lumen - Water follows osmotically - No osmotic gap

Characteristics: - Large volume (>1 L/day) - Watery (like "rice water") - Persists with fasting (doesn't stop) - No blood or pus - Stool osmotic gap <50 mOsm/kg

Calculation: 

Stool Osmotic Gap = 290 - 2([Na⁺] + [K⁺])stool

<50 mOsm/kg = Secretory
>125 mOsm/kg = Osmotic
50-125 mOsm/kg = Mixed

Causes: - Toxins: Cholera (CT), ETEC (LT/ST), Staph aureus (enterotoxin) - Hormones: VIPoma, gastrinoma, carcinoid - Bile acids: Ileal resection, bile acid malabsorption - Laxatives: Senna, bisacodyl - Villous adenoma - Medications: Colchicine, misoprostol

docs.title: 2. Osmotic Diarrhea
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Non-absorbed solutes in lumen - Water drawn into lumen osmotically - Osmotic gap present

Characteristics: - Stops with fasting (KEY feature) - Moderate volume - No blood - Stool osmotic gap >125 mOsm/kg

Causes: - Carbohydrate malabsorption: - Lactose intolerance (most common) - Fructose, sorbitol - Laxatives: Magnesium, lactulose, polyethylene glycol (PEG) - Maldigestion: Pancreatic insufficiency (steatorrhea)

docs.title: 3. Inflammatory Diarrhea
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Mucosal damage → disrupted absorption + inflammation - Exudation of blood, mucus, pus

Characteristics: - Small, frequent stools - Blood and/or mucus (dysentery) - Fever, tenesmus - Fecal leukocytes/lactoferrin POSITIVE

Causes: - Invasive infections: - Bacteria: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, C. diff, EHEC - Parasites: Entamoeba histolytica - IBD (Crohn's, UC) - Ischemic colitis - Radiation colitis

docs.title: 4. Dysmotility (Altered Transit)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Rapid transit → decreased absorption time - OR Bacterial overgrowth (slow transit)

Causes: - Rapid transit: - IBS-D (diarrhea-predominant) - Post-vagotomy - Hyperthyroidism - Diabetic neuropathy

  • Slow transit (bacterial overgrowth):
  • Scleroderma
  • Diabetes
  • Small bowel diverticula

docs.title: Pathophysiology Summary Table
docs.text: Type Volume Fasting Effect Blood/Mucus Osmotic Gap Examples Secretory Large (>1 L) Persists Absent <50 Cholera, VIPoma, Bile acids Osmotic Moderate STOPS Absent >125 Lactose intolerance, Laxatives Inflammatory Small, frequent Variable Present Variable Shigella, C. diff, IBD Dysmotility Variable Variable Absent Variable IBS-D, Hyperthyroidism

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Most common cause: Viral gastroenteritis (60-70%)
  • Self-limited: 90% resolve in <7 days
  • Mortality: Mainly in extremes of age (dehydration)

docs.title: Viral (Most Common - 60-70%)
docs.text:
1. Norovirus: - #1 cause of epidemic gastroenteritis - Outbreaks: Cruise ships, nursing homes, restaurants - Incubation: 24-48 hours - Presentation: - Acute onset nausea, vomiting (prominent) - Watery diarrhea - Cramping, low-grade fever - Duration: 1-3 days - Diagnosis: Clinical (testing rarely needed) - Management: Supportive

2. Rotavirus: - Most common in children <5 years (pre-vaccine) - Vaccine available: Reduced incidence - Presentation: Vomiting, watery diarrhea, fever - Diagnosis: Stool antigen - Management: Oral rehydration

3. Adenovirus (types 40, 41): - Children (long-lasting - up to 2 weeks)

docs.title: Bacterial
docs.text:
A. Preformed Toxin (Rapid Onset - <6 hours):

Staphylococcus aureus: - Incubation: 1-6 hours (shortest) - Source: Mayonnaise, cream pastries, food handlers - Toxin: Heat-stable enterotoxin (preformed) - Presentation: - Severe nausea, vomiting (predominant) - Watery diarrhea - NO fever - Duration: 12-24 hours (very short) - Management: Supportive

Bacillus cereus: - 2 syndromes: - Emetic (1-6 hrs): Fried rice, vomiting predominant (preformed toxin) - Diarrheal (8-16 hrs): Meat, vegetables (in-vivo toxin) - Management: Supportive

B. Secretory Toxin (Watery Diarrhea):

Vibrio cholerae: - Cholera toxin (CT): Activates adenylate cyclase → ↑ cAMP → Cl⁻ secretion - Incubation: Hours to 5 days - Presentation: - Profuse watery diarrhea ("rice-water stools") - Massive volume loss (up to 1 L/hour) - Severe dehydration (hypovolemic shock) - NO fever, NO blood - Diagnosis: - Stool microscopy: Darting motility ("shooting star") - Stool culture: TCBS agar - Management: - Aggressive fluid repletion (IV/oral) - Antibiotics: Doxycycline (reduces duration, shedding) - Alternatives: Azithromycin, ciprofloxacin

Enterotoxigenic E. coli (ETEC): - Traveler's diarrhea (#1 bacterial cause) - Heat-labile (LT) and heat-stable (ST) toxins - Presentation: Watery diarrhea, cramping, nausea - Management: - Prevention: Avoid tap water, raw food - Treatment: Supportive; Rifaximin (if severe)

C. Invasive/Inflammatory (Bloody Diarrhea - Dysentery):

Campylobacter jejuni: - Most common bacterial cause in developed countries - Source: Undercooked poultry, unpasteurized milk - Incubation: 2-5 days - Presentation: - Bloody diarrhea (colitis) - Fever, cramping - Fecal leukocytes POSITIVE - Complications: - Guillain-Barré syndrome (1-2 weeks post-infection) - Reactive arthritis - Diagnosis: Stool culture - Management: - Mild: Supportive - Severe/immunocompromised: Azithromycin or fluoroquinolone

Salmonella (non-typhoidal): - Source: Poultry, eggs, reptiles (turtles, iguanas) - Presentation: - Watery or bloody diarrhea - Fever, cramping - Complications: - Bacteremia (especially sickle cell, HIV) - Reactive arthritis - Osteomyelitis (sickle cell - Salmonella #1) - Management: - Uncomplicated: NO antibiotics (prolongs carrier state) - Severe/high-risk: Ciprofloxacin or azithromycin - High-risk: <3 months, >50 years, immunocompromised, prosthetic valves

Shigella: - Most virulent (low inoculum - 10-100 organisms) - Fecal-oral (person-to-person) - Presentation: - Bloody, mucoid diarrhea (dysentery) - High fever - Tenesmus - Complications: - Seizures (children - Shigella dysenteriae neurotoxin) - HUS (Shigella dysenteriae type 1 - Shiga toxin) - Reactive arthritis - Diagnosis: Stool culture - Management: - Antibiotics: Fluoroquinolone or azithromycin (reduces duration, transmission)

Enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC) - E. coli O157:H7: - Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) - Source: Undercooked beef, unpasteurized juice/milk - Presentation: - Bloody diarrhea (hemorrhagic colitis) - Severe cramping - NO fever (or low-grade) - Complications: - Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS): - 5-10% of cases (especially children <5 years) - Triad: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury - Presents 5-7 days after diarrhea onset - Diagnosis: - Stool culture (sorbitol-negative on MacConkey) - Shiga toxin assay (more sensitive) - Management: - Supportive ONLY - NO antibiotics (may ↑ HUS risk by releasing more toxin) - NO antimotility agents - Monitor for HUS (CBC, creatinine)

Clostridioides difficile (C. diff): - Antibiotic-associated diarrhea - Risk factors: - Recent antibiotics (within 8 weeks): - Highest risk: Clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins - Age >65, hospitalization, PPI use - Presentation: - Watery diarrhea (3-15 stools/day) - Lower abdominal cramping - Fever, leukocytosis - Fecal leukocytes POSITIVE - Complications: - Fulminant colitis: Toxic megacolon, perforation, shock - Diagnosis: - Stool PCR (toxin genes) OR - EIA (toxins A/B) - less sensitive - Colonoscopy: Pseudomembranes (yellow plaques) - Management: - Discontinue offending antibiotic (if possible) - Non-severe: - Vancomycin 125 mg PO QID × 10 days OR - Fidaxomicin 200 mg PO BID × 10 days (lower recurrence) - Severe (WBC >15,000, Cr ≥1.5× baseline): - Vancomycin 125 mg PO QID × 10 days - Fulminant (hypotension, shock, ileus, megacolon): - Vancomycin 500 mg PO QID + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TID - Consider Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) - Surgery if perforation - Recurrent (≥2 episodes): - Fidaxomicin OR FMT (90% cure rate)

D. Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Vibrio vulnificus: - Seafood (raw oysters) - Coastal exposure - V. vulnificus: Hemorrhagic bullae (especially cirrhosis, immunocompromised)

docs.title: Parasitic
docs.text:
Giardia lamblia: - Most common parasitic cause in US - Transmission: Contaminated water (camping, daycare) - Incubation: 1-2 weeks - Presentation: - Watery, foul-smelling diarrhea - Bloating, flatulence - Steatorrhea (fat malabsorption) - Chronic if untreated - Diagnosis: - Stool antigen (ELISA) - most sensitive - Stool microscopy: Trophozoites ("falling leaf" motility), cysts - String test (Enterotest) - Management: - Metronidazole 250 mg TID × 5-7 days OR - Tinidazole 2 g single dose (preferred - single dose)

Entamoeba histolytica: - Endemic: Developing countries, travelers - Presentation: - Bloody diarrhea (amoebic dysentery) - Flask-shaped ulcers (colonoscopy) - Complications: - Liver abscess ("anchovy paste" aspirate) - Diagnosis: - Stool microscopy: Trophozoites with ingested RBCs - Stool antigen (E. histolytica-specific) - Management: - Metronidazole 750 mg TID × 10 days - THEN Paromomycin (luminal agent to clear cysts)

Cryptosporidium: - Immunocompromised (HIV with CD4 <100) - Waterborne (chlorine-resistant oocysts) - Presentation: - Watery diarrhea (profuse in immunocompromised) - Self-limited in immunocompetent - Diagnosis: Stool acid-fast stain (oocysts) - Management: - Immunocompetent: Supportive - Immunocompromised: Nitazoxanide + ART (restore immunity)

docs.title: Red Flags (Require Workup):
docs.text:
• Severe dehydration
  • Bloody diarrhea (dysentery)
  • Fever ≥38.5°C (101.3°F)
  • Severe abdominal pain
  • Duration >7 days
  • Age >70 years
  • Immunocompromised
  • Recent antibiotics (C. diff)
  • Recent hospitalization
  • Outbreak (multiple cases)

docs.title: Stool Studies (if red flags present):
docs.text:
Routine: - Stool culture: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter - C. diff testing: If recent antibiotics - Fecal leukocytes/lactoferrin: Inflammatory diarrhea

Additional (based on exposure): - Ova and parasites: Travel, daycare, camping - Shiga toxin assay: Bloody diarrhea, no fever - Vibrio culture: Raw seafood, coastal exposure

docs.title: When NOT to Test:
docs.text:
• Mild, watery diarrhea
  • No red flags
  • Duration <3 days
  • Likely viral

docs.title: 1. Rehydration (Cornerstone)
docs.text:
Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS): - First-line for mild-moderate dehydration - WHO-ORS formula: - Sodium 75 mEq/L - Glucose 75 mmol/L - Potassium 20 mEq/L - Chloride, citrate

  • Mechanism: Glucose-coupled Na⁺ absorption (SGLT1 - intact even in cholera)

Indications for IV Fluids: - Severe dehydration - Shock - Altered mental status - Inability to tolerate oral (vomiting)

Fluid Choice: - Isotonic saline or Ringer's lactate - Large volumes may be needed (cholera: 200 mL/kg in 24 hrs)

docs.title: 2. Diet
docs.text:
• Continue regular diet (especially in children)
  • BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) - low fiber, bland
  • Avoid: Dairy (lactase deficiency - transient), high-fat, caffeine, alcohol

docs.title: 3. Antimotility Agents
docs.text:
Loperamide (Imodium): - Mechanism: μ-opioid agonist (slows transit, increases absorption) - Dosing: 4 mg initial, then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day) - Effective for non-inflammatory diarrhea

Contraindications: - Bloody diarrhea (invasive pathogens) - High fever - C. diff - E. coli O157:H7 (↑ HUS risk) - Toxic megacolon risk

Bismuth Subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol): - Mild benefit - Black stools (bismuth sulfide - benign)

docs.title: 4. Antibiotics
docs.text:
Indications: - Traveler's diarrhea (empiric) - Severe disease: High fever, bloody diarrhea, signs of sepsis - Immunocompromised - Specific pathogens: - Shigella (always) - Campylobacter (if severe) - C. diff - Giardia - Cholera

Empiric Regimen (Traveler's Diarrhea): - Rifaximin 200 mg TID × 3 days (non-invasive) - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 3 days (if invasive - fever, blood) - Azithromycin 1 g single dose OR 500 mg daily × 3 days - Preferred in SE Asia (fluoroquinolone resistance)

Do NOT use antibiotics: - E. coli O157:H7 (↑ HUS risk) - Uncomplicated Salmonella (prolongs carrier state)

docs.title: 5. Probiotics
docs.text:
• Lactobacillus, Saccharomyces boulardii
  • Modest benefit: Reduce duration by ~1 day
  • Best evidence: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea prevention

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: • >4 weeks duration

docs.title: Approach by Mechanism
docs.text: Key Initial Questions: 1. Stool characteristics: Watery, fatty (steatorrhea), bloody/mucoid 2. Volume: Large (small bowel) vs small frequent (colon) 3. Fasting effect: Stops (osmotic) vs persists (secretory) 4. Associated symptoms: Weight loss, pain, urgency

docs.title: 1. Watery Diarrhea
docs.text:
A. Secretory:

Clinical Features: - Large volume (>1 L/day) - Persists with fasting - No blood/mucus - Nocturnal (wakes patient from sleep)

Stool Analysis: - Osmotic gap <50 mOsm/kg - Fecal Na⁺ >70 mEq/L

Causes:

Medications/Laxatives: - Stimulant laxatives: Senna, bisacodyl - Colchicine - Metformin - SSRIs - Magnesium (if osmotic gap done on supernatant - Mg not measured)

Bile Acid Diarrhea: - Ileal resection (>100 cm): - Bile acids enter colon → secretion - Ileal disease (Crohn's): - Decreased bile acid reabsorption - Idiopathic (bile acid malabsorption): - 25-30% of chronic diarrhea - Diagnosis: - SeHCAT scan (bile acid retention - not available in US) - Therapeutic trial: Cholestyramine (bile acid sequestrant) - Treatment: - Cholestyramine 4-16 g/day

Endocrine:

VIPoma (Verner-Morrison Syndrome): - Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (VIP) secreting tumor - Presentation: - Massive watery diarrhea ("pancreatic cholera") - Hypokalemia (severe) - Achlorhydria (VIP inhibits gastric acid) - WDHA Syndrome: Watery Diarrhea, Hypokalemia, Achlorhydria - Diagnosis: - Elevated VIP (>200 pg/mL) - CT/MRI: Pancreatic mass - Treatment: - Octreotide (somatostatin analog) - Surgical resection

Carcinoid Syndrome: - Serotonin-secreting neuroendocrine tumor - Presentation: - Watery diarrhea - Flushing (face, neck) - Wheezing (bronchoconstriction) - Right-sided heart disease (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis) - Diagnosis: - 24-hour urine 5-HIAA (serotonin metabolite) - >25 mg/24 hr - Chromogranin A (elevated) - Somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (Octreoscan) - Treatment: - Octreotide - Surgical resection

Gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome): - Covered in PUD topic - Diarrhea + PUD (multiple, refractory ulcers)

Hyperthyroidism: - Increased motility - Associated symptoms: Weight loss, palpitations, tremor

Addison's Disease: - Chronic diarrhea + hyperpigmentation, hypotension

Microscopic Colitis: - Lymphocytic or Collagenous colitis - Presentation: - Chronic watery diarrhea (secretory) - Middle-aged/elderly women - Normal colonoscopy (MUST biopsy) - Associations: - Medications: NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs - Celiac disease, autoimmune conditions - Diagnosis: - Colonoscopy with biopsies (random biopsies even if normal-appearing) - Histology: - Lymphocytic: Intraepithelial lymphocytes (>20/100 epithelial cells) - Collagenous: Subepithelial collagen band (>10 μm) - Treatment: - Discontinue offending drug (NSAIDs) - Budesonide 9 mg daily (first-line) - Loperamide (symptom control) - Bismuth subsalicylate

Villous Adenoma (Large): - Secretes K⁺-rich mucus → hypokalemia

B. Osmotic:

Clinical Features: - Stops with fasting (KEY) - Moderate volume - Improves with removal of offending agent

Stool Analysis: - Osmotic gap >125 mOsm/kg

Causes:

Carbohydrate Malabsorption:

Lactose Intolerance: - Primary: Lactase deficiency (genetic - common in Asians, Africans, Native Americans) - Secondary: Post-gastroenteritis, celiac disease, Crohn's - Presentation: - Diarrhea, bloating, cramping after dairy - Diagnosis: - Hydrogen breath test (lactose given → H₂ production by colonic bacteria) - Trial lactose-free diet - Treatment: - Lactose-free diet - Lactase supplements (Lactaid)

Fructose, Sorbitol: - Fruits, artificial sweeteners - Diagnosis: Trial elimination diet

Laxatives: - Magnesium (Milk of Magnesia) - Lactulose (hepatic encephalopathy) - PEG (MiraLAX)

Maldigestion:

Pancreatic Insufficiency: - Covered in detail below (Fatty Diarrhea - Steatorrhea) - Osmotic component: Undigested nutrients

docs.title: 2. Fatty Diarrhea (Steatorrhea)
docs.text:
Clinical Features: - Bulky, greasy, foul-smelling stools - Float (high fat content) - Difficult to flush - Weight loss (fat-soluble vitamin deficiency)

Quantification: - Fecal fat >7 g/day (72-hour fecal fat collection on 100 g fat diet) - Sudan stain: Quick bedside test (fat globules)

Pathophysiology: - Maldigestion: Defective intraluminal breakdown (pancreatic/bile) - Malabsorption: Defective mucosal uptake (small bowel disease)

A. Maldigestion (Intraluminal):

Pancreatic Insufficiency:

Causes: - Chronic pancreatitis (most common) - Cystic fibrosis - Pancreatic cancer (obstructing duct) - Pancreatic resection - Zollinger-Ellison (inactivates pancreatic enzymes)

Clinical Features: - Steatorrhea (>90% exocrine function lost) - Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (A, D, E, K) - Diabetes (endocrine insufficiency) - History of pancreatitis, alcohol

Diagnosis: - Fecal elastase-1: <200 μg/g stool (low = pancreatic insufficiency) - Specific but low sensitivity - 72-hour fecal fat: >7 g/day (gold standard) - Secretin stimulation test: Research - Imaging: CT/MRI (pancreatic calcifications, atrophy, duct dilation)

Treatment: - Pancreatic enzyme replacement (PERT): - Pancrelipase (Creon, Zenpep) - 40,000-50,000 units lipase with each meal - 25,000 units with snacks - Take with meals - PPI: If inadequate response (↑ duodenal pH for enzyme activity) - Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) - Monitor: Weight, stool frequency, fat-soluble vitamins

Bile Acid Deficiency:

Causes: - Cholestasis: PBC, PSC, biliary obstruction - Ileal resection (>100 cm) or disease - Bacterial overgrowth (deconjugates bile acids)

Diagnosis: - Elevated bilirubin, ALP (cholestasis) - Low fecal elastase (if coexisting pancreatic insufficiency)

Treatment: - Treat underlying cause - Medium-chain triglycerides (MCT): Don't require bile acids for absorption - Fat-soluble vitamins

B. Malabsorption (Mucosal):

Celiac Disease:

Definition: - Autoimmune enteropathy triggered by gluten (wheat, barley, rye) - HLA-DQ2 (90%) or HLA-DQ8 (10%)

Presentation: - Diarrhea, steatorrhea - Weight loss - Bloating, flatulence - Anemia (iron, folate deficiency) - Osteoporosis (vitamin D, calcium malabsorption) - Dermatitis herpetiformis: Pruritic vesicles (extensor surfaces)

Diagnosis:

Serology (on gluten-containing diet): - IgA anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG): Most sensitive/specific - IgA anti-endomysial antibody (EMA): Highly specific - Total IgA: Rule out IgA deficiency (5% of celiac) - If IgA deficient: Use IgG-based tests (IgG-tTG, IgG-DGP)

Endoscopy: - Duodenal biopsy (gold standard): - Villous atrophy - Crypt hyperplasia - Intraepithelial lymphocytosis (>25/100 epithelial cells) - Marsh Classification: Grade severity

Additional Tests: - HLA-DQ2/DQ8: High negative predictive value (if negative, celiac unlikely) - Labs: Iron, folate, vitamin D, calcium

Treatment: - Lifelong gluten-free diet (ONLY treatment) - Serologic follow-up: tTG should normalize in 6-12 months - Endoscopic follow-up: If symptoms persist despite diet - Complications: - Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL): Rare (if non-adherent) - Refractory celiac disease: Consider steroids, immunosuppressants

Tropical Sprue: - Endemic: Tropical regions (Caribbean, India, Southeast Asia) - Presentation: Steatorrhea, folate/B12 deficiency, megaloblastic anemia - Diagnosis: Duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy - similar to celiac) - Treatment: Tetracycline + Folate × 6 months

Whipple's Disease: - Tropheryma whipplei (bacteria) - Presentation: - Steatorrhea, weight loss - Migratory arthritis (precedes GI symptoms) - Neurologic symptoms (oculomasticatory myorhythmia) - Fever, lymphadenopathy - Diagnosis: - Duodenal biopsy: PAS-positive macrophages (foamy) - PCR for T. whipplei - Treatment: Ceftriaxone × 2 weeks → TMP-SMX × 1 year

Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (SIBO):

Definition: - Excessive bacteria (>10⁵ CFU/mL) in small bowel

Risk Factors: - Anatomic: Blind loops, strictures, diverticula, post-surgical (Billroth II) - Motility disorders: Scleroderma, diabetes, chronic opioids - Achlorhydria: PPI, atrophic gastritis - Immune deficiency: IgA deficiency

Presentation: - Steatorrhea (bile acid deconjugation) - Bloating, flatulence (bacterial fermentation) - Vitamin B12 deficiency (bacteria consume B12) - Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency

Diagnosis: - Glucose or lactulose breath test: - Early H₂ rise (within 90 minutes) = SIBO - Small bowel aspirate and culture: Gold standard (invasive, not routine) - Therapeutic trial: Response to antibiotics

Treatment: - Antibiotics: - Rifaximin 550 mg TID × 14 days - Metronidazole or Ciprofloxacin - Rotate antibiotics if recurrent (cyclic therapy) - Treat underlying cause: Correct strictures, optimize motility - Probiotics: Controversial

Crohn's Disease: - Terminal ileum involvement → bile acid malabsorption, B12 deficiency - Extensive small bowel disease → malabsorption

Short Bowel Syndrome: - Extensive resection (>200 cm small bowel removed) - Presentation: Steatorrhea, malabsorption, dehydration - Management: - Nutritional support (TPN initially) - High-calorie diet, small frequent meals - Loperamide (slow transit) - Octreotide (reduce secretions) - Teduglutide (GLP-2 analog - promotes adaptation)

docs.title: 3. Inflammatory Diarrhea (Bloody/Mucoid)
docs.text:
Clinical Features: - Blood and/or mucus - Small, frequent stools - Urgency, tenesmus - Fecal leukocytes POSITIVE

Causes:

Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD): - Ulcerative Colitis - Crohn's Disease - Covered in IBD topic

Chronic Infections (Immunocompromised): - CMV colitis (HIV, transplant) - Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) - Chronic parasites: Entamoeba

Ischemic Colitis: - Elderly, vascular disease - Presentation: Abrupt bloody diarrhea, LLQ pain (splenic flexure) - Diagnosis: Colonoscopy (thumbprinting, segmental distribution) - Management: Supportive (most resolve); surgery if gangrene

Radiation Colitis: - History of pelvic radiation - Chronic bloody diarrhea - Telangiectasias (colonoscopy)

Colon Cancer: - Change in bowel habits - Hematochezia - Weight loss, anemia

docs.title: 4. Dysmotility
docs.text:
Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS):

Rome IV Criteria: - Recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week (last 3 months) associated with ≥2: 1. Related to defecation 2. Change in stool frequency 3. Change in stool form

Subtypes: - IBS-D (diarrhea-predominant) - IBS-C (constipation-predominant) - IBS-M (mixed)

Diagnosis of Exclusion: - Red flags (exclude organic disease): - Age >50 (no prior screening) - Alarm symptoms: Weight loss, blood, anemia, fever, family history of colon cancer/IBD - Nocturnal symptoms

Workup: - Minimal if no red flags: - CBC, CRP, fecal calprotectin (rule out IBD) - Celiac serology (tTG) - Thyroid function

Management: - Lifestyle: Low-FODMAP diet, stress management - Medications: - IBS-D: - Loperamide (first-line) - Rifaximin (antibiotic - reduces bloating) - Eluxadoline (μ-opioid agonist) - Alosetron (5-HT3 antagonist - women only, ischemic colitis risk) - Antispasmodics: Dicyclomine, hyoscyamine - Antidepressants: TCAs (low-dose), SSRIs

Hyperthyroidism: - Increased motility - Diarrhea + weight loss, palpitations, tremor - Diagnosis: Low TSH, elevated T4

Diabetic Autonomic Neuropathy: - Nocturnal diarrhea (alternating with constipation) - Other autonomic symptoms: Orthostatic hypotension, gastroparesis

docs.title: Step 1: History and Exam
docs.text: Key Questions: 1. Duration: Acute vs chronic 2. Stool characteristics: Watery, fatty, bloody 3. Volume: Large (small bowel) vs small frequent (colon) 4. Timing: Nocturnal, postprandial, fasting 5. Associated symptoms: Weight loss, pain, fever 6. Diet: Dairy, sorbitol, caffeine 7. Medications: Laxatives, antibiotics, metformin, PPIs 8. Exposures: Travel, well water, raw food 9. Past history: Surgery (bowel resection), radiation 10. Family history: IBD, celiac, colon cancer

docs.title: Step 2: Initial Laboratory Studies
docs.text:
Basic Labs: - CBC: Anemia (iron, B12, folate deficiency; IBD) - CMP: Electrolytes (hypokalemia - secretory), albumin (malnutrition) - TSH: Hyperthyroidism - Celiac serology: IgA-tTG (if watery or fatty diarrhea)

Stool Studies: - Fecal calprotectin: IBD vs IBS (>250 suggests IBD) - Fecal leukocytes/lactoferrin: Inflammatory - Stool culture, O&P: If infectious suspected - C. diff testing: If recent antibiotics

72-Hour Fecal Fat: - If steatorrhea suspected - >7 g/day abnormal (on 100 g fat diet)

Stool Osmotic Gap: - If watery diarrhea: - <50: Secretory - >125: Osmotic

docs.title: Step 3: Endoscopy
docs.text:
Colonoscopy with Biopsies: - Indications: - Age >50 (cancer screening due) - Alarm symptoms: Blood, weight loss, anemia - Persistent diarrhea without clear cause - Elevated fecal calprotectin

  • Obtain biopsies even if normal-appearing (microscopic colitis, celiac)

Upper Endoscopy (EGD) with Duodenal Biopsies: - Indications: - Steatorrhea (celiac, Whipple's, tropical sprue, giardia) - Positive celiac serology - Unexplained malabsorption

docs.title: Step 4: Specialized Testing
docs.text:
If Secretory Diarrhea (after excluding meds/laxatives): - Serum VIP: VIPoma - 24-hour urine 5-HIAA: Carcinoid - Serum gastrin: Gastrinoma - Serum calcitonin: Medullary thyroid cancer

If Steatorrhea (after EGD/colonoscopy): - Fecal elastase: Pancreatic insufficiency - Secretin stimulation test: Pancreatic function (rarely done) - CT/MRCP: Pancreatic imaging

If Osmotic Diarrhea: - Lactose hydrogen breath test: Lactose intolerance - Trial elimination: Fructose, sorbitol

If Suspected SIBO: - Glucose/lactulose breath test

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text:
1. Treat Underlying Cause (most important)

2. Symptomatic Management:

Antidiarrheals: - Loperamide: 2-4 mg PRN (max 16 mg/day) - Contraindicated: Bloody diarrhea, C. diff, severe IBD

  • Diphenoxylate-atropine (Lomotil): Alternative

Bile Acid Sequestrants: - Cholestyramine 4-16 g/day (bile acid diarrhea)

Dietary Modifications: - Lactose-free (lactose intolerance) - Gluten-free (celiac) - Low-FODMAP (IBS) - Low-fat (steatorrhea - if enzyme replacement inadequate)

Probiotics: - Lactobacillus, Saccharomyces boulardii - Modest benefit

docs.title: Traveler's Diarrhea
docs.text:
Definition: - ≥3 unformed stools in 24 hours during or shortly after travel

Etiology: - Bacterial (80%): ETEC (#1), Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella - Viral (10%): Norovirus - Parasitic (10%): Giardia, Cryptosporidium

Risk Factors: - Destination: High-risk (Latin America, Africa, Asia) - Food/water: Tap water, ice, raw vegetables, street food

Prevention: - "Boil it, cook it, peel it, or forget it" - Avoid tap water, ice, raw produce - Bismuth subsalicylate 525 mg QID (50% reduction) - Rifaximin 200 mg daily (prophylaxis - expensive)

Management:

Mild (1-2 stools, no symptoms): - Loperamide 4 mg initial, 2 mg after each stool (max 16 mg/day) - Bismuth subsalicylate 525 mg q30 min × 8 doses

Moderate-Severe (≥3 stools, cramps, fever): - Antibiotics: - Rifaximin 200 mg TID × 3 days (non-invasive - no fever/blood) - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 3 days (invasive) - Azithromycin 1 g single dose (SE Asia - fluoroquinolone resistance; Campylobacter)

  • Loperamide + Antibiotic: Faster symptom relief

When to Seek Care: - High fever, bloody diarrhea - No improvement in 48 hours - Severe dehydration

docs.title: Antibiotic-Associated Diarrhea (AAD)
docs.text:
Definition: - Diarrhea during or within 8 weeks of antibiotic use

Etiology: - Non-C. diff (75-80%): Altered microbiome, osmotic effect - C. diff (20-25%): Covered above

Management: - Discontinue antibiotic (if possible) - Probiotics: Lactobacillus, S. boulardii (may reduce risk) - Test for C. diff if: - Severe/persistent (>3 days) - Fever, leukocytosis - Bloody diarrhea

docs.title: HIV-Associated Diarrhea
docs.text:
Common Causes (by CD4 count):

CD4 >200: - Medication side effects (ARVs) - Bacterial (Salmonella, Campylobacter, Shigella, C. diff) - IBS

CD4 <200: - Cryptosporidium (watery, profuse) - Microsporidia - Isospora - Giardia

CD4 <100: - CMV colitis (bloody diarrhea, ulcers on colonoscopy) - Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC): Fever, weight loss

Diagnosis: - Stool studies: Culture, O&P (with modified acid-fast for Cryptosporidium, Isospora) - Colonoscopy: CMV (biopsy - inclusion bodies), MAC

Management: - Antiretroviral therapy (ART): Immune reconstitution (CRITICAL) - Specific therapy: - Cryptosporidium: Nitazoxanide (if CD4 >100); ART (if CD4 <100) - CMV: Ganciclovir - MAC: Clarithromycin + Ethambutol

docs.title: Factitious Diarrhea (Laxative Abuse)
docs.text:
Suspect: - Young women (eating disorders common) - Healthcare workers - Secretory pattern (osmotic gap <50) BUT diarrhea stops with observation - Hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis - No weight loss (despite chronic diarrhea)

Diagnosis: - Stool/urine laxative screen: - Bisacodyl, senna, aloe - Magnesium (elevated stool Mg²⁺ if not measured in osmotic gap) - Colonoscopy: Melanosis coli (anthracene laxatives - dark pigmentation)

Management: - Psychiatric evaluation - Treat eating disorder

docs.title: Acute Diarrhea Pearls
docs.text:
1. Shortest incubation: Staph aureus (1-6 hours - preformed toxin)
  2. "Rice-water stools": Cholera (profuse watery - up to 1 L/hour)
  3. Most common traveler's diarrhea: ETEC
  4. Most common bacterial (developed countries): Campylobacter
  5. Post-infectious GBS: Campylobacter jejuni
  6. Most virulent (lowest inoculum): Shigella (10-100 organisms)
  7. E. coli O157:H7:
  8. NO antibiotics (↑ HUS risk)
  9. NO fever (unlike other bacterial dysentery)
  10. Sorbitol-negative
  11. C. diff:
  12. Discontinue offending antibiotic
  13. Vancomycin 125 mg PO QID (first-line)
  14. Recurrent: Fidaxomicin or FMT (90% cure)

docs.title: Chronic Diarrhea Pearls
docs.text:
Osmotic vs Secretory: - Stops with fasting = Osmotic - Persists with fasting = Secretory - Nocturnal (wakes from sleep) = Organic (secretory, IBD) - NOT IBS

Stool Osmotic Gap: 

Gap = 290 - 2([Na⁺] + [K⁺])

<50 = Secretory
>125 = Osmotic

Steatorrhea Clues: - Bulky, greasy, foul-smelling, floating - Weight loss - Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (A, D, E, K)

Celiac Disease: - IgA-tTG (best screening) - Check total IgA (5% deficient → use IgG-based tests) - Duodenal biopsy: Villous atrophy (gold standard) - Treatment: Gluten-free diet (lifelong) - Complication: Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma

Pancreatic Insufficiency: - Fecal elastase <200 μg/g - Steatorrhea when >90% exocrine function lost - Treatment: Pancrelipase 40,000-50,000 units lipase with meals

Microscopic Colitis: - Normal colonoscopy (MUST biopsy) - Watery diarrhea (middle-aged/elderly women) - Associations: NSAIDs, PPIs, celiac - Histology: - Lymphocytic: Intraepithelial lymphocytes - Collagenous: Subepithelial collagen band - Treatment: Budesonide 9 mg daily

Secretory Diarrhea Endocrine Causes: - VIPoma: WDHA (Watery Diarrhea, Hypokalemia, Achlorhydria) - Carcinoid: Diarrhea + Flushing + Wheezing + Right heart disease - Diagnosis: 24-hour urine 5-HIAA - Gastrinoma: Diarrhea + PUD (multiple, refractory ulcers)

Bile Acid Diarrhea: - Ileal resection >100 cm or ileal Crohn's - Diagnosis: Therapeutic trial with cholestyramine - Treatment: Cholestyramine 4-16 g/day

SIBO: - Risk factors: Blind loops, scleroderma, diabetes, PPI - Presentation: Steatorrhea, bloating, B12 deficiency - Diagnosis: Breath test (early H₂ rise) - Treatment: Rifaximin 550 mg TID × 14 days

IBS: - Rome IV: Recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week × 3 months + ≥2: - Related to defecation - Change in frequency - Change in form - Red flags (exclude IBS): - Age >50, weight loss, blood, anemia, nocturnal symptoms, family history IBD/cancer

docs.title: Management Pearls
docs.text:
ORS: - Glucose-coupled Na⁺ absorption (SGLT1) - Works even in cholera (secretory)

Loperamide Contraindications: - Bloody diarrhea (invasive pathogens) - C. diff - E. coli O157:H7 (↑ HUS risk) - Toxic megacolon risk

Traveler's Diarrhea: - Prevention: "Boil, cook, peel, or forget it" - Empiric antibiotics: - Rifaximin (non-invasive - no fever/blood) - Azithromycin (SE Asia - Campylobacter) - Loperamide + antibiotic: Fastest relief

Antibiotic Use - When NOT to Use: - E. coli O157:H7: ↑ HUS risk - Uncomplicated Salmonella: Prolongs carrier state

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 30-year-old woman develops sudden onset of severe nausea and vomiting 3 hours after eating mayonnaise-based potato salad at a picnic. She has watery diarrhea but no fever. What is the MOST likely causative organism?

A) Salmonella B) Staphylococcus aureus C) Campylobacter jejuni D) Norovirus

Answer: B) Staphylococcus aureus

Explanation: - Staphylococcus aureus food poisoning: - Shortest incubation: 1-6 hours (preformed toxin) - Source: Mayonnaise, cream pastries, food handlers - Presentation: - Severe nausea, vomiting (predominant) - Watery diarrhea - NO fever - Duration: 12-24 hours (very short) - Management: Supportive

  • Other options:
  • Salmonella: Longer incubation (6-72 hrs), fever common
  • Campylobacter: Incubation 2-5 days, bloody diarrhea, fever
  • Norovirus: Incubation 24-48 hrs (longer than 3 hrs)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 25-year-old man returns from Mexico with 4 days of watery diarrhea (6-8 stools/day), cramping, and low-grade fever. He has no blood in stools. What is the MOST appropriate empiric treatment?

A) Loperamide only B) Rifaximin C) Ciprofloxacin D) Metronidazole

Answer: C) Ciprofloxacin

Explanation: - Traveler's diarrhea with fever (suggests invasive pathogen): - Empiric antibiotics: - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID × 3 days (invasive - fever present) - Azithromycin 1 g single dose (alternative; preferred in SE Asia)

  • Rifaximin is for:

    • Non-invasive (NO fever, NO blood)
    • Not effective against Campylobacter, Shigella

  • Loperamide alone: Inadequate if fever present (suggests bacterial)

  • Metronidazole: For Giardia, Entamoeba (not first-line empiric)

  • Can add loperamide to antibiotic for faster symptom relief

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 70-year-old woman with chronic watery diarrhea (8 stools/day) for 6 months undergoes colonoscopy, which appears completely normal. Random biopsies are taken. Histology shows a thickened subepithelial collagen band (15 μm). What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?

A) Lymphocytic colitis; budesonide B) Collagenous colitis; budesonide C) Ulcerative colitis; mesalamine D) Ischemic colitis; supportive care

Answer: B) Collagenous colitis; budesonide

Explanation: - Collagenous colitis (type of microscopic colitis): - Chronic watery diarrhea (secretory) - Middle-aged/elderly women - Normal colonoscopy (MUST biopsy) - Histology: Subepithelial collagen band >10 μm (this patient has 15 μm)

  • Lymphocytic colitis (other type of microscopic colitis):
  • Intraepithelial lymphocytes >20/100 epithelial cells

  • NO collagen band thickening

  • Treatment:

  • Budesonide 9 mg daily (first-line)
  • Discontinue NSAIDs/PPIs (if taking)

  • Loperamide (symptom control)

  • Associations: NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs, celiac disease

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 35-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis presents with bulky, greasy, foul-smelling stools that float and are difficult to flush. Which test will MOST likely be abnormal?

A) Stool osmotic gap B) Fecal elastase C) Fecal calprotectin D) Lactose hydrogen breath test

Answer: B) Fecal elastase

Explanation: - Steatorrhea (fatty diarrhea): - Bulky, greasy, foul-smelling, floating - Chronic pancreatitis → pancreatic insufficiency

  • Fecal elastase:
  • <200 μg/g = pancreatic insufficiency
  • Specific for pancreatic function

  • Will be LOW in this patient

  • Other tests:

  • 72-hour fecal fat: Gold standard for steatorrhea (>7 g/day)
  • Fecal calprotectin: Elevated in IBD (inflammatory), NOT maldigestion
  • Stool osmotic gap: For watery diarrhea (osmotic vs secretory)

  • Lactose breath test: Lactose intolerance

  • Treatment:

  • Pancrelipase (pancreatic enzyme replacement)
  • 40,000-50,000 units lipase with each meal
  • Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 28-year-old woman with chronic watery diarrhea has extensive workup including normal colonoscopy with biopsies, normal celiac serology, and stool studies. Stool osmotic gap is calculated as 35 mOsm/kg. Diarrhea persists despite 48-hour fast. Which endocrine cause should be investigated?

A) Hyperthyroidism B) VIPoma C) Carcinoid syndrome D) Addison's disease

Answer: B) VIPoma

Explanation: - Secretory diarrhea: - Osmotic gap <50 (this patient: 35) - Persists with fasting (key feature)

  • VIPoma (Verner-Morrison syndrome):
  • Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (VIP) secreting tumor
  • WDHA Syndrome:
    • **W**atery diarrhea (massive - "pancreatic cholera")
    • **H**ypokalemia (severe)
    • **A**chlorhydria (VIP inhibits gastric acid)
  • Diagnosis:
    • Elevated VIP (>200 pg/mL)
    • CT/MRI: Pancreatic mass

  • Treatment: Octreotide, surgical resection

  • Other options:

  • Hyperthyroidism: Dysmotility (increased motility), NOT pure secretory
  • Carcinoid: Secretory diarrhea BUT also has flushing, wheezing, right heart disease
    • Diagnosis: 24-hour urine 5-HIAA

  • Addison's: Can cause chronic diarrhea, but secretory pattern less common

  • Secretory diarrhea endocrine workup:

  • Serum VIP
  • 24-hour urine 5-HIAA
  • Serum gastrin (gastrinoma)
  • Serum calcitonin (medullary thyroid cancer)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 40-year-old man develops diarrhea (8-10 watery stools/day), cramping, and fever while hospitalized and receiving IV ceftriaxone for pneumonia. Which test should be ordered FIRST?

A) Stool culture B) C. difficile PCR C) Fecal calprotectin D) Colonoscopy

Answer: B) C. difficile PCR

Explanation: - Antibiotic-associated diarrhea: - Recent antibiotics (within 8 weeks) - Hospitalized - Watery diarrhea, cramping, fever

  • C. difficile colitis:
  • High-risk antibiotics: Clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins (patient on ceftriaxone)

  • Test: Stool PCR (most sensitive) OR EIA (toxins A/B)

  • Management:

  • Discontinue ceftriaxone (if possible)

  • Vancomycin 125 mg PO QID × 10 days

  • Other tests:

  • Stool culture: For other bacterial pathogens (lower yield in hospitalized, not first-line)
  • Fecal calprotectin: Inflammatory marker (not specific)
  • Colonoscopy: If diagnosis unclear or concern for severe/fulminant (pseudomembranes)

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 22-year-old woman with chronic diarrhea, bloating, and flatulence reports symptoms worsen after eating bread and pasta. Serology shows elevated IgA anti-tissue transglutaminase. What is the NEXT best step?

A) Start gluten-free diet immediately B) Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies C) HLA-DQ2/DQ8 testing D) Repeat serology in 3 months

Answer: B) Upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsies

Explanation: - Suspected celiac disease: - Elevated IgA-tTG (highly sensitive/specific) - Symptoms with gluten

  • Diagnosis requires:
  • Positive serology (IgA-tTG or EMA) AND

  • Duodenal biopsy confirmation (gold standard)

  • EGD with duodenal biopsies (BEFORE starting gluten-free diet):

  • Villous atrophy
  • Crypt hyperplasia
