A 55-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis develops ascites. Diagnostic paracentesis shows: Ascitic albumin 0.8 g/dL, Serum albumin 2.5 g/dL, Ascitic protein 1.2 g/dL, PMN 80 cells/mm³. What is the SAAG and interpretation?

A) SAAG 1.7; portal hypertension; no SBP B) SAAG 1.7; portal hypertension; SBP present C) SAAG 0.8; non-portal hypertension; no SBP D) SAAG 3.3; portal hypertension; no SBP

Answer: A) SAAG 1.7; portal hypertension; no SBP

Explanation: - SAAG = Serum Albumin - Ascitic Albumin - = 2.5 - 0.8 = 1.7 g/dL

  • SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL = Portal Hypertension (97% accurate)

  • This patient: SAAG 1.7 → Cirrhosis (portal hypertension)

  • SBP Diagnosis: Ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/mm³

  • This patient: PMN 80 → NO SBP

  • SAAG <1.1 g/dL = Non-portal hypertension (peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 60-year-old woman with cirrhosis is admitted with confusion. Paracentesis shows PMN 400 cells/mm³. She is started on cefotaxime. What additional medication should be given to reduce mortality and prevent hepatorenal syndrome?

A) Lactulose B) Albumin C) Furosemide D) Rifaximin

Answer: B) Albumin

Explanation: - SBP (ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/mm³)

  • Treatment:
  • Antibiotics: Cefotaxime 2 g IV q8h × 5 days

  • Albumin: CRITICAL for reducing mortality and HRS

    • 1.5 g/kg IV at diagnosis
    • 1 g/kg IV on day 3

  • Mechanism: Albumin prevents circulatory dysfunction, reduces HRS incidence

  • Especially beneficial if:

  • Creatinine >1 mg/dL
  • BUN >30 mg/dL

  • Bilirubin >4 mg/dL

  • Lactulose: For hepatic encephalopathy (not directly for SBP)

  • Rifaximin: For recurrent HE (not acute SBP)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 50-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with altered mental status. He is oriented to person only, lethargic, and has asterixis. What is the West Haven grade of hepatic encephalopathy, and what is the first-line treatment?

A) Grade 1; rifaximin B) Grade 2; lactulose C) Grade 3; lactulose D) Grade 4; liver transplant

Answer: C) Grade 3; lactulose

Explanation: - West Haven Grading:

Grade Consciousness Asterixis 1 Mild confusion, sleep disturbance Mild 2 Lethargy, disorientation Present 3 Somnolent but arousable Present 4 Coma Absent
  • This patient:
  • Somnolent (but responsive)
  • Asterixis present

  • → Grade 3

  • Treatment:

  • First-line: Lactulose 15-30 mL 2-3× daily
  • Goal: 2-3 soft stools per day

  • Add Rifaximin (550 mg BID) if recurrent HE

  • Find precipitant: GI bleed, infection, constipation, electrolytes, sedatives ("HEPATICS")

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis and ascites develops acute kidney injury. Creatinine rises from 1.0 to 2.8 mg/dL over 5 days. He has no recent diuretic use, no nephrotoxic drugs, and received albumin 1 g/kg × 2 days without improvement. Urinalysis shows no protein, no casts. What is the diagnosis?

A) Acute tubular necrosis B) Hepatorenal syndrome Type 1 C) Pre-renal azotemia D) Acute interstitial nephritis

Answer: B) Hepatorenal syndrome Type 1

Explanation: - Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS) Type 1 (HRS-AKI): - Rapid decline in renal function - Doubling of Cr to >2.5 mg/dL in <2 weeks

  • Diagnostic Criteria:
  • Cirrhosis with ascites ✓
  • ↑ Cr ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 hrs OR ≥50% from baseline ✓
  • No response to volume expansion (albumin 1 g/kg × 2 days) ✓
  • No shock ✓
  • No nephrotoxic drugs ✓

  • No structural kidney disease (no proteinuria, no casts, normal renal US) ✓

  • Management:

  • Albumin 1 g/kg day 1 (max 100 g), then 20-40 g daily
  • Vasoconstrictors:
    • Midodrine + Octreotide OR
    • Norepinephrine (more effective)
  • TIPS: Consider if pharmacologic therapy fails

  • Liver transplant: Definitive

  • ATN differentiation:

  • Muddy brown casts, tubular epithelial cells on urinalysis
  • HRS has benign urinalysis

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 45-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with dyspnea that worsens when he stands up and improves when he lies down. Oxygen saturation is 88% upright, 94% supine. ABG shows PaO₂ 72 mmHg on room air. Contrast echocardiography shows microbubbles appearing in the left atrium 5 cardiac cycles after injection. What is the diagnosis?

A) Portopulmonary hypertension B) Hepatopulmonary syndrome C) Atrial septal defect D) Pulmonary embolism

Answer: B) Hepatopulmonary syndrome

Explanation: - Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS) triad: 1. Liver disease (cirrhosis) ✓ 2. Intrapulmonary vascular dilations ✓ 3. Hypoxemia (PaO₂ <80 mmHg) ✓

  • Clinical features:
  • Platypnea: Dyspnea worse upright
  • Orthodeoxia: ↓ O₂ saturation when upright, improves supine

  • Cyanosis, clubbing

  • Diagnosis:

  • Contrast echocardiography (agitated saline):

    • Delayed microbubbles in left heart (3-6 cardiac cycles) = Intrapulmonary shunt (HPS)
    • Immediate (<3 cycles) = Intracardiac shunt (ASD, VSD)

  • Management:

  • No effective medical therapy
  • Liver transplantation: ONLY curative treatment

  • MELD exception points (prioritized for transplant)

  • Portopulmonary HTN:

  • Dyspnea on exertion
  • NO platypnea/orthodeoxia
  • Echo: Elevated RVSP
  • Right heart catheterization: Elevated mPAP, PVR

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 62-year-old woman with cirrhosis has the following labs: Bilirubin 3.5 mg/dL, INR 2.1, Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL. What is her approximate MELD score?

A) 10-15 B) 16-20 C) 21-25 D) 26-30

Answer: C) 21-25

Explanation: - MELD Score formula: 

MELD = 3.78 × ln(Bili) + 11.2 × ln(INR) + 9.57 × ln(Cr) + 6.43

  • Calculation:
  • ln(3.5) ≈ 1.25
  • ln(2.1) ≈ 0.74

  • ln(1.8) ≈ 0.59

  • MELD = 3.78(1.25) + 11.2(0.74) + 9.57(0.59) + 6.43

  • MELD ≈ 4.7 + 8.3 + 5.6 + 6.4

  • MELD ≈ 25

  • Interpretation:

  • <10: Low risk
  • 10-19: Moderate risk
  • 20-29: High risk (20-45% 3-month mortality)
  • 30-40: Very high risk

  • >40: Extremely high risk

  • MELD ≥15: Consider transplant evaluation

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 52-year-old man with cirrhosis undergoes screening for esophageal varices. Endoscopy shows medium-sized varices. He has never had variceal bleeding. What is the MOST appropriate primary prophylaxis?

A) No treatment needed B) Propranolol (non-selective beta-blocker) C) Endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL) D) TIPS procedure

Answer: B) Propranolol (non-selective beta-blocker) OR C) Endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL)

Explanation: - Primary prophylaxis (never bled, medium-large varices):

Options (EITHER is acceptable): - Non-selective beta-blocker: - Propranolol, carvedilol, or nadolol - Target HR: 55-60 bpm OR 25% reduction from baseline - Mechanism: ↓ Portal pressure

  • Endoscopic Variceal Ligation (EVL):
  • Repeat sessions every 2-4 weeks until obliteration

  • Preferred if beta-blocker contraindicated/intolerant

  • Both equally effective for primary prophylaxis

  • Small varices: No treatment (but rescreen every 1-2 years)

  • Secondary prophylaxis (after bleed):

  • Combination: Beta-blocker + EVL (better than either alone)

  • TIPS: Reserved for refractory bleeding (failed endoscopic + medical therapy)

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 48-year-old man with cirrhosis presents with ascites refractory to high-dose diuretics (spironolactone 400 mg + furosemide 160 mg daily). He requires large-volume paracentesis every 2 weeks. What is the MOST appropriate next step?

A) Increase diuretics further B) Add midodrine and octreotide C) Evaluate for TIPS or liver transplantation D) Restrict fluids to 1 L/day

Answer: C) Evaluate for TIPS or liver transplantation

Explanation: - Refractory Ascites (10% of cirrhotic patients with ascites): - Definition: Unresponsive to sodium restriction + high-dose diuretics - Spironolactone 400 mg + Furosemide 160 mg (maximum doses)

  • Management options:

Serial Large-Volume Paracentesis (LVP): - Every 2-4 weeks as needed - Albumin replacement (8 g per liter removed if >5 L) - Currently what patient is doing

TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt): - Creates shunt → ↓ portal pressure - Improves ascites, reduces paracentesis frequency - Contraindications: - Severe hepatic encephalopathy - Heart failure - Severe liver failure (MELD >18-25, depends on center) - Complication: Hepatic encephalopathy (30-50%)

Liver Transplantation: - Definitive treatment - Refractory ascites is indication for transplant evaluation

  • Midodrine + Octreotide: For hepatorenal syndrome (NOT refractory ascites alone)

  • Fluid restriction: NOT effective for ascites (only for hyponatremia if Na <125)

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Which of the following is an ABSOLUTE contraindication to liver transplantation?

A) Age 72 years B) Active alcohol use within last 6 months C) Hepatitis C with detectable viral load D) Body mass index (BMI) 42

Answer: B) Active alcohol use within last 6 months

Explanation: - Absolute Contraindications to liver transplant: - Uncontrolled sepsis - Advanced cardiopulmonary disease - Extrahepatic malignancy (uncontrolled) - Active alcohol or drug use (most programs require ≥6 months abstinence) - Anatomic abnormalities (impossible to transplant)

  • Relative Contraindications:
  • Age >70-75 (depends on center, comorbidities)
  • BMI >40 (obesity increases surgical risk)
  • HIV with detectable viral load, CD4 <100
  • Portopulmonary hypertension (mPAP >45-50 mmHg)

  • Poor social support, non-compliance

  • Active alcohol use:

  • Requires abstinence ≥6 months (most programs)

  • Addresses:

    • Liver recovery (if early cirrhosis)
    • Commitment to sobriety
    • Prevents recurrent alcohol-related liver disease post-transplant

  • Hepatitis C:

  • NOT a contraindication (even with detectable viral load)
  • Can treat with DAAs pre- or post-transplant

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 55-year-old man with cirrhosis has the following Child-Pugh parameters: No encephalopathy, no ascites, bilirubin 2.5 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, INR 1.9. What is his Child-Pugh class and 1-year survival?

A) Class A; 100% 1-year survival B) Class B; 80% 1-year survival C) Class C; 45% 1-year survival D) Class B; 60% 1-year survival

Answer: A) Class A; 100% 1-year survival

Explanation: - Child-Pugh Score:

Parameter 1 point 2 points 3 points This Patient Encephalopathy None Grade 1-2 Grade 3-4 None = 1 Ascites None Mild Moderate-severe None = 1 Bilirubin (mg/dL) <2 2-3 >3 2.5 = 2 Albumin (g/dL) >3.5 2.8-3.5 <2.8 3.2 = 2 INR <1.7 1.7-2.3 >2.3 1.9 = 2

  • Total: 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8 points

  • Wait - let me recalculate:

  • Encephalopathy: None = 1 point
  • Ascites: None = 1 point
  • Bilirubin 2.5: 2-3 range = 2 points
  • Albumin 3.2: 2.8-3.5 range = 2 points
  • INR 1.9: 1.7-2.3 range = 2 points

  • Total = 8 points

  • Classification:

  • Class A (5-6 points): 1-year survival 100%
  • Class B (7-9 points): 1-year survival 80%

  • Class C (10-15 points): 1-year survival 45%

  • This patient: 8 points = Class B, 80% 1-year survival

Corrected Answer: B) Class B; 80% 1-year survival

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Liver Cirrhosis is end-stage chronic liver disease characterized by fibrosis and nodular regeneration, leading to portal hypertension and hepatocellular dysfunction:

Etiology: Alcohol (most common in West), Hepatitis B/C, NASH, autoimmune, genetic

Complications: - Ascites: SAAG ≥1.1 (portal HTN), treat with Na restriction + diuretics (spironolactone + furosemide 100:40 ratio) - SBP: Ascitic PMN ≥250, treat with cefotaxime + albumin, prophylaxis with norfloxacin - Hepatic Encephalopathy: Precipitants (HEPATICS), treat with lactulose (2-3 stools/day) + rifaximin - Hepatorenal Syndrome: Type 1 (rapid, poor prognosis), treat with albumin + vasoconstrictors - Hepatopulmonary Syndrome: Platypnea/orthodeoxia, liver transplant only curative treatment - Variceal Bleeding: Octreotide + EVL, prophylaxis with beta-blockers

Prognostic Scores: - Child-Pugh: Class A/B/C (surgical risk, prognosis) - MELD: Transplant allocation (≥15 consider evaluation)

Treatment: Manage underlying cause, prevent progression, treat complications, liver transplantation (definitive for decompensated cirrhosis)

End of Topic 25: Liver Cirrhosis

Next Topic: Hepatitis (Viral & Autoimmune) (Medicine/26_Hepatitis.md)

Week 2 Progress: 5/18 topics complete

docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Hepatitis A
  3. Hepatitis B
  4. Hepatitis C
  5. Hepatitis D
  6. Hepatitis E
  7. Autoimmune Hepatitis
  8. Drug-Induced Liver Injury
  9. Acute Liver Failure
  10. High-Yield Points
  11. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Hepatitis: Inflammation of the liver

docs.title: Classification by Duration
docs.text:
• Acute: <6 months
  • Chronic: >6 months

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Viral: - Hepatitis A, B, C, D, E (hepatotropic viruses) - Other: EBV, CMV, HSV

Non-Viral: - Autoimmune hepatitis - Drug-induced (acetaminophen, isoniazid, NSAIDs) - Alcohol - Metabolic: Wilson's disease, hemochromatosis

docs.title: Laboratory Pattern
docs.text:
Hepatocellular Injury: - AST, ALT >> ALP - AST/ALT >1000 U/L (acute viral, drug-induced)

Cholestatic Injury: - ALP, GGT >> AST/ALT - Bilirubin elevated

Mixed Pattern: - Both elevated

docs.title: Virology
docs.text:
• Picornavirus (RNA virus)
  • Fecal-oral transmission
  • NO chronic infection
  • NO carrier state

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Endemic in developing countries (poor sanitation)
  • Outbreaks: Contaminated food/water, daycare centers
  • Groups at risk: Travelers, MSM, injection drug users, homeless

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Incubation Period: 2-6 weeks (average 28 days)

Phases:

1. Prodrome (1-2 weeks): - Flu-like symptoms: Fever, malaise, fatigue - Nausea, vomiting, anorexia - Right upper quadrant pain

2. Icteric Phase (1-3 weeks): - Jaundice (dark urine precedes jaundice) - Pruritus - Hepatomegaly (tender) - Acholic stools (pale) - Symptoms improve when jaundice appears

3. Recovery (weeks to months): - Gradual resolution

Severity: - Children: Often asymptomatic or mild - Adults: Symptomatic (70% icteric) - Elderly: More severe

NO chronic hepatitis

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Serologic Testing:

Test Interpretation Anti-HAV IgM Acute infection (positive 5-10 days after exposure, lasts 6 months) Anti-HAV IgG Past infection or vaccination (lifelong immunity) HAV RNA Rarely done (research)

Liver Enzymes: - AST, ALT: 1000-5000 U/L (hepatocellular pattern) - Bilirubin: Elevated (5-20 mg/dL) - ALP: Mildly elevated

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Supportive: - No specific antiviral therapy - Hydration, rest - Avoid alcohol, hepatotoxic drugs - Rarely progresses to acute liver failure (<0.5%)

Hospitalization: If unable to maintain oral hydration, coagulopathy, encephalopathy

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
Pre-Exposure:

Hepatitis A Vaccine (inactivated): - 2-dose series: 0 and 6-12 months - >95% effective - Indications: - All children (age 12-23 months) - Travelers to endemic areas - Chronic liver disease - MSM, injection drug users - Homeless

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis: - Unvaccinated exposed <2 weeks ago: - Hepatitis A vaccine (preferred) OR - Immune globulin (IG) 0.02 mL/kg IM (if age <12 months, immunocompromised, contraindication to vaccine)

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
• Self-limited
  • 100% recovery in most cases
  • Fulminant hepatitis: <0.5% (higher in elderly, chronic liver disease)
  • NO chronic infection
  • Lifelong immunity after infection

docs.title: Virology
docs.text:
• Hepadnavirus (DNA virus)
  • Partially double-stranded circular DNA
  • Reverse transcriptase (like retroviruses)

Viral Components: - HBsAg (surface antigen): Envelope protein - HBcAg (core antigen): Nucleocapsid protein (NOT in serum) - HBeAg (e antigen): Secreted protein (marker of high replication) - HBV DNA: Direct measure of viral load

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
Global Burden: - 296 million people chronically infected - Endemic in Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia

Transmission: - Parenteral: Blood, needlestick, injection drug use - Sexual: Unprotected sex - Perinatal: Mother to child (most common in endemic areas) - 90% become chronic if infected as neonate - 5-10% become chronic if infected as adult

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text:
Acute Infection → Recovery (95% adults) OR Chronic (5% adults, 90% neonates)

Chronic HBV Phases:

1. Immune Tolerant: - HBeAg positive - High HBV DNA (>2 × 10⁷ IU/mL) - Normal ALT - Minimal liver damage - Common in perinatal infection

2. Immune Active (HBeAg-Positive Chronic Hepatitis): - HBeAg positive - High HBV DNA (>2 × 10⁴ IU/mL) - Elevated ALT (hepatitis) - Active liver damage, fibrosis progression - Treat

3. Immune Control (Inactive Carrier): - HBeAg negative, Anti-HBe positive (seroconversion) - Low/undetectable HBV DNA (<2 × 10³ IU/mL) - Normal ALT - Minimal liver damage - Best phase (low risk progression)

4. Immune Escape (HBeAg-Negative Chronic Hepatitis): - HBeAg negative (precore/core promoter mutant) - High HBV DNA (>2 × 10⁴ IU/mL) - Elevated ALT - Active liver damage - Treat

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Acute Hepatitis B:

Incubation Period: 6 weeks to 6 months (average 90 days)

Presentation: - Asymptomatic (70% adults, >90% children) - Symptomatic (30%): - Prodrome: Fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea, RUQ pain - Icteric phase: Jaundice, dark urine, pruritus - Serum sickness-like syndrome (10%): Arthralgias, rash, fever (due to immune complex deposition)

Severe: - Fulminant hepatic failure (0.1-0.5%)

Chronic Hepatitis B: - Often asymptomatic for years/decades - Complications: - Cirrhosis (20-25% over 20-30 years) - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): Annual incidence 2-5% in cirrhosis, 0.5-1% in chronic HBV without cirrhosis

Extrahepatic Manifestations: - Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) - Membranous glomerulonephritis - Cryoglobulinemia (rare)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Serologic Markers:

Marker Meaning HBsAg Active infection (acute or chronic) Anti-HBs Immunity (recovery or vaccination) Anti-HBc IgM Acute infection Anti-HBc IgG Past or chronic infection HBeAg High viral replication, high infectivity Anti-HBe Low viral replication (seroconversion) HBV DNA Direct measure of viral load (quantitative)

Serologic Patterns:

1. Acute HBV: - HBsAg positive - Anti-HBc IgM positive (KEY for acute) - HBeAg positive (usually) - Anti-HBs negative

2. Chronic HBV: - HBsAg positive (>6 months) - Anti-HBc IgG positive - Anti-HBc IgM negative - HBeAg: Positive (active) or Negative (inactive/mutant) - HBV DNA: Measure viral load

3. Resolved HBV (Immunity): - HBsAg negative - Anti-HBs positive (protective antibody) - Anti-HBc IgG positive

4. Vaccinated: - HBsAg negative - Anti-HBs positive - Anti-HBc negative (never infected)

5. Window Period (Between HBsAg clearance and Anti-HBs appearance): - HBsAg negative - Anti-HBs negative - Anti-HBc IgM positive (ONLY marker during window)

6. Occult HBV: - HBsAg negative - Anti-HBc positive - HBV DNA positive (low level in liver)

HBV DNA Quantification: - High: >2 × 10⁷ IU/mL (immune tolerant) - Moderate: >2 × 10⁴ IU/mL (treat if elevated ALT) - Low: <2 × 10³ IU/mL (inactive carrier)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Acute Hepatitis B: - Supportive care (most resolve) - Antiviral therapy: ONLY if severe/fulminant (tenofovir or entecavir)

Chronic Hepatitis B:

Indications for Treatment: - HBV DNA >2 × 10⁴ IU/mL (HBeAg-positive) or >2 × 10³ IU/mL (HBeAg-negative) AND - Elevated ALT AND/OR Significant fibrosis (≥F2)

OR: - Cirrhosis (any HBV DNA level) - Acute liver failure - HCC - Immunosuppressive therapy planned - Pregnancy (if high viral load - prevent transmission)

First-Line Antivirals (Nucleos(t)ide Analogs):

Entecavir: - Dosing: 0.5 mg PO daily (1 mg if lamivudine-resistant) - High barrier to resistance - Potent

Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate (TDF): - Dosing: 300 mg PO daily - High barrier to resistance - Potent - Preferred in pregnancy (safe)

Tenofovir Alafenamide (TAF): - Dosing: 25 mg PO daily - Lower bone/renal toxicity than TDF - High barrier to resistance

Avoid (high resistance rates): - Lamivudine (older, high resistance) - Adefovir (weaker, nephrotoxic)

Pegylated Interferon-α: - Limited use (side effects, <30% response) - Finite duration (48 weeks) - Consider in young, non-cirrhotic, high ALT, low HBV DNA

Duration: - Indefinite in most cases (cirrhosis, HBeAg-negative) - May stop after HBeAg seroconversion + undetectable HBV DNA × ≥12 months (close monitoring) - Goal: HBsAg clearance (rare - "functional cure")

Monitoring: - HBV DNA: Every 3-6 months (goal: undetectable) - ALT: Every 3-6 months - HCC surveillance: Ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months (ALL cirrhotic + high-risk non-cirrhotic)

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
Pre-Exposure:

Hepatitis B Vaccine (Recombinant HBsAg): - 3-dose series: 0, 1, 6 months (or 0, 1-2, 4 months for some formulations) - >90% protective (Anti-HBs >10 mIU/mL) - Indications: - Universal infant/childhood vaccination - Healthcare workers - Chronic liver disease (all causes) - Chronic kidney disease, dialysis - HIV, injection drug users, MSM - Travelers to endemic areas

  • Check Anti-HBs after series if:
  • Healthcare worker, immunocompromised, dialysis
  • If Anti-HBs <10 mIU/mL: Repeat series (1-3 doses) or double dose (dialysis patients)

Post-Exposure Prophylaxis:

Needlestick, Sexual, Perinatal:

Unvaccinated: - HBIG (Hepatitis B Immune Globulin) 0.06 mL/kg IM (within 24 hours, up to 7 days) - Start HBV vaccine series (separate site)

Vaccinated: - Check Anti-HBs: - If ≥10 mIU/mL: No treatment - If <10 mIU/mL: HBIG + booster dose

Perinatal (Infant of HBsAg-positive mother): - HBIG 0.5 mL IM within 12 hours of birth - HBV vaccine within 12 hours (separate site) - Complete vaccine series (0, 1, 6 months) - Check HBsAg and Anti-HBs at 9-12 months (assess success)

docs.title: Complications
docs.text: Cirrhosis: 20-25% after 20-30 years Hepatocellular Carcinoma: 2-5%/year (cirrhosis), 0.5-1%/year (chronic HBV without cirrhosis) Fulminant Hepatic Failure: 0.1-0.5% (acute)

docs.title: Virology
docs.text:
• Flavivirus (RNA virus)
  • High mutation rate → 6 major genotypes (1-6)
  • Genotype 1: Most common in US (50%)
  • Genotype 3: Associated with steatosis, faster fibrosis

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
Global Burden: - 58 million people chronically infected

Transmission: - Parenteral: - Injection drug use (most common in US) - Blood transfusion (before 1992) - Needlestick - Tattoos (unsterile equipment) - Sexual: Low efficiency (<5% lifetime risk in monogamous couples) - Perinatal: 5-6% if mother HCV RNA-positive - NO transmission: Casual contact, sharing food/drinks

docs.title: Natural History
docs.text:
Acute HCV → Spontaneous clearance (15-25%) OR Chronic (75-85%)

Chronic HCV → Cirrhosis (15-30% over 20-30 years) → HCC (1-5%/year)

Factors accelerating fibrosis: - Alcohol - Coinfection (HBV, HIV) - Male gender - Age >40 at infection - Obesity, diabetes

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Acute HCV:

Incubation Period: 2-26 weeks (average 6-7 weeks)

Presentation: - 80% asymptomatic - 20% symptomatic: Mild flu-like illness, jaundice (rare) - Fulminant hepatitis: Very rare (<1%)

Chronic HCV: - Often asymptomatic for decades - Fatigue (most common symptom) - Discovered incidentally: Abnormal LFTs, screening

Extrahepatic Manifestations (Immune complex-mediated): - Mixed cryoglobulinemia (most common): - Palpable purpura, arthralgias, glomerulonephritis - Check: Cryoglobulins, Rheumatoid factor, low C4 - Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) - Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT) - Lichen planus

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Serologic Testing:

1. Anti-HCV Antibody: - Screening test (ELISA) - Positive: Current or past infection - Appears 4-12 weeks after exposure (window period) - Does NOT distinguish active vs resolved infection - Remains positive after cure (unlike HBV)

2. HCV RNA (Quantitative PCR): - Confirmatory test - Detects active infection (viremia) - Positive as early as 1-2 weeks after exposure (before anti-HCV) - Used for: - Confirm active infection - Monitor treatment response

Diagnostic Algorithm: 

Anti-HCV positive
     ↓
Check HCV RNA
     ↓
  HCV RNA positive → Active infection (Chronic HCV)
  HCV RNA negative → Resolved infection OR false-positive anti-HCV

Additional Tests: - HCV genotype: Determine treatment (now less important with pan-genotypic DAAs) - Fibrosis assessment: - Non-invasive: FibroScan (elastography), FIB-4, APRI score - Liver biopsy: Rarely needed

Liver Enzymes: - AST, ALT: Often normal or minimally elevated (unreliable for diagnosis) - Fluctuating levels

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
All patients with HCV RNA-positive should be treated (unless limited life expectancy)

Direct-Acting Antivirals (DAAs): - >95% cure rate (Sustained Virologic Response - SVR) - Short duration: 8-12 weeks - Well-tolerated

Pan-Genotypic Regimens (cover all genotypes):

1. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (Epclusa): - SOF 400 mg + VEL 100 mg PO daily × 12 weeks - All genotypes, compensated cirrhosis

2. Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Mavyret): - GLE 300 mg + PIB 120 mg (3 tabs) PO daily × 8 weeks - All genotypes, no cirrhosis or compensated - Contraindicated: Decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C)

3. Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir/Voxilaprevir (Vosevi): - For treatment-experienced patients

Treatment-Naïve, No Cirrhosis: - GLE/PIB × 8 weeks OR SOF/VEL × 12 weeks

Compensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A): - SOF/VEL × 12 weeks OR GLE/PIB × 12 weeks (some extend to 16 weeks)

Decompensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C): - SOF/VEL × 12 weeks + Ribavirin (if tolerated) - Avoid protease inhibitors (GLE/PIB contraindicated)

SVR Definition: - Undetectable HCV RNA ≥12 weeks after end of treatment (SVR12) - Equivalent to cure

Post-Treatment: - Check HCV RNA at 12 weeks (SVR12) - HCC surveillance: Continue if cirrhosis (ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months) - Fibrosis regression: Possible if non-cirrhotic - Reinfection: Possible (no immunity) - counsel on prevention

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
No vaccine available

Prevention Strategies: - Screening: - One-time screening for ALL adults ≥18 years (2020 CDC recommendation) - High-risk: Injection drug users, recipients of blood/organs before 1992, incarcerated, HIV-positive, hemodialysis - Pregnant women: Screen at each pregnancy - Harm reduction: Clean needles for injection drug users - Blood donor screening

Post-Exposure: - No post-exposure prophylaxis available - Monitor: Check HCV RNA at 4-6 weeks, Anti-HCV at 4-6 months

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Without Treatment: - Cirrhosis: 15-30% over 20-30 years - HCC: 1-5%/year (if cirrhosis)

With DAA Treatment: - >95% cure (SVR) - Reduced progression to cirrhosis/HCC - Improved survival

docs.title: Virology
docs.text:
• Defective RNA virus (requires HBV for replication)
  • Needs HBsAg (uses HBV envelope)

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Endemic: Mediterranean, Middle East, Central Africa, Amazon basin
  • Rare in US
  • Transmission: Same as HBV (parenteral, sexual)

docs.title: Types of Infection
docs.text:
Coinfection: - Simultaneous HBV + HDV infection - Acute hepatitis (often more severe than HBV alone) - Usually resolves (clears HBV → clears HDV)

Superinfection: - HDV infection in chronic HBV carrier - Accelerated progression to cirrhosis - 90% become chronic HDV - Worse prognosis

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Marker Coinfection Superinfection HBsAg Positive Positive Anti-HBc IgM Positive (acute HBV) Negative (chronic HBV) Anti-HDV IgM Positive (transient) Positive (persistent) HDV RNA Positive Positive (high)

Coinfection: - Anti-HBc IgM positive (acute HBV) - Anti-HDV IgM positive (may be transient)

Superinfection: - Anti-HBc IgM negative (chronic HBV) - Anti-HDV IgM positive (persistent) - HDV RNA high

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
No effective treatment: - Pegylated interferon-α: Low response rate (15-25%) - Nucleos(t)ide analogs (entecavir, tenofovir): Suppress HBV but NOT HDV - Used to prevent HBV flare, but don't affect HDV

Emerging therapies: - Bulevirtide (entry inhibitor) - approved in Europe

Supportive care Liver transplantation: If decompensated cirrhosis

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text: HBV vaccination prevents HDV (since HDV requires HBV)

docs.title: Virology
docs.text:
• Hepevirus (RNA virus)
  • Fecal-oral transmission (like HAV)
  • Genotypes:
  • 1, 2: Human-only, developing countries (waterborne outbreaks)
  • 3, 4: Zoonotic (pigs), developed countries (foodborne - undercooked pork)

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Endemic: South Asia, Middle East, Africa (genotypes 1, 2)
  • Sporadic: US, Europe (genotypes 3, 4 - undercooked pork/game meat)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Incubation Period: 2-9 weeks (average 40 days)

Acute Hepatitis: - Similar to HAV (self-limited) - Jaundice, nausea, RUQ pain

High-Risk Groups: - Pregnant women (genotype 1): - 20-30% mortality in 3rd trimester (fulminant hepatic failure) - Unique to HEV - Immunocompromised: May develop chronic HEV (genotype 3) - Transplant recipients, HIV, chemotherapy

Usually NO chronic infection (except immunocompromised with genotype 3)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Serologic Testing: - Anti-HEV IgM: Acute infection - Anti-HEV IgG: Past infection - HEV RNA: Confirm active infection (especially if immunocompromised)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Acute HEV: - Supportive care (self-limited in most)

Chronic HEV (immunocompromised): - Reduce immunosuppression (if possible) - Ribavirin × 12 weeks (off-label)

Pregnant Women: - Supportive care - High mortality risk (3rd trimester)

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
Vaccine: - HEV vaccine (Hecolin): Available in China (not US/Europe)

General Measures: - Avoid contaminated water (endemic areas) - Cook pork/game meat thoroughly

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• Chronic hepatitis caused by immune-mediated destruction of hepatocytes
  • Progressive → cirrhosis if untreated

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Bimodal: Young women (15-40 years), older adults (50-70 years)
  • Female predominance (70%)

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
Type 1 (Most common - 80%): - ANA (Antinuclear Antibody) and/or Anti-SMA (Smooth Muscle Antibody) - All ages - Better response to treatment

Type 2 (Rare - children/young adults): - Anti-LKM-1 (Liver-Kidney Microsomal antibody) - More severe, rapid progression

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Presentation: - Asymptomatic → Acute hepatitis → Cirrhosis - Acute presentation (40%): - Jaundice, fatigue, RUQ pain - May mimic acute viral hepatitis - Can present as acute liver failure - Chronic/Insidious (40%): - Fatigue, arthralgias - Discovered on abnormal LFTs - Cirrhosis at diagnosis (25%)

Associated Conditions: - Other autoimmune diseases: Thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, IBD, celiac disease, rheumatoid arthritis

Physical Exam: - Hepatomegaly - Stigmata of chronic liver disease (if cirrhotic)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Diagnostic Criteria (International AIH Group - Simplified Scoring):

Feature Points ANA or SMA ≥1:40 +1 ANA or SMA ≥1:80 +2 Anti-LKM-1 ≥1:40 +2 SLA/LP positive +2 IgG >Upper limit normal +1 IgG >1.1× ULN +2 Liver biopsy compatible +1 Liver biopsy typical +2 Viral hepatitis excluded +2

Score: - ≥6: Probable AIH - ≥7: Definite AIH

Laboratory: - AST, ALT: Elevated (100-1000 U/L) - hepatocellular pattern - Hypergammaglobulinemia: IgG >1.5× ULN (very suggestive) - Autoantibodies: - ANA (80%) - Anti-SMA (60-70%) - Anti-LKM-1 (Type 2) - Exclude viral hepatitis: Negative HBsAg, anti-HCV

Liver Biopsy (Required for diagnosis): - Interface hepatitis (piecemeal necrosis) - KEY - Lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate (plasma cells prominent) - Rosette formation (hepatocytes) - Fibrosis/cirrhosis (variable)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Indications for Treatment: - AST/ALT >10× ULN OR - AST/ALT >5× ULN + IgG >2× ULN OR - Bridging fibrosis or cirrhosis on biopsy

Do NOT treat if: - Inactive disease (normal transaminases, minimal histology) - Decompensated cirrhosis (treat complications, transplant)

First-Line:

Prednisone + Azathioprine (Standard): - Prednisone: 30-40 mg daily × 1 week, then taper to 10-20 mg daily (maintenance) - Azathioprine: 50-150 mg daily (1-2 mg/kg) - Combination preferred (lower steroid dose → fewer side effects)

Prednisone Monotherapy: - 60 mg daily × 1 week, taper to 20 mg (maintenance) - If azathioprine contraindicated (pregnancy, TPMT deficiency, cytopenias)

Response: - Biochemical remission: Normalization of AST, ALT, IgG (6-12 months in 80%) - Histologic remission: Resolution of inflammation (may take 18-24 months)

Duration: - Minimum 2-3 years (until biochemical + histologic remission) - Attempt withdrawal: After remission × ≥2 years - Relapse rate: 50-90% after withdrawal - Most require lifelong therapy

Relapse: - Restart prednisone + azathioprine

Second-Line (if azathioprine intolerant/refractory): - Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 1-2 g daily - Budesonide (9 mg daily) + azathioprine (non-cirrhotic only - first-pass metabolism) - Tacrolimus, cyclosporine (refractory cases)

Liver Transplantation: - Indications: Acute liver failure, decompensated cirrhosis, refractory disease - Recurrence post-transplant: 10-35% (continue immunosuppression)

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Without Treatment: - Cirrhosis: 40-50% at 5 years - High mortality: 40% at 5 years

With Treatment: - Excellent prognosis: 10-year survival >90% (if non-cirrhotic at diagnosis) - Cirrhosis: May still develop HCC (surveillance needed)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
• Common cause of acute liver failure in US (50%)
  • Acetaminophen = most common overall
  • Antibiotics = most common non-acetaminophen

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Intrinsic (Dose-Dependent, Predictable): - Acetaminophen (most common) - Carbon tetrachloride, mushroom toxins

Idiosyncratic (Unpredictable): - Immune-mediated: Isoniazid, nitrofurantoin, diclofenac - Metabolic: Valproic acid, tetracycline

docs.title: Common Drugs
docs.text:
Hepatocellular Pattern (AST/ALT >> ALP): - Acetaminophen (dose-dependent) - Isoniazid (idiosyncratic) - Valproic acid - Amoxicillin-clavulanate - Statins (rare)

Cholestatic Pattern (ALP >> AST/ALT): - Amoxicillin-clavulanate (most common antibiotic DILI) - Azithromycin - Oral contraceptives - Anabolic steroids

Mixed Pattern: - Phenytoin - Sulfonamides

docs.title: Acetaminophen Hepatotoxicity
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Toxic metabolite (NAPQI) produced by CYP2E1 - Glutathione detoxifies NAPQI - Overdose → glutathione depletion → NAPQI accumulates → hepatocyte necrosis

Toxic Dose: - >7.5-10 g in adults (or >150 mg/kg) - Lower doses toxic if: - Chronic alcohol use (CYP2E1 induction) - Malnutrition (↓ glutathione) - Medications (inducing CYP2E1)

Clinical Features:

Phase 1 (0-24 hours): - Nausea, vomiting (non-specific)

Phase 2 (24-72 hours): - RUQ pain, hepatomegaly - AST, ALT rise (may be >10,000 U/L)

Phase 3 (72-96 hours): - Peak hepatotoxicity - Acute liver failure: Coagulopathy, encephalopathy, jaundice - AST/ALT >3000 U/L (often >10,000)

Phase 4 (>5 days): - Recovery or death

Diagnosis: - History of ingestion - Acetaminophen level: - Plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram (if ingestion within 24 hours) - >150 μg/mL at 4 hours or >30 μg/mL at 12 hours = toxic - Elevated AST, ALT (often >10,000 U/L) - INR elevation (hepatic synthetic dysfunction)

Management:

N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) (Antidote): - Mechanism: Replenishes glutathione, detoxifies NAPQI - Indications: - Toxic level on nomogram OR - Unknown time of ingestion + acetaminophen level >10 μg/mL OR - Acute liver failure (regardless of level)

  • Most effective if started within 8 hours of ingestion

  • Still beneficial up to 24-48 hours

  • Oral NAC:

  • Loading: 140 mg/kg

  • Maintenance: 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses

  • IV NAC:

  • Loading: 150 mg/kg over 1 hour
  • Maintenance: 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours

Supportive Care: - Liver transplant evaluation if acute liver failure

Prognosis: - Excellent if NAC started early (<8 hours) - Mortality: <1% if treated early, up to 20-40% if late/untreated

docs.title: Other Drug-Induced Liver Injury
docs.text:
Isoniazid: - 10-20% asymptomatic transaminase elevation - 1% clinical hepatitis - Risk factors: Age >35, alcohol, slow acetylators - Monitor: AST, ALT monthly × 3 months, then as needed - Discontinue if AST/ALT >3× ULN with symptoms or >5× ULN asymptomatic

Statins: - Rare (<1%) serious hepatotoxicity - Asymptomatic elevation in 1-3% (often self-limited) - Check baseline AST, ALT, then as clinically indicated - Do NOT routinely monitor

Amoxicillin-Clavulanate: - Most common antibiotic DILI - Cholestatic pattern - Onset: 1-6 weeks after starting (may be after stopping)

Management: - Discontinue offending drug (most important) - Supportive care - Corticosteroids: May help if immune-mediated (phenytoin, nitrofurantoin) - Liver transplant: If acute liver failure

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• Severe acute liver injury with:
  • Hepatic encephalopathy AND
  • INR ≥1.5
  • <26 weeks duration (no pre-existing liver disease)

docs.title: Classification by Timeline
docs.text: Hyperacute: <7 days (acetaminophen) Acute: 7-21 days Subacute: 21 days to 26 weeks

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
US: - Acetaminophen (40-50%) - most common - Idiosyncratic drug reaction (10-15%) - Viral hepatitis: HBV, HAV, HEV (rare in US) - Autoimmune hepatitis (5%) - Ischemic hepatitis (shock liver) - Indeterminate (15-20%)

Less Common: - Wilson's disease (young patients) - Budd-Chiari syndrome - Acute fatty liver of pregnancy, HELLP syndrome - Amanita phalloides (mushroom poisoning)

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Hepatic Encephalopathy: - Mandatory for diagnosis - Grades 1-4 (see cirrhosis section)

Coagulopathy: - INR ≥1.5 (mandatory) - Bleeding (GI, intracranial)

Jaundice: - Bilirubin often >20 mg/dL

Multi-Organ Failure: - Cerebral edema (high ICP → herniation) - Acute kidney injury (50-80%) - Hypoglycemia (impaired gluconeogenesis) - Infection (50% - SBP, pneumonia, fungal) - Hemodynamic instability (vasodilation, ↓ SVR)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Labs: - AST, ALT: Markedly elevated (acetaminophen >10,000; viral 1000-3000) - INR: ≥1.5 (reflects synthetic dysfunction) - Bilirubin: Elevated - Ammonia: Elevated (correlates with encephalopathy severity) - Hypoglycemia: Common

Identify Etiology: - Acetaminophen level - Viral serologies: Anti-HAV IgM, HBsAg, Anti-HBc IgM, Anti-HCV, HCV RNA, HEV RNA - Autoimmune: ANA, anti-SMA, IgG - Wilson's disease (if age <40): - Low ceruloplasmin - High urinary copper - Alkaline phosphatase/Bilirubin ratio <4 (suggests Wilson's) - Hemolytic anemia (Coombs-negative) - Pregnancy test (women of childbearing age) - Drug/toxin screen

Imaging: - Doppler ultrasound: Assess hepatic vein/portal vein patency (Budd-Chiari)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Intensive Care Unit: - All patients with ALF should be in ICU

Supportive Care:

1. Hepatic Encephalopathy: - Lactulose - Avoid sedatives - Intubation if Grade 3-4 (airway protection)

2. Cerebral Edema (leading cause of death): - ICP monitoring (if Grade 3-4 encephalopathy) - Head of bed 30° - Mannitol (if ICP >20 mmHg) - Hypertonic saline (Na 145-155 mEq/L) - Avoid hypotension (maintain CPP >50 mmHg)

3. Coagulopathy: - DO NOT correct with FFP/platelets unless: - Active bleeding OR - Invasive procedure (e.g., ICP monitor placement) - INR is prognostic marker (correcting obscures assessment)

4. Hypoglycemia: - Frequent glucose monitoring - D10W infusion as needed

5. Infection: - High suspicion (impaired immunity) - Empiric antibiotics if suspected (after cultures) - Antifungal prophylaxis (fluconazole) in some centers

6. Renal Failure: - Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) (gentler on hemodynamics than hemodialysis)

7. Hemodynamic Support: - Vasopressors (norepinephrine) if hypotensive

Specific Therapies:

Acetaminophen: - N-Acetylcysteine (NAC): Even if >24 hours post-ingestion (improves outcomes in ALF)

HBV: - Entecavir or tenofovir

Autoimmune Hepatitis: - Corticosteroids (if confirmed AIH and no sepsis/GI bleed)

Wilson's Disease: - Liver transplantation (URGENT - high mortality) - Plasmapheresis (bridge to transplant)

Amanita Phalloides: - Penicillin G (high-dose) + Silibinin (not available in US)

Liver Transplantation:

Indications (King's College Criteria):

Acetaminophen: - pH <7.3 (after resuscitation) OR - All 3 of: - INR >6.5 - Creatinine >3.4 mg/dL - Grade 3-4 encephalopathy

Non-Acetaminophen: - INR >6.5 OR - Any 3 of 5: - Age <10 or >40 - Etiology: Indeterminate, drug (non-acetaminophen), Wilson's, Budd-Chiari - Jaundice >7 days before encephalopathy - INR >3.5 - Bilirubin >17.5 mg/dL

Prognosis: - Without transplant: 50-80% mortality (depends on etiology) - With transplant: 60-80% survival - Spontaneous survival: Higher in acetaminophen (60-70%) vs other causes (25-40%)

docs.title: Hepatitis Comparison Table
docs.text: Feature HAV HBV HCV HDV HEV Virus Type RNA (Picorna) DNA (Hepadna) RNA (Flavi) RNA (Defective) RNA (Hepe) Transmission Fecal-oral Parenteral, sexual, perinatal Parenteral (IDU) Same as HBV Fecal-oral Incubation 2-6 wks 6 wks-6 mo 2-26 wks Same as HBV 2-9 wks Chronic Infection NEVER 5% adults, 90% neonates 75-85% 90% (superinfection) NO (except immunocompromised GT3) Fulminant Risk <0.5% 0.1-0.5% <1% Higher than HBV alone 20-30% (pregnant) Diagnosis (Acute) Anti-HAV IgM HBsAg + Anti-HBc IgM HCV RNA (Anti-HCV later) Anti-HDV IgM Anti-HEV IgM Treatment Supportive DAAs (entecavir, tenofovir) DAAs (SOF/VEL, GLE/PIB) Peginterferon (poor) Supportive Vaccine Yes Yes NO Prevented by HBV vaccine Yes (China only) Prevention Vaccine, HBIG Vaccine, HBIG Screen, harm reduction HBV vaccine Cook pork, safe water

docs.title: Diagnostic Pearls
docs.text:
Hepatitis B Serology: - Acute: HBsAg +, Anti-HBc IgM + - Chronic: HBsAg + (>6 mo), Anti-HBc IgG + - Resolved/Immune: Anti-HBs +, Anti-HBc + - Vaccinated: Anti-HBs +, Anti-HBc negative - Window period: Only Anti-HBc IgM + (HBsAg cleared, Anti-HBs not yet appeared)

Hepatitis C: - Anti-HCV: Screening (remains + after cure) - HCV RNA: Confirms active infection (use for diagnosis)

Autoimmune Hepatitis: - Hypergammaglobulinemia (IgG >1.5× ULN) - very suggestive - ANA/SMA (Type 1), Anti-LKM-1 (Type 2) - Interface hepatitis on biopsy - Treatment: Prednisone + azathioprine

docs.title: Treatment Pearls
docs.text:
Chronic HBV: - First-line: Entecavir or tenofovir (high barrier to resistance) - Treat if: HBV DNA elevated + elevated ALT + significant fibrosis, OR cirrhosis - Duration: Usually indefinite - HCC surveillance: Ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months (ALL cirrhotic)

Chronic HCV: - Cure >95% with DAAs - Pan-genotypic: SOF/VEL, GLE/PIB - Duration: 8-12 weeks - SVR12 = cure (HCV RNA undetectable 12 weeks post-treatment)

Autoimmune Hepatitis: - Prednisone + azathioprine (first-line) - Minimum 2-3 years treatment - Relapse common after withdrawal (often require lifelong)

Acetaminophen Overdose: - N-Acetylcysteine (most effective <8 hours, but give even if late) - Loading: 140 mg/kg PO, then 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses

docs.title: Special Populations
docs.text:
Pregnancy: - HEV (genotype 1): 20-30% mortality in 3rd trimester - HBV transmission prevention: - Infant: HBIG + vaccine within 12 hours of birth - Mother: If high viral load (>200,000 IU/mL) → tenofovir in 3rd trimester

Immunocompromised: - HEV (genotype 3): May develop chronic infection - Reactivation: HBV (check HBsAg, Anti-HBc before chemotherapy/immunosuppression)

docs.title: Acute Liver Failure
docs.text:
Most Common Cause (US): Acetaminophen (40-50%)

King's College Criteria (Transplant): - Acetaminophen: pH <7.3 OR (INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + Grade 3-4 HE) - Non-acetaminophen: INR >6.5 OR any 3 of 5 criteria

Management: - ICU care - NAC (acetaminophen - give even if late) - Avoid correcting INR (unless bleeding/procedure) - Liver transplant evaluation (urgent)

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 25-year-old woman presents with jaundice and fatigue for 1 week. She recently returned from a camping trip. Labs: AST 2000 U/L, ALT 2500 U/L, bilirubin 8 mg/dL. Serologies: HBsAg negative, Anti-HBc IgG negative, Anti-HAV IgM positive, Anti-HCV negative. What is the diagnosis and expected outcome?

A) Acute hepatitis A; will develop chronic hepatitis in 75% B) Acute hepatitis A; complete recovery expected C) Acute hepatitis B; will develop chronic hepatitis in 5% D) Acute hepatitis C; complete recovery expected

Answer: B) Acute hepatitis A; complete recovery expected

Explanation: - Acute Hepatitis A: - Anti-HAV IgM positive = acute infection - Fecal-oral transmission (contaminated water - camping)

  • Course:
  • Self-limited
  • 100% recovery (NO chronic infection)

  • Lifelong immunity (Anti-HAV IgG develops)

  • Hepatitis A:

  • NEVER becomes chronic
  • Fulminant hepatitis: Rare (<0.5%)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 35-year-old man has the following hepatitis B panel: HBsAg positive, Anti-HBc IgG positive, Anti-HBc IgM negative, HBeAg negative, Anti-HBe positive, HBV DNA 500 IU/mL, ALT 35 U/L. What is his hepatitis B status?

A) Acute hepatitis B B) Chronic hepatitis B - immune active phase C) Chronic hepatitis B - inactive carrier D) Resolved hepatitis B

Answer: C) Chronic hepatitis B - inactive carrier

Explanation: - Chronic HBV - Inactive Carrier phase: - HBsAg positive (>6 months = chronic) - Anti-HBc IgG positive (chronic) - Anti-HBc IgM negative (NOT acute) - HBeAg negative, Anti-HBe positive (seroconversion occurred) - HBV DNA low (<2000 IU/mL) ✓ (500 IU/mL) - ALT normal ✓ (35 U/L)

  • Best phase of chronic HBV:
  • Minimal liver damage
  • Low progression risk

  • NO treatment needed (but monitor)

  • HCC surveillance: Still needed every 6 months (chronic HBV)

  • Other phases:

  • Acute: Anti-HBc IgM positive
  • Immune active: HBeAg+ OR HBeAg- with high HBV DNA + elevated ALT
  • Resolved: HBsAg negative, Anti-HBs +, Anti-HBc +

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 50-year-old woman with chronic hepatitis C (genotype 1) is treatment-naïve with no cirrhosis. What is the MOST appropriate treatment regimen?

A) Pegylated interferon + ribavirin × 48 weeks B) Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir × 8 weeks C) Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir × 24 weeks D) Supportive care only

Answer: B) Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir × 8 weeks

Explanation: - Chronic HCV - Treatment-Naïve, No Cirrhosis:

Pan-Genotypic DAA Options: - Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (GLE/PIB): 8 weeks (no cirrhosis) - Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (SOF/VEL): 12 weeks (all patients)

  • Shortest duration: GLE/PIB × 8 weeks (if no cirrhosis)

  • Cure rate: >95% (SVR12)

  • Interferon-based therapy: Outdated (<50% cure, significant side effects, long duration)

  • If cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A):

  • SOF/VEL × 12 weeks OR GLE/PIB × 12-16 weeks

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 30-year-old woman with chronic hepatitis B (HBsAg positive) develops acute hepatitis. Labs show: HBsAg positive, Anti-HBc IgM negative, Anti-HDV IgM positive, HDV RNA high. What type of hepatitis D infection is this?

A) HDV coinfection B) HDV superinfection C) Chronic HDV only D) Resolved HDV

Answer: B) HDV superinfection

Explanation: - HDV Superinfection: - HDV infection in chronic HBV carrier - HBsAg positive (chronic HBV) ✓ - Anti-HBc IgM negative (NOT acute HBV - chronic) ✓ - Anti-HDV IgM positive (acute HDV) ✓ - HDV RNA high ✓

  • Superinfection characteristics:
  • 90% become chronic HDV
  • Accelerated progression to cirrhosis

  • Worse prognosis

  • Coinfection (vs Superinfection):

  • Simultaneous HBV + HDV
  • Anti-HBc IgM positive (acute HBV)
  • Usually resolves (clears HBV → clears HDV)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 28-year-old woman in her 3rd trimester of pregnancy (32 weeks gestation) who recently returned from India presents with jaundice, nausea, and RUQ pain. Labs: AST 1500 U/L, ALT 1800 U/L, bilirubin 12 mg/dL. Viral serologies: Anti-HAV IgM negative, HBsAg negative, Anti-HCV negative, Anti-HEV IgM positive. What is the expected outcome?

A) Complete recovery (self-limited) B) High risk of fulminant hepatic failure (20-30% mortality) C) 75% chance of chronic hepatitis D) No risk to fetus

Answer: B) High risk of fulminant hepatic failure (20-30% mortality)

Explanation: - Hepatitis E in pregnant woman (3rd trimester): - Anti-HEV IgM positive (acute HEV) - Genotype 1 (endemic in India)

  • Pregnancy + HEV:
  • 20-30% mortality in 3rd trimester
  • Fulminant hepatic failure (unique to HEV)

  • High fetal mortality

  • Management:

  • Supportive care
  • Close monitoring for liver failure

  • Consider delivery if severe

  • Non-pregnant adults: Self-limited (like HAV)

  • Genotype 3 (US/Europe):

  • Foodborne (undercooked pork)
  • Lower risk in pregnancy
  • May become chronic in immunocompromised

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 22-year-old woman presents with jaundice and fatigue. Labs: AST 800 U/L, ALT 900 U/L, ANA 1:320, Anti-smooth muscle antibody positive, IgG 3000 mg/dL (normal <1500). Liver biopsy shows interface hepatitis and lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate. What is the first-line treatment?

A) Pegylated interferon B) Prednisone + azathioprine C) Tenofovir D) Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir

Answer: B) Prednisone + azathioprine

Explanation: - Autoimmune Hepatitis (Type 1): - Young woman (typical) - ANA positive, Anti-SMA positive - Hypergammaglobulinemia (IgG 3000 mg/dL - >2× ULN) - very suggestive - Interface hepatitis on biopsy (characteristic)

  • First-Line Treatment:
  • Prednisone + Azathioprine (combination preferred)
  • Prednisone: 30-40 mg daily → taper to 10-20 mg (maintenance)

  • Azathioprine: 50-150 mg daily (1-2 mg/kg)

  • Duration:

  • Minimum 2-3 years (until biochemical + histologic remission)

  • Most require lifelong therapy (high relapse rate after withdrawal)

  • Response: 80% achieve remission in 6-12 months

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 45-year-old man presents with altered mental status 18 hours after intentional acetaminophen overdose (20 grams). Labs: AST 8500 U/L, ALT 9200 U/L, INR 3.5, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, bilirubin 4 mg/dL, acetaminophen level 25 μg/mL, pH 7.32. What is the MOST appropriate management?

A) Activated charcoal B) N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) C) Liver transplant evaluation + NAC D) Observation only (too late for antidote)

Answer: C) Liver transplant evaluation + NAC

Explanation: - Acetaminophen-Induced Acute Liver Failure: - Massive hepatotoxicity: AST, ALT >8000 U/L - Coagulopathy: INR 3.5 - Renal failure: Cr 2.1 - Altered mental status (hepatic encephalopathy) - pH 7.32 (metabolic acidosis)

  • Management:

1. N-Acetylcysteine (NAC): - Give even if >24 hours post-ingestion - Beneficial in ALF (improves survival even when late) - NOT too late for antidote

2. Liver Transplant Evaluation: - King's College Criteria (Acetaminophen): - pH <7.3 (this patient 7.32 - borderline) OR - All 3: INR >6.5, Cr >3.4, Grade 3-4 HE - This patient: Approaching criteria (INR 3.5, Cr 2.1, altered MS) - Urgent transplant evaluation warranted

3. Supportive Care (ICU): - Manage encephalopathy, coagulopathy, renal failure

  • Activated charcoal:
  • Effective ONLY within 1-2 hours of ingestion
  • 18 hours post-ingestion = too late

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 40-year-old man with chronic hepatitis B on entecavir presents for follow-up. Labs: HBV DNA undetectable, ALT 28 U/L. He asks when he can stop treatment. What is the appropriate response?

A) Stop entecavir now (HBV DNA undetectable) B) Stop after 6 more months of undetectable HBV DNA C) Continue indefinitely (lifelong treatment) D) Switch to pegylated interferon × 48 weeks, then stop

Answer: C) Continue indefinitely (lifelong treatment)

Explanation: - Chronic HBV - Treatment Duration:

Most patients: Indefinite treatment (lifelong) - Cirrhosis: NEVER stop (high reactivation risk) - HBeAg-negative chronic hepatitis: Indefinite - HBeAg-positive: May consider stopping after HBeAg seroconversion + undetectable HBV DNA × ≥12 months (but high relapse rate)

  • Goal: HBsAg clearance (rare - "functional cure")
  • Achieved in <10% even with long-term treatment

  • HBV DNA undetectable does NOT mean cured

  • If treatment stopped:

  • High reactivation rate (especially cirrhosis)

  • Close monitoring required (HBV DNA, ALT q3 months)

  • This patient: No indication for stopping

  • Continue entecavir indefinitely
  • Monitor HBV DNA, ALT every 6 months
  • HCC surveillance every 6 months (if cirrhotic or high-risk)

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A healthcare worker sustains a needlestick injury from an HBsAg-positive patient. The healthcare worker was vaccinated for hepatitis B 10 years ago but never had Anti-HBs checked. What is the MOST appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis?

A) No treatment needed (vaccinated) B) HBIG only C) HBV vaccine booster only D) Check Anti-HBs; if <10 mIU/mL, give HBIG + booster

Answer: D) Check Anti-HBs; if <10 mIU/mL, give HBIG + booster

Explanation: - Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) - Hepatitis B:

Vaccinated Healthcare Worker (unknown response): - Check Anti-HBs: - If ≥10 mIU/mL: Adequate protection → No treatment - If <10 mIU/mL: Inadequate response → HBIG + Booster dose

  • Unvaccinated:
  • HBIG (within 24 hours, up to 7 days)

  • Start HBV vaccine series (0, 1, 6 months)

  • Known Responder (documented Anti-HBs ≥10 after vaccination):

  • No treatment (lifelong immunity, even if titer declines)

  • Known Non-Responder (no response after 6 doses):

  • HBIG × 2 doses (0 and 1 month)

  • Timing:

  • HBIG: Most effective within 24 hours (up to 7 days)
  • Vaccine: Can give simultaneously (separate site)

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 55-year-old woman with chronic hepatitis C (compensated cirrhosis, Child-Pugh A) is starting DAA therapy. Which regimen is MOST appropriate?

A) Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir × 8 weeks B) Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir × 12 weeks C) Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir × 12 weeks D) Pegylated interferon + ribavirin × 48 weeks

Answer: C) Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir × 12 weeks

Explanation: - Chronic HCV with Compensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A):

Treatment Options: - Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (SOF/VEL): 12 weeks ✓ - Safe in compensated cirrhosis - Pan-genotypic

  • Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (GLE/PIB):
  • Contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C)
  • May use in compensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A): 12-16 weeks

  • NOT 8 weeks (8 weeks only for non-cirrhotic)

  • SOF/VEL preferred in cirrhosis (better safety data)

  • Decompensated Cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C):

  • SOF/VEL × 12 weeks + Ribavirin (if tolerated)
  • Avoid protease inhibitors (GLE contraindicated)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Viral Hepatitis: - HAV, HEV: Fecal-oral, self-limited, NO chronic (except HEV in immunocompromised/pregnant) - HBV: Chronic in 5% adults (90% neonates), treat with entecavir/tenofovir (indefinite) - HCV: Chronic in 75-85%, cure >95% with DAAs (SOF/VEL, GLE/PIB) - HDV: Requires HBV, superinfection worse than coinfection

Autoimmune Hepatitis: - Young women, hypergammaglobulinemia (IgG), ANA/SMA (Type 1), interface hepatitis - Treatment: Prednisone + azathioprine (lifelong in most)

Drug-Induced Liver Injury: - Acetaminophen most common, treat with NAC (most effective <8 hours, but give even if late)

Acute Liver Failure: - Acetaminophen #1 cause (US), King's College Criteria for transplant - ICU care, avoid correcting INR (unless bleeding/procedure)

End of Topic 26: Hepatitis (Viral & Autoimmune)

Next Topic: Pancreatitis (Acute & Chronic) (Medicine/27_Pancreatitis.md)

Week 2 Progress: 6/18 topics complete

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Acute Pancreatitis
  2. Chronic Pancreatitis
  3. Complications
  4. High-Yield Points
  5. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• Acute inflammatory process of the pancreas
  • Reversible (if uncomplicated)
  • Systemic inflammatory response variable

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Mnemonic: "I GET SMASHED": - I: Idiopathic - G: Gallstones (40-50% - most common) - E: Ethanol/Alcohol (30-40%) - T: Trauma - S: Steroids - M: Mumps (and other infections) - A: Autoimmune - S: Scorpion sting, snake bite - H: Hyperlipidemia (triglycerides >1000 mg/dL), Hypercalcemia - E: ERCP - D: Drugs

Common Causes:

1. Gallstones (40-50%) - Most common overall: - Mechanism: Stone impaction at ampulla of Vater → obstruction → reflux of bile → pancreatic injury - Risk factors: Female, obesity, rapid weight loss - Small stones (<5 mm) more likely to cause pancreatitis (pass into ampulla)

2. Alcohol (30-40%) - Most common in men: - Mechanism: Direct acinar cell toxicity, increased pancreatic secretions, premature enzyme activation - Typically: Years of heavy drinking (but can occur after single binge) - Dose-dependent: >50 g/day × 5+ years

3. Hypertriglyceridemia: - Triglycerides >1000 mg/dL (risk increases, pancreatitis rare if <500) - Mechanism: Lipolysis → free fatty acids → pancreatic injury - Causes: Genetic (familial hypertriglyceridemia), poorly controlled diabetes, alcohol, medications (estrogen, retinoids)

4. Hypercalcemia: - Hyperparathyroidism (most common cause of hypercalcemia) - Mechanism: Calcium deposition in pancreatic duct, premature enzyme activation - Any cause of hypercalcemia can trigger

5. Medications: - Common: Azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine, valproic acid, sulfonamides, furosemide, thiazides, pentamidine, L-asparaginase - Mechanism: Idiosyncratic (not dose-dependent)

6. ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography): - Post-ERCP pancreatitis: 3-5% of procedures - Risk factors: Young age, female, prior pancreatitis, SOD (sphincter of Oddi dysfunction) - Prevention: Rectal indomethacin, pancreatic duct stenting

7. Trauma: - Blunt abdominal trauma (MVA - steering wheel injury) - Iatrogenic: Post-surgical

8. Autoimmune: - IgG4-related disease (Type 1 AIP) - Diffuse pancreatic enlargement, elevated IgG4 - Responds to steroids

9. Genetic: - Hereditary pancreatitis: PRSS1 mutation (cationic trypsinogen) - Cystic fibrosis: CFTR mutation

10. Anatomic: - Pancreas divisum (most common pancreatic anomaly - 5-10% population) - Minor papilla drains majority of pancreas - May predispose to pancreatitis (controversial) - Ampullary tumor

11. Vascular: - Ischemia: Hypotension, vasculitis, atheroembolic

12. Infectious: - Viral: Mumps, Coxsackie, CMV, HIV - Parasitic: Ascaris (bile duct obstruction)

13. Idiopathic (10-20%): - Consider: - Occult microlithiasis (biliary sludge) - Occult alcohol use - Genetic mutations (if recurrent)

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Normal Pancreas: - Acinar cells produce digestive enzymes in inactive form (zymogens) - Trypsinogen → Trypsin (activated in duodenum by enterokinase) - Trypsin activates other enzymes (chymotrypsinogen, proelastase, phospholipase A)

Acute Pancreatitis Cascade: 

Inciting Event (gallstone, alcohol, etc.)
         ↓
Premature intracellular trypsinogen activation
         ↓
Trypsin activates other enzymes (elastase, phospholipase A)
         ↓
Autodigestion of pancreas
         ↓
Inflammation, edema, necrosis
         ↓
Systemic inflammatory response (cytokine release)
         ↓
Capillary leak, third-spacing, organ dysfunction

Forms: - Interstitial Edematous Pancreatitis (80-90%): - Pancreatic edema, inflammation - NO necrosis - Mild, self-limited

  • Necrotizing Pancreatitis (10-20%):
  • Pancreatic parenchymal necrosis and/or peripancreatic fat necrosis
  • More severe
  • Higher complication rate

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Symptoms:

Abdominal Pain (>95%): - Severe, constant epigastric pain - Radiates to back (50%) - Sudden onset (gallstones) or after binge drinking - Relieved by sitting up/leaning forward - Worsened by supine position, eating

Nausea, Vomiting (80-90%): - Persistent, severe

Physical Examination:

Mild-Moderate: - Epigastric tenderness - Abdominal distension (ileus) - Hypoactive bowel sounds - Tachycardia, low-grade fever

Severe: - Hypotension, tachycardia (hypovolemia, SIRS) - Abdominal guarding (peritonitis) - Decreased breath sounds (pleural effusion, ARDS) - Altered mental status (severe SIRS, hypoperfusion)

Rare Signs (retroperitoneal hemorrhage): - Cullen's sign: Periumbilical ecchymosis (intraperitoneal bleeding) - Grey Turner's sign: Flank ecchymosis (retroperitoneal bleeding) - Both present in <3%, indicate severe necrotizing pancreatitis

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Revised Atlanta Classification (2012):

Acute Pancreatitis Diagnosis requires 2 of 3: 1. Abdominal pain consistent with pancreatitis (epigastric, radiating to back) 2. Serum lipase (or amylase) ≥3× upper limit of normal 3. Imaging (CT, MRI, ultrasound) consistent with pancreatitis

docs.title: Laboratory
docs.text:
Pancreatic Enzymes:

Lipase: - Preferred over amylase (more sensitive and specific) - Elevated 4-8 hours, peaks 24 hours, normalizes 8-14 days - ≥3× ULN diagnostic

Amylase: - Less specific (also elevated in: intestinal perforation, salivary gland disease, ectopic pregnancy, diabetic ketoacidosis, renal failure) - Rises/falls faster than lipase (normalizes 3-5 days)

Degree of elevation does NOT correlate with severity

Other Labs: - CBC: Hemoconcentration (Hct >44% predicts severe), leukocytosis - CMP: - Hyperglycemia (insulin deficiency, stress) - Hypocalcemia (<8 mg/dL - saponification with fat necrosis, poor prognostic sign) - Elevated BUN, creatinine (prerenal AKI from hypovolemia) - LFTs: - ALT >150 U/L: 95% PPV for gallstone pancreatitis - Elevated bilirubin, ALP (biliary obstruction) - Triglycerides: Check if >1000 (lipemic serum) - Calcium: Hypercalcemia (parathyroid-related) - Arterial Blood Gas: Hypoxemia, metabolic acidosis (severe)

docs.title: Imaging
docs.text:
Not required for diagnosis (if clinical + labs sufficient)

Indications for Imaging: - Uncertain diagnosis - No improvement after 48-72 hours - Clinical deterioration - Assess complications (necrosis, fluid collections, pseudocyst)

Ultrasound (Abdominal): - First-line for suspected gallstone pancreatitis - Detects: Gallstones, bile duct dilation, gallbladder wall thickening - Limited pancreatic visualization (bowel gas)

CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast (Pancreatic Protocol): - BEST for assessing severity and complications - Timing: - Initial: Not routinely needed - Delayed (48-72 hours): Better delineates necrosis - Findings: - Pancreatic enlargement, edema - Peripancreatic fat stranding - Fluid collections - Pancreatic necrosis: Non-enhancing areas (lack of contrast uptake) - CT Severity Index (CTSI): Predicts severity

CT Severity Index (Balthazar Score):

Grade Findings Points A Normal pancreas 0 B Pancreatic enlargement 1 C Inflammation + peripancreatic fat 2 D Single fluid collection 3 E Multiple/extensive collections 4

Necrosis: - None: 0 - <30%: +2 - 30-50%: +4 - >50%: +6

Total CTSI: 0-10 (Higher score = worse prognosis)

MRI/MRCP: - Best for: Biliary anatomy, pancreatic duct, fluid collections - MRCP: Non-invasive (vs ERCP) - Less commonly used than CT

docs.title: Severity Assessment
docs.text:
Revised Atlanta Classification (2012):

Mild: - NO organ failure - NO local or systemic complications - Usually resolves in <1 week

Moderately Severe: - Transient organ failure (<48 hours) OR - Local complications (fluid collection, necrosis) OR - Exacerbation of comorbidities

Severe: - Persistent organ failure (>48 hours) - Single or multiple organ failure - High mortality (15-30%)

Organ Failure (Modified Marshall Score):

System 0 1 2 3 4 Respiratory (PaO₂/FiO₂) >400 301-400 201-300 101-200 ≤101 Renal (Creatinine mg/dL) <1.4 1.4-1.8 1.9-3.6 3.6-4.9 >4.9 Cardiovascular (SBP mmHg) >90 <90, fluid responsive <90, pH <7.3 <90, pH <7.2 <90, pH <7.1

Score ≥2 in any system = Organ failure

Bedside Index for Severity in Acute Pancreatitis (BISAP) Score:

Assessed at 24 hours (1 point each): - B: BUN >25 mg/dL - I: Impaired mental status (GCS <15) - S: SIRS (≥2 SIRS criteria) - A: Age >60 years - P: Pleural effusion on imaging

Score: - 0-2: Low mortality (<2%) - 3-5: High mortality (>15%)

SIRS Criteria (≥2 = SIRS): - Temperature <36°C or >38°C - Heart rate >90 bpm - Respiratory rate >20/min or PaCO₂ <32 mmHg - WBC <4,000 or >12,000 or >10% bands

Ranson's Criteria:

At Admission (1 point each): - Age >55 years - WBC >16,000/mm³ - Glucose >200 mg/dL - LDH >350 U/L - AST >250 U/L

At 48 Hours (1 point each): - Hematocrit drop >10% - BUN rise >5 mg/dL - Calcium <8 mg/dL - PaO₂ <60 mmHg - Base deficit >4 mEq/L - Fluid sequestration >6 L

Score: - 0-2: Mortality <1% - 3-4: Mortality 15% - 5-6: Mortality 40% - ≥7: Mortality nearly 100%

Limited use (requires 48 hours)

docs.title: Management
docs.text: Principles: 1. Supportive care (no specific treatment) 2. Aggressive fluid resuscitation 3. Pain control 4. Nutritional support 5. Treat underlying cause 6. Prevent/manage complications

docs.title: 1. Fluid Resuscitation (MOST IMPORTANT)
docs.text:
Early aggressive hydration (first 12-24 hours): - Goal-directed fluid therapy - Isotonic crystalloid (Ringer's lactate preferred): - Initial: 15-20 mL/kg bolus - Maintenance: 250-500 mL/hour (titrate to endpoints)

Endpoints: - Urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr - Heart rate <120 bpm - MAP 65-85 mmHg - Hematocrit: Maintain 35-44% (avoid hemoconcentration)

Rationale: Third-spacing causes massive fluid shifts → hypovolemia, SIRS

Avoid over-resuscitation: Pulmonary edema, abdominal compartment syndrome

docs.title: 2. Analgesia
docs.text: Pain is severe: - Opioids: Morphine, hydromorphone, fentanyl - No evidence that morphine worsens pancreatitis (old myth about Sphincter of Oddi spasm) - Adequate pain control improves outcomes

docs.title: 3. Nutritional Support
docs.text:
Mild Pancreatitis: - NPO initially (if severe nausea/vomiting) - Resume oral feeding when pain improves and nausea resolves (no need to wait for lipase normalization) - Low-fat diet initially, advance as tolerated

Severe Pancreatitis: - Enteral nutrition (within 24-72 hours): - Preferred over parenteral (TPN) - Reduces infection, mortality, cost - Route: Nasogastric OR nasojejunal (equivalent outcomes) - TPN: ONLY if enteral not tolerated (ileus, intolerance)

docs.title: 4. Antibiotics
docs.text:
NOT routinely indicated: - NO prophylactic antibiotics in sterile necrosis (no benefit, increases resistance)

Indications: - Infected necrosis (gas in necrosis on CT, positive FNA culture) - Extrapancreatic infection (cholangitis, pneumonia, UTI)

Empiric for infected necrosis: - Carbapenems (imipenem, meropenem) OR - Fluoroquinolone + Metronidazole - Penetrate pancreatic tissue

docs.title: 5. Management of Gallstone Pancreatitis
docs.text:
Cholecystectomy: - Same admission (after resolution of pancreatitis, usually within 48-72 hours) - Prevents recurrent pancreatitis (15-30% if no cholecystectomy) - Safe once inflammation subsides - If severe: Delay until inflammation resolves (weeks), but before discharge

ERCP: - Indications: - Acute cholangitis (URGENT - within 24 hours) - Biliary obstruction (persistent jaundice, dilated CBD) - NOT routinely indicated in gallstone pancreatitis without cholangitis/obstruction

docs.title: 6. Other Causes
docs.text:
Hypertriglyceridemia-Induced: - Insulin infusion + Dextrose (if glucose <250 mg/dL) - Activates lipoprotein lipase → ↓ triglycerides - Plasmapheresis: If refractory (TG >1000 despite insulin) - Long-term: Fibrates, omega-3 fatty acids, dietary restriction

Hypercalcemia: - IV fluids - Bisphosphonates (if severe) - Treat underlying cause (hyperparathyroidism)

Alcohol: - Abstinence counseling - Thiamine, nutritional support

docs.title: Early Complications (<1 week):
docs.text:
1. Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS): - Capillary leak, third-spacing - Hypotension, tachycardia

2. Organ Failure: - Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS): - Most common cause of death in first week - Bilateral infiltrates, hypoxemia - Mechanical ventilation if severe - Acute Kidney Injury (AKI): - Prerenal (hypovolemia) → ATN - Cardiovascular: Shock, arrhythmias

3. Gastrointestinal: - Ileus (common) - GI bleeding: Stress ulcers, variceal bleeding (splenic vein thrombosis)

4. Metabolic: - Hypocalcemia (<8 mg/dL): Saponification with fat necrosis - Hyperglycemia: Insulin deficiency (transient or permanent diabetes)

docs.title: Local Complications:
docs.text:
1. Acute Peripancreatic Fluid Collections (APFC): - <4 weeks from onset - No defined wall (homogeneous fluid) - Associated with interstitial pancreatitis - Management: Observation (most resolve spontaneously)

2. Pancreatic Pseudocyst: - >4 weeks from onset - Mature, defined wall (fibrous, granulation tissue) - Homogeneous fluid (no solid debris) - Arises from APFC (if persists >4 weeks)

Presentation: - Asymptomatic (incidental) OR - Persistent pain, early satiety, nausea - Palpable epigastric mass

Complications: - Infection (fever, leukocytosis) - Rupture (peritonitis) - Hemorrhage (erosion into vessel) - Biliary/gastric outlet obstruction

Diagnosis: - CT or MRI: Well-defined, round, fluid-filled

Management: - Asymptomatic, <6 cm: Observation (40-50% resolve spontaneously) - Symptomatic or complications: - Endoscopic drainage (transpapillary or transmural - EUS-guided) - Percutaneous drainage (if infected, immature wall) - Surgical cystogastrostomy (if endoscopy fails)

3. Acute Necrotic Collection (ANC): - <4 weeks from onset - Heterogeneous (fluid + necrotic debris) - Associated with necrotizing pancreatitis - No defined wall

4. Walled-Off Necrosis (WON): - >4 weeks from onset - Mature, defined wall - Heterogeneous (fluid + solid necrotic tissue) - Arises from ANC (if persists >4 weeks)

Infected Necrosis:

Definition: - Bacterial infection of pancreatic necrosis - Occurs in 30-40% of necrotizing pancreatitis - Typically 2-4 weeks after onset

Presentation: - Persistent fever, leukocytosis - Sepsis, multi-organ failure - Clinical deterioration after initial improvement

Diagnosis: - CT: Gas within necrosis (pathognomonic) - Fine-needle aspiration (FNA): Gram stain, culture (if no gas on CT)

Management:

Antibiotics: - Carbapenem (imipenem, meropenem) OR - Fluoroquinolone + Metronidazole

Drainage/Debridement (if deterioration despite antibiotics): - Delayed intervention preferred (≥4 weeks - allow WON formation, easier to drain)

Step-Up Approach (preferred): 1. Percutaneous/endoscopic drainage first 2. Minimally invasive necrosectomy if drainage fails 3. Open necrosectomy (last resort)

Direct open necrosectomy: Higher morbidity/mortality (avoid if possible)

5. Splanchnic Vein Thrombosis: - Splenic vein thrombosis (most common) - → Left-sided portal hypertension → Gastric varices - Portal vein, superior mesenteric vein thrombosis

Diagnosis: CT angiography or Doppler ultrasound

Management: - Anticoagulation (if symptomatic or extensive) - Splenectomy (if bleeding gastric varices)

6. Pancreatic Ascites / Pleural Effusion: - Pancreatic duct disruption → leakage of pancreatic juice - High amylase in ascitic fluid or pleural fluid

Management: - ERCP with pancreatic duct stenting - Octreotide (reduce pancreatic secretions) - Surgical repair (if ERCP fails)

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Mild Pancreatitis: - Mortality <1% - Resolves in days to 1 week

Severe Pancreatitis: - Mortality 15-30% - Early deaths (<1 week): SIRS, multi-organ failure - Late deaths (>1 week): Infected necrosis, sepsis

Predictors of Severity: - Persistent organ failure (>48 hours) - Pancreatic necrosis (especially >30%) - Infected necrosis - BISAP ≥3, Ranson's ≥3, APACHE II >8

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
• Irreversible destruction of pancreatic parenchyma
  • Fibrosis, chronic inflammation
  • Exocrine and endocrine dysfunction

docs.title: Etiology
docs.text:
Mnemonic: "TIGAR-O": - T: Toxic-metabolic (Alcohol #1, smoking, hypercalcemia) - I: Idiopathic - G: Genetic (PRSS1, CFTR, SPINK1) - A: Autoimmune (IgG4-related) - R: Recurrent acute pancreatitis - O: Obstructive (stricture, tumor, pancreas divisum)

Most Common Cause: Alcohol (70-80%) - Chronic, heavy use: >5 drinks/day × 5+ years - Dose-dependent - Smoking is independent risk factor (accelerates progression)

Tropical Pancreatitis: - Endemic in India, Africa, Brazil - Young age, malnutrition, cassava consumption - Ductal stones, diabetes

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text:
Proposed Mechanisms:

1. Toxic-Metabolic (Alcohol): - Direct acinar injury - Increased protein secretion → protein plugs → ductal obstruction → stone formation - Oxidative stress

2. Ductal Obstruction: - Stones, strictures, tumors - Increased intraductal pressure → acinar atrophy, fibrosis

3. Oxidative Stress: - Free radicals → acinar cell injury → inflammation → fibrosis

Progression: 

Chronic Injury (alcohol, etc.)
       ↓
Recurrent acute inflammation
       ↓
Fibrosis (irreversible)
       ↓
Exocrine insufficiency (↓ lipase, protease, amylase)
       ↓
Endocrine insufficiency (diabetes - "pancreatogenic diabetes")

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Pain (Most common symptom - 80-90%): - Chronic, recurrent epigastric pain - Radiates to back - Worse after eating (postprandial) - Relieved by leaning forward - May decrease over time (as gland "burns out" - fibrosis replaces parenchyma)

Exocrine Insufficiency (Malabsorption): - Occurs when >90% function lost - Steatorrhea: Bulky, greasy, foul-smelling stools - Weight loss - Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (A, D, E, K)

Endocrine Insufficiency: - Diabetes mellitus ("Type 3c" - pancreatogenic): - Insulin AND glucagon deficiency - Brittle (prone to hypoglycemia - no glucagon response) - Low insulin requirement (vs Type ½) - Occurs in 30-80% over time

Physical Examination: - Often unremarkable - Epigastric tenderness - Cachexia (malabsorption) - Jaundice (if biliary stricture)

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Clinical: - History: Chronic alcohol use, recurrent pancreatitis, pain, steatorrhea, diabetes

Laboratory: - Pancreatic enzymes (amylase, lipase): - Often NORMAL (acinar atrophy) - Not useful for diagnosis - Fecal elastase-1: <200 μg/g (exocrine insufficiency) - Glucose: Elevated (diabetes) - Fat-soluble vitamins: Deficiency

Imaging:

CT Abdomen: - Pancreatic atrophy - Ductal dilation (>4 mm) - Calcifications (PATHOGNOMONIC - 30-50%) - Intraductal stones - Parenchymal calcifications - Pseudocysts

MRI/MRCP: - Better ductal detail than CT - "Chain of lakes" appearance (dilated main duct with strictures)

Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS): - Most sensitive for early chronic pancreatitis - Parenchymal changes: Hyperechoic foci, stranding, lobularity - Ductal changes: Irregularity, dilation, stones, strictures

ERCP: - Gold standard for ductal anatomy - "Chain of lakes", strictures, stones - Therapeutic (stone removal, stricture dilation, stenting) - Rarely used diagnostically (invasive - MRCP/EUS preferred)

Function Tests (Rarely done): - Secretin stimulation test: Measure bicarbonate secretion (↓ in chronic pancreatitis)

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
1. Pain (Chronic, debilitating): - Impacts quality of life

2. Exocrine Insufficiency: - Steatorrhea, malnutrition, vitamin deficiency

3. Endocrine Insufficiency: - Diabetes (30-80%)

4. Pancreatic Pseudocyst: - Common (20-40%) - Asymptomatic OR pain, early satiety, obstruction

5. Biliary Stricture: - Common bile duct compression (fibrosis, pseudocyst) - Painless jaundice, cholangitis - Management: ERCP with stenting, surgery

6. Duodenal Obstruction: - Gastric outlet obstruction - Fibrosis, pseudocyst

7. Splenic Vein Thrombosis: - Left-sided portal hypertension → gastric varices

8. Pancreatic Cancer: - Increased risk (5-15× general population) - Difficult to distinguish from chronic pancreatitis (both cause mass, ductal obstruction) - Consider if: New-onset pain, weight loss, CA 19-9 elevation

docs.title: Management
docs.text: Goals: 1. Pain control 2. Manage exocrine insufficiency 3. Manage endocrine insufficiency 4. Treat complications

docs.title: 1. Pain Management
docs.text:
Lifestyle Modifications: - Alcohol abstinence (CRITICAL) - Smoking cessation - Small, frequent, low-fat meals

Medications:

Analgesics: - Start with acetaminophen, NSAIDs - Opioids: Often required (high addiction risk - careful monitoring) - Tramadol, oxycodone, morphine

Pancreatic Enzyme Supplementation: - Controversial for pain (may reduce secretion via negative feedback) - Non-enteric coated formulations (enteric-coated inactive in duodenum) - Limited evidence

Antioxidants: - Controversial (limited evidence)

Pregabalin/Gabapentin: - Neuropathic pain component

Endoscopic Therapy: - ERCP: - Pancreatic duct stone removal (lithotripsy if large) - Stricture dilation + stenting - Helps if ductal obstruction (30-60% pain relief)

Celiac Plexus Block: - EUS-guided or CT-guided - Temporary pain relief (weeks to months)

Surgery: - Indications: Refractory pain despite medical/endoscopic therapy - Procedures: - Lateral pancreaticojejunostomy (Puestow procedure): - Dilated duct (>7 mm) - Drain duct into jejunum - Pancreatic resection: - Whipple (pancreaticoduodenectomy) - head disease - Distal pancreatectomy - tail disease - Total pancreatectomy (last resort - causes brittle diabetes)

docs.title: 2. Exocrine Insufficiency
docs.text:
Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT): - Indications: Steatorrhea, malnutrition, weight loss - Formulation: Enteric-coated (protect from gastric acid) - Dosing: - 40,000-50,000 units lipase with each meal - 20,000-25,000 units with snacks - Take with meals

Adjuncts: - Proton pump inhibitor (PPI): If inadequate response (↑ duodenal pH for enzyme activity)

Fat-Soluble Vitamins: - Supplement A, D, E, K

Diet: - High-protein, high-calorie - Low-fat if severe steatorrhea despite enzymes - Small, frequent meals

docs.title: 3. Endocrine Insufficiency (Diabetes)
docs.text: Diabetes Management: - Insulin therapy (usually required) - Brittle diabetes (prone to hypoglycemia - no glucagon) - Caution with sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk) - Frequent glucose monitoring

docs.title: 4. Complications
docs.text:
Pseudocyst: - Asymptomatic, <6 cm: Observation - Symptomatic: Endoscopic drainage (EUS-guided), surgical drainage

Biliary Stricture: - ERCP with stenting (short-term) - Surgery (hepaticojejunostomy) if long-term obstruction

Pancreatic Cancer Surveillance: - No established guidelines - High index of suspicion if new symptoms, CA 19-9 elevation

docs.title: Prognosis
docs.text:
Mortality: - 50% 10-year survival - Higher mortality than general population

Causes of Death: - Complications of chronic pancreatitis (infection, bleeding, malnutrition) - Pancreatic cancer - Complications of alcohol (cirrhosis, malnutrition)

Quality of Life: - Chronic pain impairs quality of life - Malabsorption, diabetes

Alcohol Abstinence: - Improves pain in 50% - Slows progression - Does NOT reverse fibrosis

docs.title: Acute Pancreatitis
docs.text:
Diagnosis (2 of 3): 1. Pain consistent with pancreatitis 2. Lipase ≥3× ULN (preferred over amylase) 3. Imaging consistent

Most Common Causes: - Gallstones (40-50%) - overall #1 - Alcohol (30-40%) - #1 in men - Hypertriglyceridemia (>1000 mg/dL)

Severity: - Mild: No organ failure, no complications - Severe: Persistent organ failure (>48 hours)

Management: - Aggressive fluid resuscitation (MOST IMPORTANT - Ringer's lactate 250-500 mL/hr) - Early enteral nutrition (severe - within 24-72 hours) - NO prophylactic antibiotics (sterile necrosis) - Gallstone pancreatitis: Cholecystectomy same admission - ERCP: ONLY if cholangitis or biliary obstruction (NOT routine)
