Complications: - ARDS: Most common cause of death (first week) - Infected necrosis: 30-40% of necrotizing pancreatitis (2-4 weeks) - CT: Gas in necrosis - Antibiotics + Delayed drainage (step-up approach) - Pseudocyst: >4 weeks, defined wall, homogeneous fluid - Asymptomatic <6 cm: Observe - Symptomatic: Endoscopic drainage

Cullen's Sign (periumbilical) and Grey Turner's Sign (flank): Retroperitoneal hemorrhage (rare, severe necrotizing)

ALT >150 U/L: 95% PPV for gallstone pancreatitis

docs.title: Chronic Pancreatitis
docs.text:
Most Common Cause: Alcohol (70-80%)

Pathognomonic Finding: Pancreatic calcifications on CT

Clinical Triad: 1. Chronic pain (80-90%) 2. Steatorrhea (exocrine insufficiency - when >90% function lost) 3. Diabetes (endocrine insufficiency - 30-80%)

Diagnosis: - CT: Calcifications, ductal dilation, atrophy - MRCP: "Chain of lakes" (dilated duct with strictures) - EUS: Most sensitive (early disease) - Fecal elastase: <200 μg/g (exocrine insufficiency)

Management: - Pain: Abstinence, analgesics (opioids often needed), ERCP (if ductal obstruction), surgery (refractory) - Exocrine insufficiency: PERT (40,000-50,000 units lipase with meals) + PPI - Diabetes: Insulin (brittle - prone to hypoglycemia)

Complications: - Pseudocyst (20-40%) - Biliary stricture (CBD compression) - Pancreatic cancer (5-15× increased risk)

docs.title: Key Differences: Acute vs Chronic Pancreatitis
docs.text: Feature Acute Chronic Pathology Inflammation, edema (reversible) Fibrosis (irreversible) Lipase/Amylase Elevated (≥3× ULN) Normal or mildly elevated Calcifications Absent Present (pathognomonic) Pain Severe, acute Chronic, recurrent (may "burn out") Exocrine Insufficiency Rare Common (>90% loss) Treatment Supportive, fluids Pain control, PERT, insulin

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 45-year-old woman presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting for 12 hours. Labs: Lipase 1200 U/L (normal <60), ALT 280 U/L, AST 190 U/L, bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL. Ultrasound shows gallstones. What is the MOST appropriate next step after initial stabilization and pain control?

A) Immediate cholecystectomy B) Immediate ERCP C) Cholecystectomy during same admission after pancreatitis resolves D) Discharge with outpatient cholecystectomy in 6 weeks

Answer: C) Cholecystectomy during same admission after pancreatitis resolves

Explanation: - Gallstone pancreatitis: - Lipase ≥3× ULN ✓ - ALT >150 U/L → 95% PPV for gallstones ✓ (this patient 280) - Gallstones on ultrasound ✓

  • Management:
  • Cholecystectomy same admission (after pancreatitis resolves - usually 48-72 hours)

  • Prevents recurrent pancreatitis (15-30% if no cholecystectomy)

  • ERCP indications:

  • Acute cholangitis (URGENT)
  • Biliary obstruction (persistent jaundice, dilated CBD)

  • NOT routine in gallstone pancreatitis

  • Immediate cholecystectomy: Not during acute inflammation (wait for resolution)

  • Delayed cholecystectomy (outpatient): Contraindicated (high recurrence risk)

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 55-year-old man with acute pancreatitis secondary to alcohol is admitted to the ICU. On day 3, he develops persistent hypotension (SBP 80 mmHg despite fluids) and hypoxemia (PaO₂/FiO₂ 150). What is his pancreatitis severity classification?

A) Mild B) Moderately severe C) Severe D) Cannot determine without CT

Answer: C) Severe

Explanation: - Revised Atlanta Classification:

Severe Acute Pancreatitis: - Persistent organ failure (>48 hours)

Organ Failure (Modified Marshall Score ≥2): - Respiratory: PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio (this patient 150 → score ≥2) - Cardiovascular: Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)

  • This patient:
  • Day 3 (>48 hours) ✓
  • Respiratory failure (PaO₂/FiO₂ 150) ✓
  • Cardiovascular failure (hypotension) ✓

  • → Severe pancreatitis

  • Mild: No organ failure, no complications

  • Moderately Severe: Transient organ failure (<48 hours) OR local complications

  • Severe: Persistent organ failure (>48 hours)

  • Mortality: Severe pancreatitis 15-30%

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 60-year-old man with severe acute pancreatitis has been NPO for 5 days in the ICU. He is hemodynamically stable but still has significant pain. What is the MOST appropriate nutritional management?

A) Continue NPO until pain resolves and lipase normalizes B) Start nasogastric enteral nutrition C) Start total parenteral nutrition (TPN) D) Wait for oral diet tolerance

Answer: B) Start nasogastric enteral nutrition

Explanation: - Severe Acute Pancreatitis - Nutrition:

Enteral Nutrition (within 24-72 hours): - Preferred over TPN - Benefits: - ↓ Infection - ↓ Mortality - ↓ Cost - Preserves gut barrier function

  • Route: Nasogastric OR Nasojejunal (equivalent outcomes)

  • NG feeding safe (no need for post-pyloric)

  • TPN: ONLY if enteral not tolerated (ileus, intolerance)

  • Mild pancreatitis:

  • Resume oral feeding when pain improves and nausea resolves
  • No need to wait for lipase normalization

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 50-year-old man with necrotizing pancreatitis develops fever (39°C) and leukocytosis (WBC 18,000) on day 14. CT shows a 6 cm area of pancreatic necrosis with gas bubbles within it. What is the diagnosis and MOST appropriate management?

A) Sterile necrosis; observation B) Infected necrosis; immediate open necrosectomy C) Infected necrosis; antibiotics + delayed minimally invasive drainage (step-up approach) D) Pancreatic abscess; broad-spectrum antibiotics only

Answer: C) Infected necrosis; antibiotics + delayed minimally invasive drainage (step-up approach)

Explanation: - Infected Pancreatic Necrosis: - Day 14 (typically 2-4 weeks) ✓ - Fever, leukocytosis (sepsis) ✓ - CT: Gas within necrosis (pathognomonic) ✓

  • Management:

1. Antibiotics: - Carbapenem (imipenem, meropenem) OR - Fluoroquinolone + Metronidazole

2. Drainage/Debridement: - Step-Up Approach (preferred): 1. Percutaneous or endoscopic drainage (first) 2. Minimally invasive necrosectomy (if drainage fails) 3. Open necrosectomy (last resort)

  • Delayed intervention (≥4 weeks) preferred:
  • Allows walled-off necrosis (WON) formation

  • Easier to drain, better outcomes

  • Immediate open necrosectomy: Higher mortality (avoid)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 35-year-old man with a history of recurrent acute pancreatitis presents with a 6-week history of persistent epigastric pain. CT shows a 4 cm well-defined, round, fluid-filled lesion in the pancreatic tail with a mature wall. What is the diagnosis and appropriate management?

A) Acute peripancreatic fluid collection; observation B) Pancreatic pseudocyst; immediate surgical drainage C) Pancreatic pseudocyst; observation (likely to resolve) D) Walled-off necrosis; antibiotics

Answer: C) Pancreatic pseudocyst; observation (likely to resolve)

Explanation: - Pancreatic Pseudocyst: - >4 weeks from pancreatitis onset ✓ (6 weeks) - Mature, defined wall ✓ - Homogeneous fluid (no solid debris) ✓ - Round, well-defined ✓

  • Management:
  • Asymptomatic, <6 cm: Observation (40-50% resolve spontaneously) ✓

  • Symptomatic or complications: Drainage (endoscopic preferred)

  • Acute Peripancreatic Fluid Collection (APFC):

  • <4 weeks (NOT this patient - 6 weeks)

  • No defined wall

  • Walled-Off Necrosis (WON):

  • >4 weeks
  • Heterogeneous (fluid + solid necrotic debris)
  • NOT homogeneous fluid (like this case)

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 52-year-old man with chronic alcohol use presents with chronic epigastric pain, steatorrhea, and weight loss. CT abdomen shows pancreatic atrophy and multiple calcifications. Fecal elastase is 120 μg/g. What is the MOST appropriate treatment for his steatorrhea?

A) Low-fat diet only B) Pancreatic enzyme replacement 40,000 units lipase with meals C) High-dose PPI therapy D) Octreotide injections

Answer: B) Pancreatic enzyme replacement 40,000 units lipase with meals

Explanation: - Chronic Pancreatitis with Exocrine Insufficiency: - Chronic alcohol use ✓ - Calcifications on CT (pathognomonic) ✓ - Steatorrhea ✓ - Fecal elastase <200 μg/g (exocrine insufficiency) ✓ (this patient 120)

  • Management of Exocrine Insufficiency:

Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT): - 40,000-50,000 units lipase with each meal - 20,000-25,000 units with snacks - Enteric-coated formulation (protect from gastric acid) - Take with meals

Adjuncts: - PPI: If inadequate response (↑ duodenal pH) - Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)

  • Low-fat diet: May help but NOT sufficient alone
  • Octreotide: NOT for exocrine insufficiency (reduces secretions - not replacement)

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 48-year-old woman presents with severe epigastric pain, nausea, and vomiting. Labs: Lipase 450 U/L, triglycerides 2800 mg/dL, lipemic serum. What is the MOST appropriate initial management in addition to aggressive fluid resuscitation?

A) Plasmapheresis B) Gemfibrozil C) Insulin infusion + dextrose D) Omega-3 fatty acids

Answer: C) Insulin infusion + dextrose

Explanation: - Hypertriglyceridemia-Induced Pancreatitis: - Lipase elevated ✓ - Triglycerides >1000 mg/dL ✓ (this patient 2800) - Lipemic serum (milky appearance) ✓

  • Management:

Acute: - Insulin infusion + Dextrose (if glucose <250 mg/dL): - Activates lipoprotein lipase → ↓ triglycerides rapidly - First-line

  • Plasmapheresis:
  • If refractory (TG >1000 despite insulin)
  • Labor-intensive, expensive

Long-term prevention: - Fibrates (gemfibrozil, fenofibrate) - Omega-3 fatty acids (high-dose) - Dietary fat restriction

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 65-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis develops painless jaundice. Labs: Total bilirubin 8 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 6 mg/dL, ALP 450 U/L, lipase 85 U/L. MRCP shows distal common bile duct stricture with upstream dilation. What is the MOST likely complication?

A) Pancreatic cancer B) Choledocholithiasis C) Biliary stricture from chronic pancreatitis D) Primary sclerosing cholangitis

Answer: C) Biliary stricture from chronic pancreatitis

Explanation: - Chronic Pancreatitis Complication - Biliary Stricture: - Painless jaundice ✓ - Cholestatic pattern (elevated bilirubin, ALP) ✓ - MRCP: Distal CBD stricture ✓

  • Mechanism:
  • Fibrosis or pseudocyst compressing common bile duct

  • Usually distal CBD (passes through pancreatic head)

  • Management:

  • ERCP with stenting (short-term)

  • Surgery (hepaticojejunostomy) if long-term obstruction

  • Pancreatic cancer:

  • Consider in chronic pancreatitis patients (increased risk)
  • Differentiate with CA 19-9, EUS with biopsy

  • This patient: No mass mentioned, chronic pancreatitis known

  • Choledocholithiasis:

  • Filling defect (stone) on MRCP (NOT stricture)

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A 42-year-old man with chronic pancreatitis and diabetes is started on insulin therapy. He experiences frequent episodes of severe hypoglycemia (glucose <40 mg/dL) despite low insulin doses. What is the explanation?

A) Excessive insulin dosing B) Type 3c diabetes with glucagon deficiency (brittle diabetes) C) Insulin resistance D) Factitious hypoglycemia

Answer: B) Type 3c diabetes with glucagon deficiency (brittle diabetes)

Explanation: - Pancreatogenic Diabetes (Type 3c): - Chronic pancreatitis → Destruction of islet cells - Insulin deficiency (beta cells) - Glucagon deficiency (alpha cells)

  • Brittle Diabetes:
  • Prone to hypoglycemia
  • No glucagon response to counteract insulin
  • Low insulin requirement (vs Type ½)

  • Frequent glucose fluctuations

  • Management:

  • Insulin therapy required
  • Frequent glucose monitoring
  • Caution with sulfonylureas (↑ hypoglycemia risk)
  • Glucagon kit for severe hypoglycemia

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A 38-year-old man presents with severe epigastric pain. Labs: Lipase 2000 U/L, calcium 12.5 mg/dL, PTH 185 pg/mL (elevated). What is the MOST likely underlying cause of his pancreatitis?

A) Gallstones B) Alcohol C) Hypercalcemia (hyperparathyroidism) D) Hypertriglyceridemia

Answer: C) Hypercalcemia (hyperparathyroidism)

Explanation: - Hypercalcemia-Induced Pancreatitis: - Acute pancreatitis (lipase 2000) ✓ - Hypercalcemia (calcium 12.5 mg/dL, normal 8.5-10.5) ✓ - Elevated PTH → Primary hyperparathyroidism ✓

  • Mechanism:
  • Calcium deposition in pancreatic duct

  • Premature trypsinogen activation

  • Management:

  • Acute:
    • IV fluids
    • Bisphosphonates (if severe hypercalcemia)

  • Definitive: Parathyroidectomy (if hyperparathyroidism confirmed)

  • Any cause of hypercalcemia can cause pancreatitis:

  • Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common)
  • Malignancy
  • Vitamin D intoxication
  • Granulomatous disease

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Acute Pancreatitis: - Diagnosis: 2 of 3 (pain + lipase ≥3× ULN + imaging) - Causes: Gallstones (#1 overall), Alcohol (#1 men), Hypertriglyceridemia (>1000) - Management: Aggressive fluids (Ringer's lactate), early enteral nutrition (severe), NO prophylactic antibiotics - Gallstone pancreatitis: Cholecystectomy same admission; ERCP only if cholangitis/obstruction - Complications: ARDS (early death), infected necrosis (late), pseudocyst (>4 weeks)

Chronic Pancreatitis: - Cause: Alcohol (70-80%) - Pathognomonic: Pancreatic calcifications - Triad: Pain + Steatorrhea (exocrine insufficiency) + Diabetes (endocrine insufficiency) - Management: Abstinence, PERT (40,000-50,000 units lipase with meals), insulin (brittle diabetes)

End of Topic 27: Pancreatitis (Acute & Chronic)

Next Topic: Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) (Medicine/28_Acute_Kidney_Injury.md)

Week 2 Progress: 7/18 topics complete (GI section complete, moving to Nephrology)

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Definition and Classification
  2. Etiology
  3. Pathophysiology
  4. Clinical Features and Diagnosis
  5. Management
  6. Specific Conditions
  7. High-Yield Points
  8. Practice MCQs

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Acute Kidney Injury (AKI): Rapid decline in kidney function

KDIGO Criteria (2012) - AKI if ANY of the following: 1. Serum creatinine ↑ ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours OR 2. Serum creatinine ↑ ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days OR 3. Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6 hours

docs.title: Staging (KDIGO)
docs.text:
Stage Serum Creatinine Urine Output 1 1.5-1.9× baseline OR ↑ ≥0.3 mg/dL <0.5 mL/kg/hr × 6-12 hrs 2 2.0-2.9× baseline <0.5 mL/kg/hr × ≥12 hrs 3 ≥3× baseline OR ↑ to ≥4 mg/dL OR Initiation of RRT <0.3 mL/kg/hr × ≥24 hrs OR Anuria × 12 hrs

Higher stage = Worse prognosis

docs.title: Anatomic Classification
docs.text:
Mnemonic: "Pre-Renal-Post"

1. Prerenal (60-70%) - Decreased renal perfusion 2. Intrinsic Renal (25-40%) - Kidney parenchymal damage 3. Postrenal (5-10%) - Urinary tract obstruction

docs.title: Prerenal AKI
docs.text:
Definition: ↓ Renal perfusion → ↓ GFR (kidneys structurally NORMAL)

Causes:

Hypovolemia (True volume depletion): - Hemorrhage: GI bleeding, trauma, surgery - GI losses: Vomiting, diarrhea, NG suction - Renal losses: Diuretics, osmotic diuresis (DKA, hyperglycemia), diabetes insipidus - Third-spacing: Pancreatitis, burns, cirrhosis (ascites), nephrotic syndrome - Insensible losses: Fever, sweating

Decreased Effective Arterial Blood Volume: - Heart failure (↓ cardiac output) - Cirrhosis (splanchnic vasodilation, ↓ effective volume) - Sepsis (vasodilation)

Renal Vasoconstriction: - NSAIDs: Inhibit prostaglandin → afferent arteriole constriction - ACE inhibitors / ARBs: Efferent arteriole dilation → ↓ GFR (especially if renal artery stenosis, volume depletion) - Hepatorenal syndrome - Contrast-induced nephropathy

Large Vessel Disease: - Renal artery stenosis (bilateral or solitary kidney) - Renal artery thrombosis/embolism - Abdominal aortic aneurysm

docs.title: Intrinsic Renal AKI
docs.text:
Definition: Direct kidney parenchymal injury

Classification by Anatomic Site:

docs.title: A. Tubular (Most Common Intrinsic)
docs.text:
Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) - 80-90% of intrinsic AKI:

Ischemic ATN (Most common): - Prolonged prerenal state → tubular cell death - Causes: Shock (septic, cardiogenic, hypovolemic), surgery, severe hypotension

Toxic (Nephrotoxic) ATN: - Exogenous: - Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin) - Amphotericin B - Radiocontrast (iodinated contrast) - Cisplatin (chemotherapy) - Tenofovir (antiretroviral) - Heavy metals: Lead, mercury - Ethylene glycol, methanol - Endogenous: - Myoglobin (rhabdomyolysis) - Hemoglobin (hemolysis) - Light chains (multiple myeloma - "cast nephropathy") - Uric acid (tumor lysis syndrome)

docs.title: B. Interstitial
docs.text:
Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN): - Inflammation of renal interstitium

Causes: - Drugs (75% of cases): - Antibiotics: Penicillins (methicillin), cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, rifampin, sulfonamides - PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole) - increasing cause - NSAIDs - Allopurinol - 5-ASA - Infections: Pyelonephritis, viral (CMV, EBV), Legionella - Autoimmune: SLE, Sjögren's, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease - Idiopathic

Classic Triad (only 10-30% have all 3): - Fever - Rash - Eosinophilia

docs.title: C. Glomerular
docs.text:
Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN): - Crescentic GN on biopsy - Rapid loss of kidney function (days to weeks)

Causes (by immunofluorescence pattern): 1. Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture's): Linear IF 2. Immune complex GN: Granular IF (post-infectious, lupus nephritis, IgA nephropathy) 3. Pauci-immune GN: Negative IF (ANCA-associated vasculitis)

docs.title: D. Vascular
docs.text:
Large Vessel: - Renal artery thrombosis/embolism: Atrial fibrillation, endocarditis - Renal vein thrombosis: Nephrotic syndrome, hypercoagulable state - Aortic dissection

Small Vessel: - Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA): - HUS (Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome): Shiga toxin (E. coli O157:H7), atypical (complement-mediated) - TTP (Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura): ADAMTS13 deficiency - Malignant hypertension - Scleroderma renal crisis - Atheroembolic disease (cholesterol emboli - post-catheterization)

docs.title: Postrenal AKI
docs.text:
Definition: Urinary tract obstruction

Causes:

Bladder Outlet: - BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia) - most common - Prostate cancer - Bladder cancer - Neurogenic bladder - Urethral stricture

Bilateral Ureteral Obstruction (or single kidney): - Retroperitoneal fibrosis - Pelvic malignancy (cervical, ovarian, colorectal) - Bilateral stones (rare) - Papillary necrosis: Diabetes, sickle cell, NSAIDs

Intrarenal Obstruction: - Crystals: Uric acid (tumor lysis), oxalate (ethylene glycol), sulfonamides, acyclovir, methotrexate - Light chains (myeloma cast nephropathy)

docs.title: Prerenal AKI
docs.text:
Mechanism: 

↓ Renal perfusion
      ↓
↓ GFR (reversible if perfusion restored)
      ↓
Tubular function INTACT
      ↓
Avid Na⁺ and water reabsorption (trying to restore volume)
      ↓
↑ BUN (reabsorbed), ↑ Creatinine (less reabsorbed)
      ↓
BUN:Cr ratio >20:1

Key: Kidneys are structurally normal → responds to volume/perfusion restoration

docs.title: Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN)
docs.text:
Ischemic ATN: 

Prolonged ischemia
      ↓
Tubular cell injury/death
      ↓
Loss of brush border, cell sloughing
      ↓
Tubular obstruction (cellular debris)
      ↓
Backleak of filtrate
      ↓
↓ GFR

Phases: 1. Initiation (hours to days): Injury occurs 2. Maintenance (1-2 weeks): Established AKI, oliguria 3. Recovery (days to weeks): Tubular repair, polyuria (unable to concentrate urine)

Nephrotoxic ATN: - Direct tubular cell toxicity - Proximal tubule most vulnerable (high metabolic demand)

docs.title: Acute Interstitial Nephritis
docs.text: Mechanism: - Type IV hypersensitivity (cell-mediated) - Interstitial inflammation (lymphocytes, eosinophils) - Tubular dysfunction

docs.title: Postrenal AKI
docs.text:
Mechanism: 

Urinary obstruction
      ↓
↑ Intratubular pressure
      ↓
↓ GFR
      ↓
If prolonged: Tubular damage, fibrosis

Reversibility: Depends on duration - <1 week: Fully reversible - >12 weeks: Irreversible damage

docs.title: Clinical Presentation
docs.text:
Symptoms (Often asymptomatic - discovered on labs): - Oliguria (<400 mL/day) or anuria (<100 mL/day) - Nausea, vomiting (uremia) - Confusion (uremia, electrolytes) - Edema (fluid overload) - Dyspnea (pulmonary edema)

Signs: - Volume status: Hypovolemia (prerenal) vs overload (oliguric ATN) - Rash (AIN, vasculitis) - Palpable bladder (obstruction)

docs.title: Step 1: Confirm AKI
docs.text:
Serum Creatinine: - ↑ ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 hours OR ≥1.5× baseline - Limitation: Lags behind actual GFR (takes time to equilibrate)

Urine Output: - <0.5 mL/kg/hr × 6 hours

Baseline Creatinine: - Use lowest creatinine in past 3 months (if available) - If unknown: Estimate from MDRD equation assuming GFR 75 mL/min

docs.title: Step 2: Differentiate Prerenal vs Intrinsic vs Postrenal
docs.text:
History: - Prerenal: Vomiting, diarrhea, bleeding, poor oral intake, diuretics, heart failure, cirrhosis - Intrinsic: Hypotension, nephrotoxins (drugs, contrast, rhabdomyolysis), systemic disease (vasculitis, lupus) - Postrenal: BPH, pelvic cancer, difficulty urinating

Physical Exam: - Volume status: JVP, skin turgor, mucous membranes, edema - Bladder: Palpable (obstruction), percussion - Skin: Livedo reticularis (atheroemboli, vasculitis)

Urinalysis and Microscopy (CRITICAL):

Finding Diagnosis Hyaline casts Prerenal, normal Muddy brown/granular casts ATN RBC casts Glomerulonephritis WBC casts Pyelonephritis, AIN Eosinophils AIN (urine eosinophils - low sensitivity) Crystals Uric acid, oxalate, drug crystals Proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) Nephrotic syndrome Hematuria + proteinuria Glomerulonephritis

Muddy brown casts = Pathognomonic for ATN

Urine Indices:

Index Prerenal ATN (Intrinsic) FENa (Fractional Excretion of Sodium) <1% >2% FEUrea <35% >50% Urine Na⁺ <20 mEq/L >40 mEq/L Urine Osmolality >500 mOsm/kg <350 mOsm/kg Urine Specific Gravity >1.020 <1.010 BUN:Cr Ratio >20:1 <20:1

FENa Formula: 

FENa = (Urine Na × Plasma Cr) / (Plasma Na × Urine Cr) × 100

<1% = Prerenal
>2% = ATN
1-2% = Indeterminate

Interpretation: - Prerenal: Kidneys intact → avid Na⁺ reabsorption → low urine Na⁺, low FENa - ATN: Tubular dysfunction → can't reabsorb Na⁺ → high urine Na⁺, high FENa

Limitations of FENa: - False low FENa (<1%) in ATN if: - Diuretics (use FEUrea instead - not affected by diuretics) - Chronic kidney disease - Contrast nephropathy, rhabdomyolysis - False high FENa (>1%) in prerenal if: - Diuretics - Adrenal insufficiency

FEUrea: - Use if on diuretics (not affected) - <35% = Prerenal - >50% = ATN

Imaging:

Renal Ultrasound: - First-line for suspected obstruction - Findings: - Hydronephrosis (postrenal) - Kidney size (small kidneys = chronic disease) - Renal artery Doppler (if vascular suspected)

CT Abdomen/Pelvis (without contrast): - Stones, masses, obstruction - Avoid IV contrast (nephrotoxic in AKI)

docs.title: Step 3: Specific Diagnosis
docs.text:
Labs: - CBC: Anemia (hemolysis, chronic disease), eosinophilia (AIN), schistocytes (TMA) - CMP: Electrolytes (hyperkalemia, acidosis), calcium, phosphate - CK: Rhabdomyolysis (CK >5000 U/L) - LDH, haptoglobin: Hemolysis - Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP), urine protein electrophoresis (UPEP): Myeloma - Complement (C3, C4): Low in post-infectious GN, lupus - ANA, anti-dsDNA: SLE - ANCA: Vasculitis (MPO, PR3) - Anti-GBM: Goodpasture's

Kidney Biopsy: - Indications: - Unclear etiology (especially if not responding) - Suspected glomerulonephritis (guide treatment) - Suspected AIN (if no improvement after stopping drug)

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text:
1. Identify and Treat Underlying Cause: - Prerenal: Restore volume/perfusion - Intrinsic: Stop nephrotoxins, treat infection, immunosuppression (if GN/AIN) - Postrenal: Relieve obstruction

2. Supportive Care: - Fluid management - Avoid nephrotoxins - Adjust medications for GFR - Treat complications

3. Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT) if indicated

docs.title: Prerenal AKI
docs.text:
Restore Renal Perfusion:

Hypovolemia: - IV Fluids: - Isotonic crystalloid (0.9% saline, Ringer's lactate) - Bolus: 500-1000 mL, reassess - Goal: Restore euvolemia (urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr, ↓ creatinine)

Heart Failure: - Optimize cardiac output: Diuretics (if volume overloaded), inotropes (if cardiogenic shock)

Sepsis: - Early aggressive fluids (30 mL/kg), vasopressors (norepinephrine)

Discontinue Offending Agents: - NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs (if volume depleted) - Diuretics (temporarily)

Monitor Response: - Creatinine should ↓ within 24-48 hours if prerenal

docs.title: Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN)
docs.text:
Supportive Care (NO specific treatment):

1. Identify and Remove Cause: - Stop nephrotoxins: Aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, contrast - Treat shock (ischemic ATN)

2. Fluid Management: - Euvolemia: Balance fluid intake and output - Oliguria: Restrict fluids (avoid overload) - Polyuria (recovery phase): Replace losses

3. Avoid Further Nephrotoxins: - Adjust drug doses for GFR (antibiotics, etc.)

4. Monitor and Treat Complications: - Hyperkalemia, acidosis, uremia, volume overload

5. Nutrition: - Adequate calories (prevent catabolism) - Protein: 0.8-1 g/kg/day (if not on dialysis)

Recovery: - ATN usually recovers in 7-21 days (if cause removed) - Polyuric phase: Tubules can't concentrate urine yet → large urine output (replace losses)

docs.title: Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN)
docs.text:
1. Stop Offending Drug (MOST IMPORTANT): - Recovery may take weeks even after stopping

2. Corticosteroids: - Controversial (no RCTs) - Consider if: - No improvement 3-7 days after stopping drug - Severe AKI - Biopsy-proven AIN - Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day × 2-4 weeks, taper

3. Supportive Care

docs.title: Glomerulonephritis
docs.text: Immunosuppression (depends on etiology): - Anti-GBM: Plasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids - ANCA-associated: Cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids, rituximab - Lupus nephritis: Cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate + corticosteroids - Post-infectious: Supportive (usually self-limited)

docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis
docs.text:
1. Aggressive IV Fluids: - Goal: Urine output 200-300 mL/hr - Isotonic saline: 1-2 L/hr initially

2. Alkalization (controversial): - Sodium bicarbonate in IV fluids (if pH <6.5) - Goal: Urine pH >6.5 (prevent myoglobin precipitation)

3. Monitor: - CK, potassium (↑ from muscle breakdown), calcium (↓ then ↑), phosphate (↑)

4. Avoid: - Loop diuretics: Don't prevent AKI, may worsen

docs.title: Postrenal AKI
docs.text:
Relieve Obstruction (URGENT):

Bladder Outlet Obstruction: - Foley catheter (first-line) - Suprapubic catheter (if urethral stricture)

Bilateral Ureteral Obstruction: - Percutaneous nephrostomy OR - Ureteral stents (retrograde)

Post-Obstructive Diuresis: - After relief of prolonged obstruction (days to weeks): - Massive diuresis (impaired concentrating ability) - Replace losses (0.45% saline at 50-75% of urine output) - Monitor electrolytes (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia)

docs.title: Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT)
docs.text:
Indications (Mnemonic: "AEIOU"): - A: Acidosis (severe metabolic acidosis, pH <7.1 refractory to medical management) - E: Electrolyte abnormalities (hyperkalemia >6.5 mEq/L with ECG changes, refractory) - I: Intoxications (dialyzable toxins: methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates) - O: Overload (volume overload, pulmonary edema refractory to diuretics) - U: Uremia (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding)

Additional: - Oligo-anuria (if prolonged, unresponsive) - Tumor lysis syndrome (with hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, AKI)

Modalities:

Intermittent Hemodialysis (IHD): - 3-4 hours, 3× per week - Rapid correction of electrolytes, volume - Unstable patients: May cause hypotension

Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT): - 24 hours/day, slower, continuous - Hemodynamically unstable: Better tolerated (less hypotension) - ICU setting

Peritoneal Dialysis (PD): - Rarely used for AKI (less efficient)

docs.title: Complications of AKI
docs.text:
Electrolyte Abnormalities: - Hyperkalemia: Life-threatening (arrhythmias) - Treatment: Calcium gluconate (membrane stabilization), insulin + glucose, sodium bicarbonate, diuretics, dialysis - Hyperphosphatemia: Binds calcium → hypocalcemia - Hypocalcemia: Tetany, seizures - Metabolic acidosis: Anion gap (uremia)

Volume Overload: - Pulmonary edema, hypertension - Treatment: Diuretics (if responsive), dialysis

Uremia: - Pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding (platelet dysfunction) - Treatment: Dialysis

Anemia: - Decreased erythropoietin, blood loss

Infection: - Increased risk (uremia impairs immunity)

docs.title: Contrast-Induced Nephropathy (CIN)
docs.text:
Definition: - AKI within 48-72 hours after IV iodinated contrast - Cr ↑ ≥0.5 mg/dL or ≥25% from baseline

Risk Factors: - Pre-existing CKD (eGFR <60) - MOST IMPORTANT - Diabetes mellitus - Volume depletion - Heart failure - High contrast volume - NSAIDs, diuretics

Pathophysiology: - Renal vasoconstriction + Direct tubular toxicity

Prevention:

1. IV Fluids (MOST EFFECTIVE): - Isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) or sodium bicarbonate: - 1 mL/kg/hr × 12 hours before and 12 hours after procedure - If urgent: 3 mL/kg/hr × 1 hour before, 1 mL/kg/hr × 6 hours after

2. Use Low-Osmolar or Iso-Osmolar Contrast: - Minimize volume

3. Avoid Nephrotoxins: - Hold NSAIDs, metformin (lactic acidosis risk if AKI develops)

4. N-Acetylcysteine (NAC): - Controversial (mixed evidence) - 600-1200 mg PO BID × 2 days (day before, day of procedure) - Low cost, minimal harm → often given

5. Avoid if Possible: - Consider alternative imaging (non-contrast CT, MRI without gadolinium, ultrasound)

Management (if CIN develops): - Supportive care (usually self-limited, recovers in 7-10 days) - Avoid repeat contrast until recovery

docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis
docs.text:
Definition: - Skeletal muscle breakdown → release of intracellular contents (myoglobin, CK, K⁺, phosphate)

Causes: - Trauma/Crush injury (most common) - Prolonged immobilization (fall, coma, seizure) - Drugs: Statins, fibrates, cocaine, amphetamines - Strenuous exercise (especially heat stroke) - Infections: Influenza, viral myositis - Electrolyte abnormalities: Hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia - Toxins: Alcohol, carbon monoxide

Clinical Features: - Muscle pain, weakness, swelling - Dark urine ("tea-colored" or "cola-colored" - myoglobinuria) - AKI

Diagnosis: - CK >1000 U/L (often >5000-10,000) - 5× ULN diagnostic - Peak at 24-72 hours - Myoglobinuria: Urine dipstick positive for blood (detects heme), but NO RBCs on microscopy - Hyperkalemia (muscle breakdown) - Hyperphosphatemia - Hypocalcemia (early - calcium precipitates in damaged muscle) - Hypercalcemia (late - recovery phase, calcium released) - Elevated AST, ALT (muscle contains transaminases)

Complications: - AKI (30-50%): - Myoglobin precipitates in tubules (especially if acidic urine) - Direct tubular toxicity - Renal vasoconstriction - Hyperkalemia (life-threatening) - Compartment syndrome - DIC

Management:

1. Aggressive IV Fluids (MOST IMPORTANT): - Goal: Urine output 200-300 mL/hr - Isotonic saline: 1-2 L/hr initially, then titrate - Early, aggressive hydration prevents AKI

2. Urine Alkalinization: - Sodium bicarbonate in IV fluids - Goal: Urine pH >6.5 (prevents myoglobin precipitation) - Controversial (no strong RCT evidence)

3. Treat Hyperkalemia: - Calcium gluconate (if ECG changes) - Insulin + glucose - Dialysis (if severe, refractory)

4. Monitor: - CK (daily until declining) - Electrolytes (K⁺, Ca²⁺, phosphate) - Creatinine - Urine output

5. Avoid: - Loop diuretics: Don't prevent AKI, may worsen (increase urine concentration)

6. Treat Underlying Cause: - Fasciotomy (if compartment syndrome)

Prognosis: - AKI usually recovers if treated early with fluids

docs.title: Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS)
docs.text:
Definition: - Massive cell lysis (usually after chemotherapy) → release of intracellular contents

High-Risk Malignancies: - Burkitt lymphoma (highest risk) - Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) - High-grade lymphomas - High tumor burden

Timing: - 12-72 hours after chemotherapy initiation

Laboratory TLS (≥2 of following within 3 days before or 7 days after chemotherapy): - Hyperuricemia (uric acid >8 mg/dL or ↑ 25%) - Hyperkalemia (K⁺ >6 mEq/L or ↑ 25%) - Hyperphosphatemia (phosphate >4.5 mg/dL or ↑ 25%) - Hypocalcemia (calcium <7 mg/dL or ↓ 25%)

Clinical TLS: - Laboratory TLS + ≥1 of: - Acute kidney injury - Cardiac arrhythmia/sudden death - Seizure

Pathophysiology: 

Massive cell lysis
      ↓
Release of K⁺, phosphate, nucleic acids (purines)
      ↓
Purines → Uric acid (via xanthine oxidase)
      ↓
Uric acid precipitation in renal tubules
      ↓
Obstructive nephropathy → AKI

Prevention (High-risk patients):

1. Aggressive IV Hydration: - 2-3 L/m² per day (maintain urine output >100 mL/m²/hr)

2. Allopurinol (Xanthine Oxidase Inhibitor): - Prevents NEW uric acid formation (doesn't lower existing uric acid) - 300-600 mg daily, start 24-48 hours before chemotherapy - Continue × 3-7 days after chemotherapy

OR

3. Rasburicase (Recombinant Urate Oxidase): - Converts uric acid → allantoin (more soluble, easily excreted) - Rapidly lowers uric acid (within hours) - Indications: High-risk TLS, established TLS, allopurinol contraindicated - Dosing: 0.2 mg/kg IV single dose (may repeat) - Contraindication: G6PD deficiency (causes hemolysis)

4. Avoid Urine Alkalinization: - Old practice (no longer recommended) - Risks: Calcium phosphate precipitation (worsens kidney injury)

Treatment (if TLS develops): - Aggressive fluids - Rasburicase (if uric acid >8 mg/dL) - Treat hyperkalemia (calcium, insulin/glucose, dialysis) - Dialysis (if refractory hyperkalemia, severe AKI)

docs.title: Cardiorenal Syndrome
docs.text:
Definition: - Interplay between heart and kidney dysfunction

Types:

Type 1 (Acute Cardiorenal): - Acute heart failure → AKI - Mechanism: ↓ Cardiac output → ↓ renal perfusion, venous congestion

Type 2 (Chronic Cardiorenal): - Chronic heart failure → CKD

Type 3 (Acute Renocardiac): - AKI → Acute cardiac dysfunction (volume overload, uremia)

Type 4 (Chronic Renocardiac): - CKD → Cardiac disease (LVH, heart failure)

Type 5 (Secondary): - Systemic disease (sepsis, cirrhosis) → heart AND kidney dysfunction

docs.title: AKI Definition (KDIGO Criteria)
docs.text: ANY of: - Cr ↑ ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 hours OR - Cr ↑ ≥1.5× baseline in 7 days OR - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr × 6 hours

docs.title: Prerenal vs ATN Differentiation
docs.text:
Parameter Prerenal ATN BUN:Cr >20:1 <20:1 FENa <1% >2% Urine Na⁺ <20 mEq/L >40 mEq/L Urine Osmolality >500 <350 Urine Sediment Hyaline casts Muddy brown casts Response to Fluids Improves No improvement

FENa <1% in ATN: Diuretics, contrast nephropathy, rhabdomyolysis (use FEUrea instead)

docs.title: Urinalysis Patterns
docs.text:
• Muddy brown/granular casts: ATN
  • RBC casts: Glomerulonephritis
  • WBC casts: Pyelonephritis, AIN
  • Eosinophils: AIN (low sensitivity)

docs.title: AIN Classic Triad (10-30% have all):
docs.text:
• Fever + Rash + Eosinophilia

Common Drugs: - Antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones) - PPIs (increasing cause) - NSAIDs, allopurinol

docs.title: RRT Indications: "AEIOU"
docs.text:
• **A**cidosis (pH <7.1, refractory)
  • **E**lectrolytes (K⁺ >6.5 with ECG changes)
  • **I**ntoxications (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium)
  • **O**verload (pulmonary edema, refractory)
  • **U**remia (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding)

docs.title: Contrast-Induced Nephropathy
docs.text: Prevention: - IV fluids (isotonic saline 1 mL/kg/hr × 12 hrs before and after) - MOST EFFECTIVE - Minimize contrast volume - NAC (controversial, low harm)

docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis
docs.text:
Diagnosis: - CK >5000 U/L (often >10,000) - Myoglobinuria: Urine dipstick positive for blood, NO RBCs on microscopy

Management: - Aggressive IV fluids (goal UOP 200-300 mL/hr) - MOST IMPORTANT - Alkalinize urine (controversial) - Treat hyperkalemia - Avoid loop diuretics

docs.title: Tumor Lysis Syndrome
docs.text: Prevention (high-risk): - IV hydration + Allopurinol (start 24-48 hrs before chemo) - Rasburicase (if high-risk or established TLS) - Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
A 70-year-old man with hypertension presents with fatigue. Labs: Cr 2.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.0), BUN 56 mg/dL. Urinalysis: Specific gravity 1.025, Na⁺ 15 mEq/L, no casts. FENa 0.8%. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

A) Acute tubular necrosis B) Prerenal azotemia C) Acute interstitial nephritis D) Postrenal obstruction

Answer: B) Prerenal azotemia

Explanation: - Prerenal AKI: - BUN:Cr ratio: 56/2.8 = 20:1 (>20:1 suggests prerenal) ✓ - FENa <1% (0.8%) ✓ - Urine Na⁺ <20 mEq/L (15) ✓ - High specific gravity (>1.020) ✓ - No casts (hyaline casts may be seen in prerenal)

  • Kidneys intact → avid Na⁺ reabsorption → concentrated urine

  • ATN:

  • FENa >2%
  • Urine Na⁺ >40 mEq/L
  • Muddy brown casts
  • BUN:Cr <20:1

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
A 55-year-old woman develops AKI 3 days after starting penicillin for pneumonia. She has fever (38.5°C), maculopapular rash, and eosinophilia (12%). Urinalysis shows WBC casts and eosinophils. What is the diagnosis and treatment?

A) ATN; supportive care B) AIN; stop penicillin C) AIN; stop penicillin + corticosteroids D) Pyelonephritis; continue penicillin + add gentamicin

Answer: C) AIN; stop penicillin + corticosteroids

Explanation: - Acute Interstitial Nephritis (AIN): - Drug-induced (penicillin) ✓ - Classic triad (30% have all): - Fever ✓ - Rash ✓ - Eosinophilia ✓ - Urinalysis: WBC casts, eosinophils ✓

  • Management:
  • Stop offending drug (penicillin) - MOST IMPORTANT

  • Corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day):

    • Consider if no improvement 3-7 days after stopping drug, OR severe AKI
    • This patient has classic findings → start steroids

  • Recovery: May take weeks even after stopping drug

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
A 28-year-old man is brought to the ER after being found down for several hours. Labs: CK 45,000 U/L, K⁺ 6.8 mEq/L, Cr 3.5 mg/dL, phosphate 8 mg/dL. Urine dipstick is positive for blood, but microscopy shows no RBCs. What is the MOST appropriate initial management?

A) Aggressive IV fluids B) Loop diuretics C) Immediate hemodialysis D) Sodium bicarbonate only

Answer: A) Aggressive IV fluids

Explanation: - Rhabdomyolysis: - CK >5000 U/L (45,000) ✓ - Hyperkalemia (muscle breakdown) ✓ - Hyperphosphatemia ✓ - Urine positive for blood (heme), NO RBCs = Myoglobinuria ✓ - AKI ✓

  • Management:

1. Aggressive IV Fluids (MOST IMPORTANT): - Isotonic saline 1-2 L/hr initially - Goal UOP 200-300 mL/hr - Prevents AKI

2. Treat Hyperkalemia (K⁺ 6.8): - Calcium gluconate (membrane stabilization - especially if ECG changes) - Insulin + glucose - May need dialysis if refractory

3. Urine Alkalinization (controversial): - Sodium bicarbonate (goal pH >6.5)

4. Monitor: CK, K⁺, Ca²⁺, phosphate, Cr

Avoid: - Loop diuretics: Don't prevent AKI, may worsen (↑ urine concentration)

  • Dialysis: May be needed if severe, refractory hyperkalemia or AKI, but fluids are first-line

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
A 65-year-old man with CKD (baseline Cr 2.0 mg/dL) is scheduled for contrast CT. What is the MOST effective measure to prevent contrast-induced nephropathy?

A) N-Acetylcysteine 600 mg PO BID × 2 days B) IV isotonic saline 1 mL/kg/hr × 12 hours before and after C) Hold ACE inhibitor 24 hours before D) Sodium bicarbonate 150 mEq/L at 3 mL/kg/hr × 1 hour before

Answer: B) IV isotonic saline 1 mL/kg/hr × 12 hours before and after

Explanation: - Contrast-Induced Nephropathy (CIN) Prevention:

MOST EFFECTIVE: IV Fluids - Isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) OR Sodium bicarbonate: - 1 mL/kg/hr × 12 hours before and 12 hours after procedure - If urgent: 3 mL/kg/hr × 1 hour before, 1 mL/kg/hr × 6 hours after

Other measures: - Minimize contrast volume, use low-osmolar/iso-osmolar contrast - N-Acetylcysteine (NAC): Controversial (mixed evidence) - 600-1200 mg PO BID × 2 days - Low cost, minimal harm → often given - Hold nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, metformin

  • ACE inhibitor: Holding is reasonable but less important than fluids

  • Risk factors: CKD (baseline Cr 2.0) ✓, diabetes, volume depletion

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
A 60-year-old man with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma is started on chemotherapy. On day 2, labs show: K⁺ 6.5 mEq/L, uric acid 12 mg/dL, phosphate 7 mg/dL, calcium 6.8 mg/dL, Cr 3.2 mg/dL. What is the diagnosis and MOST appropriate immediate management?

A) Tumor lysis syndrome; aggressive IV fluids + allopurinol B) Tumor lysis syndrome; aggressive IV fluids + rasburicase + treat hyperkalemia C) Rhabdomyolysis; aggressive IV fluids + bicarbonate D) AKI from chemotherapy; hold chemotherapy

Answer: B) Tumor lysis syndrome; aggressive IV fluids + rasburicase + treat hyperkalemia

Explanation: - Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS): - High-risk malignancy: Burkitt lymphoma (highest risk) ✓ - Timing: Day 2 after chemotherapy ✓ - Laboratory TLS (≥2 of): - Hyperuricemia (12 mg/dL) ✓ - Hyperkalemia (6.5 mEq/L) ✓ - Hyperphosphatemia (7 mg/dL) ✓ - Hypocalcemia (6.8 mg/dL) ✓ - Clinical TLS: Laboratory TLS + AKI ✓

  • Management:

1. Aggressive IV Fluids: - 2-3 L/m²/day

2. Rasburicase (Recombinant Urate Oxidase): - Rapidly lowers uric acid (within hours) - Indicated for established TLS (uric acid 12 mg/dL) - 0.2 mg/kg IV single dose - Contraindicated: G6PD deficiency (hemolysis)

3. Treat Hyperkalemia (K⁺ 6.5): - Calcium gluconate (if ECG changes) - Insulin + glucose - May need dialysis

4. Dialysis (if refractory hyperkalemia, severe AKI)

Allopurinol: - Prevents NEW uric acid formation - Does NOT lower existing uric acid - Used for prevention (start before chemo), NOT acute treatment

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
A 72-year-old man presents with anuria for 24 hours. Bladder scan shows 800 mL. Creatinine is 4.5 mg/dL (baseline 1.2). What is the MOST appropriate next step?

A) IV furosemide B) Foley catheter insertion C) Emergent hemodialysis D) Renal ultrasound

Answer: B) Foley catheter insertion

Explanation: - Postrenal AKI (Bladder Outlet Obstruction): - Anuria (no urine output) ✓ - Bladder scan 800 mL (distended bladder) ✓ - AKI (Cr 4.5, baseline 1.2) ✓

  • Most likely: BPH (72-year-old man)

  • Management:

1. Relieve Obstruction (URGENT): - Foley catheter (first-line for bladder outlet obstruction) - Suprapubic catheter (if urethral stricture, can't pass Foley)

2. Monitor for Post-Obstructive Diuresis: - After relief of prolonged obstruction → massive diuresis (impaired concentration) - Replace losses (0.45% saline at 50-75% of UOP) - Monitor electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺)

3. Renal function should improve after obstruction relieved

  • Renal ultrasound: Would confirm hydronephrosis, but don't delay catheter (clinical diagnosis clear)

  • Dialysis: May be needed if severe metabolic derangements, but relieve obstruction first

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
A 50-year-old woman with SLE presents with worsening kidney function. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein, RBCs 50-100/hpf, RBC casts. Creatinine is 3.2 mg/dL. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

A) Acute tubular necrosis B) Lupus nephritis (glomerulonephritis) C) Acute interstitial nephritis D) Prerenal azotemia

Answer: B) Lupus nephritis (glomerulonephritis)

Explanation: - Glomerulonephritis (specifically Lupus Nephritis): - SLE (known diagnosis) ✓ - Urinalysis: - Proteinuria (3+) ✓ - Hematuria (50-100 RBCs/hpf) ✓ - RBC casts (PATHOGNOMONIC for glomerulonephritis) ✓ - AKI ✓

RBC casts = Glomerulonephritis

  • Management:
  • Kidney biopsy (confirm lupus nephritis class, guide treatment)

  • Immunosuppression: Cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate + corticosteroids

  • ATN: Muddy brown casts

  • AIN: WBC casts, eosinophils

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
A 45-year-old man develops AKI 48 hours after cardiac catheterization. Creatinine rises from 1.2 to 2.8 mg/dL. He has livedo reticularis on lower extremities and eosinophilia. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

A) Contrast-induced nephropathy B) Atheroembolic disease (cholesterol emboli) C) Acute interstitial nephritis D) Acute tubular necrosis

Answer: B) Atheroembolic disease (cholesterol emboli)

Explanation: - Atheroembolic Disease (Cholesterol Emboli): - Post-catheterization (manipulation of atherosclerotic aorta) ✓ - Timing: Days to weeks after procedure ✓ - Livedo reticularis (blue/purple net-like pattern on skin) ✓ - Eosinophilia ✓ - AKI ✓

Other findings: - Blue toe syndrome (digital ischemia) - Hypocomplementemia (low C3, C4) - Elevated ESR

Diagnosis: - Skin or kidney biopsy: Cholesterol clefts (needle-shaped spaces in arterioles)

Management: - Supportive (no specific treatment) - Avoid further vascular procedures - Statin (stabilize plaques)

Prognosis: Often progressive (may lead to CKD)

  • Contrast-induced nephropathy:
  • AKI within 48-72 hours (similar timing)
  • NO livedo reticularis, NO eosinophilia
  • Usually recovers in 7-10 days

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
A 68-year-old woman with history of heart failure is admitted with acute decompensated heart failure. Despite aggressive diuresis, creatinine rises from 1.5 to 3.2 mg/dL. She is euvolemic. What type of cardiorenal syndrome is this?

A) Type 1 (Acute cardiorenal) B) Type 2 (Chronic cardiorenal) C) Type 3 (Acute renocardiac) D) Type 4 (Chronic renocardiac)

Answer: A) Type 1 (Acute cardiorenal)

Explanation: - Cardiorenal Syndrome Classification:

Type 1 (Acute Cardiorenal): - Acute heart failure → AKI ✓ - This patient: Acute decompensated HF → Cr rise (AKI)

Type 2 (Chronic Cardiorenal): - Chronic heart failure → CKD

Type 3 (Acute Renocardiac): - AKI → Acute cardiac dysfunction

Type 4 (Chronic Renocardiac): - CKD → Cardiac disease (LVH, HF)

Type 5 (Secondary): - Systemic disease (sepsis) → both heart and kidney

  • Mechanism (Type 1):
  • ↓ Cardiac output → ↓ renal perfusion
  • Venous congestion → ↑ renal venous pressure
  • Diuretics: May worsen prerenal state if over-diuresed

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
A patient with AKI develops peaked T waves on ECG. Potassium is 7.2 mEq/L. What is the MOST appropriate FIRST step in management?

A) Insulin + glucose B) Calcium gluconate C) Sodium bicarbonate D) Emergent hemodialysis

Answer: B) Calcium gluconate

Explanation: - Severe Hyperkalemia (K⁺ 7.2 mEq/L) with ECG changes (peaked T waves):

Treatment Steps:

1. Calcium Gluconate (FIRST - if ECG changes): - 10-20 mL of 10% calcium gluconate IV over 2-3 minutes - Mechanism: Membrane stabilization (protects myocardium) - Does NOT lower K⁺ - Onset: Immediate (within minutes) - Duration: 30-60 minutes

2. Shift K⁺ Intracellularly: - Insulin + Glucose: - 10 units regular insulin + 25 g dextrose (D50W 50 mL) IV - Onset: 15-30 minutes - Duration: 4-6 hours

  • Sodium Bicarbonate (if metabolic acidosis):
  • 50-100 mEq IV

  • Onset: 30-60 minutes

  • Albuterol (nebulized):

  • 10-20 mg
  • Onset: 30 minutes

3. Remove K⁺ from Body: - Diuretics (furosemide) - if functioning kidneys - Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) - GI exchange resin (slow, limited efficacy) - Patiromer (oral K⁺ binder) - Hemodialysis (definitive, if severe/refractory)

Sequence: Calcium first (if ECG changes) → Shift K⁺ → Remove K⁺

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Acute Kidney Injury (AKI): - Definition: Cr ↑ ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 hrs OR ≥1.5× baseline in 7 days OR UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr × 6 hrs

Classification: - Prerenal (60-70%): BUN:Cr >20:1, FENa <1%, responds to fluids - Intrinsic (25-40%): ATN (muddy brown casts, FENa >2%), AIN (fever/rash/eosinophilia), GN (RBC casts) - Postrenal (5-10%): Obstruction (hydronephrosis on ultrasound)

Management: - Prerenal: IV fluids - ATN: Supportive (remove nephrotoxins, monitor, treat complications) - AIN: Stop drug, consider steroids - Postrenal: Relieve obstruction (Foley catheter, nephrostomy) - RRT: AEIOU (acidosis, electrolytes, intoxications, overload, uremia)

Specific Conditions: - Contrast-induced nephropathy: IV fluids (most effective prevention) - Rhabdomyolysis: Aggressive fluids (goal UOP 200-300 mL/hr), avoid diuretics - Tumor lysis syndrome: Fluids + rasburicase (if established TLS)

End of Topic 28: Acute Kidney Injury

Next Topic: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) (Medicine/29_Chronic_Kidney_Disease.md)

Week 2 Progress: 8/18 topics complete

docs.title: HYPERTENSIVE DISORDERS OF PREGNANCY - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text: Hypertension in pregnancy: BP ≥140/90 mmHg on 2 occasions, 4 hours apart

docs.title: 1. CHRONIC HYPERTENSION
docs.text:
Definition: HTN before 20 weeks OR pre-existing

BP: ≥140/90 mmHg

Management: - Continue antihypertensive (avoid ACE-I, ARBs) - Monitor for superimposed pre-eclampsia - Target BP: <160/110 mmHg

NEET PG: ACE inhibitors & ARBs contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic)

docs.title: 2. GESTATIONAL HYPERTENSION
docs.text:
Definition: HTN after 20 weeks without proteinuria/end-organ damage

Features: - BP ≥140/90 mmHg - NO proteinuria - NO symptoms (headache, visual disturbances, epigastric pain)

Progression: 25-50% → Pre-eclampsia

Management: - Monitor BP, urine protein, symptoms - Deliver at 37 weeks if stable

NEET PG: Gestational HTN = HTN after 20 weeks without proteinuria

docs.title: 3. PRE-ECLAMPSIA
docs.text:
Definition: HTN after 20 weeks + Proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction

Criteria: HTN + ANY of: - Proteinuria: ≥300 mg/24hr OR Protein:Cr ratio ≥0.3 - Thrombocytopenia: Platelets <100,000 - Renal insufficiency: Creatinine >1.1 mg/dL - Liver dysfunction: Transaminases 2× normal, RUQ pain - Pulmonary edema - Cerebral symptoms: Headache, visual disturbances

NEET PG: Proteinuria NOT required for diagnosis (end-organ damage sufficient)

docs.title: PRE-ECLAMPSIA WITHOUT SEVERE FEATURES
docs.text:
BP: 140-159/90-109 mmHg No severe features

Management: - <34 weeks: Expectant (if stable) - 34-37 weeks: Individualized - ≥37 weeks: Delivery

docs.title: PRE-ECLAMPSIA WITH SEVERE FEATURES
docs.text:
BP: ≥160/110 mmHg OR severe symptoms

Severe Features (any ONE): - BP: ≥160/110 mmHg - Symptoms: - Severe headache - Visual disturbances (blurred vision, scotoma) - Epigastric/RUQ pain (liver capsule distension) - Thrombocytopenia: <100,000 - Elevated liver enzymes: >2× normal - Renal insufficiency: Cr >1.1 mg/dL - Pulmonary edema - Fetal growth restriction

NEET PG High-Yield: - Headache + visual disturbances + epigastric pain = Impending eclampsia - RUQ pain = Liver involvement (HELLP syndrome)

Management: - MgSO4 (seizure prophylaxis) - Antihypertensive if BP ≥160/110 - Delivery: - ≥34 weeks: Immediate delivery - <34 weeks: Corticosteroids + delivery within 48 hours

docs.title: 4. ECLAMPSIA
docs.text:
Definition: Seizures in pre-eclampsia (NOT attributable to other causes)

Timing: - Antepartum: 50% - Intrapartum: 20% - Postpartum: 30% (up to 6 weeks)

Management: 1. Airway protection: Left lateral position 2. MgSO4: 4g IV bolus → 1g/hr infusion 3. BP control: Labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine 4. Delivery: After stabilization (definitive treatment)

NEET PG: - MgSO4 = Drug of choice for eclampsia - Antidote: Calcium gluconate (if MgSO4 toxicity)

docs.title: 5. CHRONIC HTN WITH SUPERIMPOSED PRE-ECLAMPSIA
docs.text:
Definition: Pre-existing HTN + new-onset proteinuria/end-organ dysfunction after 20 weeks

Diagnosis: - Sudden ↑ in BP (≥160/110) - New proteinuria - Thrombocytopenia - Elevated liver enzymes

Management: Same as severe pre-eclampsia

docs.title: HELLP SYNDROME
docs.text:
Definition: Severe form of pre-eclampsia

HELLP: - **H**emolysis (schistocytes, ↑ LDH, ↑ indirect bilirubin) - **E**levated **L**iver enzymes (AST, ALT >2× normal) - **L**ow **P**latelets (<100,000)

Clinical: - RUQ/epigastric pain (most common symptom) - Nausea, vomiting - Malaise - May occur without severe hypertension (15%)

Complications: - Hepatic rupture (subcapsular hematoma) - DIC - Acute renal failure - Placental abruption

NEET PG High-Yield: - RUQ pain in pregnancy = Think HELLP - Can occur without severe HTN - Most common symptom: RUQ pain - Delivery = Only cure

Management: - MgSO4 (seizure prophylaxis) - BP control - Delivery: - ≥34 weeks: Immediate - <34 weeks: Corticosteroids + delivery within 48 hours - Platelet transfusion: If <20,000 or bleeding

docs.title: Two-Stage Model
docs.text:
Stage 1 (8-18 weeks): Abnormal placentation - Inadequate trophoblast invasion - Failure of spiral artery remodeling - Placental ischemia

Stage 2 (>20 weeks): Maternal syndrome - ↑ sFlt-1 (soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase-1): Anti-angiogenic - ↓ PlGF (Placental growth factor): Pro-angiogenic - Endothelial dysfunction - Systemic manifestations

NEET PG: ↑ sFlt-1/PlGF ratio = Pre-eclampsia

docs.title: High Risk (any ONE → Aspirin prophylaxis)
docs.text:
• Previous pre-eclampsia
  • Chronic hypertension
  • Diabetes mellitus (Type 1 or 2)
  • Renal disease
  • Autoimmune disease (SLE, APS)

docs.title: Moderate Risk (≥2 → Aspirin prophylaxis)
docs.text:
• First pregnancy
  • Age ≥35 years
  • BMI >30
  • Family history of pre-eclampsia
  • Multiple pregnancy
  • Interval >10 years between pregnancies

NEET PG: Low-dose aspirin (75-150 mg) started <16 weeks reduces pre-eclampsia risk

docs.title: Aspirin Prophylaxis
docs.text: Indication: High-risk OR ≥2 moderate risk factors Dose: 75-150 mg daily Timing: Start <16 weeks, continue until delivery Benefit: ↓ Pre-eclampsia by 10-15%

docs.title: Calcium Supplementation
docs.text:
For: Low dietary calcium intake (<600 mg/day) Dose: 1.5-2g elemental calcium daily

NEET PG: Aspirin prophylaxis for high-risk women

docs.title: Target BP
docs.text: Chronic HTN: <160/110 mmHg Pre-eclampsia: <160/110 mmHg (treat if ≥160/110)

docs.title: Antihypertensive Drugs
docs.text:
Drug Dose Route Side Effects Notes Labetalol 20-80mg q10min IV Bronchospasm DOC for acute HTN 100-400mg TDS Oral Hydralazine 5-10mg q20min IV Headache, tachycardia Alternative Nifedipine 10-20mg q30min Oral Flushing Immediate-release Methyldopa 250-500mg TDS Oral Sedation 1st line for chronic HTN

NEET PG High-Yield: - Labetalol: DOC for acute severe HTN - Methyldopa: 1st line for chronic HTN (safe in pregnancy) - Avoid: ACE-I, ARBs (teratogenic), atenolol (IUGR)

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
• Eclampsia (treatment)
  • Severe pre-eclampsia (prophylaxis)

docs.title: Regimen
docs.text:
Loading dose: 4g IV over 15-20 min Maintenance: 1g/hr IV infusion

Duration: 24 hours after delivery OR last seizure

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
• Urine output: ≥30 ml/hr
  • Respiratory rate: ≥12/min
  • Deep tendon reflexes: Present
  • Serum Mg2+: 4-8 mEq/L (therapeutic range)

docs.title: Toxicity Signs (in order)
docs.text:
1. Loss of deep tendon reflexes (7-10 mEq/L)
  2. Respiratory depression (10-13 mEq/L)
  3. Cardiac arrest (>15 mEq/L)

Antidote: Calcium gluconate (10 ml of 10% IV over 3 min)

NEET PG High-Yield: - MgSO4: DOC for eclampsia - First sign of toxicity: Loss of deep tendon reflexes - Antidote: Calcium gluconate - Contraindication: Myasthenia gravis

docs.title: Timing of Delivery
docs.text:
Condition Gestational Age Timing Gestational HTN ≥37 weeks Deliver Pre-eclampsia without severe features <37 weeks Expectant (if stable) ≥37 weeks Deliver Pre-eclampsia with severe features <34 weeks Corticosteroids + deliver within 48hr ≥34 weeks Immediate delivery Eclampsia Any Stabilize → Deliver HELLP syndrome <34 weeks Corticosteroids + deliver within 48hr ≥34 weeks Immediate delivery

NEET PG: - Delivery = Only definitive treatment - Severe pre-eclampsia at ≥34 weeks: Immediate delivery - <34 weeks: Corticosteroids (lung maturity) + delivery within 48 hours

docs.title: Corticosteroids for Fetal Lung Maturity
docs.text:
Indication: Delivery anticipated <34 weeks

Regimen: - Betamethasone: 12mg IM × 2 doses, 24 hours apart - Dexamethasone: 6mg IM q12h × 4 doses

Benefit: ↓ Respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular hemorrhage, NEC

NEET PG: Give corticosteroids if delivery expected <34 weeks

docs.title: Maternal Complications
docs.text:
1. Eclampsia: Seizures
  2. Cerebrovascular accident: ICH, thrombosis
  3. HELLP syndrome
  4. Pulmonary edema
  5. Acute kidney injury
  6. Hepatic rupture (subcapsular hematoma)
  7. Placental abruption
  8. DIC

NEET PG: Leading cause of maternal mortality in pre-eclampsia = ICH

docs.title: Fetal Complications
docs.text:
1. Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR)
  2. Oligohydramnios
  3. Placental abruption
  4. Preterm delivery
  5. Intrauterine fetal death

docs.title: Postpartum Management
docs.text:
Monitoring: - BP: Every 4 hours for 48 hours, then daily - Urine output - Symptoms: Headache, visual changes, RUQ pain

MgSO4: Continue for 24 hours postpartum

Antihypertensives: - Continue if needed - Safe in breastfeeding: Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa, enalapril

Follow-up: - BP check at 7-10 days postpartum - If persistent HTN: Evaluate for secondary causes

Contraception: Avoid estrogen-containing pills (thrombosis risk)

NEET PG: - Eclampsia can occur up to 6 weeks postpartum - Safe antihypertensives in breastfeeding: Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa

docs.title: Future Pregnancy
docs.text:
Counseling: - Recurrence risk: 10-15% (higher if severe or early-onset) - Aspirin prophylaxis: Start <16 weeks in next pregnancy - Cardiovascular risk: ↑ Lifetime risk of HTN, CVD, stroke

NEET PG: Pre-eclampsia = Risk factor for future CVD

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for eclampsia? Answer: Magnesium sulfate

  2. Antidote for MgSO4 toxicity? Answer: Calcium gluconate

  3. First sign of MgSO4 toxicity? Answer: Loss of deep tendon reflexes

  4. Delivery timing for severe pre-eclampsia at ≥34 weeks? Answer: Immediate delivery

  5. Most common symptom of HELLP syndrome? Answer: RUQ pain

  6. Prophylaxis for high-risk pre-eclampsia? Answer: Low-dose aspirin (75-150 mg) <16 weeks

  7. Drugs contraindicated in pregnancy? Answer: ACE inhibitors, ARBs

  8. Leading cause of maternal death in pre-eclampsia? Answer: Intracranial hemorrhage

docs.title: Severe Features of Pre-eclampsia
docs.text: "HEPATIC VISION" - **H**eadache (persistent, severe) - **E**pigastric pain - **P**latelets <100,000 - **A**ST/ALT elevated - **T**hrombocytopenia - **I**ncreased creatinine >1.1 - **C**erebral symptoms - **V**isual disturbances - **I**ncreased BP ≥160/110 - **S**hortness of breath (pulmonary edema) - **I**UGR (fetal) - **(O)**liguria - **(N)**ephrotic range proteinuria

docs.title: HELLP Syndrome
docs.text: "**H**elpless **E**ating **L**iver **L**osing **P**latelets" - **H**emolysis - **E**levated - **L**iver enzymes - **L**ow - **P**latelets

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know classification of hypertensive disorders
  • Understand pre-eclampsia diagnostic criteria
  • Learn severe features of pre-eclampsia
  • Memorize MgSO4 dosing and monitoring
  • Know HELLP syndrome criteria
  • Understand delivery timing for each condition
  • Learn safe antihypertensives in pregnancy
  • Know aspirin prophylaxis indications
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on hypertensive disorders

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 4-6 questions)

docs.title: ANTEPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (APH) - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text:
APH: Bleeding from or into the genital tract after 20 weeks (or 28 weeks - varies) before delivery

Incidence: 2-5% of pregnancies

docs.title: Major Causes (Placental)
docs.text:
1. Placenta previa (30%)
  2. Placental abruption (30%)

docs.title: Minor Causes (20%)
docs.text:
• Vasa previa
  • Marginal sinus rupture
  • Cervical causes: Polyp, ectropion, carcinoma
  • Vaginal causes: Trauma, infection
  • "Show" (bloody mucus plug - normal labor onset)

docs.title: Unexplained (20%)
docs.text: NEET PG: Placenta previa & Abruption = 60% of APH

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Placenta implanted in lower uterine segment, covering or near internal os

docs.title: Classification (by location)
docs.text:
Old classification: 1. Type I (Low-lying): Placenta in lower segment, NOT reaching internal os 2. Type II (Marginal): Placenta reaches but doesn't cover internal os 3. Type III (Partial): Partially covers internal os 4. Type IV (Complete/Central): Completely covers internal os

New classification (RCOG): - Minor (Low-lying): <2 cm from internal os - Major: Covering or ≥2 cm from os

NEET PG: Type IV (complete) = Most bleeding, cesarean mandatory

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
• Previous cesarean section (Most important) - scar tissue ↑ risk
  • Multiparity (↑ with parity)
  • Previous placenta previa (4-8% recurrence)
  • Multiple pregnancy
  • Advanced maternal age (>35 years)
  • Smoking
  • Previous uterine surgery (myomectomy, D&C)
  • Endometrial damage

NEET PG: Previous CS = Strongest risk factor

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Classic triad: 1. Painless bleeding 2. Bright red blood 3. Recurrent episodes

Characteristics: - Onset: Usually after 28 weeks (more common 3rd trimester) - First bleed: Usually mild ("warning hemorrhage") - Provoked by: Coitus, vaginal examination, labor - Shock: Proportionate to visible blood loss

Examination: - Soft, non-tender uterus - Malpresentation common (oblique, transverse, breech - 30%) - High presenting part (placenta prevents engagement) - Fetal heart: Usually normal (unless massive bleed)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Painless bright red bleeding (vs abruption - painful, dark) - NO vaginal examination until placenta previa excluded (can provoke massive bleeding)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
USG (Transabdominal + Transvaginal): - Diagnosis: Placental localization - Transvaginal USG: Gold standard (safe, more accurate) - Timing: 18-20 weeks (anomaly scan), repeat at 32 weeks if low-lying

Important: 90% of low-lying placentas at 20 weeks migrate up (placental migration)

MRI: If posterior placenta, concern for accreta

NEET PG: - TVS safe in placenta previa (NOT per vaginal examination) - Repeat USG at 32 weeks if low-lying at 20 weeks

docs.title: Conservative (if bleeding stopped, stable)
docs.text:
Before 37 weeks: - Admit & observe - Bed rest (until bleeding stops) - IV access, Blood grouping & cross-match - Tocolytics (if uterine contractions) - Corticosteroids (if <34 weeks for fetal lung maturity) - Iron supplementation (correct anemia) - Monitor fetus: NST, Doppler - Aim: Prolong pregnancy to 37 weeks

Criteria for discharge: - No bleeding for 48-72 hours - Hemoglobin stable - Fetal well-being confirmed

docs.title: Delivery
docs.text:
Indications: - Maternal: Massive/uncontrolled bleeding, hemodynamic instability - Fetal: Distress, maturity (≥37 weeks)

Mode: - Cesarean section: If major placenta previa (covering os) - Vaginal delivery: Possible if minor (low-lying, NOT covering os) & vertex

Timing: Elective CS at 37-38 weeks if asymptomatic

NEET PG: - Major placenta previa: CS mandatory - Elective CS at 37-38 weeks

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
Maternal: - Hemorrhage (antepartum, intrapartum, postpartum) - Placenta accreta/increta/percreta (↑ risk if previous CS) - PPH (atonic - lower segment doesn't contract well) - Hysterectomy (if uncontrolled bleeding) - Shock, DIC

Fetal: - Prematurity - IUGR - Malpresentation - Vasa previa (if velamentous cord insertion)

NEET PG: Placenta accreta common with placenta previa + previous CS

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Premature separation of normally situated placenta from uterine wall before delivery

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
By severity: 1. Grade 0 (Asymptomatic): Diagnosed after delivery (retroplacental clot) 2. Grade 1 (Mild): Vaginal bleeding, no fetal distress 3. Grade 2 (Moderate): Concealed/revealed bleeding, fetal distress, uterine tenderness 4. Grade 3 (Severe): Severe bleeding, fetal death, maternal shock, DIC

By type of bleeding: - Revealed (80%): Blood escapes through vagina - Concealed (20%): Blood trapped behind placenta (more dangerous)

NEET PG: Concealed hemorrhage more dangerous (no visible bleeding, hypovolemic shock)

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
• Hypertension (Chronic HTN, pre-eclampsia) - Most important
  • Previous abruption (10-15% recurrence)
  • Trauma (RTA, domestic violence, external cephalic version)
  • Smoking, cocaine use
  • Multiparity
  • PROM (Premature rupture of membranes)
  • Thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden, protein C/S deficiency)
  • Advanced maternal age
  • Polyhydramnios (sudden decompression)

NEET PG: Hypertension (pre-eclampsia) = Most important risk factor

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Classic presentation: 1. Painful bleeding (constant pain) 2. Dark red/clotted blood 3. Tender, tense, "woody hard" uterus (tetanic contractions) 4. Fetal distress or death

Additional: - Abdominal pain (constant, severe) - Back pain (if posterior placenta) - Uterine contractions (frequent, hypertonic) - Shock: Out of proportion to visible blood loss (if concealed) - Couvelaire uterus: Blood infiltration into myometrium (bluish discoloration)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Painful, dark blood, tender rigid uterus (vs previa - painless, bright red, soft uterus) - Shock > visible bleeding (concealed)

docs.title: Investigations
docs.text:
Laboratory: - CBC: Anemia, thrombocytopenia (if DIC) - Coagulation profile: PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer - DIC: ↓ Platelets, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ PT/aPTT, ↑ D-dimer - Renal function: ↑ Creatinine (acute tubular necrosis) - Kleihauer-Betke test: Feto-maternal hemorrhage (Rh-negative mothers)

USG: - Often normal (50% cases - USG insensitive for abruption) - May show: Retroplacental hematoma, placental thickening - Main role: Exclude placenta previa

NEET PG: USG often normal in abruption (clinical diagnosis)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Resuscitation (ABCD): - Airway, Breathing, Circulation - Large bore IV cannulas (2 lines) - Aggressive fluid resuscitation (crystalloids, blood products) - Blood transfusion: Aim Hb >7 g/dl - Correct coagulopathy: FFP, platelets, cryoprecipitate - Monitor: Vitals, urine output, fetal heart

Delivery: - If fetus alive: - Cesarean section (urgent) - if fetal distress, severe abruption - Vaginal delivery (expedite) - if mild, no fetal distress, rapid progress - ARM (artificial rupture of membranes) to ↓ intrauterine pressure - Oxytocin augmentation - If fetus dead: - Vaginal delivery (preferred) unless maternal instability - Cesarean only if uncontrolled bleeding or failed vaginal delivery

Postpartum: - Active management of 3rd stage (oxytocin) - Monitor for PPH (atonic uterus) - DIC monitoring

NEET PG High-Yield: - Live fetus + severe abruption: Emergency CS - Dead fetus: Vaginal delivery (unless uncontrolled bleeding) - ARM: Reduce intrauterine pressure

docs.title: Complications
docs.text:
Maternal: - Hemorrhagic shock - DIC (consumptive coagulopathy) - Acute kidney injury (ATN) - Couvelaire uterus: May need hysterectomy (rare) - PPH - Maternal death

Fetal: - Fetal distress - Fetal death (intrauterine) - Prematurity - IUGR - Anemia (feto-maternal hemorrhage)

NEET PG: DIC common in severe abruption (fetal thromboplastin release)

docs.title: VASA PREVIA - RARE but HIGH YIELD
Definition
docs.text: Fetal vessels (from velamentous cord insertion or accessory lobe) crossing internal os ahead of presenting part

docs.title: Risk Factors
docs.text:
• Velamentous cord insertion
  • Succenturiate (accessory) placental lobe
  • Low-lying placenta
  • IVF pregnancy

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
Classic triad: 1. Rupture of membranes (spontaneous or amniotomy) 2. Painless vaginal bleeding 3. Fetal distress (sudden bradycardia)

Key: Bleeding after rupture of membranes + fetal distress

NEET PG: Vasa previa = Bleeding after ROM + sudden fetal distress

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
Antenatal: - Color Doppler USG: Vessels over internal os - Transvaginal USG: Gold standard

Intrapartum: - Apt test: Differentiate fetal vs maternal blood - Fetal blood: Pink (resistant to alkali) - Maternal blood: Brown (denatures)

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Antenatal: - Hospitalization at 30-32 weeks - Corticosteroids for lung maturity - Elective CS at 34-36 weeks (before labor/ROM)

Intrapartum: Emergency CS (if ROM + bleeding)

NEET PG: - Vasa previa: Elective CS before labor (prevent rupture) - Apt test: Fetal vs maternal blood differentiation

docs.title: PLACENTA PREVIA vs ABRUPTION - Comparison
docs.text:
Feature Placenta Previa Placental Abruption Bleeding Painless Painful Color Bright red Dark red, clotted Onset Recurrent, small amounts Sudden, severe Uterus Soft, non-tender Tender, rigid (woody) Shock Proportionate to bleeding Out of proportion (if concealed) Fetal heart Usually normal Distress or absent Lie Malpresentation common Usually normal USG Diagnostic (placenta location) Often normal Risk factor Previous CS Hypertension

NEET PG: Key differentiator = Pain (absent in previa, present in abruption)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most common cause of APH? Answer: Placenta previa & Abruption (30% each)

  2. Painless bright red bleeding in 3rd trimester? Answer: Placenta previa

  3. Why avoid PV examination in APH? Answer: Can provoke massive bleeding in placenta previa

  4. Most important risk factor for placenta previa? Answer: Previous cesarean section

  5. Most important risk factor for abruption? Answer: Hypertension (pre-eclampsia)

  6. Concealed hemorrhage more dangerous than revealed? Answer: Yes (shock > visible bleeding, hypovolemia)

  7. Bleeding after ROM + fetal distress? Answer: Vasa previa

  8. Woody hard uterus indicates? Answer: Placental abruption (severe)

docs.title: Placenta Previa
docs.text: "PPP - **P**ainless **P**revia, **P**recious (bright red)"

docs.title: Abruption
docs.text: "APART - **A**bruption is **P**ainful, **A**gonizing, **R**igid, **T**ense"

docs.title: Vasa Previa Triad
docs.text: "**R**OM **B**leeds **F**etus (Distress)" - **R**upture of membranes - **B**leeding - **F**etal distress

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know APH definition (>20 weeks)
  • Understand placenta previa classification
  • Learn risk factors for both conditions
  • Differentiate previa vs abruption (table)
  • Know investigation findings
  • Understand management principles
  • Learn complications of both
  • Know vasa previa presentation
  • Practice 35+ MCQs on APH

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (PPH) - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text:
Primary PPH: Blood loss ≥500 mL (vaginal delivery) or ≥1000 mL (cesarean section) within 24 hours of delivery

Secondary PPH: Excessive bleeding >24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum

Severe PPH: Blood loss ≥1000 mL OR signs of hemodynamic instability

NEET PG: PPH = Blood loss ≥500 mL within 24 hours (vaginal delivery)

docs.title: Epidemiology
docs.text:
• Incidence: 5-10% of deliveries
  • Leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide (25% of maternal deaths)
  • Most common in first 24 hours (primary PPH)

docs.title: Etiology - "4 Ts"
docs.text:
Cause Percentage Mnemonic **T**one (Uterine atony) 70% MOST COMMON **T**rauma (Genital tract) 20% **T**issue (Retained products) 10% **T**hrombin (Coagulopathy) 1%

NEET PG: Uterine atony = Most common cause of PPH (70%)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Failure of uterus to contract after delivery of placenta → Bleeding from placental site

docs.title: Risk Factors - "ATONY"
docs.text:
**A**bruptio placentae, Anemia **T**wins/Multiple gestation **O**verdistended uterus (Polyhydramnios, Macrosomia) **N**ultiparous (Grand multiparity >5) **Y**oung maternal age, prolonged **Y**ears (labor)

Others: - Prolonged labor (>12 hours) - Precipitous labor (<3 hours) - Chorioamnionitis - Uterine fibroids - Previous PPH - Magnesium sulfate (tocolytic) - General anesthesia - Halogenated agents (relax uterus)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Grand multiparity: Uterine muscle fatigue - Overdistended uterus: Twins, polyhydramnios, macrosomia - Prolonged labor: Uterine exhaustion

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Uterus: Soft, boggy, poorly contracted
  • Continuous bleeding (no clots initially)
  • Per vaginal bleeding with blood clots

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Clinical: Soft, non-contracted uterus
  • Bimanual examination: Uterus does not contract with massage

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
A. Cervical Laceration - Cause: Rapid/precipitous delivery, forceps, large baby - Signs: Bright red bleeding despite contracted uterus - Diagnosis: Speculum examination (visualize cervix) - Treatment: Suturing under anesthesia

B. Vaginal Laceration - Cause: Operative delivery (forceps, vacuum) - Types: - Sulcus tears: Lateral vaginal wall - Periurethral tears - Treatment: Suturing

C. Perineal Tears (see classification below)

D. Uterine Rupture - Cause: Obstructed labor, previous cesarean scar, trauma - Signs: Sudden severe pain, shock, fetal distress - Diagnosis: Clinical + imaging - Treatment: Emergency laparotomy, repair/hysterectomy

E. Uterine Inversion - Definition: Uterus turns inside-out - Cause: Excessive cord traction, fundal pressure - Signs: Sudden shock, mass at introitus, severe pain - Treatment: Immediate manual replacement (Johnson's maneuver) + tocolysis

NEET PG: Bleeding despite contracted uterus = Genital tract trauma (do speculum exam)

docs.title: Perineal Tear Classification
docs.text:
Degree Involvement Management First Vaginal mucosa, perineal skin May not need suturing (minor) Second Perineal muscles (NOT anal sphincter) Suture in layers Third Anal sphincter involved Repair in OR by experienced surgeon Fourth Anal sphincter + Rectal mucosa Repair in OR, broad-spectrum antibiotics

NEET PG: 3rd & 4th degree tears involve anal sphincter (repair in OR)

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
A. Retained Placenta - Definition: Placenta not delivered within 30 minutes of baby - Types: - Adherent placenta: No plane of cleavage - Trapped placenta: Cervix closes before delivery - Placenta accreta/increta/percreta: Abnormal placental invasion

B. Retained Placental Fragments - Incomplete placental delivery - Cotyledon missing on inspection

C. Retained Blood Clots - Large clots prevent uterine contraction

D. Retained Membranes

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Boggy uterus despite massage
  • Intermittent bleeding

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Placental inspection: Missing cotyledon/vessels going to edge
  • Bimanual examination: Uterus larger than expected
  • Ultrasound: Echogenic material in uterine cavity

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
• Manual removal of placenta (under anesthesia)
  • Uterine curettage (if fragments)
  • Oxytocics

NEET PG: Retained placenta = Not delivered within 30 minutes → Manual removal

docs.title: Abnormal Placentation
docs.text:
Type Definition Incidence Placenta accreta Adherent to myometrium (no decidua basalis) 80% Placenta increta Invades into myometrium 15% Placenta percreta Penetrates through serosa (bladder, bowel) 5%

Risk Factors: - Previous cesarean section (MOST IMPORTANT) - Placenta previa - Multiparity - Previous uterine surgery

Diagnosis: - Antenatal: USG, MRI (loss of retroplacental clear zone) - Delivery: Placenta does not separate, bleeding

Management: - Cesarean hysterectomy (placenta left in situ) - Avoid forceful placental removal (massive hemorrhage)

NEET PG: Placenta accreta = Previous cesarean + Placenta previa (high risk)

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
Pre-existing: - Von Willebrand disease - Hemophilia carrier - ITP - Anticoagulant therapy

Acquired: - DIC (most common in obstetrics) - HELLP syndrome - Massive transfusion (dilutional coagulopathy)

docs.title: DIC in Obstetrics - Causes
docs.text:
"ASAP" - **A**bruption (placental abruption) - MOST COMMON - **S**epsis - **A**mniotic fluid embolism - **P**re-eclampsia/HELLP

Clinical Features: - Generalized oozing (IV sites, gums) - No clot formation - Petechiae, ecchymoses

Diagnosis: - ↑ PT, ↑ aPTT - ↓ Platelets (<100,000) - ↓ Fibrinogen (<200 mg/dL) - ↑ D-dimer, ↑ FDP

Management: - Treat underlying cause - FFP (fresh frozen plasma) - Cryoprecipitate (if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL) - Platelet transfusion (if <50,000)

NEET PG: Placental abruption = Most common obstetric cause of DIC

docs.title: Signs of Shock
docs.text:
• Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
  • Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)
  • Tachypnea
  • Oliguria (<30 mL/hr)
  • Altered consciousness
  • Cold, clammy skin

docs.title: Blood Loss Estimation (often underestimated)
docs.text:
Blood Loss Clinical Features 500-1000 mL Tachycardia (100-110), normal BP 1000-1500 mL Tachycardia (110-120), mild hypotension, anxiety 1500-2000 mL Tachycardia (>120), hypotension, oliguria, confusion >2000 mL Severe shock, collapse, anuria

NEET PG: Tachycardia is earliest sign of hypovolemia

docs.title: Active Management of Third Stage of Labor (AMTSL)
docs.text:
Components (after delivery of baby): 1. Oxytocin 10 units IM/IV immediately after anterior shoulder 2. Controlled cord traction (after signs of placental separation) 3. Uterine massage after placental delivery

Benefits: ↓ PPH by 60%, ↓ severe PPH, ↓ need for transfusion

NEET PG: AMTSL = Oxytocin + Controlled cord traction + Uterine massage (reduces PPH)

docs.title: Initial Resuscitation (ABCD)
docs.text: A**irway: Maintain patency **B**reathing: O2 15 L/min **C**irculation: - **2 large-bore IV cannulae (14-16G) - Rapid IV fluids (crystalloids: NS/RL) - Blood sampling: CBC, coagulation profile, cross-match **D**rugs: Uterotonics

docs.title: 1. TONE (Uterine Atony) - MOST COMMON
docs.text:
Step-by-step approach:

Step 1: Uterine Massage - Bimanual uterine compression - Rub fundus to stimulate contraction

Step 2: Uterotonics (in order of use)

A. Oxytocin (First-line): - 10 units IM/IV bolus - 20-40 units in 1L NS IV infusion - Mechanism: Contracts uterus - Side effects: Hypotension (if rapid IV), water intoxication

B. Methylergometrine (Second-line): - 0.2 mg IM (can repeat after 15 min) - Mechanism: Sustained uterine contraction - Contraindications: Hypertension, pre-eclampsia, heart disease - Side effects: HTN, nausea, vomiting

C. Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) (Third-line): - 250 μg IM (can repeat every 15 min, max 8 doses) - Mechanism: Uterine contraction - Contraindications: Asthma (bronchospasm) - Side effects: Diarrhea, fever, bronchospasm

D. Misoprostol (PGE1): - 800-1000 μg (PR/sublingual) - Mechanism: Uterine contraction - Advantage: Stable at room temperature, cheap - Side effects: Fever, shivering

NEET PG High-Yield: - First-line: Oxytocin - Methylergometrine: Contraindicated in hypertension - Carboprost: Contraindicated in asthma

Step 3: Uterine Tamponade - Intrauterine balloon (Bakri balloon, Foley catheter) - Fill with 300-500 mL saline - Provides tamponade effect

Step 4: Conservative Surgical

Uterine compression sutures: - B-Lynch suture: Brace suture (most common) - Cho suture

Pelvic vessel ligation: - Uterine artery ligation - Internal iliac (hypogastric) artery ligation

Step 5: Radiological - Uterine artery embolization (if available, hemodynamically stable)

Step 6: Hysterectomy (Last Resort) - Subtotal hysterectomy preferred (faster, less blood loss) - Total hysterectomy if cervical involvement

NEET PG: Management ladder = Massage → Oxytocin → Methylergometrine → Carboprost → Balloon → Surgery

docs.title: 2. TRAUMA
docs.text:
Cervical/Vaginal Lacerations: - Speculum examination (good lighting, anesthesia) - Suturing (absorbable sutures)

Uterine Rupture: - Emergency laparotomy - Repair (if feasible) or Hysterectomy

Uterine Inversion: - Manual replacement (Johnson's maneuver): - Cup inverted fundus in palm - Push upward through cervix - Do NOT remove placenta if still attached (causes bleeding) - Tocolytics (relax uterus): Terbutaline, nitroglycerin, halothane - After replacement: Oxytocics
